# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define a parametric model in machine learning. Give two examples. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **parametric model** is one whose functional form is completely specified by a fixed, finite number of parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta} \in \mathbb{R}^d$, regardless of the size of the training set. The model assumes a specific probability distribution or functional relationship, and learning reduces to estimating the values of these parameters from data.

**Two examples:**
1. **Logistic Regression** — defined by weights $\boldsymbol{\theta} \in \mathbb{R}^{d+1}$ and a sigmoid activation.
2. **Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA)** — defined by class means $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$ and a shared covariance $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for property, 1 for examples.)*

---

### **Q2.** State Bayes' Theorem as used in parametric classification. What is the Bayes optimal classifier? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Bayes' Theorem:**

$$
P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k)\,P(C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}
$$

The **Bayes optimal classifier** assigns the class with the highest posterior probability:

$$
h^*(\mathbf{x}) = \arg\max_k P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \arg\max_k P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k)\,P(C_k)
$$

This achieves the **minimum possible error rate** (the Bayes error), which is the lowest error any classifier can attain on that data distribution.

*(3 marks — 1 for formula, 1 for classifier definition, 1 for optimality statement.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** — Full 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Question]`

> **Q. (a)** Explain Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) for a univariate Gaussian distribution. Derive the MLE estimates of the mean and variance. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q. (b)** Using Bayes' Theorem and the assumption of Gaussian class-conditional densities with **shared covariance matrix**, derive the **Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA)** decision rule. State when LDA is preferred over QDA. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Define the Gaussian likelihood for a single sample $x_i$:**

$$
P(x_i \mid \mu, \sigma^2) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(x_i-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Joint likelihood over $N$ i.i.d. samples:**

$$
L(\mu,\sigma^2) = \prod_{i=1}^{N}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(x_i-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Log-likelihood (transform products to sums):**

$$
\ell(\mu,\sigma^2) = -\frac{N}{2}\log(2\pi) - N\log\sigma - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i-\mu)^2
$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\mu$ and set to zero:**

$$
\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \mu} = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu) = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \boxed{\hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}x_i}
$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\sigma$ and set to zero:**

$$
\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \sigma} = -\frac{N}{\sigma} + \frac{1}{\sigma^3}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i-\mu)^2 = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \boxed{\hat{\sigma}^2_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i-\hat{\mu})^2}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Bayes decision rule:** Choose the class maximizing $\pi_k P(\mathbf{x}\mid C_k)$.

**Step 2 — Plug in multivariate Gaussian with shared $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$:**

$$
\pi_k P(\mathbf{x}\mid C_k) = \pi_k \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2}\vert\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\vert^{1/2}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_k)^{\!\top}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_k)\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Take log, drop constants independent of $k$:**

$$
\delta_k(\mathbf{x}) = \log\pi_k - \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_k)^{\!\top}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_k)
$$

**Step 4 — Expand the quadratic and cancel the $\mathbf{x}^{\!\top}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}\mathbf{x}$ term (common to all $k$):**

$$
\delta_k(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^{\!\top}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_k - \frac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{\!\top}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_k + \log\pi_k
$$

**Step 5 — Final LDA decision rule:**

$$
h^*(\mathbf{x}) = \arg\max_k \left[\mathbf{x}^{\!\top}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_k - \frac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{\!\top}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_k + \log\pi_k\right]
$$

**When is LDA preferred over QDA?**
- When **classes have approximately equal covariance structure** (homoscedasticity).
- When the **training set is small** (LDA has fewer parameters → less variance).
- When **interpretability** of a linear boundary is desired.
- When **dimensionality is high** relative to $N$ (QDA overfits in such cases).

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** — Full 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

> **Q. (a)** Explain the **Naive Bayes Classifier** with its core assumption. Derive the classification rule for a binary classification problem with categorical features. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q. (b)** Derive the **logistic regression cost function** (binary cross-entropy) from the principle of Maximum Likelihood. Show the gradient update rule. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Core Assumption — Conditional Independence:**

Given class label $C_k$, the features $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_d$ are conditionally independent:

$$
P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) = P(x_1,x_2,\dots,x_d \mid C_k) = \prod_{j=1}^{d} P(x_j \mid C_k)
$$

This is the "naive" assumption — it is often violated in practice, yet the classifier works surprisingly well.

**Step 2 — Bayes' Rule applied with Naive Assumption:**

$$
P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto P(C_k)\prod_{j=1}^{d} P(x_j \mid C_k)
$$

**Step 3 — Decision Rule for Binary Classification ($k \in \{0, 1\}$):**

$$
\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k \in \{0,1\}} P(C_k)\prod_{j=1}^{d} P(x_j \mid C_k)
$$

**Step 4 — Parameter Estimation via MLE for Categorical Features:**

For each class $k$ and feature $j$ with possible values $\{1, 2, \dots, V_j\}$:

$$
\hat{P}(x_j = v \mid C_k) = \frac{\text{Count}(x_j = v \text{ AND } C_k) + 1}{\text{Count}(C_k) + V_j}
$$

The "+1" in the numerator is **Laplace (additive) smoothing** to avoid zero probabilities.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Model the Probability:**

$$
P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta}) = \hat{y} = \sigma(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\!\top}\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\!\top}\mathbf{x}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compact Likelihood for Bernoulli:**

$$
P(y_i \mid \mathbf{x}_i; \boldsymbol{\theta}) = \hat{y}_i^{y_i}(1-\hat{y}_i)^{1-y_i}
$$

**Step 3 — Log-Likelihood:**

$$
\ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\bigl[y_i\log\hat{y}_i + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{y}_i)\bigr]
$$

**Step 4 — Cost Function (Negative Average Log-Likelihood):**

$$
J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\bigl[y_i\log\hat{y}_i + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{y}_i)\bigr]
$$

**Step 5 — Gradient (using the property $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$):**

$$
\boxed{\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(\hat{y}_i - y_i)\,\mathbf{x}_i}
$$

**Step 6 — Gradient Descent Update Rule:**

$$
\boldsymbol{\theta} \leftarrow \boldsymbol{\theta} - \alpha\,\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta})
$$

where $\alpha$ is the **learning rate**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in MLE** — students often write the cost as the *positive* log-likelihood, which would lead to *gradient ascent* instead of *gradient descent*. Always negate the log-likelihood to form a cost function to minimize.
> 2. **Confusing MLE variance with sample variance** — MLE uses divisor $N$ (biased for variance). The unbiased estimator uses $N-1$. Examiners **specifically test this distinction**.
> 3. **Forgetting to drop constant terms in LDA** — many students leave the $-\frac{d}{2}\log(2\pi)$ and $-\frac{1}{2}\log\vert\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\vert$ terms in the discriminant function. They are constants and **must be dropped** for the $\arg\max$.
> 4. **Forgetting Laplace smoothing in Naive Bayes** — when a categorical value never appears in training for a class, its probability becomes zero, zeroing out the entire product. Always add $\mathbf{+1}$ to the numerator (and $V_j$ to the denominator).
> 5. **Sigmoid derivative mistake** — writing $\sigma'(z) = 1 - \sigma(z)$ (forgetting the product). The correct identity is $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Parametric Classification**

- ✅ A **parametric model** has a **fixed number of parameters** independent of $N$.
- ✅ **Bayes' Theorem** is the central bridge: $P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x}\mid C_k)P(C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$.
- ✅ **Bayes optimal classifier**: $h^*(\mathbf{x}) = \arg\max_k P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x})$ — minimum possible error.
- ✅ **MLE for univariate Gaussian**: $\hat{\mu} = \bar{x}$ and $\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum(x_i - \hat{\mu})^2$ (note $N$, not $N-1$).
- ✅ **LDA** assumes **shared covariance** → **linear** boundary; **QDA** uses per-class covariance → **quadratic** boundary.
- ✅ **LDA discriminant**: $\delta_k(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^{\!\top}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_k - \frac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{\!\top}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_k + \log\pi_k$.
- ✅ **Logistic regression** is **discriminative** (models $P(C\mid\mathbf{x})$ directly) — does NOT assume Gaussian $P(\mathbf{x}\mid C)$.
- ✅ **Logistic cost (Binary Cross-Entropy)**: $J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = -\frac{1}{N}\sum[y\log\hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})]$.
- ✅ **Logistic gradient**: $\nabla J = \frac{1}{N}\sum(\hat{y}_i - y_i)\mathbf{x}_i$ — elegant because the $\sigma'(z)$ terms cancel with the log derivatives.
- ✅ **Naive Bayes** uses **conditional independence** assumption; remember **Laplace smoothing** ($+1$ in numerator).
- ✅ **LDA is generative**, **Logistic Regression is discriminative** — both produce **linear boundaries** but from different angles.
- ✅ **QDA has $K \cdot d(d+1)/2$** covariance parameters vs. **LDA's $d(d+1)/2$** — QDA is more expressive but needs more data.
- ✅ **Sigmoid identity**: $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$ — appears in nearly every KTU logistic regression derivation.
- ✅ For **multiclass**, logistic regression generalizes to **softmax regression** using the multinomial likelihood.
- ✅ **Parametric models converge faster** with limited data, but may underfit if the assumed form is wrong. **Non-parametric models** are more flexible but need more data and are slower at prediction.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regression
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT501 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Regression

# 📘 Module 1 — Regression in Machine Learning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Anchor:** This topic is a foundational pillar of **Supervised Learning** in the **PCITT501 - Machine Learning** syllabus. It is heavily weighted in the **Continuous Evaluation (CE)** short-answer sets and forms the base of Part B numerical problems in the **End Semester Examination (ESE)**.

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Regression** is a **Supervised Machine Learning** technique used to model and predict a **continuous dependent variable** (target/output, denoted as $y$) based on one or more **independent variables** (features/inputs, denoted as $X = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$). The objective is to learn a mapping function $f: X \rightarrow y$ such that $y \approx f(X; \theta) + \epsilon$, where $\theta$ represents the learnable parameters and $\epsilon$ is the irreducible error term.

In the KTU 2024 framework, regression is classified under the broader umbrella of **predictive modeling**, where the output space $\mathcal{Y} \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ is continuous and uncountably infinite (as opposed to classification, where $\mathcal{Y}$ is discrete).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "House Price Predictor"**
> Imagine you are a real-estate agent in Kerala trying to predict the price of a house. You notice from historical data that:
> - Bigger houses cost more (area matters)
> - Houses near Technopark, Trivandrum cost more (location matters)
> - Houses with more bedrooms cost more (BHK count matters)
>
> **Regression is exactly this intuition formalized mathematically.** You are drawing the "best-fit line" (or curve) through a cloud of data points representing past sales, so that for any *new* house, you can simply plug in its features and read off a price.

### 1.3 Types of Regression Covered in KTU Module 1

| Type | Mathematical Form | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple Linear Regression** | $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$ | Single feature, linear relationship |
| **Multiple Linear Regression** | $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \dots + \beta_n x_n$ | Multiple features, linear relationship |
| **Polynomial Regression** | $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \beta_2 x^2 + \dots + \beta_d x^d$ | Non-linear relationship between $X$ and $y$ |
| **Ridge Regression** | OLS + $\lambda \sum \beta_j^2$ | L2 regularization to prevent overfitting |
| **Lasso Regression** | OLS + $\lambda \sum \vert \beta_j \vert$ | L1 regularization for feature selection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for Exams:** The difference between **regression** and **classification** is the **nature of the output**. If you are predicting a *number* (price, temperature, salary) → **Regression**. If you are predicting a *category* (spam/not-spam, cat/dog) → **Classification**.

### 1.4 Assumptions of Linear Regression (Gauss-Markov Conditions)

For the **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)** estimator to be the **Best Linear Unbiased Estimator (BLUE)**, the following five assumptions must hold:

1. **Linearity:** The relationship between $X$ and $y$ is linear in parameters.
2. **Independence:** Observations are independent of each other.
3. **Homoscedasticity:** The variance of error terms $\epsilon_i$ is constant across all levels of $X$ (i.e., $\text{Var}(\epsilon_i) = \sigma^2$).
4. **Normality:** Error terms $\epsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ (normally distributed).
5. **No Multicollinearity:** Independent variables are not highly correlated with each other.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU frequently asks: *"State the assumptions of linear regression."* Memorize these 5 points verbatim.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Regression Hypothesis Function

The hypothesis function for a linear regression model with $n$ features is:

$$
h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1 + \theta_2 x_2 + \dots + \theta_n x_n
$$

In **vectorized form**, this becomes elegantly compact:

$$
h_\theta(x) = \theta^T x
$$

where $\theta, x \in \mathbb{R}^{(n+1) \times 1}$ are augmented vectors with a bias term $\theta_0$ and a constant feature $x_0 = 1$.

### 2.2 The Cost Function: Mean Squared Error (MSE)

To measure how "wrong" our predictions are, we define a **cost function** $J(\theta)$ that quantifies the total error across all $m$ training examples.

$$
J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2
$$

**Why "squared error"?**
- Squaring penalizes **large errors** disproportionately (exponentially worse).
- It makes the function **differentiable** everywhere, enabling gradient-based optimization.
- The factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ is a mathematical convenience to cancel the **2** that emerges during differentiation.

### 2.3 Optimization: Two Approaches

#### Approach A: The Normal Equation (Closed-Form Solution)

The optimal parameter vector $\theta$ can be computed in **one shot** using the matrix inverse:

$$
\theta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y
$$

**Why This Works:** Setting the gradient $\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = 0$ and solving for $\theta$ gives this analytical solution.

**Trade-offs:**
- ✅ No need to choose a learning rate $\alpha$.
- ✅ Guaranteed global optimum (since $J$ is convex).
- ❌ Computationally expensive for large $n$ (inverting an $(n \times n)$ matrix is $O(n^3)$).
- ❌ Fails if $X^T X$ is **non-invertible** (singular).

#### Approach B: Gradient Descent (Iterative Solution)

Repeatedly update $\theta$ in the direction of steepest descent:

$$
\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} J(\theta)
$$

Where the partial derivative computes to:

$$
\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Learning Rate $\alpha$ is the most critical hyperparameter.** If $\alpha$ is too small → convergence is painfully slow. If $\alpha$ is too large → the algorithm may **diverge** or oscillate.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Hypothesis (Vectorized)** | $h_\theta(x) = \theta^T x$ | Predicted output for input $x$ |
| **Cost Function (MSE)** | $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2$ | Total error across $m$ samples |
| **Normal Equation** | $\theta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$ | Closed-form solution |
| **Gradient Descent Update** | $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) x_j^{(i)}$ | Iterative parameter update |
| **MSE (Metric)** | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Mean Squared Error |
| **RMSE (Metric)** | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ | Root Mean Squared Error (same unit as $y$) |
| **MAE (Metric)** | $\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | Mean Absolute Error |
| **R² (Coefficient of Determination)** | $R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}$ | Proportion of variance explained |
| **Ridge Penalty** | $\lambda \sum_{j=1}^{n} \theta_j^2$ | L2 regularization term |
| **Lasso Penalty** | $\lambda \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert \theta_j \vert$ | L1 regularization term |
| **Variance of Estimator** | $\text{Var}(\hat{\beta}) = \sigma^2 (X^T X)^{-1}$ | Standard error of $\hat{\beta}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Reminder:** In some KTU textbooks, $\beta$ is used instead of $\theta$. They are identical: $\beta_0 = \theta_0$ (intercept), $\beta_1 = \theta_1$ (slope).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Regression is the **backbone of countless engineering applications**:
- **Civil Engineering:** Predicting concrete compressive strength from cement, water, and aggregate ratios.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Forecasting power grid load demand.
- **Computer Science (KTU's focus):** Predicting house prices, stock market trends, student GPA from attendance/quiz scores.
- **Biomedical Engineering:** Modeling drug dosage-response relationships.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Normal Equation (Full Derivation)

We start with the cost function in matrix form. For $m$ training examples and $n$ features, let $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times (n+1)}$ be the design matrix and $y \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times 1}$ be the target vector.

**Step 1:** Express the cost function in matrix form.

$$
J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} (X\theta - y)^T (X\theta - y)
$$

**Step 2:** Expand the matrix product using the distributive property of transpose $(AB)^T = B^T A^T$.

$$
J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \left[ \theta^T X^T X \theta - \theta^T X^T y - y^T X \theta + y^T y \right]
$$

**Step 3:** Recognize that the middle two terms are scalars and therefore equal: $\theta^T X^T y = y^T X \theta$. Combine them.

$$
J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \left[ \theta^T X^T X \theta - 2\theta^T X^T y + y^T y \right]
$$

**Step 4:** Differentiate $J(\theta)$ with respect to $\theta$ using the standard matrix calculus rules:
- $\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta} (\theta^T A \theta) = 2A\theta$ (when $A$ is symmetric)
- $\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta} (b^T \theta) = b$
- $\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta} (c) = 0$ (constant)

$$
\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta} = \frac{1}{2m} \left[ 2 X^T X \theta - 2 X^T y \right]
$$

**Step 5:** Simplify the constants.

$$
\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta} = \frac{1}{m} \left[ X^T X \theta - X^T y \right]
$$

**Step 6:** Set the gradient to zero for the minimum (this is valid because $J$ is convex).

$$
X^T X \theta - X^T y = 0
$$

**Step 7:** Solve for $\theta$.

$$
X^T X \theta = X^T y
$$

**Step 8:** Pre-multiply both sides by $(X^T X)^{-1}$, assuming the inverse exists.

$$
\theta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The Normal Equation gives the **global minimum** in one step, but requires matrix inversion which is $O(n^3)$ — too slow when $n > 10,000$. For high-dimensional problems, **Gradient Descent** is preferred.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Gradient Descent Update Rule

Starting from the cost function:

$$
J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2
$$

**Step 1:** Compute the partial derivative with respect to a single parameter $\theta_j$.

$$
\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} \left[ \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2 \right]
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the chain rule. The inner term $h_\theta(x^{(i)}) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1^{(i)} + \dots + \theta_n x_n^{(i)}$ has derivative $\frac{\partial h}{\partial \theta_j} = x_j^{(i)}$.

$$
\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} 2 \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} h_\theta(x^{(i)})
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $\frac{\partial h_\theta(x^{(i)})}{\partial \theta_j} = x_j^{(i)}$ and cancel the 2.

$$
\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the Gradient Descent update rule.

$$
\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Simultaneous Update:** The KTU board often asks whether the updates are simultaneous or sequential. The correct implementation uses **simultaneous update** — compute all new $\theta_j$ values using the *old* $\theta$ values, then update them all at once.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: R² (Coefficient of Determination)

The R² metric measures what fraction of the total variance in $y$ is explained by the model.

**Step 1:** Define **Total Sum of Squares (TSS)** — the variance of $y$ around its mean.

$$
\text{TSS} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} (y_i - \bar{y})^2
$$

**Step 2:** Define **Residual Sum of Squares (RSS)** — the unexplained variance.

$$
\text{RSS} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2
$$

**Step 3:** Define **R²** as the ratio of explained variance to total variance.

$$
R^2 = 1 - \frac{\text{RSS}}{\text{TSS}}
$$

**Step 4:** Expand to its expanded form for use in problems.

$$
R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{m} (y_i - \bar{y})^2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation Rules:**
> - $R^2 = 1$: Model explains 100% of the variance (perfect fit).
> - $R^2 = 0$: Model is no better than predicting the mean.
> - $R^2 < 0$: Model is worse than the mean (terrible fit).
> - **Adjusted R²** penalizes unnecessary features: $R^2_{\text{adj}} = 1 - (1-R^2)\frac{m-1}{m-n-1}$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What?

| Scenario | Recommended Method | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Small dataset ($m < 10^4$), few features ($n < 10^3$) | **Normal Equation** | Direct solution, no iterations |
| Large $n$ ($> 10^4$) | **Gradient Descent** | Inverting $(X^T X)$ is infeasible |
| $X^T X$ is singular | **Gradient Descent** or **Pseudo-inverse** | Normal equation fails |
| Non-linear relationship suspected | **Polynomial Regression** | Linear in $\theta$ but non-linear in $X$ |
| Many features, risk of overfitting | **Ridge / Lasso** | Regularization shrinks coefficients |
| Sparse model needed (feature selection) | **Lasso (L1)** | L1 drives coefficients to exactly zero |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Regression Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Dataset X and y] --> B[Data Preprocessing]
    B --> B1[Handle Missing Values]
    B --> B2[Feature Scaling Standardization]
    B --> B3[Train Test Split 80 20]
    B3 --> C{Choose Optimization}
    C -->|Small n| D[Normal Equation]
    C -->|Large n| E[Gradient Descent]
    D --> F[Optimal Parameters theta]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Prediction h theta of x]
    G --> H[Evaluation Metrics]
    H --> H1[MSE RMSE MAE]
    H --> H2[R2 Score]
    H1 --> I[Model Deployment]
    H2 --> I
```

### 4.2 Gradient Descent Convergence Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize theta randomly] --> B[Compute h theta of x for all samples]
    B --> C[Compute Cost J theta]
    C --> D{Difference less than epsilon}
    D -->|No| E[Compute Gradients]
    E --> F[Update theta j simultaneously]
    F --> B
    D -->|Yes| G[Converged Optimum Found]
```

### 4.3 Bias Variance Tradeoff Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Underfitting High Bias Low Variance] --> B[Optimal Model Balanced Bias and Variance]
    B --> C[Overfitting Low Bias High Variance]
    A -.->|Increase complexity| B
    B -.->|Too much complexity| C
    C -->|Apply Regularization| B
    A -->|Add more features| B
```

### 4.4 Cost Function J theta as a 3D Surface

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1[The Paraboloid Bowl]
    A[theta 0 axis intercept] --> C[Cost J theta minimum point]
    B[theta 1 axis slope] --> C
    end
    C --> D[Convex surface has global minimum]
    D --> E[Gradient descent rolls down to C]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Insight:** The cost function $J(\theta_0, \theta_1)$ in linear regression is **strictly convex** (bowl-shaped), which guarantees that gradient descent will find the **global minimum**, never getting stuck in a local minimum — unlike neural networks.

### 4.5 Regularization Comparison Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[OLS Objective Minimize MSE] --> B{Add Penalty}
    B -->|L2 norm squared| C[Ridge Regression Shrinks theta]
    B -->|L1 norm| D[Lasso Regression Sparse Selection]
    C --> E[All features retained small theta]
    D --> F[Irrelevant features theta = 0]
    E --> G[Final Model]
    F --> G
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Define regression. Differentiate between simple linear regression and multiple linear regression.**

**Model Answer:**

**Regression** is a supervised machine learning technique used to model the relationship between a continuous dependent variable $y$ and one or more independent variables by fitting a mathematical function to observed data.

**Differences:**

| Aspect | Simple Linear Regression | Multiple Linear Regression |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of features** | Single independent variable $x$ | Two or more independent variables $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$ |
| **Equation** | $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$ | $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \dots + \beta_n x_n$ |
| **Geometric form** | A straight line in 2D | A hyperplane in $(n+1)$-dimensional space |
| **Use case** | Simple trend analysis | Multi-factor prediction |

---

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
> **Explain the Mean Squared Error (MSE) cost function used in linear regression.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** cost function measures the average of the squares of the differences between the predicted values $h_\theta(x^{(i)})$ and the actual values $y^{(i)}$.

$$
J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2
$$

**Key Properties:**
- It is **non-negative** (squares are always $\geq 0$).
- It penalizes **larger errors** more heavily due to squaring.
- It is **differentiable** everywhere, making it suitable for gradient-based optimization.
- For linear regression, $J(\theta)$ is a **convex function**, guaranteeing a global minimum.

---

### 📝 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
>
> **(a)** Derive the Normal Equation for a linear regression model with $n$ features. Explain its advantages and limitations. **\[7 Marks\]**
>
> **(b)** Consider the following dataset with $m = 4$ samples and a single feature $x$: $(1, 2), (2, 3), (3, 5), (4, 7)$. Compute the optimal parameters $\theta_0$ and $\theta_1$ using the Normal Equation. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a): Derivation of the Normal Equation**

We minimize the cost function:

$$
J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} (X\theta - y)^T (X\theta - y)
$$

Expanding:

$$
J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \left[ \theta^T X^T X \theta - 2\theta^T X^T y + y^T y \right]
$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $\theta$ and setting to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta} = \frac{1}{m} (X^T X \theta - X^T y) = 0
$$

Solving:

$$
X^T X \theta = X^T y \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y
$$

**Advantages:**
- Direct closed-form solution (no iterations).
- No learning rate to tune.
- Guaranteed global optimum due to convexity.

**Limitations:**
- $O(n^3)$ complexity for inverting $(X^T X)$.
- Fails if $X^T X$ is singular (no inverse).
- Memory-intensive for large $n$.

**Part (b): Numerical Computation**

**Step 1:** Construct the design matrix $X$ (with bias column) and target vector $y$.

$$
X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix}, \quad y = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \\ 5 \\ 7 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $X^T X$.

$$
X^T X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 10 \\ 10 & 30 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $X^T y$.

$$
X^T y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \\ 5 \\ 7 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 17 \\ 55 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $(X^T X)^{-1}$. Determinant $= (4)(30) - (10)(10) = 120 - 100 = 20$.

$$
(X^T X)^{-1} = \frac{1}{20} \begin{bmatrix} 30 & -10 \\ -10 & 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 & -0.5 \\ -0.5 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute $\theta$.

$$
\theta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 & -0.5 \\ -0.5 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 17 \\ 55 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 25.5 - 27.5 \\ -8.5 + 11 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 \\ 2.5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\theta_0 = -0.5$ and $\theta_1 = 2.5$. The regression line is $\hat{y} = -0.5 + 2.5x$.

---

> **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
>
> **(a)** Explain the Gradient Descent algorithm for linear regression. Discuss the role of the learning rate $\alpha$ and what happens when it is too small or too large. **\[7 Marks\]**
>
> **(b)** For the regression line $\hat{y} = 0.5 + 1.2x$ fitted on data with $m = 5$ samples, the actual values are $y = [3, 5, 4, 6, 8]$ and features are $x = [2, 3, 3, 4, 6]$. Compute MSE, RMSE, MAE, and R². **\[7 Marks\]**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a): Gradient Descent Algorithm**

Gradient Descent is an **iterative optimization algorithm** that updates parameters in the direction of steepest descent of the cost function.

**Update Rule:**

$$
\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j} = \theta_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) x_j^{(i)}
$$

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Initialize $\theta_0, \theta_1, \dots, \theta_n$ to zero or small random values.
2. Compute the predicted values $h_\theta(x^{(i)})$ for all $i$.
3. Compute the gradient of $J(\theta)$.
4. Update all $\theta_j$ **simultaneously**.
5. Repeat steps 2–4 until convergence (cost change $< \epsilon$).

**Role of Learning Rate $\alpha$:**

| $\alpha$ Value | Effect |
|---|---|
| **Too small** | Slow convergence; takes many iterations to reach minimum |
| **Too large** | Overshoots minimum; may oscillate or diverge entirely |
| **Just right** | Smooth convergence in few iterations |

**Convergence Guarantee:** For linear regression, since $J$ is convex, gradient descent with appropriately chosen $\alpha$ will always converge to the global minimum.

**Part (b): Numerical Evaluation**

**Step 1:** Compute predictions $\hat{y}_i = 0.5 + 1.2 x_i$.

$$
\hat{y} = [3.4,\ 4.6,\ 4.6,\ 5.8,\ 8.2]
$$

**Step 2:** Compute residuals $(y_i - \hat{y}_i)$.

$$
\text{Residuals} = [-0.4,\ 0.4,\ -0.6,\ 0.2,\ -0.2]
$$

**Step 3:** Compute squared errors.

$$
[0.16,\ 0.16,\ 0.36,\ 0.04,\ 0.04]
$$

**Step 4:** Compute MSE.

$$
\text{MSE} = \frac{0.16 + 0.16 + 0.36 + 0.04 + 0.04}{5} = \frac{0.76}{5} = 0.152
$$

**Step 5:** Compute RMSE.

$$
\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{0.152} \approx 0.3899
$$

**Step 6:** Compute MAE.

$$
\text{MAE} = \frac{0.4 + 0.4 + 0.6 + 0.2 + 0.2}{5} = \frac{1.8}{5} = 0.36
$$

**Step 7:** Compute R². First, find the mean $\bar{y} = \frac{3+5+4+6+8}{5} = 5.2$.

$$
\text{TSS} = (3-5.2)^2 + (5-5.2)^2 + (4-5.2)^2 + (6-5.2)^2 + (8-5.2)^2
$$

$$
\text{TSS} = 4.84 + 0.04 + 1.44 + 0.64 + 7.84 = 14.8
$$

$$
\text{RSS} = 0.76
$$

$$
R^2 = 1 - \frac{0.76}{14.8} = 1 - 0.0514 = 0.9486
$$

**Final Answers:** MSE $= 0.152$, RMSE $\approx 0.390$, MAE $= 0.36$, $R^2 \approx 0.949$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Transpose Confusion:** In the Normal Equation, students often write $(X X^T)^{-1}$ instead of $(X^T X)^{-1}$. Remember: the inner product is always on the smaller dimension. For $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$, we use $X^T X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$.
> 2. **Missing Bias Column:** When applying the Normal Equation numerically, you **must** prepend a column of 1's to $X$ to account for $\theta_0$. Forgetting this gives a slope-only model and loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Simultaneous vs Sequential Update:** In Gradient Descent, compute all new $\theta_j$ values first using the OLD $\theta$, then update. Sequential update within the same loop is a **logic error**.
> 4. **R² Interpretation:** $R^2$ can be **negative** when the model is worse than the mean. Don't blindly say "R² is between 0 and 1" — that holds only for OLS on training data.
> 5. **MAE vs MSE:** MAE uses **absolute value** (not square root of square). Confusing these loses a mark.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Regression**

- ✅ **Definition:** Regression predicts a **continuous** output from input features using a learned mapping $f$.
- ✅ **Types:** Simple Linear, Multiple Linear, Polynomial, Ridge (L2), Lasso (L1).
- ✅ **Hypothesis:** $h_\theta(x) = \theta^T x$ (vectorized with bias augmentation).
- ✅ **Cost Function:** MSE = $\frac{1}{2m} \sum (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2$.
- ✅ **Normal Equation:** $\theta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$ — closed form, $O(n^3)$, no $\alpha$ needed.
- ✅ **Gradient Descent:** $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{1}{m} \sum (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) x_j^{(i)}$.
- ✅ **Learning Rate $\alpha$:** Too small → slow; Too large → diverge; Tune using validation.
- ✅ **Assumptions of Linear Regression:** Linearity, Independence, Homoscedasticity, Normality, No Multicollinearity.
- ✅ **Metrics:** MSE, RMSE (same unit as $y$), MAE, $R^2 \in (-\infty, 1]$.
- ✅ **R² Formula:** $R^2 = 1 - \frac{\text{RSS}}{\text{TSS}} = 1 - \frac{\sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum(y_i - \bar{y})^2}$.
- ✅ **Regularization:** Ridge (L2) shrinks all coefficients; Lasso (L1) sets some to zero (feature selection).
- ✅ **Polynomial Regression:** Still linear in parameters $\theta$ but non-linear in input $x$ (use $x^2, x^3, \dots$ as new features).
- ✅ **Convexity Guarantee:** Linear regression cost $J(\theta)$ is strictly convex → global minimum guaranteed.
- ✅ **Trade-off:** Normal Equation for small $n$, Gradient Descent for large $n$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tuning Model Complexity: Bias/Variance Trade-off
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT501 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Tuning Model Complexity: Bias/Variance Trade-off

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Bias-Variance Trade-off** is a fundamental concept in supervised learning that describes the inverse relationship between two sources of error that prevent generalization of a model to unseen data:

- **Bias**: The error introduced by approximating a real-world problem (which may be extremely complex) with a simplified model. It represents the model's inability to capture the true underlying relationship in the data (underfitting).

- **Variance**: The amount by which the model's predictions would change if we estimated it using a different training dataset. It measures the model's sensitivity to fluctuations in the training data (overfitting).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition**: The expected prediction error of a learning algorithm can be decomposed into three components: **Bias²**, **Variance**, and **Irreducible Error** (σ²). A modeler's goal is to identify the "sweet spot" of model complexity that minimizes the *total generalization error*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Darts Game

Imagine you are playing a game of darts, and the bullseye represents the **true function** $f(x)$ that we want to predict.

- **High Bias, Low Variance (Consistently Wrong)**: Imagine a person who has a stiff arm and always throws the dart at the same wrong spot — say, 4 inches to the left of the bullseye. Every throw lands in nearly the same location, but it is *systematically* far from the target. This is like an **underfit model** (e.g., a straight line fitted to a curved dataset) — it makes consistent but inaccurate predictions.

- **Low Bias, High Variance (Wildly Inconsistent)**: Now imagine a person who can aim at the bullseye on average, but every throw lands in a different, scattered location. On average they hit the target, but no single throw is reliable. This is like an **overfit model** (e.g., a 100-degree polynomial fitted to 10 points) — it fits the training data perfectly but fails on new data.

- **Low Bias, Low Variance (The Expert)**: A skilled darts player who repeatedly hits near the bullseye, with small, consistent deviations. This represents the **ideal model** with the right balance of complexity.

### 1.3 Key Metrics & Components

The three core components of generalization error are:

| Component | Symbol | Nature | Controllable? |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **Bias Squared** | $\text{Bias}^2[\hat{f}(x)]$ | Systematic error from oversimplification | Yes — by increasing model complexity |
| **Variance** | $\text{Var}[\hat{f}(x)]$ | Error from sensitivity to training data fluctuations | Yes — by regularization / more data |
| **Irreducible Error** | $\sigma^2$ | Inherent noise in the data | **No** — fundamental Bayes error floor |

> [!NOTE]
> The **irreducible error** $\sigma^2$ is the theoretical lower bound of any model's error. Even a perfect model cannot go below this limit because the data itself contains random noise. All practical modeling efforts only address the reducible portion: **Bias² + Variance**.

### 1.4 Tuning Model Complexity

**Model complexity** refers to the flexibility or capacity of a hypothesis class to fit data. Common levers include:
- Polynomial degree (linear, quadratic, cubic, ...)
- Number of hidden units / layers in a neural network
- Depth of a decision tree
- Regularization strength $\lambda$ (inversely related to complexity)
- Number of features used

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight**: In KTU 2024 scheme exams, you must clearly distinguish between **training error** (which always decreases with complexity) and **test/validation error** (which first decreases, then increases — forming the classic U-curve).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Bias-Variance-Noise Decomposition

For a regression problem where the true relationship is $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$ with $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$, and our model produces prediction $\hat{f}(x; D)$ trained on dataset $D$, the **expected squared prediction error** at a fixed test point $x$ decomposes as follows:

$$\mathbb{E}_D\left[\left(y - \hat{f}(x; D)\right)^2\right] = \underbrace{\left(\text{Bias}[\hat{f}(x)]\right)^2}_{\text{Systematic Error}} + \underbrace{\text{Var}_D[\hat{f}(x)]}_{\text{Model Sensitivity}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{Irreducible Noise}}$$

### 2.2 Definitions of Each Component

Let $\bar{f}(x) = \mathbb{E}_D[\hat{f}(x; D)]$ be the **average prediction** across all possible training sets of size $N$.

- **Bias** measures how far the *average prediction* is from the truth:
$$\text{Bias}[\hat{f}(x)] = \bar{f}(x) - f(x)$$

- **Variance** measures how much *individual predictions* deviate from the *average prediction*:
$$\text{Var}_D[\hat{f}(x)] = \mathbb{E}_D\left[\left(\hat{f}(x; D) - \bar{f}(x)\right)^2\right]$$

- **Irreducible error** is the variance of the target noise:
$$\sigma^2 = \text{Var}[\varepsilon]$$

### 2.3 Behavior of Each Error as Complexity Changes

- **As complexity ↑**: Bias ↓, Variance ↑, Training error ↓, Test error initially ↓ then ↑
- **As complexity ↓**: Bias ↑, Variance ↓, Training error ↑, Test error initially ↑ then ↓
- **Underfitting region**: High bias dominates total error.
- **Overfitting region**: High variance dominates total error.
- **Sweet spot**: The complexity value that minimizes **expected test error**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Where Used |
|:--|:--------|:--------|:-----------|
| 1 | $E_D[(y-\hat{f})^2] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Var} + \sigma^2$ | Master decomposition theorem | All bias-variance derivations |
| 2 | $\text{Bias}[\hat{f}(x)] = \mathbb{E}_D[\hat{f}(x;D)] - f(x)$ | Squared for error term | Bias calculation |
| 3 | $\text{Var}[\hat{f}(x)] = \mathbb{E}_D[(\hat{f}(x;D) - \mathbb{E}_D[\hat{f}(x;D)])^2]$ | Spread of predictions | Variance calculation |
| 4 | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Mean Squared Error metric | Empirical error evaluation |
| 5 | $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2 + \frac{\lambda}{2m}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\theta_j^2$ | Regularized cost function | Complexity control |
| 6 | $\text{Generalization Error} \approx \text{Train Error} + 2 \cdot \text{Variance} \cdot \frac{d_{\text{VC}}}{N}$ | VC-theory bound (theoretical) | Justification of bias-variance |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formula table above, all absolute value or "such that" notations have been replaced with $\text{Var}$ or $\mathbb{E}_D$ to avoid vertical pipe conflicts inside the markdown table.

### 2.5 Why the Trade-off Exists — A Theoretical Insight

The trade-off arises from the **fundamental tension in function approximation**:

- A **simple hypothesis space** (e.g., linear functions) cannot represent the true complex function → high **bias**.
- A **complex hypothesis space** (e.g., high-degree polynomials) can fit any function, but with finite training data it also fits the **noise** → high **variance**.

In the limit of infinite data, variance vanishes for any reasonable model, and we are left with only bias. This is why more data is the universal cure for overfitting.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of Bias-Variance Concept |
|:---|:---|
| **Medical Diagnosis (CT Scan AI)** | High-variance models = unreliable diagnoses on new patients; high-bias = missing rare diseases |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Trade-off between reactive (low bias, high variance) and conservative (high bias, low variance) driving policies |
| **Financial Forecasting** | Stock prediction: overcomplex models memorize historical patterns; simple models miss market regimes |
| **Speech Recognition** | LSTM depth vs. dataset size: deep models with insufficient data = high variance, catastrophic failures |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Complete Derivation of the Bias-Variance Decomposition

We start with the **expected squared prediction error** at a single test point $x$, averaging over all possible training sets $D$ of the same size drawn from the data distribution.

**Step 1 — Define the quantity of interest**

We want to evaluate the average squared deviation between the true target $y$ and the model's prediction $\hat{f}(x; D)$. The expectation is taken over the distribution of training datasets:

$$\mathbb{E}_D\left[\left(y - \hat{f}(x; D)\right)^2\right]$$

**Step 2 — Introduce the average prediction as an intermediate variable**

Let us define the "average predictor" $\bar{f}(x) = \mathbb{E}_D[\hat{f}(x; D)]$, which is a deterministic quantity (no longer a random variable over $D$). We can add and subtract this term inside the squared error using the algebraic identity $(a - b) = (a - c) + (c - b)$ with $a = y$, $b = \hat{f}(x;D)$, and $c = \bar{f}(x)$:

$$y - \hat{f}(x; D) = \underbrace{\left(y - \bar{f}(x)\right)}_{A} - \underbrace{\left(\hat{f}(x; D) - \bar{f}(x)\right)}_{B}$$

Therefore, the squared error becomes:

$$\left(y - \hat{f}(x; D)\right)^2 = \left(y - \bar{f}(x)\right)^2 + \left(\hat{f}(x; D) - \bar{f}(x)\right)^2 - 2\left(y - \bar{f}(x)\right)\left(\hat{f}(x; D) - \bar{f}(x)\right)$$

**Step 3 — Take expectation over training sets**

Applying the linearity of expectation $\mathbb{E}_D[\cdot]$ term-by-term:

$$\mathbb{E}_D\left[\left(y - \hat{f}(x; D)\right)^2\right] = \mathbb{E}_D\left[\left(y - \bar{f}(x)\right)^2\right] + \mathbb{E}_D\left[\left(\hat{f}(x; D) - \bar{f}(x)\right)^2\right] - 2\,\mathbb{E}_D\left[\left(y - \bar{f}(x)\right)\left(\hat{f}(x; D) - \bar{f}(x)\right)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Analyze each term**

- *Term 1*: Since $y$ and $\bar{f}(x)$ are both constants with respect to $D$, the expectation of a constant is the constant itself:

$$\mathbb{E}_D\left[\left(y - \bar{f}(x)\right)^2\right] = \left(y - \bar{f}(x)\right)^2 = \left(\text{Bias}[\hat{f}(x)]\right)^2$$

This is the **squared bias** because $\text{Bias}[\hat{f}(x)] = \bar{f}(x) - y$, and squaring removes the sign.

- *Term 2*: By definition, this is the **variance** of the predictor:

$$\mathbb{E}_D\left[\left(\hat{f}(x; D) - \bar{f}(x)\right)^2\right] = \text{Var}_D[\hat{f}(x)]$$

- *Term 3 (Cross Term)*: Since $y$ and $\bar{f}(x)$ are constants w.r.t. $D$, they can be pulled out of the expectation:

$$\mathbb{E}_D\left[\left(y - \bar{f}(x)\right)\left(\hat{f}(x; D) - \bar{f}(x)\right)\right] = \left(y - \bar{f}(x)\right) \cdot \mathbb{E}_D\left[\hat{f}(x; D) - \bar{f}(x)\right]$$

By the definition of expectation, $\mathbb{E}_D[\hat{f}(x; D)] = \bar{f}(x)$, so:

$$\mathbb{E}_D\left[\hat{f}(x; D) - \bar{f}(x)\right] = \bar{f}(x) - \bar{f}(x) = 0$$

Therefore, the **cross term vanishes to zero**.

**Step 5 — Assemble the result**

Combining the surviving terms, we get the bias-variance decomposition for the *deterministic* part of $y$:

$$\mathbb{E}_D\left[\left(y - \hat{f}(x; D)\right)^2\right] = \text{Bias}^2[\hat{f}(x)] + \text{Var}_D[\hat{f}(x)]$$

**Step 6 — Account for irreducible noise**

Now recall that the true target is not deterministic: $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$, where $\varepsilon$ is independent zero-mean noise with variance $\sigma^2$. Substituting this:

$$\mathbb{E}_D\left[\left(f(x) + \varepsilon - \hat{f}(x; D)\right)^2\right] = \mathbb{E}_D\left[\left(f(x) - \hat{f}(x; D)\right)^2\right] + \mathbb{E}_D[\varepsilon^2] + 2\,\mathbb{E}_D\left[\varepsilon \cdot (f(x) - \hat{f}(x; D))\right]$$

The cross term vanishes because $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon] = 0$ and $\varepsilon$ is independent of $\hat{f}$. The first term is exactly what we derived in Step 5, and the second term equals $\sigma^2$.

**Step 7 — Final master equation**

$$\boxed{\mathbb{E}_D\left[\left(y - \hat{f}(x; D)\right)^2\right] = \underbrace{\left(\text{Bias}[\hat{f}(x)]\right)^2}_{\text{squared bias}} + \underbrace{\text{Var}_D[\hat{f}(x)]}_{\text{variance}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{irreducible error}}}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Error Type vs. Symptom vs. Remedy

| Error Type | Train Error | Test Error | Gap (Test − Train) | Diagnostic Symptom | Standard Remedy |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| **High Bias (Underfitting)** | High | High | **Small** | Model too simple, residuals are systematic | Add features, increase complexity, decrease regularization $\lambda$ |
| **High Variance (Overfitting)** | Low | High | **Large** | Model memorizes training data, residuals look random on train but large on test | Collect more data, feature selection, increase regularization $\lambda$, dropout, simpler model |
| **Well-Balanced (Good Fit)** | Low | Low | **Small** | Predictions are accurate and consistent | Stop training / finalize model |

### 3.3 Learning Curves — Diagnostic Tool

A **learning curve** plots training error and validation error as a function of training set size $N$:

- **High bias scenario**: Both training and validation errors converge to a *high value* with a small gap → adding more data will not help.
- **High variance scenario**: There is a *large gap* between training error (low) and validation error (high) → adding more data will help close the gap.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bias-Variance Trade-off Block Diagram

The following flowchart shows how model complexity influences each error component and the resulting total expected error.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT FEATURES AND TRAINING DATA] --> B[CHOOSE HYPOTHESIS CLASS H]
    B --> C[ESTIMATE MODEL PARAMETERS]
    C --> D[COMPUTE PREDICTIONS ON UNSEEN DATA]
    D --> E[EXPECTED GENERALIZATION ERROR]
    E --> F[BIAS SQUARED COMPONENT]
    E --> G[VARIANCE COMPONENT]
    E --> H[IRREDUCIBLE NOISE SIGMA SQUARED]
    F --> F1[INCREASES AS COMPLEXITY DECREASES]
    G --> G1[INCREASES AS COMPLEXITY INCREASES]
    H --> H1[CONSTANT FLOOR]
    F1 --> I[TOTAL GENERALIZATION ERROR U CURVE]
    G1 --> I
    H1 --> I
    I --> I1[UNDERFITTING REGION BIAS DOMINANT]
    I --> I2[SWEET SPOT MINIMUM TOTAL ERROR]
    I --> I3[OVERFITTING REGION VARIANCE DOMINANT]
    I2 --> J[OPTIMAL MODEL SELECTION]
    J --> K[FINAL DEPLOYED MODEL]
```

### 4.2 Dartboard Analogy — Visual Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HIGH BIAS LOW VARIANCE] --> A1[Throws cluster tightly but off center]
    B[LOW BIAS HIGH VARIANCE] --> B1[Throws scatter widely around center]
    C[LOW BIAS LOW VARIANCE] --> C1[Throws cluster tightly at bullseye]
    A1 --> D[UNDERFITTED MODEL]
    B1 --> E[OVERFITTED MODEL]
    C1 --> F[IDEAL GENERALIZED MODEL]
```

### 4.3 Error Decomposition — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[RAW EXPECTED PREDICTION ERROR] --> R1[TERM A BIAS SQUARED]
    R --> R2[TERM B VARIANCE]
    R --> R3[TERM C NOISE VARIANCE]
    R1 --> R1A[Source: Wrong assumptions in model family]
    R1 --> R1B[Indicator: Persistent residual patterns]
    R2 --> R2A[Source: Sensitivity to training set]
    R2 --> R2B[Indicator: Large gap between train and test error]
    R3 --> R3A[Source: Stochasticity in data generation process]
    R3 --> R3B[Indicator: Cannot be reduced by any algorithm]
    R1A --> OUT1[Remedy: Increase model complexity]
    R1B --> OUT1
    R2A --> OUT2[Remedy: Regularize or add more data]
    R2B --> OUT2
    R3A --> OUT3[Accept as fundamental limit]
    R3B --> OUT3
```

### 4.4 Model Complexity vs. Error Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[MODEL COMPLEXITY AXIS LOW TO HIGH] --> Y1[BIAS CURVE: STARTS HIGH, MONOTONICALLY DECREASES]
    X --> Y2[VARIANCE CURVE: STARTS LOW, MONOTONICALLY INCREASES]
    X --> Y3[TRAINING ERROR: MONOTONICALLY DECREASES]
    X --> Y4[TEST ERROR: U SHAPED CURVE]
    Y1 --> Z[BALANCE POINT]
    Y2 --> Z
    Y3 --> Z
    Y4 --> Z
    Z --> Z1[OPTIMAL COMPLEXITY: MINIMUM TEST ERROR]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exam**: When asked to "explain the bias-variance trade-off with a diagram," sketch the U-shaped test error curve and show the bias and variance curves as monotonically decreasing and increasing, respectively. The intersection of bias and variance typically marks the **optimal complexity region**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Define **bias** and **variance** in the context of a learning algorithm. How are they related to underfitting and overfitting?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Bias (1 mark)**: Bias is the error introduced by approximating a real-world problem with an overly simplified model. It is defined mathematically as the difference between the expected (average) prediction of the model and the true value, $\text{Bias}[\hat{f}(x)] = \mathbb{E}_D[\hat{f}(x; D)] - f(x)$. **High bias corresponds to underfitting**, where the model is too simple to capture the underlying pattern in the data (e.g., a linear model on a non-linear dataset).

- **Variance (1 mark)**: Variance is the amount by which the model's prediction would change if a different training set were used. It is defined as $\text{Var}_D[\hat{f}(x)] = \mathbb{E}_D[(\hat{f}(x; D) - \mathbb{E}_D[\hat{f}(x; D)])^2]$. **High variance corresponds to overfitting**, where the model fits the training data too closely, including its noise (e.g., a 20-degree polynomial on 30 data points).

- **Relationship (1 mark)**: Bias and variance are inversely related through model complexity — as complexity increases, bias decreases but variance increases. This is known as the **bias-variance trade-off**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is the **irreducible error** in the bias-variance decomposition? Why is it called "irreducible"?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (2 marks)**: The irreducible error, denoted $\sigma^2$, is the variance of the inherent noise $\varepsilon$ in the target variable, where $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$ and $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$. It appears as an additive constant in the bias-variance decomposition:

$$\mathbb{E}_D[(y - \hat{f}(x; D))^2] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Var} + \sigma^2$$

- **Why irreducible (1 mark)**: It is called "irreducible" because it arises from the **stochasticity inherent in the data-generation process itself** (e.g., sensor noise, unobserved variables, inherent randomness). No algorithm, however sophisticated, can predict this noise component because by definition it is independent of the input features $x$. It represents the **Bayes error floor** — the minimum possible error achievable by any model on that dataset.

---

### **PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the **bias-variance decomposition** for the expected squared prediction error of a regression model. State clearly all assumptions.

**(b) [7 marks)** With the help of a properly labeled diagram, explain how **bias², variance, and total expected error** vary as a function of model complexity. Identify and label the regions of underfitting, optimal complexity, and overfitting.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks]:**

We want to evaluate the expected squared error between the true target $y$ and the prediction $\hat{f}(x; D)$ at a test point $x$, averaged over all training sets $D$.

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
- The true relationship is $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$ where $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ is independent of $x$.
- The training set $D$ is drawn i.i.d. from the data distribution, with $|D| = N$.
- We consider squared-error loss.

**Derivation Steps (6 marks):**

Define the average prediction as $\bar{f}(x) = \mathbb{E}_D[\hat{f}(x; D)]$.

**Step 1** — Expand the squared error using the identity $(y - \hat{f}) = (y - \bar{f}) - (\hat{f} - \bar{f})$:

$$\mathbb{E}_D[(y - \hat{f})^2] = \mathbb{E}_D\left[(y - \bar{f})^2\right] + \mathbb{E}_D\left[(\hat{f} - \bar{f})^2\right] - 2\,\mathbb{E}_D\left[(y - \bar{f})(\hat{f} - \bar{f})\right]$$

**Step 2** — The cross-term vanishes because $\mathbb{E}_D[\hat{f} - \bar{f}] = 0$ (by definition of $\bar{f}$):

$$\mathbb{E}_D[(y - \hat{f})^2] = (y - \bar{f})^2 + \mathbb{E}_D[(\hat{f} - \bar{f})^2]$$

**Step 3** — Identify the two components:
- First term is the **squared bias**: $(y - \bar{f})^2 = \text{Bias}^2[\hat{f}(x)]$
- Second term is the **variance**: $\text{Var}_D[\hat{f}(x)]$

**Step 4** — Substitute $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$ and use $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon] = 0$, $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon^2] = \sigma^2$, and independence of $\varepsilon$ and $\hat{f}$ to obtain the **irreducible noise term** $\sigma^2$:

$$\boxed{\mathbb{E}_D[(y - \hat{f})^2] = \text{Bias}^2[\hat{f}(x)] + \text{Var}_D[\hat{f}(x)] + \sigma^2}$$

**Part (b) — Diagram and Explanation [7 marks]:**

- **Diagram description (3 marks)**: A graph with *Model Complexity* on the x-axis and *Error* on the y-axis, showing four curves: (i) **Bias²** — high at low complexity, decreasing monotonically toward zero, (ii) **Variance** — low at low complexity, increasing monotonically, (iii) **Training Error** — monotonically decreasing, (iv) **Test Error** — U-shaped curve.

- **Region identification (2 marks)**: 
  - *Underfitting region* (left): high bias, low variance, high test error.
  - *Optimal region* (middle): minimum test error; bias ≈ variance.
  - *Overfitting region* (right): low bias, high variance, high test error.

- **Remedies (2 marks)**:
  - For underfitting → increase model complexity, add features.
  - For overfitting → regularization, more data, feature selection, simpler model.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the concepts of **overfitting** and **underfitting** in supervised learning. How does the **training-versus-test error gap** act as a diagnostic tool to identify each scenario?

**(b) [7 marks]** A model gives a training MSE of **0.02** and a test MSE of **0.45** on a regression problem with irreducible noise $\sigma^2 = 0.10$. Compute the approximate values of $\text{Bias}^2$ and $\text{Variance}$. Identify whether the model is overfitting or underfitting and suggest **two specific remedies**.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) — Concepts and Diagnostic [7 marks]:**

- **Overfitting (2 marks)**: A model overfits when it learns the training data *too well*, including its noise and idiosyncrasies. Symptom: **Low training error** but **high test error**. The model has high variance and low bias. The hypothesis space is too rich (e.g., 20-degree polynomial on 30 points).

- **Underfitting (2 marks)**: A model underfits when it is too simple to capture the underlying structure. Symptom: **High training error** and **high test error**, with a *small* gap between them. The model has high bias and low variance.

- **Diagnostic Gap (3 marks)**: The quantity $\Delta = E_{\text{test}} - E_{\text{train}}$ is the **generalization gap**.
  - If $\Delta$ is **small** and both errors are high → underfitting.
  - If $\Delta$ is **large** (test >> train) → overfitting.
  - A **learning curve** plotting both errors against training set size $N$ provides a visual diagnostic. For overfitting, increasing $N$ closes the gap. For underfitting, increasing $N$ does not help, and a more complex model is needed.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation [7 marks]:**

Given: Train MSE = 0.02, Test MSE = 0.45, $\sigma^2 = 0.10$.

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Apply bias-variance decomposition:**

$$\text{Test MSE} \approx \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Estimate variance from the gap:**

The variance is approximately the gap between test and training MSE (the reducible portion of the gap above the noise floor):

$$\text{Variance} \approx \text{Train MSE Test MSE gap} - \text{noise} = 0.45 - 0.02 - 0.10 = 0.33$$

*(Note: Train MSE already reflects overfitting, so we use the conservative estimate from the test gap minus noise.)*

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Compute Bias²:**

$$\text{Bias}^2 = \text{Train MSE} - \text{Variance} = 0.02 - 0.33 \approx \text{small}$$

Since Train MSE is very low, the model has near-zero bias on the training set. However, the *effective* bias squared component of the test error is:

$$\text{Bias}^2 \approx \text{Train MSE} = 0.02 \quad \text{(on training distribution)}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Diagnosis and Remedies:**

- The large gap (0.43) and high variance (0.33) clearly indicate **OVERFITTING**.

- **Remedies (2 marks)**:
  1. **Increase the regularization strength** $\lambda$ in $J(\theta) = \text{MSE Loss} + \lambda \|\theta\|^2$ to penalize large weights and reduce model complexity.
  2. **Collect more training data** or use **data augmentation** so the model cannot memorize the finite training set.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**:
> - Many students write "bias = high, variance = low" without defining them — **always provide the formula** for full marks.
> - In numerical problems, students often forget to **subtract the noise term** $\sigma^2$ when computing the variance component — this costs 2 marks.
> - The cross-term vanishing in the derivation is a common step that students skip — examiners specifically check this. Always show the step where $\mathbb{E}_D[\hat{f} - \bar{f}] = 0$.
> - In diagrams, forgetting to label the **axes** (Model Complexity vs. Error) and the **three regions** (underfit, optimal, overfit) leads to a 2-mark deduction.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Board Exams:**

- [x] **Master Equation**: $\mathbb{E}_D[(y-\hat{f})^2] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Var} + \sigma^2$ — must be memorized exactly.
- [x] **Bias** = systematic error from oversimplification → underfitting.
- [x] **Variance** = sensitivity to training data → overfitting.
- [x] **Irreducible error** $\sigma^2$ is the Bayes floor — **no model can go below it**.
- [x] **Model complexity** $\uparrow$ → Bias $\downarrow$, Variance $\uparrow$ — this is the trade-off.
- [x] **Training error** always $\downarrow$ with complexity; **Test error** has a U-shape.
- [x] **Learning curve** is the diagnostic tool — gap between train/test errors identifies overfit vs. underfit.
- [x] **Derivation must include** the introduction of $\bar{f}(x)$, expansion, and the cross-term vanishing step.
- [x] **Remedies for high bias**: increase complexity, add features, decrease $\lambda$.
- [x] **Remedies for high variance**: more data, regularization, dropout, simpler model, feature selection.
- [x] **In regression**: MSE decomposition applies. In classification: 0-1 loss decomposition is more complex (uses bias and variance of the *expected risk*).
- [x] **Common exam trap**: confusing **bias** with the statistical bias of an estimator — in ML context, it specifically refers to the *model's inability to represent the true function*.
- [x] **VC-dimension** provides a theoretical link: $E_{\text{test}} \leq E_{\text{train}} + O(\sqrt{d_{\text{VC}}/N})$ — more parameters (high $d_{\text{VC}}$) increases the complexity penalty.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dimensionality Reduction - Principal Component Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT501 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dimensionality Reduction - Principal Component Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** is a *linear, unsupervised, orthogonal transformation*-based **dimensionality reduction** technique that projects high-dimensional data onto a lower-dimensional subspace called the *principal subspace*, while preserving as much of the original variance (information content) as possible.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, PCA is treated as a **feature extraction** (not feature selection) method that constructs new uncorrelated variables — called **Principal Components (PCs)** — which are linear combinations of the original features, ordered by the amount of variance they capture from the dataset.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 - Dimensionality Reduction):**
> PCA is the *flagship* algorithm students must master. The KTU 2024 Scheme expects:
> 1. Mathematical derivation of principal components.
> 2. Numerical computation of PCs on toy datasets.
> 3. Conceptual clarity on covariance, eigenvectors, and explained variance.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are a photographer asked to capture the *essence* of a 3D sculpture using only a 2D photograph.

- You wouldn't take a random photo — you would walk around the sculpture and find the **angle that captures the most variation** (the longest visible silhouette).
- That best angle is your **first principal component** — the direction in which the data "spreads out" the most.
- The second-best angle, taken at right angles to the first (because you don't want redundant information), is your **second principal component**.

PCA does exactly this for **datasets with hundreds of features** — it finds the *informative camera angles* (principal components) that summarize the data with minimal loss.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** PCA finds new axes (directions) for your data such that:
> - **Axis 1** captures the maximum spread (variance).
> - **Axis 2** is perpendicular to Axis 1 and captures the next maximum spread.
> - And so on, until you have enough axes to represent the data well.
> - You then **drop the axes with the least information**.

## 1.3 Why Do We Need PCA? (Engineering Motivation)

| Problem | How PCA Helps |
|---|---|
| **Curse of Dimensionality** in ML | Reduces features from *d* → *k* where *k ≪ d* |
| **Visualization** of high-D data | Projects to 2D or 3D for plotting |
| **Multicollinearity** in regression | PCs are uncorrelated by construction |
| **Noise Filtering** | Small-variance components often capture noise |
| **Computational Cost** | Smaller input → faster training |
| **Storage & Compression** | Reconstruct data with fewer coefficients |

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:** A "good" PCA reduction typically retains **≥ 85% to 95%** of cumulative explained variance. The exact threshold depends on the application domain.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric & Statistical Foundation

The data is treated as a set of points $X = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ in $\mathbb{R}^d$. PCA assumes:

1. **Linearity** — the principal subspace is a linear combination of original axes.
2. **Zero mean** — data is *mean-centered* before analysis.
3. **Maximum variance direction** — the *first* PC lies along the direction of greatest spread.
4. **Orthogonality** — successive PCs are mutually perpendicular.
5. **Uncorrelated components** — PCs are statistically independent in the second-order sense.

## 2.2 The Seven Operational Steps of PCA

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Favourite:** This pipeline is asked almost verbatim every semester. Memorize the order.

1. **Mean Center the Data**
   Subtract the feature-wise mean $\mu$ from every sample.

2. **Standardize (Optional)**
   Divide by feature standard deviation $\sigma$ if features are on different scales.

3. **Compute the Covariance Matrix $\Sigma$**
   A $d \times d$ symmetric matrix capturing pairwise feature variance.

4. **Solve the Eigenvalue Problem**
   Solve $\Sigma v = \lambda v$ for eigenvalues $\lambda_i$ and eigenvectors $v_i$.

5. **Sort Eigenvectors by Decreasing Eigenvalue**
   Larger $\lambda$ ⇒ more variance captured by that direction.

6. **Select Top $k$ Eigenvectors**
   Form the projection matrix $W \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$.

7. **Transform the Data**
   Compute $Z = X W \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$, the low-dimensional representation.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** This document uses `\vert` instead of `\vert` for absolute value-like notations to prevent breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notation / Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mean of feature $j$ | $\mu_j = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum\limits_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij}$ | Scalar (same units as $x$) | Step 1 output |
| 2 | Mean-centered matrix | $X_c = X - \mathbf{1}\mu^T$ | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ | Each column has zero mean |
| 3 | Covariance Matrix | $\Sigma = \dfrac{1}{n-1} X_c^T X_c$ | $\mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$, symmetric | Measures joint variability |
| 4 | Eigen Decomposition | $\Sigma v = \lambda v$ | $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$, $v \in \mathbb{R}^d$ | Core of PCA |
| 5 | Trace Identity | $\sum\limits_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i = \mathrm{tr}(\Sigma) = \sum\limits_{j=1}^{d} \mathrm{Var}(x_j)$ | Scalar | Total variance preserved |
| 6 | Explained Variance Ratio | $EVR_i = \dfrac{\lambda_i}{\sum\limits_{j=1}^{d} \lambda_j}$ | Ratio in $[0, 1]$ | $\sum EVR = 1$ |
| 7 | Cumulative Explained Variance | $CEV_k = \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i}{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i}$ | Ratio in $[0, 1]$ | Choose $k$ such that $CEV_k \geq 0.90$ |
| 8 | Projection (Encoding) | $Z = X_c W$ | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$ | Low-D representation |
| 9 | Reconstruction (Decoding) | $\hat{X} = Z W^T + \mu$ | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ | Approximate original data |
| 10 | Reconstruction Error | $J = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum\limits_{i=1}^{n} \Vert x_i - \hat{x}_i \Vert^2_2 = \sum\limits_{i=k+1}^{d} \lambda_i$ | Squared Euclidean | = sum of discarded eigenvalues |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use Case of PCA |
|---|---|
| **Image Processing** | Eigenfaces for face recognition |
| **Genomics & Bioinformatics** | Gene expression data analysis (thousands of genes) |
| **Finance** | Risk factor decomposition in portfolio analysis |
| **Signal Processing** | Noise reduction in sensor data |
| **Computer Vision** | Pre-processing step for deep learning models |
| **Anomaly Detection** | High reconstruction error $\Rightarrow$ outlier |

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Maximize Variance?

Let the data after mean-centering be $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ with rows $x_i^T$. We seek a unit direction $v \in \mathbb{R}^d$ ($\Vert v \Vert_2 = 1$) such that the projections $z_i = v^T x_i$ have **maximum variance**.

$$ \text{Var}(z) = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (v^T x_i)^2 $$

This can be rewritten in matrix form. Since $\sum x_i = 0$ (mean-centered), the projection scores are stored in $z = X v \in \mathbb{R}^n$, and:

$$ \text{Var}(z) = \frac{1}{n-1} z^T z = \frac{1}{n-1} (Xv)^T (Xv) = \frac{1}{n-1} v^T X^T X v = v^T \Sigma v $$

So the optimization problem is:

$$ \max_{v} \; v^T \Sigma v \quad \text{subject to} \quad v^T v = 1 $$

Using **Lagrange multipliers**, the Lagrangian is:

$$ \mathcal{L}(v, \lambda) = v^T \Sigma v - \lambda (v^T v - 1) $$

Taking the derivative and setting it to zero:

$$ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial v} = 2 \Sigma v - 2 \lambda v = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \Sigma v = \lambda v $$

This is the **eigenvalue equation**! Thus the optimal direction $v$ is an eigenvector of $\Sigma$, and the maximum variance equals the corresponding eigenvalue $\lambda$. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** PCA solves an optimization problem whose solution is *trivially* the eigendecomposition of the covariance matrix.

## 3.2 Analytical (Numerical) Walk-Through — 2D Example

Consider the following **2-dimensional dataset** with 5 samples:

$$ x_1 = (1, 2), \;\; x_2 = (3, 3), \;\; x_3 = (3, 5), \;\; x_4 = (5, 4), \;\; x_5 = (5, 6) $$

### Step 1 — Mean Centering

$$ \mu_x = \frac{1+3+3+5+5}{5} = \frac{17}{5} = 3.4, \quad \mu_y = \frac{2+3+5+4+6}{5} = \frac{20}{5} = 4.0 $$

Centered data:

$$ X_c = \begin{bmatrix} -2.4 & -2.0 \\ -0.4 & -1.0 \\ -0.4 & \phantom{-}1.0 \\ \phantom{-}1.6 & \phantom{-}0.0 \\ \phantom{-}1.6 & \phantom{-}2.0 \end{bmatrix} $$

### Step 2 — Covariance Matrix

First, compute $X_c^T X_c$:

$$ X_c^T X_c = \begin{bmatrix} (-2.4)^2+(-0.4)^2+(-0.4)^2+(1.6)^2+(1.6)^2 & (-2.4)(-2)+(-0.4)(-1)+(-0.4)(1)+(1.6)(0)+(1.6)(2) \\ \text{symmetric} & (-2)^2+(-1)^2+(1)^2+(0)^2+(2)^2 \end{bmatrix} $$

$$ = \begin{bmatrix} 5.76+0.16+0.16+2.56+2.56 & 4.8+0.4-0.4+0+3.2 \\ \text{sym} & 4+1+1+0+4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 11.2 & 8.0 \\ 8.0 & 10.0 \end{bmatrix} $$

Dividing by $(n-1) = 4$:

$$ \Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} 2.8 & 2.0 \\ 2.0 & 2.5 \end{bmatrix} $$

### Step 3 — Eigenvalue Equation

Solve $\det(\Sigma - \lambda I) = 0$:

$$ \det \begin{bmatrix} 2.8 - \lambda & 2.0 \\ 2.0 & 2.5 - \lambda \end{bmatrix} = (2.8-\lambda)(2.5-\lambda) - 4 = 0 $$

Expanding:

$$ 7.0 - 2.8\lambda - 2.5\lambda + \lambda^2 - 4 = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \lambda^2 - 5.3\lambda + 3.0 = 0 $$

Applying the quadratic formula:

$$ \lambda = \frac{5.3 \pm \sqrt{(5.3)^2 - 4(3.0)}}{2} = \frac{5.3 \pm \sqrt{28.09 - 12.0}}{2} = \frac{5.3 \pm \sqrt{16.09}}{2} = \frac{5.3 \pm 4.0112}{2} $$

$$ \boxed{\lambda_1 = 4.6556, \quad \lambda_2 = 0.6444} $$

> [!NOTE]
> The trace check: $\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 = 4.6556 + 0.6444 = 5.3 = 2.8 + 2.5 = \mathrm{tr}(\Sigma)$ ✓

### Step 4 — Eigenvectors

**For $\lambda_1 = 4.6556$:** Solve $(\Sigma - \lambda_1 I)v_1 = 0$:

$$ \begin{bmatrix} 2.8 - 4.6556 & 2.0 \\ 2.0 & 2.5 - 4.6556 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_{11} \\ v_{12} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} $$

$$ -1.8556 v_{11} + 2.0 v_{12} = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; v_{12} = 0.9278 v_{11} $$

Normalizing ($\Vert v_1 \Vert_2 = 1$):

$$ v_{11}^2 + (0.9278 v_{11})^2 = 1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; v_{11}^2 (1 + 0.8608) = 1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; v_{11} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.8608}} = 0.7330 $$

$$ v_{12} = 0.9278 \times 0.7330 = 0.6801 $$

$$ \boxed{v_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7330 \\ 0.6801 \end{bmatrix} \;\; \text{(PC1)}} $$

**For $\lambda_2 = 0.6444$:** Orthogonal to $v_1$ (since $\Sigma$ is symmetric):

$$ \boxed{v_2 = \begin{bmatrix} -0.6801 \\ 0.7330 \end{bmatrix} \;\; \text{(PC2)}} $$

### Step 5 — Explained Variance Analysis

$$ EVR_1 = \frac{4.6556}{5.3} = 0.8784 \;\; (87.84\%), \quad EVR_2 = \frac{0.6444}{5.3} = 0.1216 \;\; (12.16\%) $$

**Interpretation:** PC1 alone captures **87.84%** of total variance. A 1D representation (keeping only PC1) is sufficient for this dataset.

### Step 6 — Projection onto PC1

The projection matrix $W = v_1 = [0.7330, \; 0.6801]^T$. The 1D projection of the mean-centered data:

$$ z_i = v_1^T x_i^{(c)} $$

For example, for $x_1^{(c)} = (-2.4, -2.0)$:

$$ z_1 = (0.7330)(-2.4) + (0.6801)(-2.0) = -1.7592 - 1.3602 = -3.1194 $$

The complete projections:

$$ Z = X_c \, v_1 = \begin{bmatrix} -2.4 & -2.0 \\ -0.4 & -1.0 \\ -0.4 & \phantom{-}1.0 \\ \phantom{-}1.6 & \phantom{-}0.0 \\ \phantom{-}1.6 & \phantom{-}2.0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.7330 \\ 0.6801 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -3.1194 \\ -0.9732 \\ 0.3869 \\ 1.1728 \\ 2.5330 \end{bmatrix} $$

### Step 7 — Reconstruction (Verify)

$$ \hat{X} = Z v_1^T + \mu = \begin{bmatrix} -3.1194 \\ -0.9732 \\ 0.3869 \\ 1.1728 \\ 2.5330 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.7330 & 0.6801 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 3.4 \\ 4.0 \end{bmatrix} $$

For row 1:

$$ \hat{x}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} (-3.1194)(0.7330) + 3.4 \\ (-3.1194)(0.6801) + 4.0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -2.286 + 3.4 \\ -2.121 + 4.0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.114 \\ 1.879 \end{bmatrix} \approx (1, 2) = x_1 \;\; \text{(close to original)} $$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification Note:** Perfect reconstruction would require both PCs. Using only PC1 gives an approximation; the error equals the *discarded* variance $\lambda_2 = 0.6444$.

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Step | Operation | Input Dimensions | Output Dimensions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mean centering | $n \times d$ | $n \times d$ (centered) |
| 2 | Covariance | $n \times d$ | $d \times d$ |
| 3 | Eigendecomposition | $d \times d$ | $d$ eigenvalues + $d$ eigenvectors |
| 4 | Sorting & Selection | $d$ eigenvectors | $k$ eigenvectors ($W$) |
| 5 | Projection ($Z = X_c W$) | $(n \times d)(d \times k)$ | $n \times k$ |
| 6 | Reconstruction ($\hat{X} = Z W^T + \mu$) | $(n \times k)(k \times d)$ | $n \times d$ |
