# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PCA Pipeline Block Diagram

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the complete operational pipeline of Principal Component Analysis, from raw data ingestion to the final reduced representation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Input Data Matrix X with shape n by d] --> B[Compute Feature Mean Vector mu]
    B --> C[Mean Center the Data Xc equals X minus mu]
    C --> D{Optional Standardization}
    D -->|Yes features on different scales| E[Divide each feature by its standard deviation]
    D -->|No features already comparable| F[Proceed to Covariance Step]
    E --> G[Compute Covariance Matrix Sigma equals XcT Xc divided by n minus 1]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Eigendecomposition of Sigma yields eigenvalues lambda i and eigenvectors v i]
    H --> I[Sort eigenvalues in descending order lambda1 greater than lambda2 and so on]
    I --> J[Select top k eigenvectors forming Projection Matrix W of shape d by k]
    J --> K[Project Data Z equals Xc times W gives n by k reduced representation]
    K --> L{Choose Application Path}
    L --> M[Visualization in 2D or 3D scatter plot]
    L --> N[Feature Reduction input to downstream ML model]
    L --> O[Reconstruction Xhat equals Z WT plus mu for compression or denoising]
    L --> P[Anomaly Detection flag points with high reconstruction error]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Architecture — Variance Maximization View

This diagram illustrates the geometric intuition of PCA: searching for the direction (PC1) that maximizes the spread of projected points, followed by the orthogonal direction (PC2) that maximizes the remaining spread.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Original_Space["ORIGINAL 2D FEATURE SPACE"]
        A1[Point cloud in d dimensions]
    end

    subgraph Centered_Space["CENTERED SPACE"]
        B1[Subtract mean vector]
        B2[Data now centered at origin]
        B1 --> B2
    end

    subgraph PCA_Search["PCA DIRECTION SEARCH"]
        C1[Step 1 Find v1 that maximizes v1T Sigma v1 subject to v1 unit norm]
        C2[Step 2 Find v2 orthogonal to v1 with second maximum variance]
        C1 --> C2
    end

    subgraph Reduced_Space["LOW DIMENSIONAL SUBSPACE"]
        D1[Project data onto v1 yields PC1 scores]
        D2[Optionally add v2 yields PC1 plus PC2 plane]
        D1 --> D2
    end

    A1 --> B1
    B2 --> C1
    C2 --> D1
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart: How Many Components to Retain?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Begin with full eigenspectrum lambda1 to lambdad] --> CumVar[Compute Cumulative Explained Variance CEVk]
    CumVar --> Q1{Application demands minimum variance threshold}
    Q1 -->|Yes typical 90 percent| Q2[Choose smallest k such that CEVk greater than or equal to 0.90]
    Q1 -->|No fixed dimensionality desired| Q3[Use external criteria like cross validation]
    Q1 -->|Yes visualization required| Q4[Fix k equals 2 or k equals 3]
    Q2 --> End[Retain k principal components]
    Q3 --> End
    Q4 --> End
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for Exams:** Always label *inputs* and *outputs* of each block with their matrix dimensions ($n \times d$, $d \times d$, etc.). KTU examiners reward dimensional awareness.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part A question carries **3 marks**. Answers should be precise, 3–5 sentences, with any required formula or definition.

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**
**Define Principal Component Analysis. State any two of its key properties.**

**Model Answer:**
Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is an unsupervised linear dimensionality reduction technique that transforms a $d$-dimensional dataset into a $k$-dimensional subspace ($k \le d$) by finding orthogonal axes — called principal components — along which the variance of the data is maximized.

**Key properties:**
1. **Principal components are mutually orthogonal** (uncorrelated), ensuring no redundant information.
2. **PCs are linear combinations** of the original features, with weights given by the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix.
3. PCs are **ordered by decreasing variance**, with PC1 capturing the largest possible variance.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Repeat Pattern]**
**What is the role of eigenvalues in PCA? How is the number of principal components decided?**

**Model Answer:**
The **eigenvalues** of the covariance matrix quantify the amount of variance captured by each corresponding principal component. A larger eigenvalue indicates a direction of greater spread, and hence a more informative component.

**Deciding the number of components $k$:**
- Compute the **cumulative explained variance (CEV)**:

$$ CEV_k = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i} $$

- Choose the **smallest $k$** such that $CEV_k$ exceeds a chosen threshold (commonly **0.90, 0.95, or 0.99**).
- Alternatively, use a **Scree plot** and look for the "elbow point," or apply **Kaiser’s rule** (retain components with $\lambda > 1$ when data is standardized).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Part B questions carry **14 marks** with a KTU-mandated **internal choice**. Sub-parts (a) and (b) carry **7 marks each**. The KTU model demands step-by-step working and clear final answers.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) Explain the step-by-step algorithmic procedure of Principal Component Analysis. Use a neat block diagram in your explanation. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

PCA is executed as a sequential pipeline. The seven steps are:

**Step 1 — Mean Centering:** Compute the mean vector $\mu \in \mathbb{R}^d$ where $\mu_j = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij}$. Subtract $\mu$ from every row to form the centered matrix $X_c = X - \mathbf{1}\mu^T$. This ensures the data is centered at the origin, a prerequisite for variance computation.

**Step 2 — Standardization (Optional but Recommended):** When features have different units (e.g., age vs. income), divide each column of $X_c$ by its standard deviation to give all features equal weight. This is critical because PCA is sensitive to feature scaling.

**Step 3 — Covariance Matrix Computation:** Form the $d \times d$ covariance matrix:

$$ \Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1} X_c^T X_c $$

$\Sigma$ is symmetric positive semi-definite, with diagonal entries being feature variances and off-diagonal entries being feature covariances.

**Step 4 — Eigen Decomposition:** Solve the eigenvalue equation $\Sigma v = \lambda v$ to obtain $d$ eigenvalues $\{\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_d\}$ and $d$ corresponding eigenvectors $\{v_1, \ldots, v_d\}$.

**Step 5 — Sorting:** Sort eigenvalues in descending order $\lambda_1 \ge \lambda_2 \ge \ldots \ge \lambda_d \ge 0$ and reorder the eigenvectors accordingly.

**Step 6 — Selecting Top-k Components:** Pick the top $k$ eigenvectors (where $k$ is determined by the explained variance criterion) to form the projection matrix $W = [v_1 \; v_2 \; \ldots \; v_k] \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$.

**Step 7 — Projection:** Transform the data into the low-dimensional space:

$$ Z = X_c W \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k} $$

$Z$ is the new $k$-dimensional representation of the original data, ready for visualization, ML modeling, or reconstruction.

> *[Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 for the visual pipeline representation.]*

---

**(b) For the dataset $\{(1, 2), (3, 3), (3, 5), (5, 4), (5, 6)\}$, compute the principal components using PCA. Also find the variance explained by each principal component. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:** *(Direct application of the worked example in Section 3.2)*

**Mean:**
$\mu_x = (1+3+3+5+5)/5 = 3.4$, $\mu_y = (2+3+5+4+6)/5 = 4.0$.

**Mean-Centered Data:**

$$ X_c = \begin{bmatrix} -2.4 & -2.0 \\ -0.4 & -1.0 \\ -0.4 & \phantom{-}1.0 \\ \phantom{-}1.6 & \phantom{-}0.0 \\ \phantom{-}1.6 & \phantom{-}2.0 \end{bmatrix} $$

**Covariance Matrix:**

$$ \Sigma = \frac{1}{4} \begin{bmatrix} 11.2 & 8.0 \\ 8.0 & 10.0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.8 & 2.0 \\ 2.0 & 2.5 \end{bmatrix} $$

**Eigenvalues:** Solving $\lambda^2 - 5.3\lambda + 3.0 = 0$:

$$ \lambda_1 = 4.6556, \quad \lambda_2 = 0.6444 $$

**Eigenvectors:**

$$ v_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7330 \\ 0.6801 \end{bmatrix} \;\; \text{(PC1)}, \quad v_2 = \begin{bmatrix} -0.6801 \\ 0.7330 \end{bmatrix} \;\; \text{(PC2)} $$

**Explained Variance Ratio:**

$$ EVR_1 = \frac{4.6556}{5.3} = 0.8784 = 87.84\% $$

$$ EVR_2 = \frac{0.6444}{5.3} = 0.1216 = 12.16\% $$

**Conclusion:** PC1 captures **87.84%** of the total variance. A 1D representation is sufficient for this dataset since $EVR_1 \approx 88\%$, which is close to the 90% benchmark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Pattern]**

**(a) Derive the relationship between the covariance matrix and the eigenvectors in PCA. Show that the direction of maximum variance is given by the eigenvector corresponding to the largest eigenvalue. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Setup:** Let $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ be the mean-centered data. We seek a unit vector $v$ that maximizes the variance of the projected data $z = Xv$.

**Variance of Projections:**

$$ \text{Var}(z) = \frac{1}{n-1} (Xv)^T (Xv) = v^T \left(\frac{1}{n-1} X^T X\right) v = v^T \Sigma v $$

**Constrained Optimization:** Maximize $v^T \Sigma v$ subject to $v^T v = 1$.

**Lagrangian:**

$$ \mathcal{L}(v, \lambda) = v^T \Sigma v - \lambda (v^T v - 1) $$

**First-Order Condition:**

$$ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial v} = 2 \Sigma v - 2 \lambda v = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \Sigma v = \lambda v $$

This is the **eigenvalue equation**. The optimal $v$ must be an eigenvector of $\Sigma$, and the maximum variance equals the eigenvalue $\lambda$.

**Maximum Variance Direction:** Substituting $v_i$ into $v^T \Sigma v$:

$$ v_i^T \Sigma v_i = v_i^T (\lambda_i v_i) = \lambda_i v_i^T v_i = \lambda_i \quad (\text{since } v_i^T v_i = 1) $$

Hence the projected variance along eigenvector $v_i$ is exactly $\lambda_i$. To maximize variance, we choose the eigenvector with the **largest eigenvalue**.

$$ v_{\text{PC1}} = \arg\max_v v^T \Sigma v = v_1 \;\; \text{where} \;\; \Sigma v_1 = \lambda_1 v_1, \;\; \lambda_1 \ge \lambda_2 \ge \ldots $$

This proves that **PC1 = eigenvector of the largest eigenvalue**, and the second PC is the eigenvector of the second-largest eigenvalue, and so on. $\blacksquare$

---

**(b) Discuss the limitations of PCA. Explain how the choice of the number of principal components $k$ affects the bias-variance trade-off. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Limitations of PCA:**

1. **Linearity Assumption:** PCA captures only linear relationships. Non-linear manifolds (e.g., Swiss roll) cannot be unfolded by PCA; use **Kernel PCA** or **t-SNE** instead.
2. **Sensitivity to Scaling:** PCA is dominated by features with larger numeric ranges. Standardization is mandatory when features have different units.
3. **Sensitivity to Outliers:** Outliers inflate variance and distort principal components. Robust PCA variants exist.
4. **Loss of Interpretability:** PCs are linear combinations of original features and often lack physical meaning (e.g., "PC1 = 0.7 × age + 0.3 × income − 0.5 × height" is hard to interpret).
5. **Assumes Zero-Mean Gaussian-like Distribution:** PCA is optimal for Gaussian data; for heavy-tailed or multimodal distributions, alternative methods are preferred.
6. **Orthogonality Constraint:** PCs must be mutually orthogonal, which may exclude informative but correlated directions.

**Bias-Variance Trade-off in Choosing $k$:**

| $k$ Value | Effect |
|---|---|
| **$k$ too small (aggressive reduction)** | **High bias**, **low variance**. Loss of information; underfitting. The model oversimplifies. |
| **$k$ too large (minimal reduction)** | **Low bias**, **high variance**. Retains noise; overfitting. Computational cost remains high. |
| **$k = d$ (no reduction)** | No information loss, but no dimensionality reduction either. |
| **Optimal $k$** | Determined by the **elbow in the Scree plot** or by **cross-validation** on the downstream task. The goal is to retain $k$ such that $CEV_k \ge 0.90$ *and* the downstream model performs well. |

**Practical heuristic:** Plot $CEV_k$ vs $k$ and pick the **smallest $k$** where the curve plateaus (the "elbow"), which is where additional components yield diminishing variance returns.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on PCA Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mean-center** the data before computing the covariance matrix. → **−1 to −2 marks**.
> 2. **Not normalizing eigenvectors** to unit length. Examiners explicitly check $\Vert v_i \Vert_2 = 1$. → **−1 mark**.
> 3. **Mixing up the formulas** for covariance matrix ($X^T X / (n-1)$ vs $X^T X / n$). KTU prefers the unbiased estimator $\frac{1}{n-1}$. → **−0.5 mark**.
> 4. **Failing to verify the trace identity** $\sum \lambda_i = \mathrm{tr}(\Sigma)$. Examiners love this cross-check. → **−0.5 to −1 mark** if omitted.
> 5. **Not stating the explained variance ratio** explicitly with units (percent). Always show final percentage form.
> 6. **Confusing PCA with feature selection.** PCA *transforms* features; it does not select a subset. → Conceptual loss of **1–2 marks**.
> 7. **In derivation questions**, skipping the Lagrangian setup. The full optimization argument is mandatory for full marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for PCA (Module 1 — KTU 2024 Scheme):**

- **Definition:** PCA = linear, orthogonal, unsupervised dimensionality reduction via variance maximization.
- **Pipeline (in order):** Mean Center → Standardize (optional) → Covariance → Eigendecomposition → Sort → Select Top-k → Project.
- **Key Equation:** $\Sigma v = \lambda v$ — eigenvector gives PC direction, eigenvalue gives variance.
- **Variance Preservation:** Total variance is preserved ($\sum \lambda_i = \mathrm{tr}(\Sigma)$); PCA merely *redistributes* it.
- **Reconstruction Error:** = sum of discarded eigenvalues = $\sum_{i=k+1}^{d} \lambda_i$.
- **Explained Variance Ratio:** $EVR_i = \lambda_i / \sum_j \lambda_j$. Always sum-to-one across all $d$ components.
- **Cumulative Variance Threshold:** Standard choice is $CEV \ge 0.90$ for general applications; $0.95$ or $0.99$ for high-stakes domains.
- **Scree Plot:** Plot $\lambda_i$ vs $i$; look for the "elbow."
- **Kaiser's Rule:** Retain components with $\lambda > 1$ (only when data is standardized).
- **Standardization is MANDATORY** when features are on different scales (e.g., height in cm vs. weight in kg).
- **PC1 is orthogonal to PC2**, and each subsequent PC is orthogonal to all previous PCs.
- **Projection formula:** $Z = X_c W$ where $W$ holds the top $k$ eigenvectors as columns.
- **Reconstruction formula:** $\hat{X} = Z W^T + \mu$.
- **PCA is a feature extraction method**, NOT a feature selection method.
- **Limitations to memorize:** Linearity, scale-sensitivity, outlier-sensitivity, interpretability loss, orthogonality constraint.
- **Variance Maximization** derivation: Lagrangian setup with constraint $v^T v = 1$ leads to $\Sigma v = \lambda v$.
- **Trace Check:** Always verify $\sum \lambda_i = \mathrm{tr}(\Sigma)$ after computing eigenvalues.
- **For 2D numerical problems:** Compute mean → center → covariance → characteristic equation → eigenvalues → eigenvectors → EVR.
- **KTU favourite numbers:** $n = 5$, $d = 2$ toy datasets appear in almost every exam. Practice the full numerical drill.
- **Eigenvector normalization:** After computing direction, ALWAYS normalize using $v_i = v_i / \Vert v_i \Vert_2$.
- **Orthogonality check:** $v_1^T v_2 = 0$ for any two distinct PCs of a symmetric covariance matrix.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Discriminant Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT501 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Linear Discriminant Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA)** is a **supervised linear dimensionality reduction and classification technique** that projects a $d$-dimensional feature space onto a lower-dimensional subspace (at most $c-1$ dimensions for $c$ classes) by finding the linear combinations of features that best **separate two or more classes** of objects or events. The projection is chosen to maximize the ratio of **between-class variance** to **within-class variance**, ensuring maximum class separability in the reduced subspace.

Formally, LDA seeks a projection vector $\mathbf{w}$ that maximizes the **Fisher criterion**:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w}}$$

where $\mathbf{S}_B$ is the **between-class scatter matrix** and $\mathbf{S}_W$ is the **within-class scatter matrix**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PCITT501 Module 1):**
> LDA is covered under *Supervised Learning* sub-modules as a classical discriminant classifier and a foundational **generative-style discriminant model**. It bridges probabilistic Bayes decision theory and algebraic eigen-decomposition methods.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Spotlight Through a Crowded Hall" Analogy**
> 
> Imagine you are a teacher in a school auditorium with students from **3 different houses** (Red, Blue, Green), all standing and talking. From the stage (high above), you can see the entire crowd, but the students are mixed up, and the colors of their uniforms are the only way to identify groups. 
> 
> You can only shine a **single spotlight** in one direction. Where you point it determines what you can see clearly. 
> 
> - A *bad direction* (like a random angle) would compress the groups onto a thin line, mixing Red with Blue, making them indistinguishable.
> - A *great direction* would be one where, when projected onto that single line, the Red cluster sits far to the left, the Green cluster in the middle, and the Blue cluster far to the right — **with each cluster as tight (compact) as possible**.
> 
> **LDA is the mathematical algorithm that finds the best spotlight angle.** It picks the line of sight that:
> 1. **Pushes the class means as far apart as possible** (maximizes between-class distance).
> 2. **Keeps each individual class's members as close together as possible** (minimizes within-class spread).
> 
> This single best direction is the **first Linear Discriminant (LD1)**. If the classes still overlap, a second perpendicular direction (LD2) can be used, and so on — but at most $c-1$ such directions exist for $c$ classes.

### 1.3 Key Characteristics

- **Supervised**: Requires labelled training data (class labels known).
- **Linear**: Finds a linear combination of features (unlike Kernel PCA or t-SNE).
- **Parametric**: Assumes each class is approximately **multivariate Gaussian** with equal covariance matrices.
- **Dimensionality Reduction**: Reduces $d$ dimensions to at most $c-1$ dimensions.
- **Maximum Separability Criterion**: Uses Fisher's criterion, not variance preservation (unlike PCA).

### 1.4 Distinguishing LDA from PCA

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LDA vs PCA — Critical Distinction for Exams:**
> 
> | Aspect | LDA | PCA |
> |---|---|---|
> | Learning Type | Supervised | Unsupervised |
> | Objective | Class separability | Variance preservation |
> | Max Components | $\min(c-1, d)$ | $d$ |
> | Uses Labels | Yes | No |
> | Scatter Optimized | Between/Within-class | Total variance |
> 
> While PCA finds axes that **retain the most information**, LDA finds axes that **best discriminate classes**. They answer fundamentally different questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

LDA operates through a structured pipeline of six logical stages:

**Step 1 — Compute the $d$-dimensional Mean Vectors**
For each of the $c$ classes, compute the mean feature vector $\boldsymbol{\mu}_i$ over all $n_i$ samples belonging to class $i$:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_i = \frac{1}{n_i} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in D_i} \mathbf{x}$$

**Step 2 — Construct the Within-Class Scatter Matrix $\mathbf{S}_W$**
This measures the scatter (spread) of samples *around their own class mean*. Computed as the sum of per-class covariance matrices:

$$\mathbf{S}_W = \sum_{i=1}^{c} \mathbf{S}_i \quad \text{where} \quad \mathbf{S}_i = \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in D_i} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T$$

**Step 3 — Construct the Between-Class Scatter Matrix $\mathbf{S}_B$**
This measures the scatter of the *class means themselves* around the overall mean $\boldsymbol{\mu}$:

$$\mathbf{S}_B = \sum_{i=1}^{c} n_i (\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Matrix $\mathbf{S}_W^{-1} \mathbf{S}_B$**
This is the central matrix whose eigenstructure governs the solution.

**Step 5 — Solve the Generalized Eigenvalue Problem**
The optimal projection directions $\mathbf{W}$ are the eigenvectors of $\mathbf{S}_W^{-1} \mathbf{S}_B$ corresponding to the largest eigenvalues.

**Step 6 — Project and Classify**
Transform the data $\mathbf{x} \to \mathbf{y} = \mathbf{W}^T \mathbf{x}$ and use a distance-based classifier (e.g., Bayes, Euclidean distance to class means).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why maximize between-class scatter?** A larger $\mathbf{S}_B$ means class means are well-separated in the projected space, so classification boundaries have more "room" to be effective.
- **Why minimize within-class scatter?** A smaller $\mathbf{S}_W$ means each class forms a tight cluster, reducing the chance of overlap between different classes.
- **Why the ratio?** The ratio $\frac{\mathbf{S}_B}{\mathbf{S}_W}$ balances both objectives simultaneously. A large numerator AND a small denominator produce a high score.
- **Why eigenvalue decomposition?** The Fisher criterion is a **Rayleigh quotient**, which is analytically maximized when $\mathbf{w}$ is the eigenvector of $\mathbf{S}_W^{-1} \mathbf{S}_B$ with the largest eigenvalue.
- **Why at most $c-1$ dimensions?** The rank of $\mathbf{S}_B$ is at most $c-1$ because the deviations $\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}$ sum to zero (one linear dependency).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield LDA Formulas for KTU Board Exams**

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Constraint / Dimension |
|---|---|---|
| $\boldsymbol{\mu}_i = \frac{1}{n_i}\sum \mathbf{x} \in D_i \, \mathbf{x}$ | Mean vector of class $i$ | $d \times 1$ vector |
| $\boldsymbol{\mu} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{c} n_i \boldsymbol{\mu}_i$ | Overall (global) mean | $d \times 1$ vector |
| $\mathbf{S}_i = \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in D_i} (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i)(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T$ | Scatter matrix of class $i$ | $d \times d$ matrix |
| $\mathbf{S}_W = \sum_{i=1}^{c} \mathbf{S}_i$ | Within-class scatter matrix | $d \times d$, symmetric, positive semi-definite |
| $\mathbf{S}_B = \sum_{i=1}^{c} n_i (\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T$ | Between-class scatter matrix | $d \times d$, symmetric, rank $\le c-1$ |
| $\mathbf{S}_T = \mathbf{S}_W + \mathbf{S}_B$ | Total scatter matrix | $d \times d$ |
| $J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w}}$ | Fisher's criterion (objective) | Scalar to maximize |
| $(\mathbf{S}_W^{-1}\mathbf{S}_B)\mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}$ | Generalized eigenvalue problem | Solve for $\mathbf{w}, \lambda$ |
| $\text{rank}(\mathbf{S}_B) \le c-1$ | Max discriminant axes | Implies max $c-1$ projections |
| $\text{rank}(\mathbf{S}_W) \le n-c$ | Constraint on within-class rank | Requires $n > d$ typically |
| $P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) P(C_k)$ | Bayes classification in LDA space | Posterior used in discriminant |
| $\delta_k(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\mu}_k - \frac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\mu}_k + \log P(C_k)$ | Discriminant function | Bayes-optimal for Gaussian |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Boundary Condition:** LDA requires $\mathbf{S}_W$ to be **invertible (non-singular)**. When $d > n$ (high-dimensional small-sample case), regularization via $\mathbf{S}_W^{\text{reg}} = \mathbf{S}_W + \alpha \mathbf{I}$ (penalized LDA) is applied.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Face Recognition (Fisherfaces)**: Belhumeur et al. (1997) applied LDA to face recognition, outperforming Eigenfaces (PCA) under varying illumination.
- **Speech Recognition**: Used as a feature extraction step before HMM-based acoustic modelling.
- **Medical Diagnosis**: Reducing high-dimensional gene expression data ($d \approx 20{,}000$ genes) to $c-1$ discriminant axes for cancer sub-type classification.
- **Biometrics & Forensics**: Iris, fingerprint, and signature verification pipelines.
- **Quality Control in Manufacturing**: Hyperspectral imaging classification of product defects.
- **Marketing Analytics**: Customer segmentation with labelled churn/loyalty classes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Fisher Discriminant Criterion

**Goal:** Find the projection $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ that maximizes the ratio of between-class to within-class variance in the 1-D projected space.

**Step 1 — Project the class means onto $\mathbf{w}$.**

For class $i$, the projected mean is the scalar $\tilde{\mu}_i = \mathbf{w}^T \boldsymbol{\mu}_i$. In the 1-D projected space, the between-class variance becomes the squared distance of projected means from the overall projected mean $\tilde{\mu} = \mathbf{w}^T \boldsymbol{\mu}$:

$$\tilde{S}_B = \sum_{i=1}^{c} n_i (\tilde{\mu}_i - \tilde{\mu})^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{c} n_i (\mathbf{w}^T \boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \mathbf{w}^T \boldsymbol{\mu})^2$$

**Step 2 — Expand the squared term using the scalar identity $(a-b)^2 = (a-b)(a-b)^T$ for scalars (equivalent to outer product):**

$$\tilde{S}_B = \sum_{i=1}^{c} n_i \left[ \mathbf{w}^T (\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}) \right] \left[ (\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \mathbf{w} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Factor out $\mathbf{w}^T$ on the left and $\mathbf{w}$ on the right (since $\mathbf{w}$ is a column vector):**

$$\tilde{S}_B = \mathbf{w}^T \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{c} n_i (\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \right] \mathbf{w}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the bracketed matrix as the between-class scatter matrix $\mathbf{S}_B$:**

$$\tilde{S}_B = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w}$$

**Step 5 — Repeat the same expansion for the within-class scatter in projected space.** The within-class scatter of class $i$ is:

$$\tilde{S}_i = \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in D_i} (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}^T \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^2 = \mathbf{w}^T \left[ \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in D_i} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T \right] \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{S}_i \mathbf{w}$$

**Step 6 — Sum across all classes to get total within-class projected scatter:**

$$\tilde{S}_W = \sum_{i=1}^{c} \tilde{S}_i = \sum_{i=1}^{c} \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{S}_i \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{w}^T \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{c} \mathbf{S}_i \right] \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w}$$

**Step 7 — Form Fisher's criterion as the ratio to maximize:**

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{\tilde{S}_B}{\tilde{S}_W} = \frac{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Optimal Solution via Lagrange Multipliers

**Step 1 — Set up the constrained optimization.** Since scaling $\mathbf{w}$ by any non-zero scalar leaves $J(\mathbf{w})$ unchanged, we constrain the denominator: $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w} = 1$.

**Step 2 — Form the Lagrangian:**

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \lambda) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w} - \lambda (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w} - 1)$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $\mathbf{w}$ and set to zero.** Using $\frac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{w}} \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{w} = 2\mathbf{A}\mathbf{w}$ for symmetric $\mathbf{A}$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 2 \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w} - 2 \lambda \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange into the generalized eigenvalue problem:**

$$\mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \mathbf{S}_W^{-1} \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.** The optimal $\mathbf{w}$ is the eigenvector of $\mathbf{S}_W^{-1} \mathbf{S}_B$ corresponding to the largest eigenvalue $\lambda$. The eigenvalue itself equals the maximum value of $J(\mathbf{w})$:

$$J(\mathbf{w}^*) = \lambda_{\max}$$

**Step 6 — Multi-class extension.** To find $k$ discriminant directions, pick the top $k$ eigenvectors of $\mathbf{S}_W^{-1} \mathbf{S}_B$ corresponding to the $k$ largest eigenvalues. Stack them as columns to form the projection matrix $\mathbf{W} = [\mathbf{w}_1 \mid \mathbf{w}_2 \mid \cdots \mid \mathbf{w}_k]$.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (2 Classes, 2-D Features)

**Given data:**

| Class | Samples |
|---|---|
| $C_1$ | $\mathbf{x}_1 = (1, 2)^T$, $\mathbf{x}_2 = (2, 3)^T$ |
| $C_2$ | $\mathbf{x}_3 = (4, 5)^T$, $\mathbf{x}_4 = (5, 6)^T$ |

**Step 1 — Compute class means:**

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 = \frac{1}{2}\begin{pmatrix} 1+2 \\ 2+3 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1.5 \\ 2.5 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \boldsymbol{\mu}_2 = \frac{1}{2}\begin{pmatrix} 4+5 \\ 5+6 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 4.5 \\ 5.5 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute overall mean:**

$$\boldsymbol{\mu} = \frac{1}{4}\begin{pmatrix} 1+2+4+5 \\ 2+3+5+6 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compute within-class scatter matrices.**

For class $C_1$: deviations are $(1-1.5, 2-2.5)=(-0.5,-0.5)$ and $(0.5,0.5)$.

$$\mathbf{S}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} -0.5 \\ -0.5 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} -0.5 & -0.5 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.5 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 & 0.5 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & 0.5 \end{pmatrix}$$

For class $C_2$: deviations are $(-0.5,-0.5)$ and $(0.5,0.5)$ — same result:

$$\mathbf{S}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & 0.5 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\mathbf{S}_W$:**

$$\mathbf{S}_W = \mathbf{S}_1 + \mathbf{S}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 1.0 & 1.0 \\ 1.0 & 1.0 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\mathbf{S}_B$:**

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 - \boldsymbol{\mu} = \begin{pmatrix} -1.5 \\ -1.5 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \boldsymbol{\mu}_2 - \boldsymbol{\mu} = \begin{pmatrix} 1.5 \\ 1.5 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{S}_B = 2 \begin{pmatrix} -1.5 \\ -1.5 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} -1.5 & -1.5 \end{pmatrix} + 2 \begin{pmatrix} 1.5 \\ 1.5 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1.5 & 1.5 \end{pmatrix} = 2 \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 2.25 & 2.25 \\ 2.25 & 2.25 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 9 & 9 \\ 9 & 9 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Solve $\mathbf{S}_W^{-1} \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}$.**

The matrix $\mathbf{S}_W = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ is singular (determinant $= 0$). This is the **small-sample / singularity problem** in LDA. Applying ridge regularization:

$$\mathbf{S}_W^{\text{reg}} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} + 0.01 \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1.01 & 1 \\ 1 & 1.01 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\det(\mathbf{S}_W^{\text{reg}}) = 1.01^2 - 1 = 0.0201, \quad \mathbf{S}_W^{\text{reg},-1} = \frac{1}{0.0201}\begin{pmatrix} 1.01 & -1 \\ -1 & 1.01 \end{pmatrix} \approx \begin{pmatrix} 50.25 & -49.75 \\ -49.75 & 50.25 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 7 — Find eigenvectors of $\mathbf{S}_W^{\text{reg},-1} \mathbf{S}_B$.**

$$\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{S}_W^{\text{reg},-1} \mathbf{S}_B = \begin{pmatrix} 50.25 & -49.75 \\ -49.75 & 50.25 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 9 & 9 \\ 9 & 9 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 4.5 & 4.5 \\ 4.5 & 4.5 \end{pmatrix}$$

The characteristic equation $\det(\mathbf{M} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = (4.5 - \lambda)^2 - 4.5^2 = 0$ yields eigenvalues $\lambda_1 = 9, \lambda_2 = 0$.

**Step 8 — Find eigenvectors.** For $\lambda_1 = 9$:

$$\begin{pmatrix} -4.5 & 4.5 \\ 4.5 & -4.5 \end{pmatrix} \mathbf{w} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathbf{w}_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 9 — Interpret.** The single non-trivial discriminant direction is along the vector $(1,1)^T$ — i.e., the diagonal direction where classes are clearly separated. Projection of $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1$ onto $\mathbf{w}_1$ gives $\frac{1.5+2.5}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{4}{\sqrt{2}}$, and projection of $\boldsymbol{\mu}_2$ gives $\frac{4.5+5.5}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{10}{\sqrt{2}}$ — confirming clean separability.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 LDA Algorithmic Flow Architecture

The diagram below illustrates the complete operational pipeline of Linear Discriminant Analysis, from raw data input to final classification output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT Raw Labelled Dataset X with class labels Y] --> B[Step 1 Compute d-dimensional class mean vectors mu_i for each class i]
    B --> C[Step 2 Compute overall global mean vector mu]
    C --> D[Step 3 Construct Within-Class Scatter Matrix S_W by summing per-class covariance matrices]
    D --> E[Step 4 Construct Between-Class Scatter Matrix S_B using class means and global mean]
    E --> F[Step 5 Form the matrix product M equal to S_W inverse times S_B]
    F --> G[Step 6 Solve the Generalized Eigenvalue Problem M w equals lambda w]
    G --> H[Step 7 Select top k eigenvectors corresponding to k largest eigenvalues]
    H --> I[Step 8 Stack selected eigenvectors as columns to form projection matrix W of size d by k]
    I --> J[Step 9 Project original data y equals W transpose x to obtain k-dimensional representation]
    J --> K[Step 10 Apply Bayes or Distance-Based Classifier on projected y to assign class labels]
    K --> L[OUTPUT Predicted Class Labels and Reduced Feature Vectors]
```

### 4.2 Scatter Matrix Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Class 1 Samples] --> A1[Compute Deviation from mu_1]
    A1 --> A2[Form Outer Product Sum]
    A2 --> SW[Within-Class Scatter S_W]
    B[Class 2 Samples] --> B1[Compute Deviation from mu_2]
    B1 --> B2[Form Outer Product Sum]
    B2 --> SW
    C[Class c Samples] --> C1[Compute Deviation from mu_c]
    C1 --> C2[Form Outer Product Sum]
    C2 --> SW
    D[Class Means mu_i] --> D1[Compute Deviation from Global Mean mu]
    D1 --> D2[Form Weighted Outer Product Sum using n_i]
    D2 --> SB[Between-Class Scatter S_B]
    SW --> RATIO[Fisher Criterion J w equals wT S_B w over wT S_W w]
    SB --> RATIO
    RATIO --> OPT[Optimize to find best projection direction w]
```

### 4.3 LDA vs PCA Conceptual Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT[Input High-Dimensional Data X of size n by d] --> BRANCH{Supervised Labels Available?}
    BRANCH -->|No| PCA[Apply PCA]
    BRANCH -->|Yes| LDA[Apply LDA]
    PCA --> PCAOBJ[Objective: Maximize Total Variance wT S_T w]
    LDA --> LDAOBJ[Objective: Maximize Fisher Ratio wT S_B w over wT S_W w]
    PCAOBJ --> PCAOUT[Output: Top Eigenvectors of Covariance Matrix]
    LDAOBJ --> LDAOUT[Output: Top Eigenvectors of S_W inverse S_B]
    PCAOUT --> PCARESULT[Unsupervised Feature Subspace Up to d dimensions]
    LDAOUT --> LDARESULT[Supervised Feature Subspace Up to c minus 1 dimensions]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

> Define Linear Discriminant Analysis. State the Fisher criterion used in LDA.

**Model Answer:**

Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) is a supervised linear dimensionality reduction and classification technique that projects high-dimensional data onto a lower-dimensional subspace by maximizing class separability.

The Fisher criterion is:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w}}$$

where $\mathbf{S}_B$ is the between-class scatter matrix and $\mathbf{S}_W$ is the within-class scatter matrix. The optimal projection $\mathbf{w}$ maximizes this ratio.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for criterion, 1 for symbol explanation)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

> Differentiate between PCA and LDA in terms of supervised/unsupervised nature and the objective function.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | PCA | LDA |
|---|---|---|
| Type | Unsupervised | Supervised |
| Objective | Maximize total variance $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{S}_T \mathbf{w}$ | Maximize Fisher ratio $\frac{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w}}$ |
| Max Components | $d$ | $c-1$ |
| Uses Class Labels | No | Yes |

*(3 marks: 1 for each differentiating point)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**(a)** Derive the Fisher linear discriminant criterion for a two-class problem. Show that the optimal projection direction is given by the eigenvector of $\mathbf{S}_W^{-1}\mathbf{S}_B$ corresponding to its largest eigenvalue. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the following 2-D dataset, compute the within-class and between-class scatter matrices, and find the optimal LDA projection direction.

| Class | Samples |
|---|---|
| $C_1$ | $(2, 3),\ (4, 5)$ |
| $C_2$ | $(6, 1),\ (8, 3)$ |

**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — Define projection objective.** We seek $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ maximizing the ratio of between-class scatter to within-class scatter in the projected 1-D space:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w}}$$

**Step 2 — Constrain the denominator.** Since $J(\mathbf{w})$ is scale-invariant, impose $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w} = 1$.

**Step 3 — Lagrangian formulation:**

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \lambda) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w} - \lambda (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w} - 1)$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate and equate to zero:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 2\mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w} - 2\lambda \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w}$$

**Step 5 — Premultiply by $\mathbf{S}_W^{-1}$:**

$$\mathbf{S}_W^{-1} \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}$$

This is the generalized eigenvalue problem. The optimal $\mathbf{w}$ is the eigenvector corresponding to the largest eigenvalue $\lambda_{\max}$, and $J(\mathbf{w}^*) = \lambda_{\max}$.

*(7 marks: 2 for setup, 3 for derivation, 2 for final eigenvalue interpretation)*

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute class means:**

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 = \frac{1}{2}\begin{pmatrix} 2+4 \\ 3+5 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \boldsymbol{\mu}_2 = \frac{1}{2}\begin{pmatrix} 6+8 \\ 1+3 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 7 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute overall mean:**

$$\boldsymbol{\mu} = \frac{1}{4}\begin{pmatrix} 2+4+6+8 \\ 3+5+1+3 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 5 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\mathbf{S}_W$ (sum of per-class scatters):**

For $C_1$: deviations are $(-1, -1)$ and $(1, 1)$.

$$\mathbf{S}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} -1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} -1 & -1 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$$

For $C_2$: deviations are $(-1, -1)$ and $(1, 1)$.

$$\mathbf{S}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{S}_W = \mathbf{S}_1 + \mathbf{S}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 4 \\ 4 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\mathbf{S}_B$:**

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 - \boldsymbol{\mu} = \begin{pmatrix} -2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \boldsymbol{\mu}_2 - \boldsymbol{\mu} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{S}_B = 2 \begin{pmatrix} -2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} -2 & 1 \end{pmatrix} + 2 \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 2 & -1 \end{pmatrix} = 2 \begin{pmatrix} 4 & -2 \\ -2 & 1 \end{pmatrix} + 2 \begin{pmatrix} 4 & -2 \\ -2 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 16 & -8 \\ -8 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Singular $\mathbf{S}_W$ detected.** Determinant of $\mathbf{S}_W = 16 - 16 = 0$, so direct inversion fails. Apply ridge regularization with $\alpha = 0.1$:

$$\mathbf{S}_W^{\text{reg}} = \begin{pmatrix} 4.1 & 4 \\ 4 & 4.1 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \det = 16.81 - 16 = 0.81$$

$$\mathbf{S}_W^{\text{reg},-1} = \frac{1}{0.81}\begin{pmatrix} 4.1 & -4 \\ -4 & 4.1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Compute $\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{S}_W^{\text{reg},-1} \mathbf{S}_B$:**

$$\mathbf{M} = \frac{1}{0.81}\begin{pmatrix} 4.1 & -4 \\ -4 & 4.1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 16 & -8 \\ -8 & 4 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{0.81}\begin{pmatrix} 65.6+32 & -32.8-16 \\ -64-32.8 & 32+16.4 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{0.81}\begin{pmatrix} 97.6 & -48.8 \\ -96.8 & 48.4 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{M} \approx \begin{pmatrix} 120.49 & -60.25 \\ -119.51 & 59.75 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 7 — Solve eigenvalue problem.** The trace $= 180.24$ and determinant $= 120.49 \cdot 59.75 - (-60.25)(-119.51) \approx 7199.3 - 7200.4 \approx -1.1 \approx 0$. So eigenvalues are $\lambda_1 \approx 180.24, \lambda_2 \approx 0$.

**Step 8 — Eigenvector for $\lambda_1$:**

$$(\mathbf{M} - 180.24 \mathbf{I})\mathbf{w} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \begin{pmatrix} -59.75 & -60.25 \\ -119.51 & -120.49 \end{pmatrix} \mathbf{w} = 0$$

Solving: $-59.75 w_1 - 60.25 w_2 = 0 \Rightarrow w_1 \approx -w_2$. Normalizing:

$$\mathbf{w}^* = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 9 — Verify.** Project $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1$: $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(3-4) = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$. Project $\boldsymbol{\mu}_2$: $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(7-2) = \frac{5}{\sqrt{2}}$. The class means are well-separated along this direction.

*(7 marks: 1 mean, 1 S_W, 1 S_B, 2 eigenvalue problem, 2 eigenvector and verification)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024]*

**(a)** Explain the geometric and statistical interpretation of $\mathbf{S}_W$ and $\mathbf{S}_B$ in LDA. Why does LDA fail when $d > n$? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 3-class problem with equal class covariance $\mathbf{\Sigma}$, derive the Bayes-optimal discriminant function used in LDA classification. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Geometric Interpretation:**

- $\mathbf{S}_W$ (Within-Class Scatter Matrix) measures the **average spread of data points around their own class centroids**. Geometrically, it is the sum of the scatter clouds of each individual class. A small $\mathbf{S}_W$ means each class forms a **compact, tight cluster** in feature space.

- $\mathbf{S}_B$ (Between-Class Scatter Matrix) measures the **spread of class centroids around the global mean**, weighted by class size $n_i$. Geometrically, it captures the **mutual separation of class clusters**. A large $\mathbf{S}_B$ means the class centroids are **far apart**, indicating good separation.

**Statistical Interpretation:**

- $\mathbf{S}_W$ is the **pooled within-class covariance scaled by sample sizes** — a measure of within-class variance.

- $\mathbf{S}_B$ represents the **variance explained by the class label** — a measure of between-class variance attributable to class membership.

**Why LDA fails when $d > n$:**

- The rank of $\mathbf{S}_W$ is bounded by $n - c$ (since $n_i - 1$ degrees of freedom per class).

- When $d > n$, we have $n - c < d$, so $\mathbf{S}_W \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$ is **rank-deficient** (singular). Its determinant is zero, and $\mathbf{S}_W^{-1}$ does not exist.

- This breaks the generalized eigenvalue problem $\mathbf{S}_W^{-1}\mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}$.

- **Remedies**: Ridge regularization $\mathbf{S}_W + \alpha \mathbf{I}$, PCA + LDA pipeline, or penalized LDA (PLDA).

*(7 marks: 2 geometric, 2 statistical, 3 for failure cause and remedies)*

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — Bayesian framework.** Under the LDA assumption that each class $C_k$ is multivariate Gaussian with shared covariance $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ and mean $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$, the class-conditional density is:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2} \vert \mathbf{\Sigma} \vert^{1/2}} \exp\left( -\frac{1}{2} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k)^T \mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k) \right)$$

**Step 2 — Bayes' posterior.** $P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) P(C_k)$.

**Step 3 — Take the logarithm (monotonic) to obtain discriminant function:**

$$\log P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = -\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k)^T \mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1}(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k) - \frac{1}{2}\log \vert \mathbf{\Sigma} \vert - \frac{d}{2}\log 2\pi + \log P(C_k)$$

**Step 4 — Drop constants independent of $k$ (the $\vert \mathbf{\Sigma} \vert$ and $\pi$ terms are shared by all classes):**

$$\delta_k(\mathbf{x}) = -\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k)^T \mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1}(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k) + \log P(C_k)$$

**Step 5 — Expand the quadratic term:**

$$(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k)^T \mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1}(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k) = \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1} \mathbf{x} - 2\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^T \mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1} \mathbf{x} + \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^T \mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\mu}_k$$

**Step 6 — The $\mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1} \mathbf{x}$ term is also $k$-independent (since $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ is shared), so drop it:**

$$\delta_k(\mathbf{x}) = \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^T \mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1} \mathbf{x} - \frac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^T \mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\mu}_k + \log P(C_k)$$

**Step 7 — Classification rule.** Assign $\mathbf{x}$ to class $C_k^*$ that maximizes $\delta_k(\mathbf{x})$:

$$k^* = \arg\max_{k} \left[ \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^T \mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1} \mathbf{x} - \frac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^T \mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\mu}_k + \log P(C_k) \right]$$

The resulting decision boundaries are **linear** in $\mathbf{x}$ — hence the name Linear Discriminant Analysis.

*(7 marks: 2 Gaussian setup, 2 log-likelihood, 1 expansion, 2 final discriminant rule)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize the eigenvector**: Many students write $\mathbf{w} = (1, -1)^T$ instead of the unit vector $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1, -1)^T$. The projection direction is unique only up to scale, but the unit form is required for proper distance computations. **[-1 mark]**
> 
> 2. **Confusing $\mathbf{S}_B$ formula**: Some students use $\mathbf{S}_B = \sum (\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T$ without the **$n_i$ weighting factor**. The correct form **must** weight by the number of samples per class. **[-1 to -2 marks]**
> 
> 3. **Saying "LDA has $d$ components"**: The maximum number of discriminant axes is $\min(c-1, d)$, **not** $d$. The $c-1$ bound comes from rank$(\mathbf{S}_B) \le c-1$. **[-1 mark]**
> 
> 4. **Omitting the $d > n$ singularity issue**: Any question about LDA limitations requires explicit mention of the **rank-deficiency of $\mathbf{S}_W$** and the need for regularization. **[-2 marks if missing]**
> 
> 5. **Writing PCA instead of LDA in the eigenvalue equation**: The eigenvector equation is $\mathbf{S}_W^{-1} \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}$, **not** $\mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}$ (which is PCA). **[-2 marks]**
> 
> 6. **Not verifying the projection direction**: After finding $\mathbf{w}$, always project the class means onto it to confirm the projected means are well-separated. This earns full marks for "interpretation" portion.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Linear Discriminant Analysis**

**Core Identity:**
- **LDA = Supervised Linear Dimensionality Reduction + Classifier**
- Uses **labels** to find the most discriminative subspace
- Reduces $d \to \min(c-1, d)$ dimensions

**Three Essential Matrices:**
- $\mathbf{S}_W = \sum_{i=1}^{c} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in D_i} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T$ — within-class scatter
- $\mathbf{S}_B = \sum_{i=1}^{c} n_i (\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T$ — between-class scatter
- $\mathbf{S}_T = \mathbf{S}_W + \mathbf{S}_B$ — total scatter

**The Master Equation (Fisher's Criterion):**
$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w}}$$

**Solution Method (in order):**
1. Compute class means $\boldsymbol{\mu}_i$ and global mean $\boldsymbol{\mu}$.
2. Build $\mathbf{S}_W$ (sum of class scatters) and $\mathbf{S}_B$ (weighted deviations of means).
3. Solve the generalized eigenvalue problem $\mathbf{S}_W^{-1} \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}$.
4. Pick top $k$ eigenvectors, stack as columns into $\mathbf{W}$.
5. Project: $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{W}^T \mathbf{x}$.
6. Classify using Bayes discriminant or distance to projected class means.

**Critical Constraints:**
- $\mathbf{S}_W$ must be invertible — fails when $d > n$ (use regularization)
- $\text{rank}(\mathbf{S}_B) \le c-1$ — max discriminant axes is $c-1$
- Assumes **homoscedasticity** (equal covariance across classes)
- Assumes **multivariate Gaussian** class-conditional distributions

**Key Distinctions to Memorize:**
- LDA ≠ PCA (supervised vs unsupervised)
- LDA axes maximize **separability**, not **variance**
- LDA is **linear** — for non-linear separability, use Kernel LDA or KPCA
- LDA also serves as a **classifier** via the Bayes discriminant function

**The Bayes Discriminant Function (for classification after projection):**
$$\delta_k(\mathbf{x}) = \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^T \mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1} \mathbf{x} - \frac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^T \mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\mu}_k + \log P(C_k)$$

**Engineering Applications to Remember:**
- Face recognition (Fisherfaces)
- Speech recognition front-end
- Medical diagnosis (gene expression)
- Biometric authentication
- Quality control (hyperspectral imaging)

**Numerical Verification Tip:** After computing $\mathbf{w}$, always **project the class means onto it** and verify the projected means are well-separated. This single step is worth 1–2 marks on every LDA problem in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Error Metrics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT501 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Error Metrics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Error Metrics** are quantitative measures used to evaluate the performance of a Machine Learning model by computing the discrepancy between the **predicted output** $\hat{y}$ and the **actual (true) output** $y$. They provide an objective, scalar-valued assessment of how well a model has learned the underlying pattern in the data, and they form the backbone of model selection, hyperparameter tuning, and comparative benchmarking.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, error metrics are broadly classified into two major families:

1. **Classification Error Metrics** — used when the target variable is categorical (discrete labels).
2. **Regression Error Metrics** — used when the target variable is continuous (real-valued).

The choice of an error metric is **not arbitrary**; it is dictated by the problem domain, class distribution, and the relative cost of different types of errors.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Weather Forecaster:**
> Imagine a weather forecaster who predicts whether it will rain tomorrow. Every day, we compare the forecaster's prediction ("Rain" / "No Rain") with what actually happened. To judge the forecaster, we don't just ask "Was the forecaster right?" — we ask more nuanced questions:
> - *Of all the times the forecaster said "Rain", how often did it actually rain?* → **Precision**
> - *Of all the rainy days, how many did the forecaster correctly predict?* → **Recall**
> - *How often was the forecaster right overall?* → **Accuracy**
>
> Error metrics are the mathematical formalization of these questions. They tell us **not just whether** the model was wrong, but **how** and **where** it was wrong.

### 1.3 Why Error Metrics Are Central to ML

- They serve as the **objective function** (or a proxy) that learning algorithms minimize or maximize.
- They enable **model comparison** across different algorithms (e.g., Logistic Regression vs. SVM vs. Decision Tree).
- They expose **hidden failures** — e.g., a 99% accurate spam filter might be useless if it simply classifies every email as "not spam."
- They guide **threshold selection** in probabilistic classifiers.

> [!TIP]
> **Key KTU Terminology to Remember:**
> - **Loss Function**: The function that is *minimized* during model training (e.g., Cross-Entropy Loss, MSE).
> - **Error Metric**: The function that is *evaluated* after model training to report performance (e.g., Accuracy, F1-Score).
> - These are often closely related but serve different purposes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Confusion Matrix (Foundation of Classification Metrics)

The **Confusion Matrix** is a $2 \times 2$ (binary) or $N \times N$ (multi-class) table that records the counts of correct and incorrect predictions against actual labels.

For binary classification, the four cells are:

- **TP (True Positive)**: Model predicted *Positive*, and the actual label is *Positive*.
- **TN (True Negative)**: Model predicted *Negative*, and the actual label is *Negative*.
- **FP (False Positive)**: Model predicted *Positive*, but the actual label is *Negative*. (Also called **Type I Error**)
- **FN (False Negative)**: Model predicted *Negative*, but the actual label is *Positive*. (Also called **Type II Error**)

### 2.2 Classification Error Metrics — Operational Logic

Each metric is derived directly from the confusion matrix counts. The following structured breakdown explains the *why* behind each metric:

- **Accuracy**: Measures the proportion of total correct predictions.
  - *Why use it?* Intuitive and easy to communicate.
  - *When it fails?* On **imbalanced datasets** (e.g., 99% of emails are non-spam → a dumb model gets 99% accuracy by predicting "not spam" always).

- **Precision (Positive Predictive Value)**: Of all instances the model labeled as positive, how many are truly positive.
  - *Why use it?* When **False Positives are costly** (e.g., spam detection — we don't want to lose important emails to the spam folder).

- **Recall (Sensitivity / True Positive Rate)**: Of all actual positive instances, how many did the model correctly identify.
  - *Why use it?* When **False Negatives are costly** (e.g., cancer detection — we cannot afford to miss a positive case).

- **F1-Score**: The **harmonic mean** of Precision and Recall.
  - *Why use it?* Provides a **single balanced metric** when you need to trade off Precision vs. Recall. Unlike the arithmetic mean, the harmonic mean **penalizes extreme imbalances** between P and R.

- **Specificity (True Negative Rate)**: Of all actual negatives, how many were correctly identified.
  - *Why use it?* Complements Recall, especially in medical and security domains.

- **ROC-AUC**: The **Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve** plots TPR (Recall) vs. FPR at various thresholds. AUC = 1.0 is perfect; AUC = 0.5 is random guessing.

### 2.3 Regression Error Metrics — Operational Logic

Regression metrics measure the magnitude of the numerical deviation between predicted and actual continuous values:

- **MAE (Mean Absolute Error)**: Average of absolute residuals. Robust to outliers. Same unit as the target.
- **MSE (Mean Squared Error)**: Average of squared residuals. Penalizes large errors heavily. Unit is squared.
- **RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error)**: Square root of MSE. Same unit as the target. Most widely used in practice.
- **R² (Coefficient of Determination)**: Proportion of variance in the target explained by the model. $R^2 = 1$ is perfect; $R^2 = 0$ means the model is no better than the mean.
- **Adjusted R²**: Penalizes $R^2$ for adding irrelevant features. Used in multiple regression.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready reference**. Memorize the formulas and their applicability.

| Metric | Formula | Range / Unit | Best Used When |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Accuracy** | $\dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Balanced classes |
| **Error Rate** | $1 - \text{Accuracy} = \dfrac{FP + FN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Reporting misclassification rate |
| **Precision (P)** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | FP is costly |
| **Recall (R)** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | FN is costly |
| **F1-Score** | $\dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Need a balance of P and R |
| **Specificity** | $\dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Evaluating TN performance |
| **FPR** | $\dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | ROC curve axis |
| **MAE** | $\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lvert y_i - \hat{y}_i \rvert$ | Same as $y$ | Robust to outliers |
| **MSE** | $\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | $(y)^2$ | Penalize large errors |
| **RMSE** | $\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$ | Same as $y$ | General regression reporting |
| **R²** | $1 - \dfrac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}$ | $(-\infty, 1]$ | Variance explained |
| **Adjusted R²** | $1 - \dfrac{(1-R^2)(n-1)}{n-k-1}$ | $(-\infty, 1]$ | Multiple regression |

> [!NOTE]
> In all regression formulas, $n$ is the number of samples and $k$ is the number of independent features. The term $y_i$ is the actual value, $\hat{y}_i$ is the predicted value, and $\bar{y}$ is the mean of actual values.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Diagnosis**: Recall-optimized models to avoid missing cancer cases.
- **Credit Card Fraud Detection**: Precision-optimized to avoid flagging legitimate transactions.
- **Autonomous Vehicle Steering Angle Prediction (Regression)**: RMSE used because large angular errors are dangerous and must be penalized more.
- **House Price Prediction (Regression)**: MAE or RMSE depending on whether outliers (luxury homes) matter.
- **Weather Forecasting**: F1-score commonly used to balance false alarms and missed storms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of MSE and RMSE from First Principles

The **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** is the average of the squared differences between predicted and actual values. We derive it step-by-step from the concept of a residual.

**Step 1**: Define the residual (error) for the $i$-th data point.

$$e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$$

**Step 2**: Square the residual to eliminate the sign and emphasize larger errors.

$$e_i^2 = (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$

**Step 3**: Sum all squared residuals across the dataset of $n$ points.

$$SSE = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$

Here, **SSE** stands for the *Sum of Squared Errors*. This is also called the *Residual Sum of Squares (RSS)*.

**Step 4**: Average the sum by dividing by the number of samples $n$ to get the **MSE**.

$$MSE = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$

**Step 5**: Take the square root to bring the error back to the original unit of the target variable — this yields the **RMSE**.

$$RMSE = \sqrt{MSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The squaring step in MSE is what makes the metric **differenti-able** (smooth gradient everywhere) — this is why MSE is the default loss function for linear regression in the OLS (Ordinary Least Squares) method.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of R² (Coefficient of Determination)

The $R^2$ metric answers: *"What fraction of the total variability in $y$ is explained by our model?"*

**Step 1**: Define the **Total Sum of Squares (SST)** — the variability of $y$ around its own mean.

$$SST = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \bar{y})^2$$

**Step 2**: Define the **Residual Sum of Squares (SSR)** — the unexplained variability after fitting the model.

$$SSR = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$

**Step 3**: Define the **Explained Sum of Squares (SSE_exp)** — the variability explained by the model.

$$SSE_{exp} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\hat{y}_i - \bar{y})^2$$

**Step 4**: Use the partitioning identity: $SST = SSE_{exp} + SSR$.

**Step 5**: The fraction of explained variability is:

$$R^2 = \frac{SSE_{exp}}{SST} = 1 - \frac{SSR}{SST} = 1 - \frac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}$$

> [!TIP]
> If $R^2 = 1$, the model explains 100% of the variance (perfect fit). If $R^2 = 0$, the model is no better than predicting the mean. If $R^2 < 0$, the model is worse than the mean predictor (possible with overfitting on test data).

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the F1-Score from Precision and Recall

**Step 1**: Recall the harmonic mean of two numbers $a$ and $b$ is:

$$H = \frac{2ab}{a+b}$$

**Step 2**: Substitute $a = P$ (Precision) and $b = R$ (Recall).

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$$

**Step 3**: Substitute the full definitions of $P$ and $R$:

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP} \cdot \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}}{\dfrac{TP}{TP+FP} + \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}}$$

**Step 4**: Simplify (multiply numerator and denominator by $(TP+FP)(TP+FN)$):

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This last form is the most exam-friendly version of F1: it depends **only on TP, FP, FN** (TN is not needed).

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Confusion Matrix Metrics

**Problem Statement:**
A binary classifier is tested on $200$ samples. The confusion matrix is:

| | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **Actual Positive** | $TP = 50$ | $FN = 10$ |
| **Actual Negative** | $FP = 20$ | $TN = 120$ |

Compute Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, Specificity, and Error Rate.

**Step 1: Compute Accuracy**

$$Accuracy = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{50 + 120}{50 + 120 + 20 + 10} = \frac{170}{200} = 0.85$$

**Step 2: Compute Precision**

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{50}{50 + 20} = \frac{50}{70} \approx 0.714$$

**Step 3: Compute Recall**

$$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{50}{50 + 10} = \frac{50}{60} \approx 0.833$$

**Step 4: Compute F1-Score**

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 0.714 \cdot 0.833}{0.714 + 0.833} = \frac{1.190}{1.547} \approx 0.769$$

**Step 5: Compute Specificity**

$$Specificity = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{120}{120 + 20} = \frac{120}{140} \approx 0.857$$

**Step 6: Compute Error Rate**

$$Error\ Rate = 1 - Accuracy = 1 - 0.85 = 0.15$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity Check:** Precision (0.714) is lower than Recall (0.833), which means the model is **over-predicting positives** (producing more false positives). F1 = 0.769 sits between them, but closer to the lower one — this is the harmonic mean behavior.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Regression Metrics

**Problem Statement:**
A model makes the following 5 predictions for actual values $y = [3, 5, 2, 7, 4]$:

$\hat{y} = [2.5, 5.5, 1.8, 6.0, 4.5]$

Compute MAE, MSE, RMSE, and $R^2$.

**Step 1: Compute the residuals**

$$e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i = [0.5, -0.5, 0.2, 1.0, -0.5]$$

**Step 2: Compute MAE**

$$MAE = \frac{1}{5}(0.5 + 0.5 + 0.2 + 1.0 + 0.5) = \frac{2.7}{5} = 0.54$$

**Step 3: Compute MSE**

$$MSE = \frac{1}{5}(0.25 + 0.25 + 0.04 + 1.0 + 0.25) = \frac{1.79}{5} = 0.358$$

**Step 4: Compute RMSE**

$$RMSE = \sqrt{0.358} \approx 0.598$$

**Step 5: Compute $R^2$**

First, compute the mean of $y$: $\bar{y} = \frac{3+5+2+7+4}{5} = 4.2$.

$$SST = (3-4.2)^2 + (5-4.2)^2 + (2-4.2)^2 + (7-4.2)^2 + (4-4.2)^2 = 1.44 + 0.64 + 4.84 + 7.84 + 0.04 = 14.8$$

$$SSR = (0.5)^2 + (-0.5)^2 + (0.2)^2 + (1.0)^2 + (-0.5)^2 = 0.25 + 0.25 + 0.04 + 1.0 + 0.25 = 1.79$$

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{1.79}{14.8} = 1 - 0.1210 = 0.879$$

> [!NOTE]
> $R^2 = 0.879$ means the model explains **87.9%** of the variance in the target — a strong fit for this small dataset.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Taxonomy of Error Metrics

The following block diagram maps the hierarchical classification of all error metrics discussed in the KTU Module 2 syllabus, with their functional relationships.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ERROR METRICS] --> B[CLASSIFICATION METRICS]
    A --> C[REGRESSION METRICS]

    B --> B1[CONFUSION MATRIX]
    B1 --> B1A[TP TRUE POSITIVE]
    B1 --> B1B[TN TRUE NEGATIVE]
    B1 --> B1C[FP FALSE POSITIVE TYPE I ERROR]
    B1 --> B1D[FN FALSE NEGATIVE TYPE II ERROR]

    B --> B2[ACCURACY METRICS]
    B2 --> B2A[ACCURACY]
    B2 --> B2B[ERROR RATE]

    B --> B3[PERFORMANCE METRICS]
    B3 --> B3A[PRECISION]
    B3 --> B3B[RECALL SENSITIVITY]
    B3 --> B3C[SPECIFICITY TNR]
    B3 --> B3D[F1 SCORE HARMONIC MEAN]
    B3 --> B3E[FPR FALSE POSITIVE RATE]

    B --> B4[THRESHOLD METRICS]
    B4 --> B4A[ROC CURVE]
    B4A --> B4A1[AUC AREA UNDER CURVE]

    C --> C1[DISTANCE BASED]
    C1 --> C1A[MAE MEAN ABSOLUTE ERROR]
    C1 --> C1B[MSE MEAN SQUARED ERROR]
    C1 --> C1C[RMSE ROOT MEAN SQUARED ERROR]

    C --> C2[VARIANCE BASED]
    C2 --> C2A[R SQUARED]
    C2 --> C2B[ADJUSTED R SQUARED]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Confusion Matrix Visual Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MATRIX[CONFUSION MATRIX 2 BY 2]
        direction TB
        Q1[TP CORRECT POSITIVE PREDICTION]
        Q2[FN MISSED POSITIVE TYPE II ERROR]
        Q3[FP FALSE ALARM TYPE I ERROR]
        Q4[TN CORRECT NEGATIVE PREDICTION]
    end

    R1[PRECISION EQUALS TP DIVIDED BY TP PLUS FP] --> Q1
    R1 --> Q3
    R2[RECALL EQUALS TP DIVIDED BY TP PLUS FN] --> Q1
    R2 --> Q2
    R3[F1 SCORE EQUALS 2TP DIVIDED BY 2TP PLUS FP PLUS FN] --> Q1
    R3 --> Q2
    R3 --> Q3
    R4[ACCURACY EQUALS TP PLUS TN DIVIDED BY TOTAL] --> Q1
    R4 --> Q4
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Precision vs Recall Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[CLASSIFICATION PROBLEM] --> Q1{WHICH ERROR IS MORE COSTLY}

    Q1 -->|FALSE POSITIVES COSTLY| P_HIGH[HIGH PRECISION MODEL]
    P_HIGH --> P_EX[EXAMPLE SPAM FILTER LEGIT MAIL MUST NOT BE FLAGGED]

    Q1 -->|FALSE NEGATIVES COSTLY| R_HIGH[HIGH RECALL MODEL]
    R_HIGH --> R_EX[EXAMPLE CANCER DETECTION MISSING A CASE IS FATAL]

    Q1 -->|BOTH ERRORS EQUALLY COSTLY| BAL[F1 SCORE OPTIMIZATION]
    BAL --> BAL_EX[EXAMPLE INFORMATION RETRIEVAL BALANCED SEARCH ENGINE]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The trade-off diagram above is a **frequently asked 7-mark question** in KTU exams. Always justify your choice with a domain-specific example.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the terms Precision and Recall in the context of a classification problem. How are they combined to form the F1-Score?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Precision** is the ratio of correctly predicted positive observations to the total predicted positives. It answers: *"Of all instances labeled as positive, how many are truly positive?"*

$$Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

**Recall** (also called Sensitivity or True Positive Rate) is the ratio of correctly predicted positive observations to all actual positives. It answers: *"Of all actual positive instances, how many did we catch?"*

$$Recall = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

The **F1-Score** is the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall, providing a single balanced metric:

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$$

It is preferred over the arithmetic mean because it **penalizes extreme imbalance** — if either $P$ or $R$ is low, the F1-score will be low.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between MAE and RMSE. In which scenario would you prefer MAE over RMSE?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | MAE | RMSE |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Formula | $\frac{1}{n}\sum \lvert y_i - \hat{y}_i\rvert$ | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$ |
| Penalizes large errors | Linearly | Quadratically |
| Robust to outliers | Yes | No |
| Differentiability at zero | Not differentiable | Smooth everywhere |

**Scenario for MAE preference:** When the dataset contains **outliers** that should not disproportionately influence the loss, MAE is preferred. Example: Predicting median house prices where a few luxury mansions would otherwise dominate the MSE.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **write the formula for F1 but forget to substitute the values of P and R**, losing 2–3 marks.
> - Confusion matrix labels (TP/TN/FP/FN) are frequently **swapped** — always double-check by re-reading the problem statement.
> - In regression problems, students forget to take the **square root** when computing RMSE from MSE.
> - For R², students often report values **outside [0, 1]** on test data and do not interpret them correctly — a negative R² is valid and indicates the model is worse than the mean predictor.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Style]*

**(a)** Explain the **Confusion Matrix** for a binary classification problem. Define Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score with formulas. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Given the following actual and predicted labels for a binary classifier on 100 samples:

- Actual Positives: 40, Actual Negatives: 60
- TP = 35, FN = 5, FP = 10, TN = 50

Compute the **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and Specificity**. Identify whether the model favors Precision or Recall. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.A (a) — 7 Marks**

A **Confusion Matrix** is a tabular visualization of actual vs. predicted classifications. For a binary problem, it is a $2 \times 2$ table with four cells: TP, FP, FN, TN.

- **TP (True Positive)**: Predicted Positive, Actually Positive. → $35$ in our dataset
- **TN (True Negative)**: Predicted Negative, Actually Negative. → $50$
- **FP (False Positive)**: Predicted Positive, Actually Negative. → $10$ (Type I Error)
- **FN (False Negative)**: Predicted Negative, Actually Positive. → $5$ (Type II Error)

**Accuracy** is the fraction of all correct predictions:

$$Accuracy = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{35 + 50}{35 + 50 + 10 + 5} = \frac{85}{100} = 0.85$$

**Precision** measures the reliability of positive predictions:

$$Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{35}{35 + 10} = \frac{35}{45} \approx 0.778$$

**Recall** measures the model's ability to find all positive instances:

$$Recall = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{35}{35 + 5} = \frac{35}{40} = 0.875$$

**F1-Score** is the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall:

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 0.778 \cdot 0.875}{0.778 + 0.875} = \frac{1.361}{1.653} \approx 0.823$$

**Interpretation**: With Accuracy = 0.85, the model is generally good. Precision (0.778) < Recall (0.875) means the model is producing **more false positives** than false negatives — it is "over-flagging" positives. If false positives are expensive (e.g., blocking legitimate users), this model would need **threshold tuning** to increase Precision at the cost of Recall.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.A (b) — 7 Marks**

Using the same dataset: $TP = 35, FN = 5, FP = 10, TN = 50$, Total $= 100$.

**Step 1 — Accuracy:**

$$Accuracy = \frac{TP + TN}{Total} = \frac{35 + 50}{100} = 0.85$$

**Step 2 — Precision:**

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{35}{45} = 0.778$$

**Step 3 — Recall:**

$$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{35}{40} = 0.875$$

**Step 4 — F1-Score:**

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.778 \cdot 0.875}{0.778 + 0.875} = \frac{1.3616}{1.653} \approx 0.824$$

**Step 5 — Specificity:**

$$Specificity = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{50}{50 + 10} = \frac{50}{60} \approx 0.833$$

**Step 6 — Model Bias Identification:**

Since **Recall (0.875) > Precision (0.778)**, the model favors **Recall** (i.e., it catches more true positives but at the cost of raising more false alarms). This is typical of models in **medical screening**, where missing a positive case is more dangerous than a false alarm that can be ruled out by further tests.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]*

**(a)** Explain the metrics **MAE, MSE, RMSE, and R²** for regression problems. Why is RMSE preferred over MSE for reporting purposes? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A linear regression model is evaluated on 6 test samples. The actual and predicted values are:

| Sample $i$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Actual $y_i$ | $10$ | $15$ | $8$ | $20$ | $12$ | $18$ |
| Predicted $\hat{y}_i$ | $11$ | $14$ | $9$ | $18$ | $13$ | $16$ |

Compute **MAE, MSE, RMSE, and R²**. Comment on the model's performance. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.B (a) — 7 Marks**

**MAE (Mean Absolute Error)** is the average of the absolute differences between predicted and actual values:

$$MAE = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lvert y_i - \hat{y}_i\rvert$$

It is expressed in the **same unit** as the target variable and is **robust to outliers** because it uses absolute values.

**MSE (Mean Squared Error)** is the average of the squared differences:

$$MSE = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$

Squaring penalizes large errors much more heavily. Its unit is the **square** of the target's unit, which is why it is rarely reported directly.

**RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error)** is the square root of MSE:

$$RMSE = \sqrt{MSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$$

It brings the error back to the **same unit** as the target, making it interpretable. For example, if we are predicting house prices in lakhs and RMSE = 5.2, we say "the model is off by ₹5.2 lakhs on average."

**R² (Coefficient of Determination)** represents the proportion of variance in the target variable explained by the model:

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}$$

$R^2 = 1$ is a perfect fit; $R^2 = 0$ means the model is no better than predicting the mean; $R^2 < 0$ means the model is worse than the mean.

**Why RMSE is preferred over MSE for reporting:**
1. RMSE is in the **same unit** as $y$, making it interpretable.
2. MSE is in **squared units**, which is non-physical.
3. RMSE is the **square root** of a loss function, so it preserves the property of penalizing large errors while remaining easy to communicate to stakeholders.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.B (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Compute residuals and their squares:**

| $i$ | $y_i$ | $\hat{y}_i$ | $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$ | $\lvert e_i \rvert$ | $e_i^2$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 10 | 11 | $-1$ | 1 | 1 |
| 2 | 15 | 14 | $+1$ | 1 | 1 |
| 3 | 8 | 9 | $-1$ | 1 | 1 |
| 4 | 20 | 18 | $+2$ | 2 | 4 |
| 5 | 12 | 13 | $-1$ | 1 | 1 |
| 6 | 18 | 16 | $+2$ | 2 | 4 |
| **Sum** | — | — | — | **8** | **12** |

**Step 2 — Compute MAE:**

$$MAE = \frac{8}{6} = 1.333$$

**Step 3 — Compute MSE:**

$$MSE = \frac{12}{6} = 2.0$$

**Step 4 — Compute RMSE:**

$$RMSE = \sqrt{2.0} \approx 1.414$$

**Step 5 — Compute R²:**

Mean of $y$:

$$\bar{y} = \frac{10 + 15 + 8 + 20 + 12 + 18}{6} = \frac{83}{6} \approx 13.833$$

Sum of squared deviations from the mean (SST):

$$SST = (10-13.83)^2 + (15-13.83)^2 + (8-13.83)^2 + (20-13.83)^2 + (12-13.83)^2 + (18-13.83)^2$$
$$= 14.69 + 1.36 + 33.99 + 38.07 + 3.36 + 17.36 = 108.83$$

Sum of squared residuals (SSR) = $12$ (from Step 1).

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{12}{108.83} = 1 - 0.1103 = 0.8897$$

**Step 6 — Performance Commentary:**

- **MAE = 1.333**: On average, predictions deviate from actual values by ~1.33 units.
- **RMSE = 1.414**: Slightly higher than MAE, indicating a small number of larger errors (the $+2$ errors at samples 4 and 6) are penalized more.
- **R² = 0.890**: The model explains **89%** of the variance in the target — a strong fit.

> The model performs well overall. The slight gap between MAE (1.33) and RMSE (1.41) suggests the errors are not extremely skewed by outliers; the largest single error is only 2 units. For production deployment, this R² of 0.89 is considered good for most regression tasks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Error Metrics**

- **Classification metrics rely on the Confusion Matrix**: Always identify TP, TN, FP, FN correctly. TN is *not* used in Precision, Recall, or F1.
- **Accuracy is misleading on imbalanced data**: A 99% accuracy on a 99:1 imbalanced dataset is meaningless.
- **Precision vs. Recall trade-off** is the most frequently tested concept — always justify with a domain example (cancer detection → recall, spam filter → precision).
- **F1-Score = Harmonic Mean of P and R**; use the simplified form $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$ for quicker calculations in the exam.
- **MAE vs. RMSE**: MAE is robust to outliers; RMSE penalizes them. RMSE is reported more often because it shares units with the target.
- **R² can be negative** on test data — this is mathematically valid and indicates the model performs worse than predicting the mean.
- **Adjusted R²** is mandatory in multiple linear regression to penalize the addition of irrelevant features.
- **ROC-AUC = 0.5** corresponds to random guessing; **AUC = 1.0** is a perfect classifier. AUC is threshold-independent.
- **Always interpret metrics in the context of the problem** — a 0.85 F1-Score is excellent for medical diagnosis but may be unacceptable for autonomous driving.
- **Loss Function ≠ Metric**: Loss is minimized during training (e.g., Cross-Entropy, MSE); Metric is reported for evaluation (e.g., Accuracy, F1). They are related but distinct.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overfitting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITT501 - MACHINE LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Overfitting # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Overfitting** is a fundamental modeling error in supervised machine learning wherein a hypothesis $h \in \mathcal{H}$ learned from a training set $\mathcal{D}_{train}$ captures not only the underlying signal $f(x)$ but also the stochastic noise $\epsilon$ present in the samples. The result is a model that **minimizes training error** but exhibits **degraded generalization error** on unseen data $\mathcal{D}_{test}$. Mathematically, an overfit model satisfies: $$E_{train}(h) \ll E_{test}(h)$$ where $E_{train}$ and $E_{test}$ represent the empirical risk on the training distribution and the expected risk on the true data distribution respectively.
