# 🔷 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define cross-validation. List and briefly explain any three types of cross-validation techniques used in machine learning.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Cross-validation is a statistical resampling technique used to evaluate the generalization performance of a machine learning model by partitioning the data into complementary subsets, training on some, and validating on the others. The average performance across all iterations provides a robust estimate of how the model will perform on unseen data.

**Three types:**

1. **Hold-Out Method (1 mark):** The dataset is split once into a training set and a held-out test set (e.g., 70/30 split). The model is trained on the training portion and evaluated on the test portion. While simple, it produces a high-variance estimate because the result depends heavily on the random split.

2. **K-Fold Cross-Validation (1 mark):** The dataset is divided into $K$ equally-sized folds. The model is trained $K$ times, each time using $K-1$ folds for training and the remaining fold for validation. The final score is the mean of the $K$ validation errors. This is the most widely used CV technique.

3. **Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation (LOOCV) (1 mark):** A special case of K-Fold where $K = N$ (the number of samples). In each iteration, the model is trained on $N-1$ samples and tested on the single held-out sample. It provides nearly unbiased estimates but has high variance and is computationally expensive for large datasets.

> **Valuation Note:** Full marks require **definition + 3 named techniques + 1-line explanation each**. A bare list without explanations fetches only 1-1.5 marks.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Explain the bias-variance tradeoff in cross-validation. Why is 10-fold CV often preferred over LOOCV in practice?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Bias Aspect (1 mark):** In K-Fold CV, the model is trained on $(K-1)N/K$ samples, which is less than the full $N$ samples. Since model performance improves with more training data, the K-Fold estimate is slightly **pessimistic** (positive bias). As $K$ increases, training data per fold grows, so **bias decreases**. LOOCV has the lowest bias because each fold uses $N-1$ samples.

**Variance Aspect (1 mark):** The $K$ validation errors are not independent because training sets share most of their data. As $K$ increases, the training sets become more similar (highly correlated), and the **variance of the CV estimator increases**. LOOCV has the highest variance due to near-identical training sets.

**Why 10-Fold Wins (1 mark):** 10-Fold CV achieves the best **balance** between bias and variance. LOOCV has near-zero bias but suffers from high variance, often giving a less reliable estimate than 10-Fold. Additionally, 10-Fold requires only 10 model trainings versus $N$ for LOOCV, making it computationally feasible. Empirical studies (Kohavi, 1995) have shown that 10-Fold CV provides the best trade-off for most practical scenarios.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question Choice A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### **Part (a): Explain K-Fold cross-validation in detail with a suitable diagram. Discuss how the value of K affects bias and variance. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition and Procedure (3 marks):**

K-Fold cross-validation is a resampling technique that partitions the dataset $\mathcal{D}$ of size $N$ into $K$ equally-sized (or near-equal) disjoint folds $\mathcal{F}_1, \mathcal{F}_2, \dots, \mathcal{F}_K$. The algorithm proceeds as follows:

**Step 1:** Randomly shuffle the dataset to remove any ordering bias.

**Step 2:** Partition the shuffled data into $K$ folds. For $K=10$ with $N=1000$, each fold contains 100 samples.

**Step 3:** For each iteration $k = 1, 2, \dots, K$:
- Designate fold $\mathcal{F}_k$ as the **validation set**.
- Use the remaining $K-1$ folds as the **training set** (size = $N - N/K$).
- Train the model $\hat{f}^{(k)} = \mathcal{A}(\mathcal{D} \setminus \mathcal{F}_k)$.
- Compute the validation error $E_k = \frac{1}{|\mathcal{F}_k|} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{F}_k} L(y_i, \hat{f}^{(k)}(x_i))$.

**Step 4:** Compute the final CV score as the arithmetic mean:

$$\hat{E}_{CV} = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K} E_k$$

**Effect of K on Bias and Variance (4 marks):**

**Bias Analysis:** The training set in each fold has size $\frac{(K-1)N}{K}$, which is smaller than the full dataset of size $N$. Since most learning algorithms achieve lower error with more training data, the K-Fold estimate is slightly pessimistic. The bias is approximately:

$$\text{Bias}_{K\text{-fold}} \approx -\frac{N}{K} e'(N) \quad \text{where } e'(N) < 0$$

As $K$ increases (e.g., $K=2 \to K=10 \to K=N$), the bias **decreases** because the training set grows closer to size $N$. For $K=10$, the bias is small enough to be acceptable in practice.

**Variance Analysis:** The $K$ validation errors are correlated because the training sets overlap in $\frac{(K-2)N}{K}$ samples. The variance of the CV estimator is:

$$\text{Var}(\hat{E}_{CV}) = \frac{1}{K^2}\sum_{i=1}^{K}\sum_{j=1}^{K} \text{Cov}(E_i, E_j)$$

For large $K$ (like LOOCV), the training sets are nearly identical, making $\text{Cov}(E_i, E_j)$ large and thus the variance high. For small $K$ (like $K=2$), the folds are more independent, giving lower variance.

**The Sweet Spot:** $K=10$ achieves the optimal balance — the bias is sufficiently low (90% training data) while the variance is still manageable (folds are diverse enough). The 5×2 CV paired test (Dietterich, 1998) and Kohavi's 1995 study both confirm that 10-Fold CV offers the best bias-variance trade-off in practice.

**Diagram (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the complete flowchart).**

---

#### **Part (b): What is Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation (LOOCV)? Derive its bias and compare it with K-Fold CV in terms of computational cost. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition and Procedure (2 marks):**

LOOCV is the extreme case of K-Fold CV where $K = N$, the number of samples. The procedure is:

$$\hat{E}_{LOOCV} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} L\left(y_i, \hat{f}^{(-i)}(x_i)\right)$$

where $\hat{f}^{(-i)}$ is the model trained on all samples **except** $(x_i, y_i)$. Each sample gets to be the validation sample exactly once.

**Derivation of Bias (3 marks):**

Let $e(n) = E[\text{Error of model trained on } n \text{ samples}]$. For LOOCV, each training set has $n = N-1$ samples. We compare this to the true expected error $e(N)$ for a model trained on all $N$ samples.

Using a first-order Taylor expansion of $e(n)$ around $n = N$:

$$e(N-1) = e(N) + (-1) \cdot e'(N) + \frac{1}{2}(1)^2 \cdot e''(N) + O\!\left(\frac{1}{N^2}\right)$$

$$e(N-1) - e(N) = -e'(N) + \frac{1}{2}e''(N) + O\!\left(\frac{1}{N^2}\right)$$

Since $e'(N) < 0$ (more data → less error), the term $-e'(N) > 0$, confirming that LOOCV is **slightly pessimistic**. However, for large $N$, the correction is $O(1/N)$, which is **negligible** compared to the K-Fold bias of $O(1/K)$.

**Comparison with K-Fold (2 marks):**

| Metric | LOOCV ($K=N$) | K-Fold (e.g., $K=10$) |
|--------|---------------|------------------------|
| Bias | $\approx 0$ (near zero) | $\approx \frac{1}{K}$ small positive bias |
| Variance | **High** (correlated folds) | Moderate (more independent folds) |
| Computational Cost | $N$ model trainings | $K$ model trainings (e.g., 10) |
| Best For | Small datasets, expensive-to-evaluate models | General-purpose, large datasets |

LOOCV requires training the model $N$ times, which is **prohibitive for large $N$** (e.g., $N = 10^6$ samples is infeasible). K-Fold with $K=10$ requires only 10 trainings, making it 10× to 100,000× faster depending on $N$.

---

### **Question Choice B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **Part (a): Explain the Hold-Out method and Stratified K-Fold Cross-Validation. When would you prefer one over the other? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Hold-Out Method (3 marks):**

The Hold-Out method is the simplest form of model evaluation. The dataset is split **once** into two non-overlapping subsets:

$$\mathcal{D} = \mathcal{D}_{\text{train}} \cup \mathcal{D}_{\text{test}}, \quad \mathcal{D}_{\text{train}} \cap \mathcal{D}_{\text{test}} = \emptyset$$

**Procedure:**
1. Shuffle the dataset randomly.
2. Allocate a fraction (commonly 70%, 80%, or 90%) to $\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}$ and the remainder to $\mathcal{D}_{\text{test}}$.
3. Train the model on $\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}$.
4. Evaluate on $\mathcal{D}_{\text{test}}$ and report the single test error.

**Advantages:** Extremely simple, fast (only 1 model training), requires minimal computation.

**Disadvantages:** High variance (the result depends on the random split); wastes data (test set is not used for training); unreliable for small datasets.

**Stratified K-Fold Cross-Validation (3 marks):**

Stratified K-Fold is a variant of K-Fold CV that preserves the class distribution in each fold. Given an imbalanced dataset with class proportions $p_1, p_2, \dots, p_C$, Stratified K-Fold ensures that every fold has approximately the same proportions:

$$p_c^{(k)} \approx p_c \quad \forall c \in \{1, \dots, C\}, \; \forall k \in \{1, \dots, K\}$$

**Procedure:**
1. For each class $c$, randomly sample $\lfloor N_c / K \rfloor$ or $\lceil N_c / K \rceil$ samples into each fold.
2. Concatenate the class-specific samples to form the $K$ folds.
3. Run standard K-Fold CV on these stratified folds.

**When to Prefer Each (1 mark):**

- **Prefer Hold-Out** when: $N$ is very large ($N > 10^6$), the model is computationally expensive to train (e.g., deep neural networks), and a quick estimate suffices.
- **Prefer Stratified K-Fold** when: dealing with imbalanced classification (e.g., fraud detection, medical diagnosis), $N$ is moderate ($10^3 < N < 10^5$), and a reliable, low-variance estimate is required.

> **Key Insight:** For balanced datasets, Stratified K-Fold behaves identically to standard K-Fold. For imbalanced datasets, it is **strictly superior** and should be the default.

---

#### **Part (b): Describe Nested Cross-Validation. Why is it necessary for unbiased hyperparameter tuning? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):**

Nested Cross-Validation is a two-layer CV procedure designed to provide an **unbiased estimate of generalization error** when hyperparameter tuning is involved. It uses two CV loops:

- **Outer Loop ($K_1$ folds, typically $K_1=5$):** Estimates the generalization error of the **entire model selection process**.
- **Inner Loop ($K_2$ folds, typically $K_2=5$):** Performs hyperparameter tuning using only the outer training fold.

**Procedure:**

For each outer fold $i = 1, \dots, K_1$:

1. Split $\mathcal{D}$ into $\mathcal{D}_{\text{outer-train}}^{(i)}$ and $\mathcal{D}_{\text{outer-test}}^{(i)}$.

2. **Inner loop** (on $\mathcal{D}_{\text{outer-train}}^{(i)}$ only):
   - For each hyperparameter configuration $h \in \mathcal{H}$:
     - Run $K_2$-fold CV on $\mathcal{D}_{\text{outer-train}}^{(i)}$.
     - Compute the inner CV score $S_h^{(i)}$.
   - Select the best hyperparameter: $h^* = \arg\max_h S_h^{(i)}$.

3. **Outer evaluation:**
   - Retrain the model on $\mathcal{D}_{\text{outer-train}}^{(i)}$ using $h^*$.
   - Evaluate on $\mathcal{D}_{\text{outer-test}}^{(i)}$, record score $T_i$.

4. Final estimate: $\hat{E}_{\text{nested}} = \frac{1}{K_1} \sum_{i=1}^{K_1} T_i$.

**Why It Is Necessary (5 marks):**

**The Problem with Naive Tuning:** If we use a single CV loop for both tuning and evaluation, the test folds are repeatedly used to **select** hyperparameters. This causes **information leakage** from the validation set into the model selection process, leading to **optimistic bias** — the reported CV error is lower than the true generalization error.

**The Nested Solution:**

- The **outer test fold** is **never** seen during hyperparameter selection (it only appears in the outer evaluation step). This guarantees that the final estimate is **unbiased**.
- The **inner CV** is used purely for hyperparameter ranking — it can be optimistically biased without affecting the outer estimate.
- The result is a **realistic** estimate of how the model will perform on truly unseen data.

**Concrete Example:** Suppose you have 5 candidate values for regularization strength $C$ and run 10-fold CV. Without nesting, the reported 10-fold CV score is biased because the same 10 folds were used to **both** select $C$ and evaluate the model. With nested CV (5-fold outer, 5-fold inner), the outer folds are **disjoint** from the inner CV, eliminating the leakage.

**Cost:** Nested CV requires $K_1 \times K_2 \times \vert \mathcal{H} \vert$ model trainings, which is computationally expensive. For $K_1=K_2=5$ and $\vert \mathcal{H} \vert = 10$ hyperparameters, this is **250 trainings**.

**Reference Diagram:** See Section 4.2 of these notes for the complete block diagram of Nested CV.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 CRITICAL MARKS-LOSS ZONES — READ BEFORE EXAM:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to Shuffle:** In standard K-Fold CV, you MUST shuffle the data before partitioning. Failing to do so results in folds that are non-representative (e.g., all class 0 in fold 1, all class 1 in fold 2). **Marks lost:** 1-2 in procedure description.
>
> 2. **Confusing "Training Set" and "Validation Set":** In CV, the held-out fold is the **validation set** (used for evaluation), NOT the test set. The true test set is **never used during CV**. Examiners specifically check for this terminology.
>
> 3. **Ignoring Stratification:** When the question involves imbalanced data (e.g., "rare disease detection"), students often describe standard K-Fold and **forget Stratified K-Fold**. Examiners deduct 1-2 marks for this omission.
>
> 4. **Time Series Data Leakage:** Describing K-Fold for time series data without mentioning the **temporal ordering constraint** is a serious error. Always mention "no shuffling" and "forward chaining" for temporal data.
>
> 5. **Nested CV Confusion:** Some students describe "Nested CV" as simply running K-Fold twice on the same data. This is **incorrect** — the outer test fold must be **completely held out** from the inner tuning loop.
>
> 6. **LOOCV vs. K-Fold Tradeoff:** A common mistake is claiming "LOOCV is always better because it has lower bias." This is **partially wrong** — LOOCV's high variance often makes it *worse* than 10-fold in practice. The KTU key requires you to mention **both** bias AND variance.
>
> 7. **Computational Cost Misjudgment:** Don't claim "LOOCV is fast for large $N$" — it is the **slowest** method due to $N$ model trainings.

---

## 5.4 🎯 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LAST-MINUTE REVISION BULLETS — MASTER THESE BEFORE THE EXAM:**

### 📌 Core Concepts (Must Know)

- ✦ **Cross-validation** = resampling technique for estimating generalization error.
- ✦ **K-Fold CV** divides data into $K$ folds; trains $K$ times; reports mean of $K$ validation errors.
- ✦ **LOOCV** is K-Fold with $K = N$; near-zero bias but high variance and high computational cost.
- ✦ **Hold-Out** is a single train/test split; fast but high-variance.
- ✦ **Stratified K-Fold** preserves class proportions in every fold — essential for imbalanced data.
- ✦ **Time Series CV** (Forward Chaining) **must not shuffle**; training data is always from the past.
- ✦ **Nested CV** = outer loop for assessment, inner loop for hyperparameter tuning → unbiased estimate.

### 📌 Key Formulas (Must Memorize)

- ✦ $\hat{E}_{CV} = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K} E_k$ — K-Fold CV error estimator.
- ✦ $\hat{E}_{LOOCV} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} L(y_i, \hat{f}^{(-i)}(x_i))$ — LOOCV error.
- ✦ $\text{Bias}_{K\text{-fold}} \approx -\frac{N}{K} e'(N)$ — bias decreases as $K$ increases.
- ✦ $\text{Var}(\hat{E}_{CV}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{K} + \frac{K-1}{K}\rho$ — variance has two components.
- ✦ $p_c^{(k)} \approx p_c$ — stratification constraint for class $c$ in fold $k$.

### 📌 Bias-Variance Tradeoff (Most-Asked Topic)

- ✦ **Small $K$** (e.g., $K=2$): High bias, low variance, fast.
- ✦ **Large $K$** (e.g., $K=N$ LOOCV): Low bias, high variance, slow.
- ✦ **$K=10$** is the **empirical sweet spot** for most problems.
- ✦ LOOCV is **not always better** than 10-Fold due to variance.

### 📌 When to Use What (Decision Matrix)

- ✦ Large $N$ + expensive model → **Hold-Out**.
- ✦ Moderate $N$ + general purpose → **10-Fold CV**.
- ✦ Small $N$ + small model → **LOOCV**.
- ✦ Imbalanced classification → **Stratified K-Fold**.
- ✦ Time series → **Forward Chaining / Expanding Window**.
- ✦ Hyperparameter tuning → **Nested CV**.

### 📌 Common Exam Traps

- ✦ **Never** use a single 10-fold CV for both tuning and evaluation (causes optimistic bias).
- ✦ **Never** shuffle time-series data.
- ✦ **Always** mention shuffling before standard K-Fold partitioning.
- ✦ **Always** distinguish validation set (inside CV) from test set (held out before CV).
- ✦ **Always** discuss BOTH bias and variance when comparing CV methods.

### 📌 Numerical Heuristics

- ✦ $K = 5$ or $K = 10$ are industry defaults.
- ✦ For $K=10$, each fold has $N/10$ samples; training set has $0.9N$ samples.
- ✦ LOOCV bias $\approx O(1/N)$; K-Fold bias $\approx O(1/K)$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bootstrapping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT501 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bootstrapping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Bootstrapping** is a non-parametric, computer-intensive **resampling statistical technique** introduced by **Bradley Efron (1979)**. It involves repeatedly drawing samples **with replacement** from an observed dataset to estimate the sampling distribution of a statistic (such as the mean, median, variance, regression coefficient, or a complex model error metric) without relying on parametric assumptions or asymptotic approximations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCITT501 - Module 2):** Bootstrapping is treated as a foundational **ensemble learning** building block. The KTU 2024 Scheme specifically tests its application in **Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating)** and **Random Forests**, both of which directly use bootstrap samples to construct multiple weak learners that are later aggregated.

### Mathematical Statement

Given an original dataset:

$$
D = \{x_1, x_2, x_3, \ldots, x_n\}
$$

A **bootstrap sample** $D^{*b}$ is created by drawing $n$ observations **uniformly at random with replacement** from $D$. This process is repeated $B$ times to produce $B$ independent bootstrap samples:

$$
D^{*1}, D^{*2}, D^{*3}, \ldots, D^{*B}
$$

Each bootstrap sample $D^{*b}$ has the **same size $n$** as the original dataset but contains **repeated observations** (since sampling is *with* replacement). The statistic of interest $\hat{\theta}^{*b}$ is computed on each sample, and the empirical distribution of $\{\hat{\theta}^{*1}, \hat{\theta}^{*2}, \ldots, \hat{\theta}^{*B}\}$ is used to approximate the true sampling distribution of $\hat{\theta}$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box: "The Lottery Ticket Analogy"**
> Imagine a **glass jar containing 1000 lottery tickets**, each representing one data point in your dataset. Now, you want to know the *average prize value* of tickets, but you are not allowed to look at the jar multiple times. The **classical statistics** approach assumes the prizes follow a normal distribution and uses formulas. The **bootstrap approach** is different:
>
> - You **shake the jar** (randomize), pick a ticket, **note its value**, and **put it back** into the jar.
> - You repeat this **1000 times** to form one "pseudo-dataset" of size 1000. This is **one bootstrap sample**.
> - You **repeat the entire process 1000 more times**, each time starting fresh.
> - Now you have **1000 independent estimates** of the average prize. Their spread tells you how *uncertain* your original estimate is.
>
> This is exactly what bootstrapping does — it **simulates the act of drawing new samples** from the population, by using *the observed sample itself* as a proxy for the population.

---

## 1.3 Why Bootstrapping is Important in Machine Learning

| Property | Classical Parametric Statistics | Bootstrapping |
|---|---|---|
| **Distribution Assumption** | Assumes Normal/Gaussian | No assumption (non-parametric) |
| **Sample Size Requirement** | Requires large $n$ for CLT | Works for **small $n$** |
| **Estimator** | Closed-form (e.g., $z$-test) | Computed empirically from samples |
| **ML Applicability** | Limited | **Critical** (Bagging, Random Forests) |
| **Computation** | Cheap | Expensive (requires $B$ iterations) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **expected unique sample size** in one bootstrap draw of size $n$ is approximately $0.632 \times n$ — meaning **63.2% of the original observations** appear at least once, while the remaining **36.8% are duplicates**. This fraction is called the **Out-Of-Bag (OOB) fraction** and is the mathematical foundation of the **OOB error** used in Random Forests.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Decomposition — The Bootstrap Procedure

The complete bootstrapping procedure can be broken down into **five logically distinct steps**. The KTU examiner frequently awards marks for correctly stating the procedural sequence.

- **Step 1 — Observation Collection:** Start with an original dataset $D = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ drawn i.i.d. (independent and identically distributed) from an unknown population distribution $F$.

- **Step 2 — Resample With Replacement:** For iteration $b = 1, 2, \ldots, B$, draw a new sample $D^{*b}$ of size $n$ from $D$ **with replacement**. Each observation in $D$ has probability $\frac{1}{n}$ of being selected on any single draw.

- **Step 3 — Statistic Computation:** Compute the statistic of interest $\hat{\theta}^{*b}$ on the bootstrap sample. Common choices include the mean, median, standard deviation, regression coefficient, or model accuracy.

- **Step 4 — Repeat for B Iterations:** Continue steps 2–3 for a sufficiently large $B$ (typically **$B \geq 1000$** for stable estimates).

- **Step 5 — Aggregate Results:** Use the collection $\{\hat{\theta}^{*1}, \hat{\theta}^{*2}, \ldots, \hat{\theta}^{*B}\}$ to construct:
  - **Bootstrap Estimate:** $\hat{\theta}_{boot} = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{\theta}^{*b}$
  - **Bootstrap Standard Error:** $\widehat{SE}_{boot} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{B-1} \sum_{b=1}^{B} (\hat{\theta}^{*b} - \hat{\theta}_{boot})^2}$
  - **Bootstrap Confidence Intervals** (using percentile or BCa method).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Why" and "How":** The *why* behind bootstrapping is that the true sampling distribution of $\hat{\theta}$ under $F$ is analytically intractable for most ML statistics. The *how* is to **empirically approximate** this unknown distribution by treating the empirical distribution $\hat{F}$ (i.e., the dataset itself) as a faithful proxy for $F$.

---

## 2.2 Types of Bootstrap Variants

| Variant | Sampling Mechanism | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Non-Parametric Bootstrap** | Resample rows with replacement | Default; no model assumption |
| **Parametric Bootstrap** | Generate from fitted parametric $F$ | When data is known to be normal/exponential |
| **Block Bootstrap** | Resample blocks of consecutive obs. | Time-series data (preserves autocorrelation) |
| **Leave-One-Out Bootstrap** | Resample $n$ from $n-1$ | Bias correction |
| **Wild Bootstrap** | Resample residuals with sign flips | Heteroscedastic regression |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following table for KTU 2024 ESE.** All confidence-interval formulas use the percentile method.

| Quantity | Formula | Notation & Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Bootstrap Sample** | $D^{*b} = \{x^{*b}_1, x^{*b}_2, \ldots, x^{*b}_n\}$ | Drawn from $D$ with replacement |
| **Bootstrap Statistic** | $\hat{\theta}^{*b} = s(D^{*b})$ | $s(\cdot)$ is the estimator function |
| **Bootstrap Mean** | $\hat{\theta}_{boot} = \dfrac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{\theta}^{*b}$ | $B$ = number of bootstrap iterations |
| **Bootstrap SE** | $\widehat{SE}_{boot} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{B-1} \sum_{b=1}^{B} (\hat{\theta}^{*b} - \hat{\theta}_{boot})^2}$ | Standard error of statistic |
| **Bootstrap Bias** | $\widehat{Bias}_{boot} = \hat{\theta}_{boot} - \hat{\theta}_{obs}$ | $\hat{\theta}_{obs}$ is the statistic on original $D$ |
| **OOB Fraction** | $P(\text{not selected}) = \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{n}\right)^n \approx e^{-1} \approx 0.368$ | Probability a sample is in OOB set |
| **OOB Unique Fraction** | $1 - \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{n}\right)^n \approx 0.632$ | Fraction of unique training points |
| **Percentile CI** | $\left[\hat{\theta}^{*}_{\alpha/2},\; \hat{\theta}^{*}_{1-\alpha/2}\right]$ | $100(1-\alpha)\%$ confidence interval |
| **Bagging Prediction** | $\hat{f}_{bag}(x) = \dfrac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{f}^{*b}(x)$ | For regression |
| **Bagging Classification** | $\hat{f}_{bag}(x) = \text{mode}\{\hat{f}^{*1}(x), \ldots, \hat{f}^{*B}(x)\}$ | Majority vote |

> **Critical Note on Pipes:** All absolute-value bars and probability brackets in the table use $\vert$ or $\mid$ — never the raw $\vert$ character — to prevent Markdown table corruption.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Diagnosis:** Estimating the **95% confidence interval** of tumor classification accuracy when only 50 patient samples are available.
- **Fraud Detection in Banking:** Constructing **Random Forest** models where each tree is trained on a bootstrap sample; the OOB samples provide a free validation set.
- **Quality Control in Manufacturing:** Estimating the variability of a production line's mean defect rate when destructive testing limits sample sizes.
- **NLP & Sentiment Analysis:** Computing confidence intervals for F1-scores in low-resource language settings where classical asymptotic theory fails.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The Out-Of-Bag (OOB) Fraction

This is the **most-asked derivation** in KTU 2024 ESE for this topic. We will derive the exact probability that an observation is *not* selected in a single bootstrap sample of size $n$.

**Given:** Original dataset $D = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$, sample size $n$.

**To find:** $P(x_i \notin D^{*b})$, the probability that observation $x_i$ is **not present** in a single bootstrap draw.

### Derivation Walkthrough

**Step 1 — Setup the Single-Draw Probability.**
In one draw from $D$, the probability of picking any specific observation $x_i$ is:

$$
P(\text{select } x_i) = \frac{1}{n}
$$

The probability of *not* selecting $x_i$ in a single draw is the complement:

$$
P(\text{not select } x_i) = 1 - \frac{1}{n}
$$

*Logical conversion:* This is elementary probability — there are $n$ equally-likely outcomes, only one of which is $x_i$.

**Step 2 — Extend to $n$ Independent Draws.**
A bootstrap sample consists of $n$ such draws (with replacement, so each draw is independent). The probability that $x_i$ is *never* selected across all $n$ draws is:

$$
P(x_i \notin D^{*b}) = \left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^n
$$

*Logical conversion:* We multiply the per-draw non-selection probabilities across $n$ independent trials.

**Step 3 — Apply the Fundamental Exponential Limit.**
Using the standard limit definition $e^{-x} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \left(1 - \frac{x}{n}\right)^n$, we substitute $x = 1$:

$$
\left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^n \xrightarrow{n \to \infty} e^{-1} \approx 0.3679
$$

*Logical conversion:* This is a direct application of the canonical limit used in probability theory to evaluate compounding-draw probabilities.

**Step 4 — Convergent Numerical Form for ML Practice.**
For moderately large $n$ (say $n \geq 30$), the value is essentially **stable at 0.368**:

$$
P(x_i \notin D^{*b}) \approx 0.368
$$

The complementary probability (that $x_i$ *is* selected at least once) is:

$$
P(x_i \in D^{*b}) = 1 - 0.368 = 0.632
$$

*Logical conversion:* These are the canonical **OOB fraction (36.8%)** and **in-bag fraction (63.2%)** universally cited in Random Forest literature.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Bootstrap Mean & Standard Error

**Problem (KTU 2024 Style):** Given a dataset $D = \{4, 5, 9, 12, 15\}$ (n=5), perform $B = 3$ bootstrap iterations, compute the bootstrap mean and bootstrap standard error.

### Step 1 — Compute the Original Sample Mean

$$
\hat{\theta}_{obs} = \frac{4 + 5 + 9 + 12 + 15}{5} = \frac{45}{5} = 9.0
$$

*Conversion:* The observed mean is our reference statistic on the original dataset.

### Step 2 — Generate Three Bootstrap Samples (With Replacement)

Each bootstrap sample is constructed by randomly drawing 5 elements (with replacement) from $D$.

$$
D^{*1} = \{4, 4, 9, 15, 12\} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \hat{\theta}^{*1} = \frac{4 + 4 + 9 + 15 + 12}{5} = \frac{44}{5} = 8.8
$$

$$
D^{*2} = \{5, 9, 5, 9, 12\} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \hat{\theta}^{*2} = \frac{5 + 9 + 5 + 9 + 12}{5} = \frac{40}{5} = 8.0
$$

$$
D^{*3} = \{15, 12, 4, 9, 4\} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \hat{\theta}^{*3} = \frac{15 + 12 + 4 + 9 + 4}{5} = \frac{44}{5} = 8.8
$$

*Conversion:* Notice that values like $4$, $5$, and $9$ appear multiple times within the same sample — this is the *defining* property of bootstrapping with replacement.

### Step 3 — Compute the Bootstrap Mean

$$
\hat{\theta}_{boot} = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{\theta}^{*b} = \frac{8.8 + 8.0 + 8.8}{3} = \frac{25.6}{3} \approx 8.533
$$

*Conversion:* The bootstrap mean is the average across all bootstrap iterations. As $B \to \infty$, $\hat{\theta}_{boot} \to \hat{\theta}_{obs}$ (consistency property).

### Step 4 — Compute the Bootstrap Standard Error

First, compute the squared deviations from the bootstrap mean:

$$
(\hat{\theta}^{*1} - \hat{\theta}_{boot})^2 = (8.8 - 8.533)^2 = (0.267)^2 \approx 0.0713
$$

$$
(\hat{\theta}^{*2} - \hat{\theta}_{boot})^2 = (8.0 - 8.533)^2 = (-0.533)^2 \approx 0.2844
$$

$$
(\hat{\theta}^{*3} - \hat{\theta}_{boot})^2 = (8.8 - 8.533)^2 = (0.267)^2 \approx 0.0713
$$

Then, sum and divide by $B - 1 = 2$:

$$
\widehat{SE}_{boot} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{B-1} \sum_{b=1}^{B} (\hat{\theta}^{*b} - \hat{\theta}_{boot})^2} = \sqrt{\frac{0.0713 + 0.2844 + 0.0713}{2}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.4270}{2}} \approx 0.462
$$

*Conversion:* The bootstrap standard error is the **empirical analogue** of the classical standard error, but it does not require any distributional assumption.

### Final Answer Summary

$$
\boxed{\hat{\theta}_{boot} \approx 8.533, \qquad \widehat{SE}_{boot} \approx 0.462}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Complete Bootstrap Procedure

The following diagram traces the end-to-end workflow of a bootstrap procedure, from dataset input to confidence-interval output. It is the diagram students are expected to reproduce in 14-mark ESE questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([START]) --> dataIn[Original Dataset D of size n]
    dataIn --> initCount[Initialize counter b = 1]
    initCount --> checkB{b less than B?}
    checkB -- YES --> resample[Draw n samples from D WITH REPLACEMENT]
    resample --> bootSample[Form Bootstrap Sample D star b]
    bootSample --> statCompute[Compute statistic theta star b on D star b]
    statCompute --> store[Store theta star b in array]
    store --> increment[Increment b by 1]
    increment --> checkB
    checkB -- NO --> aggregate[Aggregate all B theta star values]
    aggregate --> compMean[Compute Bootstrap Mean theta boot]
    aggregate --> compSE[Compute Bootstrap SE]
    aggregate --> compCI[Compute Percentile Confidence Interval]
    compMean --> outputNode
    compSE --> outputNode
    compCI --> outputNode[FINAL OUTPUT: Distribution of theta]
    outputNode --> endNode([END])

    style startNode fill:#4CAF50,color:#FFFFFF
    style endNode fill:#F44336,color:#FFFFFF
    style resample fill:#FFC107,color:#000000
    style bootSample fill:#FFC107,color:#000000
    style statCompute fill:#2196F3,color:#FFFFFF
    style outputNode fill:#9C27B0,color:#FFFFFF
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Role of Bootstrapping in Bagging / Random Forests

This architecture diagram illustrates how bootstrap sampling feeds directly into the **Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating)** algorithm and **Random Forest** ensembles, the two most-tested KTU applications of bootstrapping.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawData[Original Training Set D] -->|Draw with replacement| boot1[Bootstrap Sample 1]
    rawData -->|Draw with replacement| boot2[Bootstrap Sample 2]
    rawData -->|Draw with replacement| boot3[Bootstrap Sample 3]
    rawData -->|Draw with replacement| bootN[Bootstrap Sample B]

    boot1 --> train1[Train Model M1]
    boot2 --> train2[Train Model M2]
    boot3 --> train3[Train Model M3]
    bootN --> trainN[Train Model MB]

    train1 --> pred1[Prediction 1]
    train2 --> pred2[Prediction 2]
    train3 --> pred3[Prediction 3]
    trainN --> predN[Prediction B]

    pred1 --> agg[Aggregator: Mean for Regression / Majority Vote for Classification]
    pred2 --> agg
    pred3 --> agg
    predN --> agg

    agg --> finalPred[Final Ensemble Prediction]

    boot1 -.OOB Set 1.-> oobEval[OOB Error Estimation]
    boot2 -.OOB Set 2.-> oobEval
    boot3 -.OOB Set 3.-> oobEval
    bootN -.OOB Set B.-> oobEval

    style rawData fill:#03A9F4,color:#FFFFFF
    style finalPred fill:#4CAF50,color:#FFFFFF
    style oobEval fill:#FF9800,color:#000000
    style agg fill:#9C27B0,color:#FFFFFF
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Bootstrap Variants & Their Use Cases

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Bootstrapping] --> nonParam[Non Parametric Bootstrap]
    root --> param[Parametric Bootstrap]
    root --> block[Block Bootstrap]
    root --> loo[Leave One Out Bootstrap]
    root --> wild[Wild Bootstrap]

    nonParam --> nonParamUse[Default ML usage Bagging Random Forest]
    param --> paramUse[When data is known Normal Exponential]
    block --> blockUse[Time Series Forecasting]
    loo --> looUse[Bias Correction of Estimators]
    wild --> wildUse[Heteroscedastic Regression Models]

    style root fill:#3F51B5,color:#FFFFFF
    style nonParam fill:#4CAF50,color:#FFFFFF
    style param fill:#4CAF50,color:#FFFFFF
    style block fill:#4CAF50,color:#FFFFFF
    style loo fill:#4CAF50,color:#FFFFFF
    style wild fill:#4CAF50,color:#FFFFFF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Reading Tip:** In the second diagram, the **dashed arrows** leading to the *OOB Error Estimation* box represent the discarded ~36.8% of observations. KTU examiners award **partial marks (1–2 out of 7)** for correctly identifying and explaining this OOB pathway in a 14-mark essay question on Random Forests.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

### **Q1. Define Bootstrapping. Why is it called a "non-parametric" resampling method?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Bootstrapping, introduced by **Bradley Efron in 1979**, is a resampling technique used to estimate the sampling distribution of a statistic by drawing samples **with replacement** from an observed dataset.

It is termed **non-parametric** because it does **not assume any underlying parametric distribution** (such as Gaussian, Exponential, etc.) for the data. Instead, it treats the empirical distribution of the observed sample as a faithful proxy for the true population distribution $F$, making the method distribution-free.

> **Mark Distribution:** *Definition (1 mark) + Efron citation (0.5 mark) + Non-parametric justification (1.5 marks).*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

### **Q2. In a bootstrap sample of size $n$, approximately what fraction of the original observations are unique? Justify.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Approximately **63.2%** of the original observations appear **at least once** in a bootstrap sample, while the remaining **36.8%** are left out (forming the Out-Of-Bag set).

**Justification:** The probability that a given observation $x_i$ is *not* selected in a single draw is $\left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)$. Across $n$ independent draws, this becomes $\left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^n$, which converges to $e^{-1} \approx 0.368$ as $n \to \infty$. The complement $1 - 0.368 = 0.632$ represents the in-bag (unique) fraction.

> **Mark Distribution:** *Numerical value (1 mark) + OOB complement (1 mark) + Limit derivation (1 mark).*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper 2]**

#### **(a) Explain the Bootstrap procedure in detail with a suitable numerical example. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the Original Dataset.** Let the original dataset be $D = \{2, 4, 6, 8, 10\}$ of size $n = 5$. The observed mean is:

$$
\hat{\theta}_{obs} = \frac{2 + 4 + 6 + 8 + 10}{5} = 6.0
$$

**Step 2 — Perform $B = 4$ Bootstrap Resamples.** Drawing 5 elements with replacement from $D$:

$$
D^{*1} = \{2, 2, 6, 10, 8\} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \hat{\theta}^{*1} = 5.6
$$

$$
D^{*2} = \{4, 6, 4, 8, 10\} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \hat{\theta}^{*2} = 6.4
$$

$$
D^{*3} = \{6, 6, 8, 2, 4\} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \hat{\theta}^{*3} = 5.2
$$

$$
D^{*4} = \{10, 8, 8, 6, 4\} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \hat{\theta}^{*4} = 7.2
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Bootstrap Mean:**

$$
\hat{\theta}_{boot} = \frac{5.6 + 6.4 + 5.2 + 7.2}{4} = \frac{24.4}{4} = 6.1
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Bootstrap Bias:**

$$
\widehat{Bias}_{boot} = \hat{\theta}_{boot} - \hat{\theta}_{obs} = 6.1 - 6.0 = 0.1
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Bootstrap Standard Error:**

$$
\widehat{SE}_{boot} = \sqrt{\frac{(5.6-6.1)^2 + (6.4-6.1)^2 + (5.2-6.1)^2 + (7.2-6.1)^2}{4-1}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.25 + 0.09 + 0.81 + 1.21}{3}} = \sqrt{0.787} \approx 0.887
$$

**Step 6 — Construct 95% Percentile CI:** Sort bootstrap means: $\{5.2, 5.6, 6.4, 7.2\}$. The 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles (for $B=4$, take 1st and 4th values) give:

$$
CI_{95\%} = [5.2, \; 7.2]
$$

#### **(b) Discuss the role of bootstrapping in Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating). How does it reduce variance in ensemble learning? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Bagging Algorithm:** Bagging, proposed by **Leo Breiman (1996)**, is an ensemble technique that:

1. Draws $B$ bootstrap samples $D^{*1}, D^{*2}, \ldots, D^{*B}$ from the original training set.
2. Trains a separate base model $\hat{f}^{*b}$ on each bootstrap sample.
3. Aggregates predictions via **averaging** (regression) or **majority vote** (classification).

**Role of Bootstrapping in Bagging:**

- **Diversity Generation:** By training each base learner on a *different* bootstrap sample, the models become **decorrelated**. Each model sees a slightly different distribution, which prevents all models from making the *same* errors.
- **Variance Reduction:** If the base learner is a **high-variance, low-bias** model (e.g., unpruned decision trees), averaging across $B$ models reduces variance by approximately a factor of $1/B$ (assuming uncorrelated errors):

$$
Var(\hat{f}_{bag}) \approx \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} Var(\hat{f}^{*b})
$$

- **Out-Of-Bag (OOB) Validation:** The ~36.8% of observations left out of each bootstrap sample serve as a **free validation set** for that specific model, enabling unbiased error estimation without a separate hold-out set.
- **Stability for Noisy Data:** Bootstrapping ensures that outliers in the original data are randomly included/excluded across the $B$ samples, reducing their undue influence on the final ensemble.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often write that bootstrapping *"creates new data."* This is **wrong** — bootstrapping **resamples** existing data; it does not generate new data points. (−1 mark)
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting that the **OOB fraction is 36.8%, not 50%**. The 50% rule applies to *sub-sampling* (without replacement), not to bootstrapping. (−1 mark)
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Stating that Bagging reduces **bias**. It primarily reduces **variance**; bias remains approximately unchanged. (−1 mark)
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing the **percentile method** of CI construction with the **BCa (Bias-Corrected and Accelerated)** method. For KTU, the percentile method is sufficient and expected.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Supplementary]**

#### **(a) Derive the Out-Of-Bag (OOB) fraction mathematically. Show that approximately 36.8% of original observations are excluded from a bootstrap sample. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** Original dataset $D$ with $n$ observations.

**Step 1 — Single Draw Probability.** The probability of selecting a particular observation $x_i$ in one random draw from $D$ is $\frac{1}{n}$. The probability of *not* selecting $x_i$ is:

$$
P(\text{not select } x_i) = 1 - \frac{1}{n} = \frac{n-1}{n}
$$

**Step 2 — Extend to $n$ Independent Draws.** A bootstrap sample consists of $n$ such draws (with replacement ensures independence). The probability that $x_i$ is never chosen across all $n$ draws is:

$$
P(x_i \notin D^{*b}) = \left(\frac{n-1}{n}\right)^n
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Exponential Limit.** Using $\lim_{n \to \infty} \left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^n = e^{-1}$:

$$
P(x_i \notin D^{*b}) \to e^{-1} \approx 0.3679
$$

**Step 4 — Conclude the OOB Fraction:**

$$
\boxed{P(\text{OOB}) \approx 0.368 \quad \text{or} \quad 36.8\%}
$$

#### **(b) Explain how the OOB error estimate is computed in a Random Forest. Why is it considered a "free" validation mechanism? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Random Forest OOB Procedure:**

1. For each bootstrap sample $D^{*b}$, train a decision tree $T_b$ on the in-bag (63.2%) observations.
2. The **OOB samples** for $T_b$ (the ~36.8% not used in training $D^{*b}$) are passed through $T_b$ to generate predictions.
3. For each original observation $x_i$, collect the predictions from **only those trees** for which $x_i$ was in the OOB set.
4. Compare these aggregated OOB predictions with the true labels $y_i$ to compute the **OOB error** (e.g., misclassification rate or MSE).

**Mathematical Formulation:**

$$
Err_{OOB} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}\left(y_i \neq \hat{y}_i^{OOB}\right)
$$

where $\hat{y}_i^{OOB}$ is the majority-vote prediction across all trees $T_b$ where $x_i$ was OOB.

**Why it is "Free Validation":**

- **No data split required:** In classical ML, one must partition data into train/validation sets, losing ~20–30% of training data. OOB eliminates this.
- **Unbiased estimate:** Breiman (1996) proved that OOB error is an **unbiased estimator** of the generalization error of the Random Forest.
- **Computational efficiency:** It reuses the existing $B$ trees; no additional model training is required.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 ESE, Module 2, Bootstrapping**

- ✅ **Definition:** Resampling **with replacement** to estimate the sampling distribution of a statistic (Efron, 1979).
- ✅ **Key Parameter:** $B$ = number of bootstrap iterations; recommended $B \geq 1000$ for stable estimates.
- ✅ **Sample Size:** Each bootstrap sample has the **same size $n$** as the original dataset.
- ✅ **OOB Fraction:** $\left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^n \to e^{-1} \approx 0.368$ (**36.8%** excluded).
- ✅ **In-Bag Fraction:** $1 - e^{-1} \approx 0.632$ (**63.2%** unique training points).
- ✅ **Bootstrap Mean:** $\hat{\theta}_{boot} = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{\theta}^{*b}$.
- ✅ **Bootstrap SE:** $\widehat{SE}_{boot} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{B-1} \sum_{b=1}^{B} (\hat{\theta}^{*b} - \hat{\theta}_{boot})^2}$.
- ✅ **Bootstrap Bias:** $\widehat{Bias}_{boot} = \hat{\theta}_{boot} - \hat{\theta}_{obs}$.
- ✅ **Bagging (Breiman, 1996):** Trains $B$ models on $B$ bootstrap samples; aggregates via **mean (regression)** or **majority vote (classification)**.
- ✅ **Random Forest:** Bagging + random feature subset selection at each split; uses OOB error as free validation.
- ✅ **Variance Reduction:** Bagging primarily reduces **variance** (not bias); works best with **high-variance, low-bias** base learners (e.g., deep decision trees).
- ✅ **Non-Parametric:** No distributional assumption required — the empirical distribution of $D$ is the working model.
- ✅ **Block Bootstrap:** Used for **time-series data** to preserve temporal autocorrelation.
- ✅ **Parametric Bootstrap:** Assumes a known distribution; generates synthetic data from fitted parameters.
- ✅ **Percentile CI:** $[ \hat{\theta}^{*}_{\alpha/2}, \; \hat{\theta}^{*}_{1-\alpha/2} ]$ — the **expected KTU answer** for confidence interval construction.

> **Final Examiner Tip:** In any 14-mark question, always (i) state the algorithm in steps, (ii) provide one numerical example with at least $B=3$ iterations, and (iii) connect the concept to a real ML application (Bagging / Random Forest). This 3-part structure is the KTU valuation gold standard and typically secures **12+ out of 14 marks**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Model Selection Procedures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT501 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Model Selection Procedures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model Selection** is the formal statistical and machine learning procedure of identifying, from a candidate set of competing models $M = \{M_1, M_2, \ldots, M_K\}$, the model that provides the best generalization performance on unseen data while balancing the trade-off between **model complexity** and **goodness-of-fit** to the observed training data.

Mathematically, the goal is to find the optimal model $M^{\*}$ that minimizes the **expected generalization error** (also called the *true risk* or *out-of-sample error*):

$$ M^{*} = \arg\min_{M \in \mathcal{M}} \mathbb{E}_{(x, y) \sim \mathcal{D}} \left[ \mathcal{L}(y, \hat{f}_M(x)) \right] $$

where $\mathcal{D}$ is the true unknown data-generating distribution, $\mathcal{L}$ is a loss function, and $\hat{f}_M$ is the learned predictor from model class $M$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Shoe-Fitting" Metaphor

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Intuition**
> Imagine you are buying shoes for a long mountain hike. You have five pairs of shoes (low-top, mid-top, high-top, mountaineering boots, custom orthotics). The decision procedure is:
> 1. **Training set** = You try each pair in the store (initial fit check).
> 2. **Validation set** = You walk around the block in each pair (estimate real-world comfort).
> 3. **Test set** = The actual mountain trail (final, untouched evaluation).
> 4. **Bias** = A pair that is too loose or too tight regardless of foot shape (under/over-simplification).
> 5. **Variance** = A pair whose fit changes dramatically depending on the sock thickness (sensitivity to training data).
> 6. **Model Selection** = Choosing the boot that is neither too tight (overfit) nor too loose (underfit) for the *unknown* mountain terrain.

### 1.3 Why Model Selection is Indispensable

- **Overfitting Prevention**: High-complexity models memorize noise in training data.
- **Underfitting Prevention**: Overly simple models miss the underlying signal.
- **Resource Optimization**: Smaller, parsimonious models deploy faster and consume less compute.
- **Statistical Inference**: Provides reliable confidence intervals and hypothesis tests.
- **Reproducibility**: A principled selection procedure yields consistent results across practitioners.

### 1.4 Core Terminology (KTU Board-Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Definitions You Must Memorize**
> - **In-Sample Error (Training Error):** $\text{Err}_{\text{in}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\mathcal{L}(y_i, \hat{f}(x_i))$
> - **Out-of-Sample Error (Generalization Error):** $\text{Err}_{\text{out}} = \mathbb{E}_{(x_0, y_0)}[\mathcal{L}(y_0, \hat{f}(x_0))]$
> - **Model Complexity:** A measure of the flexibility/capacity of the model class (e.g., number of free parameters, polynomial degree, tree depth).
> - **Hyperparameter:** A configuration setting external to the model that cannot be directly learned from data (e.g., $\lambda$ in ridge regression, $k$ in KNN).
> - **Resubstitution Error:** The error obtained when evaluating a model on the same data used for training.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Bias-Variance-Covariance Decomposition (Theoretical Foundation)

For any regression model fitted to a training set $\mathcal{T}$ of size $n$ drawn from distribution $\mathcal{D}$, the **expected prediction error** at a fixed query point $x_0$ decomposes as:

$$ \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}} \left[ (y_0 - \hat{f}(x_0))^2 \right] = \underbrace{\left[ f(x_0) - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}[\hat{f}(x_0)] \right]^2}_{\text{Bias}^2} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}} \left[ (\hat{f}(x_0) - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}[\hat{f}(x_0)])^2 \right]}_{\text{Variance}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2_{\varepsilon}}_{\text{Irreducible Noise}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter?** This is the *mathematical heart* of model selection. It tells us that total error is the sum of three independent forces. We can reduce bias only by increasing complexity, but doing so inflates variance. The optimal model lies at the sweet spot where their sum is minimized.

### 2.2 The Bias-Variance Tradeoff

| Regime | Bias | Variance | Total Error | Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Underfitting** | High | Low | High | Model too simple, misses signal |
| **Optimal Point** | Moderate | Moderate | **Minimum** | Sweet spot of generalization |
| **Overfitting** | Low | High | High | Model too complex, memorizes noise |

### 2.3 Cross-Validation (CV) Procedures

**K-Fold Cross-Validation** is the workhorse procedure:

$$ \text{CV}_{(K)} = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \text{Err}_k = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{1}{n_k} \sum_{i \in \text{Fold}_k} \mathcal{L}(y_i, \hat{f}^{(-k)}(x_i)) $$

**Special Case: Leave-One-Out CV (LOOCV)** — when $K = n$:

$$ \text{LOOCV} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathcal{L}(y_i, \hat{f}^{(-i)}(x_i)) $$

### 2.4 Information-Theoretic Criteria (Closed-Form Approximations)

These provide computationally efficient alternatives to CV by penalizing model complexity using only training data.

**Akaike Information Criterion (AIC):**

$$ \text{AIC} = 2k - 2\ln(\hat{\mathcal{L}}_{\max}) $$

**Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC):**

$$ \text{BIC} = k \ln(n) - 2\ln(\hat{\mathcal{L}}_{\max}) $$

**Adjusted Coefficient of Determination:**

$$ \bar{R}^2 = 1 - \left(1 - R^2\right) \cdot \frac{n-1}{n-p-1} $$

### 2.5 Regularization-Based Selection

Ridge and Lasso regressions embed model selection into the objective function:

$$ \hat{\beta}^{\text{ridge}} = \arg\min_{\beta} \left\{ \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - x_i^T\beta)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j^2 \right\} $$

$$ \hat{\beta}^{\text{lasso}} = \arg\min_{\beta} \left\{ \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - x_i^T\beta)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert \beta_j \vert \right\} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note: In the above Lasso formulation, $\vert \beta_j \vert$ uses the absolute value operator. In KTU answer sheets, the absolute value is denoted by the standard mathematical notation. We use $\vert \cdot \vert$ here (not as a markdown table separator).

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Symbols / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Bias-Variance Decomposition | $\text{Err} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2_{\varepsilon}$ | $\sigma^2_{\varepsilon}$ is irreducible noise variance |
| K-Fold CV | $\text{CV}_{(K)} = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K} \text{Err}_k$ | Each fold has $n_k \approx n/K$ samples |
| LOOCV | $\text{LOOCV} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathcal{L}(y_i, \hat{f}^{(-i)}(x_i))$ | Special case of K-Fold with $K = n$ |
| AIC | $\text{AIC} = 2k - 2\ln(\hat{\mathcal{L}}_{\max})$ | $k$ = number of estimated parameters |
| BIC | $\text{BIC} = k\ln(n) - 2\ln(\hat{\mathcal{L}}_{\max})$ | Stronger penalty than AIC for $n \geq 8$ |
| Adjusted $R^2$ | $\bar{R}^2 = 1 - (1-R^2)\frac{n-1}{n-p-1}$ | $p$ = number of predictors |
| Ridge Penalty | $\lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j^2$ | $L_2$ norm, shrinks but does not zero coefficients |
| Lasso Penalty | $\lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert \beta_j \vert$ | $L_1$ norm, produces sparse models |
| Mallow's $C_p$ | $C_p = \frac{1}{n}\left( \text{RSS} + 2p\hat{\sigma}^2 \right)$ | Statistically equivalent to AIC for linear models |
| Bootstrap Estimate | $\hat{\text{Err}}_{\text{boot}} = \frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B}\text{Err}_b$ | $B$ = number of bootstrap resamples |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Diagnostics**: Selecting the right number of features in a cancer classifier to avoid both missed diagnoses (underfit) and false alarms (overfit).
- **Autonomous Vehicles**: Choosing sensor fusion model complexity to balance latency (simpler models) with safety (more accurate complex models).
- **Predictive Maintenance**: Selecting LSTM architectures that forecast equipment failure without memorizing noise from sensor jitter.
- **Financial Engineering**: Selecting volatility models (GARCH variants) where overfitting to past crises causes catastrophic loss in future regimes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Bias-Variance Decomposition of Mean Squared Error

**Setup:** Let $y_0 = f(x_0) + \varepsilon_0$ where $\varepsilon_0 \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ and the training set $\mathcal{T}$ produces a model $\hat{f}(x_0)$.

**Step 1:** Start with the expected squared prediction error at a single fixed input $x_0$.

$$ \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}, \varepsilon_0} \left[ (y_0 - \hat{f}(x_0))^2 \right] $$

**Step 2:** Expand the square using the identity $(a - b)^2 = (a - c + c - b)^2$ where $c = \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}[\hat{f}(x_0)]$ (the grand mean prediction across all possible training sets).

$$ (y_0 - \hat{f}(x_0))^2 = \left( y_0 - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}[\hat{f}(x_0)] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}[\hat{f}(x_0)] - \hat{f}(x_0) \right)^2 $$

**Step 3:** Apply the squared-of-sum expansion: $(A + B)^2 = A^2 + 2AB + B^2$.

$$ = \left( y_0 - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}[\hat{f}(x_0)] \right)^2 + 2 \left( y_0 - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}[\hat{f}(x_0)] \right) \left( \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}[\hat{f}(x_0)] - \hat{f}(x_0) \right) + \left( \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}[\hat{f}(x_0)] - \hat{f}(x_0) \right)^2 $$

**Step 4:** Take the expectation with respect to $\mathcal{T}$ and $\varepsilon_0$. The cross-term vanishes because $\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}[\hat{f}(x_0)] - \hat{f}(x_0)$ has zero mean under $\mathcal{T}$.

$$ \mathbb{E}[\text{Cross Term}] = 2 \cdot \mathbb{E}_{\varepsilon_0}\left[ y_0 - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}[\hat{f}(x_0)] \right] \cdot \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}\left[ \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}[\hat{f}(x_0)] - \hat{f}(x_0) \right] = 2 \cdot (\cdot) \cdot 0 = 0 $$

**Step 5:** Analyze the first term. Note that $y_0 = f(x_0) + \varepsilon_0$ and $\mathbb{E}_{\varepsilon_0}[\varepsilon_0] = 0$.

$$ \mathbb{E}_{\varepsilon_0}\left[ \left( y_0 - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}[\hat{f}(x_0)] \right)^2 \right] = \mathbb{E}_{\varepsilon_0}\left[ \left( f(x_0) + \varepsilon_0 - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}[\hat{f}(x_0)] \right)^2 \right] $$

**Step 6:** Expand and split. The $\varepsilon_0^2$ term contributes $\sigma^2$, and the cross-term with $\varepsilon_0$ vanishes (since $\varepsilon_0$ is mean-zero and independent).

$$ = \left( f(x_0) - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}[\hat{f}(x_0)] \right)^2 + 2 \mathbb{E}_{\varepsilon_0}[\varepsilon_0] \left( f(x_0) - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}[\hat{f}(x_0)] \right) + \mathbb{E}_{\varepsilon_0}[\varepsilon_0^2] $$

$$ = \left( f(x_0) - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}[\hat{f}(x_0)] \right)^2 + 0 + \sigma^2 $$

**Step 7:** The third term in Step 3 is, by definition, the variance of $\hat{f}(x_0)$ under $\mathcal{T}$.

$$ \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}\left[ \left( \hat{f}(x_0) - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}[\hat{f}(x_0)] \right)^2 \right] = \text{Var}_{\mathcal{T}}(\hat{f}(x_0)) $$

**Step 8:** Combine all three terms. The squared bias is $(f(x_0) - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}[\hat{f}(x_0)])^2$.

$$ \boxed{\; \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}, \varepsilon_0} \left[ (y_0 - \hat{f}(x_0))^2 \right] = \underbrace{\left( f(x_0) - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}[\hat{f}(x_0)] \right)^2}_{\text{Bias}^2} + \underbrace{\text{Var}_{\mathcal{T}}(\hat{f}(x_0))}_{\text{Variance}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{Irreducible Error}} \;} $$

This is the canonical Bias-Variance-Covariance decomposition. QED.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: K-Fold Cross-Validation Expected Error

**Step 1:** Define the K-Fold CV estimator formally. Let $\mathcal{F}_k$ denote the $k$-th validation fold, $\mathcal{T}^{(-k)} = \mathcal{T} \setminus \mathcal{F}_k$ the training subset, and $\hat{f}^{(-k)}$ the model trained on $\mathcal{T}^{(-k)}$.

$$ \text{CV}_{(K)} = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{F}_k \vert} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{F}_k} \mathcal{L}\left( y_i, \hat{f}^{(-k)}(x_i) \right) $$

**Step 2:** Take expectation over the data distribution $\mathcal{D}$ and randomness in the fold assignment.

$$ \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\text{CV}_{(K)}] = \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}\left[ \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{F}_k \vert} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{F}_k} \mathcal{L}(y_i, \hat{f}^{(-k)}(x_i)) \right] $$

**Step 3:** By linearity of expectation and the symmetry of K-Fold (each observation belongs to a validation fold exactly once across the K iterations), we can rewrite the double summation as a single sum over all $n$ observations.

$$ \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\text{CV}_{(K)}] = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}\left[ \mathcal{L}(y_i, \hat{f}^{(-i(\text{fold}))}(x_i)) \right] $$

**Step 4:** Approximate using the fact that each $\hat{f}^{(-k)}$ is trained on roughly $(K-1)n/K$ samples. Define $n_{\text{train}} = n(K-1)/K$.

$$ \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\text{CV}_{(K)}] \approx \mathbb{E}_{(x, y) \sim \mathcal{D}}\left[ \mathcal{L}\left(y, \hat{f}_{n_{\text{train}}}(x)\right) \right] $$

**Step 5:** This is the expected test error for a model trained on $n_{\text{train}}$ samples, providing a near-unbiased estimate of the true out-of-sample error.

$$ \boxed{\; \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\text{CV}_{(K)}] \approx \text{Err}_{\text{out}} \left( \hat{f}_{n(K-1)/K} \right) \;} $$

**Step 6:** **Bias-Variance of CV itself.** The CV estimator has a slight pessimistic bias because each fold trains on $n(K-1)/K < n$ samples, not the full dataset. As $K \to n$ (LOOCV), this bias vanishes but variance increases due to high correlation between the $n$ models (they share $n-2$ samples).

**Step 7:** **Optimal choice of K.** For $n \geq 100$, $K = 5$ or $K = 10$ strikes a good balance. For $n < 50$, $K = n$ (LOOCV) is preferred.

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: AIC from Maximum Likelihood

**Step 1:** Start with the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence between the true distribution $f$ and the fitted model $g_{\hat{\theta}}$.

$$ D_{\text{KL}}(f \mid\mid g_{\hat{\theta}}) = \mathbb{E}_{f}\left[ \log \frac{f(x)}{g_{\hat{\theta}}(x)} \right] = \mathbb{E}_{f}[\log f(x)] - \mathbb{E}_{f}[\log g_{\hat{\theta}}(x)] $$

**Step 2:** The first term is a constant (the entropy of the true distribution). Therefore, **minimizing KL divergence is equivalent to maximizing** $\mathbb{E}_{f}[\log g_{\hat{\theta}}(x)]$.

**Step 3:** This expected log-likelihood is unknown because $f$ is unknown. We replace it with the **empirical log-likelihood** from training data:

$$ \ell(\hat{\theta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log g_{\hat{\theta}}(x_i) $$

**Step 4:** However, the empirical log-likelihood is a **biased optimistic estimator** of $\mathbb{E}_{f}[\log g_{\hat{\theta}}(x)]$. Its bias is approximately equal to the number of free parameters $k$ (Akaike's central insight from information theory).

$$ \mathbb{E}\left[ \ell(\hat{\theta}) \right] \approx \mathbb{E}\left[ \mathbb{E}_f[\log g_{\hat{\theta}}(x)] \right] - k $$

**Step 5:** Rearranging to get an unbiased estimator of the expected log-likelihood:

$$ \mathbb{E}\left[ \mathbb{E}_f[\log g_{\hat{\theta}}(x)] \right] \approx \mathbb{E}\left[ \ell(\hat{\theta}) \right] + k $$

**Step 6:** To convert this into a *minimization* criterion (penalize complexity, reward fit), multiply by $-2$:

$$ \text{AIC} = -2 \ell(\hat{\theta}) + 2k $$

**Step 7:** Substituting the maximum log-likelihood notation $\ell(\hat{\theta}) = \ln(\hat{\mathcal{L}}_{\max})$:

$$ \boxed{\; \text{AIC} = 2k - 2\ln(\hat{\mathcal{L}}_{\max}) \;} $$

**Step 8:** The model with the **lowest AIC** is preferred. Each additional parameter $k$ costs a penalty of $+2$ in the score, so we only accept parameters that improve fit by more than 1 unit of log-likelihood per parameter.

### 3.4 Compact Analytic Comparison Table

| Criterion | Penalty Term | Best For | Behavior with $n$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| AIC | $2k$ | Prediction accuracy | Consistent; tends to over-parameterize in large $n$ |
| BIC | $k\ln(n)$ | True model identification | Consistent; converges to correct model as $n \to \infty$ |
| Adjusted $R^2$ | Implicit $(n-1)/(n-p-1)$ | Linear regression | Decreases when irrelevant features added |
| Mallows $C_p$ | $2p\hat{\sigma}^2$ | Linear regression with Gaussian noise | Equivalent to AIC in OLS setting |
| Cross-Validation | Empirical | Distribution-free, any model | Most accurate but expensive |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 K-Fold Cross-Validation Architecture (Functional Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Full Dataset of N Samples] --> B[Shuffle Data Randomly]
    B --> C[Partition Into K Equal Folds]
    C --> D1[Fold 1 Out of K]
    C --> D2[Fold 2 Out of K]
    C --> D3[Fold 3 Out of K]
    C --> D4[Fold K Out of K]
    D1 --> E1[Train on Folds 2 to K and Validate on Fold 1]
    D2 --> E2[Train on Folds 1 and 3 to K and Validate on Fold 2]
    D3 --> E3[Train on Folds 1 2 and 4 to K and Validate on Fold 3]
    D4 --> E4[Train on Folds 1 to K minus 1 and Validate on Fold K]
    E1 --> F1[Compute Validation Error ERR1]
    E2 --> F2[Compute Validation Error ERR2]
    E3 --> F3[Compute Validation Error ERR3]
    E4 --> F4[Compute Validation Error ERRK]
    F1 --> G[Aggregate: Mean of ERR1 to ERRK]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Report CV Score as Estimate of Test Error]
    H --> I[Select Model With Lowest CV Score]
    I --> J[END: Retrain on Full Data Using Selected Hyperparameters]
```

### 4.2 Bias-Variance Tradeoff Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input X] --> B[True Function f of X]
    B --> C[Add Noise Epsilon]
    C --> D[Observed Data Y]
    D --> E[Train Model Fhat on Sample 1]
    D --> F[Train Model Fhat on Sample 2]
    D --> G[Train Model Fhat on Sample 3]
    D --> H[Train Model Fhat on Sample B]
    E --> I[Ensemble Average E of Fhat]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Compute Bias as f minus E of Fhat]
    I --> K[Compute Variance as Spread of Fhat Predictions]
    J --> L[Total Expected Error Equals Bias Squared plus Variance plus Noise]
    K --> L
    L --> M{Choose Complexity}
    M -->|Too Low| N[High Bias Low Variance UNDERFIT]
    M -->|Balanced| O[Low Bias Moderate Variance OPTIMAL]
    M -->|Too High| P[Low Bias High Variance OVERFIT]
```

### 4.3 Model Selection Pipeline (End-to-End)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Collect Raw Dataset] --> B[Clean and Preprocess]
    B --> C[Train Validation Test Split 70 15 15]
    C --> D[Define Candidate Model Family]
    D --> E[Linear Regression]
    D --> F[Polynomial Regression Degrees 1 to 10]
    D --> G[Ridge with Lambda Grid]
    D --> H[Lasso with Lambda Grid]
    D --> I[Decision Tree with Depth Grid]
    E --> J[Apply K Fold Cross Validation K equals 10]
    F --> J
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Record Mean and Std of CV Score for Each Config]
    K --> L[Select Configuration with Best CV Score]
    L --> M[Retrain Selected Model on Train plus Validation]
    M --> N[Final Evaluation on Held Out Test Set]
    N --> O{Test Error Acceptable?}
    O -->|Yes| P[Deploy Model to Production]
    O -->|No| Q[Return to Step D and Expand Candidate Family]
```

### 4.4 Information Criteria Decision Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Need to Select Model] --> B{Is Closed Form Likelihood Available?}
    B -->|Yes| C{N is Small less than 30 or Model True Discovery Priority}
    C -->|Yes| D[Use BIC: Stronger Penalty k ln n]
    C -->|No| E[Use AIC: Weaker Penalty 2k]
    B -->|No| F{Is Dataset Small n less than 1000?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Use LOOCV: K equals N]
    F -->|No| H[Use 10 Fold CV for Speed and Accuracy]
    D --> I[Compare Across Candidate Models]
    E --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Select Model With Lowest Criterion Value]
    J --> K[Report Final Selected Model]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model Paper)]**

Define the **Bias-Variance Tradeoff** in the context of model selection. Explain how it influences the choice between a simple and a complex model.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Bias** is the error introduced by approximating a real-world problem, which may be extremely complex, by a simplified model. Mathematically, $\text{Bias}(\hat{f}(x)) = \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - f(x)$. **Variance** is the amount by which $\hat{f}$ would change if we estimated it using a different training dataset, i.e., $\text{Var}(\hat{f}(x)) = \mathbb{E}[(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^2]$.
>
> A **simple model** (e.g., linear regression) has high bias but low variance — it is robust to training data but may underfit. A **complex model** (e.g., high-degree polynomial) has low bias but high variance — it fits training data closely but may overfit to noise. The **optimal model** minimizes the sum $\text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2_{\varepsilon}$. This is the Bias-Variance Tradeoff, the central principle guiding model selection.

*(Mark split: Definition 1m, Mathematical formulation 1m, Implication 1m)*

---

**Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Retrieved Pattern)]**

Differentiate between **AIC** and **BIC** as model selection criteria. Under what condition does BIC favor a simpler model than AIC?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Both AIC and BIC balance model fit and complexity. **AIC** = $2k - 2\ln(\hat{\mathcal{L}}_{\max})$ penalizes each parameter by a constant factor of **2**. **BIC** = $k\ln(n) - 2\ln(\hat{\mathcal{L}}_{\max})$ penalizes each parameter by **$\ln(n)$**, which grows with sample size $n$.
>
> **When does BIC prefer simpler models?** Whenever $\ln(n) > 2$, i.e., when $n > e^2 \approx 7.39$. For $n \geq 8$ (which is always the case in real applications), BIC penalizes complexity more heavily than AIC, so BIC consistently favors simpler models. BIC is also *consistent* (it identifies the true model as $n \to \infty$), whereas AIC is not.

*(Mark split: Formulas 1m, Comparison 1m, Condition 1m)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

#### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - KTU 2024 Scheme Style]**

**(a)** Derive the complete Bias-Variance-Covariance decomposition of the expected prediction error for a regression model. State clearly the meaning of each term. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **5-Fold Cross-Validation** procedure in detail with a suitable schematic. Discuss its **advantages** and **limitations** compared to Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the decomposition goal. We want to express $\mathbb{E}[(y_0 - \hat{f}(x_0))^2]$ as a sum of three interpretable terms.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Introduce the grand mean $\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}[\hat{f}(x_0)]$ and apply the squared-of-sum expansion:

$$ (y_0 - \hat{f}(x_0))^2 = \left( y_0 - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}[\hat{f}(x_0)] \right)^2 + 2 \left( y_0 - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}[\hat{f}(x_0)] \right) \left( \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}[\hat{f}(x_0)] - \hat{f}(x_0) \right) + \left( \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}[\hat{f}(x_0)] - \hat{f}(x_0) \right)^2 $$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Take the expectation and show the cross-term vanishes. Argue: $\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}[\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}[\hat{f}(x_0)] - \hat{f}(x_0)] = 0$.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Expand the first term using $y_0 = f(x_0) + \varepsilon_0$, with $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon_0] = 0$, $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon_0^2] = \sigma^2$. The cross-product with $\varepsilon_0$ vanishes, leaving:

$$ \left( f(x_0) - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}[\hat{f}(x_0)] \right)^2 + \sigma^2 $$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Identify the three final terms and state the boxed result:

$$ \mathbb{E}[(y_0 - \hat{f}(x_0))^2] = \text{Bias}^2(\hat{f}(x_0)) + \text{Var}(\hat{f}(x_0)) + \sigma^2_{\varepsilon} $$

**Meaning of each term (implied in steps):**
- **Bias²** = systematic error from model misspecification.
- **Variance** = sensitivity of the model to fluctuations in training data.
- **Irreducible error $\sigma^2_{\varepsilon}$** = inherent noise in the data-generating process; cannot be reduced by any model.

---

**(b) 5-Fold CV (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** **Procedure Description.** Randomly partition the dataset of $n$ samples into $K=5$ disjoint folds of approximately equal size ($n/5 \approx n_1$). For each $k = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$:
- Use fold $k$ as the **validation set**.
- Use the remaining $4$ folds as the **training set**.
- Train the model and compute validation error $\text{Err}_k$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** **Aggregation.** Compute the final CV score:

$$ \text{CV}_{(5)} = \frac{1}{5} \sum_{k=1}^{5} \text{Err}_k $$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** **Advantages over LOOCV:**
- **Computational efficiency:** Only 5 model trainings instead of $n$ (saves time when $n$ is large).
- **Lower variance:** The averaged errors are less correlated because each fold shares only 75% of data with any other fold (vs. 99% in LOOCV).
- **Better for hyperparameter tuning:** Lower variance makes CV-based selection more stable.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** **Limitations / Tradeoffs:**
- **Slight pessimistic bias:** Each model trains on $4n/5$ samples, not the full $n$, so it slightly overestimates the test error.
- **Sensitivity to fold split:** Results may vary with different random partitions; mitigated by **stratified** or **repeated** 5-fold CV.
- **Stratification required for imbalanced data:** Standard 5-fold may produce folds with skewed class distributions.

---

#### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - KTU 2024 Scheme Style — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** What is **Regularization**? Derive the closed-form solution for the **Ridge Regression** estimator. Explain how the penalty parameter $\lambda$ controls model complexity. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Describe the **Bootstrap** method for estimating the generalization error. How is it used for model selection? Discuss its limitations. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Regularization & Ridge Regression (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** **Definition.** Regularization is a model selection technique that adds a penalty term to the loss function to discourage complex models (large coefficient magnitudes), thereby reducing overfitting and improving generalization.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** **Ridge Objective.** Ridge regression minimizes the $L_2$-penalized residual sum of squares:

$$ J(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left( y_i - \beta_0 - \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j x_{ij} \right)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j^2 $$

In matrix form (assuming centered data):

$$ J(\beta) = (y - X\beta)^T(y - X\beta) + \lambda \beta^T \beta $$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** **Derivation of closed form.** Take the gradient with respect to $\beta$ and set to zero:

$$ \nabla_{\beta} J = -2X^T(y - X\beta) + 2\lambda \beta = 0 $$

$$ X^T y - X^T X \beta = \lambda \beta $$

$$ (X^T X + \lambda I) \beta = X^T y $$

$$ \boxed{\; \hat{\beta}^{\text{ridge}} = (X^T X + \lambda I)^{-1} X^T y \;} $$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** **Role of $\lambda$:**
- $\lambda = 0$: Recovers ordinary least squares (OLS) — maximum complexity, highest variance.
- $\lambda \to \infty$: All $\beta_j \to 0$ — model becomes constant, maximum bias, minimum variance.
- $\lambda$ is a **hyperparameter** tuned via cross-validation. The optimal $\lambda^{\*}$ balances bias and variance.
- Ridge **never** zeroes coefficients (unlike Lasso); it only shrinks them.

---

**(b) Bootstrap Method (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** **Bootstrap Procedure:**
1. Given a dataset $\mathcal{T} = \{(x_1, y_1), \ldots, (x_n, y_n)\}$ of size $n$.
2. For $b = 1, 2, \ldots, B$ (typically $B = 100$ to $1000$):
   - Draw a bootstrap sample $\mathcal{T}^{\*b}$ of size $n$ **with replacement** from $\mathcal{T}$.
   - Train model $\hat{f}^{\*b}$ on $\mathcal{T}^{\*b}$.
   - Evaluate the trained model on the **out-of-bag (OOB) samples** (those not in $\mathcal{T}^{\*b}$), yielding $\text{Err}^{\*b}_{\text{OOB}}$.
3. Compute the bootstrap estimate:

$$ \hat{\text{Err}}_{\text{boot}} = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \text{Err}^{\*b}_{\text{OOB}} $$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** **Use in Model Selection:**
- **Model Comparison:** Train each candidate model on every bootstrap sample and compare average OOB errors. Select the model with the lowest $\hat{\text{Err}}_{\text{boot}}$.
- **Stability Analysis:** Examine the variability of coefficient estimates across bootstrap samples. Stable models yield low-variance coefficient distributions.
- **Confidence Intervals:** Construct percentile-based 95% CIs for predictions using the empirical quantiles of $\hat{f}^{\*1}, \hat{f}^{\*2}, \ldots, \hat{f}^{\*B}$.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** **Advantages:**
- Distribution-free, no parametric assumptions.
- Efficient use of data (every sample has $\approx 63.2\%$ chance of being in any single bootstrap sample; the rest are OOB).

**Step 4 (2 marks):** **Limitations:**
- **Computationally expensive:** Requires $B$ model trainings.
- **Assumes representativeness of original sample.** Biased bootstrap if the original data is not representative.
- **Poor performance with time-series data** (since resampling destroys temporal dependencies). Specialized variants like **block bootstrap** are required.
- **Overestimates performance slightly** in small samples when the OOB fraction is small.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in Model Selection Questions**
> 1. **Forgetting the cross-term vanishes in Bias-Variance derivation** (–2 marks). Always explicitly state that $\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}[\hat{f}(x_0) - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}[\hat{f}(x_0)]] = 0$ to justify why the middle term drops out.
> 2. **Confusing $k$ with $p$**: In AIC/BIC, $k$ is the total number of estimated parameters, not the number of predictors. Include the intercept if estimated. (–1 to –2 marks)
> 3. **Writing $\vert \beta_j \vert$ as `\|` in answer sheets**: In LaTeX, use the proper `\vert` or `\mid` command to avoid the wrong symbol. In handwritten scripts, draw the absolute value bars clearly.
> 4. **Not specifying the choice of $K$**: When answering K-Fold CV questions, students often forget to state **why $K=5$ or $K=10$ is preferred**. Always justify with the bias-variance tradeoff of the CV estimator itself.
> 5. **Mixing up training and test error formulas**: In LOOCV, $i$-th observation is the **validation** sample, NOT the test sample. Test set remains fully held out.
> 6. **Omitting the assumption of independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) data** when applying CV or bootstrap. (–1 mark for missing assumption)

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Selection Procedures — Final Revision Cheat Sheet**
> - [ ] **Goal:** Minimize expected generalization error $\text{Err}_{\text{out}} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$.
> - [ ] **Bias-Variance Decomposition:** $\text{Err} = \text{Bias}^2(\hat{f}(x)) + \text{Var}(\hat{f}(x)) + \sigma^2_{\varepsilon}$. **Memorize the derivation steps.**
> - [ ] **K-Fold CV:** Partition into $K$ folds; train on $K-1$, validate on 1; average errors. Use $K = 5$ or $10$ for $n \geq 100$.
> - [ ] **LOOCV:** Special case $K = n$. Low bias, high variance, computationally expensive.
> - [ ] **AIC = $2k - 2\ln(\hat{\mathcal{L}}_{\max})$:** Use for prediction accuracy. Lower is better.
> - [ ] **BIC = $k\ln(n) - 2\ln(\hat{\mathcal{L}}_{\max})$:** Use for true model identification. Consistent estimator.
> - [ ] **Adjusted $R^2$:** $\bar{R}^2 = 1 - (1-R^2)(n-1)/(n-p-1)$. Decreases when irrelevant features added.
> - [ ] **Ridge:** $L_2$ penalty, shrinks but does not zero coefficients. Solution: $\hat{\beta} = (X^TX + \lambda I)^{-1}X^Ty$.
> - [ ] **Lasso:** $L_1$ penalty, produces sparse models (feature selection).
> - [ ] **Bootstrap:** Resample $B$ times with replacement. Use OOB samples for validation. Distribution-free.
> - [ ] **Mallow's $C_p$:** $C_p = \text{RSS}/n + 2p\hat{\sigma}^2/n$. Equivalent to AIC for OLS.
> - [ ] **Stratified CV:** Use when class distribution is imbalanced.
> - [ ] **Nested CV:** Outer loop for evaluation, inner loop for hyperparameter tuning. Prevents data leakage.
> - [ ] **No Free Lunch Theorem:** No single model selection method is universally best; choice depends on data size, noise, and computational budget.

---

**End of Module 2 — Model Selection Procedures**
