topics.title: Regularization Techniques - LASSO
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT501 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Regularization Techniques - LASSO

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 2 – Regression & Regularization):**
> This topic covers **L1-regularized linear regression** (LASSO), a sparse learning technique used to combat overfitting, perform implicit feature selection, and improve the generalization of high-dimensional regression models.

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**LASSO (Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator)** is a linear regression technique that performs **regularization** by adding a penalty term equal to the **L1-norm** of the coefficient vector to the residual sum of squares (RSS). The penalty is governed by a non-negative hyperparameter $\lambda$ (often denoted $\alpha$ in the KTU textbooks).

The LASSO estimator $\hat{\beta}^{\text{LASSO}}$ is defined as the solution to the constrained optimization problem:

$$\hat{\beta}^{\text{LASSO}} = \arg\min_{\beta \in \mathbb{R}^{p}} \left\{ \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \beta_0 - \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j x_{ij})^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert \beta_j \vert \right\}$$

where:
- $n$ = number of training samples
- $p$ = number of features (predictors)
- $y_i$ = response variable for the $i^{th}$ observation
- $\beta_0$ = intercept term
- $\beta_j$ = coefficient for the $j^{th}$ feature
- $\lambda \ge 0$ = **regularization strength** (tuning parameter)
- $\sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert \beta_j \vert$ = the **L1 penalty** (sum of absolute weights)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Ordinary Least Squares (OLS):** OLS minimizes only $\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$, which can lead to overfitting when $p$ is large or features are highly correlated. LASSO introduces a **budget on the magnitude of the coefficients**, forcing the model to "spend" its budget wisely — a constraint that produces **sparse solutions** (many $\beta_j$ become exactly zero).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hire an Expert Team" Story

Imagine you are a CEO hiring a panel of **$p$ experts** to predict next quarter's profit from $p$ available economic indicators. Every expert charges you \$1 per unit of confidence they express. You have a **fixed hiring budget** (the L1 ball of radius $t$).

- **Ridge (L2)** is like paying the **square** of each expert's confidence — a confident expert becomes prohibitively expensive, so all experts are hired with small, comparable salaries. **Nobody gets fired** — every feature remains in the model, just shrunk.
- **LASSO (L1)** is like paying the **absolute** confidence linearly. Since small salaries are cheap and full salaries are affordable, it is often cheaper to **fire the weak expert entirely** (pay \$0) and put the whole budget behind the strongest ones. This is precisely why LASSO produces **sparse models** — the L1 penalty "fires" irrelevant features by driving their coefficients to exactly zero.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** "LASSO is the **L1**asso — it lassoes in (shrinks) the coefficients, and when a coefficient is shrunk past zero, it is **lassoed out** (eliminated) of the model."

## 1.3 Why LASSO is Needed — Motivation in KTU Context

| Problem in Plain Regression | How LASSO Solves It |
|---|---|
| **Overfitting** when $n \approx p$ or $p > n$ | The L1 constraint limits model complexity, reducing variance. |
| **Multicollinearity** among predictors | LASSO tends to pick one representative from a correlated cluster. |
| **High-dimensional data** (genomics, text) | Produces interpretable sparse models. |
| **Curse of dimensionality** | Reduces effective dimensionality automatically. |
| **Predictor selection** required | Performs **embedded feature selection** — no separate filter/wrapper step needed. |

## 1.4 Equivalency: Penalty Form vs. Constraint Form

LASSO can be written in two mathematically equivalent forms used in KTU textbooks:

**Penalty (Lagrangian) Form:**

$$\min_{\beta} \quad \Vert y - X\beta \Vert_2^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert \beta_j \vert \le t$$

There exists a one-to-one correspondence between $\lambda \ge 0$ and $t \ge 0$. For every $t$, there is a $\lambda$ producing the same solution, and vice-versa.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Formulation — Full Derivation Pathway

### Step 1: Start with the Linear Model

We model the response as a linear combination of features:

$$y_i = \beta_0 + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j x_{ij} + \epsilon_i, \quad \epsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$$

where $\epsilon_i$ is i.i.d. Gaussian noise.

### Step 2: Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) Objective

OLS finds $\beta$ by minimizing the residual sum of squares:

$$\text{RSS}(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( y_i - \beta_0 - \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j x_{ij} \right)^2 = \Vert y - X\beta \Vert_2^2$$

In matrix notation with $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$ and $y \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$:

$$\hat{\beta}^{\text{OLS}} = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y \quad \text{(provided } X^T X \text{ is invertible)}$$

**Why this fails in high dimensions:** When $p$ approaches $n$ or features are correlated, $X^T X$ becomes **singular or ill-conditioned**, leading to:
- Unstable, high-variance estimates
- Coefficient explosion
- Poor generalization on test data

### Step 3: Adding the L1 Penalty (LASSO Objective)

To stabilize the problem, we **augment** the RSS with a penalty that constrains the size of the coefficients:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{LASSO}}(\beta) = \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( y_i - \beta_0 - \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j x_{ij} \right)^2}_{\text{Fit to training data}} + \underbrace{\lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert \beta_j \vert}_{\text{L1 regularization term}}$$

The hyperparameter $\lambda$ controls the **trade-off**:
- $\lambda = 0 \Rightarrow$ recovers the OLS solution.
- $\lambda \to \infty \Rightarrow$ all $\beta_j \to 0$ (null model).
- Intermediate $\lambda$ yields a **shrunk, sparse** estimator.

## 2.2 L1 vs L2 Penalty — The Geometric Heart of LASSO

The crucial difference between LASSO and Ridge lies in the **geometry of the constraint region** in coefficient space.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is L1 sparse but L2 not?** The intuition is purely geometric. The LASSO solution lies at the **first point of contact** between the OLS RSS contour (an ellipsoid centered at $\hat{\beta}^{\text{OLS}}$) and the constraint region. The **L1 ball has sharp corners** on the coordinate axes, and the ellipsoid is far more likely to "touch" one of these corners — yielding a sparse solution. The L2 ball (circle/sphere) is smooth and rarely touches a coordinate axis.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The table below is the **complete, exam-ready** mathematical reference for LASSO. Memorize the form of each equation and the exact meaning of every symbol.

| # | Concept | Mathematical Expression | Notes / Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | LASSO objective (penalty form) | $\min_\beta \Vert y - X\beta \Vert_2^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert \beta_j \vert$ | Standard form; $\lambda \ge 0$. |
| 2 | LASSO objective (constraint form) | $\min_\beta \Vert y - X\beta \Vert_2^2 \;\; \text{s.t.} \;\; \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert \beta_j \vert \le t$ | Equivalent; $t \ge 0$ is the budget. |
| 3 | L1 norm of coefficient vector | $\Vert \beta \Vert_1 = \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert \beta_j \vert$ | Sum of absolute values. |
| 4 | L2 norm of coefficient vector | $\Vert \beta \Vert_2 = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j^2}$ | Euclidean norm (used in Ridge). |
| 5 | Lq norm (general) | $\Vert \beta \Vert_q = \left( \sum_j \vert \beta_j \vert^q \right)^{1/q}$ | $q = 1$ gives LASSO, $q = 2$ gives Ridge. |
| 6 | Sub-gradient of $\vert \beta_j \vert$ | $\partial \vert \beta_j \vert = \begin{cases} -1 & \beta_j < 0 \\ [-1, 1] & \beta_j = 0 \\ +1 & \beta_j > 0 \end{cases}$ | Sub-differential used at the kink $\beta_j = 0$. |
| 7 | Optimality condition (stationarity) | $X^T(y - X\hat{\beta}) = \lambda \cdot \gamma \quad \text{where} \quad \gamma_j \in \partial \vert \hat{\beta}_j \vert$ | First-order KKT condition. |
| 8 | Soft-thresholding operator | $S(z, \lambda) = \text{sign}(z) \cdot \max(\vert z \vert - \lambda, \; 0)$ | Closed-form coordinate update. |
| 9 | Sparsity property | $\hat{\beta}_j^{\text{LASSO}} = 0 \;\; \text{iff} \;\; \vert X_j^T r_{-j} \vert \le \lambda$ | Coefficient vanishes if signal is weak. |
| 10 | Effective degrees of freedom | $\text{df}(\lambda) = \mathbb{E}[\text{number of non-zero } \hat{\beta}_j(\lambda)]$ | Used for AICc / Cp selection. |
| 11 | Ridge penalty term (for contrast) | $\lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j^2$ | Squared, not absolute — never zero. |
| 12 | Elastic Net penalty | $\lambda \left[ \alpha \sum_j \vert \beta_j \vert + \frac{1-\alpha}{2} \sum_j \beta_j^2 \right]$ | Hybrid L1 + L2. |

## 2.4 Soft-Thresholding Operator — The Engine of LASSO

Because the absolute value function $\vert \beta_j \vert$ is **not differentiable at $\beta_j = 0$**, classical gradient-based methods fail. Instead, LASSO admits a beautiful **closed-form coordinate-wise update** known as **soft-thresholding**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Soft-Thresholding Rule:** For a single coefficient $\beta_j$, given all other coefficients fixed, the optimal update is:
> $$\hat{\beta}_j \;\longleftarrow\; S\!\left( X_j^T r_{-j}, \; \lambda \right) = \text{sign}\!\left( X_j^T r_{-j} \right) \cdot \max\!\left( \big\vert X_j^T r_{-j} \big\vert - \lambda, \; 0 \right)$$
> where $r_{-j} = y - \sum_{k \ne j} \beta_k X_k$ is the partial residual.

**Interpretation:**
- If $\vert X_j^T r_{-j} \vert > \lambda$: $\hat{\beta}_j$ is shrunk toward zero by exactly $\lambda$.
- If $\vert X_j^T r_{-j} \vert \le \lambda$: $\hat{\beta}_j$ is set to **exactly zero** — the feature is removed.
- Compare with "hard-thresholding" (subset selection), which jumps discontinuously from $\beta_j$ to $0$ — soft-thresholding is **continuous and smooth** away from the kink.

## 2.5 Bias-Variance Trade-off (Exam Favorite)

Regularization introduces **bias** to **reduce variance**. The total expected prediction error decomposes as:

$$\text{Expected Test MSE} = \text{Bias}^2(\hat{f}) + \text{Var}(\hat{f}) + \sigma^2$$

| Method | Bias | Variance | Sparsity |
|---|---|---|---|
| OLS | Low | High | None |
| Ridge | Higher | Lower | None (all coefficients non-zero) |
| **LASSO** | **Higher (still)** | **Lower** | **Yes (some coefficients exactly zero)** |
| Elastic Net | Higher | Lower | Yes (groups correlated features) |

## 2.6 KTU Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use Case of LASSO |
|---|---|
| **Bioinformatics & Genomics** | Identifying a small set of disease-associated genes from tens of thousands. |
| **Financial Risk Modeling** | Selecting few leading indicators from hundreds of macroeconomic signals. |
| **Signal Processing / Compressed Sensing** | Recovering sparse signals from few measurements (basis pursuit denoising). |
| **Computer Vision** | Sparse coding of image patches. |
| **Text Mining (NLP)** | Sparse document classification with high-dimensional bag-of-words features. |
| **Sensor Networks & IoT** | Selecting minimum sensor subset for state estimation. |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — LASSO via Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) Estimation

LASSO can be motivated as the **MAP estimate** of $\beta$ under a **Laplace prior**.

**Step 1 — Likelihood.** Assume Gaussian noise:

$$y \mid \beta \sim \mathcal{N}(X\beta, \sigma^2 I) \;\Longrightarrow\; p(y \mid \beta) = \frac{1}{(2\pi\sigma^2)^{n/2}} \exp\!\left( -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \Vert y - X\beta \Vert_2^2 \right)$$

**Step 2 — Laplace prior on $\beta_j$.** The Laplace (double-exponential) density is:

$$p(\beta_j) = \frac{1}{2b} \exp\!\left( -\frac{\vert \beta_j \vert}{b} \right), \quad b > 0$$

The Laplace distribution has a **sharp peak at zero** and heavy tails, encoding the belief that most coefficients are zero, but the few that are non-zero may be large.

**Step 3 — Posterior via Bayes' rule.** Assuming independence of $\beta_j$:

$$p(\beta \mid y) \propto p(y \mid \beta) \, p(\beta) = \prod_i p(y_i \mid \beta) \cdot \prod_j p(\beta_j)$$

**Step 4 — Log-posterior.** Take the natural log and drop constants:

$$\log p(\beta \mid y) = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \Vert y - X\beta \Vert_2^2 - \frac{1}{b} \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert \beta_j \vert + C$$

**Step 5 — Maximize (negate to minimize).**

$$\hat{\beta}^{\text{MAP}} = \arg\min_\beta \left\{ \frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \Vert y - X\beta \Vert_2^2 + \frac{1}{b} \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert \beta_j \vert \right\}$$

**Step 6 — Identify $\lambda$.** Multiplying the objective by $2\sigma^2$ and defining $\lambda = 2\sigma^2 / b$:

$$\hat{\beta}^{\text{LASSO}} = \arg\min_\beta \left\{ \Vert y - X\beta \Vert_2^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert \beta_j \vert \right\} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Contrast with Ridge:** Ridge corresponds to a **Gaussian prior** $p(\beta_j) \propto \exp(-\beta_j^2 / 2\tau^2)$, which has its mode at zero but is smooth — coefficients are shrunk toward zero but **never exactly zero**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Soft-Thresholding Operator from KKT Conditions

This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU exams.

**Step 1 — Re-write the objective for a single coordinate.** Holding all $\beta_k$ ($k \ne j$) fixed, define the partial residual $r_{-j} = y - \sum_{k \ne j} \beta_k x_k$. The LASSO objective as a function of $\beta_j$ alone is:

$$f(\beta_j) = (r_{-j} - \beta_j x_j)^T (r_{-j} - \beta_j x_j) + \lambda \vert \beta_j \vert + \text{const}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate (sub-gradient).**

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial \beta_j} = -2 x_j^T r_{-j} + 2 \beta_j x_j^T x_j + \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(\beta_j) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Standardize the data** (a common KTU assumption) so that $x_j^T x_j = 1$:

$$2 \beta_j - 2 x_j^T r_{-j} + \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(\beta_j) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve the three cases.**

**Case A:** $\hat{\beta}_j > 0 \Rightarrow \text{sign}(\beta_j) = +1$:

$$2 \hat{\beta}_j = 2 x_j^T r_{-j} - \lambda \;\Longrightarrow\; \hat{\beta}_j = x_j^T r_{-j} - \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

But this solution is valid only if $\hat{\beta}_j > 0$, i.e., if $x_j^T r_{-j} > \lambda/2$.

**Case B:** $\hat{\beta}_j < 0 \Rightarrow \text{sign}(\beta_j) = -1$:

$$2 \hat{\beta}_j = 2 x_j^T r_{-j} + \lambda \;\Longrightarrow\; \hat{\beta}_j = x_j^T r_{-j} + \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

Valid only if $\hat{\beta}_j < 0$, i.e., if $x_j^T r_{-j} < -\lambda/2$.

**Case C:** $\hat{\beta}_j = 0$. For this to be optimal, the sub-gradient at 0 must contain 0. The sub-differential of $\vert \beta_j \vert$ at 0 is $[-1, +1]$, so the stationarity condition becomes:

$$\big\vert x_j^T r_{-j} \big\vert \le \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Combine into a single operator.** Absorbing the factor of 2 into a redefinition of $\lambda$ (standard convention), the soft-thresholding operator emerges:

$$\hat{\beta}_j = S(z, \lambda) = \text{sign}(z) \cdot \max(\vert z \vert - \lambda, \, 0), \quad \text{where} \; z = x_j^T r_{-j} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!TIP]
> **Geometric reading of soft-thresholding:** Starting from the OLS estimate $z$, the LASSO estimate is obtained by moving $z$ toward 0 by an amount $\lambda$. If $z$ is within the dead zone $(-\lambda, \lambda)$, the result is exactly 0. This is a "**soft**" or "**gradual**" form of zeroing — gentle, not abrupt like hard-thresholding.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Why L1 Induces Sparsity (Sub-differential Argument)

The necessary and sufficient **KKT condition** for $\hat{\beta}$ to be the LASSO solution is:

$$X_j^T (y - X\hat{\beta}) = \lambda \cdot \gamma_j, \quad \text{where} \quad \gamma_j \in \partial \vert \hat{\beta}_j \vert$$

Examining the structure of $\gamma_j$:

- If $\hat{\beta}_j > 0$: $\gamma_j = +1 \Rightarrow X_j^T(y - X\hat{\beta}) = +\lambda$.
- If $\hat{\beta}_j < 0$: $\gamma_j = -1 \Rightarrow X_j^T(y - X\hat{\beta}) = -\lambda$.
- If $\hat{\beta}_j = 0$: $\gamma_j \in [-1, +1] \Rightarrow \big\vert X_j^T(y - X\hat{\beta}) \big\vert \le \lambda$.

The last case is the **sparsity-inducing** condition. As long as the **partial correlation** of feature $j$ with the residual is bounded by $\lambda$, the optimal choice is to **set $\hat{\beta}_j = 0$**. Thus, $\lambda$ acts as a **threshold** for variable inclusion.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Equivalence of Penalty and Constraint Forms

**Claim:** For any $\lambda \ge 0$, there exists a $t \ge 0$ such that:

$$\min_\beta \; \Vert y - X\beta \Vert_2^2 + \lambda \sum_j \vert \beta_j \vert \quad\equiv\quad \min_{\beta \, : \, \sum_j \vert \beta_j \vert \le t} \Vert y - X\beta \Vert_2^2$$

**Proof Sketch (KTU level):**

By Lagrangian duality, the constrained problem has Lagrangian:

$$\mathcal{L}(\beta, \lambda) = \Vert y - X\beta \Vert_2^2 + \lambda \!\left( \sum_j \vert \beta_j \vert - t \right)$$

Minimizing over $\beta$ for a fixed $\lambda$ yields:

$$\hat{\beta}(\lambda) = \arg\min_\beta \left\{ \Vert y - X\beta \Vert_2^2 + \lambda \sum_j \vert \beta_j \vert \right\}$$

Now, define $t(\lambda) = \sum_j \vert \hat{\beta}_j(\lambda) \vert$. By construction, $\hat{\beta}(\lambda)$ lies on the boundary of the L1 ball of radius $t(\lambda)$, so it is also the solution to the constraint problem with budget $t(\lambda)$. Monotonicity of $t(\lambda)$ in $\lambda$ (verified by perturbation arguments) establishes a one-to-one mapping. $\blacksquare$

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — LASSO vs. Ridge

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is the **single most important comparison** for KTU Module 2 questions. Commit it to memory.

| Property | OLS | Ridge (L2) | **LASSO (L1)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Penalty term | None | $\lambda \sum \beta_j^2$ | $\lambda \sum \vert \beta_j \vert$ |
| Constraint region geometry | $\mathbb{R}^p$ | Sphere | Diamond (L1 ball) |
| Closed-form solution | Yes | Yes (linear system) | No (convex but non-smooth) |
| Coefficient shrinkage | None | Continuous, never 0 | Continuous, can be 0 |
| Feature selection | No | No | **Yes (built-in)** |
| Handles $p > n$? | No (singular) | Yes | Yes |
| Handles correlated features | Indifferent | Shrinks them together | Picks one, drops others |
| Solution method | Normal equations | Cholesky / SVD | Coordinate descent / LARS |
| Bayesian prior | Uniform (improper) | Gaussian on $\beta_j$ | Laplace on $\beta_j$ |
| Computational cost | $O(p^3 + np^2)$ | $O(p^3 + np^2)$ | $O(np \cdot k)$ for $k$ iterations |
| Unique solution? | Yes (if $X^TX$ invertible) | Yes | Yes (strictly convex + proper) |

## 3.6 Selection of $\lambda$ — Cross-Validation Procedure (Standard KTU Protocol)

**Step 1:** Construct a grid of $\lambda$ values, e.g., on the log scale:

$$\lambda_k = \lambda_{\max} \cdot r^k, \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, K, \quad r \in (0, 1)$$

with $\lambda_{\max} = \max_j \vert x_j^T y \vert$ (the smallest $\lambda$ that zeroes out all coefficients).

**Step 2:** For each $\lambda_k$, perform **$K$-fold cross-validation** (typically $K = 10$):

$$\text{CV}(\lambda_k) = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{f=1}^{K} \frac{1}{n_f} \sum_{i \in \text{fold}_f} \left( y_i - \hat{y}_i^{(-f)}(\lambda_k) \right)^2$$

**Step 3:** Choose $\lambda^{\star} = \arg\min_k \text{CV}(\lambda_k)$ for minimum test error, or use the **one-standard-error rule** for a more parsimonious model.
