# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

### Q1. Define LASSO regression. How does it differ from ordinary least squares (OLS) regression?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

LASSO (Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator) is a regularized regression technique that minimizes the residual sum of squares subject to an **L1 penalty** on the magnitude of the coefficients:

$$\min_\beta \left\{ \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \beta_0 - \sum_j \beta_j x_{ij})^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert \beta_j \vert \right\}$$

Differences from OLS:

1. **OOS objective** has no penalty term ($\lambda = 0$), whereas LASSO adds the L1 penalty $\lambda \sum \vert \beta_j \vert$.
2. OLS has **no mechanism to set coefficients to zero**, while LASSO performs **automatic feature selection** by driving irrelevant coefficients to exactly zero.
3. OLS is **unstable** when $p > n$ or features are collinear, whereas LASSO remains well-defined and produces **sparse, interpretable** models.
4. OLS is the **unbiased minimum-variance** estimator (under Gauss-Markov); LASSO sacrifices some bias to gain a **substantial reduction in variance**, lowering test MSE.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

### Q2. What is the role of the regularization parameter $\lambda$ in LASSO? Explain its effect on model coefficients.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The regularization parameter $\lambda \ge 0$ controls the **strength of the L1 penalty** and, consequently, the **complexity** of the fitted model. Its effects are:

1. **$\lambda = 0$:** No regularization. LASSO reduces to OLS, and all coefficients take their unshrunk values.
2. **$\lambda$ small:** Mild penalty. Coefficients are slightly shrunk, but most remain non-zero.
3. **$\lambda$ moderate:** Significant shrinkage. Weak features have their coefficients pushed to **exactly zero**, performing **embedded feature selection**.
4. **$\lambda \to \infty$:** Infinite penalty. All coefficients collapse to zero (the null model), underfitting the data.

Mathematically, $\lambda$ acts as a **threshold** in the KKT condition: a feature $j$ is included in the model if and only if its partial correlation with the residual exceeds $\lambda$, i.e., $\vert X_j^T(y - X\hat{\beta}) \vert > \lambda$. The optimal $\lambda$ is typically chosen via **$K$-fold cross-validation** to minimize the expected test error.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 2, End-Semester Estimation Pattern]**

### ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**Q.A.** (a) **State and explain the LASSO optimization problem in both penalty form and constraint form. Show that the two forms are equivalent. Differentiate LASSO from Ridge regression in terms of the constraint region geometry, sparsity, and prior distribution. (7 marks)**

(b) **Derive the soft-thresholding operator as the coordinate-wise update rule for the LASSO estimator. Starting from the LASSO objective, derive the optimality conditions and show explicitly how a coefficient is set to exactly zero. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Penalty vs. Constraint & LASSO vs. Ridge

**Penalty form of LASSO:**

$$\hat{\beta}^{\text{LASSO}} = \arg\min_{\beta \in \mathbb{R}^p} \left\{ \Vert y - X\beta \Vert_2^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert \beta_j \vert \right\}$$

Here, the objective is the **RSS + L1 penalty**. The hyperparameter $\lambda \ge 0$ controls shrinkage.

**Constraint form of LASSO:**

$$\hat{\beta}^{\text{LASSO}} = \arg\min_\beta \Vert y - X\beta \Vert_2^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert \beta_j \vert \le t$$

Here, $t \ge 0$ is the maximum permitted L1 budget.

**Proof of equivalence (Lagrangian duality):** The constrained problem is equivalent to the unconstrained one because the L1 constraint is **convex** and the objective is **strictly convex**, so Slater's condition holds. The Lagrangian is:

$$\mathcal{L}(\beta, \lambda, \mu) = \Vert y - X\beta \Vert_2^2 + \lambda \left( \sum_j \vert \beta_j \vert - t \right)$$

For a fixed $t$, minimizing over $\beta$ gives the penalty form, with the Lagrange multiplier playing the role of $\lambda$. Conversely, for fixed $\lambda$, the solution $\hat{\beta}(\lambda)$ has L1 norm $t(\lambda) = \sum_j \vert \hat{\beta}_j(\lambda) \vert$, so it is also the solution to the constraint problem. **Hence the two forms are equivalent, with a 1-to-1 monotone mapping between $\lambda$ and $t$.**

**LASSO vs. Ridge comparison:**

| Aspect | Ridge (L2) | LASSO (L1) |
|---|---|---|
| Constraint region | Sphere: $\sum \beta_j^2 \le t$ | Diamond: $\sum \vert \beta_j \vert \le t$ |
| Sparsity | No (all $\beta_j$ are non-zero) | Yes (some $\beta_j$ exactly zero) |
| Prior on $\beta_j$ | Gaussian: $\exp(-\beta_j^2 / 2\tau^2)$ | Laplace: $\exp(-\vert \beta_j \vert / b)$ |
| Solution | Smooth, closed-form via linear system | Non-smooth at 0, requires iterative method |
| Handles $p \gg n$? | Yes, but keeps all features | Yes, and performs selection |

**Geometric intuition (3 marks awarded here):** The OLS estimate is the unconstrained minimum of RSS. The feasible region (sphere for Ridge, diamond for LASSO) surrounds the origin. The LASSO solution is the first point of tangency between the expanding RSS contour and the diamond. Because the **diamond has corners on the axes**, the contour typically first touches a corner, producing a coefficient of zero. The **sphere is smooth**, so tangency almost never lands on an axis.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Soft-Thresholding Derivation

**Step 1 — Set up the single-coordinate objective.** Fix all $\beta_k$ for $k \ne j$. Define the partial residual $r_{-j} = y - \sum_{k \ne j} \beta_k x_k$. The LASSO objective as a function of $\beta_j$ alone is:

$$f(\beta_j) = (r_{-j} - \beta_j x_j)^T(r_{-j} - \beta_j x_j) + \lambda \vert \beta_j \vert + \text{const}$$

Expanding:

$$f(\beta_j) = r_{-j}^T r_{-j} - 2 \beta_j x_j^T r_{-j} + \beta_j^2 x_j^T x_j + \lambda \vert \beta_j \vert + C$$

**Step 2 — Sub-differentiate.** The derivative of $\vert \beta_j \vert$ does not exist at 0, so we use the sub-differential:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial \beta_j} = -2 x_j^T r_{-j} + 2 \beta_j (x_j^T x_j) + \lambda \cdot s_j = 0$$

where $s_j = \text{sign}(\beta_j)$ if $\beta_j \ne 0$, and $s_j \in [-1, 1]$ if $\beta_j = 0$.

**Step 3 — Standardize** so that $x_j^T x_j = 1$:

$$2 \beta_j = 2 x_j^T r_{-j} - \lambda s_j$$

**Step 4 — Solve case by case.**

*Case 1:* Assume $\hat{\beta}_j > 0$ ($s_j = +1$). Then $\hat{\beta}_j = x_j^T r_{-j} - \lambda/2$. For consistency, we need $x_j^T r_{-j} > \lambda/2$.

*Case 2:* Assume $\hat{\beta}_j < 0$ ($s_j = -1$). Then $\hat{\beta}_j = x_j^T r_{-j} + \lambda/2$. For consistency, we need $x_j^T r_{-j} < -\lambda/2$.

*Case 3:* $\hat{\beta}_j = 0$. The sub-gradient condition requires the residual to balance the L1 sub-differential: $\big\vert x_j^T r_{-j} \big\vert \le \lambda/2$.

**Step 5 — Unified soft-thresholding operator.** Re-defining $\lambda$ to absorb the factor of 2 (standard convention), and letting $z = x_j^T r_{-j}$:

$$\boxed{\;\hat{\beta}_j = S(z, \lambda) = \text{sign}(z) \cdot \max\!\left( \big\vert z \big\vert - \lambda, \; 0 \right)\;}$$

**Step 6 — Demonstration of sparsity.** If $\vert z \vert \le \lambda$, then $\max(\vert z \vert - \lambda, 0) = 0$, hence $\hat{\beta}_j = 0$. The coefficient is **eliminated from the model exactly** — not just shrunk. This is the mathematical heart of LASSO's feature-selection capability. $\blacksquare$

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**Q.B.** (a) **Explain the geometric interpretation of LASSO. With the help of a 2D diagram, show why LASSO yields sparse solutions while Ridge does not. Also discuss the bias-variance trade-off for LASSO. (7 marks)**

(b) **Describe the coordinate descent algorithm for solving the LASSO problem. How is the regularization parameter $\lambda$ chosen in practice? Discuss $K$-fold cross-validation. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Geometric Interpretation & Bias-Variance

**Geometric setup.** Consider the RSS function $J(\beta) = \Vert y - X\beta \Vert_2^2$. Its level sets $\{ \beta : J(\beta) \le c \}$ are **ellipsoids** in $\mathbb{R}^p$, centered at the OLS solution $\hat{\beta}^{\text{OLS}}$ (when unique). The LASSO constraint region is the **L1 ball**:

$$\mathcal{B}_1(t) = \left\{ \beta \in \mathbb{R}^p : \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert \beta_j \vert \le t \right\}$$

In 2D ($p = 2$), this ball is a **diamond** with vertices on the coordinate axes at $(\pm t, 0)$ and $(0, \pm t)$.

**The LASSO solution** is the first point of contact between the smallest ellipsoid (smallest $c$) that intersects $\mathcal{B}_1(t)$ and the diamond. As we shrink the RSS ellipsoid by moving the budget $t$ inward from $\infty$, the contact point traces a path.

**Why sparsity?** For generic positions of the OLS center, the diamond's **sharp corner** on the $\beta_1$-axis is the most likely point of first contact, because corners protrude farther into the ellipsoid than smooth sides. At a corner, one coordinate (here $\beta_2$) is **exactly zero**. Ridge, whose constraint is a circle (smooth, no corners), almost always touches the ellipse at a point with both coordinates strictly non-zero. Hence LASSO produces sparse solutions; Ridge does not.

**Diagram (describe in exam):** Draw concentric ellipses centered at the OLS solution, expanding outward. Draw the diamond (LASSO) and circle (Ridge) of equal area. Mark:
- Circle (Ridge) tangent to the **innermost** ellipse at a point with **both** coordinates non-zero.
- Diamond (LASSO) tangent to the **innermost** ellipse at a **vertex** on an axis, with one coordinate **equal to zero**.

**Bias-variance trade-off.** LASSO introduces a **shrinkage bias** because the constraint pulls coefficients away from their OLS values. In exchange, the **variance** of the estimator is **reduced** (sometimes substantially) because the model is no longer free to overfit. The expected test error decomposes as:

$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[(y_0 - \hat{f}(x_0))^2\right] = \underbrace{\text{Bias}^2(\hat{f}(x_0))}_{\text{increases with } \lambda} + \underbrace{\text{Var}(\hat{f}(x_0))}_{\text{decreases with } \lambda} + \sigma^2$$

There is a **U-shaped curve** of test error vs. $\lambda$. Cross-validation finds the bottom of this U, balancing bias and variance optimally.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Coordinate Descent & $\lambda$ Selection

**Coordinate Descent Algorithm for LASSO:**

1. **Standardize** the predictors: $x_j \leftarrow (x_j - \bar{x}_j) / \sqrt{\sum_i (x_{ij} - \bar{x}_j)^2}$, and center the response $y \leftarrow y - \bar{y}$.
2. **Initialize** $\hat{\beta} = (0, 0, \ldots, 0)$.
3. **Repeat until convergence** (e.g., $\max_j \vert \hat{\beta}_j^{\text{new}} - \hat{\beta}_j^{\text{old}} \vert < 10^{-6}$):
   - For each $j = 1, 2, \ldots, p$:
     - Compute the partial residual: $r_{-j} = y - \sum_{k \ne j} \hat{\beta}_k x_k$.
     - Compute $z_j = x_j^T r_{-j}$ (the simple least-squares coefficient if all other $\beta_k$ were fixed).
     - Apply soft-thresholding: $\hat{\beta}_j \leftarrow S(z_j, \lambda) = \text{sign}(z_j) \cdot \max(\vert z_j \vert - \lambda, 0)$.
4. **Return** the converged sparse coefficient vector $\hat{\beta}$.

**Complexity:** Each full cycle through all $p$ features costs $O(np)$. Total cost is $O(np \cdot k)$ for $k$ outer iterations until convergence. This is **dramatically cheaper** than generic convex QP solvers for large $p$.

**Why coordinate descent works:** The LASSO objective is **convex and separable** in the coordinates once others are fixed. Each single-coordinate minimization has a **closed-form solution** (soft-thresholding), so no inner optimization is needed. Convergence to the global optimum is guaranteed because the objective is convex (no local minima).

**Selection of $\lambda$ — $K$-Fold Cross-Validation Protocol:**

1. **Construct** a grid of $\lambda$ values, typically on a logarithmic scale: $\lambda_k = \lambda_{\max} \cdot r^k$ for $k = 0, 1, \ldots, K$, with $r = 0.01$ or $0.001$ and $\lambda_{\max} = \max_j \vert x_j^T y \vert$.
2. **Randomly partition** the training data into $K$ equal-sized folds.
3. **For each** $\lambda_k$:
   - For each fold $f = 1, \ldots, K$:
     - Fit LASSO on the $K - 1$ training folds.
     - Compute mean-squared error on the held-out fold $f$.
   - Average across folds to get $\text{CV}(\lambda_k) = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{f=1}^{K} \text{MSE}_f(\lambda_k)$.
4. **Select** $\lambda^{\star} = \arg\min_k \text{CV}(\lambda_k)$.
5. **Refit** the LASSO on the **full** training set using $\lambda^{\star}$.

**Standard error rule:** Often, a more **parsimonious** $\lambda$ is chosen by the **1-SE rule**: pick the largest $\lambda$ whose CV error is within one standard error of the minimum. This produces a sparser, simpler model with comparable test error.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU students make (and where they lose marks):**
>
> 1. **Confusing the penalty term.** LASSO uses $\sum \vert \beta_j \vert$ (L1), NOT $\sum \beta_j^2$ (L2 — Ridge). Many students swap these and write Ridge's formula as LASSO's, losing 2–3 marks immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting the sub-differential.** Because $\vert \beta_j \vert$ is non-differentiable at 0, the derivation of soft-thresholding **must** use the sub-differential with the three cases ($\beta_j > 0$, $\beta_j < 0$, $\beta_j = 0$). Students who write a single "derivative = 0" equation lose the 2 marks allocated to the case analysis.
> 3. **Not stating the symmetry $L(-t) = -L(t)$** when writing the soft-thresholding operator. The correct form is $\text{sign}(z) \cdot \max(\vert z \vert - \lambda, 0)$, not simply $\max(z - \lambda, 0)$.
> 4. **Skipping the KKT condition.** KTU expects explicit mention of the KKT / optimality conditions. The sparsity criterion $\vert x_j^T r_{-j} \vert \le \lambda$ is a **must-state** for full marks.
> 5. **Confusing "sparsity" with "small coefficients."** Sparsity means **exactly zero**, not just small. Ridge coefficients are small but never zero — that is not sparsity.
> 6. **Omitting the intercept treatment.** The intercept $\beta_0$ is **not penalized** in LASSO. Writing the L1 penalty starting from $j = 0$ is a common error.
> 7. **Not specifying the assumption $x_j^T x_j = 1$.** Soft-thresholding's clean form requires standardized predictors. Mention this explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for LASSO — Module 2**
>
> ✅ LASSO = **L1-penalized** least squares: $\min \Vert y - X\beta \Vert_2^2 + \lambda \sum \vert \beta_j \vert$.
> ✅ Penalty form $\iff$ constraint form via **Lagrangian duality** (one-to-one mapping between $\lambda$ and $t$).
> ✅ L1 norm = sum of absolute values; L2 norm = square root of sum of squares.
> ✅ Geometric shape of L1 constraint = **diamond (corners on axes)**; L2 = **circle (smooth)**.
> ✅ LASSO is **sparse** because ellipsoids touch the diamond at **corners**, where one coordinate is zero.
> ✅ **Soft-thresholding operator** $S(z, \lambda) = \text{sign}(z) \cdot \max(\vert z \vert - \lambda, 0)$ is the coordinate update.
> ✅ LASSO ↔ **Laplace (double-exponential) prior** on $\beta_j$; Ridge ↔ Gaussian prior.
> ✅ KKT / sparsity condition: $\vert x_j^T r_{-j} \vert \le \lambda \;\Rightarrow\; \hat{\beta}_j = 0$.
> ✅ **Sub-gradient** of $\vert \beta_j \vert$ is $-1$ (if neg), $[-1, +1]$ (at 0), $+1$ (if pos).
> ✅ **Coordinate descent** is the standard solver: cyclically apply soft-thresholding per coordinate.
> ✅ $\lambda$ is selected by **$K$-fold cross-validation** (typically $K = 10$); use the **1-SE rule** for parsimony.
> ✅ LASSO introduces **bias** to **reduce variance**; total test MSE has a U-shape vs. $\lambda$.
> ✅ LASSO handles **$p > n$**; OLS does not. LASSO gives **interpretable** sparse models.
> ✅ LASSO picks **one** feature from a correlated group; **Elastic Net** picks the whole group.
> ✅ The intercept $\beta_0$ is **not penalized**.
> ✅ For KTU: always state the standardizing assumption $x_j^T x_j = 1$ when deriving soft-thresholding.
> ✅ LASSO = **L1**asso, lassoes coefficients **in** (shrinks) and **out** (zeros irrelevant ones).
> ✅ Applications: **genomics, finance, compressed sensing, NLP, sensor selection** — all benefit from LASSO's sparsity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ridge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT501 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ridge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ridge Regression** (also called **L2 Regularization** or **Tikhonov Regularization**) is a regularized variant of Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regression that modifies the cost function by adding a penalty term proportional to the **sum of the squares of the magnitude of the coefficients**. This penalty shrinks the coefficient estimates toward zero (but never exactly to zero), thereby reducing model variance, mitigating **multicollinearity**, and combating **overfitting**.

The formal KTU 2024 scheme definition states:

Ridge Regression minimizes the following penalized residual sum of squares:

$$J(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( y_i - \beta_0 - \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j x_{ij} \right)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j^2$$

Where:
- $\beta_j$ are the regression coefficients (weights)
- $\lambda \geq 0$ is the **tuning/regularization parameter** (also called **shrinkage parameter**)
- The second term $\lambda \sum \beta_j^2$ is the **L2 penalty term**
- Note: The intercept $\beta_0$ is **not** penalized (standard convention)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Mountain Climber with a Heavy Backpack"** ⛰️

Imagine an Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regression model as a mountain climber trying to find the lowest valley (minimum error). OLS freely walks down the slope without restriction — it can take huge, aggressive steps and may end up in a sharp, narrow valley that looks deep but is unstable (this is **overfitting** — high variance).

Now imagine giving that same climber a **heavy backpack connected by elastic cords to the summit**. The climber can still reach low ground, but the backpack's weight (the **L2 penalty**) pulls them back toward the origin (smaller coefficients). The result? The climber settles in a **broader, shallower, more stable valley** — the model is slightly biased (sacrifices some training accuracy) but has dramatically **lower variance** and **better generalization** on unseen data.

**Key Intuition Points:**
- **λ = 0** → backpack is weightless → behavior identical to OLS
- **λ = ∞** → backpack is impossibly heavy → all coefficients → 0
- **λ is a knob** you tune using **cross-validation**

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Notation |
|--------|---------|-------------------|
| **λ (lambda)** | Regularization parameter | Hyperparameter, λ ≥ 0 |
| **α (alpha)** | Alternative name for λ (scikit-learn) | Hyperparameter |
| **L2 norm** | $\vert\vert \beta \vert\vert_2^2 = \sum \beta_j^2$ | Squared L2 norm of coefficients |
| **RSS** | Residual Sum of Squares | $\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ |
| **df(λ)** | Effective degrees of freedom | $p - \text{trace}(H_\lambda)$ |

## 1.4 Why Ridge Regression? — Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Ridge Regression is introduced specifically to solve three core problems in OLS:
> 1. **Multicollinearity** — when predictor variables are highly correlated, $X^T X$ becomes near-singular (non-invertible)
> 2. **Overfitting** — when $p \approx n$ or $p > n$ (more features than samples)
> 3. **High variance** — coefficient estimates become unstable and sensitive to small data changes

By adding $\lambda I$ to $X^T X$, Ridge **guarantees invertibility** and stabilizes the solution.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The Ridge mechanism operates on these logical pillars:

### Pillar 1: The Penalized Cost Function
- Standard OLS minimizes: $RSS = \sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$
- Ridge adds a penalty: $J(\beta) = RSS + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j^2$
- The penalty **grows quadratically** with coefficient magnitude
- Large coefficients are "taxed" heavily → they shrink

### Pillar 2: The Shrinkage Effect
- Every coefficient is reduced in absolute magnitude: $\vert \hat{\beta}_j^{Ridge} \vert \leq \vert \hat{\beta}_j^{OLS} \vert$
- This is called **shrinkage** or **weight decay**
- Coefficients are biased toward zero, but **never reach exactly zero** (unlike Lasso)

### Pillar 3: The Bias-Variance Trade-off
- Ridge **increases bias** slightly (model is less flexible)
- Ridge **decreases variance** significantly (model is more stable)
- Net effect: **lower Mean Squared Error (MSE)** on test data
- Optimal λ is found where total MSE = Bias² + Variance + Irreducible Error is minimized

### Pillar 4: The Geometric Interpretation
- OLS solution = unconstrained minimum (a point in p-dimensional space)
- Ridge constraint region = sphere: $\sum \beta_j^2 \leq t$ (a ball)
- Ridge solution = **first point of contact** between the RSS contour (ellipsoid) and the constraint ball
- The L2 ball has **smooth, rounded edges** → solutions rarely sit exactly on axes → no exact zeros

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Save this table — it covers 90% of KTU exam numerical questions on Ridge Regression.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Key Insight |
|---|-------------------|------------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Ridge Cost Function | $J(\beta) = (y - X\beta)^T(y - X\beta) + \lambda \beta^T \beta$ | OLS RSS + λ × L2 norm squared |
| 2 | Closed-Form Solution | $\hat{\beta}_{Ridge} = (X^T X + \lambda I)^{-1} X^T y$ | Compare with OLS: $(X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$ |
| 3 | OLS Normal Equation | $\hat{\beta}_{OLS} = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$ | Special case of Ridge when λ = 0 |
| 4 | Penalty Term (L2) | $\lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j^2 = \lambda \vert\vert \beta \vert\vert_2^2$ | Quadratic penalty |
| 5 | Gradient (for Gradient Descent) | $\nabla J(\beta) = -2X^T(y - X\beta) + 2\lambda \beta$ | Used in iterative solvers |
| 6 | GD Update Rule | $\beta := \beta - \alpha(-2X^T(y - X\beta) + 2\lambda \beta)$ | α is learning rate |
| 7 | Eigen Decomposition Form | $\hat{\beta}_{Ridge} = \sum_{j=1}^{p} \frac{d_j^2}{d_j^2 + \lambda} \cdot \frac{\langle y, u_j \rangle}{d_j} u_j$ | Shows per-direction shrinkage |
| 8 | Shrinkage Factor | $\frac{d_j^2}{d_j^2 + \lambda}$ | Each principal direction shrinks by this factor |
| 9 | Effective DoF | $df(\lambda) = \sum_{j=1}^{p} \frac{d_j^2}{d_j^2 + \lambda}$ | Approaches 0 as λ → ∞ |
| 10 | Hat Matrix | $H_\lambda = X(X^T X + \lambda I)^{-1} X^T$ | Fitted values: $\hat{y} = H_\lambda y$ |
| 11 | Standardized Ridge | Use standardized X and y, then revert | Essential for fair penalty |
| 12 | Intercept Treatment | $\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \sum \beta_j \bar{x}_j$ | NOT penalized |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** The penalty term in Ridge **EXCLUDES the intercept** $\beta_0$. Penalizing the intercept would force predictions toward zero regardless of the mean of y.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications

Ridge Regression is widely deployed in:

- **Bioinformatics & Genomics**: When the number of genes (p) vastly exceeds sample size (n) — p >> n — Ridge provides stable gene-expression predictors.
- **Financial Econometrics**: Stock prediction with highly correlated macroeconomic indicators (multicollinearity is rampant).
- **Computer Vision (Image Processing)**: Inverse problems like image deblurring are solved using Tikhonov regularization (Ridge).
- **Signal Processing**: Noise filtering and inverse filtering in communications systems.
- **Recommendation Systems**: Matrix factorization with regularization to prevent overfitting user-item ratings.
- **Autonomous Systems**: Sensor fusion where multiple correlated sensors feed a regression model.

## 2.4 Selection of λ — Practical Strategy

The regularization parameter λ is selected using:
1. **k-Fold Cross-Validation** (most common — k = 5 or 10)
2. **Generalized Cross-Validation (GCV)** — approximation that avoids refitting
3. **AIC / BIC criteria** — information-theoretic balance
4. **Validation set approach** — hold out 20-30% of data

The λ that minimizes CV-error is chosen as optimal.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Closed-Form Solution of Ridge Regression

**Goal:** Derive $\hat{\beta}_{Ridge} = (X^T X + \lambda I)^{-1} X^T y$ from the cost function.

### Step 1: Express the Ridge Cost Function in Matrix Form

Starting from the scalar form:

$$J(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( y_i - \beta_0 - \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j x_{ij} \right)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j^2$$

In matrix notation (assuming data is centered, so we omit $\beta_0$ for the derivation, then re-introduce):

$$J(\beta) = (y - X\beta)^T (y - X\beta) + \lambda \beta^T \beta$$

**Explanation:** The first term is the squared Euclidean norm of the residual vector $(y - X\beta)$, and the second term is λ times the squared L2 norm of the coefficient vector.

### Step 2: Expand the Quadratic Term

Expand $(y - X\beta)^T(y - X\beta)$:

$$J(\beta) = y^T y - 2\beta^T X^T y + \beta^T X^T X \beta + \lambda \beta^T \beta$$

**Explanation:** Using the identity $(A - B)^T(A - B) = A^T A - A^T B - B^T A + B^T B$ with $A = y$ and $B = X\beta$, then noting $y^T X \beta = \beta^T X^T y$ (scalar transpose).

### Step 3: Take the Gradient with Respect to β

Differentiate $J(\beta)$ with respect to $\beta$, treating it as a vector:

$$\frac{\partial J(\beta)}{\partial \beta} = -2 X^T y + 2 X^T X \beta + 2 \lambda \beta$$

**Explanation:** 
- $\partial(y^T y)/\partial \beta = 0$ (constant)
- $\partial(-2\beta^T X^T y)/\partial \beta = -2 X^T y$
- $\partial(\beta^T X^T X \beta)/\partial \beta = 2 X^T X \beta$ (using $\partial(x^T A x)/\partial x = 2Ax$ for symmetric A)
- $\partial(\lambda \beta^T \beta)/\partial \beta = 2 \lambda \beta$

### Step 4: Set the Gradient Equal to Zero (First-Order Optimality Condition)

$$-2 X^T y + 2 X^T X \beta + 2 \lambda \beta = 0$$

Divide both sides by 2:

$$- X^T y + X^T X \beta + \lambda \beta = 0$$

### Step 5: Rearrange to Isolate β

$$X^T X \beta + \lambda \beta = X^T y$$

Factor out β on the left side:

$$(X^T X + \lambda I) \beta = X^T y$$

### Step 6: Solve for β by Left-Multiplying by the Inverse

Pre-multiply both sides by $(X^T X + \lambda I)^{-1}$:

$$(X^T X + \lambda I)^{-1} (X^T X + \lambda I) \beta = (X^T X + \lambda I)^{-1} X^T y$$

The left side simplifies to $I \beta = \beta$:

$$\boxed{\hat{\beta}_{Ridge} = (X^T X + \lambda I)^{-1} X^T y}$$

**Explanation:** The matrix $(X^T X + \lambda I)$ is **always invertible** when λ > 0, even if $X^T X$ is singular. This is the core mathematical reason Ridge solves multicollinearity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Gradient Descent Update Rule for Ridge

**Goal:** Derive the iterative update rule for Ridge using Gradient Descent.

### Step 1: Recall the Cost Function

$$J(\beta) = (y - X\beta)^T (y - X\beta) + \lambda \beta^T \beta$$

### Step 2: Compute the Gradient (already derived above)

$$\nabla J(\beta) = -2 X^T y + 2 X^T X \beta + 2 \lambda \beta = -2 X^T(y - X\beta) + 2 \lambda \beta$$

### Step 3: Factor the Gradient

$$\nabla J(\beta) = 2 \left[ X^T(X\beta - y) + \lambda \beta \right]$$

### Step 4: Apply the Gradient Descent Update Rule

The general GD update rule is:

$$\beta^{(t+1)} = \beta^{(t)} - \eta \cdot \nabla J(\beta^{(t)})$$

where $\eta$ is the learning rate. Substituting:

$$\beta^{(t+1)} = \beta^{(t)} - \eta \cdot 2 \left[ X^T(X\beta^{(t)} - y) + \lambda \beta^{(t)} \right]$$

Simplify (absorbing the 2 into η):

$$\beta^{(t+1)} = \beta^{(t)} - \eta \left[ X^T(X\beta^{(t)} - y) + \lambda \beta^{(t)} \right]$$

### Step 5: Final Update Form (often seen in ML libraries)

$$\boxed{\beta^{(t+1)} = (1 - 2\eta \lambda) \beta^{(t)} + \eta X^T(y - X\beta^{(t)})}$$

**Explanation:** The factor $(1 - 2\eta \lambda)$ is the **weight decay** term — at each step, coefficients are scaled down by this factor, which is why Ridge is also called "**weight decay**" in deep learning literature.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Eigen Decomposition Form (Singular Value Interpretation)

**Goal:** Show how Ridge shrinks each principal component direction independently.

### Step 1: Apply SVD to X

Let the Singular Value Decomposition of X be:

$$X = U D V^T$$

where:
- $U$ is $n \times p$ with orthonormal columns
- $D$ is $p \times p$ diagonal with singular values $d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_p$
- $V$ is $p \times p$ orthogonal

### Step 2: Express the OLS Solution in SVD Form

The OLS solution is:

$$\hat{\beta}_{OLS} = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y = V D^{-1} U^T y$$

### Step 3: Express the Ridge Solution in SVD Form

Substituting $X^T X = V D^2 V^T$ into the Ridge solution:

$$\hat{\beta}_{Ridge} = (V D^2 V^T + \lambda I)^{-1} V D U^T y$$

Since $V$ is orthogonal, $V^T V = I$, so:

$$\hat{\beta}_{Ridge} = V (D^2 + \lambda I)^{-1} V^T V D U^T y = V (D^2 + \lambda I)^{-1} D U^T y$$

### Step 4: Write the Per-Component Form

Since $(D^2 + \lambda I)^{-1} D$ is diagonal with entries $\frac{d_j}{d_j^2 + \lambda}$:

$$\boxed{\hat{\beta}_{Ridge} = \sum_{j=1}^{p} \frac{d_j^2}{d_j^2 + \lambda} \cdot \frac{\langle y, u_j \rangle}{d_j} v_j}$$

**Explanation:** The factor $\frac{d_j^2}{d_j^2 + \lambda}$ is the **shrinkage factor** for the j-th principal direction:
- If $d_j^2 \gg \lambda$ (high-variance direction): factor → 1 → minimal shrinkage
- If $d_j^2 \ll \lambda$ (low-variance direction): factor → 0 → strong shrinkage
- This means Ridge **selectively shrinks unstable directions** more than stable ones — a beautiful property!

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison: OLS vs Ridge

| Property | OLS (λ = 0) | Ridge (λ > 0) |
|----------|-------------|----------------|
| Solution | $(X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$ | $(X^T X + \lambda I)^{-1} X^T y$ |
| Invertibility | Requires full-rank X | **Always invertible** for λ > 0 |
| Bias | Unbiased | **Biased** (introduces shrinkage bias) |
| Variance | High (unstable) | **Lower** (stabilized) |
| Coefficients | Can be large | Shrunk toward 0 |
| Multicollinearity | Fails | **Handled** |
| Feature selection | No | No (use Lasso instead) |
| p > n case | Singular system | **Solvable** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Ridge Regression Workflow — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Raw Dataset X, y"] --> B["Train-Test Split 80-20"]
    B --> C["Standardize Features Mean=0, Std=1"]
    C --> D["Select Lambda Grid 0.001 to 1000"]
    D --> E["K-Fold Cross Validation k=5 or 10"]
    E --> F["Fit Ridge Model for each Lambda"]
    F --> G["Compute CV Mean Squared Error"]
    G --> H{"Optimal Lambda Found?"}
    H -- No --> D
    H -- Yes --> I["Retrain on Full Training Data"]
    I --> J["Predict on Test Set"]
    J --> K["Evaluate RMSE, R-squared, MAE"]
    K --> L["Final Ridge Model Deployed"]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style L fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style H fill:#c62828,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style D fill:#f57c00,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Geometric Interpretation — Constraint Region

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["RSS Contour Ellipsoids Centered at OLS"] --> C["Intersection Point = Ridge Solution"]
    B["L2 Constraint Sphere Sum of beta squared less than t"] --> C
    C --> D["Shrunk Coefficients Closer to Origin"]
    C --> E["No Exact Zeros Smooth Boundary"]
    C --> F["Stable, Biased Estimate"]

    style A fill:#1565c0,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style B fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style C fill:#c62828,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Bias-Variance Trade-off Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0["Lambda = 0"] --> HVAR["High Variance, Low Bias OLS Overfits"]
    LOP["Lambda = Lambda Optimal"] --> BAL["Balanced Bias-Variance Minimum Test Error"]
    LINF["Lambda -> Infinity"] --> HB["High Bias, Low Variance Underfits"]

    HVAR --> TOT1["High Test MSE"]
    BAL --> TOT2["Minimum Test MSE"]
    HB --> TOT3["High Test MSE"]

    style LOP fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style BAL fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style TOT2 fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style HVAR fill:#c62828,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style HB fill:#c62828,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style TOT1 fill:#c62828,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style TOT3 fill:#c62828,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Ridge Coefficient Path Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Lambda = 0 All Coeffs at OLS values"] --> B["Lambda Increases"]
    B --> C["Coefficients Shrink Smoothly"]
    C --> D["Approach But Never Equal Zero"]
    D --> E["Lambda = Infinity All Coeffs Approaching Zero"]
    E --> F["Intercept Beta_0 Unchanged Always"]

    style A fill:#0d47a1,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style F fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style F fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.5 Mathematical Architecture — Derivation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Ridge Cost Function J of beta"] --> B["Matrix Formulation"]
    B --> C["Compute Gradient dJ by d beta"]
    C --> D["Set to Zero First Order Condition"]
    D --> E["Rearrange XTX plus lambda I times beta equals XTy"]
    E --> F["Multiply by Inverse"]
    F --> G["Closed Form Solution Beta Ridge"]

    G --> H["Eigen Decomposition Form"]
    G --> I["Gradient Descent Iterative Form"]
    G --> J["Standardized Form Practical"]

    style A fill:#1a237e,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style G fill:#1b5e20,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style H fill:#e65100,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style I fill:#e65100,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style J fill:#e65100,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define Ridge Regression. Why is it preferred over Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) in the presence of multicollinearity?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Ridge Regression is a regularized regression technique that adds an L2 penalty term (proportional to the sum of squared coefficients) to the OLS cost function:

$$J(\beta) = \sum (y_i - X\beta)^2 + \lambda \sum \beta_j^2$$

It is preferred over OLS in the presence of multicollinearity because:
1. In OLS, the matrix $X^T X$ becomes **near-singular** (non-invertible) when predictors are highly correlated, leading to unstable coefficient estimates.
2. Ridge adds $\lambda I$ to $X^T X$, **guaranteeing invertibility** even with perfect multicollinearity.
3. The penalty shrinks coefficient magnitudes, **reducing variance** at the cost of slight bias — a favorable trade-off under multicollinearity.

> **[Mark Allocation: 1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark explanation of multicollinearity handling]**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Q: Explain the role of the regularization parameter λ in Ridge Regression. What happens when λ = 0 and λ → ∞?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The regularization parameter λ controls the **strength of the penalty** applied to the magnitude of coefficients.

- **When λ = 0:** The penalty term vanishes, and Ridge reduces to **ordinary OLS regression**. The model has no shrinkage and may overfit.

- **When λ → ∞:** The penalty dominates the cost function. To minimize $J(\beta)$, all coefficients are forced toward **zero** (but never exactly zero), leaving only the intercept. The model underfits severely.

- **Intermediate λ:** Produces a trade-off between bias and variance. Optimal λ is selected via **cross-validation** to minimize test error.

> **[Mark Allocation: 1 mark role + 1 mark λ=0 case + 1 mark λ=∞ case]**

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**Q: (a)** Derive the closed-form solution for the Ridge Regression coefficients. Show all steps clearly. State the geometric interpretation of the constraint region. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** Consider a dataset with two features and the following design matrix and target vector (already centered):

$$X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 \\ 3 & 6 \end{bmatrix}, \quad y = \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 9 \\ 14 \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute the OLS estimate and the Ridge estimate for λ = 1. Comment on the effect of regularization. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A (a):

**Step 1:** State the Ridge cost function in matrix form:

$$J(\beta) = (y - X\beta)^T (y - X\beta) + \lambda \beta^T \beta$$

**Step 2:** Expand the quadratic term:

$$J(\beta) = y^T y - 2\beta^T X^T y + \beta^T X^T X \beta + \lambda \beta^T \beta$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate with respect to β and equate to zero:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta} = -2 X^T y + 2 X^T X \beta + 2 \lambda \beta = 0$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange to isolate β:

$$(X^T X + \lambda I)\beta = X^T y$$

**Step 5:** Solve by left-multiplying by the inverse:

$$\hat{\beta}_{Ridge} = (X^T X + \lambda I)^{-1} X^T y$$

**Geometric Interpretation (2 marks):** The L2 constraint defines a spherical region $\sum \beta_j^2 \leq t$ (a ball) in coefficient space. The Ridge solution is the **first point of tangency** between the RSS contour ellipsoids (centered at the OLS solution) and the L2 constraint ball. Since the ball has smooth, curved boundaries with no corners on the axes, coefficients are shrunk but **never reach exactly zero**.

---

#### Solution to Question A (b):

**Step 1: Compute $X^T X$**

$$X^T X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & 4 & 6 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 \\ 3 & 6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1+4+9 & 2+8+18 \\ 2+8+18 & 4+16+36 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 14 & 28 \\ 28 & 56 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Compute $X^T y$**

$$X^T y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & 4 & 6 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 9 \\ 14 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 + 18 + 42 \\ 10 + 36 + 84 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 65 \\ 130 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Check OLS — note that $X^T X = \begin{bmatrix} 14 & 28 \\ 28 & 56 \end{bmatrix}$ has determinant $= 14 \times 56 - 28 \times 28 = 784 - 784 = 0$ (singular!)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students will try to compute OLS here and fail because $X^T X$ is **singular** (column 2 = 2 × column 1, perfect multicollinearity). This is exactly the scenario where Ridge becomes essential! OLS is **not computable** in this dataset.

**Step 4: Compute Ridge estimate with λ = 1**

$$X^T X + \lambda I = \begin{bmatrix} 14 & 28 \\ 28 & 56 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 15 & 28 \\ 28 & 57 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Compute the inverse of the augmented matrix**

$$\det(X^T X + \lambda I) = 15 \times 57 - 28 \times 28 = 855 - 784 = 71$$

$$(X^T X + \lambda I)^{-1} = \frac{1}{71}\begin{bmatrix} 57 & -28 \\ -28 & 15 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6: Compute the Ridge coefficients**

$$\hat{\beta}_{Ridge} = \frac{1}{71}\begin{bmatrix} 57 & -28 \\ -28 & 15 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 65 \\ 130 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \frac{1}{71}\begin{bmatrix} 57 \times 65 - 28 \times 130 \\ -28 \times 65 + 15 \times 130 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{71}\begin{bmatrix} 3705 - 3640 \\ -1820 + 1950 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{71}\begin{bmatrix} 65 \\ 130 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\boxed{\hat{\beta}_{Ridge} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.915 \\ 1.831 \end{bmatrix}}$$

**Comment on Regularization Effect (2 marks):**
- OLS is **undefined** (singular matrix — cannot invert).
- Ridge **rescues** the problem by adding λI, making the matrix invertible.
- The Ridge solution is **well-defined and finite**.
- The coefficients are shrunk and stable, avoiding the blow-up that OLS would have produced.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the Bias-Variance trade-off in the context of Ridge Regression. Sketch the relationship between λ and test MSE, bias, and variance. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** For a dataset with n = 100 samples and p = 50 features, the eigenvalues of $X^T X$ are $d_1^2 = 100, d_2^2 = 80, \ldots, d_{50}^2 = 1$ (decreasing sequence). Compute the shrinkage factors for directions j = 1, 10, 30, 50 when λ = 10. Comment on which directions shrink the most. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B (a):

The total expected prediction error (test MSE) decomposes as:

$$E[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2] = \text{Bias}^2(\hat{f}(x)) + \text{Var}(\hat{f}(x)) + \sigma^2$$

Where $\sigma^2$ is the irreducible error.

**OLS (λ = 0):** Low bias, **high variance** → model fits training data too closely, capturing noise → **overfitting** → high test MSE.

**Ridge (λ > 0):**
- **Bias increases** — coefficients are shrunk away from their true values.
- **Variance decreases** — coefficients are more stable across different training sets.
- **Net effect:** Test MSE is **lower** than OLS for moderate λ.

**As λ → ∞:** Bias becomes very high (model is too simple), variance → 0, but model **underfits** — test MSE rises again.

**Optimal λ:** The point where the Bias² + Variance curve reaches its **global minimum**.

The typical sketch (textual description since diagrams above are in Mermaid):
- Test MSE is a **U-shaped curve** with minimum at optimal λ
- Bias² curve is **monotonically increasing**
- Variance curve is **monotonically decreasing**

---

#### Solution to Question B (b):

The shrinkage factor for the j-th principal direction is:

$$s_j = \frac{d_j^2}{d_j^2 + \lambda}$$

Given λ = 10, compute for j = 1, 10, 30, 50.

The eigenvalues follow a decreasing pattern. Let us model them as $d_j^2 = 101 - j$ (a representative decreasing sequence). Then:

- For **j = 1:** $d_1^2 = 100$

$$s_1 = \frac{100}{100 + 10} = \frac{100}{110} = 0.909$$

- For **j = 10:** $d_{10}^2 = 91$

$$s_{10} = \frac{91}{91 + 10} = \frac{91}{101} = 0.901$$

- For **j = 30:** $d_{30}^2 = 71$

$$s_{30} = \frac{71}{71 + 10} = \frac{71}{81} = 0.877$$

- For **j = 50:** $d_{50}^2 = 51$

$$s_{50} = \frac{51}{51 + 10} = \frac{51}{61} = 0.836$$

| Direction j | Eigenvalue $d_j^2$ | Shrinkage Factor $s_j$ | % Retained |
|-------------|--------------------|------------------------|------------|
| 1 | 100 | 0.909 | 90.9% |
| 10 | 91 | 0.901 | 90.1% |
| 30 | 71 | 0.877 | 87.7% |
| 50 | 51 | 0.836 | 83.6% |

**Comment (2 marks):**
- **High-variance directions** (large $d_j^2$, e.g., j = 1) are shrunk the **least** — they retain ~91% of their information.
- **Low-variance directions** (small $d_j^2$, e.g., j = 50) are shrunk the **most** — only ~84% retained.
- Ridge **adaptively stabilizes** the noisy, low-variance components while preserving the informative, high-variance ones — this is the elegance of L2 regularization.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Forgetting to exclude the intercept** $\beta_0$ from the penalty → lose 1-2 marks.
> 2. **Not stating units/dimensions** of λ or coefficients in numericals → lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing Ridge with Lasso** (L1) — Ridge never zeros coefficients, Lasso does → critical conceptual error.
> 4. **Skipping matrix non-singularity check** in numericals — must verify $X^T X + \lambda I$ is invertible.
> 5. **Writing $(X^T X)^{-1}$ in formula instead of $(X^T X + \lambda I)^{-1}$** → marks deducted for missing the regularization term.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Ridge Regression**

✅ **Definition:** Ridge Regression = OLS + L2 penalty on coefficients (sum of squares of weights)

✅ **Cost Function:** $J(\beta) = RSS + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j^2$ (intercept not penalized)

✅ **Closed-Form Solution:** $\hat{\beta}_{Ridge} = (X^T X + \lambda I)^{-1} X^T y$ — always exists for λ > 0

✅ **OLS Comparison:** OLS is Ridge with λ = 0

✅ **Geometric Form:** L2 ball (sphere) in coefficient space; Ridge = tangency point with RSS ellipsoid

✅ **SVD Form:** $\hat{\beta}_{Ridge} = \sum_{j} \frac{d_j^2}{d_j^2 + \lambda} \cdot \frac{u_j^T y}{d_j} v_j$

✅ **Shrinkage:** Coefficients shrunk toward zero, **never exactly zero** (key difference from Lasso)

✅ **Hyperparameter λ:** Selected via cross-validation; λ = 0 → OLS; λ → ∞ → null model

✅ **Standardization:** Features **must be standardized** before applying Ridge (so penalty is fair across scales)

✅ **Handles:** Multicollinearity, p > n cases, overfitting, high variance

✅ **Doesn't Handle:** Feature selection (use Lasso for that), non-linear patterns (use polynomial features or kernel methods)

✅ **Bias-Variance Trade-off:** Ridge increases bias slightly, decreases variance significantly → net lower test MSE

✅ **Weight Decay Connection:** The GD update form $\beta := (1 - 2\eta\lambda)\beta + \eta X^T(y - X\beta)$ is identical to **L2 weight decay** in deep learning

✅ **Intercept:** $\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \sum \beta_j \bar{x}_j$ (estimated separately, not penalized)

✅ **Effective Degrees of Freedom:** $df(\lambda) = \sum_j \frac{d_j^2}{d_j^2 + \lambda}$ — decreases as λ increases

✅ **Practical Use:** Standard preprocessing = mean-center + unit-variance scale; then fit; then revert intercept

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Learning with Trees - Decision Trees
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT501 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Learning with Trees - Decision Trees

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Decision Tree** is a non-parametric, supervised machine learning algorithm used for both classification and regression tasks. It represents a hierarchical, tree-structured model of decisions and their possible consequences, where each internal node represents a "test" on an attribute, each branch represents the outcome of that test, and each leaf node represents a class label (in classification) or a continuous value (in regression).

Mathematically, a decision tree partitions the feature space $\mathcal{X}$ into a set of axis-aligned rectangular regions $\{R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_J\}$, and assigns a prediction (class or value) to each region. The tree is built using a top-down, **greedy recursive partitioning** strategy known as **recursive binary splitting** (in CART) or **multi-way splitting** (in ID3/C4.5).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Decision trees form the foundation of ensemble methods (Random Forests, Gradient Boosted Trees). Mastering the impurity measures—**Entropy**, **Gini Index**, and **Information Gain**—is the primary learning outcome for Module 2.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "20 Questions" Game

Imagine you are playing the classic guessing game *"20 Questions"* with a friend who has secretly thought of an animal. You are allowed only yes/no questions:

- **Q1:** "Is it a mammal?" → **Yes** (you eliminate birds, fish, reptiles)
- **Q2:** "Does it live on land?" → **No** (you narrow it to dolphins, whales, sea otters)
- **Q3:** "Is it larger than a human?" → **Yes** (you arrive at "Whale!")

This is *exactly* how a decision tree works. Each question is a **decision node** (split), each answer routes you down a **branch**, and the final guess is the **leaf node**. The goal of the learning algorithm is to construct the *most informative sequence of questions* — questions that maximally reduce uncertainty.

## 1.3 Why Decision Trees Matter in Engineering

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Medical Diagnosis** | Predicting disease from symptoms |
| **Credit Risk Scoring** | Loan approval classification |
| **Fault Detection** | Industrial sensor-based diagnosis |
| **Remote Sensing** | Land-cover classification from satellite data |
| **Bio-informatics** | Gene expression classification |

> [!NOTE]
> Decision trees are highly valued in KTU-style problems because they are **interpretable** (white-box models), require **minimal data preprocessing** (no feature scaling), and can handle **both numerical and categorical** data.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Glossary

- **Root Node**: The topmost node containing the entire dataset; performs the first split.
- **Internal Node**: A node that performs a test on an attribute.
- **Leaf/Terminal Node**: A node that holds a class label (no further splitting).
- **Branch/Edge**: A path corresponding to one outcome of a test.
- **Splitting Criterion**: A metric (Entropy, Gini, etc.) used to choose the best attribute.
- **Pruning**: The process of removing subtrees to prevent overfitting.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Decision Tree Learning Framework

A decision tree is constructed by recursively partitioning the training data. The learning algorithm must address three fundamental questions:

1. **Which attribute to split on at each node?** (The *splitting criterion*)
2. **When should splitting stop?** (The *stopping condition*)
3. **How to assign class labels to leaves?** (The *leaf prediction rule*)

### 2.1.1 The Greedy, Top-Down Induction Paradigm

The most common family of algorithms—**ID3**, **C4.5**, and **CART**—use a *greedy* approach:

> At each node, choose the attribute that provides the **largest reduction in impurity** (or equivalently, the **largest information gain**), then recurse on the resulting child nodes.

This is *greedy* because it makes the locally optimal choice at each step without backtracking. Greedy choices do not always yield a globally optimal tree, but they are computationally tractable.

## 2.2 Impurity Measures — The Mathematical Core

### 2.2.1 Shannon Entropy (used in ID3 / C4.5)

For a dataset $S$ with $C$ classes and class probabilities $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_C$:

$$H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i \log_2(p_i)$$

**Boundary Conditions:**
- $H(S) = 0$ when the dataset is **pure** (all instances belong to one class).
- $H(S) = \log_2 C$ when classes are **uniformly distributed** (maximum disorder).

### 2.2.2 Gini Index (used in CART)

$$Gini(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i^2$$

**Boundary Conditions:**
- $Gini(S) = 0$ for a pure node.
- $Gini(S)$ is maximized when classes are equally distributed; for $C=2$, the maximum is $0.5$.

### 2.2.3 Classification Error

$$Error(S) = 1 - \max_i p_i$$

This is rarely used for splitting (insensitive to changes) but useful for pruning decisions.

## 2.3 Information Gain and Gain Ratio

### 2.3.1 Information Gain (IG)

Information Gain is the **reduction in entropy** achieved by partitioning $S$ on attribute $A$:

$$IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in Values(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$$

where $S_v$ is the subset of $S$ where $A = v$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ID3 Algorithm's Splitting Rule:** Select the attribute $A^*$ that maximizes $IG(S, A)$.

### 2.3.2 The Bias Problem with Information Gain

$IG$ is **biased toward attributes with many values**. For example, an attribute like "Date of Birth" would have near-zero entropy after splitting but is useless for generalization. C4.5 addresses this via **Gain Ratio**.

### 2.3.3 Split Information (Intrinsic Value)

$$SI(S, A) = -\sum_{v \in Values(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2\!\left(\frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}\right)$$

### 2.3.4 Gain Ratio (used in C4.5)

$$GR(S, A) = \frac{IG(S, A)}{SI(S, A)}$$

This **normalizes** information gain by the information content of the split itself. Attributes with many values (high $SI$) are penalized.

## 2.4 Gini Gain (CART Criterion)

$$GGini(S, A) = Gini(S) - \sum_{v \in Values(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} Gini(S_v)$$

CART selects the attribute maximizing $GGini$, equivalent to minimizing the **weighted Gini of children**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Metric | Formula | Range | Used By | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Entropy** | $-\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $[0,\; \log_2 C]$ | ID3, C4.5 | Log base 2 |
| **Gini Index** | $1 - \sum p_i^2$ | $[0,\; 1 - \tfrac{1}{C}]$ | CART | Computationally cheaper |
| **Class Error** | $1 - \max_i p_i$ | $[0,\; 1 - \tfrac{1}{C}]$ | Pruning | Less sensitive |
| **Information Gain** | $H(S) - \sum \tfrac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | $[0,\; H(S)]$ | ID3 | Biased to multi-valued attrs |
| **Split Info** | $-\sum \tfrac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \tfrac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ | $[0,\; \log_2 \vert S \vert]$ | C4.5 | Intrinsic split value |
| **Gain Ratio** | $\tfrac{IG(S,A)}{SI(S,A)}$ | $[0,\; 1]$ | C4.5 | Normalized IG |
| **Gini Gain** | $Gini(S) - \sum \tfrac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} Gini(S_v)$ | $[0,\; Gini(S)]$ | CART | Drop in impurity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These impurity metrics are not exclusive to trees — they underpin **Random Forest feature importance** (Mean Decrease in Gini / Permutation Importance) and are foundational in **XGBoost / LightGBM** as well. Mastering them here pays dividends across the ML stack.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Building a Decision Tree with ID3

Consider the classic **"Play Tennis"** dataset with 14 instances, 4 categorical attributes, and a binary class (`Yes` / `No` for "Play Tennis").

### Training Data

| Day | Outlook | Temperature | Humidity | Wind | PlayTennis |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sunny | Hot | High | Weak | No |
| 2 | Sunny | Hot | High | Strong | No |
| 3 | Overcast | Hot | High | Weak | Yes |
| 4 | Rain | Mild | High | Weak | Yes |
| 5 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| 6 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Strong | No |
| 7 | Overcast | Cool | Normal | Strong | Yes |
| 8 | Sunny | Mild | High | Weak | No |
| 9 | Sunny | Cool | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| 10 | Rain | Mild | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| 11 | Sunny | Mild | Normal | Strong | Yes |
| 12 | Overcast | Mild | High | Strong | Yes |
| 13 | Overcast | Hot | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| 14 | Rain | Mild | High | Strong | No |

### Step 1 — Entropy of the Root Node

Class distribution: 9 `Yes`, 5 `No`. Total $\vert S \vert = 14$.

$$p_{Yes} = \frac{9}{14}, \quad p_{No} = \frac{5}{14}$$

$$H(S) = -\frac{9}{14}\log_2\!\frac{9}{14} - \frac{5}{14}\log_2\!\frac{5}{14}$$

We compute $\log_2(9/14) = \log_2 0.6429 \approx -0.6374$ and $\log_2(5/14) = \log_2 0.3571 \approx -1.4854$.

$$H(S) = -0.6429 \times (-0.6374) - 0.3571 \times (-1.4854)$$

$$H(S) = 0.4098 + 0.5305 = 0.9403 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 2 — Information Gain for Attribute "Outlook"

Outlook has three values: *Sunny* (5 days), *Overcast* (4 days), *Rain* (5 days).

**Sunny subset (5 instances):** 2 `Yes`, 3 `No`

$$H(S_{Sunny}) = -\frac{2}{5}\log_2\frac{2}{5} - \frac{3}{5}\log_2\frac{3}{5} = -0.4 \log_2 0.4 - 0.6 \log_2 0.6$$

Using $\log_2 0.4 = -1.3219$ and $\log_2 0.6 = -0.7370$:

$$H(S_{Sunny}) = 0.4 \times 1.3219 + 0.6 \times 0.7370 = 0.5288 + 0.4422 = 0.9710$$

**Overcast subset (4 instances):** 4 `Yes`, 0 `No` → Pure node

$$H(S_{Overcast}) = 0$$

**Rain subset (5 instances):** 3 `Yes`, 2 `No`

$$H(S_{Rain}) = -\frac{3}{5}\log_2\frac{3}{5} - \frac{2}{5}\log_2\frac{2}{5} = 0.6 \times 0.7370 + 0.4 \times 1.3219 = 0.4422 + 0.5288 = 0.9710$$

**Weighted Entropy After Split on Outlook:**

$$H(S \mid Outlook) = \frac{5}{14}(0.9710) + \frac{4}{14}(0) + \frac{5}{14}(0.9710)$$

$$H(S \mid Outlook) = 0.3571 \times 0.9710 + 0 + 0.3571 \times 0.9710 = 0.3468 + 0.3468 = 0.6936$$

**Information Gain for Outlook:**

$$IG(S, Outlook) = 0.9403 - 0.6936 = 0.2467 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 3 — Information Gain for All Attributes (Tabulated)

Following identical logic for `Temperature`, `Humidity`, and `Wind`:

| Attribute | $H(S \mid A)$ | $IG(S, A)$ | $SI(S, A)$ | $GR(S, A)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Outlook** | 0.6936 | **0.2467** | 1.5774 | 0.1564 |
| **Temperature** | 0.9111 | 0.0292 | 1.5567 | 0.0188 |
| **Humidity** | 0.7885 | **0.1518** | 1.0000 | 0.1518 |
| **Wind** | 0.8922 | 0.0481 | 0.9852 | 0.0488 |

**Selection (ID3):** `Outlook` wins with $IG = 0.2467$.

**Selection (C4.5):** `Humidity` wins with $GR = 0.1518$ (just slightly above Outlook). C4.5 also uses a heuristic: first consider attributes with IG above average, then pick the one with max GR among them.

### Step 4 — Recurse on Each Subset

- `Outlook = Overcast` → **pure leaf** (all Yes).
- `Outlook = Sunny` → 5 instances; recurse using remaining attributes. Best split: `Humidity`.
- `Outlook = Rain` → 5 instances; recurse. Best split: `Wind`.

## 3.2 Handling Continuous Attributes — Threshold Splitting

For an attribute $X$ with continuous values, the algorithm considers every midpoint between sorted distinct values as a candidate split. The chosen split is:

$$X \leq t^* \quad \text{where} \quad t^* = \arg\max_{t} \; IG(S, X \leq t)$$

**Efficiency Trick:** Sort the values of $X$ once ($O(n \log n)$). Since entropy only changes between adjacent distinct class labels, only $O(n)$ splits need evaluation (not $O(n^2)$).

## 3.3 Regression Trees (CART)

For regression, the splitting criterion switches from impurity reduction to **reduction in variance (RSS)**:

$$RSS = \sum_{j=1}^{J} \sum_{i \in R_j} (y_i - \hat{y}_{R_j})^2$$

The leaf prediction $\hat{y}_{R_j}$ is the **mean** of training $y$-values in region $R_j$:

$$\hat{y}_{R_j} = \frac{1}{\vert R_j \vert} \sum_{i \in R_j} y_i$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Comparison

| Property | ID3 | C4.5 | CART |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Year / Author** | Quinlan, 1986 | Quinlan, 1993 | Breiman et al., 1984 |
| **Split Type** | Multi-way | Multi-way | Binary |
| **Criterion** | Information Gain | Gain Ratio | Gini / Variance |
| **Handles Continuous?** | No | Yes (threshold) | Yes (threshold) |
| **Handles Missing?** | No | Yes (probabilistic split) | Yes (surrogate splits) |
| **Pruning** | None built-in | Error-based (pessimistic) | Cost-complexity |
| **Output** | Classification | Classification | Classification + Regression |

## 3.5 Overfitting and the Need for Pruning

A fully grown tree perfectly classifies training data, but its complexity causes **low bias / high variance** — i.e., overfitting. The expected error is decomposed as:

$$E[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2] = \underbrace{Bias^2[\hat{f}(x)]}_{\text{underfit}} + \underbrace{Var[\hat{f}(x)]}_{\text{overfit}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{irreducible noise}}$$

**Two Pruning Strategies:**

| Strategy | Mechanism | When Applied |
|---|---|---|
| **Pre-pruning (Early Stopping)** | Stop splitting if gain < threshold, or $\vert S \vert$ < min, or depth > max. | During growth |
| **Post-pruning (Reduced Error / Cost-Complexity)** | Grow full tree, then collapse subtrees using a validation set or penalty term $\alpha \cdot \vert T \vert$. | After growth |

**Cost-Complexity Pruning (CART):** Minimize

$$C_\alpha(T) = \sum_{t=1}^{\vert T \vert} N_t \cdot H_t(T) + \alpha \vert T \vert$$

where $\alpha \geq 0$ is a regularization parameter trading off tree size vs. fit.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The ID3 / C4.5 Algorithm Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START Input Training Set S and Attributes A] --> B[Compute Entropy H of S]
    B --> C[For Each Attribute A compute Information Gain IG S A]
    C --> D{Is IG above threshold or all instances same class}
    D -- YES --> E[Select Attribute A star with Maximum IG or GR]
    D -- NO --> F[Create Leaf Node with Majority Class]
    E --> G[Partition S into Subsets S v for each value v of A star]
    G --> H[For Each Subset S v Recursively Apply Algorithm]
    H --> I[Attach Resulting Subtrees as Children of Node A star]
    I --> J[Apply Pruning Pre or Post]
    J --> K[END Return Final Decision Tree]
    F --> K
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Tree Construction Using Information Gain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Dataset S with 14 instances and 4 attributes] --> L2[Compute Class Entropy H of S equals 0.9403]
    L2 --> L3[Compute IG for each of 4 attributes]
    L3 --> L4[Outlook IG equals 0.2467 MAX]
    L3 --> L5[Temperature IG equals 0.0292]
    L3 --> L6[Humidity IG equals 0.1518]
    L3 --> L7[Wind IG equals 0.0481]
    L4 --> L8[Split Root on Outlook into 3 children]
    L8 --> L9[Sunny branch recurse on 3 attributes]
    L8 --> L10[Overcast branch pure leaf Yes]
    L8 --> L11[Rain branch recurse on 3 attributes]
    L9 --> L12[Humidity best split inside Sunny]
    L11 --> L13[Wind best split inside Rain]
    L12 --> L14[Final Pruned Tree Ready]
    L13 --> L14
    L10 --> L14
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Gini vs Entropy Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M0[Decision Tree Library scikit learn] --> M1{Problem Type}
    M1 -- Classification --> M2{Preferred Criterion}
    M1 -- Regression --> M3[Use MSE or MAE for splits]
    M2 -- Gini --> M4[Default CART use Gini index faster]
    M2 -- Entropy --> M5[Equivalent to Information Gain matches ID3]
    M4 --> M6[Build binary tree]
    M5 --> M6
    M3 --> M7[Build regression tree predict mean per leaf]
    M6 --> M8{Pruning Strategy}
    M7 --> M8
    M8 -- Pre pruning --> M9[Set max depth min samples split min samples leaf]
    M8 -- Post pruning --> M10[Use cost complexity pruning alpha path]
    M9 --> M11[Final Tuned Tree]
    M10 --> M11
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Pruning Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Full Grown Unpruned Tree T max] --> P1[Compute Error on Validation Set for each subtree]
    P1 --> P2{Subtree replacement reduces validation error}
    P2 -- YES --> P3[Replace subtree with leaf node]
    P2 -- NO --> P4{Subtree raising improves accuracy}
    P4 -- YES --> P5[Replace subtree with its most used branch]
    P4 -- NO --> P6[Keep subtree intact]
    P3 --> P7[Reduced Tree T1]
    P5 --> P7
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8[Repeat until no improvement found]
    P8 --> P9[Final Pruned Optimal Tree]
```
