# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Define entropy and information gain in the context of decision tree learning. State the boundary conditions of entropy.**

**Model Answer:**

Entropy is a measure of impurity (or disorder) in a dataset $S$ containing $C$ classes:

$$H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i \log_2 p_i$$

**Boundary Conditions:**
- $H(S) = 0$ when the dataset is **pure** (all instances belong to a single class).
- $H(S) = \log_2 C$ when the classes are uniformly distributed (maximum uncertainty).

Information Gain $IG(S, A)$ is the **reduction in entropy** of $S$ achieved by partitioning on attribute $A$:

$$IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$$

A high $IG$ indicates that attribute $A$ creates child subsets that are purer than the parent, making it a good splitting candidate.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Why is information gain biased toward attributes with many values, and how does C4.5's gain ratio overcome this bias?**

**Model Answer:**

Information Gain rewards attributes that produce many small, pure child partitions. An attribute with extremely high cardinality (e.g., "Customer ID" or "Date of Birth") can split the data into near-singleton subsets, each with $H \approx 0$, yielding $IG$ close to $H(S)$ — but such attributes have **zero generalization power**.

C4.5 normalizes IG by the **Split Information** $SI(S, A)$, defined as the entropy of $S$ *with respect to the partition itself*:

$$GR(S, A) = \frac{IG(S, A)}{SI(S, A)} = \frac{IG(S, A)}{-\sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}}$$

Since $SI$ is large for high-cardinality attributes, the ratio penalizes them, producing a fairer ranking. C4.5 also restricts the candidate set to attributes whose $IG$ is above the *average IG* before picking the maximum $GR$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1 — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. Forgetting to specify the **logarithm base** (base 2 for entropy).
> 2. Computing $IG$ for the **wrong attribute** (mixing up which subset is $S_v$).
> 3. Failing to **convert probabilities to fractions** before applying $\log_2$.
> 4. In regression tree questions, students often compute **mean of $y$ in leaves** as "median" — this loses 2 marks.
> 5. Confusing **Gini Gain** (which is maximized) with **Gini Index** (which is minimized). Read the question carefully.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Compute the **information gain** for each attribute of the following dataset, and identify the root node split using the ID3 algorithm. **(7 marks)**

| $X_1$ | $X_2$ | $X_3$ | Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | M | High | Yes |
| A | L | Low | No |
| B | M | High | No |
| B | L | High | Yes |
| A | M | Low | No |
| A | H | High | Yes |
| B | H | High | No |
| A | M | Low | No |

**(b)** After choosing the root, the algorithm recurses on one of its branches (the one containing 4 instances). Continue the tree construction for **one more level** by computing information gain for the remaining attributes on that branch. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Entropy of $S$:**

Class distribution: 3 `Yes`, 5 `No`. $H(S) = -\frac{3}{8}\log_2\frac{3}{8} - \frac{5}{8}\log_2\frac{5}{8}$

$\log_2(3/8) = -1.4150$, $\log_2(5/8) = -0.6781$

$$H(S) = 0.375 \times 1.4150 + 0.625 \times 0.6781 = 0.5306 + 0.4238 = 0.9544$$

**Step 2 — IG for $X_1$ (values A, B):**

- $X_1 = A$: 5 instances. Yes: 2, No: 3. $H = -0.4\log_2 0.4 - 0.6\log_2 0.6 = 0.9710$
- $X_1 = B$: 3 instances. Yes: 1, No: 2. $H = -\tfrac{1}{3}\log_2\tfrac{1}{3} - \tfrac{2}{3}\log_2\tfrac{2}{3} = 0.3333 \times 1.5850 + 0.6667 \times 0.5850 = 0.5283 + 0.3900 = 0.9183$

$$H(S \mid X_1) = \tfrac{5}{8}(0.9710) + \tfrac{3}{8}(0.9183) = 0.6069 + 0.3444 = 0.9513$$

$$IG(S, X_1) = 0.9544 - 0.9513 = \mathbf{0.0031}$$

**Step 3 — IG for $X_2$ (values L, M, H):**

- $X_2 = L$: 2 instances, 1 Yes / 1 No. $H = 1.0$ (max disorder)
- $X_2 = M$: 4 instances, 1 Yes / 3 No. $H = -0.25\log_2 0.25 - 0.75\log_2 0.75 = 0.5 + 0.3113 = 0.8113$
- $X_2 = H$: 2 instances, 1 Yes / 1 No. $H = 1.0$

$$H(S \mid X_2) = \tfrac{2}{8}(1.0) + \tfrac{4}{8}(0.8113) + \tfrac{2}{8}(1.0) = 0.25 + 0.4056 + 0.25 = 0.9056$$

$$IG(S, X_2) = 0.9544 - 0.9056 = \mathbf{0.0488}$$

**Step 4 — IG for $X_3$ (values High, Low):**

- $X_3 = \text{High}$: 5 instances, 3 Yes / 2 No. $H = 0.9710$
- $X_3 = \text{Low}$: 3 instances, 0 Yes / 3 No. $H = 0$ (pure leaf)

$$H(S \mid X_3) = \tfrac{5}{8}(0.9710) + \tfrac{3}{8}(0) = 0.6069$$

$$IG(S, X_3) = 0.9544 - 0.6069 = \mathbf{0.3475}$$

**Decision:** $X_3$ has the highest $IG$ → **Root node split is on $X_3$**.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

The branch $X_3 = \text{High}$ contains 5 instances:

| $X_1$ | $X_2$ | Class |
|---|---|---|
| A | M | Yes |
| B | L | Yes |
| A | H | Yes |
| A | M | Yes |
| B | H | No |

Class distribution: 4 `Yes`, 1 `No`.

**Entropy of branch:**

$$H = -\tfrac{4}{5}\log_2\tfrac{4}{5} - \tfrac{1}{5}\log_2\tfrac{1}{5} = 0.8 \times 0.3219 + 0.2 \times 2.3219 = 0.2575 + 0.4644 = 0.7219$$

**IG for $X_1$ (in this branch):**

- $X_1 = A$: 3 instances, 3 Yes / 0 No. $H = 0$
- $X_1 = B$: 2 instances, 1 Yes / 1 No. $H = 1.0$

$$H(\text{branch} \mid X_1) = \tfrac{3}{5}(0) + \tfrac{2}{5}(1.0) = 0.4$$

$$IG(\text{branch}, X_1) = 0.7219 - 0.4 = \mathbf{0.3219}$$

**IG for $X_2$ (in this branch):**

- $X_2 = M$: 2 instances, 2 Yes / 0 No. $H = 0$
- $X_2 = L$: 1 instance, 1 Yes / 0 No. $H = 0$
- $X_2 = H$: 2 instances, 1 Yes / 1 No. $H = 1.0$

$$H(\text{branch} \mid X_2) = \tfrac{2}{5}(0) + \tfrac{1}{5}(0) + \tfrac{2}{5}(1.0) = 0.4$$

$$IG(\text{branch}, X_2) = 0.7219 - 0.4 = \mathbf{0.3219}$$

**Tie-breaker:** Both $X_1$ and $X_2$ have $IG = 0.3219$. ID3 typically picks the first encountered (here $X_1$), and the resulting children yield one pure node ($X_1 = A$ → all Yes) and one mixed node ($X_1 = B$ → still requires $X_2$ for further splitting).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Define the **Gini Index**. For a node containing 6 instances of class $C_1$ and 10 instances of class $C_2$, compute the Gini index and the classification error. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **pre-pruning and post-pruning** strategies for decision trees. How does cost-complexity pruning (CART) work? Write the cost-complexity function. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Gini Index Definition:** The Gini index at node $t$ measures the impurity as the probability of misclassifying a randomly chosen instance if labeled according to the class distribution at that node:

$$Gini(t) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i^2$$

**Computation:**

$$p_1 = \frac{6}{16} = 0.375, \quad p_2 = \frac{10}{16} = 0.625$$

$$Gini = 1 - (0.375)^2 - (0.625)^2 = 1 - 0.1406 - 0.3906 = 1 - 0.5312 = \mathbf{0.4688}$$

**Classification Error:**

$$Error = 1 - \max(p_1, p_2) = 1 - 0.625 = \mathbf{0.375}$$

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Pre-pruning (Early Stopping):**

The tree growth is halted *before* it perfectly fits the training data. Common stopping conditions include:
- Maximum tree depth $d_{max}$ reached.
- Minimum number of samples at a node $n_{min}$ (e.g., stop if $\vert S \vert < 10$).
- Minimum impurity reduction $\Delta H_{min}$ (do not split if $IG < \Delta H_{min}$).
- No attribute yields positive information gain.

**Advantage:** Computationally cheap.
**Disadvantage:** *Horizon effect* — a split that seems useless early may lead to a highly informative split a few levels down.

**Post-pruning (Reduced-Error / Cost-Complexity Pruning):**

Grow the full tree $T_{max}$, then iteratively replace subtrees with leaf nodes if doing so **improves validation accuracy** (or a penalized objective).

**Cost-Complexity Pruning (CART):** Define for any subtree $T \subseteq T_{max}$:

$$C_\alpha(T) = \sum_{t=1}^{\vert T \vert} N_t \cdot H_t(T) + \alpha \vert T \vert$$

where:
- $N_t$ = number of training samples at node $t$.
- $H_t(T)$ = entropy (or Gini) at node $t$.
- $\vert T \vert$ = number of terminal nodes in $T$.
- $\alpha \geq 0$ is a **regularization parameter**.

For each $\alpha$, there exists a unique smallest subtree $T_\alpha$ minimizing $C_\alpha$. As $\alpha$ increases, $T_\alpha$ shrinks. A sequence of trees $T_{max} \supset T_1 \supset T_2 \supset \cdots \supset \{root\}$ is generated, and the best $\alpha$ is selected by **k-fold cross-validation**.

The effective $\alpha$ for pruning a subtree $T_t$ rooted at node $t$ is:

$$\alpha_{eff}(t) = \frac{R(t) - R(T_t)}{\vert T_t \vert - 1}$$

where $R(\cdot)$ is the total impurity. The node $t$ that minimizes $\alpha_{eff}$ is the next to be pruned.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Decision Trees**

- **Three Impurity Measures:** Entropy, Gini, Classification Error. Always remember the **log base 2** for entropy.
- **Information Gain = Reduction in Entropy:** $IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$.
- **ID3** uses IG, supports only categorical attributes, no pruning.
- **C4.5** uses Gain Ratio, supports continuous attributes, uses pessimistic error pruning.
- **CART** uses Gini (classification) / Variance (regression), produces binary trees, uses cost-complexity pruning.
- **Information Gain is biased** to high-cardinality attributes; Gain Ratio fixes this by dividing by Split Information.
- **Decision Tree for Regression** uses RSS minimization and predicts the **mean** of $y$ in each leaf.
- **Overfitting** is combated via **pruning** (pre or post) or by setting **constraints** (max depth, min samples per leaf).
- **Gini Index Formula** $Gini = 1 - \sum p_i^2$ is computationally cheaper than entropy (no logarithm).
- **Continuous attributes** require candidate-threshold selection; only midpoints between adjacent distinct class labels need evaluation.
- **Missing values** are handled in C4.5 via probabilistic splits and in CART via surrogate splits.
- **Decision trees are unstable** — small data changes can produce very different trees. This is the **primary motivation** for ensemble methods (Random Forest, Boosting).
- **Boundary values to remember:** $H \in [0, \log_2 C]$, $Gini \in [0, 1 - 1/C]$, $IG \in [0, H(S)]$, $GR \in [0, 1]$.
- **Cost-complexity pruning** explicitly trades training fit vs. tree size via the parameter $\alpha$.
- **Bias-Variance trade-off:** Unpruned trees → low bias / high variance; heavily pruned trees → high bias / low variance.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 2 — Learning with Trees Notes.** Ensure you can compute IG, Gini, and Gain Ratio from scratch on a small dataset, as KTU frequently tests these computations in Part B questions worth 14 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types - Classification and Regression Trees
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT501 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types - Classification and Regression Trees

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Classification and Regression Trees (CART)** is a non-parametric, supervised machine learning algorithm introduced by **Leo Breiman, Jerome Friedman, Richard Olshen, and Charles Stone in 1984**. CART constructs binary decision trees by recursively partitioning the feature space into homogeneous sub-regions, producing either a **Classification Tree** (when the target variable is categorical/discrete) or a **Regression Tree** (when the target variable is continuous/numerical).

The CART algorithm uses a top-down, greedy approach known as **recursive binary splitting**, where at each internal node the algorithm selects the feature and split-point that maximizes the **purity gain** (or equivalently, minimizes the impurity of the resulting child nodes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** CART is fundamentally a *binary splitting* algorithm. Unlike C4.5 (which can produce multi-way splits), CART always produces exactly two child nodes per split — this is the most frequently tested KTU distinguishing feature.

### 1.2 The Two Tree Types

| Tree Type | Target Variable | Output | Splitting Criterion | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Classification Tree** | Categorical (Nominal/Ordinal) | Class Label | Gini Impurity / Entropy | Spam detection, Disease diagnosis, Loan approval |
| **Regression Tree** | Continuous (Real-valued) | Numerical Value | MSE / Variance Reduction | House price prediction, Temperature forecasting, Sales estimation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:** The target variable $y$ dictates the tree type. If $y \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, k\}$ → Classification Tree. If $y \in \mathbb{R}$ → Regression Tree.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Medical Diagnostic Flowchart

Imagine a doctor diagnosing a patient. The doctor doesn't run every test at once. Instead, the doctor asks **one question at a time** in a structured flowchart:

1. *"Is the patient a child or an adult?"* → Splits the patient population into two groups
2. *"Does the child have a fever above 102°F?"* → Further splits the "child" branch
3. *"Is the throat red?"* → Continues narrowing down

Each question **splits the population into two sub-groups** (binary splits) and the *first question* the doctor asks is the one that **best separates sick from healthy patients**. This is exactly how CART works:

- **Patient population** = Training dataset
- **Doctor's questions** = Tests on feature values (e.g., "Is Age > 30?")
- **Most informative question** = The split that maximizes purity
- **Final diagnosis** = Leaf node prediction

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition for KTU Students:** CART is like building the *shortest possible* flowchart that gets the right answer for the most patients, asking the *most decisive* question first at every step.

### 1.4 Fundamental Properties of CART

- **Binary Splits:** Every internal node has exactly two children (left and right).
- **Greedy Top-Down Construction:** The tree is built from root downward; the best split is chosen at the current node without lookahead.
- **Non-Parametric:** Makes no assumption about the underlying data distribution.
- **Handles Mixed Data:** Works with both numerical and categorical predictors.
- **Prone to Overfitting:** Without pruning, CART can grow to 100% training accuracy but fail on test data.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Mechanism

The CART algorithm executes the following sequence:

**Step 1 — Initialization:**
- The root node $R_0$ contains the entire training dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$.
- Compute the impurity $I(R_0)$ of the root.

**Step 2 — Split Search (Greedy Search):**
- For every feature $X_j$ ($j = 1, 2, \ldots, p$), scan every candidate split point $s$ such that the feature values are partitioned into:
  $$X_j \leq s \quad \text{and} \quad X_j > s$$
- For each candidate $(X_j, s)$, compute the resulting reduction in impurity:
  $$\Delta I(j, s) = I(R_{\text{parent}}) - \left[\frac{N_L}{N} I(R_L) + \frac{N_R}{N} I(R_R)\right]$$
  where $N_L, N_R$ are the sample counts in left and right child nodes, and $N = N_L + N_R$.

**Step 3 — Best Split Selection:**
- Choose the pair $(j^*, s^*)$ that maximizes $\Delta I$:
  $$(j^*, s^*) = \arg\max_{j, s} \, \Delta I(j, s)$$

**Step 4 — Recursive Partitioning:**
- Apply the chosen split to create two child nodes $R_L$ and $R_R$.
- Recursively apply Steps 2–3 on each child node that satisfies the stopping criteria (minimum samples, maximum depth, etc.).

**Step 5 — Leaf Node Assignment (Prediction Rule):**
- **For Classification Tree:** Each leaf node predicts the **majority class** among the training samples that fall into it:
  $$\hat{y}_{\text{leaf}} = \arg\max_{c} \, P(Y = c \mid x \in R_{\text{leaf}})$$
- **For Regression Tree:** Each leaf node predicts the **mean** of the target values:
  $$\hat{y}_{\text{leaf}} = \frac{1}{|R_{\text{leaf}}|} \sum_{x^{(i)} \in R_{\text{leaf}}} y^{(i)}$$

**Step 6 — Pruning (Post-Pruning via Cost-Complexity):**
- After full growth, prune the tree backward using the cost-complexity criterion:
  $$R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha \vert T \vert$$
  where $R(T)$ is the training error (misclassification rate or SSE), $\vert T \vert$ is the number of terminal nodes (leaves), and $\alpha \geq 0$ is a regularization parameter.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gini Impurity | $G(t) = 1 - \sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c^2$ | $p_c$ = proportion of class $c$ at node $t$ | Classification |
| 2 | Entropy | $H(t) = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c \log_2 p_c$ | $p_c$ = proportion of class $c$ at node $t$ | Classification |
| 3 | Information Gain | $IG = H(\text{parent}) - \sum_{k \in \{L,R\}} \frac{N_k}{N} H(R_k)$ | Weighted entropy reduction | Classification |
| 4 | Gini Gain | $\Delta G = G(\text{parent}) - \sum_{k \in \{L,R\}} \frac{N_k}{N} G(R_k)$ | Weighted Gini reduction | Classification |
| 5 | Mean Squared Error (MSE) at Node | $\text{MSE}(t) = \frac{1}{N_t} \sum_{i \in t} (y_i - \bar{y}_t)^2$ | Variance of target in node $t$ | Regression |
| 6 | Variance Reduction | $\Delta \text{Var} = \text{Var}(\text{parent}) - \sum_{k} \frac{N_k}{N} \text{Var}(R_k)$ | Reduction in squared error | Regression |
| 7 | Sum of Squared Errors | $\text{SSE}(t) = \sum_{i \in t} (y_i - \bar{y}_t)^2$ | Total squared deviation at node | Regression |
| 8 | Cost-Complexity Criterion | $R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha \vert T \vert$ | $R(T)$ = error, $\vert T \vert$ = leaves | Pruning |
| 9 | Leaf Prediction (Classification) | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_c \, p_c$ | Majority voting | Classification |
| 10 | Leaf Prediction (Regression) | $\hat{y} = \bar{y}_t = \frac{1}{N_t} \sum_{i \in t} y_i$ | Mean of target values | Regression |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Common KTU Exam Trap:** Do NOT confuse **Gini Impurity** with **Gini Coefficient** (economics). In CART, Gini Impurity is a measure of node *disorder* in classification. Always remember: $G = 0$ means a perfectly pure node, and $G$ approaches $1 - \frac{1}{C}$ for maximum impurity.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | Application | Tree Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Healthcare** | Cancer staging from biopsy features | Classification |
| **Finance** | Credit card fraud detection | Classification |
| **Energy** | Predicting power consumption from weather data | Regression |
| **Manufacturing** | Predicting equipment Remaining Useful Life (RUL) | Regression |
| **Telecom** | Customer churn prediction | Classification |
| **Civil Engineering** | Concrete compressive strength prediction | Regression |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Gini Impurity — First Principles

Gini Impurity measures the probability of **incorrectly classifying** a randomly chosen element from the node if it were labeled according to the class distribution of that node.

**Step 1:** Suppose node $t$ contains $N_t$ samples distributed across $C$ classes. The probability that a randomly drawn sample belongs to class $c$ is:
$$p_c = \frac{N_c}{N_t}$$

**Step 2:** The probability of misclassification using the majority class strategy is the probability that we draw a sample that is *not* the predicted class. If the predicted class is $c^*$, the misclassification probability is:
$$P(\text{misclass}) = \sum_{c \neq c^*} p_c = 1 - p_{c^*}$$

**Step 3:** The *expected* misclassification probability, assuming the predicted class equals the true class (i.e., the *impurity*), is:
$$G(t) = \sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c (1 - p_c) = \sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c - \sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c^2$$

**Step 4:** Since $\sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c = 1$, we simplify:
$$G(t) = 1 - \sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c^2$$

This is the **Gini Impurity** formula. The minimum value $G(t) = 0$ occurs when $p_c = 1$ for some $c$ (pure node), and the maximum occurs when all classes are equally likely.

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (2-class case):**

Consider a node with $N = 10$ samples, $N_1 = 4$ (Class A) and $N_2 = 6$ (Class B).

$$p_A = \frac{4}{10} = 0.4 \quad \text{and} \quad p_B = \frac{6}{10} = 0.6$$

$$G(t) = 1 - (0.4^2 + 0.6^2) = 1 - (0.16 + 0.36) = 1 - 0.52 = 0.48$$

So the Gini impurity of this node is **0.48**.

### 3.2 Derivation of Entropy — Information Theory Origin

Entropy in CART comes from **Shannon's Information Theory (1948)**. It measures the average amount of "surprise" or "information" in identifying the class of a randomly drawn sample.

**Step 1:** The information (or surprise) of observing an event with probability $p$ is:
$$I(p) = -\log_2(p)$$

The negative sign ensures surprise is non-negative. The logarithm base 2 gives the result in *bits*.

**Step 2:** The expected surprise (i.e., entropy) is the weighted average over all possible class outcomes:
$$H(t) = \sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c \cdot I(p_c) = \sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c \cdot (-\log_2 p_c)$$

**Step 3:** Therefore:
$$H(t) = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c \log_2 p_c$$

**Step 4 — Boundary Analysis:**

- **Pure node** ($p_c = 1$ for some $c$, others = 0): $H = 0$ (no uncertainty).
- **Maximum impurity** (uniform distribution, $p_c = 1/C$):
$$H_{\max} = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} \frac{1}{C} \log_2 \frac{1}{C} = \log_2 C$$

For binary classification ($C=2$), $H_{\max} = \log_2 2 = 1$ bit.

**Step 5 — Numerical Example (same data as 3.1):**

$$H(t) = -(0.4 \log_2 0.4 + 0.6 \log_2 0.6)$$

$$= -(0.4 \times (-1.3219) + 0.6 \times (-0.7370))$$

$$= -(-0.5288 - 0.4422) = 0.9710 \text{ bits}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Information Gain (Gini vs. Entropy)

**Step 1 — The split quality measure** is the weighted impurity reduction:
$$\Delta I = I(\text{parent}) - \left[\frac{N_L}{N} I(R_L) + \frac{N_R}{N} I(R_R)\right]$$

**Step 2 — Substitution for Gini:**
$$\Delta G = G(\text{parent}) - \frac{N_L}{N} G(R_L) - \frac{N_R}{N} G(R_R)$$

**Step 3 — Substitution for Entropy (Information Gain):**
$$\text{IG} = H(\text{parent}) - \frac{N_L}{N} H(R_L) - \frac{N_R}{N} H(R_R)$$

**Step 4 — Full Worked Example for a Binary Split:**

Suppose a parent node $P$ has 20 samples (12 Class A, 8 Class B). A candidate split produces:
- Left child $L$: 10 samples (9 A, 1 B)
- Right child $R$: 10 samples (3 A, 7 B)

**Parent Gini:**
$$G(P) = 1 - \left[\left(\frac{12}{20}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{8}{20}\right)^2\right] = 1 - (0.36 + 0.16) = 0.48$$

**Left Child Gini:**
$$G(L) = 1 - \left[\left(\frac{9}{10}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{1}{10}\right)^2\right] = 1 - (0.81 + 0.01) = 0.18$$

**Right Child Gini:**
$$G(R) = 1 - \left[\left(\frac{3}{10}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{7}{10}\right)^2\right] = 1 - (0.09 + 0.49) = 0.42$$

**Weighted Gini of Children:**
$$G_{\text{children}} = \frac{10}{20}(0.18) + \frac{10}{20}(0.42) = 0.09 + 0.21 = 0.30$$

**Gini Gain (split quality):**
$$\Delta G = 0.48 - 0.30 = 0.18$$

> This is the **gain** the split provides. A higher $\Delta G$ indicates a better split. CART will pick the split with the maximum $\Delta G$ over all candidate features and thresholds.

### 3.4 Regression Tree Split Criterion — Variance Reduction Derivation

**Step 1:** For a regression tree, we want leaf nodes to have minimal variance in $y$ so that the mean $\bar{y}_t$ is a good predictor. The within-node variance is:
$$\text{Var}(t) = \frac{1}{N_t} \sum_{i \in t} (y_i - \bar{y}_t)^2 = \text{MSE}(t)$$

**Step 2:** The reduction in variance from splitting parent $P$ into children $L, R$ is:
$$\Delta \text{Var} = \text{Var}(P) - \left[\frac{N_L}{N} \text{Var}(L) + \frac{N_R}{N} \text{Var}(R)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Example:**

Parent node $P$ contains $N = 8$ samples with target $y = [3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10]$.
$$\bar{y}_P = 6.5, \quad \text{Var}(P) = \frac{(3-6.5)^2 + (4-6.5)^2 + \ldots + (10-6.5)^2}{8} = \frac{42}{8} = 5.25$$

Split at $y = 6$: $L = [3,4,5]$ and $R = [7,8,9,10]$.
$$\bar{y}_L = 4, \quad \text{Var}(L) = \frac{1+0+1}{3} = 0.667$$
$$\bar{y}_R = 8.5, \quad \text{Var}(R) = \frac{2.25+0.25+0.25+2.25}{4} = 1.25$$

$$\Delta \text{Var} = 5.25 - \left(\frac{3}{8}(0.667) + \frac{5}{8}(1.25)\right) = 5.25 - (0.25 + 0.781) = 4.219$$

> This split achieves a **variance reduction of 4.219**, indicating it is highly informative.

### 3.5 Cost-Complexity Pruning (CCP) Derivation

**Step 1:** For a fully grown tree $T_{\max}$, the cost-complexity function is:
$$R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha \vert T \vert$$

where $R(T)$ is the total tree error and $\vert T \vert$ is the number of terminal nodes.

**Step 2:** The effective error per leaf is:
$$R_{\text{leaf}} = r(t) \cdot p(t)$$

where $r(t)$ is the resubstitution error rate at leaf $t$ and $p(t) = N_t / N$ is its probability.

**Step 3:** For each internal node $t$, define the effective $\alpha$ that makes the subtree $T_t$ collapse to a single leaf:
$$\alpha_{\text{eff}}(t) = \frac{R(t) - R(T_t)}{\vert T_t \vert - 1}$$

**Step 4:** Pruning procedure:
1. Compute $\alpha_{\text{eff}}(t)$ for every internal node $t$.
2. Find the node with the smallest $\alpha_{\text{eff}}$ and collapse its subtree to a leaf.
3. Repeat until only the root remains.
4. Use cross-validation to pick the optimal $\alpha^*$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CART Algorithm — Top-Down Tree Building Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START: Load Training Data D]) --> initB[Initialize Root Node R0 with All Samples]
    initB --> computeC[Compute Impurity of R0]
    computeC --> checkD{Stopping Criteria Met?}
    checkD -- YES --> leafE[Assign Leaf Node Prediction]
    checkD -- NO --> scanF[For Each Feature Xj and Split Point s]
    scanF --> gainG[Compute Gini Gain or Variance Reduction for Each Candidate Split]
    gainG --> bestH[Select Best Split j-star s-star with Maximum Gain]
    bestH --> splitI[Partition Data into Left Node RL and Right Node RR]
    splitI --> recurL[Recursively Build Left Subtree on RL]
    recurL --> recurR[Recursively Build Right Subtree on RR]
    recurR --> growJ[Tree T-max is Fully Grown]
    leafE --> growJ
    growJ --> pruneK[Apply Cost Complexity Pruning]
    pruneK --> cvM[Use Cross Validation to Select Optimal Alpha]
    cvM --> finalN[Output Optimal Pruned Tree T-alpha]
    finalN([END: Tree Ready for Prediction])
```

### 4.2 CART Tree Structure (Internal Node Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Root Node: All N samples] --> splitCheckA{Test: X1 less than t1}
    rootA --> splitCheckB{Test: X1 greater than or equal t1}
    splitCheckA --> nodeB[Internal Node: Subset NL samples]
    splitCheckB --> nodeC[Internal Node: Subset NR samples]
    nodeB --> testD{Test: X2 less than t2}
    nodeB --> testE{Test: X2 greater than or equal t2}
    testD --> leafF[Leaf 1: Predict Class C1]
    testE --> leafG[Leaf 2: Predict Class C2]
    testD --> leafF2[Leaf 3: Predict Class C3]
    testE --> leafG2[Leaf 4: Predict Class C4]
```

### 4.3 Classification vs. Regression Tree — Block Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CLASS["CLASSIFICATION TREE"]
        c1[Input: Categorical Target Y] --> c2[Split Criterion: Gini or Entropy]
        c2 --> c3[Leaf Prediction: Majority Class Label]
        c3 --> c4[Output: Discrete Class C in 1 to K]
    end
    subgraph REG["REGRESSION TREE"]
        r1[Input: Continuous Target Y in R] --> r2[Split Criterion: MSE or Variance Reduction]
        r2 --> r3[Leaf Prediction: Mean of Y values]
        r3 --> r4[Output: Real Number y-hat in R]
    end
    c1 -.compare.-> r1
    c4 -.compare.-> r4
```

### 4.4 Pruning Phase — Cost-Complexity Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    fullA[Fully Grown Tree T-max] --> computeA[Compute alpha effective for every internal node]
    computeA --> minB[Find internal node t with minimum alpha effective]
    minB --> collapseC[Collapse subtree at t into a single leaf]
    collapseC --> checkD{Is T a single root node?}
    checkD -- NO --> computeA
    checkD -- YES --> seriesE[Generate nested sequence T0 superset T1 superset T2 ... superset Tn]
    seriesE --> cvF[Use k fold cross validation on training set]
    cvF --> selectG[Select optimal tree T alpha that minimizes CV error]
    selectG([FINAL PRUNED TREE])
```

---
