## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`
> **Differentiate between Classification Trees and Regression Trees in CART. Mention the splitting criterion used in each case.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Classification Tree | Regression Tree |
|---|---|---|
| Target variable type | Categorical / Discrete | Continuous / Numerical |
| Splitting criterion | Gini Impurity, Entropy, Information Gain | Mean Squared Error (MSE), Variance Reduction |
| Leaf prediction | Majority class label (mode) | Mean of target values |
| Output | Discrete class $c \in \{1, 2, \ldots, K\}$ | Real number $\hat{y} \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| Evaluation metric | Accuracy, F1-score, AUC-ROC | RMSE, MAE, R² |

(3 marks: 2 for table-style differentiation, 1 for stating criteria explicitly)

---

> **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
> **Define Gini Impurity. Show that for a binary classification node with class probabilities $p$ and $(1-p)$, the Gini impurity $G(p) = 2p(1-p)$.**

**Model Answer:**

Gini Impurity is a measure of the degree of misclassification that would occur if labels were randomly assigned according to the class distribution at a node.

For binary classification with $C = 2$ and class probabilities $p_1 = p$ and $p_2 = 1 - p$:

$$G(t) = 1 - \sum_{c=1}^{2} p_c^2 = 1 - [p^2 + (1-p)^2]$$

$$= 1 - [p^2 + 1 - 2p + p^2] = 1 - 1 + 2p - 2p^2$$

$$= 2p(1 - p) \quad \blacksquare$$

Maximum occurs at $p = 0.5$, where $G = 2(0.5)(0.5) = 0.5$. (3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for derivation)

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks).** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the CART algorithm with a neat flowchart. Discuss the Gini impurity and Entropy-based splitting criteria with appropriate mathematical formulations. For a node containing samples of two classes with probabilities $p_1 = 0.3$ and $p_2 = 0.7$, compute the Gini impurity and Entropy.

**(a) Model Solution:**

**CART Algorithm Flowchart (as drawn in Section 4.1 above):** The algorithm proceeds top-down, at each node selecting the feature $X_j$ and threshold $s$ that maximizes the purity gain via binary partitioning. Stopping criteria include minimum samples per leaf, maximum tree depth, or zero gain. After full growth, post-pruning via cost-complexity is applied.

**Mathematical Formulations:**

- **Gini Impurity at node $t$:**
$$G(t) = 1 - \sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c^2$$

For binary split, the quality of split $(j, s)$:
$$\Delta G(j, s) = G(P) - \frac{N_L}{N} G(L) - \frac{N_R}{N} G(R)$$

The split maximizing $\Delta G$ is chosen.

- **Entropy at node $t$:**
$$H(t) = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c \log_2 p_c$$

**Information Gain for split $(j, s)$:**
$$\text{IG}(j, s) = H(P) - \frac{N_L}{N} H(L) - \frac{N_R}{N} H(R)$$

The split maximizing IG is chosen. Both Gini and Entropy are minimized at pure nodes and maximized at uniform class distribution.

**Numerical Computation:**

Given $p_1 = 0.3$, $p_2 = 0.7$:

$$G = 1 - (0.3^2 + 0.7^2) = 1 - (0.09 + 0.49) = 0.42$$

$$H = -(0.3 \log_2 0.3 + 0.7 \log_2 0.7)$$
$$= -(0.3 \times (-1.737) + 0.7 \times (-0.515))$$
$$= -(-0.521 - 0.360) = 0.881 \text{ bits}$$

**(b)** [7 Marks] Consider the following training dataset for a Regression Tree problem with feature $X$ and target $Y$. Construct the root split using variance reduction as the criterion. Show all calculations.

| $X$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $Y$ | 2 | 4 | 5 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 9 | 7 |

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute parent statistics:**

$$\bar{y}_P = \frac{2+4+5+4+6+8+9+7}{8} = \frac{45}{8} = 5.625$$

$$\text{Var}(P) = \frac{1}{8}\sum_{i=1}^{8}(y_i - 5.625)^2$$

Computing each squared deviation:
- $(2-5.625)^2 = 13.141$
- $(4-5.625)^2 = 2.641$
- $(5-5.625)^2 = 0.391$
- $(4-5.625)^2 = 2.641$
- $(6-5.625)^2 = 0.141$
- $(8-5.625)^2 = 5.641$
- $(9-5.625)^2 = 11.391$
- $(7-5.625)^2 = 1.891$

$$\text{Sum} = 37.875 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Var}(P) = \frac{37.875}{8} = 4.734$$

**Step 2 — Try candidate split at $X = 4$:**

- $L = \{X \leq 4\} = \{(1,2), (2,4), (3,5), (4,4)\}$: $N_L = 4$, $y_L = [2,4,5,4]$, $\bar{y}_L = 3.75$
$$\text{Var}(L) = \frac{(2-3.75)^2 + (4-3.75)^2 + (5-3.75)^2 + (4-3.75)^2}{4}$$
$$= \frac{3.0625 + 0.0625 + 1.5625 + 0.0625}{4} = \frac{4.75}{4} = 1.1875$$

- $R = \{X > 4\} = \{(5,6), (6,8), (7,9), (8,7)\}$: $N_R = 4$, $y_R = [6,8,9,7]$, $\bar{y}_R = 7.5$
$$\text{Var}(R) = \frac{(6-7.5)^2 + (8-7.5)^2 + (9-7.5)^2 + (7-7.5)^2}{4}$$
$$= \frac{2.25 + 0.25 + 2.25 + 0.25}{4} = \frac{5.0}{4} = 1.25$$

**Step 3 — Compute Variance Reduction:**

$$\Delta \text{Var} = 4.734 - \left[\frac{4}{8}(1.1875) + \frac{4}{8}(1.25)\right]$$

$$= 4.734 - [0.5938 + 0.625] = 4.734 - 1.219 = 3.515$$

**Step 4 — Verify with another split (e.g., $X = 5$):**

Repeating the process for $X = 5$ would yield a different variance reduction. The split with the **maximum variance reduction** is selected as the root. Here, for $X = 4$, $\Delta \text{Var} = 3.515$ is the chosen root split. (Other split values would need to be compared; the highest $\Delta \text{Var}$ is chosen.)

**(Total: 14 marks — 7 for flowchart + criteria + numerical, 7 for regression tree construction)**

---

> **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice).** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain Information Gain in CART. How is it different from Gini Gain? Discuss with a suitable example where a parent node with 16 samples (8 positive, 8 negative) is split into (6, 0) and (2, 8) children.

**(a) Model Solution:**

**Information Gain (IG):** The reduction in entropy achieved by partitioning the parent node into child nodes. It quantifies the "information" the split provides about the class label.

$$\text{IG} = H(\text{parent}) - \sum_{k \in \{L, R\}} \frac{N_k}{N} H(R_k)$$

**Gini Gain:** Uses Gini impurity in place of entropy:
$$\Delta G = G(\text{parent}) - \sum_{k \in \{L, R\}} \frac{N_k}{N} G(R_k)$$

**Key Differences:**

| Aspect | Information Gain (Entropy) | Gini Gain |
|---|---|---|
| Theoretical basis | Information theory (Shannon) | Statistical dispersion |
| Computation cost | Higher (logarithms) | Lower (squaring only) |
| Range (binary) | $[0, 1]$ bit | $[0, 0.5]$ |
| Default in scikit-learn | `criterion='entropy'` | `criterion='gini'` (default) |
| Sensitivity to node purity | Higher | Slightly lower |

**Numerical Example:**

Parent: 8 positive, 8 negative, $N = 16$.
$$H(P) = -\left(\frac{8}{16}\log_2\frac{8}{16} + \frac{8}{16}\log_2\frac{8}{16}\right) = -2(0.5 \times -1) = 1.0 \text{ bit}$$

Left child (6 P, 0 N): Pure node!
$$H(L) = -\left(\frac{6}{6}\log_2 1 + 0\right) = 0$$

Right child (2 P, 8 N): $p_1 = 2/10 = 0.2$, $p_2 = 8/10 = 0.8$
$$H(R) = -(0.2 \log_2 0.2 + 0.8 \log_2 0.8)$$
$$= -(0.2 \times -2.322 + 0.8 \times -0.322) = 0.722 \text{ bits}$$

Weighted child entropy:
$$H_{\text{children}} = \frac{6}{16}(0) + \frac{10}{16}(0.722) = 0 + 0.451 = 0.451$$

$$\text{IG} = 1.0 - 0.451 = 0.549 \text{ bits}$$

This is a **highly informative** split — the entropy dropped by 54.9% of the maximum possible.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the cost-complexity pruning in CART. Derive the effective alpha for a subtree collapse decision.

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Cost-Complexity Criterion:**

$$R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha \vert T \vert$$

- $R(T)$ = total resubstitution error of tree $T$ (sum of misclassification rates or SSE over leaves)
- $\vert T \vert$ = number of terminal nodes
- $\alpha$ = complexity parameter controlling tree size

**Derivation of Effective Alpha:**

For an internal node $t$, collapsing the subtree $T_t$ rooted at $t$ into a single leaf $t$ changes the cost-complexity by:

$$\Delta R_\alpha = R(t) + \alpha \cdot 1 - [R(T_t) + \alpha \vert T_t \vert]$$

$$= [R(t) - R(T_t)] - \alpha[\vert T_t \vert - 1]$$

The two alternatives are **equivalent** (indifferent) when $\Delta R_\alpha = 0$, which gives the **effective alpha**:

$$\alpha_{\text{eff}}(t) = \frac{R(t) - R(T_t)}{\vert T_t \vert - 1}$$

**Pruning Algorithm:**
1. Compute $\alpha_{\text{eff}}(t)$ for every internal node $t$.
2. Find $t^* = \arg\min_t \alpha_{\text{eff}}(t)$.
3. Collapse the subtree at $t^*$ into a leaf.
4. Repeat until only the root remains.
5. Use **k-fold cross-validation** to select $\alpha^*$ that minimizes CV error.
6. The optimal pruned tree $T_{\alpha^*}$ is the final model.

**Practical Note:** In scikit-learn, this is implemented via `DecisionTreeClassifier(criterion='gini', ccp_alpha=α)`.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (Resulting in Mark Deduction):**
>
> 1. **Confusing CART with ID3/C4.5:** Many students mistakenly claim CART supports multi-way splits. **FACT:** CART is strictly **binary splitting** at every node. Multi-way splits are an ID3/C4.5 feature.
>
> 2. **Wrong leaf prediction for regression:** Students often write "median" instead of "mean" as the leaf prediction. **FACT:** Standard CART regression trees predict the **mean** of training targets in the leaf. (Some variants like **MAE criterion** use median — but the default MSE-based tree uses mean.)
>
> 3. **Forgetting the weighted impurity formula:** A common error is computing $\Delta G$ as $G(P) - G(L) - G(R)$ instead of the **weighted** version $G(P) - \frac{N_L}{N}G(L) - \frac{N_R}{N}G(R)$. This causes incorrect split selection.
>
> 4. **Mixing Gini Impurity with Gini Coefficient:** In economics, the Gini coefficient is a measure of inequality. In CART, Gini impurity is a **node disorder metric**. Using the wrong context will be penalized.
>
> 5. **Omitting the logarithm base:** Always write $\log_2$ for entropy in CART, not $\ln$ or $\log_{10}$. Examiners specifically check this.
>
> 6. **Ignoring the cost-complexity pruning step:** Full-grown CART trees are overfit. A complete answer must include **pruning** as a mandatory step.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (CART):**

- **CART = Classification and Regression Trees** — Breiman et al., 1984.
- **Two flavors:** Classification Tree (categorical $Y$) and Regression Tree (continuous $Y$).
- **Binary splits only** — distinguishing feature of CART.
- **Algorithm:** Top-down greedy recursive binary splitting.
- **Split selection:** Maximize impurity reduction $\Delta I$ (Gini Gain for classification, Variance Reduction for regression).
- **Gini Impurity:** $G(t) = 1 - \sum p_c^2$, range $[0, 1 - 1/C]$.
- **Entropy:** $H(t) = -\sum p_c \log_2 p_c$, range $[0, \log_2 C]$.
- **Information Gain:** $\text{IG} = H(P) - \sum \frac{N_k}{N} H(R_k)$.
- **Regression criterion:** Variance reduction = $\text{Var}(P) - \sum \frac{N_k}{N} \text{Var}(R_k)$.
- **Leaf prediction (Classification):** Majority class (mode).
- **Leaf prediction (Regression):** Mean of $Y$ values.
- **Pruning:** Cost-complexity with $R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha \vert T \vert$.
- **Effective alpha:** $\alpha_{\text{eff}}(t) = \frac{R(t) - R(T_t)}{\vert T_t \vert - 1}$.
- **Stopping criteria:** Minimum samples per leaf, max depth, min impurity decrease.
- **Advantages:** Interpretable, handles mixed data, no feature scaling needed, captures non-linear relationships.
- **Disadvantages:** Unstable (high variance), prone to overfitting, biased toward features with more levels.
- **Key practical tip:** Always use cross-validation to select $\alpha$ and max depth — never deploy a fully grown tree.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feature Selection Measures - Entropy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT501 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Feature Selection Measures - Entropy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (Shannon Entropy)

In Information Theory and Machine Learning, **Entropy** is a foundational impurity measure that quantifies the **average amount of information** (or uncertainty/disorder) contained in a random variable. It was introduced by **Claude E. Shannon** in his 1948 paper *"A Mathematical Theory of Communication."*

For a discrete random variable $X$ that can take $n$ possible distinct outcomes $\lbrace x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n \rbrace$ with probability distribution $P(X = x_i) = p_i$, the Shannon Entropy is rigorously defined as:

$$H(X) \;=\; -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_{2}(p_i)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Entropy is the base impurity function used by classical decision tree algorithms (e.g., **ID3**, **C4.5**) to greedily select the *best* feature for splitting a node. A feature that maximally reduces entropy provides the highest **Information Gain (IG)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Surprise Coin")

Imagine you are predicting the weather:
- **Case A:** A biased coin where it rains **every single day**. The outcome is certain — you are never "surprised."  
- **Case B:** A fair coin where rain or shine is equally likely — you are **maximally surprised** every morning because anything can happen.

Entropy formalizes this *surprise*. A deterministic variable has **zero** surprise (zero entropy). A perfectly balanced variable maximizes surprise (entropy = $\log_2 n$ bits for $n$ classes).

| Scenario | Probability of Rain ($p$) | Entropy $H$ (bits) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Always rains | 1.0 | 0.00 |
| 50/50 Weather | 0.5 | 1.00 |
| Equally likely among 4 seasons | 0.25 each | 2.00 |

## 1.3 Why Entropy Matters for Feature Selection

In supervised learning, when we have a labeled dataset $S$ with class labels, we want to find a feature $A$ that, when used to partition $S$ into subsets, makes the resulting child nodes **as pure as possible** (i.e., low entropy). The reduction in parent entropy minus the weighted child entropy is the **Information Gain**, which is the ID3 splitting criterion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Binary Entropy Function:** For the very common binary classification case ($n=2$), entropy simplifies to $H(p) = -p\log_2 p - (1-p)\log_2(1-p)$, reaching its maximum of **1 bit** at $p = 0.5$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic Behind Entropy

The function $H(X)$ is constructed from three axioms (Shannon's uniqueness theorem):

1. **Continuity:** $H$ is a continuous function of the probabilities $p_i$.
2. **Maximality:** $H$ is maximized when all outcomes are equiprobable ($p_i = 1/n$).
3. **Additivity (Recursion):** Splitting an $n$-outcome experiment into two stages preserves the total information.

From these, the **only** function satisfying them is $H(X) = -K\sum p_i \log p_i$. With base-2 logs, $K=1$ and the unit is the **bit**.

## 2.2 Key Properties of Entropy

- **Non-negativity:** $H(X) \ge 0$, because $0 \le p_i \le 1 \Rightarrow \log p_i \le 0$.
- **Strict positivity:** $H(X) = 0$ if and only if $X$ is **deterministic** (one outcome with $p=1$).
- **Upper bound:** $H(X) \le \log_2 n$, with equality when the distribution is uniform.
- **Symmetry:** Permuting class labels does not change $H$.
- **Concavity:** $H$ is a concave function of its probability vector (useful in optimization proofs).

## 2.3 Conditional Entropy

Conditional entropy measures the remaining uncertainty about $Y$ given the value of $X$. It is the weighted average of the entropy of each partition created by $X$:

$$H(Y \mid X) \;=\; \sum_{x \in X} P(X = x)\, H(Y \mid X = x)$$

## 2.4 Information Gain (ID3 Splitting Criterion)

$$\boxed{\;IG(S, A) \;=\; H(S) \;-\; H(S \mid A) \;=\; H(S) \;-\; \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}\, H(S_v)\;}$$

A feature $A^*$ is chosen as the splitting attribute at any node if it maximizes $IG(S, A)$ among all candidate features. Equivalently, it minimizes the **weighted child entropy** $H(S \mid A)$.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Notes / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Shannon Entropy** | $H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Unit: **bits** (base 2) |
| **Binary Entropy** | $H(p) = -p\log_2 p - (1-p)\log_2(1-p)$ | Max = **1 bit** at $p = 0.5$ |
| **Conditional Entropy** | $H(Y \mid X) = \sum_x P(x)\,H(Y \mid X = x)$ | Weighted average |
| **Information Gain** | $IG(S,A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$ | Always $\ge 0$ |
| **Weighted Split Entropy** | $H(S \mid A) = \sum_v \dfrac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | Splits weighted by size |
| **Entropy Upper Bound** | $H_{\max} = \log_2 c$ | Reached at uniform $p_i = 1/c$ |
| **Gain Ratio (C4.5)** | $GR = \dfrac{IG(S,A)}{\text{SplitInfo}(S,A)}$ | Normalizes $IG$ to penalize high-cardinality features |
| **Gini Impurity** (contrast) | $Gini(S) = 1 - \sum p_i^{\,2}$ | Used by CART; differs from entropy but similar shape |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** Entropy-based feature selection underpins spam filtering, medical diagnosis systems, credit-risk scoring, and any tree-based ensemble (Random Forests, XGBoost pre-pruning). It is also the foundation of the **KL-Divergence**, **Cross-Entropy Loss**, and **Mutual Information** used across modern deep learning.

## 2.6 Intuition Behind IG

- **High $IG$** $\Rightarrow$ The feature $A$ effectively reduces disorder; it is *informative*.
- **Low $IG$** $\Rightarrow$ The feature barely helps — child nodes are as mixed as the parent.
- **$IG = 0$** $\Rightarrow$ The feature provides **no classification benefit** at all.
<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation of the Maximum Entropy Bound

We want to prove that for a $c$-class discrete distribution, $H(S) \le \log_2 c$.

**Step 1 — Define the function to maximize.**  
Using calculus (or Lagrange multipliers), we seek $\max H$ subject to the constraint $\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i = 1$.

**Step 2 — Formulate the Lagrangian.**

$$\mathcal{L}(p_1, \dots, p_c, \lambda) \;=\; -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i \;-\; \lambda \left(\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i - 1\right)$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate w.r.t. $p_i$.**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial p_i} \;=\; -\log_2 p_i \;-\; \frac{1}{\ln 2} \;-\; \lambda \;=\; 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $p_i$.**  
Because the equation is symmetric across all $i$, the optimum must have $p_1 = p_2 = \dots = p_c$. Combined with the constraint $\sum p_i = 1$, we get $p_i = 1/c$ for all $i$.

**Step 5 — Substitute back.**

$$H_{\max} \;=\; -\sum_{i=1}^{c} \frac{1}{c} \log_2 \!\left(\frac{1}{c}\right) \;=\; \frac{1}{c}\sum_{i=1}^{c} \log_2 c \;=\; \log_2 c$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion.** The entropy is bounded by $\log_2 c$, and equality holds only for the uniform distribution. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Entropy & Information Gain on a Toy Dataset

Consider a training set $S$ with **14 instances** for a binary classification problem: **9 positive (+)** and **5 negative (−)**. We are evaluating a candidate feature $A$ (say, *"Humidity"*) that partitions $S$ into three branches:

| Subset | Size | Positive (+) | Negative (−) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $S_1$ (High) | 5 | 4 | 1 |
| $S_2$ (Normal) | 6 | 3 | 3 |
| $S_3$ (Low) | 3 | 2 | 1 |

### Step 1 — Compute the Parent Entropy $H(S)$

The class probabilities in $S$ are $p_+ = 9/14$ and $p_- = 5/14$.

$$H(S) \;=\; -\frac{9}{14}\log_2\!\left(\frac{9}{14}\right) \;-\; \frac{5}{14}\log_2\!\left(\frac{5}{14}\right)$$

**Intermediate calculations:**

$$\log_2\!\left(\frac{9}{14}\right) \;=\; \log_2 9 - \log_2 14 \;\approx\; 3.1699 - 3.8074 \;\approx\; -0.6375$$

$$\log_2\!\left(\frac{5}{14}\right) \;=\; \log_2 5 - \log_2 14 \;\approx\; 2.3219 - 3.8074 \;\approx\; -1.4855$$

**Substitute:**

$$H(S) \;\approx\; -0.6429 \times (-0.6375) \;-\; 0.3571 \times (-1.4855)$$

$$H(S) \;\approx\; 0.4098 \;+\; 0.5304 \;\approx\; 0.9402 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Each Child Entropy

**For $S_1$ (4+, 1−):** $p_+ = 4/5,\; p_- = 1/5$.

$$H(S_1) \;=\; -\frac{4}{5}\log_2\!\left(\frac{4}{5}\right) \;-\; \frac{1}{5}\log_2\!\left(\frac{1}{5}\right) \;\approx\; 0.7219 \text{ bits}$$

**For $S_2$ (3+, 3−):** $p_+ = 3/6 = 0.5,\; p_- = 0.5$.

$$H(S_2) \;=\; -0.5\log_2 0.5 - 0.5\log_2 0.5 \;=\; 1.0000 \text{ bits}$$

**For $S_3$ (2+, 1−):** $p_+ = 2/3,\; p_- = 1/3$.

$$H(S_3) \;=\; -\frac{2}{3}\log_2\!\left(\frac{2}{3}\right) \;-\; \frac{1}{3}\log_2\!\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) \;\approx\; 0.9183 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Weighted Split Entropy $H(S \mid A)$

$$H(S \mid A) \;=\; \frac{5}{14} H(S_1) \;+\; \frac{6}{14} H(S_2) \;+\; \frac{3}{14} H(S_3)$$

$$H(S \mid A) \;\approx\; 0.3571 \times 0.7219 \;+\; 0.4286 \times 1.0000 \;+\; 0.2143 \times 0.9183$$

$$H(S \mid A) \;\approx\; 0.2578 \;+\; 0.4286 \;+\; 0.1968 \;\approx\; 0.8832 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Information Gain

$$IG(S, A) \;=\; H(S) \;-\; H(S \mid A) \;\approx\; 0.9402 \;-\; 0.8832 \;\approx\; 0.0570 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation

The feature $A$ provides only **0.057 bits** of information. Compare this with other features (e.g., *"Outlook"* might yield 0.247 bits). Since the ID3 algorithm picks the feature with the **highest IG**, *Outlook* would be chosen over *Humidity* at the root node.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always show the **log base-2 calculations** explicitly. Marks are awarded for the breakdown, not just the final answer. A common slip is using natural log ($\ln$) and forgetting to convert, leading to the wrong unit (nats instead of bits).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Entropy-Based Feature Selection Pipeline

The block diagram below maps the operational flow of entropy-driven feature selection inside a classical decision tree learner.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Start: Labeled Dataset S]) --> rawData[Raw Training Set S with class labels]
    rawData --> computeParent[Step 1: Compute Parent Entropy H of S]
    computeParent --> featureLoop[Step 2: For each candidate feature A]
    featureLoop --> splitStep[Step 3: Partition S by values of A into subsets S sub v]
    splitStep --> childEntropy[Step 4: Compute H of S sub v for every v]
    childEntropy --> weightedAvg[Step 5: Compute weighted average H of S given A]
    weightedAvg --> igCalc[Step 6: Compute Information Gain IG]
    igCalc --> compareLoop{Step 7: IG greater than current best}
    compareLoop -- yes --> updateBest[Update best feature A star to current A]
    compareLoop -- no --> keepBest[Keep previous best feature]
    updateBest --> nextFeature[Move to next feature]
    keepBest --> nextFeature
    nextFeature --> checkDone{All features evaluated}
    checkDone -- no --> featureLoop
    checkDone -- yes --> selectRoot[Step 8: Select A star as splitting node]
    selectRoot --> recurseLeft[Step 9: Recurse on left child subset]
    selectRoot --> recurseRight[Step 10: Recurse on right child subset]
    recurseLeft --> stopCond1{Pure node or max depth}
    stopCond1 -- yes --> leafL[Create leaf node]
    stopCond1 -- no --> rawData
    recurseRight --> stopCond2{Pure node or max depth}
    stopCond2 -- yes --> leafR[Create leaf node]
    stopCond2 -- no --> rawData
    leafL --> endNode([End: Decision Tree Built])
    leafR --> endNode
```

## 4.2 Binary Entropy Curve (Conceptual Schematic)

The curve below illustrates the **canonical shape** of the binary entropy function $H(p)$ for a two-class problem. This shape is fundamental — every board-exam question on entropy involves recognizing that $H$ is **maximal at $p = 0.5$** and **zero at $p = 0$ and $p = 1$**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    originNode([Origin p equals 0]) --> lowPointNode[H equals 0 bits: Pure node]
    lowPointNode --> midValNode[p equals 0.3 region]
    midValNode --> peakValNode[p equals 0.5: H equals 1 bit, maximum disorder]
    peakValNode --> highValNode[p equals 0.7 region]
    highValNode --> endPureNode([p equals 1: H equals 0 bits: Pure node])
    lowPointNode --- noteA["Node purity corresponds to H equals 0"]
    peakValNode --- noteB["Maximum impurity corresponds to H equals 1"]
```

## 4.3 Information Gain Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootS[Node S, Entropy H of S] --> splitFeat[Split using feature A]
    splitFeat --> childS1[Child S1]
    splitFeat --> childS2[Child S2]
    splitFeat --> childS3[Child S3]
    childS1 --> hS1[Compute H of S1]
    childS2 --> hS2[Compute H of S2]
    childS3 --> hS3[Compute H of S3]
    hS1 --> weightedCombine[Weighted Sum gives H of S given A]
    hS2 --> weightedCombine
    hS3 --> weightedCombine
    weightedCombine --> igReduce[IG equals H of S minus H of S given A]
    igReduce --> rankFeat[Rank features by IG]
    rankFeat --> pickBest[Pick A star that maximizes IG]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Shannon's Entropy. List any four of its important properties.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer:**

Shannon's Entropy of a discrete random variable $X$ with probability distribution $p_i$ is:

$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$$

**Properties (any four):**

1. **Non-negativity:** $H(X) \ge 0$ for all distributions.
2. **Bounded above:** $H(X) \le \log_2 n$, with equality for uniform distribution.
3. **Determinism:** $H(X) = 0$ iff $X$ is deterministic.
4. **Concavity:** $H$ is a concave function of the probability vector.
5. **Symmetry:** Permuting class labels leaves $H$ unchanged.
6. **Continuity:** $H$ is continuous in each $p_i$.

---

### **Q2. What role does entropy play in feature selection for decision tree induction?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Entropy serves as the **impurity measure** for a node in a decision tree. The goal of feature selection is to find the feature that, when used to split the dataset, **maximally reduces** the entropy of the resulting child nodes. This reduction is quantified as the **Information Gain (IG)**:

$$IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$$

The feature with the highest IG (equivalently, the lowest weighted child entropy) is greedily chosen by algorithms such as **ID3** and **C4.5** as the splitting attribute. Features with $IG = 0$ provide no classification benefit and are discarded.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]`

**(a) Derive Shannon's Entropy formula from first principles and explain its key properties. State and prove the upper bound $H(X) \le \log_2 n$.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

*Derivation:* Shannon imposed three axioms on a measure of information $H(p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n)$:
1. Continuity in each $p_i$.
2. Monotonicity (more equiprobable outcomes $\Rightarrow$ higher $H$).
3. Recursive grouping (splitting an $n$-outcome experiment into sub-experiments preserves $H$).

The unique function satisfying all three is:

$$H(p_1, \dots, p_n) = -K \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log p_i$$

With base-2 logarithms and $K=1$, $H$ is measured in **bits**.

*Proof of upper bound:* Using the concavity of $\log$ and Jensen's inequality:

$$\log_2 n \;=\; \log_2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot \frac{1}{p_i} \;\ge\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 \frac{1}{p_i} \;=\; H(X)$$

Equality holds iff $p_i = 1/n$ for all $i$ (uniform distribution). $\blacksquare$

*Key properties:* Non-negativity, concavity, symmetry, continuity, and the bounded range $0 \le H \le \log_2 n$.

---

**(b) Consider a dataset $S$ of 16 instances with 10 belonging to class **C1** and 6 to class **C2**. A candidate feature $A$ splits $S$ into two subsets: $S_1$ with (8 C1, 2 C2) and $S_2$ with (2 C1, 4 C2). Compute the entropy of $S$, the entropy of each subset, and the Information Gain of feature $A$.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Parent Entropy:**

$$H(S) = -\frac{10}{16}\log_2 \frac{10}{16} - \frac{6}{16}\log_2 \frac{6}{16}$$

$$H(S) = -0.625 \times \log_2 0.625 - 0.375 \times \log_2 0.375$$

$$H(S) \approx -0.625 \times (-0.6781) - 0.375 \times (-1.4150) \approx 0.4238 + 0.5306 \approx 0.9544 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Child Entropies:**

For $S_1$ (8 C1, 2 C2): $p_{C1} = 0.8,\; p_{C2} = 0.2$.

$$H(S_1) = -0.8\log_2 0.8 - 0.2\log_2 0.2 \approx -0.8 \times (-0.3219) - 0.2 \times (-2.3219)$$

$$H(S_1) \approx 0.2575 + 0.4644 \approx 0.7219 \text{ bits}$$

For $S_2$ (2 C1, 4 C2): $p_{C1} = 0.333,\; p_{C2} = 0.667$.

$$H(S_2) = -\frac{1}{3}\log_2 \frac{1}{3} - \frac{2}{3}\log_2 \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.9183 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 — Weighted Split Entropy:**

$$H(S \mid A) = \frac{10}{16} \times 0.7219 + \frac{6}{16} \times 0.9183$$

$$H(S \mid A) \approx 0.625 \times 0.7219 + 0.375 \times 0.9183 \approx 0.4512 + 0.3444 \approx 0.7956 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4 — Information Gain:**

$$IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A) \approx 0.9544 - 0.7956 \approx 0.1588 \text{ bits}$$

**Interpretation:** Feature $A$ reduces entropy by ~0.16 bits. If another feature $B$ has a higher IG (e.g., 0.32 bits), then $B$ is preferred at this node.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a) Define Conditional Entropy and Information Gain. Derive the mathematical relationship between them, and explain why Information Gain is non-negative.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

*Conditional Entropy:* Given a feature $A$ that partitions $S$ into subsets $S_v$, the conditional entropy is the weighted average of child entropies:

$$H(S \mid A) = \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$$

*Information Gain:* It is the **difference** between the parent entropy and the weighted child entropy:

$$IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$$

*Derivation of $IG \ge 0$:* This follows from the **concavity** of entropy. By Jensen's inequality, the weighted average of the child entropies is bounded above by the parent entropy:

$$H(S) = H\!\left( \sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \right) \ge \sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v) = H(S \mid A)$$

Therefore, $IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A) \ge 0$, with equality only when $A$ provides no information. $\blacksquare$

*Interpretation:* Splitting on a feature cannot increase disorder on average; the best features are those that come closest to zero entropy in the children.

---

**(b) The table below shows a small training set. Compute the entropy of the target attribute **Play** and then determine which feature (Outlook, Wind, or Humidity) should be chosen as the root node using Information Gain.**

| Day | Outlook | Humidity | Wind | Play |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | Sunny | High | Weak | No |
| 2 | Sunny | High | Strong | No |
| 3 | Overcast | High | Weak | Yes |
| 4 | Rain | High | Weak | Yes |
| 5 | Rain | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| 6 | Rain | Normal | Strong | No |
| 7 | Overcast | Normal | Strong | Yes |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Entropy of Play (Target):**  
Counts: 4 Yes, 3 No; total = 7.

$$H(\text{Play}) = -\frac{4}{7}\log_2 \frac{4}{7} - \frac{3}{7}\log_2 \frac{3}{7} \approx 0.9852 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — IG for Outlook:**
- *Sunny:* {No, No} → $H = 0$
- *Overcast:* {Yes, Yes} → $H = 0$
- *Rain:* {Yes, Yes, No} → $p_{Yes} = 2/3,\; p_{No} = 1/3 \Rightarrow H \approx 0.9183$

$$H(\text{Play} \mid \text{Outlook}) = \frac{2}{7}(0) + \frac{2}{7}(0) + \frac{3}{7}(0.9183) \approx 0.3936$$

$$IG(\text{Play}, \text{Outlook}) = 0.9852 - 0.3936 \approx 0.5916 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 — IG for Humidity:**
- *High:* {No, No, Yes, Yes} → $p_{Yes} = 1/2 \Rightarrow H = 1.0$
- *Normal:* {Yes, Yes, No, Yes} → $p_{Yes} = 3/4 \Rightarrow H \approx 0.8113$

$$H(\text{Play} \mid \text{Humidity}) = \frac{4}{7}(1.0) + \frac{3}{7}(0.8113) \approx 0.5714 + 0.3477 \approx 0.9191$$

$$IG(\text{Play}, \text{Humidity}) \approx 0.9852 - 0.9191 \approx 0.0661 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4 — IG for Wind:**
- *Weak:* {No, Yes, Yes, Yes} → $p_{Yes} = 3/4 \Rightarrow H \approx 0.8113$
- *Strong:* {No, No, Yes} → $p_{Yes} = 1/3 \Rightarrow H \approx 0.9183$

$$H(\text{Play} \mid \text{Wind}) = \frac{4}{7}(0.8113) + \frac{3}{7}(0.9183) \approx 0.4636 + 0.3936 \approx 0.8572$$

$$IG(\text{Play}, \text{Wind}) \approx 0.9852 - 0.8572 \approx 0.1280 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 5 — Decision:**

| Feature | IG (bits) |
| :--- | :---: |
| **Outlook** | **0.5916** ← Highest |
| Wind | 0.1280 |
| Humidity | 0.0661 |

Since **Outlook** has the maximum Information Gain, it is selected as the **root node** of the ID3 decision tree.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Log base confusion:** Students frequently compute $\ln$ instead of $\log_2$, leading to entropy in *nats* instead of *bits*. Always use base-2 unless the question specifies otherwise.
> 2. **Missing weighting:** A common mistake is to *unweighted-average* the child entropies instead of weighting by $|S_v|/|S|$. This produces a wrong $H(S \mid A)$ and an incorrect IG.
> 3. **$0 \log 0$ trap:** When a child subset has only one class ($H = 0$), do NOT attempt to compute $0 \log 0$ — by convention, this term is defined as **0**.
> 4. **Picking the wrong feature:** Students often pick the feature with the *lowest* IG thinking it is "best." Remember: **higher IG = better feature** (greater reduction in disorder).
> 5. **Skipping the weighted ratio:** Always write $\frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ explicitly; this earns method marks even if the arithmetic is slightly off.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 (Module 2 — Entropy)**

- **Definition:** $H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i$, where $p_i$ is the proportion of class $i$ in $S$.
- **Unit:** Bits (base-2 log). Use natural log only if explicitly asked.
- **Range:** $0 \le H(S) \le \log_2 c$. $H = 0$ iff node is **pure**.
- **Binary entropy max:** At $p = 0.5$, $H = 1$ bit (maximum disorder).
- **Conditional Entropy:** $H(S \mid A) = \sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$.
- **Information Gain:** $IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$. **Choose the feature that maximizes IG.**
- **Non-negativity:** $IG \ge 0$ always (follows from concavity of entropy / Jensen's inequality).
- **Conventions:** $0 \log_2 0 = 0$; never compute $\log$ of zero directly.
- **Algorithm mapping:** **ID3 → entropy + IG**; **C4.5 → Gain Ratio** $IG / \text{SplitInfo}$; **CART → Gini Impurity**.
- **Limitations:** IG is biased toward features with many distinct values — C4.5 corrects this with Gain Ratio.
- **Higher-order uses:** Entropy is the foundation of **Cross-Entropy Loss**, **KL-Divergence**, **Mutual Information**, and **Decision Tree pruning** (Minimum Description Length principle).
- **Exam mantra:** *"High IG = High Information = Best Feature to Split."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Information Gain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT501 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Information Gain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Information Gain (IG)** is a statistical metric used in decision tree learning algorithms (notably **ID3** and **C4.5**) to measure the **reduction in entropy** (uncertainty) of the target variable achieved by partitioning the dataset on a particular feature attribute. Mathematically, it is the difference between the **prior entropy** of the entire dataset and the **conditional (weighted) entropy** of the dataset after splitting on attribute $A$.

Formally, for a dataset $S$ and attribute $A$:

$$\text{IG}(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$$

Where:
- $H(S)$ = Entropy of the original dataset $S$ (measure of disorder).
- $H(S \mid A)$ = Weighted average entropy of subsets created by splitting on $A$.

The attribute that **maximizes** Information Gain is selected as the splitting criterion at each node of the decision tree.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "20 Questions" Game:**
>
> Imagine you are playing the game *"20 Questions"* where you must guess a mystery object by asking yes/no questions. A *good* question is one that **drastically reduces the space of possible answers**. For example, asking *"Is it alive?"* cuts the possibilities in half, whereas asking *"Is it green?"* leaves the space mostly intact.
>
> **Information Gain** is exactly the mathematical quantification of *"How much did this question (attribute) reduce my uncertainty?"* The higher the IG, the better the attribute is at organizing the data into pure, homogeneous classes. The decision tree algorithm greedily picks the attribute with the **highest** IG at every node — just like a smart 20-Questions player picks the most informative question first.

## 1.3 Core Terminology

- **Entropy:** A measure of impurity, randomness, or disorder in a dataset, originating from **Shannon's Information Theory (1948)**. For a binary classification, $H \in [0, 1]$.
- **Conditional Entropy:** The expected entropy of the target after partitioning by attribute $A$.
- **Information Gain:** Reduction in entropy caused by knowing the value of attribute $A$. Always **non-negative** for discrete attributes under standard definitions.
- **Pure Node:** A node where all samples belong to a single class ($H = 0$).
- **Greedy Splitting:** Decision trees choose splits **locally optimally** (max IG at that node), without guaranteeing global optimality.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Conceptual Breakdown

### Step 1 — Compute the Prior Entropy of the Target

The dataset $S$ contains $c$ distinct classes with proportions $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_c$. Shannon's entropy is:

$$H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2(p_i)$$

- **Why?** Entropy reaches its **maximum** (most disorder) when classes are uniformly distributed, and **minimum (zero)** when all instances belong to one class.
- The base of the logarithm is **2** (bits). Convention in information theory.

### Step 2 — Partition the Dataset on Attribute $A$

Attribute $A$ with $v$ distinct values $\{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_v\}$ partitions $S$ into $v$ subsets $\{S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_v\}$.

### Step 3 — Compute the Weighted Conditional Entropy

Each subset $S_j$ has its own entropy $H(S_j)$. The conditional entropy is the **size-weighted average**:

$$H(S \mid A) = \sum_{j=1}^{v} \frac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert} \cdot H(S_j)$$

- **Why weighted?** Larger subsets carry more influence on the overall impurity. Weight $\frac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ is the **prior probability** of subset $S_j$.

### Step 4 — Compute Information Gain

$$\boxed{\text{IG}(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)}$$

- The attribute maximizing IG is selected.
- The algorithm **recurses** on each child subset.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Range / Boundary Conditions | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|----------------------------|-------|
| 1 | Shannon Entropy | $H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $0 \le H(S) \le \log_2 c$ | $0$ when pure; max when uniform |
| 2 | Binary Entropy | $H = -p\log_2 p - (1-p)\log_2(1-p)$ | $0 \le H \le 1$ | Used for 2-class problems |
| 3 | Conditional Entropy | $H(S \mid A) = \sum_j \frac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_j)$ | $0 \le H(S \mid A) \le H(S)$ | Weighted average of subset entropies |
| 4 | Information Gain | $\text{IG}(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$ | $\text{IG} \ge 0$ | Higher = better split |
| 5 | Split Information | $H_{\text{split}}(A) = -\sum_j \frac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \frac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ | $H_{\text{split}} \ge 0$ | Used in **Gain Ratio** (C4.5) |
| 6 | Gain Ratio | $\text{GR}(A) = \frac{\text{IG}(S,A)}{H_{\text{split}}(A)}$ | $\text{GR} \ge 0$ | Normalizes IG to penalize multi-valued attributes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention used in KTU exam questions:** Probability $p = 0$ contributes $0$ to entropy (limit: $\lim_{p \to 0^+} p \log_2 p = 0$).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Diagnosis Systems:** Selects the most discriminative symptom for early-stage disease classification.
- **Spam Filtering:** Identifies the email header feature (e.g., specific keywords) that best separates spam from legitimate mail.
- **Fraud Detection in Banking:** Picks the transaction attribute (amount, location, time) that most reduces uncertainty about fraud.
- **Industrial Quality Control:** Determines the manufacturing process variable (temperature, pressure) most responsible for defects.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** Consider the following training dataset $S$ of 14 instances. The target attribute is **"Play Tennis"** (Yes / No). Compute the **Information Gain** for the attribute **"Wind"**.

| Day | Outlook | Temp | Humidity | **Wind** | Play |
|-----|---------|------|----------|----------|------|
| 1   | Sunny   | Hot  | High     | Weak     | No   |
| 2   | Sunny   | Hot  | High     | Strong   | No   |
| 3   | Overcast| Hot | High     | Weak     | Yes  |
| 4   | Rain    | Mild | High     | Weak     | Yes  |
| 5   | Rain    | Cool | Normal   | Weak     | Yes  |
| 6   | Rain    | Cool | Normal   | Strong   | No   |
| 7   | Overcast| Cool| Normal   | Strong   | Yes  |
| 8   | Sunny   | Mild | High     | Weak     | No   |
| 9   | Sunny   | Cool | Normal   | Weak     | Yes  |
| 10  | Rain    | Mild | Normal   | Weak     | Yes  |
| 11  | Sunny   | Mild | Normal   | Strong   | Yes  |
| 12  | Overcast| Mild | High     | Strong   | Yes  |
| 13  | Overcast| Hot  | Normal   | Weak     | Yes  |
| 14  | Rain    | Mild | High     | Strong   | No   |

### Step 1 — Class Distribution in $S$

Out of 14 instances: **9 Yes** and **5 No**.

$$p_{\text{Yes}} = \frac{9}{14}, \quad p_{\text{No}} = \frac{5}{14}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Prior Entropy $H(S)$

$$H(S) = -\frac{9}{14} \log_2 \frac{9}{14} - \frac{5}{14} \log_2 \frac{5}{14}$$

Compute each term separately:

$$\log_2 \frac{9}{14} = \log_2 9 - \log_2 14 = 3.1699 - 3.8074 = -0.6374$$

$$\log_2 \frac{5}{14} = \log_2 5 - \log_2 14 = 2.3219 - 3.8074 = -1.4854$$

Therefore:

$$H(S) = -\left(\frac{9}{14}\right)(-0.6374) - \left(\frac{5}{14}\right)(-1.4854)$$

$$H(S) = 0.4097 + 0.5305 = 0.9402 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 3 — Partition on "Wind"

**Wind = Weak:** Instances {1, 3, 4, 5, 8, 9, 10, 13} → 8 total.
- Yes: {3, 4, 5, 9, 10, 13} → 6
- No: {1, 8} → 2

**Wind = Strong:** Instances {2, 6, 7, 11, 12, 14} → 6 total.
- Yes: {7, 11, 12} → 3
- No: {2, 6, 14} → 3

### Step 4 — Compute Entropy of Each Subset

For **Wind = Weak**:

$$H(S_{\text{Weak}}) = -\frac{6}{8} \log_2 \frac{6}{8} - \frac{2}{8} \log_2 \frac{2}{8}$$

$$\log_2 \frac{6}{8} = \log_2 0.75 = -0.4150, \quad \log_2 \frac{2}{8} = -2.0000$$

$$H(S_{\text{Weak}}) = -0.75 \times (-0.4150) - 0.25 \times (-2.0000)$$

$$H(S_{\text{Weak}}) = 0.3113 + 0.5000 = 0.8113 \text{ bits}$$

For **Wind = Strong** (perfect 50-50 split — maximum binary entropy):

$$H(S_{\text{Strong}}) = -\frac{3}{6} \log_2 \frac{3}{6} - \frac{3}{6} \log_2 \frac{3}{6}$$

$$H(S_{\text{Strong}}) = -0.5 \times (-1) - 0.5 \times (-1) = 1.0000 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 5 — Compute Weighted Conditional Entropy

$$H(S \mid \text{Wind}) = \frac{8}{14} H(S_{\text{Weak}}) + \frac{6}{14} H(S_{\text{Strong}})$$

$$H(S \mid \text{Wind}) = \left(\frac{8}{14}\right)(0.8113) + \left(\frac{6}{14}\right)(1.0000)$$

$$H(S \mid \text{Wind}) = 0.4636 + 0.4286 = 0.8922 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 6 — Compute Information Gain for Wind

$$\text{IG}(S, \text{Wind}) = H(S) - H(S \mid \text{Wind})$$

$$\text{IG}(S, \text{Wind}) = 0.9402 - 0.8922 = 0.0480 \text{ bits}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Splitting on "Wind" reduces entropy by only **0.048 bits** — a very poor split. We would prefer attributes with much higher IG (e.g., "Outlook" typically yields $\text{IG} \approx 0.246$).

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Attribute | $H(S)$ | $H(S \mid A)$ | $\text{IG}(S, A)$ | Rank |
|-----------|--------|---------------|-------------------|------|
| Outlook   | 0.9402 | 0.6935        | **0.2467**        | 1st  |
| Humidity  | 0.9402 | 0.7885        | **0.1517**        | 2nd  |
| Wind      | 0.9402 | 0.8922        | **0.0480**        | 3rd  |
| Temp      | 0.9402 | 0.9108        | **0.0294**        | 4th  |

> The root of the ID3 decision tree is chosen as **Outlook** (highest IG).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Information Gain Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Dataset S with target Y] --> B[Compute prior entropy H of S using class proportions]
    B --> C[For each candidate attribute A]
    C --> D[Partition S into subsets by values of A]
    D --> E[Compute entropy of each subset Sj]
    E --> F[Compute weighted sum conditional entropy H of S given A]
    F --> G[Subtract to get IG of S and A]
    G --> H{All attributes evaluated?}
    H -->|No| C
    H -->|Yes| I[Select attribute with MAX IG]
    I --> J[Make it the root of decision node]
    J --> K[Recurse on each child subset until pure]
    K --> L[End: Decision Tree built]
```

## 4.2 Entropy vs. Probability (Binary Classification)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[p equals 0 or p equals 1] --> B[H equals 0: PURE NODE]
    C[p equals 0.5] --> D[H equals 1: MAXIMUM DISORDER]
    E[p between 0 and 1] --> F[0 less than H less than 1: PARTIAL IMPURITY]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree Root Selection Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Space]
        A1[Outlook: IG equals 0.2467]
        A2[Humidity: IG equals 0.1517]
        A3[Wind: IG equals 0.0480]
        A4[Temperature: IG equals 0.0294]
    end
    subgraph ARGMIN[Argmax Selector]
        B[max IG = 0.2467]
    end
    INPUT --> ARGMIN
    ARGMIN --> C[Root Node = Outlook]
    C --> D[Branch: Sunny subset]
    C --> E[Branch: Overcast subset pure Yes]
    C --> F[Branch: Rain subset]
```

## 4.4 ID3 Algorithmic Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Phase 1: Base Case Check] --> Q{All samples in one class?}
    Q -->|Yes| R[Return leaf labeled with class]
    Q -->|No| S[Phase 2: Split Selection]
    S --> T[Compute IG for every attribute]
    T --> U[Pick attribute A* with max IG]
    U --> V[Phase 3: Recursive Partitioning]
    V --> W[Create child node for each value of A*]
    W --> X[Recurse ID3 on each child subset]
    X --> Y[Return complete decision tree]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]
**Define Information Gain. Why is it preferred over random attribute selection in decision tree induction?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Information Gain (IG) is a measure of the **reduction in entropy** of the target variable achieved by partitioning the dataset on a given attribute $A$. It is defined as:

$$\text{IG}(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$$

where $H(S)$ is the entropy of the original set and $H(S \mid A)$ is the weighted average entropy after splitting on $A$.

**Why preferred (2 marks):**
1. **Quantitative basis** — Provides a rigorous mathematical criterion rather than arbitrary choice.
2. **Maximizes information** — Selects the attribute that contributes the most to classifying the target, leading to **smaller, more accurate trees**.
3. **Greedy optimality** — At each node, ID3 makes the locally optimal split, ensuring impurity reduction is maximized at every level.
4. **Reduces overfitting risk** — Pure partitions early in the tree mean fewer training errors and better generalization.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**What is Shannon Entropy? State its value when (i) all samples belong to one class, and (ii) classes are equally distributed in a 2-class problem.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Shannon Entropy is a foundational concept from **Information Theory (1948)** that quantifies the **average amount of information (in bits)** needed to identify the class of a randomly drawn sample from a set $S$. It is given by:

$$H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i$$

**(i) Pure node (all samples same class):** When $p_i = 1$ for some $i$ and $0$ for others,

$$H(S) = -1 \cdot \log_2 1 = 0 \text{ bits}$$

**(ii) Equally distributed 2-class problem:** When $p_1 = p_2 = 0.5$,

$$H(S) = -0.5 \log_2 0.5 - 0.5 \log_2 0.5 = 1 \text{ bit (maximum)}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting to use **$\log_2$** instead of $\log_{10}$ or natural log. KTU expects base **2** for entropy.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Omitting the **weighting** $\frac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ when computing $H(S \mid A)$. Simply averaging the subset entropies yields **0 marks** for that step.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Failing to convert probabilities to fractions before substitution. Always reduce to $\frac{a}{b}$ form.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Rounding intermediate steps. Carry **at least 4 decimal places** until the final answer.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Not explicitly stating the **interpretation** of IG (i.e., which attribute to choose as the root). Examiners award marks for conclusion statements.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — Information Gain Computation

**Q3(a). [7 Marks]** Given the dataset below with target attribute **"Buys Computer"**, calculate the **Information Gain** for the attribute **"Income"** and **"Student"** separately. Show all intermediate calculations.

| RID | Age    | Income  | Student | Credit | Buys Computer |
|-----|--------|---------|---------|--------|---------------|
| 1   | Youth  | High    | No      | Fair   | No            |
| 2   | Youth  | High    | No      | Excellent | No         |
| 3   | Middle | High    | No      | Fair   | Yes           |
| 4   | Senior | Medium  | No      | Fair   | Yes           |
| 5   | Senior | Low     | Yes     | Fair   | Yes           |
| 6   | Senior | Low     | Yes     | Excellent | No         |
| 7   | Middle | Low     | Yes     | Excellent | Yes         |
| 8   | Youth  | Medium  | No      | Fair   | No            |
| 9   | Youth  | Low     | Yes     | Fair   | Yes           |
| 10  | Senior | Medium  | Yes     | Fair   | Yes           |
| 11  | Youth  | Medium  | Yes     | Excellent | Yes         |
| 12  | Middle | Medium  | No      | Excellent | Yes         |
| 13  | Middle | High    | Yes     | Fair   | Yes           |
| 14  | Senior | Medium  | No      | Excellent | No           |

**Step 1 — Class Frequencies**

Yes = 9, No = 5, Total = 14.

**Step 2 — Prior Entropy**

$$H(S) = -\frac{9}{14}\log_2 \frac{9}{14} - \frac{5}{14}\log_2 \frac{5}{14} = 0.9402 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 — IG for "Income"**

Partition on Income:
- **High** (rows 1, 2, 3, 13): Yes=2, No=2 → $H = 1.0000$
- **Medium** (rows 4, 8, 10, 11, 12, 14): Yes=4, No=2 → $H = 0.9183$
- **Low** (rows 5, 6, 7, 9): Yes=3, No=1 → $H = 0.8113$

Weighted entropy:

$$H(S \mid \text{Income}) = \frac{4}{14}(1.0000) + \frac{6}{14}(0.9183) + \frac{4}{14}(0.8113)$$

$$H(S \mid \text{Income}) = 0.2857 + 0.3936 + 0.2318 = 0.9111$$

$$\boxed{\text{IG}(S, \text{Income}) = 0.9402 - 0.9111 = 0.0291 \text{ bits}}$$

**Step 4 — IG for "Student"**

Partition on Student:
- **Yes** (rows 5, 6, 7, 9, 11, 13): Yes=6, No=1 → $H = 0.5917$
- **No** (rows 1, 2, 3, 4, 8, 10, 12, 14): Yes=3, No=4 → $H = 0.9852$

Weighted entropy:

$$H(S \mid \text{Student}) = \frac{7}{14}(0.5917) + \frac{7}{14}(0.9852)$$

$$H(S \mid \text{Student}) = 0.2959 + 0.4926 = 0.7885$$

$$\boxed{\text{IG}(S, \text{Student}) = 0.9402 - 0.7885 = 0.1517 \text{ bits}}$$

**Q3(b). [7 Marks]** Which attribute should be selected as the root of the decision tree? Justify your answer using the concept of Information Gain. Also, list **two limitations** of Information Gain as a splitting criterion.

**Model Solution:**

| Attribute | Information Gain |
|-----------|------------------|
| Age       | 0.2467           |
| Income    | 0.0291           |
| Student   | 0.1517           |
| Credit    | 0.0480           |

> The attribute **"Age"** has the **highest Information Gain (0.2467 bits)**, so it is selected as the **root node** of the ID3 decision tree. This choice maximally reduces uncertainty in classifying "Buys Computer" at the first split.

**Two Limitations of Information Gain:**

1. **Bias toward high-cardinality attributes:** Attributes with many distinct values (e.g., "Date", "Name", "ID") tend to produce near-pure partitions and artificially inflate IG. This is mitigated by the **Gain Ratio** in the C4.5 algorithm.
2. **Greedy, myopic selection:** ID3 picks the locally optimal attribute at each node without backtracking, which may lead to **suboptimal global trees**, especially in problems with complex feature interactions.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Comparative Analysis & Bias

**Q4(a). [7 Marks]** Explain with a numerical example why **Information Gain is biased** toward attributes with a **large number of distinct values**. Show how the **Gain Ratio** (used in C4.5) addresses this issue.

**Model Solution:**

Consider a dataset of 14 students with attributes "Student ID" (14 unique values) and "Passed" (Yes/No). Each student has a **unique** Student ID.

- Splitting on "Student ID" creates 14 pure subsets (each with 1 sample), so:

$$H(S \mid \text{Student ID}) = 0$$

$$\text{IG}(S, \text{Student ID}) = 0.9402 - 0 = 0.9402 \text{ bits (maximum!)}$$

This is the **maximum possible IG** — yet "Student ID" is **completely useless** for prediction! This demonstrates IG's **bias toward high-cardinality attributes**.

**Fix via Gain Ratio (C.4.5):**

$$\text{GR}(A) = \frac{\text{IG}(S, A)}{H_{\text{split}}(A)}$$

For "Student ID" with 14 values each having $\frac{1}{14}$ probability:

$$H_{\text{split}}(\text{Student ID}) = -\sum_{j=1}^{14} \frac{1}{14} \log_2 \frac{1}{14} = \log_2 14 = 3.8074$$

$$\text{GR}(\text{Student ID}) = \frac{0.9402}{3.8074} = 0.2469$$

For a more useful attribute with 2 values (each $\frac{7}{14}$):

$$H_{\text{split}} = -2 \times 0.5 \log_2 0.5 = 1.0$$

$$\text{GR} = \frac{\text{IG}}{1.0} = \text{IG}$$

Thus, Gain Ratio **normalizes** IG, penalizing attributes that spread the data thinly.

**Q4(b). [7 Marks]** A dataset has 10 samples, of which 4 are positive and 6 are negative. An attribute $A$ splits the data into subsets of sizes 4, 4, and 2 with class distributions (4+, 0−), (0+, 4−), and (0+, 2−) respectively. Calculate (i) the entropy of the original dataset, (ii) the conditional entropy after splitting, and (iii) the Information Gain.

**Step 1 — Original Entropy**

$$H(S) = -\frac{4}{10}\log_2 \frac{4}{10} - \frac{6}{10}\log_2 \frac{6}{10}$$

$$H(S) = -0.4 \log_2 0.4 - 0.6 \log_2 0.6$$

$$H(S) = -0.4(-1.3219) - 0.6(-0.7370) = 0.5288 + 0.4422 = 0.9710 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Entropy of Each Subset**

- Subset 1 (4+, 0−): pure → $H = 0$
- Subset 2 (0+, 4−): pure → $H = 0$
- Subset 3 (0+, 2−): pure → $H = 0$

**Step 3 — Conditional Entropy**

$$H(S \mid A) = \frac{4}{10}(0) + \frac{4}{10}(0) + \frac{2}{10}(0) = 0 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4 — Information Gain**

$$\boxed{\text{IG}(S, A) = 0.9710 - 0 = 0.9710 \text{ bits (perfect split)}}$$

**Conclusion:** Attribute $A$ produces a **perfect, pure partition** — it is an ideal root-node candidate.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Definition:** $\text{IG}(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$ — measures the **information** an attribute contributes to classification.
- **Logarithm Base:** Always $\log_2$ for entropy in decision tree contexts. Result is in **bits**.
- **Entropy Range:** $0 \le H \le \log_2 c$; binary case: $0 \le H \le 1$.
- **Conditional Entropy Formula:** Must be **weighted by subset size fraction** $\frac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert}$.
- **Maximum IG:** Achieved when the split yields **completely pure** subsets.
- **Bias of IG:** Favors attributes with **many distinct values**; rectified by **Gain Ratio** (C4.5) or **Gini Index** (CART).
- **Pure Node:** $H = 0$ — algorithm terminates (becomes a leaf).
- **Greedy Nature:** ID3 makes **locally optimal** decisions, not globally optimal.
- **Always carry 4-decimal intermediates:** Avoids accumulation of rounding error.
- **Statement Format for KTU answers:** Always conclude with: *"The attribute with maximum IG is selected as the root node."*
- **Related Metrics to Know:** Gini Impurity $Gini(S) = 1 - \sum_i p_i^2$, Classification Error $E(S) = 1 - \max_i p_i$ — used in CART.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ID3 Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT501 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** ID3 Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ID3 (Iterative Dichotomiser 3)** is a greedy, top-down, divide-and-conquer decision tree induction algorithm developed by **Ross Quinlan (1986)**. It builds a classification tree by recursively partitioning the training dataset using the attribute that yields the **maximum Information Gain** (equivalently, the minimum weighted entropy) at each internal node.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, ID3 is positioned under **Module 2 (Decision Tree Learning)** and is evaluated under Course Outcome **CO2**: *Apply suitable machine learning algorithms for real-world datasets and analyze the results.* It is a **supervised, non-parametric, classification** algorithm that produces a multivariate decision boundary in the form of an axis-aligned tree.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Doctor" Intuition

Imagine a doctor diagnosing whether a patient has the flu. Instead of running every expensive test on every patient, the doctor learns a mental flowchart from past cases:

1. *Does the patient have a fever?* → If **Yes**, run test A; if **No**, ask another question.
2. *Is the season winter?* → If **Yes**, likely flu; if **No**, less likely.

The doctor is essentially building a **decision tree** where each question *splits* the patient population into purer groups. **ID3 automates this exact intuition**:

- It treats every training example as a "patient case."
- It searches for the **most diagnostic question** (attribute with the highest information gain) — the one that, when asked, maximally reduces uncertainty about the diagnosis.
- It then **recursively** applies the same logic to each resulting sub-group.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Iterative Dichotomiser"?** *Dichotomiser* means a thing that splits into two (or more) groups. *Iterative* refers to its repeated, recursive application. *3* is simply the version number (predecessors were ID1 and ID2).

## 1.3 Key Terminology Anchors

| Term | KTU Syllabus Meaning |
|------|----------------------|
| **Entropy** $H(S)$ | A measure of impurity / disorder in a dataset $S$. |
| **Information Gain** $IG(S, A)$ | Reduction in entropy achieved by splitting $S$ on attribute $A$. |
| **Root Node** | The attribute with the highest Information Gain at the top of the tree. |
| **Leaf Node** | A terminal node that outputs the class label. |
| **Recursive Partitioning** | The algorithmic backbone — splitting until pure or stopping criteria met. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Logic — The Operational "Why" and "How"

ID3 follows a strict, deterministic decision flow at every node:

**Step 1 — Compute current impurity.** Calculate the entropy $H(S)$ of the dataset $S$ arriving at the current node.

**Step 2 — Evaluate every attribute.** For each candidate attribute $A$ with possible values $\{v_1, v_2, \dots, v_k\}$, partition $S$ into subsets $S_{v_1}, S_{v_2}, \dots, S_{v_k}$ and compute the weighted post-split entropy $H_A(S)$.

**Step 3 — Compute Information Gain.** $IG(S, A) = H(S) - H_A(S)$. The attribute with the **highest** $IG$ is selected as the splitting attribute.

**Step 4 — Branch.** Create a child node for each value of the chosen attribute and recursively invoke ID3 on each subset.

**Step 5 — Base cases (termination).** Stop when:
- All samples in the node belong to one class (pure node → leaf).
- No remaining attributes to split on (majority voting → leaf).
- The dataset is empty (majority of parent → leaf).
- A pre-pruning threshold (e.g., minimum samples per leaf) is reached.

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" behind Information Gain:** A high $IG$ means the attribute pushes the dataset closer to purity — every question that *resolves uncertainty* in the real world also resolves entropy in information theory. This is no coincidence; **Shannon's entropy** was designed exactly to quantify uncertainty.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Interpretation |
|---|----------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | **Entropy of a binary target** | $H(S) = -p_{\oplus}\log_2 p_{\oplus} - p_{\ominus}\log_2 p_{\ominus}$ | Impurity of dataset $S$; ranges $[0, 1]$ bits. |
| 2 | **Entropy of a C-class target** | $H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Generalization to multi-class problems. |
| 3 | **Conditional (Post-Split) Entropy** | $H_A(S) = \sum_{v \in Values(A)} \dfrac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | Weighted average entropy after splitting on $A$. |
| 4 | **Information Gain** | $IG(S, A) = H(S) - H_A(S)$ | Reduction in entropy caused by splitting on $A$. |
| 5 | **Split Information** | $SplitInfo(S, A) = -\sum_{v \in Values(A)} \dfrac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \dfrac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ | Intrinsic value of split (used in C4.5, not ID3). |
| 6 | **Gain Ratio** | $GR(S, A) = \dfrac{IG(S, A)}{SplitInfo(S, A)}$ | Normalized gain (C4.5 successor). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Rule:** $|S_v|$ denotes cardinality of subset $S_v$ (the count of instances where attribute $A = v$). Always write as `\vert S_v \vert` in LaTeX to avoid markdown table pipe conflicts.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

ID3 forms the conceptual backbone of modern tree-based ensemble methods such as **Random Forests** and **Gradient Boosted Trees (XGBoost, LightGBM)**. The Information Gain criterion (and its variants like Gini impurity) is used at every node of thousands of trees in production ML pipelines for medical diagnosis, credit scoring, churn prediction, and fault detection.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix — Worked Example

We will use the classic **"Play Tennis"** training set (14 instances) — a KTU-favourite dataset that has appeared in numerous model papers.

## 3.1 The Dataset

| Day | Outlook | Temperature | Humidity | Wind | PlayTennis |
|-----|---------|-------------|----------|------|------------|
| 1  | Sunny   | Hot         | High     | Weak | No         |
| 2  | Sunny   | Hot         | High     | Strong | No       |
| 3  | Overcast| Hot         | High     | Weak | Yes        |
| 4  | Rain    | Mild        | High     | Weak | Yes        |
| 5  | Rain    | Cool        | Normal   | Weak | Yes        |
| 6  | Rain    | Cool        | Normal   | Strong | No       |
| 7  | Overcast| Cool        | Normal   | Strong | Yes      |
| 8  | Sunny   | Mild        | High     | Weak | No         |
| 9  | Sunny   | Cool        | Normal   | Weak | Yes        |
| 10 | Rain    | Mild        | Normal   | Weak | Yes        |
| 11 | Sunny   | Mild        | Normal   | Strong | Yes      |
| 12 | Overcast| Mild        | High     | Strong | Yes      |
| 13 | Overcast| Hot         | Normal   | Weak | Yes        |
| 14 | Rain    | Mild        | High     | Strong | No       |

## 3.2 Step 1 — Root Entropy $H(S)$

Total samples $= 14$; **Yes = 9**, **No = 5**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S) &= -\frac{9}{14}\log_2\!\left(\frac{9}{14}\right) - \frac{5}{14}\log_2\!\left(\frac{5}{14}\right) \\
&= -0.6429 \times (-0.6375) - 0.3571 \times (-1.4854) \\
&= 0.4099 + 0.5305 \\
&= \mathbf{0.940\ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The dataset is moderately impure — close to the maximum entropy (1.0 bit) for a binary class.

## 3.3 Step 2 — Information Gain for Each Candidate Attribute

### 3.3.1 Gain for **Outlook**

Subsets:
- $S_{\text{Sunny}}$ = 5 samples (2 Yes, 3 No)
- $S_{\text{Overcast}}$ = 4 samples (4 Yes, 0 No)
- $S_{\text{Rain}}$ = 5 samples (3 Yes, 2 No)

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S_{\text{Sunny}}) &= -\tfrac{2}{5}\log_2\tfrac{2}{5} - \tfrac{3}{5}\log_2\tfrac{3}{5} = 0.971 \\
H(S_{\text{Overcast}}) &= -\tfrac{4}{4}\log_2\tfrac{4}{4} - 0 = 0 \\
H(S_{\text{Rain}}) &= -\tfrac{3}{5}\log_2\tfrac{3}{5} - \tfrac{2}{5}\log_2\tfrac{2}{5} = 0.971
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{Outlook}}(S) &= \tfrac{5}{14}(0.971) + \tfrac{4}{14}(0) + \tfrac{5}{14}(0.971) = 0.347 + 0 + 0.347 = 0.694 \\
IG(S, \text{Outlook}) &= 0.940 - 0.694 = \mathbf{0.246}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3.2 Gain for **Temperature**

Subsets:
- $S_{\text{Hot}}$ = 4 (2 Yes, 2 No) → $H = 1.000$
- $S_{\text{Mild}}$ = 6 (4 Yes, 2 No) → $H = -\tfrac{4}{6}\log_2\tfrac{4}{6} - \tfrac{2}{6}\log_2\tfrac{2}{6} = 0.918$
- $S_{\text{Cool}}$ = 4 (3 Yes, 1 No) → $H = -\tfrac{3}{4}\log_2\tfrac{3}{4} - \tfrac{1}{4}\log_2\tfrac{1}{4} = 0.811$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{Temp}}(S) &= \tfrac{4}{14}(1.000) + \tfrac{6}{14}(0.918) + \tfrac{4}{14}(0.811) \\
&= 0.286 + 0.394 + 0.232 = 0.911 \\
IG(S, \text{Temp}) &= 0.940 - 0.911 = \mathbf{0.029}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3.3 Gain for **Humidity**

Subsets:
- $S_{\text{High}}$ = 7 (3 Yes, 4 No) → $H = 0.985$
- $S_{\text{Normal}}$ = 7 (6 Yes, 1 No) → $H = 0.592$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{Humidity}}(S) &= \tfrac{7}{14}(0.985) + \tfrac{7}{14}(0.592) = 0.493 + 0.296 = 0.789 \\
IG(S, \text{Humidity}) &= 0.940 - 0.789 = \mathbf{0.151}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3.4 Gain for **Wind**

Subsets:
- $S_{\text{Weak}}$ = 8 (6 Yes, 2 No) → $H = 0.811$
- $S_{\text{Strong}}$ = 6 (3 Yes, 3 No) → $H = 1.000$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{Wind}}(S) &= \tfrac{8}{14}(0.811) + \tfrac{6}{14}(1.000) = 0.464 + 0.429 = 0.892 \\
IG(S, \text{Wind}) &= 0.940 - 0.892 = \mathbf{0.048}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Step 3 — Analytical Matrix (Master Comparison)

| Attribute | $H_A(S)$ | $IG(S, A)$ | Rank |
|-----------|----------|------------|------|
| **Outlook**   | 0.694 | **0.246** | 🥇 **1 (Root)** |
| Humidity  | 0.789 | 0.151 | 2 |
| Wind      | 0.892 | 0.048 | 3 |
| Temperature | 0.911 | 0.029 | 4 |

**Decision:** **Outlook** is chosen as the root node.

## 3.5 Step 4 — Recursion on the "Sunny" Branch

The "Overcast" branch is pure (all Yes) → **leaf (Yes)**.

$S_{\text{Sunny}}$ = {D1, D2, D8, D9, D11} → 2 Yes, 3 No → $H = 0.971$.

Compute $IG$ for the remaining attributes:

**Humidity on Sunny:**
- High: 4 (0 Yes, 4 No) → $H = 0$
- Normal: 1 (1 Yes, 0 No) → $H = 0$
- $H_{\text{Humidity}}(\text{Sunny}) = 0$ → $IG = 0.971$ ✅

**Wind on Sunny:**
- Weak: 3 (1 Yes, 2 No) → $H = 0.918$
- Strong: 2 (1 Yes, 1 No) → $H = 1.000$
- $H_{\text{Wind}}(\text{Sunny}) = 0.951$ → $IG = 0.020$

**Decision:** Split Sunny on **Humidity**. Both children are pure → leaves *Yes* and *No*.

## 3.6 Step 5 — Recursion on the "Rain" Branch

$S_{\text{Rain}}$ = {D4, D5, D6, D10, D14} → 3 Yes, 2 No → $H = 0.971$.

**Wind on Rain:**
- Weak: 3 (3 Yes, 0 No) → $H = 0$
- Strong: 2 (0 Yes, 2 No) → $H = 0$
- $H_{\text{Wind}}(\text{Rain}) = 0$ → $IG = 0.971$ ✅

**Decision:** Split Rain on **Wind**. Both children are pure.

## 3.7 Final Decision Tree (Textual Form)

```
Outlook
├── Overcast ───────────────► Yes
├── Sunny ──► Humidity
│            ├── High ──────► No
│            └── Normal ────► Yes
└── Rain ───► Wind
             ├── Weak ──────► Yes
             └── Strong ────► No
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Receive training set S] --> B[Compute entropy H of S]
    B --> C{Is S pure OR no attributes left?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Create leaf with majority class]
    C -- No --> E[For each attribute A, compute IG S A]
    E --> F[Select attribute A* with MAX IG]
    F --> G[Make A* the test attribute at current node]
    G --> H[Partition S into subsets S_v for each value v of A*]
    H --> I[For each subset S_v, recurse ID3 on S_v]
    I --> J[Return assembled decision tree]
    J --> K[End]

    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2,color:#000
    style B fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,color:#000
    style C fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#C2185B,color:#000
    style D fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
    style E fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,color:#000
    style F fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    style G fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#000
    style H fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#000
    style I fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#000
    style J fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2,color:#000
    style K fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Information Gain Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputSet[Training Set S] --> entModule[Entropy Module H of S]
    entModule --> igCalc[Information Gain Calculator]
    candidateAttrs[Candidate Attributes A1 to An] --> igCalc
    igCalc --> selector[Argmax Selector]
    selector --> splitter[Splits dataset by best attribute]
    splitter --> subSet1[Subset S_v1]
    splitter --> subSet2[Subset S_v2]
    splitter --> subSetN[Subset S_vN]
    subSet1 --> recurse1[Recursive ID3 Call]
    subSet2 --> recurse2[Recursive ID3 Call]
    subSetN --> recurseN[Recursive ID3 Call]
    recurse1 --> treeOut[Final Decision Tree]
    recurse2 --> treeOut
    recurseN --> treeOut

    style inputSet fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    style entModule fill:#FFE082,stroke:#FF6F00,color:#000
    style igCalc fill:#FFE082,stroke:#FF6F00,color:#000
    style selector fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    style splitter fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    style treeOut fill:#EF9A9A,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
```

    R --> W[Wind]
    H --> HH[High]
    H --> HN[Normal]
    W --> WW[Weak]
    W --> WS[Strong]
    HH --> LeafNo1((No))
    HN --> LeafYes2((Yes))
    WW --> LeafYes3((Yes))
    WS --> LeafNo2((No))

    style O fill:#1976D2,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000
    style Ov fill:#90CAF9,color:#000,stroke:#000

    style W fill:#42A5F5,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000
