<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define Information Gain. How is it used in the ID3 algorithm? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Information Gain $IG(S, A)$ of an attribute $A$ with respect to a dataset $S$ is defined as the **reduction in entropy** achieved by partitioning $S$ according to the values of $A$:
$$IG(S, A) = H(S) - H_A(S)$$
where $H(S)$ is the entropy of $S$ and $H_A(S)$ is the weighted post-split entropy. In ID3, at every internal node, the algorithm computes $IG$ for **every** remaining attribute and greedily selects the one with the **maximum** Information Gain as the test attribute, because it provides the greatest discrimination between classes.

---

### **Q2.** Explain the concept of entropy with a suitable example. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Entropy $H(S)$, in the ID3 context, is a measure of **impurity** or **randomness** in a dataset. For a binary classification problem with probabilities $p$ and $(1-p)$:
$$H(S) = -p\log_2 p - (1-p)\log_2(1-p)$$
- $H = 0$ when the dataset is **pure** (all samples belong to one class).
- $H = 1$ (maximum for binary) when the classes are equally distributed.

**Example:** A node containing 5 Yes and 5 No has $H = 1.0$ bit (maximum impurity). A node containing 10 Yes and 0 No has $H = 0$ bits (perfectly pure).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme regulation: Attempt **either** full Question A **or** full Question B. Both options carry 14 marks.

### **Question A — Choice 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat diagram, explain the **ID3 algorithm**. List the steps involved.

**Model Solution:**

The ID3 (Iterative Dichotomiser 3) algorithm builds a decision tree from a labeled training set using Information Gain as the splitting criterion.

**Step 1:** Calculate the entropy $H(S)$ of the entire dataset.
**Step 2:** For each attribute $A$, calculate the Information Gain $IG(S, A)$.
**Step 3:** Select the attribute $A^*$ with the highest $IG$ as the decision attribute for the current node.
**Step 4:** Create a branch for each value $v$ of $A^*$.
**Step 5:** Partition the dataset into subsets $S_v$ where $A = v$.
**Step 6:** Recursively invoke ID3 on each $S_v$ using the remaining attributes.
**Step 7:** Stop recursion when a subset is pure, empty, or no attributes remain (majority voting).

**Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 flowchart.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss **any four limitations** of the ID3 algorithm.

**Model Solution:**

1. **No handling of continuous attributes:** ID3 requires categorical features; numeric attributes must be pre-discretized.
2. **Bias toward multi-valued attributes:** Attributes with many values (e.g., Date, ID) tend to have spuriously high information gain, producing shallow but uninformative trees. (C4.5 fixes this via Gain Ratio.)
3. **No pruning mechanism:** ID3 grows the tree until pure leaves, leading to severe **overfitting** on noisy training data.
4. **Greedy local optimization:** ID3 makes locally optimal splits at each node with no global look-ahead, so it can miss the globally optimal tree.
5. **Missing value handling:** ID3 has no native strategy for missing attribute values.
6. **Sensitivity to small data changes:** The tree structure can be highly unstable with respect to minor variations in the training set.

---

### **Question B — Choice 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Given the following dataset, construct the decision tree using the **ID3 algorithm**.

| Age | Income | Student | Credit Rating | Buys Computer |
|-----|--------|---------|---------------|---------------|
| ≤30 | High   | No      | Fair          | No            |
| ≤30 | High   | No      | Excellent     | No            |
| 31–40| High  | No      | Fair          | Yes           |
| >40 | Medium | No      | Fair          | Yes           |
| >40 | Low    | Yes     | Fair          | Yes           |
| >40 | Low    | Yes     | Excellent     | No            |
| 31–40| Low    | Yes     | Excellent     | Yes           |
| ≤30 | Medium | No      | Fair          | No            |
| ≤30 | Low    | Yes     | Fair          | Yes           |
| >40 | Medium | Yes     | Fair          | Yes           |
| ≤30 | Medium | Yes     | Excellent     | Yes           |
| 31–40| Medium | No      | Excellent     | Yes           |
| 31–40| High   | Yes     | Fair          | Yes           |
| >40 | Medium | No      | Excellent     | No            |

**Model Solution:**

Total = 14; **Yes = 9, No = 5**.

$$
H(S) = -\tfrac{9}{14}\log_2\tfrac{9}{14} - \tfrac{5}{14}\log_2\tfrac{5}{14} = 0.940
$$

**Gain for Age:**
- ≤30: 5 samples (2 Yes, 3 No) → $H = 0.971$
- 31–40: 4 samples (4 Yes, 0 No) → $H = 0$
- >40: 5 samples (3 Yes, 2 No) → $H = 0.971$

$$
H_{\text{Age}}(S) = \tfrac{5}{14}(0.971) + \tfrac{4}{14}(0) + \tfrac{5}{14}(0.971) = 0.694
$$
$$
IG(\text{Age}) = 0.940 - 0.694 = \mathbf{0.246}
$$

**Gain for Income:**
- High: 4 (2 Yes, 2 No) → $H = 1.000$
- Medium: 6 (4 Yes, 2 No) → $H = 0.918$
- Low: 4 (3 Yes, 1 No) → $H = 0.811$

$$
H_{\text{Income}}(S) = \tfrac{4}{14}(1.000) + \tfrac{6}{14}(0.918) + \tfrac{4}{14}(0.811) = 0.911
$$
$$
IG(\text{Income}) = 0.940 - 0.911 = \mathbf{0.029}
$$

**Gain for Student:**
- Yes: 7 (6 Yes, 1 No) → $H = 0.592$
- No: 7 (3 Yes, 4 No) → $H = 0.985$

$$
H_{\text{Student}}(S) = \tfrac{7}{14}(0.592) + \tfrac{7}{14}(0.985) = 0.789
$$
$$
IG(\text{Student}) = 0.940 - 0.789 = \mathbf{0.151}
$$

**Gain for Credit Rating:**
- Fair: 8 (6 Yes, 2 No) → $H = 0.811$
- Excellent: 6 (3 Yes, 3 No) → $H = 1.000$

$$
H_{\text{Credit}}(S) = \tfrac{8}{14}(0.811) + \tfrac{6}{14}(1.000) = 0.892
$$
$$
IG(\text{Credit}) = 0.940 - 0.892 = \mathbf{0.048}
$$

**Master Comparison Table:**

| Attribute | $IG$ |
|-----------|------|
| **Age**       | **0.246** |
| Student    | 0.151 |
| Credit     | 0.048 |
| Income     | 0.029 |

**Root = Age.** Recursing on the Age = 31–40 branch yields all Yes (pure leaf). For Age ≤30 and Age >40 subtrees, subsequent splits must be computed similarly (Student emerges as the next best discriminator in practice, producing a tree depth of typically 2–3 levels).

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compare **ID3** with the **CART algorithm**.

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | ID3 | CART |
|---------|-----|------|
| **Year / Author** | Quinlan, 1986 | Breiman et al., 1984 |
| **Output type** | Multi-way classification tree | Binary classification **or** regression tree |
| **Splitting criterion** | Information Gain (entropy) | **Gini Index** (or MSE for regression) |
| **Number of branches per node** | Multi-way (one per attribute value) | Strictly binary |
| **Handles continuous attributes?** | No (must pre-discretize) | Yes (binary threshold search) |
| **Pruning** | None (overfits) | Cost-complexity post-pruning |
| **Successor** | C4.5, C5.0 | Survives as the basis of Random Forests |
| **Computational cost per split** | Higher (log-based entropy) | Slightly lower (Gini has no log) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse **Gini Index** with **Entropy** and write the wrong formula in derivations. Remember:
> - **Entropy:** $H = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ (uses logarithm)
> - **Gini:** $Gini = 1 - \sum p_i^2$ (no logarithm)
>
> Additionally, **do not** claim ID3 handles continuous attributes — that is a hallmark of CART and C4.5. Examiners deduct **1 full mark** for this factual error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ ID3 = **Iterative Dichotomiser 3** by Ross Quinlan (1986); greedy, top-down, recursive.
- ✅ Core metric: **Information Gain** $IG(S, A) = H(S) - H_A(S)$.
- ✅ Entropy formula: $H(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ (range: $[0, \log_2 c]$ bits for $c$ classes).
- ✅ Always select the attribute with **maximum** Information Gain at each node.
- ✅ Stopping criteria: pure node, empty subset, no remaining attributes (use majority voting).
- ✅ ID3 only handles **categorical** features and produces **multi-way** splits.
- ✅ Major limitations: bias toward high-cardinality attributes, no pruning, no missing-value handling, no continuous support.
- ✅ Successors: **C4.5** (Gain Ratio + pruning + continuous support), **C5.0** (commercial), **CART** (binary splits + Gini).
- ✅ For KTU numericals: always show **three tables** — initial entropy, per-attribute IG, and final rank/decision table.
- ✅ Decision tree is built **top-down**, evaluated **bottom-up**, and the leaf node predicts the class label.
- ✅ Watch the unit of entropy — it is measured in **bits** (because of $\log_2$).
- ✅ Memorize both the **Entropy** and **Gini** formulas — they are frequently swapped in exam traps.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Issues in Decision Tree Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT501 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Issues in Decision Tree Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of supervised learning under the KTU 2024 syllabus, a **Decision Tree** is a non-parametric, hierarchical model that recursively partitions the feature space into axis-aligned regions, predicting the target class (or value) by traversing from the root node to a leaf node based on attribute tests. **Decision Tree Learning** refers to the algorithmic procedure of inducing such a tree from a labeled training set using a top-down, greedy, divide-and-conquer strategy (e.g., ID3, C4.5, CART).

**Issues in Decision Tree Learning** are the set of practical, theoretical, and operational challenges that arise during the *induction*, *generalization*, and *deployment* phases of decision tree models. These issues directly determine whether the learned model will perform well on *unseen* data, not merely on the training set. The principal issues catalogued in the KTU PCITT501 syllabus include:

- **Overfitting** (the most critical issue)
- **Underfitting** and poor generalization
- **Handling Continuous-Valued Attributes**
- **Handling Missing Attribute Values**
- **Attribute Selection Bias** (multi-valued attribute problem)
- **Pruning Strategies** (Pre-pruning vs. Post-pruning)
- **Class Imbalance Sensitivity**
- **Instability** (high variance to small data perturbations)
- **Scalability with Large Datasets**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The classification of these "issues" is specifically tied to the *Quinlan* and *Mitchell* framework. The university expects students to identify, define, and propose remedies (e.g., pruning, gain ratio, imputation) for each issue.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a medical student learning to diagnose diseases by studying 100 patient case sheets. If you memorize every patient's exact symptoms, age, blood pressure, and outcome, you will ace the original test (training accuracy ≈ 100%) but fail spectacularly on a new patient (test accuracy ≈ poor). **You have *overfit* your mental model to the noise and idiosyncrasies of the 100 specific patients.**

Now imagine the opposite: you ignore the nuances and just say "every patient with a fever has the flu." You will be wrong often. **You have *underfit*.**

A decision tree faces the exact same dilemma. The "issues in decision tree learning" are precisely the traps that cause the algorithm to either memorize noise (overfitting) or oversimplify patterns (underfitting), along with practical problems like missing blood-test results in patient files (missing values) or a single lab report split into 50 normal/abnormal sub-categories (multi-valued attribute bias).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** Issues in decision tree learning are essentially the *reasons your tree might be wrong on new data* — even when it looks perfect on training data.

### 1.3 Key Quantitative Metrics Used to Diagnose These Issues

- **Training Error** $\ E_{train} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}[y_i \neq \hat{y}_i]$
- **Generalization (Test) Error** $\ E_{test}$
- **Tree Complexity** measured by number of nodes $\vert T \vert$ or tree depth $\ d$
- **Misclassification Cost** $\ C(M)$

These metrics are written in **bold** because they appear repeatedly in KTU numerical and 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Central Issue: Overfitting

Overfitting is formally defined as the condition where a hypothesis $h \in H$ has a *lower* error than another hypothesis $h' \in H$ on the training data, but a *higher* error on the entire instance distribution. Quoting the canonical Mitchell (1997) formulation:

> A hypothesis $h$ overfits the training data if there exists some alternative hypothesis $h'$ such that $E_{train}(h) < E_{train}(h')$ but $E_{overall}(h) > E_{overall}(h')$.

**Why does this happen in decision trees?**

- The hypothesis space of decision trees is *infinite* — we can keep splitting until every leaf is pure (training error = 0).
- The greedy recursive partitioning has no built-in "stop" criterion tied to *true* information gain on the population.
- As tree size $\vert T \vert$ grows, training error monotonically decreases, but test error reaches a minimum and *then rises* — the classic U-curve.

### 2.2 Taxonomy of Issues (Structured Logic)

**Issue Group A — Generalization Issues**
1. **Overfitting**: Tree becomes too complex, fits noise.
2. **Underfitting**: Tree too simple, misses real patterns.
3. **Bias–Variance Trade-off**: Decision trees are *low-bias, high-variance* learners — a single tree can change drastically with a 1% perturbation in training data.

**Issue Group B — Data Quality Issues**
1. **Missing Values**: Some training instances have unknown attribute values. How do we (a) split? (b) classify?
2. **Continuous Attributes**: Require binary or multi-way discretization (e.g., $A \leq t$ splits in CART).
3. **Irrelevant Attributes**: Greedy selection may briefly favor them; they waste splits and inflate tree size.

**Issue Group C — Attribute Selection Issues**
1. **Multi-Valued Attribute Bias**: Information Gain (ID3) prefers attributes with many distinct values (e.g., "Patient ID") that have high entropy reduction but zero predictive value.
2. **Gain Ratio (C4.5)** corrects this by dividing by *split information* $IV(a)$.
3. **Gini Index (CART)** avoids the bias naturally because it does not use log-based entropy.

**Issue Group D — Post-Construction Issues**
1. **Pruning Needed**: After full growth, replace subtrees with leaves to remove overfitting.
2. **Computational Cost**: Some pruning and split-search algorithms are $O(n^2)$ or worse.
3. **Concept Drift**: In streaming data, the optimal tree changes over time.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Purpose / Unit |
|---|---------|----------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Information Gain (ID3) | $IG(S, a) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in V(a)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | Attribute selection; bits |
| 2 | Entropy (Shannon) | $H(S) = - \sum_{c} p_c \log_2 p_c$ | Impurity of set $S$; bits |
| 3 | Split Information (C4.5) | $IV(a) = - \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ | Normalizer for multi-valued bias |
| 4 | Gain Ratio | $GR(a) = \dfrac{IG(a)}{IV(a)}$ | Bias-corrected selection metric |
| 5 | Gini Index | $Gini(S) = 1 - \sum_c p_c^2$ | CART impurity; dimensionless $[0, 0.5]$ |
| 6 | Gini Gain | $\Delta G = Gini(S) - \sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} Gini(S_v)$ | CART split criterion |
| 7 | Reduced-Error Pruning | Replace subtree with leaf if $E_{validation}(leaf) \leq E_{validation}(subtree)$ | Post-pruning rule |
| 8 | Cost-Complexity (CART) | $C_\alpha(T) = \sum_{t \in leaves} N_t \cdot r(t) + \alpha \vert leaves(T) \vert$ | Tree selection under penalty $\alpha$ |
| 9 | Pessimistic Error (Quinlan) | $e_{pess} = \dfrac{e + \frac{z^2}{2N}}{1 + \frac{z^2}{N}}$ | Statistical correction in pruning |
| 10 | Ockham's Regularizer | Penalty term proportional to $\vert T \vert$ | Bias–variance control |
| 11 | Classification Error | $E = \frac{misclassified}{N}$ | Leaf node error |
| 12 | Laplace Correction | $P(c) = \frac{N_c + 1}{N + k}$ | Probability with $k$ classes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas power real-world systems in medical diagnosis (clinical decision support), credit scoring, fraud detection, customer churn, and remote-sensed image classification. Pruning and gain ratio are the two single most-deployed techniques in production decision-tree systems worldwide (XGBoost, Random Forest, LightGBM all trace lineage to these formulas).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why Information Gain is Biased Toward Multi-Valued Attributes

We want to show formally that $IG(S, a)$ can be inflated simply by increasing $\vert V(a) \vert$, the number of distinct values of attribute $a$.

**Step 1 — Start with the entropy of the partition induced by $a$:**

$$
H_{after}(S, a) = \sum_{v \in V(a)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)
$$

**Step 2 — Take the extreme case where each subset $S_v$ contains exactly one instance.** This happens when every value of $a$ is unique (e.g., a "Patient ID" attribute).

Since each singleton has $H(S_v) = 0$ (a single instance is perfectly pure — it is its own class with probability 1):

$$
H_{after}(S, a) = \sum_{v=1}^{N} \frac{1}{N} \cdot 0 = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the information gain:**

$$
IG(S, a) = H(S) - H_{after}(S, a) = H(S) - 0 = H(S)
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

The information gain is *maximally* equal to the original entropy $H(S)$, **regardless of whether attribute $a$ is predictive or not**. Therefore $IG$ will *always* prefer the unique-identifier attribute. This is the **multi-valued attribute bias**.

**Step 5 — The C4.5 fix (Gain Ratio):**

The *split information* $IV(a)$ is also computed for the same attribute:

$$
IV(a) = - \sum_{v=1}^{N} \frac{1}{N} \log_2 \frac{1}{N} = \log_2 N
$$

Therefore:

$$
GR(a) = \frac{IG(a)}{IV(a)} = \frac{H(S)}{\log_2 N}
$$

As $N \to \infty$, $GR \to 0$, correctly penalizing the multi-valued attribute.

### 3.2 Derivation: Cost-Complexity Pruning (CART)

The CART algorithm (Breiman et al., 1984) selects the optimal subtree $T_\alpha$ by minimizing the cost-complexity function:

$$
C_\alpha(T) = \sum_{t \in leaves(T)} R(t) + \alpha \cdot \vert leaves(T) \vert
$$

where:
- $R(t) = N_t \cdot r(t)$ is the *resubstitution error* at leaf $t$
- $N_t$ = number of training instances reaching leaf $t$
- $r(t) = $ misclassification rate at leaf $t$
- $\alpha \geq 0$ is the *complexity parameter*

**Step 1 — Write the effective error per leaf:**

For leaf $t$ with class distribution $\{N_{t,c}\}_{c=1}^{k}$:

$$
r(t) = 1 - \max_c \frac{N_{t,c}}{N_t}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the increase in $C_\alpha$ when a subtree $T_t$ rooted at internal node $t$ is collapsed into a single leaf:**

$$
\Delta C = R(t) + \alpha - \sum_{t' \in leaves(T_t)} R(t') - \alpha \cdot (\vert leaves(T_t) \vert - 1)
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\Delta C = R(t) - \sum_{t' \in leaves(T_t)} R(t') + \alpha \cdot (2 - \vert leaves(T_t) \vert)
$$

**Step 3 — Find the threshold $\alpha$ at which collapse becomes optimal:**

Setting $\Delta C = 0$:

$$
\alpha = \frac{\sum_{t' \in leaves(T_t)} R(t') - R(t)}{\vert leaves(T_t) \vert - 1}
$$

**Step 4 — The pruning sequence:**

CART grows $T_{max}$, then iteratively collapses the internal node $t$ with the *smallest* $\alpha$ value, producing a finite sequence of nested subtrees $T_0 \supset T_1 \supset T_2 \supset \dots \supset \{root\}$. The optimal tree is then chosen by cross-validation on a held-out set, picking the $\alpha$ that minimizes $E_{cv}$.

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example (KTU-style):**

Suppose an internal node $t$ has 30 instances with $r(t) = 0.20$ and a 3-leaf subtree $T_t$ with leaves $t_1, t_2, t_3$ having:

- $N_{t_1} = 12, r(t_1) = 0.08 \Rightarrow R(t_1) = 0.96$
- $N_{t_2} = 10, r(t_2) = 0.10 \Rightarrow R(t_2) = 1.00$
- $N_{t_3} = 8, \ r(t_3) = 0.125 \Rightarrow R(t_3) = 1.00$

Subtree misclassification cost:

$$
\sum R(t_i) = 0.96 + 1.00 + 1.00 = 2.96
$$

Cost of collapsing to a single leaf:

$$
R(t) = 30 \times 0.20 = 6.00
$$

The threshold:

$$
\alpha = \frac{2.96 - 6.00}{3 - 1} = \frac{-3.04}{2} = -1.52
$$

Since $\alpha < 0$, collapse is *never* optimal at any non-negative $\alpha$. **Decision: keep the subtree.** This is the rigorous CART pruning math.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pre-Pruning vs. Post-Pruning

| Criterion | Pre-Pruning (Early Stopping) | Post-Pruning (Subtree Replacement) |
|---|---|---|
| When applied | During tree growth | After full $T_{max}$ is built |
| Stopping rule | $\chi^2$ test, $IG < \theta$, min samples, max depth | Replace subtree by leaf if it improves validation score |
| Risk | **Horizon effect** — stops too early | Higher compute cost |
| Data need | Single training set | Requires *separate* validation set or uses pessimistic estimate |
| Computation | Faster | Slower but more accurate |
| Used in | ID3, C4.5 (with thresholds) | CART, C4.5, Reduced-Error Pruning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse *stopping* (pre-pruning) with *pruning* (post-pruning). They are *different* operations with different theoretical bases.

### 3.4 Handling Missing Values: The Probabilistic Split (C4.5 Method)

If an instance $x$ has missing value for test attribute $a$, C4.5 distributes $x$ *fractionally* down *all* branches of the split, weighted by:

$$
w_v = \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}
$$

The instance's effective weight at child $v$ becomes:

$$
w(x, v) = w(x) \cdot w_v
$$

Class probability at leaf $t$ is updated as:

$$
P(c \mid t) = \frac{\sum_{x: x \in t,\ class(x) = c} w(x)}{\sum_{x \in t} w(x)}
$$

This fractional-instance method is the most rigorous way to handle missing values during both *training* and *inference*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Taxonomy of Issues in Decision Tree Learning

```mermaid
graph TD
    RootA[Issues in Decision Tree Learning]
    RootA --> B1[Generalization Issues]
    RootA --> B2[Data Quality Issues]
    RootA --> B3[Selection Bias Issues]
    RootA --> B4[Post Construction Issues]

    B1 --> C1[Overfitting]
    B1 --> C2[Underfitting]
    B1 --> C3[Bias Variance Tradeoff]

    B2 --> D1[Missing Values]
    B2 --> D2[Continuous Attributes]
    B2 --> D3[Irrelevant Attributes]

    B3 --> E1[Multi Valued Bias]
    B3 --> E2[Noisy Attributes]

    B4 --> F1[Pruning Required]
    B4 --> F2[Class Imbalance]
    B4 --> F3[Instability]
    B4 --> F4[Scalability Limit]

    C1 --> G1[Solution Pruning]
    C1 --> G2[Solution Early Stopping]
    C1 --> G3[Solution Cross Validation]
    E1 --> H1[Solution Gain Ratio]
    E1 --> H2[Solution Gini Index]
    D1 --> I1[Solution Fractional Instances]
    D1 --> I2[Solution Imputation]
    D2 --> J1[Solution Binary Discretization]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Decision Tree Learning Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Input Training Data D] --> P2[Attribute Selection Module]
    P2 --> P3[Choose Best Attribute via IG or GR or Gini]
    P3 --> P4[Recursive Partitioning Engine]
    P4 --> P5{Stopping Condition Met}
    P5 -- No --> P2
    P5 -- Yes --> P6[Full Tree Tmax]
    P6 --> P7[Pruning Engine]
    P7 --> P8{Pre Prune or Post Prune}
    P8 -- Pre Prune --> P9[Apply Early Stop Threshold]
    P8 -- Post Prune --> P10[Apply Reduced Error or Cost Complexity]
    P9 --> P11[Final Tree T]
    P10 --> P11[Final Tree T]
    P11 --> P12[Inference Module]
    P12 --> P13[Handle Missing Values at Test Time]
    P13 --> P14[Output Prediction]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Bias-Variance Trade-off in Tree Complexity

```mermaid
graph TD
    K1[Tree Depth d] --> K2[Training Error Decreases]
    K1 --> K3[Test Error Follows U Curve]
    K3 --> K4[Minimum at Optimal Depth d star]
    K4 --> K5[Left of d star equals Underfitting]
    K4 --> K6[Right of d star equals Overfitting]
    K5 --> K7[High Bias Low Variance]
    K6 --> K8[Low Bias High Variance]
    K7 --> K9[Pruning or Add Features]
    K8 --> K10[Pruning or Regularize or Ensemble]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block is a functional unit of the decision tree learning process. The first diagram classifies the *issues*, the second shows the *engineer's pipeline* (with pruning and missing-value handling as explicit modules), and the third provides the *theoretical map* of how tree complexity connects to over/underfitting.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*What is overfitting in the context of decision tree learning? State any two techniques to avoid it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Overfitting occurs when a decision tree is grown so complex that it perfectly classifies the training set, including its noise, but performs poorly on unseen data. Formally, hypothesis $h$ overfits the training data if $E_{train}(h) < E_{train}(h')$ but $E_{overall}(h) > E_{overall}(h')$ for some alternative $h'$. *(1.5 marks)*

Two techniques to avoid overfitting:
1. **Pre-pruning (Early Stopping):** Halt tree growth when the information gain drops below a threshold $\theta$ or when a node has fewer than $N_{min}$ samples. *(0.75 marks)*
2. **Post-pruning (Subtree Replacement):** Grow the full tree $T_{max}$ and then replace subtrees with leaves if the replacement improves validation accuracy (e.g., reduced-error pruning or cost-complexity pruning). *(0.75 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Explain the multi-valued attribute problem in Information Gain. How does Gain Ratio solve it?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Information Gain $IG(S, a) = H(S) - \sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ is biased toward attributes with many distinct values. In the extreme case where each value of $a$ is unique (e.g., a "Patient ID" attribute), each subset $S_v$ becomes a singleton with $H(S_v) = 0$, making $IG(S, a) = H(S)$ — the *maximum possible* gain, even though attribute $a$ has zero predictive power. *(1.5 marks)*

**Gain Ratio** (C4.5) corrects this by normalizing $IG$ with the *split information* $IV(a) = -\sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}$:

$$
GR(a) = \frac{IG(a)}{IV(a)}
$$

For a unique-identifier attribute, $IV(a) = \log_2 N$ which grows with $N$, so $GR \to 0$, correctly penalizing the multi-valued attribute. *(1.5 marks)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option I

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Discuss the various issues encountered during decision tree learning. Classify them into generalization issues and data quality issues, providing one remedy for each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain **Reduced-Error Pruning** and **Cost-Complexity Pruning** with their respective algorithms and a comparative analysis. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks:**

Decision tree learning faces the following issues:

**Generalization Issues** *(3.5 marks)*:
1. **Overfitting** — the tree fits noise in training data. *Remedy:* Post-pruning using a validation set.
2. **Underfitting** — the tree is too shallow to capture real patterns. *Remedy:* Allow deeper growth, use a stronger attribute selection metric, or relax stopping criteria.
3. **Bias–Variance Trade-off** — single decision trees are low-bias but high-variance (unstable). *Remedy:* Use ensemble methods (Random Forest, Boosting) or strong regularization.

**Data Quality Issues** *(3.5 marks)*:
1. **Missing Attribute Values** — *Remedy:* Fractional-instance splitting (C4.5) or imputation with mean/mode/most-frequent-value.
2. **Continuous-Valued Attributes** — require binary or multi-way split point selection. *Remedy:* Sort values and evaluate candidate thresholds (CART, Fayyad–Irani discretization).
3. **Irrelevant Attributes** — *Remedy:* Feature selection preprocessing (mutual information, $\chi^2$ test) and gain-ratio-based selection.

---

**Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Reduced-Error Pruning (Quinlan 1987)** *(3.5 marks)*:
- **Algorithm:**
  1. Split data into training set $D_{train}$ and validation set $D_{val}$.
  2. Grow the full tree $T_{max}$ on $D_{train}$.
  3. For each internal node $t$ (in bottom-up order): replace subtree at $t$ with a leaf labeled by the majority class of instances reaching $t$, *only if* the replacement decreases (or does not increase) the error on $D_{val}$.
  4. Stop when no further replacement reduces $D_{val}$ error.
- **Advantage:** Theoretically well-grounded; prunes the *smallest* subtree whose validation error is no worse.
- **Disadvantage:** Requires a separate validation set, which reduces effective training data.

**Cost-Complexity Pruning (CART)** *(3.5 marks)*:
- **Algorithm:**
  1. Grow $T_{max}$ until all leaves are pure.
  2. For each internal node $t$ with subtree $T_t$, compute the *effective alpha*:

$$
\alpha_{eff}(t) = \frac{R(t) - R(T_t)}{\vert leaves(T_t) \vert - 1}
$$

where $R(\cdot)$ is the resubstitution error weighted by sample count.

  3. Iteratively collapse the node with the *smallest* $\alpha_{eff}$ to produce a nested sequence $T_0 \supset T_1 \supset \cdots \supset \{root\}$.
  4. Use 10-fold cross-validation to pick the optimal $\alpha^*$ and corresponding tree $T_{\alpha^*}$.

- **Advantage:** Does not require a separate validation set; uses training-data statistics.
- **Disadvantage:** Computationally heavier; relies on the pessimistic error estimate.

**Comparative Analysis:** *(included within the 3.5 marks above)*
Reduced-Error Pruning is simpler and uses real validation, while Cost-Complexity Pruning is more principled (statistical) and works with the full training data, but is more complex.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option II

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** With a suitable example, explain how **Information Gain** is biased toward attributes with many values. Show mathematically how **Gain Ratio** overcomes this bias. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the strategies used to handle **continuous attributes** and **missing values** in decision tree learning, with the algorithm steps for each. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Example illustrating the bias:** *(3 marks)*

Consider a dataset of 10 patients with attributes {Temperature, Headache, PatientID} and target Disease. The PatientID attribute uniquely identifies each patient and has 10 distinct values.

- For attribute PatientID: each $S_v$ has 1 instance $\Rightarrow H(S_v) = 0$ for all $v$. So $IG = H(S) - 0 = H(S)$, the *maximum* possible gain.
- Information Gain will *always* prefer PatientID over Temperature or Headache, even though PatientID has zero predictive value on a new patient.

**Mathematical proof that Gain Ratio overcomes this:** *(4 marks)*

For PatientID with $N$ unique values, the split information is:

$$
IV(\text{PatientID}) = - \sum_{v=1}^{N} \frac{1}{N} \log_2 \frac{1}{N} = \log_2 N
$$

The gain ratio is:

$$
GR(\text{PatientID}) = \frac{H(S)}{\log_2 N}
$$

As $N$ grows, $\log_2 N \to \infty$, so $GR(\text{PatientID}) \to 0$. In contrast, for a *binary* predictive attribute (e.g., Headache = Yes/No), $IV \approx 1$ bit, so $GR$ is approximately equal to $IG$, preserving its genuine predictive value. Hence Gain Ratio correctly suppresses the multi-valued attribute bias.

---

**Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Handling Continuous Attributes** *(3.5 marks)*:
- **Strategy:** Convert continuous attribute $A$ into a *binary* or *multi-way* categorical attribute by finding optimal split points.
- **Algorithm (CART-style):**
  1. Sort training instances by the continuous attribute $A$: $a_{(1)} \leq a_{(2)} \leq \dots \leq a_{(N)}$.
  2. For each candidate split point $t_i = \dfrac{a_{(i)} + a_{(i+1)}}{2}$ between consecutive distinct values, evaluate the gain (e.g., Gini gain) of the binary split $A \leq t_i$ vs. $A > t_i$.
  3. Choose the $t_i^*$ that maximizes the gain.
- The resulting test is "Is $A \leq t_i^*$?" (binary), and the procedure is repeated recursively for each subset.
- Multi-way discretization (Fayyad–Irani) finds *all* optimal split points at once and is used in C4.5.

**Handling Missing Values** *(3.5 marks)*:
- **Strategy 1 — Discard:** Remove instances with missing values (lossy, but simple).
- **Strategy 2 — Imputation:** Fill in the missing value with the attribute's mean, mode, or model-predicted value.
- **Strategy 3 — Fractional Instances (C4.5):**
  1. When evaluating the split on attribute $a$, include instances with missing $a$ in the calculation by *probabilistically distributing* them across all branches with weights $w_v = \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}$.
  2. For *inference*, the test instance with missing $a$ is sent down *all* branches, and its class probability is the weighted sum of class probabilities at the leaves.
- **Strategy 4 — Surrogate Splits (CART):** Use a backup attribute that mimics the primary split's behavior.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Don't write "pruning reduces complexity" without defining what complexity is.** You must mention the *number of leaves* $\vert leaves(T) \vert$ explicitly.
> 2. **Don't confuse Gain Ratio with Gini Index.** Gain Ratio is a C4.5 entropy-based metric; Gini is the CART impurity metric. They are *not* interchangeable.
> 3. **For missing values, never say "ignore missing values" as the only solution.** Examiners expect at least two strategies (imputation + fractional instances).
> 4. **Always state the formula of IG before applying it.** A 7-mark question on bias reduction without the explicit $IG = H(S) - \sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ formula loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **In cost-complexity pruning, explicitly write the $\alpha_{eff}$ formula** — losing the denominator $\vert leaves(T_t) \vert - 1$ is a common 1-mark deduction.
> 6. **For 14-mark questions, do not skip the comparative analysis** between pre-pruning and post-pruning — it is a guaranteed 1–2 mark item.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Overfitting is THE central issue** in decision tree learning — know its formal definition by heart.
- ✅ **Information Gain** is biased toward **multi-valued attributes** (e.g., unique IDs) — proven by the $H_{after} = 0$ derivation.
- ✅ **Gain Ratio** = $IG / IV$ where $IV = -\sum_v p_v \log_2 p_v$ is the *split information* that normalizes for the bias.
- ✅ **Gini Index** $Gini(S) = 1 - \sum_c p_c^2$ is CART's alternative to entropy and is *not* biased toward multi-valued attributes.
- ✅ **Pre-pruning** (early stopping) and **post-pruning** (subtree replacement) are the two remedies for overfitting.
- ✅ **Cost-complexity pruning** uses $C_\alpha(T) = \sum_t R(t) + \alpha \vert leaves(T) \vert$ and the *effective alpha* criterion to find the optimal subtree.
- ✅ **Reduced-error pruning** requires a separate *validation set* and is conceptually simpler than cost-complexity.
- ✅ **Continuous attributes** are handled by sorting and evaluating candidate split points (binary test $A \leq t^*$).
- ✅ **Missing values** are best handled by the **fractional-instance method** of C4.5.
- ✅ Decision trees are **low-bias, high-variance** learners — they overfit easily but can capture complex patterns.
- ✅ **Ensemble methods** (Random Forest, Boosting) are the *modern* solution to the instability issue.
- ✅ Always state the *Ockham's razor* principle: prefer the smallest tree consistent with the data.
- ✅ Tree complexity is measured by **number of leaves** $\vert leaves(T) \vert$, **depth** $d$, or **total nodes** $\vert T \vert$.
- ✅ KTU expects: formula + intuition + example for every issue discussed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ensemble Learning - Boosting - Bagging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT501 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ensemble Learning - Boosting - Bagging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Ensemble Learning — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ensemble Learning** is a meta-learning paradigm in which multiple *base learners* (also called *weak learners* or *base models*) are strategically combined to produce a single *strong learner* whose generalisation performance is significantly better than that of any individual constituent model. The foundational principle rests on the theoretical results of **Kearns & Valiant (1989)** and **Schapire (1990)**, who proved that weak learners — models that perform only marginally better than random guessing — can be boosted into arbitrarily strong learners provided certain conditions of *diversity* and *accuracy* are satisfied.

The three primary mathematical prerequisites for an ensemble to outperform its individual members are:

1. **Statistical Condition** — When the hypothesis space is too large relative to the training set, a single learner may find multiple equally-valid hypotheses. Averaging over them reduces the risk of picking the wrong one.
2. **Computational Condition** — Many learning algorithms apply local search (e.g., greedy hill-climbing) and may get stuck in local optima. Ensembles can approximate the global optimum better.
3. **Representational Condition** — The true decision boundary may not be representable by any single hypothesis in the chosen class, but a weighted combination of hypotheses can approximate it.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** Ensemble techniques fall under the *supervised learning* cluster in **Module 2**, and the **Course Outcomes (CO2)** directly demand the ability to *analyse the bias-variance trade-off in ensemble methods and design combined models for improved prediction*.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Wisdom of Crowds"

Imagine a medical diagnosis scenario where **200 doctors** independently examine a patient's X-ray. Individually, each doctor has only **78% accuracy**. However, when their independent verdicts are aggregated via *majority vote*, the collective accuracy rises to **~96%**. The condition for this improvement is that the doctors' mistakes must be **largely uncorrelated** — they must err on *different* patients, not the *same* ones.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for Students:** The *accuracy* of an ensemble grows when its members are **(i) individually reasonable** (better than random) and **(ii) mutually diverse** (they make *different* errors). The central engineering challenge of every ensemble algorithm is therefore the deliberate cultivation of **diversity** among base learners.

---

### 1.3 Bagging — Bootstrap Aggregating

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating)**, introduced by **Leo Breiman (1996)**, is a *parallel* ensemble method that trains each base learner on a different **bootstrap sample** of the training data and combines their outputs by **uniform voting** (classification) or **simple averaging** (regression).

- **Variance Reduction Goal:** Bagging primarily targets the **variance** component of prediction error.
- **Base Learner:** Typically **unpruned decision trees** (high variance, low bias).
- **Most Famous Instantiation:** **Random Forest** (Breiman, 2001), which adds a *random feature subspace* at each split.

### 1.4 Boosting — Sequential Error Correction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boosting** is a *sequential* ensemble method in which base learners are trained one after another, with each subsequent learner focusing disproportionately on the data points that its predecessors *misclassified*. The final prediction is a **weighted vote** of all learners, where the weight of each learner is proportional to its training accuracy.

- **Bias Reduction Goal:** Boosting primarily targets the **bias** component of prediction error.
- **Foundational Algorithms:** **AdaBoost (Adaptive Boosting)** — Freund & Schapire, 1997; **Gradient Boosting** — Friedman, 2001; **XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost** — modern scalable variants.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** Boosting does *not* "boost" the data. It boosts the *weights* assigned to data points. The training set itself remains static; only the *importance* of each example evolves across iterations.

---

### 1.5 Bagging vs Boosting — A First-Glance Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> The following comparative snapshot is essential for KTU Part A questions. Memorise the contrasting axes.

| Dimension | **Bagging** | **Boosting** |
|---|---|---|
| Training Mode | Parallel (independent) | Sequential (dependent) |
| Primary Target | Reduce **Variance** | Reduce **Bias** |
| Sample Weights | Uniform across all samples | Updated each iteration |
| Base Learner Weight | Equal for all | Proportional to accuracy |
| Overfitting Risk | **Low** (resilient) | **High** (needs regularisation) |
| Canonical Example | **Random Forest** | **AdaBoost**, **Gradient Boosting** |
| Combiner Rule | Simple Vote / Mean | Weighted Vote / Sum |
| Required Diversity | From **bootstrap sampling** | From **re-weighting / re-labelling** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Bias–Variance–Noise Decomposition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Before dissecting Bagging and Boosting, you must internalise the **bias-variance decomposition** of expected prediction error, because the entire rationale of these two methods is anchored in it.

For a regression task with target $y$ and prediction $\hat{f}(x)$, the expected squared error at a point $x$ decomposes as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}\!\left[\,(y - \hat{f}(x))^{2}\,\right] &= \underbrace{\bigl(\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - f(x)\bigr)^{2}}_{\text{Bias}^{2}} \\
&\quad + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}\!\left[\bigl(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]\bigr)^{2}\right]}_{\text{Variance}} \\
&\quad + \underbrace{\sigma^{2}}_{\text{Irreducible Noise}}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Bagging** leaves bias largely unchanged but **shrinks variance** by a factor proportional to the number of uncorrelated base learners.
- **Boosting** sequentially **shrinks bias** by fitting residuals, though excessive iterations can inflate variance.

---

### 2.2 Bagging — Operational Logic Step-by-Step

**Inputs:** Training set $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_{i}, y_{i})\}_{i=1}^{N}$, number of base learners $M$, base learning algorithm $\mathcal{A}$.

**Procedure:**

1. **Bootstrap Sampling:** For $m = 1, 2, \dots, M$, draw a bootstrap sample $\mathcal{D}_{m}$ of size $N$ from $\mathcal{D}$ *with replacement*. On average, each $\mathcal{D}_{m}$ contains $\approx 63.2\%$ unique samples from $\mathcal{D}$.
2. **Parallel Training:** Train an independent base learner $\hat{f}_{m}$ on $\mathcal{D}_{m}$ using $\mathcal{A}$.
3. **Aggregation:**
   - *Regression:* $\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}(x) = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{m=1}^{M}\hat{f}_{m}(x)$
   - *Classification:* $\hat{y}_{\text{bag}}(x) = \arg\max_{c}\,\sum_{m=1}^{M}\mathbb{1}\!\left[\hat{f}_{m}(x) = c\right]$ (majority vote)

**Why does this work?** If the $M$ base learners are *i.i.d.* with variance $\sigma^{2}$ and pairwise covariance $\rho\,\sigma^{2}$ (where $\rho$ is the correlation coefficient), then:

$$
\text{Var}\!\left(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}\right) = \rho\,\sigma^{2} + \frac{1-\rho}{M}\,\sigma^{2}
$$

As $M \to \infty$, the variance converges to $\rho\,\sigma^{2}$ — a *floor* determined by inter-model correlation, not by sample size. This is precisely why **Random Forest** injects randomness at the *feature-split level* in addition to bootstrap sampling, driving $\rho$ downward.

---

### 2.3 Boosting — AdaBoost Operational Logic

**Algorithm: AdaBoost.M1 (Discrete AdaBoost)**

**Inputs:** $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_{i}, y_{i})\}_{i=1}^{N}$ with $y_{i} \in \{-1, +1\}$, number of iterations $M$.

**Initialisation:** Assign uniform weights $w_{i}^{(1)} = \frac{1}{N}$ for all $i$.

**For** $m = 1, 2, \dots, M$:

1. Train base learner $h_{m}$ on $\mathcal{D}$ using weights $w^{(m)}$.
2. Compute the *weighted* training error:
   $$\epsilon_{m} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_{i}^{(m)}\,\mathbb{1}\!\left[y_{i} \neq h_{m}(x_{i})\right]$$
3. Compute the learner confidence:
   $$\alpha_{m} = \frac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{1 - \epsilon_{m}}{\epsilon_{m}}\right)$$
4. Update the sample weights:
   $$w_{i}^{(m+1)} = \frac{w_{i}^{(m)}\,\exp\!\left(-\alpha_{m}\,y_{i}\,h_{m}(x_{i})\right)}{Z_{m}}$$
   where $Z_{m}$ is the normalising constant $Z_{m} = \sum_{i} w_{i}^{(m)}\exp(-\alpha_{m}y_{i}h_{m}(x_{i}))$.

**Final Classifier:**
$$H(x) = \text{sign}\!\left(\sum_{m=1}^{M} \alpha_{m}\,h_{m}(x)\right)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** The exponential weight update causes the weights of *misclassified* samples to *grow* and those of *correctly classified* samples to *shrink*. Consequently, the next base learner is forced to *concentrate* on the previously-erroneous regions of input space.

---

### 2.4 Gradient Boosting — The Generalised View

Friedman (2001) reframed Boosting as **gradient descent in function space**. Instead of explicitly up-weighting samples, each new learner $h_{m}$ is trained to fit the **negative gradient** of a chosen loss function $\mathcal{L}$:

$$
r_{im} = -\left[\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}(y_{i}, F(x_{i}))}{\partial F(x_{i})}\right]_{F = F_{m-1}}$$

For **squared-error loss** $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}(y - F)^{2}$, these pseudo-residuals reduce to the *ordinary residuals* $r_{im} = y_{i} - F_{m-1}(x_{i})$, recovering the classic "boosting fits residuals" intuition.

The ensemble update is:
$$F_{m}(x) = F_{m-1}(x) + \nu \cdot h_{m}(x)$$
where $\nu \in (0, 1]$ is the **learning rate** (shrinkage), a critical regulariser.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **board-exam ready cheat sheet**. Tables below use `\vert` in place of the vertical bar character to preserve markdown rendering.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Symbol Key |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bias–Variance Decomposition | $\text{MSE} = \text{Bias}^{2} + \text{Variance} + \sigma^{2}$ | $\sigma^{2}$ = irreducible noise |
| 2 | Bagging Aggregation (Regression) | $\hat{f}_{\text{bag}} = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{m=1}^{M}\hat{f}_{m}$ | $M$ = number of learners |
| 3 | Bagging Variance (i.i.d. base) | $\text{Var}(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}) = \frac{1}{M^{2}}\sum_{i=1}^{M}\sum_{j=1}^{M}\text{Cov}(\hat{f}_{i},\hat{f}_{j})$ | Reduces with low correlation |
| 4 | Bagging Variance (correlated) | $\text{Var}(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}) = \rho\,\sigma^{2} + \frac{1-\rho}{M}\sigma^{2}$ | $\rho$ = pairwise correlation |
| 5 | Bootstrap Coverage | $P(\text{not selected}) = \left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right)^{N} \to e^{-1} \approx 0.368$ | $\approx 36.8\%$ unused (OOB) |
| 6 | OOB Unique Sample Rate | $1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right)^{N} \approx 0.632$ | $\approx 63.2\%$ in each bag |
| 7 | AdaBoost Confidence | $\alpha_{m} = \frac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{1-\epsilon_{m}}{\epsilon_{m}}\right)$ | $\epsilon_{m}$ = weighted error |
| 8 | AdaBoost Weight Update | $w_{i}^{(m+1)} = \frac{w_{i}^{(m)}\exp(-\alpha_{m}\,y_{i}\,h_{m}(x_{i}))}{Z_{m}}$ | $Z_{m}$ = normaliser |
| 9 | AdaBoost Final Hypothesis | $H(x) = \text{sign}\!\left(\sum_{m=1}^{M}\alpha_{m}\,h_{m}(x)\right)$ | Weighted sign sum |
| 10 | Gradient Boosting Pseudo-Residual | $r_{im} = -\left[\frac{\partial\mathcal{L}(y_{i},F)}{\partial F}\right]_{F=F_{m-1}}$ | $\mathcal{L}$ = loss function |
| 11 | Gradient Boosting Update | $F_{m}(x) = F_{m-1}(x) + \nu \cdot h_{m}(x)$ | $\nu$ = learning rate |
| 12 | Training Error Bound (AdaBoost) | $\text{err}_{\text{train}} \le \prod_{m=1}^{M} 2\sqrt{\epsilon_{m}(1-\epsilon_{m})}$ | Exponential decay |
| 13 | Margin Interpretation | $\text{margin}(x,y) = y\sum_{m}\alpha_{m}h_{m}(x)\big/\sum_{m}\vert\alpha_{m}\vert$ | Larger margin = better generalisation |
| 14 | Bagging Effective $\rho$ Reduction | $\rho_{\text{RF}} = \rho_{\text{bag}} - \Delta$ | $\Delta$ from random feature subsets |

---

### 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Bagging (Random Forests):** Used in **banking** (credit scoring), **remote sensing** (land-cover classification), **bioinformatics** (gene expression analysis), where variance is the dominant source of error.
- **Boosting (XGBoost / LightGBM):** Dominates **Kaggle competitions** (≈70% of winning tabular-data solutions pre-deep-learning era), **click-through rate prediction** in ad-tech, **anomaly detection**, and **search ranking** (e.g., Yahoo!, Microsoft Bing historically used boosted tree ensembles).
- **OOB Estimation:** Provides a *free* cross-validation estimate in Bagging, saving $\approx 30\%$ of computational cost compared to a separate validation set.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Variance of a Bagged Estimator

**Claim:** If $M$ i.i.d. base learners $\hat{f}_{1}, \dots, \hat{f}_{M}$ each have variance $\sigma^{2}$ and pairwise correlation $\rho$, then:

$$
\text{Var}\!\left(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}\right) = \rho\,\sigma^{2} + \frac{1 - \rho}{M}\,\sigma^{2}
$$

**Step 1 — Define the bagged estimator:**
$$
\hat{f}_{\text{bag}} = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{m=1}^{M}\hat{f}_{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the variance operator to a sum:**
$$
\text{Var}\!\left(\frac{1}{M}\sum_{m=1}^{M}\hat{f}_{m}\right) = \frac{1}{M^{2}}\,\text{Var}\!\left(\sum_{m=1}^{M}\hat{f}_{m}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Expand using the bilinearity of covariance:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}\!\left(\sum_{m=1}^{M}\hat{f}_{m}\right) &= \sum_{m=1}^{M}\text{Var}(\hat{f}_{m}) + 2\sum_{i < j}\text{Cov}(\hat{f}_{i},\hat{f}_{j})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\text{Var}(\hat{f}_{m}) = \sigma^{2}$ and $\text{Cov}(\hat{f}_{i},\hat{f}_{j}) = \rho\,\sigma^{2}$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}\!\left(\sum_{m=1}^{M}\hat{f}_{m}\right) &= M\sigma^{2} + 2\binom{M}{2}\rho\sigma^{2} \\
&= M\sigma^{2} + M(M-1)\rho\sigma^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Divide by $M^{2}$ to recover the bagged variance:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}) &= \frac{M\sigma^{2} + M(M-1)\rho\sigma^{2}}{M^{2}} \\
&= \frac{\sigma^{2}}{M} + \frac{(M-1)\rho\sigma^{2}}{M} \\
&= \rho\sigma^{2} + \frac{1-\rho}{M}\sigma^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Take the limit as $M \to \infty$:**
$$
\lim_{M \to \infty} \text{Var}(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}) = \rho\,\sigma^{2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Adding more base learners reduces the variance *asymptotically to* $\rho\,\sigma^{2}$, never to zero. The only way to drive the residual variance further down is to **decrease the correlation $\rho$** — which is exactly what Random Forest does via random feature selection.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: AdaBoost's Exponential Loss Minimisation

**Claim:** AdaBoost is equivalent to greedily minimising the **exponential loss** $\mathcal{L}_{\exp} = \mathbb{E}\!\left[e^{-yF(x)}\right]$ where $F(x) = \sum_{m}\alpha_{m}h_{m}(x)$.

**Step 1 — Write the empirical exponential loss:**
$$
\mathcal{L}(F) = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\exp\!\left(-y_{i}F(x_{i})\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\exp\!\left(-y_{i}\sum_{m=1}^{M}\alpha_{m}h_{m}(x_{i})\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Suppose $F_{m-1}$ is fixed, and we wish to choose the next $h_{m}$ and $\alpha_{m}$ to minimise $\mathcal{L}$. The update $F_{m} = F_{m-1} + \alpha_{m}h_{m}$ gives:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_{m} &= \sum_{i=1}^{N}\exp\!\left(-y_{i}\bigl(F_{m-1}(x_{i}) + \alpha_{m}h_{m}(x_{i})\bigr)\right) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{N}w_{i}^{(m)}\exp\!\left(-\alpha_{m}\,y_{i}h_{m}(x_{i})\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $w_{i}^{(m)} = e^{-y_{i}F_{m-1}(x_{i})}$ encodes the cumulative weight assigned so far.

**Step 3 — Separate correctly- and incorrectly-classified samples.** Since $y_{i}h_{m}(x_{i}) \in \{-1, +1\}$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_{m} &= e^{-\alpha_{m}}\sum_{i:\,y_{i}=h_{m}}w_{i}^{(m)} + e^{+\alpha_{m}}\sum_{i:\,y_{i}\neq h_{m}}w_{i}^{(m)} \\
&= e^{-\alpha_{m}}(1 - W_{\text{err}}) + e^{+\alpha_{m}}W_{\text{err}}
\end{aligned}
$$
where $W_{\text{err}} = \sum_{i:\,y_{i}\neq h_{m}}w_{i}^{(m)} = \epsilon_{m}$ is the weighted error.

**Step 4 — Minimise $\mathcal{L}_{m}$ with respect to $h_{m}$ first.** For a fixed $\alpha_{m}$, this is minimised by choosing $h_{m}$ to minimise $\epsilon_{m}$ — i.e., the *weighted* training error.

**Step 5 — Minimise with respect to $\alpha_{m}$** (treat $\epsilon_{m}$ as fixed):
$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{m}}{\partial \alpha_{m}} = -e^{-\alpha_{m}}(1-\epsilon_{m}) + e^{+\alpha_{m}}\epsilon_{m} = 0
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $\alpha_{m}$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
e^{+\alpha_{m}}\epsilon_{m} &= e^{-\alpha_{m}}(1 - \epsilon_{m}) \\
e^{2\alpha_{m}} &= \frac{1-\epsilon_{m}}{\epsilon_{m}} \\
\therefore \alpha_{m} &= \frac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{1-\epsilon_{m}}{\epsilon_{m}}\right) \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Favourite:** The fact that $\alpha_{m} \to \infty$ as $\epsilon_{m} \to 0$ tells us that *highly accurate* learners are weighted much more heavily, and conversely that learners with $\epsilon_{m} \geq 0.5$ yield $\alpha_{m} \leq 0$ (effectively excluded by the algorithm).

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — AdaBoost on a Toy Dataset

> [!NOTE]
> This illustrative worked example mirrors the style of KTU numerical/analytical problems.

**Toy Dataset (3 points, binary classification $y \in \{-1, +1\}$):**

| Index $i$ | $x_{i}$ | $y_{i}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | $+1$ |
| 2 | 2 | $+1$ |
| 3 | 3 | $-1$ |

**Iteration 1:**

Initial uniform weights: $w^{(1)} = (1/3, 1/3, 1/3)$.

Suppose the chosen weak learner is the threshold $h_{1}(x) = +1$ if $x \leq 2$, else $-1$.

Predictions: $h_{1}(1)=+1$, $h_{1}(2)=+1$, $h_{1}(3)=-1$. *All three are correct* ⇒ $\epsilon_{1} = 0$.

This is degenerate; the algorithm halts. To make it non-trivial, suppose instead $h_{1}(x) = -1$ if $x \leq 2$, else $+1$.

Then $h_{1}(1)=-1$, $h_{1}(2)=-1$, $h_{1}(3)=+1$, all *wrong* ⇒ $\epsilon_{1} = 1$. Also degenerate (AdaBoost requires $\epsilon_{m} < 0.5$).

**Revised Toy Learner (non-trivial):** Let $h_{1}(x) = +1$ if $x \leq 1.5$, else $-1$. Then:
- $h_{1}(1) = +1$ ✓
- $h_{1}(2) = -1$ ✗
- $h_{1}(3) = -1$ ✓

So 2 correct, 1 wrong ⇒ $\epsilon_{1} = 1/3$.

**Compute $\alpha_{1}$:**
$$
\alpha_{1} = \frac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{1 - 1/3}{1/3}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\ln(2) \approx 0.347
$$

**Update weights:** $Z_{1} = (1/3)e^{-0.347} + (1/3)e^{+0.347} + (1/3)e^{-0.347}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
e^{-0.347} &\approx 0.707 \\
e^{+0.347} &\approx 1.414 \\
Z_{1} &= (1/3)(0.707) + (1/3)(1.414) + (1/3)(0.707) = (1/3)(2.828) \approx 0.943
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{1}^{(2)} &= \frac{(1/3)(0.707)}{0.943} \approx 0.250 \\
w_{2}^{(2)} &= \frac{(1/3)(1.414)}{0.943} \approx 0.500 \\
w_{3}^{(2)} &= \frac{(1/3)(0.707)}{0.943} \approx 0.250
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Observation:** The weight of sample 2 — the one that $h_{1}$ misclassified — *doubled* from $1/3$ to $0.500$. This is precisely the "re-weighting" mechanism of AdaBoost: subsequent learners will be forced to pay *twice* the attention to point 2.

**Iteration 2:** A new learner $h_{2}$ is trained on these re-weighted samples. The algorithm continues until the weighted error falls below the stopping threshold or $M$ iterations are completed.

---

### 3.4 OOB (Out-of-Bag) Error Estimation

For each bootstrap sample $\mathcal{D}_{m}$, the samples *not* drawn form the *out-of-bag* set $\text{OOB}_{m} = \mathcal{D} \setminus \mathcal{D}_{m}$. For prediction at a point $x_{i}$, use only those learners $h_{m}$ for which $x_{i} \in \text{OOB}_{m}$:

$$
\hat{y}_{\text{OOB}}(x_{i}) = \arg\max_{c}\sum_{m:\,x_{i}\in\text{OOB}_{m}}\mathbb{1}\!\left[h_{m}(x_{i}) = c\right]
$$

The OOB error is:
$$
\text{Err}_{\text{OOB}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbb{1}\!\left[\hat{y}_{\text{OOB}}(x_{i}) \neq y_{i}\right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Property:** As $N \to \infty$, $\text{Err}_{\text{OOB}}$ converges to the **leave-one-out cross-validation error** at negligible additional computational cost.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Ensemble Learning — Master Architecture Flow

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the *parallel* nature of Bagging and the *sequential* nature of Boosting, along with their respective aggregation mechanisms. All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[ORIGINAL DATASET D] --> B0[BAGGING PATH]
    A0 --> C0[BOOSTING PATH]
    B0 --> D0[BOOTSTRAP SAMPLE 1]
    B0 --> E0[BOOTSTRAP SAMPLE 2]
    B0 --> F0[BOOTSTRAP SAMPLE 3]
    D0 --> G0[BASE LEARNER H1]
    E0 --> H0[BASE LEARNER H2]
    F0 --> I0[BASE LEARNER H3]
    G0 --> J0[UNIFORM AGGREGATION VOTE OR MEAN]
    H0 --> J0
    I0 --> J0
    J0 --> K0[FINAL BAGGING OUTPUT]
    C0 --> L0[INITIAL LEARNER H1 ON UNIFORM WEIGHTS]
    L0 --> M0[COMPUTE WEIGHTED ERROR EPSILON 1]
    M0 --> N0[COMPUTE LEARNER WEIGHT ALPHA 1]
    N0 --> O0[UPDATE SAMPLE WEIGHTS W1 W2 W3]
    O0 --> P0[LEARNER H2 ON REWEIGHTED DATA]
    P0 --> Q0[COMPUTE WEIGHTED ERROR EPSILON 2]
    Q0 --> R0[COMPUTE LEARNER WEIGHT ALPHA 2]
    R0 --> S0[UPDATE SAMPLE WEIGHTS AGAIN]
    S0 --> T0[WEIGHTED SIGN COMBINATION]
    T0 --> U0[FINAL BOOSTING OUTPUT]
    K0 --> V0[ENSEMBLE PREDICTION]
    U0 --> V0
```

### 4.2 Bagging Internal Mechanics — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[FULL TRAINING SET N] --> B1[RANDOM SAMPLE WITH REPLACEMENT N TIMES]
    B1 --> C1[BOOTSTRAP 1 63 PERCENT UNIQUE]
    B1 --> C2[BOOTSTRAP 2 63 PERCENT UNIQUE]
    B1 --> C3[BOOTSTRAP M 63 PERCENT UNIQUE]
    C1 --> D1[UNPRUNED TREE T1]
    C2 --> D2[UNPRUNED TREE T2]
    C3 --> D3[UNPRUNED TREE TM]
    D1 --> E1[OOB 1 ABOUT 37 PERCENT]
    D2 --> E2[OOB 2 ABOUT 37 PERCENT]
    D3 --> E3[OOB M ABOUT 37 PERCENT]
    D1 --> F1[INDIVIDUAL PREDICTION]
    D2 --> F2[INDIVIDUAL PREDICTION]
    D3 --> F3[INDIVIDUAL PREDICTION]
    F1 --> G1[MAJORITY VOTE OR MEAN]
    F2 --> G1
    F3 --> G1
    G1 --> H1[FINAL ENSEMBLE OUTPUT]
    E1 --> I1[OOB ERROR ESTIMATE]
    E2 --> I1
    E3 --> I1
```

### 4.3 Boosting Sequential Re-weighting Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A2[DATASET WITH WEIGHTS W INITIALLY UNIFORM] --> B2[TRAIN WEAK LEARNER HM]
    B2 --> C2[COMPUTE WEIGHTED ERROR EPSILON M]
    C2 --> D2{IS EPSILON LESS THAN 0.5}
    D2 -->|NO| E2[ABORT ENSEMBLE INSUFFICIENT WEAK LEARNER]
    D2 -->|YES| F2[COMPUTE ALPHA M 0.5 LOG 1 MINUS EPSILON OVER EPSILON]
    F2 --> G2[UPDATE WEIGHTS WI EXP OF MINUS ALPHA Y H]
    G2 --> H2[NORMALISE WEIGHTS BY ZM]
    H2 --> I2[ACCUMULATE FM PLUS ALPHA M HM]
    I2 --> J2{REACHED M ITERATIONS}
    J2 -->|NO| B2
    J2 -->|YES| K2[FINAL CLASSIFIER SIGN OF SUM ALPHA M HM]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading aid:** In the **Bagging** diagram, notice the *parallel* branches; learners do not exchange information. In the **Boosting** diagram, observe the *cyclic feedback loop* — each iteration's output flows back into the next iteration's input. This visual asymmetry is the single most important structural difference between the two paradigms and is a frequent KTU diagram-sketch question.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define ensemble learning. What are the necessary conditions under which an ensemble of weak learners can outperform a single strong learner?**

**Model Answer:**

Ensemble learning is a machine learning paradigm in which multiple base learners (also called weak learners) are combined to produce a single predictive model whose performance is superior to that of any individual constituent. The three necessary conditions, derived from the foundational works of **Kearns & Valiant (1989)** and **Dietterich (2000)**, are:

1. **Statistical condition** — the hypothesis space is too rich relative to the limited training data, leading to multiple plausible hypotheses; averaging them reduces the risk of selecting the wrong one.
2. **Computational condition** — a single learning algorithm may get trapped in local optima during greedy optimisation; combining multiple starting points mitigates this.
3. **Representational condition** — the true target function may lie outside the hypothesis class of any individual learner but can be expressed as a weighted combination of several such classes.

For an ensemble to outperform a single member, the base learners must be **(a) individually better than random guessing** and **(b) mutually diverse** — their errors must be largely uncorrelated.

*(Word count: ~135 | Marks: 3)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between Bagging and Boosting in terms of (i) training strategy, (ii) error component targeted, and (iii) sensitivity to overfitting.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Bagging | Boosting |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Training strategy | **Parallel** — each learner is trained independently on a bootstrap sample | **Sequential** — each learner is trained on the *re-weighted* data left by its predecessor |
| (ii) Error component | Targets **variance** reduction | Targets **bias** reduction |
| (iii) Overfitting sensitivity | **Low** — averaging smooths out idiosyncratic noise | **High** — aggressive re-weighting can fit noise; requires regularisation (e.g., shrinkage, early stopping) |

*(Word count: ~80 | Marks: 3)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the **Bagging algorithm** in detail. Describe how bootstrap sampling creates training subsets and how the final prediction is aggregated. Include a derivation showing why averaging reduces variance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a **Random Forest**? Explain how it differs from a vanilla bagged-tree ensemble and derive the asymptotic variance bound $\rho\sigma^{2} + \frac{1-\rho}{M}\sigma^{2}$. Discuss the role of the **Out-of-Bag (OOB) error** in model selection. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Bagging Algorithm — Step-by-Step Description:**

1. **Input:** Training set $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_{i}, y_{i})\}_{i=1}^{N}$, number of base learners $M$, base learner $\mathcal{A}$ (commonly an unpruned decision tree).
2. **For** $m = 1, 2, \dots, M$:
   - Draw a bootstrap sample $\mathcal{D}_{m}$ of size $N$ from $\mathcal{D}$ **with replacement**.
   - Train a base learner $\hat{f}_{m}$ on $\mathcal{D}_{m}$ using $\mathcal{A}$.
3. **Aggregate predictions:**
   - **Regression:** $\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}(x) = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{m=1}^{M}\hat{f}_{m}(x)$
   - **Classification:** $\hat{y}_{\text{bag}}(x) = \arg\max_{c}\sum_{m=1}^{M}\mathbb{1}\!\left[\hat{f}_{m}(x)=c\right]$

**Variance Reduction — Derivation:**

Let each $\hat{f}_{m}$ have variance $\sigma^{2}$ and pairwise correlation $\rho$. Then:

$$
\text{Var}\!\left(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}\right) = \text{Var}\!\left(\frac{1}{M}\sum_{m=1}^{M}\hat{f}_{m}\right)
$$

Using the bilinearity of covariance and the fact that there are $M$ variance terms and $\binom{M}{2}$ covariance terms in the upper triangle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}) &= \frac{1}{M^{2}}\left[\sum_{m=1}^{M}\sigma^{2} + 2\binom{M}{2}\rho\sigma^{2}\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{M^{2}}\left[M\sigma^{2} + M(M-1)\rho\sigma^{2}\right] \\
&= \frac{\sigma^{2}}{M} + \frac{(M-1)\rho\sigma^{2}}{M} \\
&= \rho\sigma^{2} + \frac{1-\rho}{M}\sigma^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

As $M \to \infty$, this approaches $\rho\sigma^{2}$, which is the irreducible correlation floor. The training error of each base learner is unaffected (bias is preserved), but the variance is dramatically reduced — Bagging excels when the base learner is **high-variance, low-bias** (e.g., deep unpruned trees). **(7 marks distributed across the algorithm description, aggregation mechanism, and the derivation above.)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Random Forest — Definition and Differences:**

A **Random Forest** (Breiman, 2001) is an ensemble of decision trees that introduces **two sources of randomness**:

1. **Bootstrap sampling** of training data (as in Bagging).
2. **Random feature selection at each split** — when choosing the best split at a node, the algorithm considers only a random subset of $k$ features out of the total $p$ (typically $k = \sqrt{p}$ for classification, $k = p/3$ for regression).

**Why This Matters:**

Bagging alone is insufficient to drive down the inter-tree correlation $\rho$ when trees are built on similar data, especially in high-dimensional settings where the same dominant features are repeatedly selected. By restricting the candidate features at each split, Random Forest forces **decorrelated** trees, reducing $\rho$ and therefore the asymptotic variance floor $\rho\sigma^{2}$.

**Asymptotic Variance Bound** (derived in part (a) above):

$$
\lim_{M \to \infty} \text{Var}(\hat{f}_{\text{RF}}) = \rho_{\text{RF}}\,\sigma^{2} \quad \text{where} \quad \rho_{\text{RF}} < \rho_{\text{bag}}
$$

**OOB Error — Model Selection Tool:**

For each bootstrap sample $\mathcal{D}_{m}$, the unselected samples (≈36.8%) form the OOB set $\text{OOB}_{m}$. For each training point $x_{i}$, aggregate the predictions of *only those* trees for which $x_{i} \in \text{OOB}_{m}$:

$$
\hat{y}_{\text{OOB}}(x_{i}) = \arg\max_{c}\sum_{m:\,x_{i}\in\text{OOB}_{m}}\mathbb{1}\!\left[\hat{f}_{m}(x_{i})=c\right]
$$

The OOB error $\text{Err}_{\text{OOB}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbb{1}\!\left[\hat{y}_{\text{OOB}}(x_{i}) \neq y_{i}\right]$ is an **unbiased estimator of the test error** without requiring a held-out validation set. It is therefore invaluable for **hyperparameter tuning** (e.g., choosing $k$, tree depth, $M$). **(7 marks.)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Describe the **AdaBoost algorithm** in full mathematical detail. Derive the formula for the learner weight $\alpha_{m} = \frac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{1-\epsilon_{m}}{\epsilon_{m}}\right)$ by minimising the exponential loss. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **Gradient Boosting**? Show that the squared-error variant of Gradient Boosting is equivalent to "fitting the residuals" at each iteration. Discuss two regularisation techniques used to prevent overfitting. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**AdaBoost Algorithm — Full Description:**

**Inputs:** $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_{i}, y_{i})\}_{i=1}^{N}$ with $y_{i} \in \{-1, +1\}$, iterations $M$.

**Initialisation:** $w_{i}^{(1)} = \frac{1}{N}$ for all $i$.

**For** $m = 1, \dots, M$:

1. Train base learner $h_{m}$ on $\mathcal{D}$ using weights $w^{(m)}$.
2. Compute weighted error:
   $$\epsilon_{m} = \sum_{i=1}^{N}w_{i}^{(m)}\mathbb{1}\!\left[y_{i} \neq h_{m}(x_{i})\right]$$
3. **Abort if** $\epsilon_{m} \geq 0.5$.
4. Compute learner weight:
   $$\alpha_{m} = \frac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{1-\epsilon_{m}}{\epsilon_{m}}\right)$$
5. Update weights:
   $$w_{i}^{(m+1)} = \frac{w_{i}^{(m)}\exp\!\left(-\alpha_{m}\,y_{i}\,h_{m}(x_{i})\right)}{Z_{m}}$$
   where $Z_{m} = \sum_{i=1}^{N}w_{i}^{(m)}\exp(-\alpha_{m}y_{i}h_{m}(x_{i}))$.

**Final Hypothesis:** $H(x) = \text{sign}\!\left(\sum_{m=1}^{M}\alpha_{m}h_{m}(x)\right)$.

**Derivation of $\alpha_{m}$:**

The exponential loss after iteration $m$ is:
$$
\mathcal{L}_{m} = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\exp\!\left(-y_{i}\bigl(F_{m-1}(x_{i}) + \alpha_{m}h_{m}(x_{i})\bigr)\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{N}w_{i}^{(m)}\exp\!\left(-\alpha_{m}\,y_{i}h_{m}(x_{i})\right)
$$

Separating correctly and incorrectly classified points:
$$
\mathcal{L}_{m} = e^{-\alpha_{m}}(1-\epsilon_{m}) + e^{+\alpha_{m}}\epsilon_{m}
$$

Taking the derivative and setting it to zero:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d\mathcal{L}_{m}}{d\alpha_{m}} &= -e^{-\alpha_{m}}(1-\epsilon_{m}) + e^{+\alpha_{m}}\epsilon_{m} = 0 \\
e^{2\alpha_{m}}\epsilon_{m} &= 1 - \epsilon_{m} \\
\alpha_{m} &= \frac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{1-\epsilon_{m}}{\epsilon_{m}}\right) \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** When $\epsilon_{m} \to 0$, $\alpha_{m} \to \infty$, so highly accurate learners dominate. When $\epsilon_{m} \to 0.5$, $\alpha_{m} \to 0$, so the learner effectively contributes nothing. **(7 marks.)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Gradient Boosting — Definition:**

Gradient Boosting (Friedman, 2001) generalises boosting by performing **gradient descent in function space**. Given a differentiable loss function $\mathcal{L}(y, F(x))$, at each iteration $m$ a base learner $h_{m}$ is fit to the **negative gradient** (pseudo-residuals):

$$
r_{im} = -\left[\frac{\partial\mathcal{L}\bigl(y_{i}, F(x_{i})\bigr)}{\partial F(x_{i})}\right]_{F = F_{m-1}}
$$

**Equivalence to "Fitting Residuals" for Squared-Error Loss:**

For $\mathcal{L}(y, F) = \frac{1}{2}(y - F)^{2}$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial F} = -(y - F) \quad \Rightarrow \quad r_{im} = y_{i} - F_{m-1}(x_{i})
$$

This is precisely the **residual** between the true target and the current prediction. Thus, in the squared-error case, Gradient Boosting reduces to iteratively fitting a new learner to the *current residuals* — recovering the classic "boosting fits residuals" intuition.

**Two Regularisation Techniques:**

1. **Shrinkage (Learning Rate $\nu$):** The update is dampened as $F_{m} = F_{m-1} + \nu \cdot h_{m}$ with $\nu \in (0, 1]$ (e.g., $\nu = 0.1$). Small $\nu$ requires more iterations but yields a smoother, more generalisable fit. Empirically, the optimal number of trees scales as $M_{\text{opt}} \propto 1/\nu$.

2. **Stochastic Gradient Boosting:** At each iteration, fit $h_{m}$ on a random sub-sample (without replacement) of the training data, typically a fraction $f \in (0.5, 0.8)$. This injects variance reduction (à la Bagging) into the boosting process and dramatically accelerates training on large datasets.

3. *(Bonus — Tree-Specific Regularisation)*: Limit the maximum depth of base trees, the minimum samples per leaf, or apply L2 regularisation on leaf values (as in XGBoost). **(7 marks.)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Avoid These!**
>
> 1. **Confusing sample weights with sample importance scores** — students often write that Boosting *"modifies the dataset"*; it does not. The dataset is fixed; only the **weights** change. (-1 mark)
> 2. **Omitting the normalising constant $Z_{m}$** in the AdaBoost weight update. The update is meaningless without it, and the examiner will deduct at least 1 mark.
> 3. **Writing "Bagging reduces overfitting"** without specifying *variance reduction* — examiners expect the precise term. (-1 mark)
> 4. **Forgetting the correlation term $\rho$** in the variance derivation; many students incorrectly state $\text{Var}(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}) = \sigma^{2}/M$ only, missing the $\rho\sigma^{2}$ asymptotic floor. (-2 marks)
> 5. **Mixing up AdaBoost and Gradient Boosting objectives** — AdaBoost minimises the **exponential loss**; Gradient Boosting minimises an **arbitrary differentiable loss** via gradient descent. Examiners frequently test this distinction. (-2 marks)
> 6. **No diagram in long answers** — KTU valuation key explicitly awards up to **2 marks** for a clean schematic in Part B. Always include one!

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 / Ensemble Learning**

- ✅ **Ensemble Learning** = combining multiple base learners to produce a strong learner with better generalisation.
- ✅ **Three necessary conditions** for ensemble success: statistical, computational, representational.
- ✅ **Bagging** = **Bootstrap Aggregating**; *parallel*; reduces **variance**; combines by *uniform vote/mean*.
- ✅ **Bootstrap sample** contains ≈**63.2%** unique samples; ≈**36.8%** are Out-of-Bag (OOB).
- ✅ **OOB error** ≈ leave-one-out cross-validation at near-zero additional cost.
- ✅ **Bagging variance** = $\rho\sigma^{2} + \frac{1-\rho}{M}\sigma^{2}$ → floor of $\rho\sigma^{2}$ as $M \to \infty$.
- ✅ **Random Forest** = Bagging + **random feature subspace** at each split; reduces inter-tree correlation $\rho$.
- ✅ **Boosting** = *sequential*; reduces **bias**; combines by **weighted vote/sign sum**.
- ✅ **AdaBoost** weight update: $w_{i}^{(m+1)} \propto w_{i}^{(m)} \exp(-\alpha_{m} y_{i} h_{m}(x_{i}))$.
- ✅ **AdaBoost learner weight**: $\alpha_{m} = \frac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{1-\epsilon_{m}}{\epsilon_{m}}\right)$; requires $\epsilon_{m} < 0.5$.
- ✅ **AdaBoost = greedy minimisation of exponential loss** $\mathcal{L}_{\exp} = e^{-yF}$.
- ✅ **Gradient Boosting** fits the **negative gradient** (pseudo-residuals) of any differentiable loss.
- ✅ For **squared-error loss**, pseudo-residuals = ordinary residuals → "boosting fits residuals."
- ✅ **Regularisers for boosting**: shrinkage (learning rate $\nu$), stochastic sub-sampling, depth limits, L2 on leaf values.
- ✅ **Overfitting risk**: Bagging is robust; Boosting is high — needs careful regularisation.
- ✅ **Canonical instantiations**: Bagging → **Random Forest**; Boosting → **AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost, LightGBM**.
- ✅ **Final-combiners**: Bagging = simple average/vote; Boosting = weighted sum of confidences.
- ✅ **Margin interpretation**: AdaBoost maximises the classification margin — a key generalisation guarantee.
- ✅ **Memorise the contrast table** (Section 1.5) — it appears in nearly every KTU Module-2 paper.
- ✅ **Always include a diagram** in Part B answers for the allocated 2 marks.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "PUSe-WAC"** for Bagging vs Boosting: **P**arallel vs **S**equential; **U**niform vs **W**eighted aggregation; **A**verage vs **A**dditive (gradient) update. This single sentence captures the entire conceptual asymmetry of the two paradigms.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Support Vector Machine - Optimal Separating Hyperplane
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT501 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Support Vector Machine - Optimal Separating Hyperplane

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** is a discriminative supervised learning classifier formally defined within the framework of **statistical learning theory** and the **VC (Vapnik–Chervonenkis) dimension**. For a binary classification task on a linearly separable dataset, an SVM seeks the **Optimal Separating Hyperplane (OSH)** — the unique hyperplane that maximizes the *margin* (the perpendicular distance) between itself and the closest training samples of each class.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimal Separating Hyperplane (OSH):** The hyperplane $H : \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ that separates two classes while **maximizing the geometric margin** $M = \dfrac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ between the two parallel canonical bounding hyperplanes. The data points that lie exactly on these bounding hyperplanes are termed **support vectors**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Widest Road Builder"

Imagine you are a civil engineer asked to construct a **straight road** through a crowded market to separate vegetable vendors (Class +1) on the left from fruit vendors (Class −1) on the right.

- You are **not** trying to find *any* road that separates them — almost infinitely many such roads exist.
- You want the **widest possible road** so that even if a vendor pushes their cart a little closer, the road still keeps the two groups safely apart.
- The **width of the road is the margin** $M$.
- The vendors whose carts are *just touching the curbs* of your road are the **support vectors** — they alone determine where the road goes.
- If a new vendor arrives, you decide their class based on which side of the road they stand on.

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Intuition:** The OSH is the hyperplane equidistant from the two convex hulls of the classes, oriented so the perpendicular distance to the nearest point of either hull is *maximized*. This is the heart of SVM's superior generalization ability.

### 1.3 Key Mathematical Entities (Standard Metrics in **bold**)

| Symbol | Standard Term | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $\mathbf{w}$ | **Weight vector** | Normal vector perpendicular to the hyperplane |
| $b$ | **Bias term** | Signed distance offset of the hyperplane from origin |
| $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ | **Class label** | Binary target assignment |
| $\alpha_i$ | **Lagrange multiplier** | Non-negative dual variable (≥ 0) |
| $\xi_i$ | **Slack variable** | Permits soft-margin violations |
| $C$ | **Penalty parameter** | Trade-off between margin width and misclassification |
| $M = 2 / \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$ | **Geometric margin** | Twice the perpendicular distance to nearest support vector |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Stepwise Block

1. **Input Assumption:** Training set $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ with $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ is *linearly separable* in feature space.
2. **Hyperplane Hypothesis:** A candidate decision surface is $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$, producing the classifier $\hat{y} = \text{sign}(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b)$.
3. **Why maximize the margin?** From PAC-learning theory, a *larger* margin reduces the upper bound on the **expected risk** (generalization error), giving better performance on unseen data. The margin acts as a *regularizer*.
4. **Canonical Formulation:** We *rescale* $(\mathbf{w}, b)$ so that the closest point satisfies $\vert \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b \vert = 1$. This is always possible because if $\mathbf{w}, b$ solve the problem, then $\lambda \mathbf{w}, \lambda b$ also solve it.
5. **Two parallel margin hyperplanes** emerge:
   - $H_1 : \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = +1$ (positive class side)
   - $H_2 : \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = -1$ (negative class side)
6. **Geometric distance** from any point $\mathbf{x}_0$ to the hyperplane: $d = \dfrac{\vert \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_0 + b \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$.
7. **Margin width** between $H_1$ and $H_2$: $M = \dfrac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$.
8. **Maximize $M$** ⟺ **Minimize $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$** ⟺ **Minimize $\tfrac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$** (the squared form is preferred for quadratic programming tractability).
9. **Solve the constrained QP** using Lagrange duality → recover the **sparse** weight vector $\mathbf{w}^* = \sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i^* y_i \mathbf{x}_i$, where only the support vectors have $\alpha_i^* > 0$.
10. **Predict** new point $\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}}$ via $f(\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}}) = \text{sign}\!\left(\sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i^* y_i (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_{\text{new}}) + b^*\right)$.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical cheat sheet** for solving any KTU 14-mark problem on Optimal Separating Hyperplane.

| # | Formula | Description | Constraint / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ | Decision hyperplane | Linear classifier |
