| 2 | $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$ | Canonical separation constraint | $\forall i = 1, \dots, N$ |
| 3 | $\min_{\mathbf{w},b} \tfrac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$ | Primal objective (hard margin) | Subject to (2) |
| 4 | $\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \sum_i \alpha_i - \tfrac{1}{2} \sum_i \sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j)$ | Dual objective (hard margin) | $\alpha_i \geq 0,\ \sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$ |
| 5 | $\mathbf{w}^* = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i^* y_i \mathbf{x}_i$ | Optimal weight vector | Sparse in $\boldsymbol{\alpha}$ |
| 6 | $b^* = y_k - \mathbf{w}^{*\top} \mathbf{x}_k$ | Optimal bias (any support vector $k$) | $\alpha_k^* > 0$ |
| 7 | $M = 2 / \Vert \mathbf{w}^* \Vert$ | Geometric margin width | $M > 0$ |
| 8 | $d(\mathbf{x}_0, H) = \vert \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_0 + b \vert / \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$ | Perpendicular distance from $\mathbf{x}_0$ to $H$ | $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert \neq 0$ |
| 9 | $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i,\ \xi_i \geq 0$ | Soft-margin constraint | $\xi_i$ = slack |
| 10 | $\min \tfrac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum_i \xi_i$ | Soft-margin primal objective | $C > 0$ |
| 11 | $\alpha_i ( y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i) = 0$ | KKT complementary slackness | At optimum |
| 12 | $(C - \mu_i)\,\xi_i = 0$ | KKT stationarity for slack | $\mu_i \geq 0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL ENGINEERING INSIGHT:** The solution is **sparse** — only the **support vectors** (where $\alpha_i^* > 0$) influence $\mathbf{w}^*$ and therefore the decision boundary. All other training points are *inactive*. This sparsity gives SVM efficient inference and is why the model is robust to outliers that are not on the margin.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bioinformatics:** Microarray cancer classification (high-dimensional, small-sample regime).
- **Computer Vision:** Face detection, hand-written digit recognition (MNIST benchmark).
- **Geotechnical Engineering:** Landslide susceptibility mapping using geospatial features.
- **Fault Diagnosis:** Mechanical vibration signal classification for rotating machinery.
- **Text Mining:** Spam filtering and sentiment polarity classification.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — From Margin Maximization to Quadratic Program

**Starting idea:** Maximize the geometric margin $M = 2 / \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max_{\mathbf{w},b}\ \frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} \quad &\Longleftrightarrow \quad \min_{\mathbf{w},b}\ \frac{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}{2} \\
&\Longleftrightarrow \quad \min_{\mathbf{w},b}\ \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why the squared form?** Because $\tfrac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$ is a strictly convex quadratic, guaranteeing a **unique global minimum** when solvable — essential for the convex optimization theory underlying SVM.

### 3.2 Derivation II — Lagrange Dual of the Hard-Margin SVM

**Step 1.** Introduce a non-negative Lagrange multiplier $\alpha_i \geq 0$ per training point, transforming the constrained minimization into the **Lagrangian**:

$$
L(\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \left[ y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \right]
$$

**Step 2.** Set the partial derivatives of $L$ w.r.t. the **primal variables** $\mathbf{w}$ and $b$ to zero (stationarity conditions for an interior optimum).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{w}} &= \mathbf{w} - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i = 0 \\[4pt]
\frac{\partial L}{\partial b} &= -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3.** From the first stationarity condition we obtain the **representation of the optimal weight vector**:

$$
\mathbf{w}^* = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i^* y_i \mathbf{x}_i
$$

From the second:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i^* y_i = 0
$$

**Step 4.** Substitute $\mathbf{w}^*$ back into $L$ to obtain the **Wolfe dual** (a function of $\boldsymbol{\alpha}$ alone):

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_D(\boldsymbol{\alpha}) &= \frac{1}{2}\left(\sum_i \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i\right)^\top \left(\sum_j \alpha_j y_j \mathbf{x}_j\right) - \sum_i \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i^\top \left(\sum_j \alpha_j y_j \mathbf{x}_j\right) - b\sum_i \alpha_i y_i + \sum_i \alpha_i \\
&= \sum_i \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_i \sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5.** Therefore the **dual optimization problem** is:

$$
\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \quad W(\boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j)
$$

subject to $\alpha_i \geq 0$ and $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$.

### 3.3 Derivation III — Recovering the Bias $b^*$

By **KKT complementary slackness**, for every $i$:

$$
\alpha_i^* \left[ y_i(\mathbf{w}^{*\top} \mathbf{x}_i + b^*) - 1 \right] = 0
$$

For any support vector $\mathbf{x}_k$ with $\alpha_k^* > 0$, the bracket must vanish, so:

$$
y_k (\mathbf{w}^{*\top} \mathbf{x}_k + b^*) - 1 = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad b^* = y_k - \mathbf{w}^{*\top} \mathbf{x}_k
$$

In practice one **averages** $b^*$ over all support vectors for numerical stability.

### 3.4 Derivation IV — Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Standard)

> **Problem.** Consider 4 training points in $\mathbb{R}^2$:
> $\mathbf{x}_1 = (1, 1),\ y_1 = +1$; $\mathbf{x}_2 = (1, -1),\ y_2 = +1$; $\mathbf{x}_3 = (-1, -1),\ y_3 = -1$; $\mathbf{x}_4 = (-1, 1),\ y_4 = -1$.
> Find the OSH using the dual formulation.

**Step A.** Note that the dataset is symmetric and linearly separable. The natural OSH is the $x_2$-axis (i.e., the horizontal line $x_2 = 0$).

**Step B.** A hyperplane $x_2 = 0$ has the form $0\cdot x_1 + 1\cdot x_2 + 0 = 0$, hence $\mathbf{w} = (0, 1)^\top$, $b = 0$.

**Step C.** Compute $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert = 1$, so margin $M = 2/1 = \mathbf{2}$.

**Step D.** Verify canonical constraints (all 4 should equal $\pm 1$ since points lie on the margin):
- $y_1(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_1 + b) = +1 \cdot 1 = +1$ ✓
- $y_2(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_2 + b) = +1 \cdot (-1) = -1$ ✓
- $y_3(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_3 + b) = -1 \cdot (-1) = +1$ ✓
- $y_4(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_4 + b) = -1 \cdot 1 = -1$ ✓

**Step E.** Compute the **Gram matrix** $G_{ij} = y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j)$:

$$
G = \begin{pmatrix} +2 & 0 & 0 & -2 \\ 0 & +2 & -2 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 & +2 & 0 \\ -2 & 0 & 0 & +2 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step F.** Solve the dual. By symmetry, the optimal $\boldsymbol{\alpha}^* = (1, 1, 1, 1)^\top$.

**Step G.** Recover $\mathbf{w}^* = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i = 1\cdot(+1)(1,1) + 1\cdot(+1)(1,-1) + 1\cdot(-1)(-1,-1) + 1\cdot(-1)(-1,1) = (0, 2)^\top$.

**Step H.** Normalize (divide by 2): $\mathbf{w}^* = (0, 1)^\top$. The hyperplane is $x_2 = 0$, margin $= 2$. ✓

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Soft-Margin Extension Summary

| Scenario | Primal | Dual Objective | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hard margin** | $\min \tfrac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$ | $\max \sum \alpha_i - \tfrac{1}{2}\sum\sum \alpha_i\alpha_j y_i y_j K_{ij}$ | Perfectly separable data |
| **Soft margin (C-form)** | $\min \tfrac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C\sum \xi_i$ | Same dual but $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ | Overlapping classes, noisy data |
| **ν-SVM** | $\min \tfrac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 - \nu\rho + \tfrac{1}{N}\sum \xi_i$ | Dual with $\nu$-bound on margin errors | Controlling fraction of support vectors |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — OSH Training Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Training Data x_i, y_i] --> B[Linear Separability Check]
    B -- Separable --> C[Formulate Primal QP: Min 0.5 * ||w||^2]
    B -- Not Separable --> D[Introduce Slack xi_i, Penalty C]
    D --> C2[Soft-Margin Primal QP]
    C --> E[Construct Lagrangian L w, b, alpha]
    C2 --> E2[Construct Lagrangian L w, b, alpha, mu]
    E --> F[Stationarity: dL/dw = 0, dL/db = 0]
    E2 --> F2[Stationarity plus KKT Conditions]
    F --> G[Wolfe Dual: max sum alpha_i minus 0.5 sum sum alpha_i alpha_j y_i y_j x_i^T x_j]
    F2 --> G2[Box-Constrained Dual: 0 <= alpha_i <= C]
    G --> H[Quadratic Programming Solver]
    G2 --> H
    H --> I[Recover w star = sum alpha_i star y_i x_i]
    I --> J[Recover b star from Support Vectors]
    J --> K[Optimal Separating Hyperplane H star]
    K --> L[Classifier: f x = sign w star^T x + b star]
```

### 4.2 Geometric Schematic — Margin, Support Vectors, and Hyperplane

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P["Positive Class - y = +1"]
        P1((x1)) --- P2((x2)) --- P3((x3))
    end
    subgraph N["Negative Class - y = -1"]
        N1((x4)) --- N2((x5)) --- N3((x6))
    end
    H1["H1: w^T x + b = +1"]:::margin
    H0["H star: w^T x + b = 0  OPTIMAL HYPERPLANE"]:::opt
    H2["H2: w^T x + b = -1"]:::margin
    P3 -. lies on H1 .- H1
    N1 -. lies on H2 .- H2
    H1 --- H0
    H0 --- H2
    W["Weight vector w  (normal to H star)"]:::vec --> H0
    classDef opt fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef margin fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef vec fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Decision-Flow Block Diagram — Inference at Test Time

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T[New Test Point x_new] --> U[Compute Inner Products x_i^T x_new for all Support Vectors]
    U --> V[Weighted Sum: sum alpha_i star y_i times x_i^T x_new]
    V --> W[Add Bias b star]
    W --> X{Sign of Result greater than 0?}
    X -- Yes --> Y[Predict Class +1]
    X -- No --> Z[Predict Class -1]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The gold hyperplane in Section 4.2 is the **OSH**; the blue lines are the **canonical margin hyperplanes** $H_1, H_2$; the green arrow is the **weight vector** $\mathbf{w}$ orthogonal to the OSH; the small circles touching $H_1$ and $H_2$ are the **support vectors** — they are the *only* training points that influence $\mathbf{w}^*$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the term **support vector** in the context of an SVM. Why are they called *support* vectors?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *support vector* is a training sample $(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)$ that lies **exactly on one of the margin hyperplanes** $H_1$ or $H_2$, i.e. it satisfies $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) = 1$. Equivalently, its Lagrange multiplier $\alpha_i^* > 0$. They are called *support* vectors because they *support* (determine) the position and orientation of the optimal separating hyperplane; if any non-support-vector were moved, the OSH would not change, but moving a support vector *would* shift it. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** State the **objective function** and **constraints** of the hard-margin SVM primal optimization problem.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The hard-margin SVM primal is a convex quadratic program:

$$
\min_{\mathbf{w},\, b} \ \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2
$$

subject to the *canonical separation constraints*:

$$
y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1, \quad \forall i = 1, 2, \dots, N
$$

The objective minimizes the squared norm of the weight vector, which is equivalent to maximizing the geometric margin $M = 2/\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$. The constraints enforce that every training point lies on the correct side of the canonical hyperplane with functional margin at least 1. **(3 marks)**

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Module 3 Internal Choice Pattern (KTU 2024):** Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** With the help of a clearly labelled diagram, explain the **geometric intuition** of the **Optimal Separating Hyperplane** in a binary SVM. Define margin, support vectors, and weight vector in your answer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Formulate the **hard-margin SVM** as a convex optimization problem. Starting from the margin-maximization objective, derive the **Lagrange dual** problem and state the **KKT conditions** at the optimum. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Geometric Intuition (7 marks):**

For a binary classification problem with linearly separable classes, infinitely many hyperplanes can separate them. The SVM selects the **unique** hyperplane that maximizes the *geometric margin* — the perpendicular distance between the hyperplane and the closest training samples of each class.

- **Hyperplane $H : \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$**: the decision boundary.
- **Weight vector $\mathbf{w}$**: normal to $H$, pointing perpendicular to it; its magnitude is inversely related to the margin.
- **Two parallel margin hyperplanes** $H_1 : \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = +1$ and $H_2 : \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = -1$ touch the nearest points of each class.
- **Support vectors**: the data points that lie precisely on $H_1$ or $H_2$. They alone determine the OSH.
- **Margin $M = 2 / \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$**: the distance between $H_1$ and $H_2$. A larger margin generally yields better generalization to unseen data (statistical learning theory).

The decision rule for a new point $\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}}$ is:

$$
\hat{y} = \text{sign}\!\left(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_{\text{new}} + b\right)
$$

**(7 marks — 2 marks for diagram description, 2 marks for definitions, 2 marks for decision rule, 1 mark for generalization statement)**

---

**(b) Hard-Margin Formulation and Dual Derivation (7 marks):**

**Primal Problem:**

$$
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b}\ \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1,\ \forall i
$$

**Lagrangian (introduce $\alpha_i \geq 0$):**

$$
L(\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i\left[y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1\right]
$$

**Stationarity conditions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} L = 0 &\Rightarrow \mathbf{w}^* = \sum_i \alpha_i^* y_i \mathbf{x}_i \\
\frac{\partial L}{\partial b} = 0 &\Rightarrow \sum_i \alpha_i^* y_i = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Wolfe Dual (substitute back):**

$$
\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \sum_i \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_i \sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \alpha_i \geq 0,\ \sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0
$$

**KKT conditions** at optimum:
1. Primal feasibility: $y_i(\mathbf{w}^{*\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b^*) - 1 \geq 0$.
2. Dual feasibility: $\alpha_i^* \geq 0$.
3. Complementary slackness: $\alpha_i^*[y_i(\mathbf{w}^{*\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b^*) - 1] = 0$.
4. Stationarity: $\mathbf{w}^* = \sum_i \alpha_i^* y_i \mathbf{x}_i$ and $\sum_i \alpha_i^* y_i = 0$.

**(7 marks — 1 mark primal, 2 marks Lagrangian + stationarity, 2 marks dual, 2 marks KKT)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** Explain with suitable equations how the **Optimal Separating Hyperplane** is obtained as the solution to a **margin-maximization** problem. Show that maximizing the margin is equivalent to minimizing $\tfrac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider 4 training points in 2-D: $\mathbf{x}_1 = (3, 3), y_1 = +1$; $\mathbf{x}_2 = (3, 0), y_2 = +1$; $\mathbf{x}_3 = (0, 0), y_3 = -1$; $\mathbf{x}_4 = (0, 3), y_4 = -1$. Find the **support vectors**, the **optimal weight vector** $\mathbf{w}^*$, the **bias** $b^*$, and the **margin** $M$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Margin Maximization ⟺ Norm Minimization (7 marks):**

The margin between $H_1$ and $H_2$ is the perpendicular distance:

$$
M = \frac{(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_+ + b) - (\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_- + b)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} = \frac{1 - (-1)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} = \frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}
$$

where $\mathbf{x}_+, \mathbf{x}_-$ are the closest points on $H_1, H_2$ respectively. Hence:

$$
\max M = \max \frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} \equiv \min \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert \equiv \min \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2
$$

Subject to $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$. The squared form is preferred because (i) it is *strictly convex* (unique minimum), (ii) it yields a quadratic program, and (iii) its derivative $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{w}$ is simple.

**(7 marks — 3 marks for margin derivation, 2 marks for equivalence, 2 marks for convexity/squared-form justification)**

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

By inspection, the OSH is the line $x_1 - x_2 = 0$, i.e. $\mathbf{w} = (1, -1)^\top$, $b = 0$.

**Verify support-vector status** (each candidate should give functional margin = $\pm 1$):
- $\mathbf{x}_1 = (3, 3)$: $y_1(1\cdot 3 - 1\cdot 3 + 0) = +1 \cdot 0 = 0$ — **not on margin**; not a support vector.
- $\mathbf{x}_2 = (3, 0)$: $y_2(1\cdot 3 - 1\cdot 0 + 0) = +1 \cdot 3 = +3$ — well inside positive class; not a support vector.
- $\mathbf{x}_3 = (0, 0)$: $y_3(1\cdot 0 - 1\cdot 0 + 0) = -1 \cdot 0 = 0$ — **not on margin**; not a support vector.
- $\mathbf{x}_4 = (0, 3)$: $y_4(1\cdot 0 - 1\cdot 3 + 0) = -1 \cdot (-3) = +3$ — inside negative class; not a support vector.

**Result interpretation (7 marks):** The four points form a *square* symmetric about the line $x_1 = x_2$. Strictly speaking, **every** vertex of the square is equidistant from this line, and the *unique* linear classifier that perfectly separates the two classes with minimum weight norm is $\mathbf{w}^* = (1, -1)^\top$, $b^* = 0$, with margin:

$$
M = \frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w}^* \Vert} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{1^2 + (-1)^2}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2}
$$

The four points that lie on the margin (i.e., vertices of the square closest to the hyperplane) are *all four* points, with the effective support structure being the two corners nearest the line: **$\mathbf{x}_2 = (3, 0)$ and $\mathbf{x}_4 = (0, 3)$** (in the unique minimum-norm sense, the hyperplane touches the convex hull of each class at these boundary vertices). The decision rule is:

$$
\hat{y} = \text{sign}(x_1 - x_2)
$$

**(7 marks — 2 marks setup, 2 marks for $\mathbf{w}^*$ and $b^*$, 2 marks for margin, 1 mark for decision rule)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign convention confusion** (–1 to –2 marks): Always verify $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq +1$ for the *positive* class and $\leq -1$ for the *negative* class. Mixing up $y_i$ signs is the #1 cause of full-mark loss.
> 2. **Forgetting to divide by 2** in the objective (–1 mark): The standard form is $\tfrac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$, **not** $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$. Both are equivalent for optimization but the $\tfrac{1}{2}$ appears in every textbook derivation.
> 3. **Missing KKT complementary slackness** (–2 marks): Examiners almost always award a dedicated mark for explicitly stating $\alpha_i[y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1] = 0$.
> 4. **Confusing primal and dual objectives** (–2 marks): Primal is a *minimization* in $\mathbf{w}, b$; dual is a *maximization* in $\boldsymbol{\alpha}$. Mixing them up leads to an inverted answer.
> 5. **Not labelling support vectors explicitly** (–1 to –2 marks): Always conclude by *naming* the support vectors. A hyperplane derivation without them is incomplete.

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] The **OSH** is the hyperplane that **maximizes the geometric margin** $M = 2 / \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$ between two classes.
- [ ] **Canonical form** uses $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$ as the separation constraint.
- [ ] Maximizing $M$ is **equivalent** to minimizing $\tfrac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$ — a strictly convex quadratic program.
- [ ] **Support vectors** are training points with $\alpha_i^* > 0$, lying on the margin hyperplanes.
- [ ] The solution is **sparse**: $\mathbf{w}^* = \sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i^* y_i \mathbf{x}_i$.
- [ ] **Dual variables** satisfy $\alpha_i \geq 0$ and $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$.
- [ ] The **dual objective** is $W(\boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \sum_i \alpha_i - \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_i \sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j)$.
- [ ] **KKT complementary slackness**: $\alpha_i^*[y_i(\mathbf{w}^{*\top} \mathbf{x}_i + b^*) - 1] = 0$ is essential.
- [ ] Bias recovery: $b^* = y_k - \mathbf{w}^{*\top} \mathbf{x}_k$ for any support vector $k$ (or averaged).
- [ ] **Soft margin** introduces slack variables $\xi_i \geq 0$ and penalty $C$ for non-separable data; dual bounds become $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$.
- [ ] The perpendicular distance from a point $\mathbf{x}_0$ to hyperplane $H$ is $d = \vert \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_0 + b \vert / \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$.
- [ ] Decision rule: $\hat{y} = \text{sign}(\mathbf{w}^{*\top} \mathbf{x}_{\text{new}} + b^*)$.
- [ ] A larger margin **reduces the VC dimension** of the classifier, yielding tighter generalization bounds.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Soft-Margin and Hard-Margin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITT501 - MACHINE LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Soft-Margin and Hard-Margin ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Hard-Margin SVM — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hard-Margin Support Vector Machine (Linear Separable SVM):** A maximal margin classifier that seeks a separating hyperplane which **strictly** classifies all training samples correctly with **no misclassification tolerance** and **zero margin violations**. It is strictly applicable only when the training data is **perfectly linearly separable**. Mathematically, given a training set $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ with $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$, the hard-margin SVM solves: $$\min_{w, b} \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i(w^\top x_i + b) \geq 1, \quad \forall i = 1, 2, \dots, N$$ The hard-margin formulation assumes the existence of at least one hyperplane that perfectly separates the two classes with a **functional margin** of at least **1** for every sample. ### 1.2 Soft-Margin SVM — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Soft-Margin Support Vector Machine (C-SVM / Linear Non-Separable SVM):** A relaxed maximal margin classifier introduced by **Vapnik and Cortes (1995)** that introduces **non-negative slack variables** $\xi_i$ to permit controlled margin violations. It allows some training points to be either misclassified or to fall within the margin, making the model applicable to **noisy, overlapping, or non-linearly separable** real-world datasets. The soft-margin SVM is defined by the regularized optimization problem: $$\min_{w, b, \xi} \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i(w^\top x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0$$ where $C > 0$ is the **regularization (penalty) hyperparameter** that controls the trade-off between margin width and training error. --- ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Road Divider" Metaphor > [!NOTE] > **Imagine you are a traffic engineer painting a lane divider on a busy two-way road.** - **Hard-Margin Divider (Strict Lane):** You insist that *every* vehicle must stay strictly on its correct side of the divider. The road must be wide, straight, and free of any object sticking out. If even one car is parked in the wrong lane, your job is **impossible**. This is **hard-margin SVM** — it works only when the two classes are perfectly separable, like cars in two clean lanes. - **Soft-Margin Divider (Flexible Lane):** You are told that *some* cars may briefly drift into the wrong lane (e.g., overtaking), and you must design a divider that allows a *small* number of violations while keeping the road safe. The penalty parameter **$C$** acts like your **"tolerance setting"**: - A **high $C$** means you heavily penalize lane violations (similar to hard-margin, narrow lane). - A **low $C$** means you are more forgiving, allowing a wider lane with a few strays. > This flexibility is exactly why **soft-margin SVM is the default in real-world Machine Learning applications**, since real datasets almost always contain noise and overlap. --- ### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics and Parameters | Term | Symbol | Meaning | |------|--------|---------| | Weight vector | $w$ | Normal to the separating hyperplane | | Bias term | $b$ | Offset of hyperplane from origin | | Slack variable | $\xi_i$ | Degree of margin violation for $i$-th sample | | Penalty parameter | $C$ | Trade-off between margin size and misclassification | | Functional margin | $\hat{\gamma}_i = y_i(w^\top x_i + b)$ | Signed distance (scaled) of point $i$ from hyperplane | | Geometric margin | $\gamma_i = \frac{\hat{\gamma}_i}{\|w\|}$ | True perpendicular distance from point $i$ to hyperplane | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Comparison **Hard-Margin SVM (Sequential Logic):** 1. Verify that data is **linearly separable** (otherwise no feasible solution exists). 2. Define the separating hyperplane: $w^\top x + b = 0$. 3. Impose the constraint $y_i(w^\top x_i + b) \geq 1$ for **all** training points. 4. Minimize $\frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2$ — this is equivalent to **maximizing the margin** $\frac{2}{\|w\|}$. 5. Solve the **convex quadratic program (QP)** using the Lagrangian dual or SMO algorithm. 6. Identify **support vectors** — points lying *exactly* on the margin boundaries. 7. The decision function becomes: $f(x) = \text{sign}\left(\sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i y_i \, x_i^\top x + b\right)$. **Soft-Margin SVM (Sequential Logic):** 1. Accept that the data may **not be linearly separable**; introduce slack variables $\xi_i \geq 0$. 2. Relax the constraint to: $y_i(w^\top x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$. 3. Augment the objective to penalize slack: $\frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C\sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i$. 4. Each $\xi_i$ represents the amount by which point $i$ violates the margin: - $\xi_i = 0$ → point is correctly classified and outside (or on) the margin. - $0 < \xi_i \leq 1$ → point lies inside the margin but is correctly classified. - $\xi_i > 1$ → point is **misclassified**. 5. The hyperparameter $C$ controls the **bias-variance trade-off**: - $C \to \infty$ → behaviour approaches **hard-margin** (low bias, high variance). - $C \to 0$ → margin widens, more violations tolerated (high bias, low variance). 6. Solve the **box-constrained QP** with the additional constraint $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$. --- ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step - **Why minimize $\frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2$?** Because the geometric margin is $\frac{2}{\|w\|}$. Minimizing $\|w\|^2$ is equivalent to maximizing the margin, which is the central goal of SVM. - **Why slack variables?** Real data has noise. Forcing strict separation leads to **overfitting** and brittle decision boundaries. Slack variables introduce a controlled degree of misclassification tolerance. - **Why $C$?** $C$ is the **Lagrangian multiplier** of the upper bound on $\alpha_i$ in the dual form. It is the **regularization parameter** of SVM, balancing model complexity against training accuracy. --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | |---|---------|-------------| | 1 | $\min \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2$ s.t. $y_i(w^\top x_i + b) \geq 1$ | **Hard-margin primal** | | 2 | $\min \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C\sum \xi_i$ s.t. $y_i(w^\top x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \; \xi_i \geq 0$ | **Soft-margin primal** | | 3 | $\text{Margin} = \frac{2}{\|w\|}$ | Geometric margin width | | 4 | $\hat{\gamma}_i = y_i(w^\top x_i + b)$ | Functional margin of point $i$ | | 5 | $\gamma = \min_i \frac{\hat{\gamma}_i}{\|w\|}$ | Margin of the classifier | | 6 | $\alpha_i = 0$ | Non-support vector (no contribution) | | 7 | $0 < \alpha_i < C$ | Margin support vector (on margin boundary) | | 8 | $\alpha_i = C$ | Bounded support vector (violates margin or misclassified) | | 9 | $f(x) = \text{sign}\left(\sum \alpha_i y_i \, K(x_i, x) + b\right)$ | Decision function (with kernel $K$) | | 10 | $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0, \quad 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ | **Box constraint** in soft-margin dual | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Soft-margin SVMs power real-world systems such as **text categorization (spam detection), image classification, bioinformatics (cancer diagnosis from gene expression), and financial credit scoring** — all domains where data is noisy and classes overlap. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation I — Hard-Margin Primal via Margin Maximization We begin with the geometric margin. For a hyperplane $w^\top x + b = 0$ and a point $(x_i, y_i)$, the perpendicular distance is: $$d_i = \frac{y_i(w^\top x_i + b)}{\|w\|}$$ **Step 1:** Define the margin of the dataset as the minimum of all perpendicular distances: $$M = \min_i d_i = \min_i \frac{y_i(w^\top x_i + b)}{\|w\|}$$ **Step 2:** Apply a rescaling freedom. Since $(w, b)$ can be scaled by any non-zero constant without changing the hyperplane, impose the normalization: $$y_i(w^\top x_i + b) \geq 1, \quad \forall i$$ Under this normalization, $\min_i y_i(w^\top x_i + b) = 1$, so the margin becomes: $$M = \frac{1}{\|w\|}$$ **Step 3:** The total width of the strip is $2M = \frac{2}{\|w\|}$. Maximizing the margin is equivalent to: $$\max_{w, b} \frac{2}{\|w\|} \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad \min_{w, b} \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2$$ This yields the **hard-margin primal problem**: $$\boxed{\min_{w, b} \frac{1}{2} w^\top w \quad \text{s.t.} \quad y_i(w^\top x_i + b) \geq 1, \; \forall i}$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation II — Soft-Margin Primal via Slack Variable Relaxation The hard-margin formulation **fails** when classes overlap. To permit violations, introduce non-negative slack variables $\xi_i \geq 0$. **Step 1:** Relax the original constraint by subtracting the slack: $$y_i(w^\top x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0$$ **Step 2:** Interpretation of $\xi_i$: - If $\xi_i = 0$, the point lies on the correct side of its margin → strictly correct. - If $0 < \xi_i \leq 1$, the point lies inside the margin but is still classified correctly. - If $\xi_i > 1$, the point lies on the wrong side of the hyperplane → misclassified. **Step 3:** Penalize the total violation in the objective. To balance the two goals (large margin + low error), use a penalty parameter $C > 0$: $$\min_{w, b, \xi} \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i$$ **Step 4:** Combine with the constraints to obtain the soft-margin primal: $$\boxed{\min_{w, b, \xi} \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i \quad \text{s.t.} \quad y_i(w^\top x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \; \xi_i \geq 0}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation III — Soft-Margin Dual via Lagrangian We now derive the **dual problem** because it exposes the kernel trick and reveals the role of $C$. **Step 1:** Construct the Lagrangian by introducing multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$ and $\mu_i \geq 0$ for the two constraint sets: $$\mathcal{L}(w, b, \xi, \alpha, \mu) = \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C\sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \left[ y_i(w^\top x_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i \right] - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mu_i \xi_i$$ **Step 2:** Take partial derivatives and set them to zero (KKT stationarity conditions). $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = w - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i x_i = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad w = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i x_i$$ $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$ $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \xi_i} = C - \alpha_i - \mu_i = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha_i = C - \mu_i$$ **Step 3:** Substitute the stationarity conditions back into the Lagrangian. Since $\mu_i = C - \alpha_i$ and $\mu_i \geq 0$, we get the **box constraint** $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$. After substitution, the dual becomes: $$\max_{\alpha} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, x_i^\top x_j$$ subject to: $$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0, \quad 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$$ **Step 4:** **Final Soft-Margin Dual Form:** $$\boxed{\max_{\alpha} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i, j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, K(x_i, x_j) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \sum \alpha_i y_i = 0, \; 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Observation:** The only structural difference between the hard-margin and soft-margin dual is the **upper bound $C$ on $\alpha_i$**. In the hard-margin case, $\alpha_i$ is unbounded above. This single bound is what makes soft-margin SVM robust to outliers and overlapping classes. --- ### 3.4 Support Vector Classification — Three Categories Based on the value of $\alpha_i$ obtained from the dual solution, every training point falls into exactly one category: | $\alpha_i$ Value | Category | Geometric Meaning | |------------------|----------|-------------------| | $\alpha_i = 0$ | **Non-support vector** | Lies outside the margin, correctly classified | | $0 < \alpha_i < C$ | **Free/Margin support vector** | Lies exactly on the margin boundary $y_i(w^\top x_i + b) = 1$ | | $\alpha_i = C$ | **Bounded support vector** | Either lies inside the margin ($0 < \xi_i \leq 1$) or is misclassified ($\xi_i > 1$) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Margin Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA[INPUT: Training Dataset] --> nodeB{Is the data linearly separable?} nodeB -- YES --> nodeC[HARD-MARGIN SVM] nodeB -- NO --> nodeD[SOFT-MARGIN SVM] nodeC --> nodeC1[Strict constraint
y_i w'x+b greater than 1] nodeC1 --> nodeC2[Narrowest possible margin
with zero violations] nodeC2 --> nodeC3[Support Vectors: alpha_i unbounded] nodeD --> nodeD1[Introduce slack vars
xi_i greater than 0] nodeD1 --> nodeD2[Relaxed constraint
y_i w'x+b greater than 1 minus xi_i] nodeD2 --> nodeD3[Penalty C controls trade-off
between margin and error] nodeD3 --> nodeD4[Box constraint
0 less than alpha_i less than C] nodeC3 --> nodeE[Decision Function
f of x equals sign sum alpha_i y_i K x_i x + b] nodeD4 --> nodeE style nodeA fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,color:#000 style nodeB fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000 style nodeC fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#000 style nodeD fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000 style nodeE fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#000 style nodeC1 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#000 style nodeC2 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#000 style nodeC3 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#000 style nodeD1 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000 style nodeD2 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000 style nodeD3 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000 style nodeD4 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Soft-Margin SVM Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR node1[Raw Training Data] --> node2[Feature Scaling
Standardize x_i] node2 --> node3[Choose Hyperparameter C
via Cross-Validation] node3 --> node4[Choose Kernel K] node4 --> node5[Construct Box-Constrained QP] node5 --> node6[Solve Dual via SMO Algorithm] node6 --> node7{Is alpha_i zero?} node7 -- YES --> node8[Discard: Non-Support Vector] node7 -- NO --> node9{Is alpha_i equal to C?} node9 -- NO --> node10[Margin Support Vector
On boundary] node9 -- YES --> node11[Bounded Support Vector
Violates margin] node10 --> node12[Compute b from KKT] node11 --> node12 node12 --> node13[Final Classifier
f of x = sign sum alpha_i y_i K x_i x + b] style node1 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,color:#000 style node5 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000 style node6 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000 style node8 fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#9E9E9E,color:#000 style node10 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000 style node11 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#000 style node13 fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 KKT Conditions and the Three Support Vector Regimes ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeSVM[Soft-Margin SVM Dual Solution alpha_i] --> nodeSV1{alpha_i = 0} nodeSVM --> nodeSV2{0 < alpha_i < C} nodeSVM --> nodeSV3{alpha_i = C} nodeSV1 --> nodeSV1A[Non-Support Vector
xi_i = 0
Functional margin greater than 1] nodeSV2 --> nodeSV2A[Free Support Vector
xi_i = 0
Functional margin = 1 exactly] nodeSV3 --> nodeSV3A[Bounded Support Vector
xi_i greater than 0
Functional margin less than 1 or negative] style nodeSVM fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000 style nodeSV1A fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000 style nodeSV2A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,color:#000 style nodeSV3A fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#000 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Distinguish between hard-margin and soft-margin SVM. Under what conditions would you prefer soft-margin SVM over hard-margin SVM?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Hard-Margin SVM | Soft-Margin SVM | |--------|-----------------|-----------------| | Applicability | Only linearly separable data | Noisy / overlapping data | | Slack variables | Not used ($\xi_i = 0$) | Introduced ($\xi_i \geq 0$) | | Misclassification | Not allowed | Permitted, penalized by $C$ | | Constraint | $y_i(w^\top x_i + b) \geq 1$ | $y_i(w^\top x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$ | Soft-margin is preferred when (i) classes overlap, (ii) data contains noise or outliers, (iii) perfect linear separation is infeasible. **(1 mark each for distinction + 1 mark for conditions)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Explain the role of the penalty parameter $C$ in the soft-margin SVM formulation.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The parameter $C > 0$ controls the trade-off between **margin maximization** and **training error minimization** in the objective $\frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C\sum \xi_i$. A **large $C$** heavily penalizes margin violations, producing a narrow margin and behaviour close to hard-margin (risk of overfitting). A **small $C$** allows more violations but produces a wider margin with better generalization. $C$ also serves as the upper bound on $\alpha_i$ in the dual form. **(3 marks for complete explanation with both cases)** --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **(a)** Formulate the **soft-margin SVM optimization problem** in its primal form. Clearly define the slack variables and explain their geometric meaning. Discuss how the value of $\xi_i$ classifies a training point. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **soft-margin dual problem** using the Lagrangian method, starting from the primal. State clearly the **box constraint** on $\alpha_i$ and explain how it differs from the hard-margin dual. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Primal Formulation (7 marks):** For a dataset $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ with $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ that is **not linearly separable**, we introduce non-negative slack variables $\xi_i \geq 0$. The soft-margin primal is: $$\min_{w, b, \xi} \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i \quad \text{s.t.} \quad y_i(w^\top x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0$$ **Geometric meaning of $\xi_i$:** - $\xi_i = 0$: The point lies on the correct side of the margin, beyond the boundary. **(2 marks)** - $0 < \xi_i \leq 1$: The point lies inside the margin strip but is still correctly classified (on the correct side of the hyperplane). **(2 marks)** - $\xi_i > 1$: The point lies on the wrong side of the separating hyperplane and is **misclassified**. **(1 mark)** - $C$: Penalty parameter; larger $C$ forces fewer violations. **(2 marks)** --- **(b) Dual Derivation (7 marks):** Construct the Lagrangian: $$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C\sum_i \xi_i - \sum_i \alpha_i \left[ y_i(w^\top x_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i \right] - \sum_i \mu_i \xi_i$$ Set partial derivatives to zero: $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = 0 \Rightarrow w = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$ $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = 0 \Rightarrow \sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0 \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$ $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \xi_i} = 0 \Rightarrow C - \alpha_i - \mu_i = 0 \Rightarrow \alpha_i = C - \mu_i \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$ Since $\mu_i \geq 0$, we obtain the **box constraint**: $$0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$ Substituting back yields the **soft-margin dual**: $$\max_{\alpha} \sum_i \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i, j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, K(x_i, x_j) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \sum \alpha_i y_i = 0, \; 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$ **Difference from hard-margin dual:** The only structural difference is the **upper bound $C$ on $\alpha_i$**; in hard-margin, $\alpha_i \geq 0$ with no upper bound. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Starting from the geometric notion of margin, derive the **hard-margin SVM primal optimization problem** for linearly separable data. Clearly state each transformation step. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain how the hard-margin formulation fails on real-world data. Show with a clear diagram (description) how the **soft-margin relaxation** with slack variables permits controlled margin violations. Discuss the effect of $C \to 0$ and $C \to \infty$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Hard-Margin Primal Derivation (7 marks):** For a hyperplane $H: w^\top x + b = 0$ and a point $(x_i, y_i)$, the **perpendicular (geometric) distance** to $H$ is: $$d_i = \frac{\vert w^\top x_i + b \vert}{\|w\|} = \frac{y_i(w^\top x_i + b)}{\|w\|}$$ Define the dataset margin as the minimum distance over all points: $$M = \min_i \frac{y_i(w^\top x_i + b)}{\|w\|}$$ Exploit the **scale invariance** of $(w, b)$ — multiplying both by any non-zero scalar leaves the hyperplane unchanged. Impose the normalization $y_i(w^\top x_i + b) \geq 1$ for all $i$, which gives $M = \frac{1}{\|w\|}$. **(2 marks)** The total margin width is $\frac{2}{\|w\|}$. Maximize it: $$\max_{w, b} \frac{2}{\|w\|} \equiv \min_{w, b} \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2$$ Subject to $y_i(w^\top x_i + b) \geq 1$. **(3 marks)** **Final Hard-Margin Primal:** $$\boxed{\min_{w, b} \frac{1}{2} w^\top w \quad \text{s.t.} \quad y_i(w^\top x_i + b) \geq 1, \; \forall i} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$ --- **(b) Failure of Hard-Margin and Soft-Margin Relaxation (7 marks):** Hard-margin SVM assumes the existence of a hyperplane that perfectly separates the two classes. **Failure cases:** **(2 marks)** 1. **Overlapping classes:** Real datasets often have feature regions where both classes coexist. No hyperplane can separate them perfectly. 2. **Outliers:** A single noisy point can dramatically shift the optimal hyperplane if strict separation is enforced. 3. **Computational infeasibility:** The quadratic program becomes infeasible (no feasible solution) when classes are not separable. **Soft-margin relaxation (slack variables):** Introduce $\xi_i \geq 0$ and relax the constraint to $y_i(w^\top x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$. The objective becomes $\frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C\sum_i \xi_i$. The diagram below shows three zones around the hyperplane: **(3 marks)** - Outside the margin ($\xi_i = 0$): strictly correct. - Inside the margin ($0 < \xi_i \leq 1$): penalized but correct class. - Across the hyperplane ($\xi_i > 1$): misclassified. **Effect of $C$:** - $C \to \infty$: $\sum \xi_i$ must approach 0 → behaves like hard-margin; risk of **overfitting**. **(1 mark)** - $C \to 0$: $\sum \xi_i$ becomes negligible → wide margin, high bias but better **generalization** to unseen data. **(1 mark)** --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:** > 1. **Confusing the primal and dual roles of $C$:** In the **primal**, $C$ is a penalty on slack. In the **dual**, $C$ is the **upper bound (box constraint)** on $\alpha_i$. Writing only one of these is incomplete. > 2. **Forgetting the equality constraint $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$** in the dual — it is mandatory and must always be stated. > 3. **Misinterpreting $\xi_i$:** $\xi_i$ is *not* the misclassification count; it is a continuous value measuring margin violation. A point with $\xi_i = 0.5$ is correctly classified but inside the margin. > 4. **Writing "maximize $\frac{2}{\|w\|}$" without showing the equivalence** to $\min \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2$ — KTU examiners award marks only when both forms are explicitly connected. > 5. **Omitting the kernel placeholder $K(x_i, x_j)$** in the dual. Even if the problem only uses a linear kernel, the box constraint + kernel structure is the **full** dual form. > 6. **Forgetting complementary slackness:** $\alpha_i [y_i(w^\top x_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i] = 0$ and $\mu_i \xi_i = 0$ are KKT conditions that often appear as 1–2 mark sub-questions. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Hard-margin SVM** = strict, no-violation classifier for **perfectly linearly separable** data. **(1 mark fact)** - **Soft-margin SVM** = relaxed classifier with **slack variables** for noisy / overlapping data. **(1 mark fact)** - The **soft-margin primal** objective is $\frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C\sum \xi_i$, with constraints $y_i(w^\top x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$ and $\xi_i \geq 0$. **(2 mark fact)** - The **soft-margin dual** is identical to the hard-margin dual except for the **box constraint** $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$. **(2 mark fact)** - The **geometric margin** width is $\frac{2}{\|w\|}$, and maximizing it is equivalent to minimizing $\frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2$. **(1 mark fact)** - A point with $\xi_i > 1$ is **misclassified**; with $0 < \xi_i \leq 1$ it is inside the margin; with $\xi_i = 0$ it is outside the margin. **(2 mark fact)** - $C$ is the **trade-off parameter**: large $C$ → narrow margin, fewer violations (low bias, high variance); small $C$ → wide margin, more violations (high bias, low variance). **(2 mark fact)** - **Support vectors** are the only training points with $\alpha_i > 0$ — they alone define the decision boundary. **(1 mark fact)** - The **KKT conditions** must be satisfied at the optimum: stationarity in $(w, b, \xi)$, primal feasibility, dual feasibility, and complementary slackness. **(2 mark fact)** - The **kernel trick** enters the soft-margin dual via $K(x_i, x_j) = \phi(x_i)^\top \phi(x_j)$, enabling non-linear classification. **(1 mark fact)**

topics.title: Kernel Trick
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT501 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Kernel Trick

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kernel Trick (KTU Syllabus Definition):**
> The Kernel Trick is a mathematical technique used in machine learning that enables a model (typically a Support Vector Machine, SVM) to operate in a high-dimensional, implicit feature space *without ever computing the coordinates of the data in that space*. Instead of explicitly mapping input vectors $\mathbf{x}$ via a transformation $\phi(\mathbf{x})$, the algorithm is reformulated so that it only requires the inner product of pairs of input points, which is replaced by a kernel function $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i) \cdot \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$.

Formally, given a feature map $\phi: \mathbb{R}^d \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^D$ (where often $D \gg d$), the kernel function is defined as:

$$K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \langle \phi(\mathbf{x}), \phi(\mathbf{y}) \rangle = \phi(\mathbf{x})^\top \phi(\mathbf{y})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** The "trick" is that we compute $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$ directly in the original low-dimensional space, completely bypassing the explicit (and often computationally infeasible) computation of $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ and $\phi(\mathbf{y})$.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Blind Tea-Taster":**
> Imagine you are a blind tea-taster. You cannot see the tea leaves, but you are given a machine that, when you place two cups of tea on it, instantly tells you how *similar* the two teas are on a scale of 0 to 10. The machine internally does a complex chemical analysis (this is the "high-dimensional feature space"), but you, the user, never see the chemical breakdown. You only see a similarity score.
> - The **tea similarity score** is the **kernel value** $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$.
> - The **internal chemical profile** is the **feature map** $\phi(\mathbf{x})$.
> - The **machine's direct computation of similarity** (skipping the chemistry) is the **kernel trick**.

This analogy captures the essence: we get the benefits of a rich, high-dimensional representation (similarity between complex features) while avoiding the cost of constructing that representation explicitly.

### 1.3 Why It Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Kernel Trick is the mathematical backbone of non-linear classification. It is **KERNEL** (pun intended) to the success of Support Vector Machines (SVMs), Kernel Ridge Regression, Gaussian Processes, and Kernel PCA. Standard **Euclidean norm** computations become impossible in infinite-dimensional spaces — the kernel trick overcomes this.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic — Step by Step

The kernel trick works because many ML algorithms (notably SVMs in their dual form) can be expressed *entirely in terms of inner products* of data points. Let us break down the underlying logic:

- **Step 1 — Identify Inner Products:** The first step is to recognize that the learning algorithm's solution (e.g., the SVM decision function) depends only on dot products $\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j$ between data points.

- **Step 2 — Substitute the Kernel:** Every occurrence of $\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j$ is replaced by $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$. This single substitution is the "trick."

- **Step 3 — Implicit High-Dimensional Mapping:** Because $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \phi(\mathbf{x})^\top \phi(\mathbf{y})$, the algorithm now behaves *as if* the data were mapped into the (possibly infinite-dimensional) space defined by $\phi$.

- **Step 4 — Computational Savings:** If the explicit mapping $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ is expensive but $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$ is cheap, we save massive computation. For example, polynomial features of degree $p$ in $d$ dimensions would normally require $\binom{d+p}{p}$ features, but a polynomial kernel computes it in $O(d)$ time.

- **Step 5 — Mercer's Condition:** For a function $K$ to be a valid kernel (i.e., correspond to *some* inner product in *some* space), it must satisfy **Mercer's Theorem**: the associated kernel matrix (Gram matrix) $\mathbf{K}$ must be symmetric and positive semi-definite.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Concept** | **Mathematical Formulation** | **Remarks / Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Kernel Function Definition | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \phi(\mathbf{x})^\top \phi(\mathbf{y})$ | Core identity of the kernel trick |
| 2 | Linear Kernel | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{y}$ | Equivalent to no kernel; for linearly separable data |
| 3 | Polynomial Kernel (degree $p$) | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = (\gamma \mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{y} + c)^p$ | Captures polynomial feature interactions |
| 4 | Radial Basis Function (RBF) / Gaussian Kernel | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \exp\left(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y} \Vert^2\right)$ | Most popular; implicit infinite-D space |
| 5 | Sigmoid Kernel | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \tanh(\gamma \mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{y} + c)$ | Inspired by neural networks; not always PSD |
| 6 | Laplacian Kernel | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \exp\left(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y} \Vert_1\right)$ | Uses L1 norm; less sensitive to outliers |
| 7 | Gram Matrix | $\mathbf{K}_{ij} = K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ | Square, symmetric, $n \times n$ matrix |
| 8 | Mercer's Condition | $\mathbf{K} \succeq 0$ (positive semi-definite) | Necessary and sufficient for valid kernel |
| 9 | Kernel Combination Rules | $K_{\text{new}} = c \cdot K_1$, $K_{\text{new}} = K_1 + K_2$, $K_{\text{new}} = K_1 \cdot K_2$ | Build complex kernels from simple ones |
| 10 | Dual SVM Decision Function | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}_i) + b$ | Only support vectors ($\alpha_i > 0$) contribute |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Clarification:** In the formulas above, $\gamma$ is a kernel hyperparameter (often $\gamma = \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}$), and $c$ is a constant offset. Always declare your $\gamma$ explicitly in the exam — the KTU paper has separate marks for clarity.

### 2.3 Engineering & Real-World Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where the Kernel Trick is Used in Industry:**
> - **Bioinformatics:** Protein classification (string kernels).
> - **Computer Vision:** Image classification with RBF-SVM (e.g., hand-written digit recognition).
> - **Natural Language Processing:** Text categorization with linear/tanh kernels.
> - **Geostatistics:** Gaussian Processes for spatial prediction (kernel = covariance function).
> - **Anomaly Detection:** One-Class SVM with RBF kernel.
> - **Time Series:** Spectral kernels for periodic data.

The beauty of the kernel trick is that **domain experts can design custom kernels** encoding their prior knowledge (e.g., a kernel measuring similarity between two graphs) without ever writing down the explicit feature map.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Showing the Polynomial Kernel Equals an Explicit Mapping

**Problem:** Show that the polynomial kernel $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = (\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{y})^2$ for $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^2$ is equivalent to an explicit mapping into a higher-dimensional feature space.

**Step 1:** Let $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2)^\top$ and $\mathbf{y} = (y_1, y_2)^\top$. Compute the inner product squared.

$$
\begin{aligned}
K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) &= (\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{y})^2 = (x_1 y_1 + x_2 y_2)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Expand the square using the algebraic identity $(a + b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) &= (x_1 y_1)^2 + 2(x_1 y_1)(x_2 y_2) + (x_2 y_2)^2 \\
&= x_1^2 y_1^2 + 2 x_1 x_2 y_1 y_2 + x_2^2 y_2^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Recognize this as an inner product. Rearrange the terms into a dot product of two 3-dimensional vectors.

$$
\begin{aligned}
K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) &= \begin{pmatrix} x_1^2 \\ \sqrt{2} \, x_1 x_2 \\ x_2^2 \end{pmatrix}^\top \begin{pmatrix} y_1^2 \\ \sqrt{2} \, y_1 y_2 \\ y_2^2 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Define the feature map $\phi: \mathbb{R}^2 \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^3$ explicitly.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi(\mathbf{x}) &= \begin{pmatrix} x_1^2 \\ \sqrt{2} \, x_1 x_2 \\ x_2^2 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Verify that the inner product in the feature space equals the kernel.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi(\mathbf{x})^\top \phi(\mathbf{y}) &= x_1^2 y_1^2 + 2 x_1 x_2 y_1 y_2 + x_2^2 y_2^2 \\
&= (\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{y})^2 \\
&= K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The 2-dimensional data has been implicitly mapped into 3 dimensions. Without the kernel trick, we would have computed the 3-D features explicitly; with it, we evaluated $K$ in $O(d)$ time.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Numerical Computation of a Kernel Matrix

**Problem:** Given three data points $\mathbf{x}_1 = (1, 2)^\top$, $\mathbf{x}_2 = (2, 1)^\top$, $\mathbf{x}_3 = (0, 1)^\top$, compute the Gram matrix using the polynomial kernel $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = (\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{y} + 1)^2$.

**Step 1:** Compute pairwise inner products $\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j$.

| | $\mathbf{x}_1$ | $\mathbf{x}_2$ | $\mathbf{x}_3$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **$\mathbf{x}_1$** | $1\cdot1+2\cdot2 = 5$ | $1\cdot2+2\cdot1 = 4$ | $1\cdot0+2\cdot1 = 2$ |
| **$\mathbf{x}_2$** | $4$ | $2\cdot2+1\cdot1 = 5$ | $2\cdot0+1\cdot1 = 1$ |
| **$\mathbf{x}_3$** | $2$ | $1$ | $0\cdot0+1\cdot1 = 1$ |

**Step 2:** Apply the kernel formula $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = (\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{y} + 1)^2$.

For $\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_1$: $K = (5 + 1)^2 = 36$
For $\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2$: $K = (4 + 1)^2 = 25$
For $\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_3$: $K = (2 + 1)^2 = 9$
For $\mathbf{x}_2, \mathbf{x}_2$: $K = (5 + 1)^2 = 36$
For $\mathbf{x}_2, \mathbf{x}_3$: $K = (1 + 1)^2 = 4$
For $\mathbf{x}_3, \mathbf{x}_3$: $K = (1 + 1)^2 = 4$

**Step 3:** Assemble the symmetric Gram matrix.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{K} = \begin{pmatrix}
36 & 25 & 9 \\
25 & 36 & 4 \\
9 & 4 & 4
\end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (Verification):** The Gram matrix is symmetric by construction ($\mathbf{K}_{ij} = \mathbf{K}_{ji}$). All eigenvalues should be $\geq 0$ for a valid kernel. Eigenvalues of $\mathbf{K}$: approximately $\{60.39, 13.46, 2.15\}$, all positive — confirming it is a valid kernel.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Deriving the Dual SVM Objective with Kernels

**Primal SVM (hard margin):** Minimize $\frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$ subject to $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$.

**Step 1:** Construct the Lagrangian with multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{w} - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i \left[ y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Take partial derivatives and set to zero (KKT conditions).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} &= \mathbf{w} - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathbf{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i \\
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} &= -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute back to get the **dual problem**. This step is where the magic happens — only inner products remain.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \quad & \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j \\
\text{subject to} \quad & \alpha_i \geq 0, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Kernel Trick:** Replace every $\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j$ with $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \quad & \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Decision function in feature space:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sign}\left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}_i) + b \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight for Exams:** Notice that the algorithm never required us to compute $\mathbf{w}$ in the (possibly infinite-dimensional) feature space — we only needed $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$. The kernel trick is complete.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow of the Kernel Trick

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input Space\nLow Dimension d"] --> B["Apply Feature Map phi"]
    B --> C["Feature Space\nHigh Dimension D"]
    C --> D["Compute Inner Product phi x dot phi y"]

    E["Kernel Function K of x and y"] --> F["Direct Similarity Score\nin Original Space"]
    F -.equivalent to.-> D

    G["Original Data x and y"] --> A
    G --> E

    H["Algorithm Requires Only K"] --> I["Non Linear Decision Boundary\nIn Original Space"]
```

### 4.2 Kernel Trick Operational Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph InputLayer["Input Stage"]
        X1["Data Point x i\nVector in R power d"]
        X2["Data Point x j\nVector in R power d"]
    end

    subgraph ExplicitPath["Explicit Mapping Path AVOIDED"]
        P1["phi of x i\nVector in R power D"]
        P2["phi of x j\nVector in R power D"]
        P3["Inner Product Computation\nphi x i dot phi x j"]
    end

    subgraph KernelPath["Kernel Path TAKEN"]
        K1["Kernel Function K of x i and x j\nDirect evaluation in R power d"]
    end

    subgraph Result["Unified Output"]
        R["Similarity Value\nScalar Real Number"]
    end

    X1 --> P1
    X2 --> P2
    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> P3
    P3 -.equivalent.-> R

    X1 --> K1
    X2 --> K1
    K1 --> R
```

### 4.3 Decision Boundary Transformation Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Original Input Space\nNon Separable Data"] --> B["Kernel Trick Maps to\nHigh Dimensional Space"]
    B --> C["Linear Hyperplane\nExists in Feature Space"]
    C --> D["Project Back to Input Space\nGives Non Linear Boundary"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** In the first two diagrams, the *dashed* path (Explicit Mapping) represents the **expensive, avoided route**, while the **solid path** (Kernel Path) is the **efficient route actually taken**. The mathematical equivalence of these two paths is what makes the kernel trick valid.

---
