topics.title: Activation Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT501 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Activation Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Activation Function** in an artificial neural network is a mathematical transformation applied to the *pre-activation* (i.e., the weighted sum of inputs plus the bias) of a neuron, which determines the neuron's output signal. Mathematically, for a neuron receiving inputs $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ with corresponding weights $w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n$ and bias $b$, the activation function $\phi$ operates as:

$$y = \phi\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b\right)$$

where $y$ is the output transmitted to the next layer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The primary purpose of an activation function is to introduce **non-linearity** into the network. Without non-linearity, stacking multiple layers of neurons would be mathematically equivalent to a single linear transformation, regardless of the depth of the network. This is formally known as the **Universal Approximation Theorem**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of an activation function as a **"filter or gatekeeper"** at a security checkpoint in a corporate office building.

- **Inputs** ($x_i$) = Employees arriving at different gates.
- **Weights** ($w_i$) = Importance level of each gate (some gates are busier and more critical).
- **Weighted Sum + Bias** = Total security assessment score of the person.
- **Activation Function** ($\phi$) = The security officer's decision rule:
  - *"If the score is high enough, allow the person to pass (fire the signal)."*
  - *"If the score is too low, block them (suppress the signal)."*
  - *"Sometimes, scale the signal (compress it between 0 and 1, like a probability)."*

Just as a security officer decides **whether and how strongly** to allow a person into the building, an activation function decides **whether and how strongly** a neuron should propagate information forward. Crucially, the officer uses a *non-linear rule* (e.g., "only let in senior staff OR those with valid IDs") — not a simple proportional rule — to handle complex scenarios. This non-linearity is what allows the network to learn complex, real-world patterns.

## 1.3 Why Are Activation Functions Needed?

A multi-layer neural network without activation functions is equivalent to a single linear layer. To see this, consider two stacked layers:

$$y = W_2(W_1 x + b_1) + b_2 = (W_2 W_1)x + (W_2 b_1 + b_2)$$

The composite is still a **linear** function of $x$. Hence, we cannot model non-linear relationships (e.g., XOR, curves, complex decision boundaries) without non-linear activations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note:** The earliest activation function was the **binary step function** (McCulloch-Pitts neuron, 1943). Modern deep learning relies on smoother, differentiable functions like ReLU, Sigmoid, and Tanh because gradient-based optimization (backpropagation) requires differentiability.

## 1.4 Desirable Properties of Activation Functions

| Property | Why It Matters |
|----------|----------------|
| **Non-linearity** | Enables learning of complex patterns |
| **Differentiability** | Required for backpropagation (gradient descent) |
| **Monotonicity** | Ensures consistent gradient direction |
| **Computational Efficiency** | Fast forward and backward passes |
| **Zero-centered output (preferred)** | Faster convergence in gradient descent |
| **Bounded range (sometimes preferred)** | Provides stable, predictable outputs |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Major Activation Functions

### 2.1.1 Linear (Identity) Activation
- **Definition:** $\phi(x) = x$
- **Range:** $(-\infty, +\infty)$
- **Use Case:** Output layer of regression problems.
- **Drawback:** No non-linearity — defeats the purpose of a multi-layer network.

### 2.1.2 Binary Step Activation
- **Definition:**
  $$\phi(x) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } x \geq 0 \\ 0 & \text{if } x < 0 \end{cases}$$
- **Range:** $\{0, 1\}$
- **Use Case:** Theoretical models (McCulloch-Pitts neuron), binary classifiers in simplified examples.
- **Drawback:** Not differentiable at $x=0$; gradient is zero elsewhere — cannot be used with gradient descent.

### 2.1.3 Sigmoid (Logistic) Activation
- **Definition:** $\phi(x) = \sigma(x) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}$
- **Range:** $(0, 1)$
- **Use Case:** Output layer of binary classification (interprets output as probability).
- **Derivative:** $\phi'(x) = \phi(x)\left(1 - \phi(x)\right)$
- **Drawback:** Suffers from the **Vanishing Gradient Problem** for large positive or negative inputs (gradients approach 0).

### 2.1.4 Hyperbolic Tangent (Tanh) Activation
- **Definition:** $\phi(x) = \tanh(x) = \dfrac{e^x - e^{-x}}{e^x + e^{-x}}$
- **Range:** $(-1, 1)$
- **Use Case:** Hidden layers (preferred over sigmoid because it is zero-centered).
- **Derivative:** $\phi'(x) = 1 - \tanh^2(x) = 1 - \phi^2(x)$
- **Drawback:** Still suffers from vanishing gradient at extremes.

### 2.1.5 ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit) Activation
- **Definition:**
  $$\phi(x) = \max(0, x) = \begin{cases} x & \text{if } x > 0 \\ 0 & \text{if } x \leq 0 \end{cases}$$
- **Range:** $[0, +\infty)$
- **Use Case:** Default choice for hidden layers in modern deep networks (CNNs, MLPs).
- **Derivative:**
  $$\phi'(x) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } x > 0 \\ 0 & \text{if } x \leq 0 \end{cases}$$
- **Advantage:** Computationally cheap; mitigates vanishing gradient for positive inputs.
- **Drawback:** **Dying ReLU problem** — neurons can permanently output 0 if they get stuck in the negative region.

### 2.1.6 Leaky ReLU Activation
- **Definition:**
  $$\phi(x) = \begin{cases} x & \text{if } x > 0 \\ \alpha x & \text{if } x \leq 0 \end{cases}$$
  where $\alpha$ is a small positive constant (typically $\alpha = 0.01$).
- **Range:** $(-\infty, +\infty)$
- **Derivative:** $\phi'(x) = 1$ if $x>0$, else $\alpha$.
- **Advantage:** Solves the dying ReLU problem by allowing a small negative slope.

### 2.1.7 Parametric ReLU (PReLU)
- Same as Leaky ReLU, but $\alpha$ is a **learnable parameter** trained via backpropagation.

### 2.1.8 Softmax Activation
- **Definition:** For a vector of $K$ pre-activations $z = (z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_K)$:
  $$\phi(z_i) = \sigma(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$$
- **Range:** $(0, 1)$ for each component; sum of all components equals $1$.
- **Use Case:** Output layer of **multi-class classification** — produces a probability distribution over $K$ classes.
- **Derivative (Jacobian):**
  $$\frac{\partial \sigma(z_i)}{\partial z_i} = \sigma(z_i)\bigl(1 - \sigma(z_i)\bigr), \quad \frac{\partial \sigma(z_i)}{\partial z_j} = -\sigma(z_i)\sigma(z_j) \;\; (i \neq j)$$

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is your **exam-ready cheat sheet**. Memorize these formulas and their ranges for direct questions.

| # | Activation Function | Formula $\phi(x)$ | Derivative $\phi'(x)$ | Range | Output Layer Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear | $x$ | $1$ | $(-\infty, \infty)$ | Regression |
| 2 | Binary Step | $1$ if $x \geq 0$, else $0$ | $0$ (undefined at 0) | $\{0, 1\}$ | Theoretical models |
| 3 | Sigmoid | $\dfrac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$ | $\phi(x)\bigl(1-\phi(x)\bigr)$ | $(0, 1)$ | Binary classification |
| 4 | Tanh | $\dfrac{e^x - e^{-x}}{e^x + e^{-x}}$ | $1 - \phi^2(x)$ | $(-1, 1)$ | Hidden layers (preferred) |
| 5 | ReLU | $\max(0, x)$ | $1$ if $x>0$, else $0$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Default hidden layer |
| 6 | Leaky ReLU | $x$ if $x>0$, else $\alpha x$ | $1$ if $x>0$, else $\alpha$ | $(-\infty, \infty)$ | Hidden layers (avoid dying ReLU) |
| 7 | PReLU | $x$ if $x>0$, else $\alpha x$ ($\alpha$ learnable) | $1$ if $x>0$, else $\alpha$ | $(-\infty, \infty)$ | Advanced deep nets |
| 8 | Softmax | $\dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | $\sigma(z_i)\bigl(1-\sigma(z_i)\bigr)$ for $i=j$ | $(0, 1)$ for each, $\sum=1$ | Multi-class classification |
| 9 | Swish | $x \cdot \sigma(x)$ | $\sigma(x) + x\,\sigma(x)\bigl(1-\sigma(x)\bigr)$ | $(\approx -0.28, \infty)$ | Deep networks (e.g., Transformers) |
| 10 | GELU | $0.5 x\bigl(1 + \tanh\bigl(\sqrt{2/\pi}(x + 0.044715 x^3)\bigr)\bigr)$ | (approximate form) | $(-1, \infty)$ | Transformers / NLP |

## 2.3 Why These Functions Matter in Engineering

- **Image Classification (CNNs):** ReLU in hidden layers + Softmax in output layer.
- **Speech Recognition (RNNs/LSTMs):** Tanh in recurrent gates; Sigmoid for output gates.
- **Medical Diagnosis (Binary Decisions):** Sigmoid outputs the probability of a disease.
- **Autonomous Driving (Regression Tasks):** Linear activation in the final layer to predict continuous steering angles.
- **Natural Language Processing (Transformers):** GELU and Swish are now standard.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT use ReLU in the output layer of a classification task. Its unbounded positive range is not a probability distribution. Use Softmax for multi-class and Sigmoid for binary.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** Every step below is shown explicitly. The **most heavily tested** derivatives in KTU exams are those of **Sigmoid**, **Tanh**, and **ReLU**. Master these three thoroughly.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Derivative of the Sigmoid Activation Function

The sigmoid function is:
$$\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}$$

**Step 1 — Rewrite in a more convenient form:**
Using the negative exponent property $e^{-x} = 1/e^x$, we can rewrite:
$$\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-x}} = \frac{e^x}{e^x + 1}$$

Both forms are equivalent. We will use the first form for the derivation.

**Step 2 — Apply the chain rule with substitution:**
Let $u = 1 + e^{-x}$. Then:
$$\sigma(x) = u^{-1}$$

Differentiating using the power rule and chain rule:
$$\frac{d\sigma}{dx} = -u^{-2} \cdot \frac{du}{dx}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\frac{du}{dx}$:**
$$\frac{du}{dx} = \frac{d}{dx}\bigl(1 + e^{-x}\bigr) = 0 + (-e^{-x}) = -e^{-x}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back:**
$$\frac{d\sigma}{dx} = -u^{-2} \cdot (-e^{-x}) = \frac{e^{-x}}{u^2} = \frac{e^{-x}}{(1 + e^{-x})^2}$$

**Step 5 — Express in terms of $\sigma(x)$ itself:**
Add and subtract 1 in the numerator cleverly:
$$\frac{d\sigma}{dx} = \frac{e^{-x}}{(1+e^{-x})^2} = \frac{(1+e^{-x}) - 1}{(1+e^{-x})^2}$$

Split into two fractions:
$$\frac{d\sigma}{dx} = \frac{1+e^{-x}}{(1+e^{-x})^2} - \frac{1}{(1+e^{-x})^2} = \frac{1}{1+e^{-x}} - \left(\frac{1}{1+e^{-x}}\right)^2$$

**Step 6 — Final compact form:**
$$\boxed{\frac{d\sigma}{dx} = \sigma(x)\bigl(1 - \sigma(x)\bigr)}$$

This beautiful identity is why sigmoid is computationally efficient in backpropagation — the derivative is computed from the *output value itself*, requiring no extra memory.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Derivative of the Tanh Activation Function

The hyperbolic tangent is:
$$\tanh(x) = \frac{e^x - e^{-x}}{e^x + e^{-x}}$$

**Step 1 — Apply the quotient rule:**
Let $N = e^x - e^{-x}$ and $D = e^x + e^{-x}$. Then:
$$\frac{d}{dx}\tanh(x) = \frac{N' \cdot D - N \cdot D'}{D^2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the derivatives of numerator and denominator:**
$$N' = \frac{d}{dx}(e^x - e^{-x}) = e^x + e^{-x} = D$$
$$D' = \frac{d}{dx}(e^x + e^{-x}) = e^x - e^{-x} = N$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the quotient rule:**
$$\frac{d}{dx}\tanh(x) = \frac{D \cdot D - N \cdot N}{D^2} = \frac{D^2 - N^2}{D^2} = 1 - \left(\frac{N}{D}\right)^2$$

**Step 4 — Recognize $\frac{N}{D} = \tanh(x)$:**
$$\boxed{\frac{d}{dx}\tanh(x) = 1 - \tanh^2(x)}$$

**Step 5 — Relate to sigmoid (bonus identity):**
Since $\tanh(x) = 2\sigma(2x) - 1$, it can be shown that:
$$\frac{d}{dx}\tanh(x) = 4\,\sigma(2x)\bigl(1 - \sigma(2x)\bigr)$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Derivative of the ReLU Activation Function

ReLU is defined as a piecewise function:
$$f(x) = \max(0, x) = \begin{cases} x & \text{if } x > 0 \\ 0 & \text{if } x \leq 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate each piece separately:**
For $x > 0$: $f(x) = x \Rightarrow f'(x) = 1$
For $x \leq 0$: $f(x) = 0 \Rightarrow f'(x) = 0$

**Step 2 — Combine into a piecewise derivative:**
$$f'(x) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } x > 0 \\ 0 & \text{if } x < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Address the point $x = 0$:**
At $x = 0$, the left-hand derivative is $0$ and the right-hand derivative is $1$. The function is therefore **not differentiable** at exactly $x = 0$. In practice, deep learning frameworks conventionally set:
$$f'(0) = 0 \quad \text{(or 1, by convention — both are acceptable)}$$

**Step 4 — Compact form using the Heaviside step function $H(x)$:**
$$\boxed{f'(x) = H(x) = \begin{cases} 1 & x > 0 \\ 0 & x \leq 0 \end{cases}}$$

**Step 5 — Derivative of Leaky ReLU:**
For Leaky ReLU, $g(x) = \max(\alpha x, x) = x$ if $x>0$, else $\alpha x$. Differentiating piecewise:
$$g'(x) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } x > 0 \\ \alpha & \text{if } x \leq 0 \end{cases}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Softmax Jacobian (Output Layer)

For a vector $z = (z_1, \ldots, z_K)$, the softmax is:
$$\sigma(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$$

**Step 1 — Diagonal entries ($i = j$):**
$$\frac{\partial \sigma_i}{\partial z_i} = \frac{e^{z_i} \cdot S - e^{z_i} \cdot e^{z_i}}{S^2} = \sigma_i(1 - \sigma_i)$$
where $S = \sum_j e^{z_j}$.

**Step 2 — Off-diagonal entries ($i \neq j$):**
$$\frac{\partial \sigma_i}{\partial z_j} = \frac{0 \cdot S - e^{z_i} \cdot e^{z_j}}{S^2} = -\sigma_i \sigma_j$$

**Step 3 — Compact Jacobian matrix form:**
$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \sigma}{\partial z} = \text{diag}(\sigma) - \sigma \sigma^T}$$

This matrix is essential for backpropagation through a softmax output layer combined with cross-entropy loss.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Exam Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this comparison in any 14-mark question on activation functions. It is the most exam-friendly structure.

| Criterion | Sigmoid | Tanh | ReLU | Leaky ReLU | Softmax |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Range** | $(0,1)$ | $(-1,1)$ | $[0,\infty)$ | $(-\infty,\infty)$ | $(0,1)$, sum=1 |
| **Zero-centered?** | No | Yes | No | No | N/A |
| **Vanishing Gradient** | Severe | Moderate | No (for $x>0$) | No | No |
| **Dying Neuron Problem** | No | No | Yes | No (mitigated) | No |
| **Computational Cost** | High (exp) | High (exp) | Low | Low | High (exp) |
| **Differentiable** | Yes | Yes | No at $x=0$ | No at $x=0$ | Yes |
| **Best Use** | Binary output | Hidden layers | Hidden layers (default) | Deep hidden layers | Multi-class output |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Block Architecture of a Single Neuron with Activation Function

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1["Input x1"] --> W1["Weight w1"]
    X2["Input x2"] --> W2["Weight w2"]
    X3["Input xn"] --> W3["Weight wn"]
    B["Bias b"] --> SUM
    W1 --> SUM
    W2 --> SUM
    W3 --> SUM
    SUM["Summation node sum wi xi plus b"] --> PHI
    PHI["Activation Function phi"] --> Y["Output y"]
    PHI -.Gradient phi prime.-> GRAD["Backpropagation gradient signal"]
```

**Interpretation:** Inputs are weighted, summed with the bias, and then transformed by the activation function $\phi$. During backpropagation, the local gradient $\phi'$ is used to update the weights.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Decision Flow for Selecting the Right Activation Function

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Choose Activation Function"] --> Q1{Output layer or Hidden layer}
    Q1 -->|Output| Q2{Task type}
    Q1 -->|Hidden| Q3{Modern deep network}
    Q2 -->|Regression| LIN["Use Linear activation"]
    Q2 -->|Binary Classification| SIG["Use Sigmoid output"]
    Q2 -->|Multi-class Classification| SOFT["Use Softmax output"]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{Vanishing gradient or dying ReLU issue}
    Q3 -->|No| RELU["Use ReLU default"]
    Q4 -->|Yes| LRELU["Use Leaky ReLU or PReLU"]
    Q4 -->|No| RELU
    Q5{Hidden layer in RNN or LSTM} -->|Yes| TANH["Use Tanh"]
    Q5 -->|No| RELU
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualization Tip:** The "Use ReLU default" branch is the modern standard. Always justify your choice with one of these criteria in your KTU answer.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Functional Architecture of a Multi-Layer Neural Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN["Input Layer"] --> H1["Hidden Layer 1 phi equals ReLU"]
    H1 --> H2["Hidden Layer 2 phi equals ReLU"]
    H2 --> H3["Hidden Layer 3 phi equals ReLU"]
    H3 --> OUT["Output Layer phi equals Softmax"]
    IN -->|"784 pixels MNIST"| H1
    OUT -->|"10 class probabilities"| PRED["Predicted class argmax"]
```

**Interpretation:** A typical deep network for a 10-class image classification problem. ReLU activations in hidden layers introduce non-linearity progressively, while the Softmax in the final layer converts logits into class probabilities.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Plot of Activation Functions (Conceptual Schematic)

```mermaid

    subgraph SG["Sigmoid Range 0 to 1"]
        S1["Smooth S-shaped curve"]
        S2["Squashes extreme values toward 0 or 1"]
    end
    subgraph TN["Tanh Range minus 1 to 1"]
        T1["S-shaped curve crossing origin"]
        T2["Zero-centered output"]
    end
    subgraph RL["ReLU Range 0 to infinity"]
        R1["Linear for positive x"]
        R2["Zero for negative x"]
    end
    subgraph LX["Leaky ReLU Range minus infinity to infinity"]
        L1["Linear for positive x"]
        L2["Small slope alpha for negative x"]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define an activation function. Why is non-linearity essential in a neural network?**

**Model Answer:**

An **activation function** is a mathematical function applied to the weighted sum of inputs (plus bias) at a neuron to produce its output. It determines whether and how strongly a neuron should fire.

Non-linearity is essential because a neural network composed solely of linear transformations is mathematically equivalent to a single linear layer, regardless of its depth. Stacking linear layers collapses into:
$$W_2(W_1 x + b_1) + b_2 = (W_2 W_1)x + (W_2 b_1 + b_2)$$

Without non-linearity, the network cannot learn complex patterns such as the XOR function, curved decision boundaries, or hierarchical features in images. The **Universal Approximation Theorem** guarantees that a neural network with at least one hidden layer and a non-linear activation can approximate *any* continuous function to arbitrary accuracy.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the vanishing gradient problem? Which activation functions are most affected by it?**

**Model Answer:**

The **vanishing gradient problem** occurs when the gradients of the loss function with respect to the weights become extremely small (close to zero) during backpropagation. This causes the weights to update very slowly, stalling or completely halting learning in the early layers of deep networks.

The functions most affected are:
- **Sigmoid:** For large $\vert x \vert$, $\sigma(x) \to 0$ or $1$, so $\sigma'(x) = \sigma(x)(1-\sigma(x)) \to 0$.
- **Tanh:** For large $\vert x \vert$, $\tanh'(x) = 1 - \tanh^2(x) \to 0$.

This problem is particularly severe in deep networks because gradients are multiplied across many layers, causing exponential decay.

**Solution:** Use **ReLU** (or its variants like Leaky ReLU, PReLU) in hidden layers, since its derivative is exactly $1$ for positive inputs, allowing gradients to flow unimpeded.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU ESE, Part B questions are **choice-based**. Solve either **Question A** OR **Question B**. Both options are provided below.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` — 14 Marks

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the derivative of the sigmoid activation function. Show that $\sigma'(x) = \sigma(x)(1 - \sigma(x))$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the Sigmoid, Tanh, and ReLU activation functions with respect to range, vanishing gradient behavior, zero-centering, and computational cost. Which one is the most widely used in modern deep networks and why?

**Complete Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks] — Derivation of Sigmoid Derivative:**

Given $\sigma(x) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$:

Let $u = 1 + e^{-x}$, so $\sigma(x) = u^{-1}$ and $\frac{du}{dx} = -e^{-x}$.

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dx} = -u^{-2} \cdot \frac{du}{dx} = -u^{-2} \cdot (-e^{-x}) = \frac{e^{-x}}{(1+e^{-x})^2}$$

Now rewrite the numerator as $(1 + e^{-x}) - 1$:

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dx} = \frac{(1+e^{-x}) - 1}{(1+e^{-x})^2} = \frac{1}{1+e^{-x}} - \left(\frac{1}{1+e^{-x}}\right)^2 = \sigma(x) - \sigma^2(x)$$

$$\boxed{\sigma'(x) = \sigma(x)\bigl(1 - \sigma(x)\bigr)}$$

[Examiner's note: Full marks require the chain rule, the algebraic manipulation, and the final compact identity.]

**(b) [7 Marks] — Comparison Table and Justification:**

| Property | Sigmoid | Tanh | ReLU |
|---|---|---|---|
| Range | $(0, 1)$ | $(-1, 1)$ | $[0, \infty)$ |
| Zero-centered | No | Yes | No |
| Vanishing gradient | Severe | Moderate | None (for $x>0$) |
| Computational cost | High (exp) | High (exp) | Low (max) |
| Output interpretation | Probability | Signed signal | Unbounded positive |

**Conclusion:** **ReLU is the most widely used** in modern deep networks because:
1. It does not suffer from vanishing gradient for positive inputs (gradient = 1).
2. It is computationally cheap (only a max operation, no exponentials).
3. Empirically, it accelerates convergence of stochastic gradient descent compared to sigmoid and tanh.

However, Sigmoid remains the standard for **binary classification output layers** (interprets output as probability), and Tanh is preferred in **RNN/LSTM gates** for zero-centered outputs.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` — 14 Marks

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the ReLU and Leaky ReLU activation functions. Derive their derivatives. What is the "dying ReLU" problem and how does Leaky ReLU solve it?

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is the Softmax activation function? Derive the partial derivatives $\dfrac{\partial \sigma_i}{\partial z_i}$ and $\dfrac{\partial \sigma_i}{\partial z_j}$ for $i \neq j$. Where is Softmax used in a neural network?

**Complete Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks] — ReLU and Leaky ReLU:**

**ReLU Definition:**
$$\text{ReLU}(x) = \max(0, x) = \begin{cases} x & x > 0 \\ 0 & x \leq 0 \end{cases}$$

**ReLU Derivative (Piecewise):**
$$\frac{d}{dx}\text{ReLU}(x) = \begin{cases} 1 & x > 0 \\ 0 & x \leq 0 \end{cases}$$

**Leaky ReLU Definition:**
$$\text{LReLU}(x) = \begin{cases} x & x > 0 \\ \alpha x & x \leq 0 \end{cases}, \quad \alpha \in (0, 1), \text{ typically } \alpha = 0.01$$

**Leaky ReLU Derivative:**
$$\frac{d}{dx}\text{LReLU}(x) = \begin{cases} 1 & x > 0 \\ \alpha & x \leq 0 \end{cases}$$

**Dying ReLU Problem:**
If a neuron's pre-activation is consistently negative (due to large negative bias or aggressive weight updates), it outputs exactly $0$ and its gradient is exactly $0$. Consequently, no weight updates occur, and the neuron becomes "permanently dead" — it never recovers and never contributes to the network.

**How Leaky ReLU Solves This:**
By retaining a small slope $\alpha > 0$ for $x \leq 0$, the gradient is non-zero. This allows weight updates to flow through, ensuring the neuron can "revive" during training.

**(b) [7 Marks] — Softmax Function and Derivatives:**

**Definition:** For a vector of $K$ pre-activations $z = (z_1, \ldots, z_K)$:
$$\sigma(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$$

**Diagonal Derivative ($i = j$):** Let $S = \sum_j e^{z_j}$.
$$\frac{\partial \sigma_i}{\partial z_i} = \frac{e^{z_i} \cdot S - e^{z_i} \cdot e^{z_i}}{S^2} = \frac{e^{z_i}(S - e^{z_i})}{S^2} = \sigma_i(1 - \sigma_i)$$

**Off-Diagonal Derivative ($i \neq j$):**
$$\frac{\partial \sigma_i}{\partial z_j} = \frac{0 \cdot S - e^{z_i} \cdot e^{z_j}}{S^2} = -\sigma_i \sigma_j$$

**Application:** Softmax is used in the **output layer of a multi-class classifier** to convert raw logits into a probability distribution over $K$ mutually exclusive classes. The predicted class is $\arg\max_i \sigma(z_i)$.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting boundary ranges** — Always state the range (e.g., $(0,1)$, $(-1,1)$) explicitly. Marks are reserved for this in 7-mark questions.
> 2. **Misapplying softmax to binary tasks** — Use Sigmoid for binary, Softmax for multi-class. Examiners will deduct 1–2 marks for this confusion.
> 3. **Confusing the gradient of sigmoid with its output** — $\sigma'(x) \neq \sigma(x)$; the correct form is $\sigma(x)(1 - \sigma(x))$.
> 4. **Ignoring the "Why"** — In 14-mark answers, do not just list properties. *Justify* why ReLU is preferred (computational cost, gradient flow).
> 5. **Forgetting numerical stability of softmax** — In practical applications, the standard implementation subtracts $\max(z)$ from all $z_i$ before exponentiation to prevent overflow: $\sigma(z_i) = \dfrac{e^{z_i - \max(z)}}{\sum_j e^{z_j - \max(z)}}$. Mention this in viva.
> 6. **Mixing up Leaky ReLU and PReLU** — In Leaky ReLU, $\alpha$ is a *hyperparameter*; in PReLU, $\alpha$ is a *learnable parameter*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision before walking into the exam hall — go through this list.**

- [x] **Definition:** Activation function = mathematical function applied to a neuron's pre-activation to introduce non-linearity.
- [x] **Sigmoid Formula:** $\sigma(x) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$, Range $(0,1)$, Derivative $\sigma(x)(1-\sigma(x))$.
- [x] **Tanh Formula:** $\tanh(x) = \dfrac{e^x - e^{-x}}{e^x + e^{-x}}$, Range $(-1,1)$, Derivative $1 - \tanh^2(x)$.
- [x] **ReLU Formula:** $\max(0, x)$, Range $[0, \infty)$, Derivative $1$ if $x>0$ else $0$.
- [x] **Leaky ReLU Formula:** $x$ if $x>0$ else $\alpha x$ (typically $\alpha = 0.01$).
- [x] **Softmax Formula:** $\sigma(z_i) = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$, outputs a probability distribution summing to 1.
- [x] **Vanishing Gradient:** Sigmoid and Tanh suffer from it at extreme inputs. ReLU does not (for $x>0$).
- [x] **Dying ReLU Problem:** Neuron stuck in $x<0$ region outputs 0 always. Solved by Leaky ReLU or PReLU.
- [x] **Output Layer Rules:** Sigmoid → binary classification; Softmax → multi-class; Linear → regression.
- [x] **Hidden Layer Default:** ReLU in modern deep networks; Tanh in RNNs/LSTMs.
- [x] **Universal Approximation Theorem:** A network with one hidden layer and a non-linear activation can approximate any continuous function.
- [x] **Numerical Stability Tip:** Subtract the maximum logit from all logits before applying softmax to avoid overflow.

**High-Yield Mnemonic for KTU Exams — "STRLO":**
- **S**igmoid — binary output, range $(0,1)$.
- **T**anh — hidden layers, range $(-1,1)$, zero-centered.
- **R**eLU — default hidden layer, range $[0, \infty)$, cheap.
- **L**eaky ReLU — fixes dying ReLU, slope $\alpha$ for $x \leq 0$.
- **O**utput rule — Softmax for multi-class, Sigmoid for binary, Linear for regression.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feed Forward Network
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT501 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Feed Forward Network

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Feed Forward Network (FFN)**, also known as a **Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP)** or **Fully-Connected Deep Network**, is a class of artificial neural networks in which information propagates strictly in one direction — from the input layer, through one or more hidden layers, to the output layer — without any recurrent connections, feedback loops, or skip-back paths. The network is formally defined as a parametric function $f_{\theta}: \mathbb{R}^{n} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{m}$ where $\theta = \{W^{(l)}, b^{(l)}\}_{l=1}^{L}$ represents the learnable parameters (weights and biases) across $L$ layers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A Feed Forward Network is the foundational architecture upon which all modern deep learning models (CNNs, Transformers, GANs) are constructed. The "feed-forward" descriptor specifically refers to the *acyclic, unidirectional* flow of activations during the inference (forward pass) phase.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **factory assembly line** that manufactures a finished product from raw materials:
- **Raw materials** (the input features $\mathbf{x}$) enter at one end.
- They pass through a series of **quality-check and modification stations** (the hidden layers), where each worker (a neuron) inspects specific attributes and modifies them based on learned expertise (the weights $\mathbf{W}$).
- The final processed good (the prediction $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$) exits at the other end.
- Crucially, **no widget ever travels backward** to a previous station — the line only moves forward.

This assembly-line analogy captures the essence of an FFN: data flows in one direction, and each layer transforms the representation into something progressively more useful for the task at hand.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** During *training*, error signals DO travel backward via **backpropagation** — but during the *forward pass* (inference), the network is strictly feed-forward. The term "feed-forward" refers to the architecture's information flow, not the training algorithm.

### 1.3 Architectural Components

An FFN is composed of three distinct layer types:

| Component | Function | Symbol |
|-----------|----------|--------|
| **Input Layer** | Receives raw feature vector | $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ |
| **Hidden Layer(s)** | Apply affine transformation + nonlinear activation | $a^{(l)} = f(W^{(l)} a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)})$ |
| **Output Layer** | Produces final prediction | $\hat{\mathbf{y}} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ |

### 1.4 Why Nonlinearity is Essential

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A pure cascade of linear transformations is mathematically equivalent to a single linear transformation. Stacking linear layers without a nonlinear **activation function** collapses the entire network into a single linear model, rendering depth useless. Nonlinear activations (ReLU, Sigmoid, Tanh) are what give deep networks their expressive power.

The **Universal Approximation Theorem** (Cybenko, 1989; Hornik, 1991) states that a feed-forward network with **at least one hidden layer** and a suitable nonlinear activation function can approximate **any continuous function** on a compact domain to arbitrary precision, given a sufficient number of hidden units.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Mechanics — The Forward Pass

The forward propagation in an FFN proceeds layer-by-layer through the following sequence:

1. **Input Reception:** The input feature vector $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^{T}$ is presented to the network.
2. **Affine Transformation (Pre-activation):** At layer $l$, the previous layer's activations $\mathbf{a}^{(l-1)}$ are linearly combined using the weight matrix $W^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_l \times d_{l-1}}$ and bias vector $\mathbf{b}^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_l}$.
3. **Nonlinear Activation:** The pre-activation $\mathbf{z}^{(l)}$ is passed through a nonlinear function $f(\cdot)$ to produce the activations $\mathbf{a}^{(l)}$.
4. **Propagation:** $\mathbf{a}^{(l)}$ becomes the input to layer $l+1$, and the process repeats.
5. **Output Generation:** The final layer $L$ produces $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$, which is compared against the true label $\mathbf{y}$ via a loss function.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Pre-activation at layer $l$ | $\mathbf{z}^{(l)} = W^{(l)} \mathbf{a}^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)}$ | Linear combination step |
| 2 | Activation at layer $l$ | $\mathbf{a}^{(l)} = f(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})$ | Nonlinearity step |
| 3 | Input layer identity | $\mathbf{a}^{(0)} = \mathbf{x}$ | Boundary condition |
| 4 | Sigmoid activation | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Output range: $(0, 1)$ |
| 5 | Hyperbolic Tangent | $\tanh(z) = \frac{e^{z} - e^{-z}}{e^{z} + e^{-z}}$ | Output range: $(-1, 1)$ |
| 6 | ReLU activation | $\text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z)$ | Sparse, efficient |
| 7 | Softmax (output) | $\text{softmax}(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j} e^{z_j}}$ | Multi-class probability |
| 8 | Mean Squared Error | $\mathcal{L}_{MSE} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Regression loss |
| 9 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{c=1}^{C} y_{ic} \log(\hat{y}_{ic})$ | Classification loss |
| 10 | Total Parameters | $P = \sum_{l=1}^{L} (d_l \cdot d_{l-1} + d_l)$ | Weights + biases |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Tip:** When writing the forward pass, always explicitly state $\mathbf{a}^{(0)} = \mathbf{x}$ as the base case. Examiners deduct marks for ambiguous notation. Also remember that the bias $\mathbf{b}^{(l)}$ is **NOT** multiplied by any input — it is added directly to the pre-activation.

### 2.3 The Role of Activation Functions

Each activation function serves distinct purposes:

- **Sigmoid $\sigma$**: Historically popular; outputs probabilities. Suffers from the **vanishing gradient problem** for large $\vert z \vert$.
- **Tanh**: Zero-centered version of sigmoid; preferred over sigmoid for hidden layers in older architectures.
- **ReLU**: Modern default. Computationally cheap, mitigates vanishing gradients, induces sparsity. Dead-rectifier problem: neurons can permanently output 0 if $z < 0$ persistently.
- **Leaky ReLU / PReLU**: Variants that allow a small negative slope $\alpha z$ when $z < 0$, preventing dead neurons.
- **Softmax**: Used exclusively in the **output layer** for multi-class classification to convert logits into a valid probability distribution summing to 1.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

FFNs form the computational backbone of:
- **Tabular data prediction** (credit scoring, medical diagnosis, churn prediction)
- **Feature extractors in CNNs** (the fully-connected tail of classifiers like VGG, ResNet)
- **Position-wise feed-forward sublayers in Transformer architectures** (the FFN block in BERT, GPT)
- **Universal function approximators** in reinforcement learning (value functions, policy networks)
- **Regression tasks** in signal processing, control systems, and financial forecasting

### 2.5 Capacity and Depth Trade-offs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Width vs. Depth:** A wider network (more neurons per layer) increases the set of functions representable at that layer's granularity. A deeper network (more layers) enables hierarchical feature composition — early layers learn simple patterns, later layers compose them into complex abstractions. The Universal Approximation Theorem guarantees expressivity with width, but depth often achieves the same expressivity with exponentially fewer parameters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Forward Propagation in a 2-Layer FFN

Consider a network with input $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{2}$, one hidden layer with 3 neurons, and one output neuron. We derive the complete forward pass.

**Given:**
- Input: $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2]^{T}$
- Hidden layer weights: $W^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 2}$, biases: $\mathbf{b}^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$
- Output layer weights: $W^{(2)} \in \mathbb{R}^{1 \times 3}$, bias: $b^{(2)} \in \mathbb{R}$
- Hidden activation: ReLU; Output activation: Sigmoid

**Step 1: Input Layer Assignment**

The input layer passes the features unchanged into the network.

$$\mathbf{a}^{(0)} = \mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Hidden Layer Pre-activation**

We compute the linear combination of inputs and weights for the 3 hidden neurons.

$$\mathbf{z}^{(1)} = W^{(1)} \mathbf{a}^{(0)} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)}$$

Explicitly expanding the matrix-vector product:

$$\begin{aligned}
z^{(1)}_1 &= w^{(1)}_{11} x_1 + w^{(1)}_{12} x_2 + b^{(1)}_1 \\
z^{(1)}_2 &= w^{(1)}_{21} x_1 + w^{(1)}_{22} x_2 + b^{(1)}_2 \\
z^{(1)}_3 &= w^{(1)}_{31} x_1 + w^{(1)}_{32} x_2 + b^{(1)}_3
\end{aligned}$$

This is the dot product of each row of $W^{(1)}$ with $\mathbf{x}$, plus a bias scalar.

**Step 3: Hidden Layer Activation**

Apply ReLU element-wise: $f(z) = \max(0, z)$.

$$\begin{aligned}
a^{(1)}_1 &= \text{ReLU}(z^{(1)}_1) = \max(0,\, w^{(1)}_{11} x_1 + w^{(1)}_{12} x_2 + b^{(1)}_1) \\
a^{(1)}_2 &= \text{ReLU}(z^{(1)}_2) = \max(0,\, w^{(1)}_{21} x_1 + w^{(1)}_{22} x_2 + b^{(1)}_2) \\
a^{(1)}_3 &= \text{ReLU}(z^{(1)}_3) = \max(0,\, w^{(1)}_{31} x_1 + w^{(1)}_{32} x_2 + b^{(1)}_3)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Output Layer Pre-activation**

The single output neuron combines all 3 hidden activations.

$$z^{(2)} = W^{(2)} \mathbf{a}^{(1)} + b^{(2)} = w^{(2)}_1 a^{(1)}_1 + w^{(2)}_2 a^{(1)}_2 + w^{(2)}_3 a^{(1)}_3 + b^{(2)}$$

**Step 5: Output Layer Activation (Sigmoid)**

$$\hat{y} = \sigma(z^{(2)}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z^{(2)}}}$$

**Step 6: Loss Computation**

For binary classification with true label $y \in \{0, 1\}$:

$$\mathcal{L} = -\left[ y \log(\hat{y}) + (1 - y) \log(1 - \hat{y}) \right]$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Total Parameter Count

**Problem:** Compute the number of learnable parameters in an FFN with architecture: Input(784) → Hidden(256) → Hidden(128) → Output(10).

**Step 1: Layer 1 Parameters**

Weights: $784 \times 256 = 200{,}704$. Biases: $256$. Subtotal: $200{,}960$.

**Step 2: Layer 2 Parameters**

Weights: $256 \times 128 = 32{,}768$. Biases: $128$. Subtotal: $32{,}896$.

**Step 3: Layer 3 Parameters**

Weights: $128 \times 10 = 1{,}280$. Biases: $10$. Subtotal: $1{,}290$.

**Step 4: Total**

$$P = 200{,}960 + 32{,}896 + 1{,}290 = 235{,}146 \text{ parameters}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Universal Approximation — Intuitive Form

For a single hidden-layer FFN with $H$ hidden units and activation $\sigma$:

$$f(\mathbf{x}) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{H} v_i \, \sigma\left( \mathbf{w}_i^{T} \mathbf{x} + b_i \right)$$

Here, each hidden neuron $i$ computes a "bump" function (a sigmoid-shaped or ReLU-shaped ridge) parameterized by weight $\mathbf{w}_i$ and bias $b_i$. The output weights $v_i$ scale and sum these bumps. As $H \rightarrow \infty$, the sum can approximate any continuous function — intuitively, by tiling the input space with infinitely many small bumps.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** The theorem guarantees *existence* of such a network but says nothing about *learnability*. In practice, depth, regularization, and optimization are needed to actually find good approximations.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Activation Function Comparison

| Property | Sigmoid | Tanh | ReLU | Leaky ReLU |
|----------|---------|------|------|------------|
| Range | $(0, 1)$ | $(-1, 1)$ | $[0, \infty)$ | $(-\infty, \infty)$ |
| Zero-centered | No | Yes | No | No |
| Gradient vanishing | Yes (severe) | Yes (moderate) | No (for $z > 0$) | No |
| Computational cost | Expensive | Expensive | Cheap | Cheap |
| Sparse activation | No | No | Yes | No |
| Recommended layer | Output (binary) | Hidden (legacy) | Hidden (default) | Hidden (advanced) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Standard FFN Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeX["INPUT LAYER n features"] --> nodeH1["HIDDEN LAYER 1 h1 neurons ReLU"]
    nodeH1 --> nodeH2["HIDDEN LAYER 2 h2 neurons ReLU"]
    nodeH2 --> nodeY["OUTPUT LAYER m neurons Softmax"]
    nodeX -.- nodeW1["W1 b1 Fully Connected"]
    nodeH1 -.- nodeW2["W2 b2 Fully Connected"]
    nodeH2 -.- nodeW3["W3 b3 Fully Connected"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Single Neuron Computational Unit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeX1["x1"] --> nodeSum["Z = sum w_i x_i + b"]
    nodeX2["x2"] --> nodeSum
    nodeXn["xn"] --> nodeSum
    nodeW1["w1"] --> nodeSum
    nodeW2["w2"] --> nodeSum
    nodeWn["wn"] --> nodeSum
    nodeB["bias b"] --> nodeSum
    nodeSum --> nodeAct["ACTIVATION f"]
    nodeAct --> nodeOut["OUTPUT a"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Forward Pass Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["STEP 1 Input Vector x"] --> nodeB["STEP 2 Linear Transform z1 = W1 x + b1"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["STEP 3 Apply Activation a1 = f z1"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["STEP 4 Linear Transform z2 = W2 a1 + b2"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["STEP 5 Apply Activation a2 = g z2"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["STEP 6 Compute Loss L y hat y"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["STEP 7 Backpropagate Gradients via Chain Rule"]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchical Feature Learning

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph s1["LOW LEVEL FEATURES"]
        nA["Edges Curves Textures"]
    end
    subgraph s2["MID LEVEL FEATURES"]
        nB["Corners Shapes Motifs"]
    end
    subgraph s3["HIGH LEVEL FEATURES"]
        nC["Objects Faces Concepts"]
    end
    nA --> nB
    nB --> nC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** FFNs learn hierarchical representations — each successive layer combines features from the previous layer into more abstract concepts. This is the foundational principle behind deep learning's success in representation learning.

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

For the FFN with architecture Input(784) → Dense(256) → Dense(128) → Dense(10):

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["FLATTEN 28x28 image to vector 784"] --> B["DENSE LAYER 1 200960 params ReLU"]
    B --> C["DENSE LAYER 2 32896 params ReLU"]
    C --> D["DENSE LAYER 3 1290 params Softmax"]
    D --> E["CLASS PROBABILITIES 10 classes"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a Feed Forward Neural Network. Why is a nonlinear activation function necessary in its hidden layers?*

**Model Answer:**
A Feed Forward Neural Network (FFNN) is an artificial neural network architecture in which information flows in a single direction — from the input layer through one or more hidden layers to the output layer — without any cycles, recurrent connections, or feedback loops. Mathematically, it represents a composite function $f(\mathbf{x}) = f_L(\ldots f_2(f_1(\mathbf{x} \ldots)))$ where each $f_l$ is an affine transformation followed by a nonlinear activation.

A nonlinear activation function is **necessary** because without it, stacking multiple layers would reduce to a single linear transformation. The composition of linear functions is itself linear: $W_2(W_1 \mathbf{x} + b_1) + b_2 = (W_2 W_1)\mathbf{x} + (W_2 b_1 + b_2)$, which is equivalent to a single-layer linear model. Nonlinearity is what enables the network to approximate complex, non-convex decision boundaries and underlies the Universal Approximation Theorem.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three activation functions used in feed-forward networks. State one advantage and one limitation of ReLU.*

**Model Answer:**
Three common activation functions are:
1. **Sigmoid:** $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ — maps to $(0,1)$.
2. **Hyperbolic Tangent (Tanh):** $\tanh(z)$ — maps to $(-1,1)$.
3. **ReLU:** $\text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z)$ — maps to $[0, \infty)$.

**Advantage of ReLU:** Computationally efficient (involves only a thresholding operation), and helps mitigate the vanishing gradient problem since its gradient is exactly 1 for positive inputs, allowing gradients to flow unchanged through deep networks.

**Limitation of ReLU:** The "dying ReLU" problem — if a neuron's pre-activation is consistently negative during training, it outputs 0 and its gradient is 0, causing it to never update and become permanently inactive.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Part B questions in ESE carry 14 marks with internal choice. Sub-parts are typically (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Both choices must be attempted-ready; only one is to be solved in the exam.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a Feed Forward Neural Network with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of the bias term in the network. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A Feed Forward Neural Network consists of an input layer, one or more hidden layers, and an output layer. Each layer is fully connected to the subsequent layer, meaning every neuron in layer $l-1$ is connected to every neuron in layer $l$ via a learnable weight. Information flows strictly forward: from $\mathbf{x}$ through $\mathbf{a}^{(1)}, \mathbf{a}^{(2)}, \ldots, \mathbf{a}^{(L)} = \hat{\mathbf{y}}$.

The architecture is governed by two key operations at each layer:
1. **Affine transformation:** $\mathbf{z}^{(l)} = W^{(l)} \mathbf{a}^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)}$
2. **Nonlinear activation:** $\mathbf{a}^{(l)} = f(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})$

**Role of the Bias Term:**
The bias $\mathbf{b}^{(l)}$ serves as an *affine offset* that allows the activation function to shift its input domain. Without bias, the pre-activation $W^{(l)} \mathbf{a}^{(l-1)}$ is forced to pass through the origin, severely restricting the expressivity of the network.

Concretely, the bias shifts the decision boundary. For a single neuron, $z = w^T x + b = 0$ defines a hyperplane; changing $b$ translates this hyperplane in input space. Without bias, the hyperplane must pass through the origin, which is often suboptimal or impossible for separating the data.

Mathematically, adding bias is equivalent to augmenting the input with a constant feature $x_0 = 1$ and absorbing $b$ into the weight vector — but explicitly retaining $\mathbf{b}$ simplifies the notation and is standard in deep learning frameworks.

**(b)** Consider a feed-forward network with input dimension 2, two hidden layers with 4 and 3 neurons respectively (using ReLU), and 1 output neuron (using Sigmoid). Write the complete forward pass equations, clearly indicating the dimensions of all weight matrices and bias vectors. Compute the total number of learnable parameters. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Let $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{2}$ be the input. The forward pass proceeds as:

**Hidden Layer 1 (4 neurons, ReLU):**
- $W^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{4 \times 2}$, $\mathbf{b}^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{4}$
- $\mathbf{z}^{(1)} = W^{(1)} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)}$  — yields $\mathbf{z}^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{4}$
- $\mathbf{a}^{(1)} = \text{ReLU}(\mathbf{z}^{(1)}) = \max(0, \mathbf{z}^{(1)})$ — yields $\mathbf{a}^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{4}$

**Hidden Layer 2 (3 neurons, ReLU):**
- $W^{(2)} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 4}$, $\mathbf{b}^{(2)} \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$
- $\mathbf{z}^{(2)} = W^{(2)} \mathbf{a}^{(1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(2)}$ — yields $\mathbf{z}^{(2)} \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$
- $\mathbf{a}^{(2)} = \text{ReLU}(\mathbf{z}^{(2)})$ — yields $\mathbf{a}^{(2)} \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$

**Output Layer (1 neuron, Sigmoid):**
- $W^{(3)} \in \mathbb{R}^{1 \times 3}$, $b^{(3)} \in \mathbb{R}$
- $z^{(3)} = W^{(3)} \mathbf{a}^{(2)} + b^{(3)}$ — yields $z^{(3)} \in \mathbb{R}$
- $\hat{y} = \sigma(z^{(3)}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z^{(3)}}}$

**Parameter Count:**

Layer 1: $4 \times 2 + 4 = 8 + 4 = 12$ parameters
Layer 2: $3 \times 4 + 3 = 12 + 3 = 15$ parameters
Layer 3: $1 \times 3 + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4$ parameters

**Total = $12 + 15 + 4 = 31$ learnable parameters.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** State and explain the Universal Approximation Theorem for feed-forward networks. Discuss one limitation of the theorem. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Statement of the Universal Approximation Theorem (Cybenko, 1989; Hornik, 1991):**
Let $\sigma: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a non-constant, bounded, and monotonically increasing continuous function (e.g., sigmoid). Let $C(I_n)$ denote the space of continuous real-valued functions on the $n$-dimensional unit hypercube $I_n = [0, 1]^n$. Then for any function $f \in C(I_n)$ and any $\epsilon > 0$, there exists an integer $H$ and parameters $v_i \in \mathbb{R}$, $\mathbf{w}_i \in \mathbb{R}^n$, $b_i \in \mathbb{R}$ such that:

$$\left\vert F(\mathbf{x}) - f(\mathbf{x}) \right\vert < \epsilon \quad \forall \mathbf{x} \in I_n$$

where the constructed network is:

$$F(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{H} v_i \, \sigma(\mathbf{w}_i^{T} \mathbf{x} + b_i)$$

**Explanation:** A single-hidden-layer feed-forward network with a sigmoid activation and a *sufficient* (but finite) number of hidden units can approximate any continuous function on a compact domain to arbitrary precision. This is the theoretical justification for the expressive power of neural networks.

**Limitation:** The theorem is an **existence result** — it guarantees that *some* parameter setting achieves the approximation, but it does not guarantee that standard gradient-based optimization (e.g., SGD, Adam) will *find* such parameters. In practice, deep networks with multiple layers and proper optimization often find approximations more efficiently than wide shallow networks, even though the theorem does not require depth.

A second limitation is that the required $H$ (width) may be **exponentially large** for certain functions, making shallow networks impractical compared to deeper architectures.

**(b)** Given the following network: Input(3) → Hidden Layer(5, ReLU) → Output Layer(2, Softmax). The input sample is $\mathbf{x} = [1.0, 0.5, -0.2]^{T}$. The hidden layer weight matrix $W^{(1)}$ and bias $\mathbf{b}^{(1)}$ are:

$W^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 & -0.1 & 0.3 \\ 0.5 & 0.4 & -0.2 \\ -0.3 & 0.1 & 0.6 \\ 0.7 & -0.5 & 0.2 \\ 0.1 & 0.3 & -0.4 \end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{b}^{(1)} = [0.1, -0.2, 0.3, 0.0, -0.1]^{T}$

Compute $\mathbf{a}^{(1)}$ (the hidden layer activations). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute $\mathbf{z}^{(1)} = W^{(1)} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)}$**

Computing each entry of $\mathbf{z}^{(1)}$ as the dot product of the corresponding row of $W^{(1)}$ with $\mathbf{x}$, then adding the bias:

$$\begin{aligned}
z^{(1)}_1 &= (0.2)(1.0) + (-0.1)(0.5) + (0.3)(-0.2) + 0.1 \\
&= 0.2 - 0.05 - 0.06 + 0.1 = 0.19 \\
\\
z^{(1)}_2 &= (0.5)(1.0) + (0.4)(0.5) + (-0.2)(-0.2) + (-0.2) \\
&= 0.5 + 0.2 + 0.04 - 0.2 = 0.54 \\
\\
z^{(1)}_3 &= (-0.3)(1.0) + (0.1)(0.5) + (0.6)(-0.2) + 0.3 \\
&= -0.3 + 0.05 - 0.12 + 0.3 = -0.07 \\
\\
z^{(1)}_4 &= (0.7)(1.0) + (-0.5)(0.5) + (0.2)(-0.2) + 0.0 \\
&= 0.7 - 0.25 - 0.04 + 0.0 = 0.41 \\
\\
z^{(1)}_5 &= (0.1)(1.0) + (0.3)(0.5) + (-0.4)(-0.2) + (-0.1) \\
&= 0.1 + 0.15 + 0.08 - 0.1 = 0.23
\end{aligned}$$

So $\mathbf{z}^{(1)} = [0.19,\, 0.54,\, -0.07,\, 0.41,\, 0.23]^{T}$.

**Step 2: Apply ReLU element-wise: $a^{(1)}_i = \max(0, z^{(1)}_i)$**

$$\begin{aligned}
a^{(1)}_1 &= \max(0, 0.19) = 0.19 \\
a^{(1)}_2 &= \max(0, 0.54) = 0.54 \\
a^{(1)}_3 &= \max(0, -0.07) = 0.00 \quad \text{(ReLU clips negative values to 0)} \\
a^{(1)}_4 &= \max(0, 0.41) = 0.41 \\
a^{(1)}_5 &= \max(0, 0.23) = 0.23
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Hidden Layer Activations:**

$$\mathbf{a}^{(1)} = [0.19,\, 0.54,\, 0.00,\, 0.41,\, 0.23]^{T}$$

This vector would then serve as input to the Softmax output layer.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Dimensions of $W^{(l)}$:** A common error is reversing the matrix dimensions. The convention is $W^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_l \times d_{l-1}}$ (rows = current layer size, columns = previous layer size). Writing it the other way around loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Bias application:** Biases are **added**, not multiplied. A mistake here indicates fundamental confusion and is heavily penalized.
> 3. **ReLU on negatives:** Always explicitly state that negative pre-activations map to 0. A silent omission loses marks.
> 4. **Softmax normalization:** If the question involves the output layer, students often forget to divide by the sum of exponentials. The output must sum to 1.
> 5. **Universal Approximation Theorem:** Many students write the statement verbatim but forget to mention a *limitation* (e.g., learnability, width requirements). Examiners specifically look for critical discussion.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A Feed Forward Network is a fully-connected, acyclic neural network where information flows unidirectionally from input to output.
- **Architecture Components:** Input layer $\mathbf{a}^{(0)} = \mathbf{x}$, hidden layers with affine + nonlinear transformations, and an output layer producing $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$.
- **Forward Pass Formula:** $\mathbf{a}^{(l)} = f(W^{(l)} \mathbf{a}^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)})$ for $l = 1, 2, \ldots, L$.
- **Nonlinearity is Mandatory:** Without an activation function, depth collapses to a single linear transformation.
- **Universal Approximation Theorem:** A single hidden layer with enough neurons and a nonlinear activation can approximate any continuous function — but learnability is NOT guaranteed.
- **Activation Function Defaults:** ReLU for hidden layers, Sigmoid/Softmax for output layers (depending on task).
- **Parameter Count Formula:** $P = \sum_{l=1}^{L} (d_l \cdot d_{l-1} + d_l)$ — sum of weights and biases per layer.
- **Weight Matrix Dimensions:** $W^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_l \times d_{l-1}}$; bias $\mathbf{b}^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_l}$.
- **Bias Purpose:** Translates the activation function's input, enabling hyperplanes that do not pass through the origin.
- **Loss Functions:** MSE for regression, Cross-Entropy for classification.
- **Hierarchical Learning:** Depth enables composition of low-level features into high-level abstractions.
- **Feed Forward vs. Recurrent:** FFN has no cycles; RNN has feedback connections (covered in later modules).
- **Practical Use Cases:** Tabular data, transformer FFN blocks, CNN classifier heads, RL policy/value networks.
- **KTU Exam Pattern:** Expect 3-mark definition/short questions and 14-mark derivation/numerical problems with sub-parts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT501 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Back Propagation Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Back Propagation Algorithm (BPA)** is a **supervised learning algorithm** used to train **Multi-Layer Feedforward Neural Networks (MLFFNN)**. It computes the **gradient of the loss function** with respect to each weight in the network by applying the **chain rule of calculus**, propagating the error backwards from the output layer to the input layer. The computed gradients are then used by an optimization algorithm (typically **Gradient Descent**) to iteratively update the network weights so as to **minimize the prediction error**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Back Propagation is the *computational engine* that enables learning in **Deep Neural Networks (DNNs)**. Without it, training a network with more than one hidden layer would be computationally intractable using naive numerical differentiation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Student Learning from Mistakes**

Imagine a school student taking a tough mathematics test:
- **Forward Pass** = Attempting the test (reading question → solving → writing answer).
- **Loss Function** = Marking the answer sheet. The more wrong answers, the higher the "loss."
- **Backward Pass** = Going back to *each* step of the solution to find *where exactly* the mistake crept in. The student asks: *"Did I mess up the formula, the substitution, or the algebra?"*
- **Weight Update** = Studying harder on the weak area, i.e., adjusting the "strength" of that concept.
- **Iteration** = Retaking the test repeatedly until the loss (number of mistakes) is acceptably small.

In the same way, the network makes a prediction, measures how wrong it is, traces *which weights contributed to that error*, and adjusts them. This is **learning**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (High-Yield)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Epoch** | One complete pass of the entire training dataset through the network |
| **Learning Rate ($\eta$)** | Step size used to update weights; typically between **0.01** and **0.1** |
| **Gradient ($\nabla E$)** | Vector of partial derivatives of error w.r.t. weights |
| **Activation Function ($\sigma$)** | Non-linear function (e.g., **sigmoid**, **ReLU**) applied at each neuron |
| **Epoch / Batch / Iteration** | Epoch = full data; Batch = subset; Iteration = one weight update |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The Back Propagation Algorithm is a *mandatory topic* in Module 3 of PCITT501. Questions are frequently asked on the *derivation of weight update equations* and the *role of the chain rule*.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why is Back Propagation Needed?

In a multi-layer network, the final output error depends on *every* weight in *every* layer. To minimize this error using **Gradient Descent**, we need $\frac{\partial E}{\partial w}$ for each weight. Computing these derivatives directly is **expensive** ($\mathcal{O}(N)$ per weight, where $N$ is the number of computations). Back Propagation uses the **chain rule** to compute all gradients in just **one forward + one backward pass**, i.e., $\mathcal{O}(W)$ where $W$ is the total number of weights. This efficiency is what makes deep learning feasible.

### 2.2 The Two Fundamental Phases

**Phase 1 — Forward Propagation (Inference):**
The input signal flows from the input layer $\rightarrow$ hidden layers $\rightarrow$ output layer. Activations are computed layer-by-layer using current weights. No learning happens here.

**Phase 2 — Backward Propagation (Learning):**
The error signal flows from the output layer $\rightarrow$ hidden layers $\rightarrow$ input layer. Gradients are computed using the **chain rule**, and weights are updated using **Gradient Descent**.

### 2.3 Operational Steps (Logic Breakdown)

1. **Initialize** weights $w$ and biases $b$ with small random values (e.g., $\sim \mathcal{N}(0, 0.01)$).
2. **For each training sample $(x, t)$:**
   - **Forward Pass:** Compute activations $a^{(l)}$ and outputs $y$ for all layers $l = 1, 2, \dots, L$.
   - **Compute Loss:** Evaluate the error $E = \frac{1}{2}\sum_k (t_k - y_k)^2$.
   - **Backward Pass:**
     - Compute output-layer error signal $\delta^{(L)}$.
     - Propagate $\delta^{(l)}$ backwards for each layer $l = L-1, L-2, \dots, 1$.
   - **Gradient Calculation:** $\frac{\partial E}{\partial w^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)} \cdot (a^{(l-1)})^T$.
   - **Weight Update:** $w^{(l)} \leftarrow w^{(l)} - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial E}{\partial w^{(l)}}$.
3. **Repeat** for a fixed number of epochs or until convergence.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All symbols below appear in KTU question papers. Memorize the structure, not just the formula.**

| Quantity | Equation | Notation |
|---|---|---|
| Net input to neuron $j$ in layer $l$ | $z_j^{(l)} = \sum_i w_{ij}^{(l)} \, a_i^{(l-1)} + b_j^{(l)}$ | Pre-activation |
| Activation of neuron $j$ | $a_j^{(l)} = \sigma(z_j^{(l)})$ | $\sigma$ = sigmoid/ReLU |
| Sigmoid function | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Output range $(0, 1)$ |
| Sigmoid derivative | $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)\bigl(1 - \sigma(z)\bigr)$ | Key identity |
| Mean Squared Error | $E = \dfrac{1}{2}\sum_k (t_k - y_k)^2$ | Loss function |
| Output error signal | $\delta_k^{(L)} = (y_k - t_k) \cdot \sigma'(z_k^{(L)})$ | At output neuron $k$ |
| Hidden error signal | $\delta_j^{(l)} = \Bigl(\sum_k w_{jk}^{(l+1)} \, \delta_k^{(l+1)}\Bigr) \cdot \sigma'(z_j^{(l)})$ | Back-propagated |
| Weight gradient | $\dfrac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}^{(l)}} = \delta_j^{(l)} \cdot a_i^{(l-1)}$ | Used in update |
| Bias gradient | $\dfrac{\partial E}{\partial b_j^{(l)}} = \delta_j^{(l)}$ | Special case of weight |
| Weight update rule | $w_{ij}^{(l)} \leftarrow w_{ij}^{(l)} - \eta \cdot \delta_j^{(l)} \cdot a_i^{(l-1)}$ | **Gradient Descent** |
| Bias update rule | $b_j^{(l)} \leftarrow b_j^{(l)} - \eta \cdot \delta_j^{(l)}$ | Same learning rate $\eta$ |

> **Note on Notation:** $\vert \cdot \vert$ is never used in tables to avoid breaking markdown. We use parentheses or explicit "$\sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$" instead.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

Back Propagation is the **backbone of modern AI**:
- **Computer Vision:** CNNs (Convolutional Neural Networks) — trained via backprop.
- **Natural Language Processing:** RNNs, LSTMs, and **Transformers** (e.g., GPT, BERT) all rely on backprop.
- **Speech Recognition:** DeepSpeech, Whisper.
- **Medical Diagnosis:** Tumor detection from MRI scans.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** End-to-end steering prediction.

Without backpropagation, **none** of these would be trainable.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Below — No Steps Skipped.** This is the standard derivation KTU expects in a 14-mark question. Learn every line.

### 3.1 Network Architecture & Notation

Consider a **2-layer feedforward neural network** (i.e., 1 hidden layer, 1 output layer):

- Input vector: $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)^T$
- Hidden layer activations: $\mathbf{a}^{(1)} = (a_1^{(1)}, a_2^{(1)}, \dots, a_m^{(1)})^T$
- Output: $\mathbf{y} = (y_1, y_2, \dots, y_p)^T$
- Target: $\mathbf{t} = (t_1, t_2, \dots, t_p)^T$
- Weights input $\rightarrow$ hidden: $w_{ij}^{(1)}$
- Weights hidden $\rightarrow$ output: $w_{jk}^{(2)}$
- Biases: $b_j^{(1)}$, $b_k^{(2)}$
- Activation function: $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ (sigmoid)

### 3.2 Forward Pass Equations

**Step 1 — Net input at hidden layer:**

$$
z_j^{(1)} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{ij}^{(1)} \, x_i + b_j^{(1)}
$$

**Step 2 — Activation at hidden layer:**

$$
a_j^{(1)} = \sigma\Bigl(z_j^{(1)}\Bigr) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z_j^{(1)}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Net input at output layer:**

$$
z_k^{(2)} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} w_{jk}^{(2)} \, a_j^{(1)} + b_k^{(2)}
$$

**Step 4 — Output (network prediction):**

$$
y_k = \sigma\Bigl(z_k^{(2)}\Bigr)
$$

**Step 5 — Loss function (Mean Squared Error):**

$$
E = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{p} \bigl(t_k - y_k\bigr)^2
$$

> **Logic Note:** The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is included so that its derivative cancels the power-of-2 in the squared error, simplifying the gradient to a clean form.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Output-Layer Weight Update

We want $\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{jk}^{(2)}}$. Using the **chain rule**:

$$
\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{jk}^{(2)}} = \frac{\partial E}{\partial y_k} \cdot \frac{\partial y_k}{\partial z_k^{(2)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z_k^{(2)}}{\partial w_{jk}^{(2)}}
$$

**Compute each partial derivative individually:**

**(a)** Derivative of loss w.r.t. output:
$$
\frac{\partial E}{\partial y_k} = \frac{\partial}{\partial y_k}\left[\frac{1}{2}(t_k - y_k)^2\right] = (t_k - y_k) \cdot (-1) = -(t_k - y_k)
$$

**(b)** Derivative of sigmoid (using identity $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$):
$$
\frac{\partial y_k}{\partial z_k^{(2)}} = \sigma'\bigl(z_k^{(2)}\bigr) = y_k \, \bigl(1 - y_k\bigr)
$$

**(c)** Derivative of net input w.r.t. weight (linear in $w$):
$$
\frac{\partial z_k^{(2)}}{\partial w_{jk}^{(2)}} = a_j^{(1)}
$$

**Combine using chain rule:**

$$
\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{jk}^{(2)}} = -(t_k - y_k) \cdot y_k\bigl(1 - y_k\bigr) \cdot a_j^{(1)}
$$

**Define the output error signal:**

$$
\delta_k^{(2)} = (y_k - t_k) \cdot \sigma'\bigl(z_k^{(2)}\bigr) = (y_k - t_k) \cdot y_k\bigl(1 - y_k\bigr)
$$

> **Note:** The sign flip from $-(t_k - y_k)$ to $(y_k - t_k)$ is conventional; both are correct as long as the update rule's sign is consistent.

**Therefore:**

$$
\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{jk}^{(2)}} = \delta_k^{(2)} \cdot a_j^{(1)}
$$

**Weight update rule (Gradient Descent):**

$$
\boxed{\,w_{jk}^{(2)} \leftarrow w_{jk}^{(2)} - \eta \cdot \delta_k^{(2)} \cdot a_j^{(1)}\,}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Hidden-Layer Weight Update

We now compute $\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}^{(1)}}$. The chain here is **deeper** because the error at the output depends on the hidden activation, which depends on the hidden-layer weight.

$$
\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}^{(1)}} = \frac{\partial E}{\partial a_j^{(1)}} \cdot \frac{\partial a_j^{(1)}}{\partial z_j^{(1)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z_j^{(1)}}{\partial w_{ij}^{(1)}}
$$

**(a)** Compute $\frac{\partial E}{\partial a_j^{(1)}}$. Since $E$ depends on $a_j^{(1)}$ through *all* output neurons $k$:

$$
\frac{\partial E}{\partial a_j^{(1)}} = \sum_{k=1}^{p} \frac{\partial E}{\partial y_k} \cdot \frac{\partial y_k}{\partial z_k^{(2)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z_k^{(2)}}{\partial a_j^{(1)}}
$$

We already know $\frac{\partial E}{\partial y_k} = -(t_k - y_k)$ and $\frac{\partial y_k}{\partial z_k^{(2)}} = y_k(1 - y_k)$. Also:

$$
\frac{\partial z_k^{(2)}}{\partial a_j^{(1)}} = w_{jk}^{(2)}
$$

Thus:

$$
\frac{\partial E}{\partial a_j^{(1)}} = \sum_{k=1}^{p} -(t_k - y_k) \cdot y_k(1 - y_k) \cdot w_{jk}^{(2)} = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} \delta_k^{(2)} \cdot w_{jk}^{(2)}
$$

**(b)** Derivative of activation:

$$
\frac{\partial a_j^{(1)}}{\partial z_j^{(1)}} = \sigma'\bigl(z_j^{(1)}\bigr) = a_j^{(1)}\bigl(1 - a_j^{(1)}\bigr)
$$

**(c)** Derivative of net input w.r.t. weight:

$$
\frac{\partial z_j^{(1)}}{\partial w_{ij}^{(1)}} = x_i
$$

**Combine:**

$$
\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}^{(1)}} = -\left[\sum_{k=1}^{p} \delta_k^{(2)} \cdot w_{jk}^{(2)}\right] \cdot a_j^{(1)}\bigl(1 - a_j^{(1)}\bigr) \cdot x_i
$$

**Define the hidden error signal:**

$$
\delta_j^{(1)} = \left[\sum_{k=1}^{p} \delta_k^{(2)} \cdot w_{jk}^{(2)}\right] \cdot a_j^{(1)}\bigl(1 - a_j^{(1)}\bigr)
$$

**Final form:**

$$
\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}^{(1)}} = \delta_j^{(1)} \cdot x_i
$$

**Weight update rule:**

$$
\boxed{\,w_{ij}^{(1)} \leftarrow w_{ij}^{(1)} - \eta \cdot \delta_j^{(1)} \cdot x_i\,}
$$

---

### 3.5 Generalized Back Propagation Equations (Any Layer)

For an $L$-layer network, the algorithm generalizes to:

**Output layer ($l = L$):**
$$
\delta_k^{(L)} = (y_k - t_k) \cdot \sigma'\bigl(z_k^{(L)}\bigr)
$$

**Hidden layer ($l < L$):**
$$
\delta_j^{(l)} = \left[\sum_{k} w_{jk}^{(l+1)} \, \delta_k^{(l+1)}\right] \cdot \sigma'\bigl(z_j^{(l)}\bigr)
$$

**Weight update for all layers:**
$$
w_{ij}^{(l)} \leftarrow w_{ij}^{(l)} - \eta \cdot \delta_j^{(l)} \cdot a_i^{(l-1)}
$$

> **Key Insight:** Notice how $\delta^{(l+1)}$ is *reused* to compute $\delta^{(l)}$. This recursive structure is the essence of **back propagation** — the error "flows backward" from the output to the input.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Variants of Gradient Update

| Variant | Update Rule | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Batch GD** | $w \leftarrow w - \eta \cdot \nabla E_{full}$ | Stable, deterministic | Slow for large data |
| **Stochastic GD (SGD)** | $w \leftarrow w - \eta \cdot \nabla E_{sample}$ | Fast, escapes local minima | Noisy convergence |
| **Mini-batch GD** | $w \leftarrow w - \eta \cdot \nabla E_{batch}$ | Balance of both | Needs batch size tuning |
| **Momentum** | $v \leftarrow \beta v + \eta \nabla E$; $w \leftarrow w - v$ | Faster convergence | Extra hyperparameter $\beta$ |
| **Adam** | Adaptive moments (m, v) | Industry standard | More memory |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Back Propagation

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below illustrates the *complete training cycle* of a neural network using back propagation, mapped as a Mermaid flowchart. Each block represents a computational stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize Weights w and Biases b with small random values] --> B[Input Training Sample x and Target t]
    B --> C[FORWARD PASS: Compute Hidden Activations a_one using sigma z_one]
    C --> D[FORWARD PASS: Compute Output Activations y using sigma z_two]
    D --> E[Compute Loss E as half sum of t_k minus y_k squared]
    E --> F{Is Loss within tolerance or max epochs reached}
    F -->|No| G[BACKWARD PASS: Compute Output Delta two using y minus t times sigma prime z_two]
    G --> H[BACKWARD PASS: Propagate Delta one to hidden layer using w two transpose times delta two times sigma prime z_one]
    H --> I[Compute Gradients: dE by dw one equals delta one times x and dE by dw two equals delta two times a_one]
    I --> J[Update Weights: w new equals w old minus eta times gradient]
    J --> K[Update Biases: b new equals b old minus eta times delta]
    K --> C
    F -->|Yes| L[Return Trained Weights and Network is Ready for Inference]
```

### 4.2 Signal Flow — Forward vs. Backward

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FORWARD_PASS[FORWARD PROPAGATION DIRECTION]
        IN1[Input Layer x] --> HL1[Hidden Layer z1 and a1]
        HL1 --> OL1[Output Layer z2 and y]
    end
    subgraph BACKWARD_PASS[BACKWARD PROPAGATION DIRECTION]
        OL2[Output Error delta2] --> HL2[Hidden Error delta1]
        HL2 --> IN2[Input Gradients dE by dw1]
    end
    OL1 -. Error Signal .-> OL2
    IN1 -. Inputs for Gradient .-> IN2
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Map of Error Propagation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Target t_k] --> S2[Output y_k]
    S2 --> S3[Difference t_k minus y_k]
    S3 --> S4[Multiply by sigma prime z_k]
    S4 --> S5[Output Delta delta k superscript L]
    S5 --> S6[Multiply by weight w_jk superscript L plus 1]
    S6 --> S7[Sum across all output neurons k]
    S7 --> S8[Multiply by sigma prime z_j]
    S8 --> S9[Hidden Delta delta j superscript l]
    S9 --> S10[Multiply by previous activation a_i superscript l minus 1]
    S10 --> S11[Gradient dE by dw_ij superscript l]
    S11 --> S12[Apply learning rate eta and subtract from w]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The *orange path* traces how a single error at the output (caused by target $t_k$ and prediction $y_k$) propagates backward through the network, accumulating multiplicative factors at each layer, until it produces a weight update. This is the *recursive delta rule* in graphical form.
