## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam Model — Dec 2023, Q1, Module 3]**
>
> **Q1. Define the Back Propagation Algorithm. List its two main phases.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Back Propagation is a supervised learning algorithm used to train multi-layer feedforward neural networks by computing the gradient of the loss function with respect to each weight, using the chain rule of calculus. **(1.5 marks)**
>
> Its two main phases are:
> 1. **Forward Propagation:** Input is passed through the network layer-by-layer to compute activations and the final output. **(0.75 mark)**
> 2. **Backward Propagation:** The error at the output is propagated backward to compute gradients, and weights are updated using gradient descent. **(0.75 mark)**

> **[KTU University Exam Model — May 2024, Q2, Module 3]**
>
> **Q2. What is the role of the learning rate ($\eta$) in back propagation? What happens if it is too small or too large?**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The learning rate $\eta$ controls the **step size** of the weight update during gradient descent. **(1 mark)**
>
> - If $\eta$ is **too small**, convergence is very slow and the model may get stuck in local minima. **(1 mark)**
> - If $\eta$ is **too large**, the loss may oscillate or diverge, causing the network to fail to learn. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **Choose ONE of the following two questions:**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Derivation + Activation Functions**

> **[KTU University Exam Model — Dec 2023, Q9, Module 3]**

**(a)** Derive the weight update equation for a 2-layer feedforward neural network using the back propagation algorithm. Clearly show the forward pass, the output error signal, and the hidden-layer error signal. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the following activation functions used in back propagation, with their derivatives: **Sigmoid**, **Tanh**, and **ReLU**. Mention one advantage and one disadvantage of each. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Forward Pass (1 mark):**
- Hidden pre-activation: $z_j^{(1)} = \sum_i w_{ij}^{(1)} x_i + b_j^{(1)}$
- Hidden activation: $a_j^{(1)} = \sigma(z_j^{(1)})$
- Output pre-activation: $z_k^{(2)} = \sum_j w_{jk}^{(2)} a_j^{(1)} + b_k^{(2)}$
- Output: $y_k = \sigma(z_k^{(2)})$

**Step 2 — Loss (0.5 mark):**
$$
E = \frac{1}{2}\sum_k (t_k - y_k)^2
$$

**Step 3 — Output Layer Error Signal (2 marks):**
Using chain rule:
$$
\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{jk}^{(2)}} = \frac{\partial E}{\partial y_k} \cdot \frac{\partial y_k}{\partial z_k^{(2)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z_k^{(2)}}{\partial w_{jk}^{(2)}}
$$
- $\frac{\partial E}{\partial y_k} = -(t_k - y_k)$
- $\frac{\partial y_k}{\partial z_k^{(2)}} = y_k(1 - y_k)$
- $\frac{\partial z_k^{(2)}}{\partial w_{jk}^{(2)}} = a_j^{(1)}$

Defining $\delta_k^{(2)} = (y_k - t_k) \cdot y_k(1 - y_k)$:
$$
\boxed{w_{jk}^{(2)} \leftarrow w_{jk}^{(2)} - \eta \cdot \delta_k^{(2)} \cdot a_j^{(1)}}
$$

**Step 4 — Hidden Layer Error Signal (2.5 marks):**
$$
\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}^{(1)}} = \frac{\partial E}{\partial a_j^{(1)}} \cdot \frac{\partial a_j^{(1)}}{\partial z_j^{(1)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z_j^{(1)}}{\partial w_{ij}^{(1)}}
$$
- $\frac{\partial E}{\partial a_j^{(1)}} = -\sum_k \delta_k^{(2)} w_{jk}^{(2)}$
- $\frac{\partial a_j^{(1)}}{\partial z_j^{(1)}} = a_j^{(1)}(1 - a_j^{(1)})$
- $\frac{\partial z_j^{(1)}}{\partial w_{ij}^{(1)}} = x_i$

Defining $\delta_j^{(1)} = \bigl(\sum_k \delta_k^{(2)} w_{jk}^{(2)}\bigr) \cdot a_j^{(1)}(1 - a_j^{(1)})$:
$$
\boxed{w_{ij}^{(1)} \leftarrow w_{ij}^{(1)} - \eta \cdot \delta_j^{(1)} \cdot x_i}
$$

**Step 5 — Concluding remark (1 mark):** The recursive $\delta$ computation is what makes back propagation efficient — gradients for all weights are computed in a single forward + backward pass.

---

**Part (b) — Activation Functions (7 marks):**

| Function | Formula | Derivative | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sigmoid** | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | $\sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ | Smooth, probabilistic output | Vanishing gradient at extremes |
| **Tanh** | $\tanh(z) = \frac{e^z - e^{-z}}{e^z + e^{-z}}$ | $1 - \tanh^2(z)$ | Zero-centered (better than sigmoid) | Still suffers from vanishing gradient |
| **ReLU** | $\text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z)$ | $1$ if $z > 0$, else $0$ | Computationally cheap, no vanishing gradient for $z > 0$ | "Dying ReLU" problem (neurons stuck at 0) |

> **Mark split:** 1 mark per row + 1 mark for the "Dying ReLU" explanation + 1 mark for concluding that **ReLU is the default in modern deep networks**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Practical Aspects + Issues**

> **[KTU University Exam Model — May 2024, Q10, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **forward pass** and **backward pass** of the back propagation algorithm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **vanishing gradient** and **exploding gradient** problems in back propagation. How can they be addressed? **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks):**

- **(2 marks) Block Diagram:** A clear flowchart showing input $\rightarrow$ hidden layer $\rightarrow$ output layer, with separate arrows for forward signal and backward error signal.
- **(2 marks) Forward Pass:** Input is propagated through each layer using $a_j = \sigma(\sum_i w_{ij} x_i + b_j)$ until the output $y$ is produced. Loss $E$ is then computed.
- **(2 marks) Backward Pass:** Error signal $\delta$ is computed at the output, then propagated backwards layer by layer using the recursive formula $\delta_j^{(l)} = \bigl(\sum_k w_{jk}^{(l+1)} \delta_k^{(l+1)}\bigr) \cdot \sigma'(z_j^{(l)})$.
- **(1 mark) Weight Update:** Weights are adjusted as $w \leftarrow w - \eta \cdot \delta \cdot a$.

---

**Part (b) — Vanishing & Exploding Gradients (7 marks):**

**Vanishing Gradient Problem (2.5 marks):**
- When back propagating through many layers, gradients are multiplied by $\sigma'(z) \leq 0.25$ (for sigmoid) at each layer.
- After many layers, $\nabla w \to 0$, so early layers stop learning (**"gradient vanishes"**).
- Networks fail to converge; training stalls.

**Exploding Gradient Problem (2.5 marks):**
- Conversely, if weights are large, gradients can multiply to enormous values.
- Weights diverge, loss becomes NaN, training fails.

**Solutions (2 marks):**
1. **Use ReLU activation** — derivative is 1 for $z > 0$, preventing vanishing.
2. **Batch Normalization** — normalizes layer inputs to stabilize gradients.
3. **Gradient Clipping** — caps gradient norm to prevent explosion.
4. **Better initialization** — He initialization or Xavier initialization.
5. **Residual connections (ResNets)** — provide gradient highways in very deep networks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Delta:** A very common mistake is using $\delta_k = (t_k - y_k)$ instead of $(y_k - t_k)$, or forgetting to flip the sign in the weight update. The *update rule* must subtract the gradient — so if $\delta$ is defined as $(y - t)$, the update is $w \leftarrow w - \eta \delta a$. If $\delta$ is defined as $(t - y)$, the update becomes $w \leftarrow w + \eta \delta a$. **Be consistent!**
> 2. **Missing Sigmoid Derivative:** Many students write $\frac{\partial E}{\partial w}$ correctly for the output layer but forget to multiply by $\sigma'(z)$ when computing the hidden-layer delta. This is the single most common error — it costs **2 to 3 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing Activation with Pre-activation:** Be clear that $z$ is the *linear combination* (pre-activation) and $a = \sigma(z)$ is the *activation*. Mixing them up corrupts the entire derivation.
> 4. **Not Defining $\delta$:** Examiners expect you to explicitly define the error signal $\delta$ at each layer. Simply writing the final weight update equation without the intermediate $\delta$ loses marks.
> 5. **Forgetting Bias Updates:** The bias $b$ also has a gradient (equal to $\delta$). Examiners often give 1 mark just for mentioning the bias update.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize These Before the Exam**

- [x] **BPA = Forward Pass + Backward Pass + Weight Update.**
- [x] **Loss function (MSE):** $E = \frac{1}{2}\sum_k (t_k - y_k)^2$.
- [x] **Sigmoid derivative:** $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$ — used **everywhere** in delta calculations.
- [x] **Output delta:** $\delta_k^{(L)} = (y_k - t_k) \cdot \sigma'(z_k^{(L)})$.
- [x] **Hidden delta (recursive):** $\delta_j^{(l)} = \bigl(\sum_k w_{jk}^{(l+1)} \delta_k^{(l+1)}\bigr) \cdot \sigma'(z_j^{(l)})$.
- [x] **Weight gradient:** $\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}^{(l)}} = \delta_j^{(l)} \cdot a_i^{(l-1)}$.
- [x] **Bias gradient:** $\frac{\partial E}{\partial b_j^{(l)}} = \delta_j^{(l)}$.
- [x] **Update rule:** $w \leftarrow w - \eta \cdot \delta \cdot a$ and $b \leftarrow b - \eta \cdot \delta$.
- [x] **Learning rate ($\eta$):** Too small = slow / stuck; too large = oscillation / divergence.
- [x] **Chain rule** is the *mathematical core* of back propagation.
- [x] **Vanishing gradient** → solved by **ReLU**, **He init**, **BatchNorm**, **ResNets**.
- [x] **Exploding gradient** → solved by **gradient clipping**, **L2 regularization**, **smaller $\eta$**.
- [x] **BPA works only with differentiable activation functions.**
- [x] **Stochastic / Mini-batch / Batch GD** — all use the same BPA math; only the data subset differs.
- [x] **Generalization:** $\delta^{(L)}$ for output, recursive $\delta^{(l)}$ for hidden, then update $w$ and $b$.
- [x] **Computational efficiency:** BPA computes all gradients in $\mathcal{O}(W)$ time per sample, where $W$ is total weights.
- [x] **Examiner's tip:** Always state the *assumed network architecture* (number of layers, activation function, loss) before deriving.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unsupervised Learning - Clustering Methods - K-Means
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT501 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Unsupervised Learning - Clustering Methods - K-Means

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Unsupervised Learning: The Foundational Context

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme)**
> **Unsupervised Learning** is a paradigm of Machine Learning wherein the model is trained on an **unlabeled dataset** $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$ where $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$. The objective is to discover hidden patterns, intrinsic structures, or meaningful groupings within the data without any supervisory signal (no target labels $y$ are provided).

The three principal families of unsupervised learning tasks relevant to the **KTU Module 4** syllabus are:

1. **Clustering** — Grouping similar data points into $K$ partitions.
2. **Dimensionality Reduction** — Projecting high-dimensional data to a lower-dimensional manifold (e.g., PCA, t-SNE).
3. **Association Rule Mining** — Discovering interesting co-occurrence relationships (e.g., Apriori algorithm).

This study note focuses exclusively on the **partition-based clustering** technique known as **K-Means**.

---

### 1.2 What is Clustering?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clustering** is the process of partitioning a set of $n$ observations into $K$ disjoint groups called **clusters**, such that observations within the same cluster are highly **intra-similar** and observations across different clusters are highly **inter-dissimilar**.

Formally, given a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$, a clustering algorithm produces a partition:

$$C = \{C_1, C_2, \dots, C_K\} \quad \text{where} \quad \bigcup_{k=1}^{K} C_k = \mathcal{D} \quad \text{and} \quad C_i \cap C_j = \emptyset \;\; \forall \;\; i \neq j$$

---

### 1.3 K-Means Clustering — The Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition**
> **K-Means (MacQueen, 1967)** is an iterative, centroid-based, **partitional clustering algorithm** that aims to partition $n$ data points into $K$ pre-specified, non-overlapping clusters by minimizing the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)** — also called the **inertia** or **distortion** of the clustering.

The algorithm operates on the principle of **expectation–maximization-style** iterative refinement:

- **Step E (Expectation)**: Assign each data point to its **nearest centroid**.
- **Step M (Maximization)**: Recompute each centroid as the **arithmetic mean** of all points assigned to it.

This two-step alternation continues until a **convergence criterion** is satisfied (typically, no change in cluster assignments or centroids).

---

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Sorting" Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of K-Means like organizing a messy library.** 📚
>
> Imagine you walk into a library with **10,000 books scattered randomly on the floor** (your unlabeled data). The librarian tells you: *"Sort these into exactly $K = 5$ genres."* You don't know the genres in advance — you just know the books must end up in 5 piles.
>
> **How would you do it?**
> 1. **First Guess**: Randomly pick 5 books and place them on 5 separate tables. These are your initial **centroids** (representative books for each genre).
> 2. **Assign**: Now, walk through every book on the floor. For each book, look at the 5 tables, and place it on the table whose representative book looks *most similar* (closest in topic). This is the **E-step** (assignment).
> 3. **Update Tables**: After all books are placed, look at each table and find the *average topic* of all the books on it. Replace the representative book on that table with one that matches this average. This is the **M-step** (centroid update).
> 4. **Repeat**: Go through the floor again — some books might now be closer to a *different* table's representative. Reassign them.
> 5. **Stop**: When no book changes tables, you're done. The 5 piles are your **clusters**.
>
> **Key Insight**: You didn't need a pre-existing genre list. The algorithm **discovered the groupings** purely from the similarity structure of the books. K-Means works identically with data points in $\mathbb{R}^d$, using **Euclidean distance** as the similarity metric.

---

### 1.5 Key Terminology Matrix (Board-Exam Ready)

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|------|--------|------------|
| Number of Clusters | $K$ | User-specified hyperparameter defining the desired number of clusters |
| Centroid | $\mu_k$ | The mean vector of all points in cluster $C_k$ |
| Cluster Assignment | $c_i$ | The cluster index to which data point $x_i$ is assigned |
| Inertia / WCSS | $J$ | The objective function that K-Means minimizes |
| Feature Dimension | $d$ | Number of attributes describing each data point |
| Dataset Cardinality | $n$ | Total number of data points in the dataset |

---

### 1.6 Why K-Means? — Real-World Engineering Motivation

K-Means is one of the **most widely deployed** clustering algorithms in industry and academia due to:

- **Time Complexity**: $O(n \cdot K \cdot I \cdot d)$ where $I$ is the number of iterations — scales linearly with $n$.
- **Simplicity**: Only one hyperparameter ($K$) and intuitive geometric interpretation.
- **Engineering Applications**:
  - **Customer Segmentation** in marketing analytics.
  - **Image Compression** via vector quantization (color quantization).
  - **Document Clustering** for news article grouping.
  - **Anomaly Detection** as a by-product (points far from all centroids).
  - **Sensor Network Clustering** in IoT-based smart systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mathematical Objective Function

The K-Means algorithm seeks to find the partition $C = \{C_1, C_2, \dots, C_K\}$ and the corresponding centroid set $\mathcal{M} = \{\mu_1, \mu_2, \dots, \mu_K\}$ that **minimizes the Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)**:

$$J(C, \mathcal{M}) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert_2^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Observation**: The objective function $J$ is **non-convex** in $(C, \mathcal{M})$ jointly, which means K-Means is **guaranteed to converge to a local minimum**, not necessarily the global optimum. This is a **frequently tested conceptual point** in board examinations.

Where:
- $\Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert_2^2 = \sum_{j=1}^{d}(x_{ij} - \mu_{kj})^2$ is the **squared Euclidean distance** (L2 norm squared).
- $x_i \in C_k$ denotes that data point $x_i$ belongs to cluster $C_k$.

---

### 2.2 The Two-Step Iterative Update Rules

The K-Means algorithm alternates between two optimization steps that, when each is optimized independently, monotonically decrease $J$ (by the **Lloyd's descent lemma**).

#### Step 1 — Cluster Assignment (E-Step / Lloyd's First Foray)

Given fixed centroids $\mu_1, \dots, \mu_K$, assign each data point to the **nearest centroid**:

$$c_i = \arg\min_{k \in \{1, \dots, K\}} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert_2^2 \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

This is equivalent to a **Voronoi tessellation** of the feature space — each point falls into the Voronoi cell of its nearest centroid.

#### Step 2 — Centroid Update (M-Step / Lloyd's Second Foray)

Given fixed assignments $c_1, \dots, c_n$, recompute each centroid as the **arithmetic mean** of the points in its cluster:

$$\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i \quad \text{for } k = 1, 2, \dots, K$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the mean?** Setting $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mu_k} = 0$ yields $\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert}\sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$. The centroid that minimizes the sum of squared distances to a set of points is provably their **arithmetic mean**.

---

### 2.3 The Complete K-Means Algorithm (Lloyd's Algorithm)

**Input:** Dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, \dots, x_n\}$, number of clusters $K$, convergence threshold $\epsilon$.

**Output:** Final partition $C^*$ and centroids $\mathcal{M}^*$.

1. **Initialize** $K$ centroids $\{\mu_1^{(0)}, \dots, \mu_K^{(0)}\}$ (randomly or via **K-Means++**).
2. **Repeat** for $t = 0, 1, 2, \dots$ until convergence:
   - **(a) Assignment Step:** For each $x_i$, compute $c_i^{(t+1)} = \arg\min_k \Vert x_i - \mu_k^{(t)} \Vert_2^2$.
   - **(b) Update Step:** For each $k$, compute $\mu_k^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_k^{(t+1)} \vert}\sum_{x_i \in C_k^{(t+1)}} x_i$.
   - **(c) Convergence Check:** If $\max_k \Vert \mu_k^{(t+1)} - \mu_k^{(t)} \Vert_2 < \epsilon$ or $J$ plateaus, **stop**.
3. **Return** $(C^*, \mathcal{M}^*)$.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **board-exam-ready reference**. Memorize these equations thoroughly.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Purpose / Usage |
|---|------------------|------------------------|----------------|
| 1 | WCSS Objective Function | $J = \sum_{k=1}^{K}\sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert_2^2$ | Function that K-Means minimizes |
| 2 | Assignment Rule | $c_i = \arg\min_k \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert_2^2$ | Assigns each point to nearest centroid |
| 3 | Centroid Update Rule | $\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert}\sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$ | Updates centroid to cluster mean |
| 4 | Squared Euclidean Distance | $d(x_i, \mu_k)^2 = \sum_{j=1}^{d}(x_{ij} - \mu_{kj})^2$ | Default similarity metric in K-Means |
| 5 | Total Cluster Cardinality | $\sum_{k=1}^{K} \vert C_k \vert = n$ | Sum of all cluster sizes equals dataset size |
| 6 | Convergence Condition | $\Vert \mu_k^{(t+1)} - \mu_k^{(t)} \Vert_2 \leq \epsilon$ | Stopping criterion based on centroid shift |
| 7 | Time Complexity | $O(n \cdot K \cdot I \cdot d)$ | $I$ = iterations until convergence |
| 8 | Space Complexity | $O(n \cdot d + K \cdot d)$ | Stores dataset and $K$ centroids |
| 9 | Elbow Method — Inertia | $J(K) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert_2^2$ | Plot $J(K)$ vs $K$; optimal $K$ at "elbow" |
| 10 | Silhouette Score | $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$ | $a(i)$ = mean intra-cluster distance, $b(i)$ = mean nearest-cluster distance |
| 11 | K-Means++ Probability | $P(x_i) = \frac{D(x_i)^2}{\sum_{x_j} D(x_j)^2$ | Probability proportional to squared distance from nearest existing centroid |
| 12 | Cluster Compactness | $\text{Compactness}(C_k) = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert}\sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert_2^2$ | Average intra-cluster squared distance |

---

### 2.5 K-Means++ Initialization — A Critical Enhancement

> [!TIP]
> **Why K-Means++ matters for the board exam:**
> Naive random initialization can lead to **poor local minima**, empty clusters, or slow convergence. **K-Means++** (Arthur & Vassilvitskii, 2007) addresses this by seeding the initial centroids in a **diversity-promoting** probabilistic manner.

**Algorithm:**
1. Choose the first centroid $\mu_1$ uniformly at random from $\mathcal{D}$.
2. For each remaining point $x_i$, compute $D(x_i) = \min_{j < k} \Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert_2$ (distance to nearest already-chosen centroid).
3. Select the next centroid $\mu_k$ with probability proportional to $D(x_i)^2$.
4. Repeat steps 2–3 until $K$ centroids are chosen.
5. Proceed with standard Lloyd's iterations.

**Theoretical Guarantee:** K-Means++ achieves an expected WCSS within $O(\log K)$ of the optimal solution — a **strong approximation guarantee**.

---

### 2.6 Choosing the Optimal K — The Elbow Method

Since $K$ is a user-specified hyperparameter, we need a heuristic to choose it. The **Elbow Method** plots $J(K)$ (WCSS) against $K$:

- As $K$ increases, $J(K)$ **monotonically decreases** (more clusters → tighter fit).
- At some $K$, the rate of decrease sharply slows, forming an **"elbow"**.
- The optimal $K$ is at this elbow point, representing the best **bias-variance trade-off** in the clustering.

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative Metrics for Choosing K:**
> - **Silhouette Score** $s(i) \in [-1, 1]$: Higher is better; $s(i) \approx 1$ means well-clustered.
> - **Davies-Bouldin Index**: Lower is better; measures average cluster similarity.
> - **Gap Statistic**: Compares $J(K)$ to its expectation under a null reference distribution.

---

### 2.7 Engineering Utility Summary

| Domain | K-Means Application |
|--------|---------------------|
| **Telecommunications** | Cell tower traffic pattern clustering for load balancing |
| **Medical Imaging** | Tumor segmentation in MRI/CT scans via pixel clustering |
| **Recommender Systems** | User segmentation for collaborative filtering |
| **Smart Agriculture** | Sensor data clustering for crop health zone identification |
| **Cybersecurity** | Network intrusion detection via anomalous traffic clusters |
| **Bioinformatics** | Gene expression profile clustering for disease subtype discovery |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why the Centroid is the Arithmetic Mean

**Statement:** Given a fixed cluster assignment $C_k$, the centroid $\mu_k$ that minimizes $\sum_{x_i \in C_k}\Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert_2^2$ is the arithmetic mean $\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert}\sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$.

**Proof (Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation):**

**Step 1: Write the objective for a single cluster.**

$$J_k(\mu_k) = \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert_2^2 = \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \sum_{j=1}^{d} (x_{ij} - \mu_{kj})^2$$

*Logic: We expand the squared L2 norm as a sum of squares across all $d$ feature dimensions and all $\vert C_k \vert$ points in cluster $k$.*

**Step 2: Take the partial derivative with respect to $\mu_{kj}$ (the $j$-th coordinate of the centroid).**

$$\frac{\partial J_k}{\partial \mu_{kj}} = \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \frac{\partial}{\partial \mu_{kj}} \sum_{j'=1}^{d}(x_{ij'} - \mu_{kj'})^2$$

*Logic: Since the squared norm is separable across dimensions, only the term with $j' = j$ survives the derivative.*

**Step 3: Apply the chain rule.**

$$\frac{\partial J_k}{\partial \mu_{kj}} = \sum_{x_i \in C_k} 2(x_{ij} - \mu_{kj}) \cdot (-1) = -2\sum_{x_i \in C_k}(x_{ij} - \mu_{kj})$$

*Logic: The derivative of $(x_{ij} - \mu_{kj})^2$ with respect to $\mu_{kj}$ is $2(x_{ij} - \mu_{kj}) \cdot (-1) = -2(x_{ij} - \mu_{kj})$.*

**Step 4: Set the derivative to zero to find the critical point (first-order optimality condition).**

$$-2\sum_{x_i \in C_k}(x_{ij} - \mu_{kj}) = 0 \implies \sum_{x_i \in C_k}(x_{ij} - \mu_{kj}) = 0$$

*Logic: Setting the gradient to zero is necessary for a minimum (the second derivative $2 > 0$ confirms it's a minimum, not a maximum).*

**Step 5: Distribute the sum and solve for $\mu_{kj}$.**

$$\sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_{ij} - \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \mu_{kj} = 0 \implies \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_{ij} = \vert C_k \vert \cdot \mu_{kj}$$

$$\therefore \quad \mu_{kj} = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_{ij}$$

*Logic: The second term simplifies because $\mu_{kj}$ is a constant with respect to the summation index $i$, so it appears $\vert C_k \vert$ times.*

**Step 6: State the result in vector form.**

$$\boxed{\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i}$$

*Logic: Since this holds for every coordinate $j \in \{1, \dots, d\}$ independently, the result generalizes to the full $d$-dimensional centroid vector.* $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: K-Means by Hand (KTU Board Favorite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **classic KTU board question pattern**: *"Apply K-Means clustering to the given 2D dataset for $K=2$ and show two iterations."*

**Dataset:** $\mathcal{D} = \{(1,1), (1,2), (2,1), (8,8), (9,9), (8,9)\}$, $K = 2$.

**Initial Centroids (arbitrary):** $\mu_1^{(0)} = (1,1)$, $\mu_2^{(0)} = (8,8)$.

---

#### Iteration 1

**Step 1.1: Compute Euclidean distance from each point to both centroids.**

| Point $x_i$ | $d(x_i, \mu_1^{(0)})$ | $d(x_i, \mu_2^{(0)})$ | Assignment $c_i$ |
|-------------|----------------------|----------------------|------------------|
| $(1,1)$ | $0$ | $\sqrt{98} \approx 9.90$ | $C_1$ |
| $(1,2)$ | $1$ | $\sqrt{85} \approx 9.22$ | $C_1$ |
| $(2,1)$ | $1$ | $\sqrt{85} \approx 9.22$ | $C_1$ |
| $(8,8)$ | $\sqrt{98} \approx 9.90$ | $0$ | $C_2$ |
| $(9,9)$ | $\sqrt{128} \approx 11.31$ | $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$ | $C_2$ |
| $(8,9)$ | $\sqrt{113} \approx 10.63$ | $1$ | $C_2$ |

*Logic: We compute $\sqrt{(x_{i1}-\mu_{k1})^2 + (x_{i2}-\mu_{k2})^2}$ for each point-centroid pair. The point goes to the cluster with the smaller distance.*

**Step 1.2: Update centroids by computing cluster means.**

$$C_1 = \{(1,1), (1,2), (2,1)\} \implies \mu_1^{(1)} = \left(\frac{1+1+2}{3}, \frac{1+2+1}{3}\right) = \left(\frac{4}{3}, \frac{4}{3}\right) \approx (1.33, 1.33)$$

$$C_2 = \{(8,8), (9,9), (8,9)\} \implies \mu_2^{(1)} = \left(\frac{8+9+8}{3}, \frac{8+9+9}{3}\right) = \left(\frac{25}{3}, \frac{26}{3}\right) \approx (8.33, 8.67)$$

*Logic: Apply the formula $\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert}\sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$ for each cluster.*

**Step 1.3: Compute WCSS for monitoring convergence.**

$$J^{(1)} = \Vert(1,1)-(1.33,1.33)\Vert^2 + \Vert(1,2)-(1.33,1.33)\Vert^2 + \Vert(2,1)-(1.33,1.33)\Vert^2 + \Vert(8,8)-(8.33,8.67)\Vert^2 + \dots$$

$$J^{(1)} \approx 0.22 + 0.45 + 0.45 + 0.45 + 0.79 + 0.34 = 2.70$$

---

#### Iteration 2

**Step 2.1: Recompute distances with updated centroids.**

| Point $x_i$ | $d(x_i, \mu_1^{(1)})$ | $d(x_i, \mu_2^{(1)})$ | Assignment $c_i$ |
|-------------|----------------------|----------------------|------------------|
| $(1,1)$ | $\sqrt{0.11} \approx 0.33$ | $\sqrt{116.11} \approx 10.78$ | $C_1$ |
| $(1,2)$ | $\sqrt{0.45} \approx 0.67$ | $\sqrt{109.78} \approx 10.48$ | $C_1$ |
| $(2,1)$ | $\sqrt{0.45} \approx 0.67$ | $\sqrt{109.78} \approx 10.48$ | $C_1$ |
| $(8,8)$ | $\sqrt{87.78} \approx 9.37$ | $\sqrt{0.45} \approx 0.67$ | $C_2$ |
| $(9,9)$ | $\sqrt{105.11} \approx 10.25$ | $\sqrt{0.79} \approx 0.89$ | $C_2$ |
| $(8,9)$ | $\sqrt{93.78} \approx 9.68$ | $\sqrt{0.45} \approx 0.67$ | $C_2$ |

*Logic: Same distance calculation but with the new centroids. Assignments remain unchanged from Iteration 1.*

**Step 2.2: Centroids remain the same** (since assignments didn't change, means are identical).

$$\mu_1^{(2)} = \mu_1^{(1)} = (1.33, 1.33), \quad \mu_2^{(2)} = \mu_2^{(1)} = (8.33, 8.67)$$

**Step 2.3: Convergence detected.** The algorithm **terminates** with $J^{(2)} = J^{(1)} = 2.70$.

**Final Partition:**
$$C_1^* = \{(1,1), (1,2), (2,1)\}, \quad C_2^* = \{(8,8), (9,9), (8,9)\}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — K-Means Variants Comparison

| Algorithm | Initialization | Convergence Guarantee | Empty Cluster Handling | Best Use Case |
|-----------|---------------|----------------------|----------------------|---------------|
| **Lloyd's K-Means** | Random | Local minimum | May produce empty clusters | General-purpose, large $n$ |
| **K-Means++** | Probabilistic (D²) | $O(\log K)$ approx. of optimum | Resampling | Default choice in scikit-learn |
| **Mini-Batch K-Means** | K-Means++ | Approximate, faster | Resampling | Very large $n$ (online setting) |
| **K-Medoids (PAM)** | Random | Local minimum | Robust to outliers | Small $n$, non-Euclidean metrics |
| **Fuzzy C-Means** | Random | Local min of fuzzy objective | Soft assignments | Overlapping cluster boundaries |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Complete K-Means Algorithm Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([START]) --> inputNode[/Receive Dataset D and K/]
    inputNode --> initNode{Initialize Centroids}
    initNode -->|Option A: Random| randInit[Pick K random points as mu1 to muK]
    initNode -->|Option B: K-Means++| kppInit[Sample first centroid uniformly, then sample others with D-squared probability]
    randInit --> loopStart
    kppInit --> loopStart
    loopStart[/Enter Iteration t/]
    loopStart --> assignStep[ASSIGNMENT STEP: For each xi, compute c_i = argmin_k of distance from xi to mu_k]
    assignStep --> emptyCheck{Any Empty Cluster?}
    emptyCheck -->|Yes| reinitEmpty[Reinitialize that centroid to the point farthest from any existing centroid]
    emptyCheck -->|No| updateStep
    reinitEmpty --> updateStep[UPDATE STEP: For each k, compute mu_k as mean of points in C_k]
    updateStep --> compWCSS[Compute WCSS J for this iteration]
    compWCSS --> convCheck{Convergence Check: Did centroids change less than epsilon OR did J plateau}
    convCheck -->|No| loopStart
    convCheck -->|Yes| finalOutput[/Output final clusters C* and centroids mu*/]
    finalOutput --> endNode([END])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide for KTU Board Answers:**
> - The **outer loop** alternates between two optimization sub-steps (Assignment and Update).
> - The **empty cluster check** is a critical practical safeguard — K-Means can produce empty clusters if all points are farther from a centroid.
> - The **convergence check** typically uses a small threshold $\epsilon \in [10^{-4}, 10^{-6}]$ on centroid displacement.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: K-Means Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph preprocessing [Preprocessing Stage]
        rawData[Raw Unlabeled Data X] --> norm[Feature Normalization StandardScaler or MinMax]
        norm --> dimRed{Dimensionality Reduction Needed?}
        dimRed -->|Yes n large| pcaStep[Apply PCA to reduce d]
        dimRed -->|No| algoCore
        pcaStep --> algoCore
    end
    subgraph algoCore [K-Means Core Engine]
        hpInput[Hyperparameter K] --> kpp[K-Means Plus Plus Initialization]
        rng[Random Seed for Reproducibility] --> kpp
        kpp --> lloydIter[Lloyd Iteration Loop]
        lloydIter --> eStep[Vectorized Distance Computation n by K matrix]
        eStep --> mStep[Mean Update for each of K centroids]
        mStep --> convMon[Convergence Monitor inertia and centroid shift]
        convMon -->|Not Converged| lloydIter
        convMon -->|Converged| outputStage
    end
    subgraph outputStage [Post Processing and Evaluation]
        labels[Cluster Labels c1 to cn] --> inertia[Inertia J WCSS]
        labels --> silh[Silhouette Score]
        labels --> vis[Visualization t-SNE or PCA scatter plot with cluster colors]
        inertia --> elbowPlot[Elbow Curve J versus K]
        silh --> elbowPlot
    end
    preprocessing --> algoCore
    algoCore --> outputStage
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Normalization Matters:** K-Means uses Euclidean distance, which is **scale-sensitive**. A feature measured in thousands (e.g., income in ₹) will dominate a feature measured in units (e.g., age in years). Always apply **StandardScaler** ($z = (x - \mu)/\sigma$) or **MinMaxScaler** before K-Means.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Voronoi Tessellation Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    space[Feature Space R to the d] --> v1[Voronoi Cell 1 around mu1]
    space --> v2[Voronoi Cell 2 around mu2]
    space --> v3[Voronoi Cell 3 around mu3]
    space --> v4[Voronoi Cell K around muK]
    v1 --> bound1[Boundary where d x to mu1 equals d x to mu2]
    v2 --> bound1
    v2 --> bound2[Boundary where d x to mu2 equals d x to mu3]
    v3 --> bound2
    v3 --> bound3[Boundary where d x to mu3 equals d x to muK]
    v4 --> bound3
    bound1 --> assign[Assignment Rule: x belongs to cluster k if x is in Voronoi Cell k]
    bound2 --> assign
    bound3 --> assign
```

> [!TIP]
> **Geometric Intuition:** Each centroid "owns" a **Voronoi cell** — the set of all points closer to it than to any other centroid. The boundaries between cells are equidistant **perpendicular bisectors** between centroid pairs. K-Means literally partitions the feature space into these Voronoi cells.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, Part A consists of short-answer questions testing **Remember** and **Understand** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy. Answers should be concise (3–4 sentences maximum).

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] (3 Marks)**
**Define K-Means clustering. State the objective function it minimizes.**

**Model Answer:**
K-Means is an iterative, unsupervised, partition-based clustering algorithm that groups $n$ unlabeled data points into $K$ pre-specified clusters by minimizing the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)**, defined as:

$$J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert_2^2$$

where $\mu_k$ is the centroid (mean) of cluster $C_k$. The algorithm alternates between assigning points to the nearest centroid and recomputing centroids as cluster means until convergence.

*(Examiner's Note: A complete answer requires the formal objective function. Award 2 marks for the definition and 1 mark for the equation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — December 2023] (3 Marks)**
**What is the Elbow Method? How is it used to determine the optimal number of clusters in K-Means?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Elbow Method** is a heuristic for selecting the optimal $K$ in K-Means clustering. It involves:

1. Running K-Means for a range of $K$ values (e.g., $K = 1, 2, \dots, 15$).
2. Computing the WCSS (inertia) $J(K)$ for each $K$.
3. Plotting $J(K)$ versus $K$.

As $K$ increases, $J(K)$ decreases monotonically. The optimal $K$ is at the **"elbow point"** — the value of $K$ after which the rate of decrease sharply diminishes, indicating diminishing returns from adding more clusters. This point balances **model fit** (low $J$) against **model complexity** (low $K$).

*(Examiner's Note: Mentioning the trade-off between underfitting and overfitting earns full marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B questions are **long-answer, application-level** problems testing **Apply**, **Analyze**, and **Evaluate** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy. Each question has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the K-Means clustering algorithm in detail. Clearly state the objective function, the two iterative steps, and the convergence criterion. Mention any **two limitations** of the algorithm. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The K-Means algorithm partitions $n$ data points into $K$ clusters by minimizing the **WCSS objective**:

$$J = \sum_{k=1}^{K}\sum_{x_i \in C_k}\Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert_2^2$$

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Initialization:** Choose $K$ initial centroids $\{\mu_1^{(0)}, \dots, \mu_K^{(0)}\}$ randomly or via K-Means++.

2. **Assignment Step (E-Step):** For each data point $x_i$, assign it to the cluster with the nearest centroid:
$$c_i = \arg\min_{k \in \{1,\dots,K\}} \Vert x_i - \mu_k^{(t)} \Vert_2^2$$

3. **Update Step (M-Step):** Recompute each centroid as the mean of all points assigned to it:
$$\mu_k^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert}\sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$$

4. **Convergence Check:** Iterate steps 2–3 until $\max_k \Vert \mu_k^{(t+1)} - \mu_k^{(t)} \Vert_2 < \epsilon$ (typically $\epsilon = 10^{-4}$) or $J$ plateaus.

**Convergence Guarantee:** K-Means is guaranteed to converge in a finite number of iterations because $J$ is monotonically non-increasing and bounded below by 0. However, it converges to a **local minimum**, not necessarily global.

**Two Limitations:**
1. **Sensitivity to Initialization:** Different initial centroids can lead to different final clusters. K-Means++ mitigates this.
2. **Assumes Spherical, Equal-Sized Clusters:** K-Means uses Euclidean distance and implicitly assumes clusters are convex and isotropic. It performs poorly on elongated, irregular, or overlapping clusters.
3. **Requires Specifying $K$:** The user must predefine $K$, which is often unknown a priori.
4. **Sensitive to Outliers:** Outliers can drastically shift centroids due to the squared distance objective.

---

**(b)** Consider the dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(2,10), (2,6), (1,2), (5,8), (5,4), (10,1), (7,5), (8,9)\}$. Apply **K-Means clustering with $K = 3$** using initial centroids $\mu_1 = (2,10)$, $\mu_2 = (5,8)$, $\mu_3 = (1,2)$. Show **two complete iterations** and state the final cluster assignments. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Iteration 1:**

**Step 1: Distance computation and assignment.**

| Point $x_i$ | $d(x_i, \mu_1)$ | $d(x_i, \mu_2)$ | $d(x_i, \mu_3)$ | Cluster |
|-------------|----------------|----------------|----------------|---------|
| $(2,10)$ | $0$ | $\sqrt{13} \approx 3.61$ | $\sqrt{65} \approx 8.06$ | $C_1$ |
| $(2,6)$ | $4$ | $\sqrt{13} \approx 3.61$ | $\sqrt{17} \approx 4.12$ | $C_2$ |
| $(1,2)$ | $\sqrt{65} \approx 8.06$ | $\sqrt{52} \approx 7.21$ | $0$ | $C_3$ |
| $(5,8)$ | $\sqrt{13} \approx 3.61$ | $0$ | $\sqrt{52} \approx 7.21$ | $C_2$ |
| $(5,4)$ | $\sqrt{45} \approx 6.71$ | $\sqrt{16} = 4$ | $\sqrt{20} \approx 4.47$ | $C_2$ |
| $(10,1)$ | $\sqrt{145} \approx 12.04$ | $\sqrt{74} \approx 8.60$ | $\sqrt{82} \approx 9.06$ | $C_2$ |
| $(7,5)$ | $\sqrt{50} \approx 7.07$ | $\sqrt{9} = 3$ | $\sqrt{45} \approx 6.71$ | $C_2$ |
| $(8,9)$ | $\sqrt{37} \approx 6.08$ | $\sqrt{10} \approx 3.16$ | $\sqrt{98} \approx 9.90$ | $C_2$ |

*Clusters after Iteration 1:*
- $C_1 = \{(2,10)\}$
- $C_2 = \{(2,6), (5,8), (5,4), (10,1), (7,5), (8,9)\}$
- $C_3 = \{(1,2)\}$

**Step 2: Centroid updates.**

$$\mu_1^{(1)} = (2, 10)$$

$$\mu_2^{(1)} = \left(\frac{2+5+5+10+7+8}{6}, \frac{6+8+4+1+5+9}{6}\right) = \left(\frac{37}{6}, \frac{33}{6}\right) \approx (6.17, 5.50)$$

$$\mu_3^{(1)} = (1, 2)$$

---

**Iteration 2:**

**Step 1: Reassign points using updated centroids.**

| Point $x_i$ | $d(x_i, \mu_1^{(1)})$ | $d(x_i, \mu_2^{(1)})$ | $d(x_i, \mu_3^{(1)})$ | Cluster |
|-------------|----------------------|----------------------|----------------------|---------|
| $(2,10)$ | $0$ | $\sqrt{43.4} \approx 6.59$ | $\sqrt{65} \approx 8.06$ | $C_1$ |
| $(2,6)$ | $4$ | $\sqrt{20.4} \approx 4.52$ | $\sqrt{17} \approx 4.12$ | $C_1$ |
| $(1,2)$ | $\sqrt{65} \approx 8.06$ | $\sqrt{43.4} \approx 6.59$ | $0$ | $C_3$ |
| $(5,8)$ | $\sqrt{13} \approx 3.61$ | $\sqrt{6.8} \approx 2.61$ | $\sqrt{52} \approx 7.21$ | $C_2$ |
| $(5,4)$ | $\sqrt{45} \approx 6.71$ | $\sqrt{2.4} \approx 1.55$ | $\sqrt{20} \approx 4.47$ | $C_2$ |
| $(10,1)$ | $\sqrt{145} \approx 12.04$ | $\sqrt{30.1} \approx 5.49$ | $\sqrt{82} \approx 9.06$ | $C_2$ |
| $(7,5)$ | $\sqrt{50} \approx 7.07$ | $\sqrt{0.9} \approx 0.95$ | $\sqrt{45} \approx 6.71$ | $C_2$ |
| $(8,9)$ | $\sqrt{37} \approx 6.08$ | $\sqrt{12.4} \approx 3.52$ | $\sqrt{98} \approx 9.90$ | $C_2$ |

*Updated clusters after Iteration 2:*
- $C_1 = \{(2,10), (2,6)\}$
- $C_2 = \{(5,8), (5,4), (10,1), (7,5), (8,9)\}$
- $C_3 = \{(1,2)\}$

**Step 2: Centroid updates for Iteration 2.**

$$\mu_1^{(2)} = \left(\frac{2+2}{2}, \frac{10+6}{2}\right) = (2, 8)$$

$$\mu_2^{(2)} = \left(\frac{5+5+10+7+8}{5}, \frac{8+4+1+5+9}{5}\right) = (7, 5.4)$$

$$\mu_3^{(2)} = (1, 2)$$

*Note: The algorithm would continue to iterate; further iterations would show $(2,6)$ possibly migrating to $C_1$ (still closest) and assignments stabilizing. The point $(2,6)$ now has $d = \sqrt{(2-2)^2 + (6-8)^2} = 2$ to $\mu_1$ vs $d = \sqrt{25 + 0.16} \approx 5.01$ to $\mu_2$, so it stays in $C_1$.*

**Final Cluster Assignments (after 2 iterations):**
- $C_1^* = \{(2,10), (2,6)\}$ with centroid $(2, 8)$
- $C_2^* = \{(5,8), (5,4), (10,1), (7,5), (8,9)\}$ with centroid $(7, 5.4)$
- $C_3^* = \{(1,2)\}$ with centroid $(1, 2)$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Discuss **five major limitations** of the K-Means algorithm and explain the techniques used to overcome each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| # | Limitation | Explanation | Overcoming Technique |
|---|-----------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Manual specification of $K$** | The number of clusters must be predefined; choosing wrong $K$ yields poor results | **Elbow Method**, **Silhouette Score**, **Gap Statistic** for automatic $K$ selection |
| 2 | **Sensitivity to initialization** | Random initialization can trap the algorithm in poor local minima or produce empty clusters | **K-Means++** initialization (D²-weighted probabilistic seeding) with $O(\log K)$ approximation guarantee |
| 3 | **Assumes isotropic, spherical clusters** | The Euclidean distance metric implicitly assumes clusters are convex and equally sized; fails on elongated or irregular shapes | Use **GMM (Gaussian Mixture Models)**, **DBSCAN**, or **Spectral Clustering** for non-spherical clusters |
| 4 | **Sensitivity to outliers** | Squared distance amplifies the influence of outliers, distorting centroids | Use **K-Medoids (PAM)** which uses actual data points as centroids, or apply **outlier removal** preprocessing |
| 5 | **Scale sensitivity** | Features with larger numerical ranges dominate the distance computation | **Standardization** (z-score normalization) or **Min-Max scaling** before clustering |
| 6 | **Linear separability assumption** | Cannot discover clusters with complex non-linear boundaries | **Kernel K-Means** (maps to higher-dimensional space) or **Spectral Clustering** |
| 7 | **Empty cluster problem** | A centroid may end up with no assigned points, leaving it "stranded" | **K-Means++ reseeding** or relocating empty centroids to the point farthest from any existing centroid |

---

**(b)** Compare and contrast **K-Means clustering** with **Hierarchical (Agglomerative) clustering** across at least **five dimensions**. State one scenario where each is preferred. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Dimension | K-Means | Hierarchical (Agglomerative) |
|-----------|---------|------------------------------|
| **Cluster Shape** | Assumes spherical, isotropic clusters | Can discover arbitrary-shaped clusters |
| **Number of Clusters $K$** | Must be specified in advance | Determined post-hoc via dendrogram cutting |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(n \cdot K \cdot I \cdot d)$ — fast, linear in $n$ | $O(n^3)$ naive, $O(n^2 \log n)$ with heuristics — slow for large $n$ |
| **Scalability** | Highly scalable to millions of points (Mini-Batch variant) | Limited to $\sim 10,000$ points due to quadratic memory |
| **Output Type** | Flat partition (single level) | Dendrogram (hierarchy of nested clusters at all granularities) |
| **Determinism** | Non-deterministic (depends on initialization) | Deterministic (no random initialization) |
| **Distance Metric** | Almost always Euclidean | Flexible: single, complete, average, Ward linkage |
| **Cluster Size Sensitivity** | Sensitive to outliers and scale | Less sensitive to outliers (especially with average linkage) |
| **Interpretability** | Centroid-based, intuitive | Dendrogram provides rich multi-level structure |

**Preferred Scenarios:**
- **K-Means is preferred for**: Large-scale customer segmentation of millions of users in e-commerce platforms where speed matters and clusters are roughly spherical.
- **Hierarchical is preferred for**: Gene expression analysis in bioinformatics where the **dendrogram** reveals evolutionary/taxonomic relationships at multiple levels of granularity.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in K-Means Questions:**
>
> 1. **❌ Forgetting the squaring in WCSS:** Students often write $\sum \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert$ instead of $\sum \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$. The **squared** Euclidean distance is essential. *(−1 mark)*
>
> 2. **❌ Confusing $K$-Means with $K$-Nearest Neighbors (KNN):** These are **completely different algorithms**. K-Means is **unsupervised clustering**; KNN is **supervised classification**. This is a **fatal conceptual error** in board exams. *(−2 to −3 marks)*
>
> 3. **❌ Stating K-Means guarantees global optimum:** The objective $J$ is **non-convex**; K-Means converges to a **local minimum only**. Always qualify this statement. *(−1 mark)*
>
> 4. **❌ Skipping the iteration tables in numerical problems:** The board examiner **must see** a clear distance-computation table for every iteration. Hand-waving "and the points are assigned" loses 2–3 marks. *(−2 to −3 marks)*
>
> 5. **❌ Not applying standardization in practical questions:** If a question gives features with different units (e.g., age vs. salary), failure to mention **feature scaling** as a preprocessing step is a mark-losing omission in application-level questions. *(−1 mark)*
>
> 6. **❌ Using $|$ inside LaTeX tables:** Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in answer sheets to avoid broken rendering of absolute value notation. *(Presentation mark deduction)*

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam!**

**Core Concepts:**
- ✅ K-Means is an **unsupervised, iterative, partition-based** clustering algorithm.
- ✅ **Objective**: Minimize $J = \sum_{k=1}^{K}\sum_{x_i \in C_k}\Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert_2^2$ (WCSS/Inertia).
- ✅ **Two-Step Iteration**: (1) Assign to nearest centroid, (2) Update centroid as cluster mean.
- ✅ **Convergence**: Centroid displacement below threshold $\epsilon$ or $J$ plateaus.
- ✅ K-Means converges to a **local minimum** (not global); $J$ is non-convex.

**Critical Formulas:**
- ✅ Assignment: $c_i = \arg\min_k \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert_2^2$
- ✅ Centroid Update: $\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert}\sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$
- ✅ Complexity: $O(n \cdot K \cdot I \cdot d)$ time, $O(nd + Kd)$ space
- ✅ K-Means++ probability: $P(x_i) \propto D(x_i)^2$

**Choosing $K$:**
- ✅ **Elbow Method**: Plot $J(K)$ vs $K$; choose the "elbow".
- ✅ **Silhouette Score**: $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}} \in [-1, 1]$.
- ✅ **Gap Statistic**: Compare to null distribution.

**Practical Engineering Knowledge:**
- ✅ Always **standardize features** before applying K-Means.
- ✅ Use **K-Means++** for initialization in practice.
- ✅ K-Means fails on **non-spherical clusters** → use GMM, DBSCAN, or Spectral Clustering.
- ✅ K-Means is **sensitive to outliers** → use K-Medoids for robustness.
- ✅ For **large-scale data**, use **Mini-Batch K-Means**.

**Common Exam One-Liners to Memorize:**
- ✅ "K-Means minimizes the within-cluster sum of squared Euclidean distances."
- ✅ "The algorithm alternates between assignment and update steps (Lloyd's algorithm)."
- ✅ "K-Means guarantees convergence to a local minimum in finite iterations."
- ✅ "The elbow point in the $J$ vs $K$ plot gives the optimal number of clusters."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Expectation-Maximization Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT501 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Expectation-Maximization Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Expectation-Maximization (EM) Algorithm** is an iterative numerical optimization method used to find **Maximum Likelihood Estimates (MLE)** or **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)** estimates of parameters in statistical models that involve **latent (hidden) variables** or **incomplete/missing data**.

Mathematically, given observed data $X$, hidden variables $Z$, and model parameters $\theta$, the EM algorithm iteratively maximizes the following **auxiliary function (Q-function)**:

$$Q(\theta, \theta^{(t)}) = \mathbb{E}_{Z \mid X, \theta^{(t)}} \left[ \log P(X, Z \mid \theta) \right]$$

where $\theta^{(t)}$ represents the current parameter estimate at iteration $t$, and the goal is to monotonically increase the observed-data log-likelihood $\ell(\theta) = \log P(X \mid \theta)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 4):** The EM algorithm is the foundational unsupervised learning tool for **probabilistic latent variable models**, especially **Gaussian Mixture Models (GMMs)**, **Hidden Markov Models (HMMs)**, and density estimation tasks where direct MLE is analytically intractable.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two Coins Puzzle"

Imagine you are given **two biased coins A** and **B**, but you are not told which coin was tossed each time. You only see the sequence of outcomes (Heads/Tails), say `H, T, T, H, H, T, ...`. You want to estimate the bias (probability of Heads) of both coins.

You guess initial biases: $\theta_A = 0.6$, $\theta_B = 0.5$.

- **Step 1 (Guess which coin was used):** Using your current bias estimates, compute the *probability* that each flip came from coin A vs. coin B. This is the **soft assignment** — you don't decide, you *expect*.
- **Step 2 (Recompute biases from expected counts):** Use the expected counts (not actual counts, since you don't know the truth) to recalculate the biases of each coin.

Repeat the two steps; the biases converge to the best possible answer given the ambiguity. This is exactly the EM spirit: **Guess → Expect → Maximize → Repeat**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not use MLE directly?** If we knew which coin was used for each toss, MLE would be trivial (just count Heads/Tails per coin). But because the coin assignment is **hidden**, summing over all possibilities creates an intractable log of sums. EM cleverly **avoids this by iterating**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Table

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Observed Data ($X$)** | Data we can measure directly |
| **Latent Variable ($Z$)** | Hidden/unobserved variable (e.g., cluster membership) |
| **Complete Data Log-Likelihood** | $\log P(X, Z \mid \theta)$ — likelihood if $Z$ were known |
| **Observed Data Log-Likelihood** | $\log P(X \mid \theta) = \log \sum_Z P(X, Z \mid \theta)$ |
| **E-Step** | Compute expected value of complete-data log-likelihood under $P(Z \mid X, \theta^{(t)})$ |
| **M-Step** | Maximize this expectation w.r.t. $\theta$ to obtain $\theta^{(t+1)}$ |
| **ELBO** | Evidence Lower Bound — quantity guaranteed to increase each iteration |
| **Convergence** | When $\vert \ell(\theta^{(t+1)}) - \ell(\theta^{(t)}) \vert < \epsilon$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Problem: Why EM is Needed

For many probabilistic models, the observed-data log-likelihood has the form:

$$\ell(\theta) = \log P(X \mid \theta) = \log \sum_{Z} P(X, Z \mid \theta)$$

The **logarithm outside the summation** prevents analytic solution because:

$$\log \sum_Z P(X, Z \mid \theta) \neq \sum_Z \log P(X, Z \mid \theta)$$

This is the fundamental obstacle. EM solves it by **alternating between two simpler sub-problems**.

## 2.2 The Two Iterative Steps — Structured Breakdown

### **E-Step (Expectation Step)**

**Goal:** Construct a lower bound on the log-likelihood that touches it at the current parameter estimate.

**Action:** Compute the posterior distribution of the latent variables given the current parameters:

$$P(Z \mid X, \theta^{(t)}) = \frac{P(X, Z \mid \theta^{(t)})}{P(X \mid \theta^{(t)})}$$

Then form the Q-function (expected complete-data log-likelihood):

$$Q(\theta, \theta^{(t)}) = \sum_{Z} P(Z \mid X, \theta^{(t)}) \, \log P(X, Z \mid \theta)$$

> **Intuition:** "Given what I currently believe, what do I expect the hidden variable to look like on average?"

### **M-Step (Maximization Step)**

**Goal:** Maximize the Q-function with respect to $\theta$.

**Action:**

$$\theta^{(t+1)} = \arg\max_{\theta} \, Q(\theta, \theta^{(t)})$$

> **Intuition:** "Assuming my expectation of hidden variables is correct, what parameters best explain the data?"

## 2.3 The Monotonic Improvement Guarantee

A beautiful property of EM is the following inequality, proven via **Jensen's Inequality**:

$$\ell(\theta^{(t+1)}) \geq \ell(\theta^{(t)})$$

The observed log-likelihood **never decreases** across iterations. This is why EM is guaranteed to converge to at least a **local maximum** (or saddle point).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description |
|---|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | $\ell(\theta) = \log P(X \mid \theta)$ | Observed-data log-likelihood (the objective) |
| 2 | $P(Z \mid X, \theta^{(t)}) = \dfrac{P(X,Z \mid \theta^{(t)})}{\sum_{Z'} P(X,Z' \mid \theta^{(t)})}$ | Posterior over latent variables (E-step) |
| 3 | $Q(\theta, \theta^{(t)}) = \mathbb{E}_{Z \mid X, \theta^{(t)}}[\log P(X, Z \mid \theta)]$ | Auxiliary Q-function (expectation of complete log-likelihood) |
| 4 | $\theta^{(t+1)} = \arg\max_{\theta} Q(\theta, \theta^{(t)})$ | M-step maximization |
| 5 | $\text{ELBO}(\theta, \theta^{(t)}) = Q(\theta, \theta^{(t)}) - \text{KL}\big(q(Z) \,\Vert\, P(Z\mid X,\theta^{(t)})\big)$ | Evidence Lower Bound |
| 6 | $\log P(X \mid \theta) \geq \mathbb{E}_{Q}[\log P(X, Z \mid \theta)] - \mathbb{E}_{Q}[\log Q(Z)]$ | ELBO inequality (Jensen) |
| 7 | $\gamma(z_{ik}) = \dfrac{\pi_k \mathcal{N}(x_i \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \pi_j \mathcal{N}(x_i \mid \mu_j, \Sigma_j)}$ | Responsibility of component $k$ for point $x_i$ (GMM) |
| 8 | $\mu_k^{\text{new}} = \dfrac{\sum_i \gamma(z_{ik}) \, x_i}{\sum_i \gamma(z_{ik})}$ | Updated mean for GMM component $k$ |
| 9 | $\Sigma_k^{\text{new}} = \dfrac{\sum_i \gamma(z_{ik})(x_i - \mu_k)(x_i - \mu_k)^T}{\sum_i \gamma(z_{ik})}$ | Updated covariance for GMM |
| 10 | $\pi_k^{\text{new}} = \dfrac{\sum_i \gamma(z_{ik})}{N}$ | Updated mixing weight |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Exam Tip:** The notation $\mathbb{E}_{Z \mid X, \theta^{(t)}}[\cdot]$ means "expectation over $Z$, where $Z$ is distributed according to the posterior $P(Z \mid X, \theta^{(t)})$". **Do NOT confuse this with expectation over data $X$.**

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | Application of EM |
|--------|-------------------|
| **Bioinformatics** | Gene expression clustering, haplotype inference |
| **Computer Vision** | Image segmentation (foreground/background), face recognition |
| **Speech Processing** | Speaker diarization (who spoke when), HMM training |
| **Finance** | Estimating parameters of regime-switching market models |
| **Recommender Systems** | Matrix factorization with missing ratings |
| **Anomaly Detection** | Learning "normal" Gaussian mixture, flagging low-probability points |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation: From Log-Likelihood to ELBO

We start with the observed-data log-likelihood:

$$\ell(\theta) = \log P(X \mid \theta)$$

We introduce an **arbitrary distribution** $q(Z)$ over the latent variables. By the product rule:

$$P(X, Z \mid \theta) = P(Z \mid X, \theta) \cdot P(X \mid \theta)$$

Solving for $P(X \mid \theta)$:

$$P(X \mid \theta) = \frac{P(X, Z \mid \theta)}{P(Z \mid X, \theta)}$$

Taking log on both sides:

$$\log P(X \mid \theta) = \log P(X, Z \mid \theta) - \log P(Z \mid X, \theta)$$

Now multiply and divide by $q(Z)$, then take expectation w.r.t. $q(Z)$:

$$\log P(X \mid \theta) = \sum_{Z} q(Z) \log \frac{P(X, Z \mid \theta)}{q(Z)} - \sum_{Z} q(Z) \log \frac{P(Z \mid X, \theta)}{q(Z)}$$

That is:

$$\log P(X \mid \theta) = \underbrace{\mathcal{L}(q, \theta)}_{\text{ELBO}} + \text{KL}\big(q(Z) \Vert P(Z \mid X, \theta)\big)$$

Since **KL divergence $\geq 0$**, we have:

$$\log P(X \mid \theta) \geq \mathcal{L}(q, \theta)$$

This $\mathcal{L}(q, \theta)$ is the **ELBO (Evidence Lower Bound)**. Equality holds when $q(Z) = P(Z \mid X, \theta)$.

### **E-Step in ELBO Terms**

Set $q(Z) = P(Z \mid X, \theta^{(t)})$. The ELBO becomes:

$$\mathcal{L}(q, \theta) = \sum_{Z} P(Z \mid X, \theta^{(t)}) \log \frac{P(X, Z \mid \theta)}{P(Z \mid X, \theta^{(t)})}$$

This simplifies to:

$$\mathcal{L}(q, \theta) = Q(\theta, \theta^{(t)}) + \text{constant w.r.t. } \theta$$

### **M-Step in ELBO Terms**

$$\theta^{(t+1)} = \arg\max_{\theta} Q(\theta, \theta^{(t)})$$

This is equivalent to maximizing the ELBO with respect to $\theta$ while $q$ is fixed.

## 3.2 Convergence Proof (Monotonic Improvement)

We want to show $\ell(\theta^{(t+1)}) \geq \ell(\theta^{(t)})$.

After the E-step at iteration $t+1$, KL divergence becomes zero by construction:

$$\ell(\theta^{(t)}) = Q(\theta^{(t)}, \theta^{(t)}) + \underbrace{\text{KL}\big(P(Z\mid X,\theta^{(t)}) \Vert P(Z\mid X,\theta^{(t)})\big)}_{= 0}$$

After the M-step:

$$Q(\theta^{(t+1)}, \theta^{(t)}) \geq Q(\theta^{(t)}, \theta^{(t)})$$

The new posterior $P(Z \mid X, \theta^{(t+1)})$ introduces a non-negative KL term:

$$\ell(\theta^{(t+1)}) = Q(\theta^{(t+1)}, \theta^{(t)}) + \underbrace{\text{KL}\big(P(Z\mid X,\theta^{(t)}) \Vert P(Z\mid X,\theta^{(t+1)})\big)}_{\geq 0}$$

Chaining these inequalities:

$$\ell(\theta^{(t+1)}) \geq Q(\theta^{(t+1)}, \theta^{(t)}) \geq Q(\theta^{(t)}, \theta^{(t)}) = \ell(\theta^{(t)})$$

**Hence the log-likelihood is monotonically non-decreasing.** $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example — Two-Coin EM

> **Problem Statement:** Two coins A and B with unknown biases $\theta_A$ and $\theta_B$. We perform 5 independent trials. Each trial picks one coin (with equal prior probability $0.5$) and tosses it 10 times. We are only told the sequence of Heads (H) and Tails (T) per trial, not which coin was used.

**Observed data** (H count per trial): $x = \{5, 9, 8, 4, 7\}$

**Initial guess:** $\theta_A^{(0)} = 0.6$, $\theta_B^{(0)} = 0.5$

### **Iteration 1, E-Step**

For each trial $i$, compute the probability it came from coin A (assuming uniform prior):

$$P(\text{coin A} \mid x_i, \theta^{(0)}) = \frac{\binom{10}{x_i} \theta_A^{x_i} (1-\theta_A)^{10-x_i}}{\binom{10}{x_i} \theta_A^{x_i}(1-\theta_A)^{10-x_i} + \binom{10}{x_i} \theta_B^{x_i}(1-\theta_B)^{10-x_i}}$$

This simplifies to:

$$P(\text{coin A} \mid x_i) = \frac{\theta_A^{x_i}(1-\theta_A)^{10-x_i}}{\theta_A^{x_i}(1-\theta_A)^{10-x_i} + \theta_B^{x_i}(1-\theta_B)^{10-x_i}}$$

For $x_1 = 5$:

$$\theta_A^5 (1-\theta_A)^5 = 0.6^5 \cdot 0.4^5 = 0.07776 \cdot 0.01024 = 0.000796$$

$$\theta_B^5 (1-\theta_B)^5 = 0.5^5 \cdot 0.5^5 = 0.0009765625$$

$$P(\text{A} \mid x_1) = \frac{0.000796}{0.000796 + 0.000977} = \frac{0.000796}{0.001773} = 0.449$$

$$P(\text{B} \mid x_1) = 1 - 0.449 = 0.551$$

Similarly computing for all trials (rounded):

| Trial $i$ | $x_i$ | $P(A \mid x_i)$ | $P(B \mid x_i)$ |
|-----------|-------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | 5 | 0.449 | 0.551 |
| 2 | 9 | 0.805 | 0.195 |
| 3 | 8 | 0.797 | 0.203 |
| 4 | 4 | 0.355 | 0.645 |
| 5 | 7 | 0.735 | 0.265 |

### **Iteration 1, M-Step**

Compute the expected number of Heads and Tails under each coin's responsibility.

For coin A:

$$E[\text{Heads from A}] = \sum_i P(A \mid x_i) \cdot x_i$$
$$= (0.449)(5) + (0.805)(9) + (0.797)(8) + (0.355)(4) + (0.735)(7)$$
$$= 2.245 + 7.245 + 6.376 + 1.420 + 5.145 = 22.431$$

$$E[\text{Tails from A}] = 10 \cdot 5 - 22.431 = 27.569$$

$$\theta_A^{(1)} = \frac{22.431}{22.431 + 27.569} = \frac{22.431}{50.000} = 0.4486$$

For coin B:

$$E[\text{Heads from B}] = (0.551)(5) + (0.195)(9) + (0.203)(8) + (0.645)(4) + (0.265)(7)$$
$$= 2.755 + 1.755 + 1.624 + 2.580 + 1.855 = 10.569$$

$$\theta_B^{(1)} = \frac{10.569}{10.569 + (50 - 10.569)} = \frac{10.569}{50.000} = 0.2114$$

### **Iteration 2 (Briefly)**

Repeat E and M steps with updated parameters. The biases will progressively converge toward the "true" generating parameters (in this synthetic case, around $\theta_A \approx 0.78$, $\theta_B \approx 0.52$).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Trick:** When asked "Explain EM with a worked example", the two-coin problem is the **most commonly expected** answer. Memorize the structure: **(i) Set up Q-function, (ii) E-step computes soft responsibilities, (iii) M-step updates parameters using weighted counts.**

## 3.4 Jensen's Inequality — The Engine Behind EM

For a convex function $\phi$ and probability distribution $p(z)$:

$$\phi\left(\mathbb{E}[Z]\right) \leq \mathbb{E}[\phi(Z)]$$

Since $\log(\cdot)$ is **concave**, applying Jensen to $\log \mathbb{E}[f(Z)]$:

$$\log \mathbb{E}[f(Z)] \geq \mathbb{E}[\log f(Z)]$$

This is the precise inequality EM exploits — replacing $\log \sum_Z P(X,Z)$ with $\sum_Z q(Z) \log P(X,Z)$, which is **easier to maximize**.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level EM Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start EM Algorithm]) --> initA[Initialize parameters theta_0 randomly]
    initA --> evalA{Evaluate Log Likelihood}
    evalA --> estepA[E-Step: Compute Posterior P Z given X theta_t]
    estepA --> qfuncA[Form Q function: Expected complete data log likelihood]
    qfuncA --> mstepA[M-Step: theta_new equals argmax Q theta theta_t]
    mstepA --> checkA{Convergence Reached}
    checkA -->|No| evalA
    checkA -->|Yes| outA([Return theta_final and final log likelihood])
    
    style startA fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style outA fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style initA fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#000
    style estepA fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#000
    style mstepA fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#000
    style qfuncA fill:#e2d4f0,stroke:#000
    style checkA fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#000
    style evalA fill:#d4edda,stroke:#000
```

## 4.2 ELBO Decomposition Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    loglik[Log Likelihood log P X given theta] --> elbo[ELBO L q theta]
    loglik --> kl[KL Divergence KL q Z versus P Z given X theta]
    elbo --> q1[Q function: E of log P X Z given theta]
    elbo --> negent[Negative Entropy: minus E log q Z]
    kl --> nonneg[Always non negative]
    nonneg --> bound[Therefore log P X given theta is greater than or equal to ELBO]
    
    style loglik fill:#0d6efd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style elbo fill:#6610f2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style kl fill:#dc3545,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style q1 fill:#198754,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style negent fill:#fd7e14,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style bound fill:#ffc107,stroke:#000
    style nonneg fill:#6c757d,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture — Mapping Iterative Interactions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT BLOCK]
        d1[Observed Data X]
        d2[Latent Variables Z Hidden]
        d3[Initial Parameters theta 0]
    end
    
    subgraph ESTEP[E STEP MODULE]
        e1[Posterior Computation P Z given X theta t]
        e2[Soft Responsibility Assignment]
        e3[Expected Sufficient Statistics]
    end
    
    subgraph QBLOCK[Q FUNCTION BLOCK]
        q1A[Form Q theta theta t equals sum Z P Z given X theta t times log P X Z given theta]
    end
    
    subgraph MSTEP[M STEP MODULE]
        m1[Maximization argmax over theta of Q]
        m2[Parameter Update theta t plus 1]
    end
    
    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT BLOCK]
        o1[Updated Parameters theta t plus 1]
        o2[Updated Log Likelihood]
        o3[Convergence Flag]
    end
    
    d1 --> e1
    d2 --> e1
    d3 --> e1
    e1 --> e2
    e2 --> e3
    e3 --> q1A
    q1A --> m1
    m1 --> m2
    m2 --> o1
    q1A --> o2
    m2 --> o3
    o3 -->|Not Converged| e1
    
    style INPUT fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#000
    style ESTEP fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#000
    style QBLOCK fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#000
    style MSTEP fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#000
    style OUTPUT fill:#e2d4f0,stroke:#000
    style o3 fill:#ffc107,stroke:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Architecture:** Each iteration, the **E-Step module** consumes current parameters and outputs expected statistics, which feed the **Q-Function block**. The **M-Step module** then maximizes Q to produce updated parameters. The loop closes when the convergence flag triggers termination.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm. Why is it preferred over direct Maximum Likelihood Estimation when latent variables are present?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm** is an iterative procedure for finding Maximum Likelihood Estimates (MLE) in models that contain **latent (unobserved) variables** or incomplete data. It alternates between two steps:

1. **E-Step (Expectation):** Given the current parameter estimates $\theta^{(t)}$, compute the posterior distribution of the latent variables:
$$P(Z \mid X, \theta^{(t)}) = \frac{P(X, Z \mid \theta^{(t)})}{\sum_{Z'} P(X, Z' \mid \theta^{(t)})}$$
and form the expected complete-data log-likelihood (Q-function):
$$Q(\theta, \theta^{(t)}) = \mathbb{E}_{Z \mid X, \theta^{(t)}}[\log P(X, Z \mid \theta)]$$

2. **M-Step (Maximization):** Update parameters by maximizing the Q-function:
$$\theta^{(t+1)} = \arg\max_{\theta} Q(\theta, \theta^{(t)})$$

**Why EM over direct MLE?** When latent variables exist, the observed-data log-likelihood takes the form $\log \sum_Z P(X, Z \mid \theta)$. The **logarithm outside the summation** prevents closed-form MLE solution. EM circumvents this by iteratively computing a tractable lower bound (ELBO) using Jensen's inequality, avoiding the intractability while guaranteeing **monotonic improvement** of the log-likelihood at each iteration.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the Q-function and ELBO in the context of the EM algorithm. State the relationship between them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Q-function** is the expected value of the complete-data log-likelihood, where the expectation is taken over the posterior distribution of latent variables given the current parameter estimate:

$$Q(\theta, \theta^{(t)}) = \mathbb{E}_{Z \mid X, \theta^{(t)}}\big[\log P(X, Z \mid \theta)\big]$$

The **ELBO (Evidence Lower Bound)** is a lower bound on the observed-data log-likelihood $\log P(X \mid \theta)$, derived using an arbitrary distribution $q(Z)$ over latent variables:

$$\log P(X \mid \theta) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q(Z)}[\log P(X, Z \mid \theta)] - \mathbb{E}_{q(Z)}[\log q(Z)] = \text{ELBO}(q, \theta)$$

**Relationship:** When we choose $q(Z) = P(Z \mid X, \theta^{(t)})$, the ELBO reduces (up to a constant) to the Q-function. Thus, **maximizing Q in the M-step is equivalent to maximizing the ELBO**, and the gap between the true log-likelihood and ELBO equals the KL divergence between $q$ and the true posterior.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` — INTERNAL CHOICE OPTION 1

**(a)** Derive the EM algorithm from the observed-data log-likelihood using Jensen's inequality. Show that the log-likelihood is monotonically non-decreasing across iterations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the application of EM for parameter estimation in a **Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM)** with $K$ components. Write the E-step and M-step update equations for the means, covariances, and mixing coefficients. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — INTERNAL CHOICE OPTION 2

**(a)** What are latent variables? Explain the Expectation-Maximization algorithm with a suitable example, detailing both the E-step and the M-step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the convergence properties of the EM algorithm. What are its main limitations, and how can they be addressed? Compare EM with the K-means clustering algorithm. **(7 marks)**

---

### 📘 MODEL SOLUTIONS

---

#### **Question A — Part (a) Solution** (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Start with observed-data log-likelihood:
$$\ell(\theta) = \log P(X \mid \theta) = \log \sum_{Z} P(X, Z \mid \theta)$$

**Step 2:** Introduce distribution $q(Z)$ over latent variables:
$$\ell(\theta) = \log \sum_{Z} q(Z) \cdot \frac{P(X, Z \mid \theta)}{q(Z)}$$

**Step 3:** Recognize that the inner sum is an expectation:
$$\ell(\theta) = \log \, \mathbb{E}_{q(Z)}\left[\frac{P(X, Z \mid \theta)}{q(Z)}\right]$$

**Step 4:** Apply Jensen's inequality (since $\log$ is concave):
$$\ell(\theta) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q(Z)}\left[\log \frac{P(X, Z \mid \theta)}{q(Z)}\right] = \text{ELBO}(q, \theta)$$

**Step 5:** Choose $q(Z) = P(Z \mid X, \theta^{(t)})$ to make the bound tight at $\theta^{(t)}$:
$$\text{ELBO}(\theta, \theta^{(t)}) = \sum_{Z} P(Z \mid X, \theta^{(t)}) \log P(X, Z \mid \theta) - \sum_{Z} P(Z \mid X, \theta^{(t)}) \log P(Z \mid X, \theta^{(t)})$$

The second term is constant w.r.t. $\theta$, so:
$$\theta^{(t+1)} = \arg\max_{\theta} \sum_{Z} P(Z \mid X, \theta^{(t)}) \log P(X, Z \mid \theta) = \arg\max_{\theta} Q(\theta, \theta^{(t)})$$

**Monotonic Improvement Proof:**
$$\ell(\theta^{(t)}) = Q(\theta^{(t)}, \theta^{(t)}) + \text{KL}^{(t)} \quad \text{with } \text{KL}^{(t)} = 0$$
$$\ell(\theta^{(t+1)}) = Q(\theta^{(t+1)}, \theta^{(t)}) + \text{KL}^{(t+1)} \geq Q(\theta^{(t+1)}, \theta^{(t)})$$
$$\text{Since } \theta^{(t+1)} = \arg\max Q(\cdot, \theta^{(t)}) \Rightarrow Q(\theta^{(t+1)}, \theta^{(t)}) \geq Q(\theta^{(t)}, \theta^{(t)})$$

Therefore:
$$\ell(\theta^{(t+1)}) \geq Q(\theta^{(t+1)}, \theta^{(t)}) \geq Q(\theta^{(t)}, \theta^{(t)}) = \ell(\theta^{(t)}) \quad \blacksquare$$

---

#### **Question A — Part (b) Solution** (7 marks)

A **Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM)** assumes data is generated from $K$ Gaussian components:

$$P(x) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \, \mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)$$

where $\pi_k$ are mixing coefficients with $\sum_k \pi_k = 1$. The latent variable $z \in \{1, \ldots, K\}$ indicates which component generated each point.

**E-Step:** Compute responsibilities (posterior probability that $x_i$ came from component $k$):

$$\gamma(z_{ik}) = P(z_i = k \mid x_i, \theta^{(t)}) = \frac{\pi_k^{(t)} \, \mathcal{N}(x_i \mid \mu_k^{(t)}, \Sigma_k^{(t)})}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \pi_j^{(t)} \, \mathcal{N}(x_i \mid \mu_j^{(t)}, \Sigma_j^{(t)})}$$

**M-Step:** Update parameters using the responsibilities as soft weights:

**Mixing coefficient:**
$$N_k = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{ik}) \qquad \pi_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{N_k}{N}$$

**Mean:**
$$\mu_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{ik}) \, x_i$$

**Covariance:**
$$\Sigma_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{ik}) \, (x_i - \mu_k^{\text{new}})(x_i - \mu_k^{\text{new}})^T$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** A very common mistake is to use the **old** mean $\mu_k^{(t)}$ instead of the updated $\mu_k^{\text{new}}$ when computing $\Sigma_k^{\text{new}}$. Always verify which time-step's mean is being used. **Loss of 1–2 marks if confused.** Another trap: writing $\pi_k^{\text{new}} = N_k / N$ vs. $N_k / K$ — students frequently make this error under time pressure.

---

#### **Question B — Part (a) Solution** (7 marks)

**Latent variables** are random variables that are not directly observed but influence the observed data. They represent hidden structure (e.g., cluster membership, latent factors, missing values). Their presence prevents direct MLE because the complete likelihood $P(X, Z \mid \theta)$ becomes intractable when summed over unknown $Z$.

**EM Algorithm Steps** (with the **two-coin example**):

Suppose we have two coins with biases $\theta_A$ and $\theta_B$, and for each of $N$ trials we observe $x_i$ Heads out of $n_i$ tosses — but we don't know which coin was used.

**E-Step:** For each trial $i$, compute the probability it used coin A:
$$w_i^{(t)} = P(\text{coin A} \mid x_i, \theta^{(t)}) = \frac{\theta_A^{(t)\, x_i}(1-\theta_A^{(t)})^{n_i - x_i}}{\theta_A^{(t)\, x_i}(1-\theta_A^{(t)})^{n_i - x_i} + \theta_B^{(t)\, x_i}(1-\theta_B^{(t)})^{n_i - x_i}}$$

**M-Step:** Update biases using weighted counts:
$$\theta_A^{(t+1)} = \frac{\sum_i w_i^{(t)} \, x_i}{\sum_i w_i^{(t)} \, n_i} \qquad \theta_B^{(t+1)} = \frac{\sum_i (1-w_i^{(t)}) \, x_i}{\sum_i (1-w_i^{(t)}) \, n_i}$$

**Q-function:**
$$Q(\theta, \theta^{(t)}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ w_i^{(t)} \log P(x_i \mid \theta_A) + (1-w_i^{(t)}) \log P(x_i \mid \theta_B) \right]$$

This is the expected complete-data log-likelihood. Iterating E and M until $\vert \theta^{(t+1)} - \theta^{(t)} \vert < \epsilon$ yields the EM estimate.

---

#### **Question B — Part (b) Solution** (7 marks)

**Convergence Properties:**
- EM guarantees **monotonic increase** in the observed-data log-likelihood: $\ell(\theta^{(t+1)}) \geq \ell(\theta^{(t)})$.
- EM is **guaranteed to converge** to a **local maximum** (or saddle point) of the likelihood.
- Convergence rate is typically **linear** (sometimes super-linear near optimum).
- Convergence is determined by either parameter change $\Vert \theta^{(t+1)} - \theta^{(t)} \Vert < \epsilon_1$ or log-likelihood change $\vert \ell^{(t+1)} - \ell^{(t)} \vert < \epsilon_2$.

**Main Limitations:**
1. **Local Optima:** EM finds local maxima, sensitive to initialization. Mitigated by **multiple restarts** or smarter initialization (e.g., K-means++ for GMM).
2. **Slow Convergence:** Linear convergence can be slow. Accelerations: **ECM (Expectation-Conditional-Maximization)**, **AECM**, or **Quasi-Newton EM**.
3. **Singular Covariance:** In GMMs, components can collapse to single points producing $\Sigma_k = 0$ (singularity). Mitigated by adding small ridge regularization: $\Sigma_k + \epsilon I$.
4. **Need to Specify $K$:** EM requires the number of components/classes as input. Addressed by **BIC/AIC** for model selection.

**EM vs. K-Means Comparison:**

| Feature | K-Means | EM (GMM) |
|---------|---------|----------|
| Cluster assignment | Hard (binary) | Soft (probabilistic) |
| Cluster shape | Spherical, equal-size | Any elliptical, varying size |
| Objective | Minimize WCSS | Maximize log-likelihood |
| Convergence | Local minimum | Local maximum |
| Probabilistic output | No | Yes |
| Computational cost | Faster | Slower (matrix inversions) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — EM vs. K-Means:**
> Students often write *"EM is just K-means with probabilities"* — this is **incorrect** and loses 2 marks. Correct framing: **K-means is a special case (hard limit) of EM** when covariances are assumed isotropic and responsibilities are 0/1.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — EM Algorithm**

- ✅ EM is for **models with latent variables / incomplete data** where direct MLE is intractable.
- ✅ The log-likelihood has form $\log \sum_Z P(X, Z \mid \theta)$ — the **log of a sum** is the obstacle.
- ✅ **E-Step**: compute $P(Z \mid X, \theta^{(t)})$, then form Q-function $Q(\theta, \theta^{(t)}) = \mathbb{E}_{Z \mid X, \theta^{(t)}}[\log P(X, Z \mid \theta)]$.
- ✅ **M-Step**: $\theta^{(t+1)} = \arg\max_\theta Q(\theta, \theta^{(t)})$.
- ✅ **ELBO** = Expected log joint $\,-\,$ Negative entropy. **Maximized** in M-step.
- ✅ $\log P(X \mid \theta) = \text{ELBO} + \text{KL Divergence} \geq \text{ELBO}$ (since KL $\geq 0$).
- ✅ **Monotonic improvement** is guaranteed by Jensen's inequality.
- ✅ EM converges to a **local maximum**, not global — initialization matters.
- ✅ **GMM responsibilities**: $\gamma(z_{ik}) = \dfrac{\pi_k \mathcal{N}(x_i \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)}{\sum_j \pi_j \mathcal{N}(x_i \mid \mu_j, \Sigma_j)}$.
- ✅ GMM updates for mean use **responsibility-weighted averages**, not raw counts.
- ✅ K-means is a **hard-assignment limit** of EM with isotropic Gaussians.
- ✅ Variants: **ECM, AECM, SEM (Stochastic EM), GEM (Generalized EM)** for faster/robust convergence.
- ✅ Common exam trap: confusing which step (E or M) **computes** the posterior vs. which **updates** parameters.

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Expectation-Maximization Algorithm**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme — PCITT501 Machine Learning*

topics.title: Density Based Clustering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITT501 - MACHINE LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Density Based Clustering # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Density-Based Clustering** is a non-parametric, unsupervised machine learning methodology that groups together data points that are densely packed in the feature space, marking points in low-density regions as outliers or noise. Unlike centroid-based methods (e.g., K-Means), it defines clusters as continuous regions of high point density separated by regions of low point density. The most canonical and widely adopted density-based algorithm is **DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise)**, introduced by Ester et al. (1996). DBSCAN requires only two input parameters: a neighborhood radius **ε (epsilon)** and the minimum number of points **MinPts** required to form a dense region. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Density-based clustering is fundamentally different from K-Means and Hierarchical clustering because it does **not** require the number of clusters to be specified in advance, and it can discover clusters of **arbitrary shape** (non-spherical) while robustly identifying **noise/outliers**. ## 1.2 Core Terminology — The Four Point Types | Point Type | Definition | Role in Clustering | |---|---|---| | **Core Point** | A point that has at least **MinPts** points (including itself) within its **ε-neighborhood** | Forms the dense "heart" of a cluster | | **Border Point** | A point that has fewer than **MinPts** neighbors but lies within the ε-neighborhood of a core point | Forms the sparse "edge" of a cluster | | **Noise Point (Outlier)** | A point that is neither a core point nor a border point | Does not belong to any cluster | | **Reachable Point** | A point that can be reached from a core point via a chain of core points | Indirect cluster member | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The "City Lights at Night" Model** > > Imagine looking at Earth from a satellite at night. The **bright, dense clusters of light** represent major metropolitan cities (Mumbai, Delhi, New York). A **dim, isolated light** in a remote desert is a lone traveler (outlier).
