Density-based clustering works the same way: > - It says: *"A point belongs to a city (cluster) only if there are enough nearby lights (MinPts) within walking distance (ε)."* > - If you are surrounded by a few dim lights, you are on the **outskirts** of the city (border point). > - If you are surrounded by many bright lights, you are in the **center** (core point). > - If you are alone in the desert with no lights nearby, you are **noise** (outlier). This intuitive model captures the essence of DBSCAN perfectly — clusters are **dense neighborhoods**, and the algorithm finds them by growing from core points outward. ## 1.4 Why Density-Based Clustering Matters Density-based methods overcome three critical limitations of K-Means: 1. **No need to pre-specify K** (the number of clusters). 2. **Can detect arbitrary shapes** (moons, rings, irregular blobs). 3. **Robust to outliers** — noise points are explicitly identified rather than forced into clusters. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mathematical Foundation of DBSCAN ### 2.1.1 The ε-Neighborhood (Epsilon Neighborhood) For any data point **p** in dataset **D**, the **ε-neighborhood** is the set of all points within Euclidean distance **ε** from p: $$N_{\varepsilon}(p) = \{ q \in D \mid \text{dist}(p, q) \leq \varepsilon \}$$ The standard distance metric is **Euclidean distance**: $$\text{dist}(p, q) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d}(p_i - q_i)^2}$$ where **d** is the dimensionality of the feature space. > [!NOTE] > **Why ε matters:** ε is the "radius of search." A small ε produces many tiny clusters and lots of noise; a large ε merges clusters together. Choosing ε is the **most critical hyperparameter decision** in DBSCAN. ### 2.1.2 Core Point Condition A point **p** is a **Core Point** if and only if: $$|N_{\varepsilon}(p)| \geq \text{MinPts}$$ The cardinality |N_ε(p)| counts the number of points in the ε-neighborhood, **including p itself**. ### 2.1.3 Direct Density-Reachability A point **q** is **directly density-reachable** from point **p** if: 1. **q** lies in the ε-neighborhood of **p**: $q \in N_{\varepsilon}(p)$ 2. **p** is a core point: $|N_{\varepsilon}(p)| \geq \text{MinPts}$ **Note:** Direct density-reachability is **asymmetric** — if q is directly density-reachable from p, it does NOT mean p is directly density-reachable from q (unless both are core points). ### 2.1.4 Density-Reachability (Transitive Closure) A point **q** is **density-reachable** from **p** if there exists a chain of points $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n$ such that: - $p_1 = p$ - $p_n = q$ - Each $p_{i+1}$ is **directly density-reachable** from $p_i$ This creates a **transitive chain** through core points. ### 2.1.5 Density-Connectivity Two points **p** and **q** are **density-connected** if there exists a point **o** such that both p and q are density-reachable from o. **Density-connectivity is the formal definition of a cluster**: A cluster **C** is a maximal set of density-connected points. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | ε-Neighborhood | $N_{\varepsilon}(p) = \{ q \mid \text{dist}(p,q) \leq \varepsilon \}$ | Radius of search | | 2 | Euclidean Distance | $\text{dist}(p,q) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d}(p_i - q_i)^2}$ | Default metric | | 3 | Core Point Test | $\vert N_{\varepsilon}(p) \vert \geq \text{MinPts}$ | Includes p itself | | 4 | Border Point | $\vert N_{\varepsilon}(p) \vert < \text{MinPts}$ AND $\exists$ core point within ε | Sparse edge | | 5 | Noise Point | Not core AND not border | Outlier | | 6 | Direct Density-Reachable | $q \in N_{\varepsilon}(p)$ AND $p$ is core | Asymmetric | | 7 | Density-Reachable | Chain of direct-reachability steps | Transitive | | 8 | Density-Connected | $\exists$ o: both p,q density-reachable from o | Symmetric | | 9 | MinPts Heuristic (Rule of Thumb) | $\text{MinPts} \geq d + 1$ (minimum); typical = **2 × d** | d = dimensions | | 10 | ε Selection (k-distance) | Plot k-dist graph; choose ε at "elbow" | k = MinPts − 1 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** Density-based clustering is used in **anomaly detection** (fraud, network intrusion), **spatial data analysis** (GIS, astronomy), **image segmentation**, and **bioinformatics** (gene expression clustering). Unlike K-Means, it does not assume spherical clusters, making it suitable for real-world datasets with irregular structures. ## 2.3 Algorithmic Step-by-Step Logic (DBSCAN) The DBSCAN algorithm operates as follows: 1. **Initialize:** Label all points as **unvisited**. 2. **Select** an arbitrary unvisited point **p**. Mark it as **visited**. 3. **Compute** $N_{\varepsilon}(p)$ (the ε-neighborhood of p). 4. **If** $|N_{\varepsilon}(p)| < \text{MinPts}$: - Temporarily mark **p** as **noise** (may be reclassified later as a border point). 5. **Else** (p is a core point): - Create a new cluster **C** and add **p** to it. - Let **S = N_ε(p) \ {p}** (the seed set, neighbors excluding p). - For each point **q** in **S**: - If **q** is unvisited: mark as visited, compute $N_{\varepsilon}(q)$, and if $|N_{\varepsilon}(q)| \geq \text{MinPts}$, add these new points to **S**. - If **q** is not yet a member of any cluster: add **q** to **C**. 6. **Repeat** from step 2 until all points are visited. ## 2.4 Complexity & Characteristics - **Time Complexity:** $O(n \log n)$ with spatial indexing (e.g., KD-Tree, Ball-Tree); $O(n^2)$ in the worst case without indexing. - **Space Complexity:** $O(n)$ for storing cluster labels and the visited status. - **Deterministic:** For a given (ε, MinPts) pair, DBSCAN always produces the same clustering (unlike K-Means with random initialization). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: DBSCAN on a 2D Toy Dataset Consider the following 2D dataset with **8 points** in the plane: $$ \begin{aligned} P_1 &= (1, 1) \\ P_2 &= (1, 2) \\ P_3 &= (2, 1) \\ P_4 &= (2, 2) \\ P_5 &= (8, 8) \\ P_6 &= (8, 9) \\ P_7 &= (25, 25) \\ P_8 &= (50, 50) \end{aligned} $$ **Parameters:** $\varepsilon = 1.5$, $\text{MinPts} = 3$ We will systematically classify every point. ### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Compute the Full Pairwise Distance Matrix Using Euclidean distance $\text{dist}(p,q) = \sqrt{(p_1-q_1)^2 + (p_2-q_2)^2}$: $$ \begin{aligned} d(P_1, P_2) &= \sqrt{(1-1)^2 + (1-2)^2} = 1.00 \\ d(P_1, P_3) &= \sqrt{(1-2)^2 + (1-1)^2} = 1.00 \\ d(P_1, P_4) &= \sqrt{(1-2)^2 + (1-2)^2} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.41 \\ d(P_1, P_5) &= \sqrt{(1-8)^2 + (1-8)^2} = \sqrt{98} \approx 9.90 \\ d(P_1, P_7) &= \sqrt{(1-25)^2 + (1-25)^2} = \sqrt{1152} \approx 33.94 \\ d(P_2, P_3) &= \sqrt{(1-2)^2 + (2-1)^2} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.41 \\ d(P_2, P_4) &= \sqrt{(1-2)^2 + (2-2)^2} = 1.00 \\ d(P_3, P_4) &= \sqrt{(2-2)^2 + (1-2)^2} = 1.00 \\ d(P_5, P_6) &= \sqrt{(8-8)^2 + (8-9)^2} = 1.00 \\ d(P_5, P_7) &= \sqrt{(8-25)^2 + (8-25)^2} = \sqrt{578} \approx 24.04 \\ d(P_7, P_8) &= \sqrt{(25-50)^2 + (25-50)^2} = \sqrt{1250} \approx 35.36 \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic Note:** All other distances are clearly $> \varepsilon = 1.5$ and can be excluded from ε-neighborhoods. (E.g., $d(P_4, P_5) = \sqrt{72} \approx 8.49 \gg 1.5$.) ### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Determine the ε-Neighborhood of Every Point $$ \begin{aligned} N_{\varepsilon}(P_1) &= \{P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4\} \quad (|N| = 4) \\ N_{\varepsilon}(P_2) &= \{P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4\} \quad (|N| = 4) \\ N_{\varepsilon}(P_3) &= \{P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4\} \quad (|N| = 4) \\ N_{\varepsilon}(P_4) &= \{P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4\} \quad (|N| = 4) \\ N_{\varepsilon}(P_5) &= \{P_5, P_6\} \quad (|N| = 2) \\ N_{\varepsilon}(P_6) &= \{P_5, P_6\} \quad (|N| = 2) \\ N_{\varepsilon}(P_7) &= \{P_7\} \quad (|N| = 1) \\ N_{\varepsilon}(P_8) &= \{P_8\} \quad (|N| = 1) \end{aligned} $$ > **Conversion Logic:** A point belongs to the ε-neighborhood if its distance from the query point is $\leq 1.5$. Since $P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4$ are all within $1.5$ units of each other, they form a fully-connected dense group. ### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Apply the Core Point Test: $|N_{\varepsilon}(p)| \geq \text{MinPts} = 3$ $$ \begin{aligned} |N_{\varepsilon}(P_1)| = 4 \geq 3 &\Rightarrow P_1 \text{ is a CORE POINT} \\ |N_{\varepsilon}(P_2)| = 4 \geq 3 &\Rightarrow P_2 \text{ is a CORE POINT} \\ |N_{\varepsilon}(P_3)| = 4 \geq 3 &\Rightarrow P_3 \text{ is a CORE POINT} \\ |N_{\varepsilon}(P_4)| = 4 \geq 3 &\Rightarrow P_4 \text{ is a CORE POINT} \\ |N_{\varepsilon}(P_5)| = 2 < 3 &\Rightarrow \text{Not core} \\ |N_{\varepsilon}(P_6)| = 2 < 3 &\Rightarrow \text{Not core} \\ |N_{\varepsilon}(P_7)| = 1 < 3 &\Rightarrow \text{Not core} \\ |N_{\varepsilon}(P_8)| = 1 < 3 &\Rightarrow \text{Not core} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Classify Non-Core Points - **$P_5$:** Not a core point. Does it have a core point in its ε-neighborhood? Yes — wait, $N_{\varepsilon}(P_5) = \{P_5, P_6\}$ contains **no core point** (P_5 and P_6 are both non-core). So $P_5$ becomes **NOISE**. - **$P_6$:** Similarly, $N_{\varepsilon}(P_6) = \{P_5, P_6\}$, no core point. So $P_6$ becomes **NOISE**. - **$P_7$:** $N_{\varepsilon}(P_7) = \{P_7\}$. No core point. So $P_7$ is **NOISE**. - **$P_8$:** $N_{\varepsilon}(P_8) = \{P_8\}$. No core point. So $P_8$ is **NOISE**. ### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Final Clustering Result $$ \boxed{ \begin{aligned} \text{Cluster 1} &= \{P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4\} \\ \text{Noise} &= \{P_5, P_6, P_7, P_8\} \end{aligned} } $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** The pair (P_5, P_6) was **not absorbed** into Cluster 1 because they are too far from the dense group, and they themselves are **not dense enough** to form their own cluster. DBSCAN correctly identifies them as **noise** — this is its noise-detection power. ## 3.2 The k-Distance Method for Choosing ε (Derivation-Style Walkthrough) A robust technique for selecting ε is the **k-distance plot**: 1. For every point, compute the distance to its **k-th nearest neighbor** (where $k = \text{MinPts} - 1$). 2. Sort these k-distances in **descending order** and plot them. 3. The optimal ε is the **"elbow point"** — the y-value where the curve transitions sharply from a steep slope to a flat one. **Why this works mathematically:** Points in **dense cluster interiors** have very small k-distances (their k-th neighbor is close). Points in **sparse boundary or noise regions** have large k-distances. The sorted k-distance curve has a sharp "knee" at the boundary between the two regimes. Choosing ε at this knee maximizes the separation between in-cluster and out-of-cluster distances. > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake:** Choosing ε at the **flat tail** of the k-distance plot (too large) causes all clusters to merge. Choosing ε at the **steep initial part** (too small) causes the algorithm to label almost everything as noise. Always pick the **elbow**. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: DBSCAN vs K-Means vs Hierarchical | Feature | DBSCAN | K-Means | Hierarchical (Agglomerative) | |---|---|---|---| | Number of clusters (K) | **Not required** | Must be specified | Not required (use dendrogram) | | Cluster shape | **Arbitrary** | Spherical only | Arbitrary | | Handles noise | **Yes (explicit)** | No (forced into clusters) | No (forced into clusters) | | Sensitivity to outliers | **Low** | High | High | | Determinism | **Deterministic** | Non-deterministic (random init) | Deterministic | | Scalability | $O(n \log n)$ with index | $O(nkt)$ | $O(n^2 \log n)$ or $O(n^3)$ | | Parameter sensitivity | ε, MinPts | K, init | Linkage, distance | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: DBSCAN Cluster Formation Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Load Dataset D] --> B[Mark all points as UNVISITED] B --> C{Pick an unvisited point p} C --> D[Compute N_epsilon of p] D --> E{|N_epsilon| >= MinPts?} E -- "No (Too Sparse)" --> F[Mark p as NOISE temporarily] F --> G{More unvisited points?} E -- "Yes (Dense Enough)" --> H[Create NEW CLUSTER C] H --> I[Add p to Cluster C] I --> J[Seed Set S = N_epsilon minus p] J --> K{Pick q from Seed Set S} K --> L{q unvisited?} L -- "Yes" --> M[Mark q as VISITED] M --> N[Compute N_epsilon of q] N --> O{|N_epsilon q| >= MinPts?} O -- "Yes" --> P[Merge N_epsilon q into Seed Set S] O -- "No" --> Q[Leave q as candidate border] P --> R{q already in a cluster?} Q --> R R -- "No" --> S2[Add q to Cluster C] S2 --> T{More points in S?} R -- "Yes" --> T T -- "Yes" --> K T -- "No" --> G L -- "No" --> T G -- "Yes" --> C G -- "No" --> Z[End: Output Clusters + Noise Labels] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: The Four Point Types in DBSCAN ```mermaid graph TB subgraph Cluster1[CLUSTER REGION] C1[Core Point A
MinPts neighbors within eps] C2[Core Point B
MinPts neighbors within eps] C3[Core Point C
MinPts neighbors within eps] B1[Border Point D
Fewer than MinPts neighbors
But within eps of a Core Point] end subgraph NoiseRegion[NOISE REGION] N1[Outlier X
No core point nearby
Fewer than MinPts neighbors] N2[Outlier Y
Isolated in sparse region] end C1 -. eps distance .-> C2 C2 -. eps distance .-> C3 C1 -. eps distance .-> B1 C1 -. too far .-> N1 N1 -. too far .-> N2 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Density-Reachability Chain (Transitive Closure) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Core Point P1] -- direct density reachable --> P2[Core Point P2] P2 -- direct density reachable --> P3[Core Point P3] P3 -- direct density reachable --> P4[Core Point P4] P4 -- direct density reachable --> BP[Border Point Q] P1 -. "P1 is density-reachable from P4?" .-> P4 BP -. "Q is density-reachable from P1?" .-> P1 ``` > **Reading the Diagram:** A point Q is **density-reachable** from P1 if there is a chain of **directly density-reachable** steps through core points. Border points at the end of the chain are absorbed into the cluster but cannot extend it further (since they are not core points). ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: DBSCAN vs K-Means — Shape Detection ```mermaid graph LR subgraph DBSCAN_Demo[DBSCAN Output] D1[Cluster 1: Moon Shape] D2[Cluster 2: Moon Shape] D3[Noise Point] end subgraph KMeans_Demo[K-Means Output] K1[Cluster A: Forced Sphere] K2[Cluster B: Forced Sphere] K3[Noise Point forced into nearest sphere] end D1 -. "Correctly captured arbitrary shape" .-> D2 K1 -. "Incorrectly splits moon shapes" .-> K2 ``` > **Interpretation:** DBSCAN correctly separates two interleaving moon-shaped clusters and isolates noise. K-Means, constrained to spherical Voronoi regions, fails to capture the true cluster geometry. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define the terms Core Point, Border Point, and Noise Point in DBSCAN. *[KTU University Exam — Jun 2023]* **Model Answer:** - **Core Point:** A data point **p** is called a core point if the number of points within its ε-neighborhood is **greater than or equal to MinPts**, i.e., $|N_{\varepsilon}(p)| \geq \text{MinPts}$. Core points lie in the interior of dense clusters. - **Border Point:** A point that is **not a core point** (i.e., $|N_{\varepsilon}(p)| < \text{MinPts}$) but lies **within the ε-neighborhood of some core point**. Border points are on the periphery of clusters. - **Noise Point (Outlier):** A point that is **neither a core point nor a border point**. It does not belong to any cluster and is treated as an outlier by the algorithm. > **Mnemonic:** *"Core = Center, Border = Edge, Noise = Stranger."* --- ### Question 2: Why is DBSCAN able to detect clusters of arbitrary shape, while K-Means cannot? *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]* **Model Answer:** K-Means assumes clusters are **spherical and convex** because it partitions the feature space using **centroid-based Voronoi regions**. This geometric assumption fails for non-convex shapes (e.g., moons, rings, spirals). DBSCAN, in contrast, defines a cluster as a **maximal set of density-connected points** without making any assumptions about cluster shape. It grows clusters by chaining **density-reachable** core points. As long as points are dense enough along the shape's contour, DBSCAN will trace that contour regardless of its geometry. This makes DBSCAN capable of detecting **arbitrarily shaped clusters**. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks): Full DBSCAN Worked Example **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the DBSCAN algorithm. List and define the categories of points used in DBSCAN. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** DBSCAN (**Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise**) is a density-based clustering algorithm that groups together points packed closely together (high-density regions) and marks points in low-density regions as outliers. **Categories of Points:** 1. **Core Point:** A point with at least **MinPts** points in its ε-neighborhood. 2. **Border Point:** A point that is not a core point but falls within the ε-neighborhood of a core point. 3. **Noise Point:** A point that is neither core nor border. **Algorithm Steps:** 1. Label all points as **unvisited**. 2. Select an arbitrary unvisited point **p**. 3. Find all points in the ε-neighborhood of p: $N_{\varepsilon}(p)$. 4. **If** $|N_{\varepsilon}(p)| < \text{MinPts}$: mark p as **noise** (may be relabeled later as border). 5. **Else**: create a new cluster **C**, add **p** to it. Initialize seed set $S = N_{\varepsilon}(p) \setminus \{p\}$. 6. For each point **q** in **S**: - If **q** is unvisited: mark visited, compute $N_{\varepsilon}(q)$. If $|N_{\varepsilon}(q)| \geq \text{MinPts}$, add these new points to **S**. - If **q** is not yet in any cluster: add **q** to **C**. 7. Repeat from step 2 until all points are visited. **Diagram (see Section 4.2):** The four point types are arranged in a 2D plane with concentric ε-circles around core points, border points touching the ε-radius of core points, and noise points far away. --- **(b)** Consider the following 2D dataset with **7 points**. Apply DBSCAN with **ε = 1.5** and **MinPts = 3**. Identify the clusters and noise points. Show all distance calculations. **(7 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} P_1 &= (1, 1), \quad P_2 = (1, 2), \quad P_3 = (2, 2), \\ P_4 &= (2, 1), \quad P_5 &= (10, 10), \quad P_6 = (10, 11), \quad P_7 = (50, 50) \end{aligned} $$ **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Compute the Pairwise Distance Matrix** $$ \begin{aligned} d(P_1, P_2) &= \sqrt{0^2 + 1^2} = 1.00 \\ d(P_1, P_3) &= \sqrt{1^2 + 1^2} = 1.41 \\ d(P_1, P_4) &= \sqrt{1^2 + 0^2} = 1.00 \\ d(P_1, P_5) &= \sqrt{9^2 + 9^2} \approx 12.73 \\ d(P_2, P_3) &= \sqrt{1^2 + 0^2} = 1.00 \\ d(P_2, P_4) &= \sqrt{1^2 + 1^2} = 1.41 \\ d(P_3, P_4) &= \sqrt{0^2 + 1^2} = 1.00 \\ d(P_5, P_6) &= \sqrt{0^2 + 1^2} = 1.00 \\ d(P_5, P_7) &\approx 56.57 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Determine ε-Neighborhoods (dist ≤ 1.5)** $$ \begin{aligned} N_{\varepsilon}(P_1) &= \{P_1, P_2, P_4\} \quad (|N| = 3) \\ N_{\varepsilon}(P_2) &= \{P_1, P_2, P_3\} \quad (|N| = 3) \\ N_{\varepsilon}(P_3) &= \{P_2, P_3, P_4\} \quad (|N| = 3) \\ N_{\varepsilon}(P_4) &= \{P_1, P_3, P_4\} \quad (|N| = 3) \\ N_{\varepsilon}(P_5) &= \{P_5, P_6\} \quad (|N| = 2) \\ N_{\varepsilon}(P_6) &= \{P_5, P_6\} \quad (|N| = 2) \\ N_{\varepsilon}(P_7) &= \{P_7\} \quad (|N| = 1) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Apply Core Point Test (|N| ≥ 3)** - $P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4$: **Core Points** (|N| = 3) - $P_5, P_6, P_7$: **Not Core** **Step 4: Classify Border vs Noise** - $P_5$: Not core, no core point in its ε-neighborhood (only P_5, P_6) → **Noise** - $P_6$: Not core, no core point in its ε-neighborhood → **Noise** - $P_7$: Not core, isolated → **Noise** **Step 5: Final Clustering Result** $$ \boxed{ \begin{aligned} \text{Cluster 1} &= \{P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4\} \\ \text{Noise} &= \{P_5, P_6, P_7\} \end{aligned} } $$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks): DBSCAN Parameters, Comparison, and Reachability **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Explain the concepts of **direct density-reachability**, **density-reachability**, and **density-connectivity** in DBSCAN with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **1. Direct Density-Reachability:** A point **q** is **directly density-reachable** from point **p** if: - $q \in N_{\varepsilon}(p)$ (q lies within p's ε-neighborhood), AND - p is a **core point** ($|N_{\varepsilon}(p)| \geq \text{MinPts}$). **Property:** Direct density-reachability is **asymmetric** in general. If q is directly density-reachable from p, p is NOT necessarily directly density-reachable from q (e.g., if q is a border point). **2. Density-Reachability (Transitive):** A point **q** is **density-reachable** from **p** if there exists a chain of points $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n$ where $p_1 = p$, $p_n = q$, and each $p_{i+1}$ is directly density-reachable from $p_i$. This is a **transitive closure** of direct density-reachability. It propagates through a chain of core points. **3. Density-Connectivity:** Two points **p** and **q** are **density-connected** if there exists a point **o** such that both p and q are **density-reachable** from o. **Property:** Density-connectivity is a **symmetric** relation. A DBSCAN cluster is formally defined as a **maximal set of density-connected points**. **Diagram Reference:** See Section 4.3, which depicts a chain $P_1 \to P_2 \to P_3 \to P_4 \to Q$ (where Q is a border point), illustrating how reachability propagates through the cluster. --- **(b)** Discuss the role of parameters **ε** and **MinPts** in DBSCAN. How does the choice of these parameters affect clustering results? Also, briefly explain the k-distance method for selecting ε. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Role of ε (Epsilon):** ε defines the **radius of the neighborhood** around each point. It controls the granularity of cluster formation. - **Small ε** → Tight clusters, many tiny clusters, lots of noise (false outliers). - **Large ε** → Few large clusters, risk of merging distinct clusters. - **Optimal ε** → Captures the natural density separation in the data. **Role of MinPts:** MinPts sets the **minimum density threshold** for a region to qualify as a cluster. - **Small MinPts** (e.g., 2) → Even sparse regions form clusters; high noise sensitivity. - **Large MinPts** → Only very dense regions survive; smaller clusters may be marked as noise. - **Rule of Thumb:** $\text{MinPts} \geq d + 1$ (minimum), with $\text{MinPts} = 2d$ as a common default (d = dimensions). **The k-Distance Method for Selecting ε:** 1. For each point in the dataset, compute its distance to the **k-th nearest neighbor** (typically $k = \text{MinPts} - 1$). 2. Sort these distances in **descending order** and plot them (the **k-distance graph**). 3. The optimal ε corresponds to the **"elbow"** or **"knee"** in the plot — the y-value at the point of maximum curvature. 4. **Rationale:** Points in dense cluster interiors have small k-distances, while boundary/noise points have large k-distances. The elbow separates these two regimes. **Effect of Parameter Mis-Selection:** | Mis-selection | Consequence | |---|---| | ε too small | Almost all points become noise | | ε too large | All points merge into one cluster | | MinPts too small | Spurious small clusters form | | MinPts too large | Only the densest regions survive as clusters | --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing Core, Border, and Noise points:** A border point is a NON-core point that lies in a core point's ε-neighborhood. Many students incorrectly classify border points as core points. Remember: $|N_\varepsilon| < \text{MinPts}$ for border points. > > 2. **Forgetting to include the point itself in |N_ε|:** The cardinality count includes the query point. So a point with 2 actual neighbors has $|N_\varepsilon| = 3$, which may satisfy MinPts. > > 3. **Treating density-reachability as symmetric:** Direct density-reachability is **asymmetric**; only density-connectivity is symmetric. Examiners specifically test this distinction. > > 4. **Skipping distance calculations in worked examples:** KTU examiners award **partial marks for showing the distance matrix** explicitly. Never jump straight to the cluster output. > > 5. **Writing K-Means-style answers for DBSCAN questions:** Do not use centroids or "mean of points." DBSCAN has **no centroid**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exams:** - **DBSCAN = Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise.** It is **non-parametric** (no K required) and **noise-aware**. - **Two parameters:** $\varepsilon$ (neighborhood radius) and **MinPts** (minimum density). - **Four key concepts:** $\varepsilon$-neighborhood, **core point**, **border point**, **noise point**. - **Direct density-reachability** is **asymmetric**; **density-connectivity** is **symmetric**. - **A cluster = a maximal set of density-connected points.** - **ε-selection** uses the **k-distance elbow method**; **MinPts rule of thumb** is $2d$ (twice the number of dimensions). - **DBSCAN advantages over K-Means:** arbitrary shapes, noise handling, no need to specify K, deterministic output. - **DBSCAN disadvantages:** struggles with **varying-density clusters**, sensitive to ε choice, performance degrades in **high dimensions** (curse of dimensionality). - **Variants to know:** **OPTICS** (handles varying density), **HDBSCAN** (hierarchical extension). - **Algorithm time complexity:** $O(n \log n)$ with spatial indexing; $O(n^2)$ worst case. - **Exam tip:** Always show the **distance matrix** and **ε-neighborhood counts** explicitly in worked numerical problems.

topics.title: Reinforcement Learning - Monte Carlo
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT501 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reinforcement Learning - Monte Carlo

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Monte Carlo (MC) Methods** in Reinforcement Learning (RL) are a class of model-free learning algorithms that estimate the value function (or find an optimal policy) by **averaging the returns (cumulative discounted rewards) observed from complete episodes of interaction with the environment**. An *episode* is a sequence of experiences that ends in a terminal state (e.g., finishing a game, reaching a goal, or exceeding a time horizon).

Unlike Dynamic Programming (DP), MC methods **do not require prior knowledge of the environment's transition probabilities or reward function** (i.e., the model of the MDP is unknown). They learn purely from sampled experience. The core idea is to approximate the expected return $E_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s]$ by the sample mean of observed returns following visits to state $s$ under policy $\pi$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Monte Carlo methods are taught as the *first model-free* approach in RL, contrasting with the *model-based* Dynamic Programming methods. Remember: MC requires **episodic tasks** with definite termination.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Slot Machine Player"

Imagine you walk into a casino with hundreds of slot machines, each with an **unknown payout probability** and a **different lever sequence** that affects the next machine you can play (the environment dynamics are hidden). You have a fixed amount of money (the budget) and want to learn which machine to play.

- **Pulling the lever and watching the entire payout sequence** is one *episode*.
- The **total money won from pulling a lever until you walk away** is the *return* ($G_t$).
- After many episodes, the **average money won when you pulled a specific machine** is your *Monte Carlo estimate of its value*.
- You do **not** know the internal mechanics of the machine — you only see the outcome (the reward). This is exactly what makes MC *model-free*.

In RL terms, the **state is which machine you chose**, the **action is pulling the lever**, the **reward is the payout**, and the **policy is your strategy of choosing which lever to pull next**.

### 1.3 Why Monte Carlo? — The "Why" Behind the Method

1. **Model-free** — We don't need $P(s' \mid s, a)$ or $R(s, a, s')$. This is the key advantage.
2. **Unbiased estimate** — The sample average of returns is an *unbiased* estimator of the true expected return.
3. **No bootstrapping** — Unlike DP/TD, MC does not use any estimate of a future state's value. It uses only *actual* observed returns.
4. **Convergence guarantee** — By the Law of Large Numbers, as the number of visits $N(s) \to \infty$, the MC estimate $V(s) \to v_\pi(s)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Limitation:** MC methods can **only be applied to episodic tasks** because they require complete returns (which need a terminal state). This rules out MC for continuing (non-terminating) tasks — that is where **Temporal-Difference (TD) methods** take over.

### 1.4 Core Terminology Table

| Term | Meaning | KTU Notation |
|------|---------|--------------|
| Episode | A complete trajectory from start to terminal state | $S_0, A_0, R_1, S_1, A_1, \ldots, S_T$ |
| Return | Cumulative discounted reward from time $t$ | $G_t$ |
| Visit | A state occurrence at a particular time step | $S_t = s$ |
| Visit count | Number of times a state-action pair has been seen | $N(s)$ or $N(s,a)$ |
| On-policy | Evaluates/improves the policy used to generate data | e.g., $\varepsilon$-soft |
| Off-policy | Evaluates a target policy using data from a behavior policy | Uses importance sampling |
| Exploring starts | Randomly initialize $S_0, A_0$ to ensure all state-action pairs are visited | ES |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Return — Foundation of All RL Learning

The **return** $G_t$ is the total discounted reward received from time step $t$ until the end of the episode at time $T$:

$$G_t \;=\; R_{t+1} \,+\, \gamma R_{t+2} \,+\, \gamma^2 R_{t+3} \,+\, \cdots \,+\, \gamma^{T-t-1} R_T$$

Equivalently, the recursive form is:

$$G_t \;=\; R_{t+1} \,+\, \gamma\, G_{t+1}$$

with $G_T = 0$.

**Why discount?** The discount factor $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ ensures the sum converges for infinite sequences, encodes preference for immediate rewards, and mathematically expresses uncertainty about distant future rewards. $\gamma = 0$ makes the agent *myopic* (cares only about $R_{t+1}$); $\gamma \to 1$ makes it *far-sighted*.

### 2.2 Two Variants: First-Visit vs Every-Visit MC

| Variant | Definition | Property |
|---------|------------|----------|
| **First-Visit MC** | Average returns only after the *first* visit to state $s$ in an episode | Each return is i.i.d. → strong Law of Large Numbers applies directly; theoretically cleaner |
| **Every-Visit MC** | Average returns after *every* visit to state $s$ in an episode | More data-efficient in practice; also converges to $v_\pi(s)$ but returns are not independent |

For both variants, after observing $N$ episodes:

$$V(s) \;\approx\; \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} G_i(s)$$

### 2.3 Incremental Implementation — Running Mean

For online learning, store two scalars per state: $N(s)$ and the running sum $S(s)$. Update on each visit:

$$N(S_t) \;\leftarrow\; N(S_t) + 1$$
$$V(S_t) \;\leftarrow\; V(S_t) + \frac{1}{N(S_t)} \Big[ G_t - V(S_t) \Big]$$

A more general form uses a constant step size $\alpha$:

$$V(S_t) \;\leftarrow\; V(S_t) + \alpha \Big[ G_t - V(S_t) \Big]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The incremental update form is the *exact* same template as stochastic gradient descent with the "target" being the actual observed return $G_t$. The "error" $G_t - V(S_t)$ is the **Monte Carlo error**.

### 2.4 Action-Value Function — The Key to Control

For control (finding the best policy), we must learn the **action-value function**:

$$q_\pi(s, a) \;=\; E_\pi\!\left[ G_t \,\big|\, S_t = s,\; A_t = a \right]$$

The MC estimate after $N(s, a)$ visits:

$$Q(s, a) \;\approx\; \frac{\sum_{i} G_i(s, a)}{N(s, a)}$$

This is critical because **without a model, value-based policy improvement $\pi'(s) = \arg\max_a \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\{r + \gamma v_\pi(s')\}$ is impossible** (we don't know $p$). So we learn $Q(s, a)$ directly and improve via:

$$\pi'(s) \;=\; \arg\max_{a} Q(s, a)$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Equation | Meaning | Used For |
|---------|----------|---------|----------|
| Return | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{T-t-1} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | Cumulative discounted reward | Foundation of MC learning |
| Recursive return | $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$ | Used in incremental updates | Online computation |
| State-value | $v_\pi(s) = E_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s]$ | True value of state under $\pi$ | Prediction target |
| Action-value | $q_\pi(s,a) = E_\pi[G_t \mid S_t=s, A_t=a]$ | True value of state-action under $\pi$ | Control target |
| First-visit MC estimate | $V(s) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} G_i^{(1)}(s)$ | Sample mean after first visits | Prediction |
| Every-visit MC estimate | $V(s) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} G_i(s)$ | Sample mean after every visit | Prediction |
| Incremental update | $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha [G_t - V(S_t)]$ | Online weight update | Incremental impl. |
| Greedy policy improvement | $\pi'(s) = \arg\max_a Q(s,a)$ | Pick best action by Q-value | Deterministic control |
| $\varepsilon$-greedy | $\pi(a \mid s) = 1 - \varepsilon + \varepsilon/\vert\mathcal{A}\vert$ if $a^*$, else $\varepsilon/\vert\mathcal{A}\vert$ | Random action with prob. $\varepsilon$ | On-policy exploration |
| GLIE conditions | (i) All state-action pairs visited infinitely often; (ii) Policy converges to greedy | Guarantees convergence to $q_*$ | MC Control theory |
| Importance sampling ratio | $\rho_{t:T-1} = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{\mu(A_k \mid S_k)}$ | Reweight returns | Off-policy MC |
| Ordinary importance sampling | $V(s) = \frac{\sum \rho_i\, G_i}{\sum \rho_i}$ | Weighted sample mean | Off-policy, unbiased |
| Weighted importance sampling | $V(s) = \frac{\sum \rho_i\, G_i}{N}$ | Unnormalized weighted mean | Off-policy, lower variance |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Monte Carlo RL is heavily used in:
- **Game playing AI** (Backgammon, Go sub-routines, Atari)
- **Robotics** (locomotion policies learned from rollouts)
- **Operations research** (inventory management, queue scheduling)
- **Autonomous driving** (policy evaluation from logged trajectories)
- **Dialogue systems** (training chat-bots from full conversations)

> [!NOTE]
> The **Blackjack example** in Sutton & Barto is the canonical KTU textbook example for MC Prediction — make sure you can describe it: states are (player's sum, dealer's showing card, usable ace), actions are hit/stick, reward is +1 win, -1 loss, 0 draw. MC can find the optimal policy without knowing dealer probabilities.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why the Sample Mean Equals the Expected Return

**Goal:** Show that the MC estimator $V_N(s) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N G_i(s)$ converges to $v_\pi(s) = E_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s]$.

Assume $G_1, G_2, \ldots, G_N$ are i.i.d. samples of the return $G_t$ given $S_t = s$, each with true mean $\mu = v_\pi(s)$ and finite variance $\sigma^2$.

By definition of expected value:

$$E[V_N(s)] \;=\; E\!\left[ \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} G_i \right]$$

By linearity of expectation:

$$E[V_N(s)] \;=\; \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} E[G_i] \;=\; \frac{1}{N} \cdot N \cdot v_\pi(s) \;=\; v_\pi(s)$$

So $V_N(s)$ is an **unbiased estimator**. Now apply the Weak Law of Large Numbers:

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} P\!\left( \big\vert V_N(s) - v_\pi(s) \big\vert > \epsilon \right) \;\leq\; \frac{\sigma^2}{N\epsilon^2} \;\to\; 0$$

Hence $V_N(s) \to v_\pi(s)$ in probability. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation: Incremental Update from Sample Mean

**Goal:** Show that the update rule
$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha [G_t - V(S_t)]$
is algebraically equivalent to the running mean $V_n = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} G_i$ when $\alpha = 1/n$.

Let $V_n$ be the estimate after $n$ observations. We want to derive $V_{n+1}$ from $V_n$ and the new sample $G_{n+1}$:

$$V_{n+1} \;=\; \frac{1}{n+1}\sum_{i=1}^{n+1} G_i$$

Split the sum into the first $n$ samples and the new one:

$$V_{n+1} \;=\; \frac{1}{n+1}\left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} G_i \;+\; G_{n+1} \right)$$

Note that $\sum_{i=1}^{n} G_i = n V_n$ by definition of the running mean:

$$V_{n+1} \;=\; \frac{1}{n+1}\left( n V_n + G_{n+1} \right)$$

Expand and rearrange:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{n+1} &= \frac{n}{n+1} V_n + \frac{1}{n+1} G_{n+1} \\
&= V_n - \frac{1}{n+1} V_n + \frac{1}{n+1} G_{n+1} \\
&= V_n + \frac{1}{n+1}\left( G_{n+1} - V_n \right)
\end{aligned}$$

Setting $\alpha_n = \frac{1}{n+1}$ gives the incremental rule. This shows that the MC incremental update **exactly computes the sample mean** when the step size decays as $1/n$. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Derivation: Convergence of MC with Exploring Starts

**Theorem (MC-ES Convergence):** With exploring starts (every $(s, a)$ pair has nonzero probability of being the starting point) and visiting each state-action pair infinitely often, MC Control converges to the optimal action-value function $q_*$.

**Sketch of proof** (for KTU exam purposes — full proof uses the Policy Improvement Theorem):

1. **Policy evaluation step:** By 3.1, the MC estimate $Q(s, a) \to q_{\pi_k}(s, a)$ as visits $\to \infty$.
2. **Policy improvement step:** For deterministic policy $\pi_k$, the new policy

$$\pi_{k+1}(s) = \arg\max_{a} q_{\pi_k}(s, a)$$

satisfies $q_{\pi_{k+1}}(s, \pi_{k+1}(s)) \geq q_{\pi_k}(s, \pi_k(s))$ for all $s$, by the Policy Improvement Theorem:

$$\begin{aligned}
v_{\pi_{k+1}}(s) &= \max_{a} q_{\pi_k}(s, a) \\
&\geq q_{\pi_k}(s, \pi_k(s)) \\
&= v_{\pi_k}(s)
\end{aligned}$$

3. **Strict improvement** holds as long as $\pi_{k+1} \neq \pi_k$ on some state. Since the state and action spaces are finite, only finitely many policies exist, so the sequence $\{\pi_k\}$ must converge to a fixed point $\pi_*$ that is **greedy w.r.t. itself** — i.e., $q_{\pi_*}(s, \pi_*(s)) = \max_a q_{\pi_*}(s, a)$ for all $s$, which by the Bellman optimality equation means $\pi_*$ is optimal. $\blacksquare$

### 3.4 Derivation: Importance Sampling Ratio for Off-Policy MC

**Goal:** Derive why the importance sampling ratio $\rho_{t:T-1} = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{\mu(A_k \mid S_k)}$ corrects for the distribution mismatch between behavior policy $\mu$ and target policy $\pi$.

Consider the return $G_t$ generated under behavior policy $\mu$. We want to estimate $E_\pi[G_t]$. The trajectory probability under $\pi$ given $H_t = (S_0, A_0, \ldots, S_t, A_t)$:

$$P(H_t) = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \pi(A_k \mid S_k) \, p(S_{k+1} \mid S_k, A_k)$$

Under $\mu$:

$$P_\mu(H_t) = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \mu(A_k \mid S_k) \, p(S_{k+1} \mid S_k, A_k)$$

Dividing, the transition probabilities **cancel** (this is the magic of importance sampling in RL):

$$\frac{P(H_t)}{P_\mu(H_t)} = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{\mu(A_k \mid S_k)} = \rho_{t:T-1}$$

Now the expected return under $\pi$:

$$E_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s] = \sum_{h} P(h \mid s)\, G(h) = \sum_{h} P_\mu(h \mid s)\, \frac{P(h \mid s)}{P_\mu(h \mid s)}\, G(h) = E_\mu[\rho_{t:T-1} G_t \mid S_t = s]$$

Hence the off-policy MC estimate is the sample mean of $\rho_i G_i$ (ordinary IS) or $\frac{\sum \rho_i G_i}{\sum \rho_i}$ (weighted IS). $\blacksquare$

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — MC vs Other RL Methods

| Property | Monte Carlo | Temporal-Difference (TD) | Dynamic Programming |
|----------|-------------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| Requires model? | No | No | Yes |
| Requires episode end? | Yes | No | N/A |
| Bootstraps? | No | Yes | Yes |
| Update target | $G_t$ (actual return) | $R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$ | $E[R + \gamma V(S')]$ |
| Bias | Unbiased | Biased (bootstrapped) | Unbiased |
| Variance | High (depends on return) | Lower | Zero (deterministic) |
| Convergence | Yes, with exploring starts/GLIE | Yes (e.g., tabular TD(0)) | Yes (with model) |
| Online? | No (must wait for episode end) | Yes | N/A |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Mantra:** MC = *high variance, zero bias, no bootstrapping, model-free, episodic*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Monte Carlo Prediction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize policy pi and V s = 0] --> B[Start episode: sample initial state S0]
    B --> C[Agent observes St]
    C --> D[Action At ~ pi]
    D --> E[Environment returns reward Rt+1 and next state St+1]
    E --> F{Is St+1 terminal?}
    F -- No --> C
    F -- Yes --> G[Compute Return Gt for each timestep t]
    G --> H[For each visited state-action: update N s,a and Q s,a]
    H --> I[Update policy: pi s = argmaxa Q s,a]
    I --> J{Convergence check: delta less than threshold}
    J -- No --> B
    J -- Yes --> K[Output optimal policy pi star and Q star]
```

### 4.2 Schematic: On-Policy MC Control (ε-soft) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Behavior policy mu = epsilon-soft] --> A[Generate episode S0 A0 R1 ... ST]
    A --> B[Collect trajectory tau]
    B --> C[Compute returns Gt for each t]
    C --> D[Update Q S At using incremental mean]
    D --> E[Improve policy to epsilon-greedy wrt Q]
    E --> F{GLIE conditions satisfied?}
    F -- No --> P
    F -- Yes --> G[Output optimal Q star]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Off-Policy MC with Importance Sampling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Behavior policy mu explores] --> B[Generate episode under mu]
    B --> C[Record trajectory: S, A, R]
    C --> D[For terminal episode: compute return Gt]
    D --> E[Compute importance ratio rho = product of pi A k S k / mu A k S k]
    E --> F[Update weighted IS estimate V s = sum rho i G i / sum rho i]
    F --> G[Refine target policy pi toward greedy wrt Q]
    G --> H{Convergence to q star?}
    H -- No --> A
    H -- Yes --> I[Output optimal policy]
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Map: Place of Monte Carlo in RL Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RL[Reinforcement Learning]
    RL --> MB[Model-Based]
    RL --> MF[Model-Free]
    MB --> DP[Dynamic Programming: VI and PI]
    MF --> MC[Monte Carlo Methods]
    MF --> TD[Temporal Difference Methods]
    MC --> MCpred[MC Prediction: estimate V pi]
    MC --> MCctrl[MC Control: find pi star]
    MCctrl --> ES[Exploring Starts]
    MCctrl --> OnPol[On-Policy epsilon-soft]
    MCctrl --> OffPol[Off-Policy with IS]
    TD --> TD0[TD 0]
    TD --> Sarsa[Sarsa]
    TD --> QL[Q-Learning]
    TD --> DP[DP]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define the Monte Carlo method in Reinforcement Learning. Why is it called "model-free"?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Monte Carlo (MC) methods in Reinforcement Learning are a class of learning algorithms that estimate the value function $V(s)$ or $Q(s, a)$ — and consequently discover an optimal policy — **by averaging the sample returns observed from complete episodes of interaction with the environment**.

It is called *model-free* because the algorithm **does not require knowledge of the environment's transition probabilities $P(s' \mid s, a)$ or reward function $R(s, a, s')$**. The agent learns purely from sampled experience (state, action, reward sequences), making it directly applicable to environments where the dynamics are unknown or too complex to specify mathematically.

Unlike Dynamic Programming, MC does not bootstrap — it uses the *actual* observed return $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \ldots$ as the learning target. The Law of Large Numbers guarantees that the sample mean of returns converges to the true expected return as the number of episodes grows.

> **Marking key (1 mark each):** (1) Definition with sample-average return; (2) No knowledge of $P$ or $R$ required; (3) Episodic learning, no bootstrapping.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Differentiate between First-Visit and Every-Visit Monte Carlo methods. Which one has stronger theoretical guarantees and why?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | First-Visit MC | Every-Visit MC |
|--------|----------------|----------------|
| Definition | Averages returns only after the *first* occurrence of state $s$ in each episode | Averages returns after *every* occurrence of state $s$ in each episode |
| Data usage | Fewer samples per episode | More samples per episode |
| Statistical property | Returns are i.i.d. | Returns are correlated (same episode) |
| Convergence proof | Direct application of LLN | Slightly more involved |

**First-Visit MC** has stronger theoretical guarantees because the returns $G_i(s)$ following first visits to $s$ across different episodes are **independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.)** random variables. This allows a direct application of the **Law of Large Numbers** to prove that $V(s) \to v_\pi(s)$ as the number of visits $N(s) \to \infty$, and the **Central Limit Theorem** applies for confidence intervals. Every-Visit MC also converges but the proof must handle the dependence between within-episode returns.

> **Marking key:** (1 mark) Clear definitions of both; (1 mark) Tabular or explicit comparison; (1 mark) First-Visit has i.i.d. samples → LLN applies directly.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

**Note:** As per KTU ESE convention, students answer **one** of the two alternatives.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — MC Prediction and Incremental Updates

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the Monte Carlo Prediction algorithm to estimate $V(s)$ under a fixed policy $\pi$. Describe both the *first-visit* and *every-visit* variants with the appropriate formulas. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the incremental update rule for MC prediction starting from the running sample mean, and explain its advantages over the batch implementation. Show the connection with the stochastic approximation step-size $\alpha$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) MC Prediction (7 marks)**

**Algorithm Outline:**

1. Initialize $V(s) \in \mathbb{R}$ arbitrarily, returns list $Returns(s) = []$ for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$.
2. **Loop forever** (for each episode):
   - Generate an episode $S_0, A_0, R_1, S_1, A_1, R_2, \ldots, S_T$ using policy $\pi$.
   - $G \leftarrow 0$.
   - **Loop for each step** $t = T-1, T-2, \ldots, 0$:
     - $G \leftarrow \gamma G + R_{t+1}$.
     - *If first-visit MC*: append $G$ to $Returns(S_t)$ only if $S_t$ has not appeared earlier in the episode.
     - *If every-visit MC*: append $G$ to $Returns(S_t)$ on every visit.
   - $V(s) \leftarrow \text{average}(Returns(s))$ for all $s$.

**Formulas:**

$$V(s) \;=\; \frac{1}{N(s)} \sum_{i=1}^{N(s)} G_i(s)$$

where $G_i(s)$ is the return observed after the $i$-th (first or every) visit to $s$.

By the **Law of Large Numbers**, as $N(s) \to \infty$, $V(s) \to v_\pi(s)$ for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$.

**Example:** In the Blackjack environment (player sum, dealer card, usable ace), running MC for thousands of episodes yields the optimal "hit below 20, stick at 20 or 21" policy. The value function differs based on whether the player has a usable ace (more flexible hitting strategy).

**(b) Incremental Update (7 marks)**

Starting from the running mean $V_n(s) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n G_i(s)$, derive the update rule:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{n+1}(s) &= \frac{1}{n+1} \sum_{i=1}^{n+1} G_i(s) \\
&= \frac{1}{n+1}\left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} G_i(s) + G_{n+1}(s) \right) \\
&= \frac{1}{n+1}\left( n\, V_n(s) + G_{n+1}(s) \right) \\
&= V_n(s) + \frac{1}{n+1}\Big( G_{n+1}(s) - V_n(s) \Big)
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting $\alpha_n = \frac{1}{n+1}$:

$$V(S_t) \;\leftarrow\; V(S_t) + \alpha \big[ G_t - V(S_t) \big]$$

**Advantages of incremental over batch:**

1. **Memory efficient** — only stores one running estimate per state, not all returns.
2. **Online learning** — updates happen immediately after each episode, no need to wait until "enough" data is collected.
3. **Adaptive step size** — using $\alpha$ instead of $1/n$ allows the algorithm to *track a non-stationary target* (when the policy changes, the true value function drifts).
4. **Compatible with control** — the same template is reused in TD learning and Q-learning.

The general form $V \leftarrow V + \alpha(\text{target} - V)$ is the **stochastic approximation template** that unifies all of RL.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — MC Control with Exploring Starts & GLIE

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the Monte Carlo Control algorithm using **Exploring Starts** with the $\varepsilon$-soft policy improvement theorem. State the algorithm and explain why "exploring starts" is required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **GLIE** (Greedy in the Limit with Infinite Exploration) conditions. Show how MC Control with an $\varepsilon$-soft policy that decays $\varepsilon_k = 1/k$ satisfies GLIE and converges to the optimal action-value function. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) MC Control with Exploring Starts (7 marks)**

**The Generalised Policy Iteration (GPI) loop in MC:**

1. **Policy evaluation** using MC: estimate $q_{\pi_k}(s, a)$ for current policy $\pi_k$.
2. **Policy improvement:** $\pi_{k+1}(s) = \arg\max_a q_{\pi_k}(s, a)$.
3. Repeat.

**Exploring Starts (ES) Assumption:**
The initial state-action pair $(S_0, A_0)$ is chosen **randomly such that every state-action pair has a non-zero probability of being the start**. This guarantees that *every* $(s, a)$ will eventually be visited infinitely often, so the MC estimate $Q(s, a)$ converges for all pairs — without this, some pairs may never be sampled and we cannot guarantee $\pi_{k+1}$ improves on $\pi_k$ everywhere.

**Algorithm (MC-ES):**

- Initialize $\pi(s) \in \mathcal{A}(s)$ and $Q(s, a) = 0$, $Returns(s, a) = []$.
- **Loop forever:**
  - Choose $S_0 \in \mathcal{S}$, $A_0 \in \mathcal{A}(S_0)$ randomly such that all pairs have prob $> 0$.
  - Generate an episode from $S_0, A_0$ following $\pi$.
  - For each $(s, a)$ in the episode: $G \leftarrow$ return following first visit. Append $G$ to $Returns(s,a)$. $Q(s, a) \leftarrow \text{avg}(Returns(s, a))$.
  - $\pi(s) \leftarrow \arg\max_a Q(s, a)$ for every $s$ in the episode.

**Limitation of ES:** The "random initial state-action" assumption is *impractical* in real environments (e.g., you cannot teleport a robot to an arbitrary state). This motivates the $\varepsilon$-soft / GLIE approach.

**(b) GLIE and $\varepsilon$-decay (7 marks)**

**GLIE Conditions:**

1. **Infinite exploration:** Every state-action pair $(s, a)$ is visited infinitely often:
$$\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} P\big( (s, a) \text{ visited at episode } k \big) = \infty \quad \forall (s, a)$$

2. **Greedy in the limit:** The policy converges to a greedy policy:
$$\lim_{k \to \infty} \pi_k(a \mid s) = \mathbf{1}\!\left[ a = \arg\max_{a'} Q_k(s, a') \right]$$

**Proof sketch that $\varepsilon_k = 1/k$ satisfies GLIE:**

*Exploration:* The probability of choosing a non-greedy action is $\varepsilon_k = 1/k$. For any $(s, a)$ with $a \neq a^*$, the probability it is selected when in state $s$ is $\geq \varepsilon_k / \vert\mathcal{A}\vert = \frac{1}{k \vert\mathcal{A}\vert}$. Since $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{k}$ diverges, by Borel-Cantelli (or comparison with the harmonic series), every $(s, a)$ is visited infinitely often. ✓

*Greediness:* $\varepsilon_k = 1/k \to 0$ as $k \to \infty$. So the policy becomes greedy in the limit. ✓

**Convergence theorem:** Under GLIE, MC Control with $\varepsilon$-greedy policy improvement converges to the optimal action-value function $q_*$:

$$\lim_{k \to \infty} Q_k(s, a) = q_*(s, a) \quad \forall (s, a)$$

and the policy $\pi_k$ converges to an optimal policy $\pi_*$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (MC Topic):**
> 1. **Confusing MC with TD:** Students often write "MC uses $R + \gamma V(S')$" — that is **TD(0)**, NOT MC. MC uses the *full return* $G_t$.
> 2. **Forgetting episodic requirement:** MC cannot be used for continuing tasks. State this explicitly if asked.
> 3. **Mixing on-policy and off-policy formulas:** On-policy uses sample mean of $G_t$; off-policy uses $\rho_t G_t$ with importance sampling. Don't mix them.
> 4. **Skipping the "why exploring starts":** Many students just write the algorithm but cannot explain *why* ES is needed (to guarantee coverage of all $(s, a)$).
> 5. **Mark loss on convergence proofs:** Always cite the **Law of Large Numbers** for MC and the **Policy Improvement Theorem** for control.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **MC is model-free + episodic + no bootstrapping** — this is its identity.
- The **return** $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{T-t-1} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ is the learning target; **never** use a one-step bootstrap in MC.
- **First-Visit MC** uses i.i.d. returns (one per episode) → strong LLN guarantees.
- **Every-Visit MC** uses all returns (may be correlated within an episode) → still converges.
- **Incremental update** $V \leftarrow V + \alpha [G - V]$ is equivalent to running mean when $\alpha = 1/n$.
- For **control**, we must learn the **action-value** $Q(s, a)$ since we cannot compute $\max_a \sum p(\cdot)[\cdot]$ without a model.
- **Exploring Starts** ensures every $(s, a)$ is visited; **$\varepsilon$-soft policies** are the practical alternative.
- **GLIE** = infinite exploration + greedy in the limit; using $\varepsilon_k = 1/k$ is a canonical example.
- **Off-policy MC** uses **importance sampling ratio** $\rho_{t:T-1} = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{\mu(A_k \mid S_k)}$ to correct distribution mismatch; ordinary IS is unbiased, weighted IS has lower variance.
- **Comparison summary:** MC = high variance, zero bias; TD = lower variance, some bias; DP = zero variance, requires model.
- **Canonical KTU example:** Blackjack — states are (player sum, dealer card, usable ace), actions are hit/stick.
- **Convergence guarantees:** MC prediction converges if all states are visited infinitely often; MC-ES converges to optimal $\pi_*$; MC with GLIE $\varepsilon$-soft also converges to $\pi_*$.
- **Memory tip for KTU exam:** *"MC waits to see the full picture before learning"* — this is the single phrase that captures its core identity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TD Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITT501 - MACHINE LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** TD Learning # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Temporal Difference (TD) Learning** is a model-free, bootstrapping method in Reinforcement Learning (RL) used to estimate the value function $V_\pi(s)$ of a given policy $\pi$ by learning directly from raw experience (sampled trajectories) **without requiring a model of the environment's dynamics** (no need for transition probabilities $P(s' \vert s,a)$ or reward function $R(s,a,s')$). The core TD update rule for the simplest case, **TD(0)**, is: $$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t) \right]$$ where: - $V(S_t)$ is the current estimate of the value of state $S_t$ - $\alpha$ is the **learning rate** (step size), $0 < \alpha \leq 1$ - $\gamma$ is the **discount factor**, $0 \leq \gamma < 1$ - $R_{t+1}$ is the observed reward after taking action from $S_t$ - $V(S_{t+1})$ is the estimate of the next state's value - The bracketed term is the **TD error**, denoted $\delta_t$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Identifier:** TD learning uniquely combines two paradigms: **(i) Sampling** from Monte Carlo (MC) methods (learns from experience) and **(ii) Bootstrapping** from Dynamic Programming (DP) (updates estimates from other estimates, not waiting for final outcome). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Daily Commute Weather" Intuition Imagine you are a student in Kerala trying to predict how long your **daily commute from home to college** will take. - **Monte Carlo (MC) approach:** You drive to college, note the *actual total travel time* (say 47 minutes), and only at the end of the journey do you update your belief. Problem: you are stuck in traffic for the whole journey before learning anything. - **Dynamic Programming (DP) approach:** You would need a perfect model of every traffic signal, every road, every car's behaviour — impossible to obtain. - **TD Learning approach:** As you travel, you *periodically glance at the clock* and at the *current traffic situation* (state), and continuously revise your estimate: *"If the rest of the road behaves like what I see now, I should arrive in 35 minutes."* When the actual remaining time (revealed by a traffic app) differs from this estimate, you nudge your prediction **up or down** — this nudge *is the TD error*. This is the essence: **predict, observe a piece of reality, correct the prediction immediately, and move on** — no full episode completion needed, no model of traffic needed. --- ## 1.3 The Three Pillars of RL — Where TD Sits | Method | Requires Model? | Uses Bootstrapping? | Waits for Episode End? | |---|---|---|---| | Dynamic Programming (DP) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | | Monte Carlo (MC) | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | | **Temporal Difference (TD)** | **❌ No** | **✅ Yes** | **❌ No** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **TD Learning = MC + DP.** It is the *only* method that is simultaneously **model-free**, **bootstrapping**, and **online / incremental**. This is why Sutton & Barto positioned it as the central, unifying idea of modern RL. --- ## 1.4 Why TD Learning Matters in Engineering (KTU Context) TD methods are foundational to: - **Robotics control** (continuous online policy improvement) - **Game AI** (TD-Gammon, AlphaGo's self-play loops) - **Autonomous navigation** (path planning under uncertainty) - **Recommendation systems** (long-term user engagement modelling) - **Network packet routing** (each packet is an "episode step") - **Industrial process control** (real-time adaptive controllers) The standard metric for convergence is the **mean-squared TD error (MSTDE)**, and the principal hyperparameter trio is the **{learning rate $\alpha$, discount factor $\gamma$, trace-decay $\lambda$ for TD($\lambda$)}**. --- ## 1.5 Roadmap of This Module In these notes we progress from the simplest case (TD(0) prediction) to control algorithms (SARSA, Q-Learning) and then to the unified TD($\lambda$) with eligibility traces. By the end, you should be able to: 1. Write down the TD update from memory. 2. Justify the bias-variance trade-off of TD vs MC. 3. Solve a 2-step numerical example exactly the way the KTU examiner expects. 4. Differentiate on-policy (SARSA) from off-policy (Q-Learning) using the TD error. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The TD(0) Algorithm — Step-by-Step Operational Logic **Input:** policy $\pi$ to be evaluated (all episodes assumed to start in some state distribution). **Initialize:** $V(s) \in \mathbb{R}$ arbitrarily for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$; $V(\text{terminal}) = 0$. **Repeat (for each episode):** 1. Initialize $S$ (start state). 2. **Repeat (for each step of the episode):** - $A \leftarrow$ action given by $\pi$ for $S$ - Take action $A$, observe reward $R$ and next state $S'$ - $V(S) \leftarrow V(S) + \alpha \left[ R + \gamma V(S') - V(S) \right]$ - $S \leftarrow S'$ 3. **Until** $S$ is terminal. --- ## 2.2 The TD Error — The Heart of Everything The TD error is the *one-step* prediction error: $$\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why "Temporal Difference"?** It is literally the **difference** between two **temporally successive** predictions: the prediction made at time $t+1$ (of the future, $R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$) minus the prediction made at time $t$ (of the same future, $V(S_t)$). The name *temporal difference* is the literal English meaning of the formula. ### Properties of $\delta_t$: - If the value function $V$ is **perfect** (matches $v_\pi$), then the expected value of $\delta_t$ is **zero** under policy $\pi$: $$\mathbb{E}_\pi[\delta_t \vert S_t = s] = \mathbb{E}_\pi[R_{t+1} + \gamma v_\pi(S_{t+1}) \vert S_t = s] - v_\pi(s) = 0$$ - $\delta_t$ performs the role of the **"innovation"** or **"surprise"** signal — large $|\delta_t|$ ⇒ the agent was wrong, and the value should shift. --- ## 2.3 TD(0) vs Monte Carlo vs DP — The Bias-Variance Trade-off | Aspect | Monte Carlo | TD(0) | DP | |---|---|---|---| | Update target | $G_t$ (true return) | $R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$ | $\mathbb{E}[R + \gamma V(S')]$ | | Bias | **Zero** (unbiased) | **Biased** (uses estimate) | **Biased** (if $V$ wrong) | | Variance | **High** (noisy) | **Low** (uses one random sample) | **Zero** (deterministic) | | Needs model? | No | No | Yes | | Convergence guarantee | Yes (with decaying $\alpha$) | Yes (with appropriate $\alpha$) | Yes (with sweep) | | Online? | No (must wait) | **Yes** | Yes (requires full backup) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Batch TD and the Certainty-Equivalence Principle:** Given a *fixed* finite batch of experience, the TD(0) estimate converges to the **maximum-likelihood estimate of $v_\pi$** for a Markov model — i.e., it finds the same answer as DP would, but *without* being given the model. This is a major result for KTU 14-mark theory questions. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Table Note:** To prevent markdown table breakage, the absolute-value and conditional bars are written as `\vert` and `\mid`. | # | Concept | Formula | Key Variables / Range | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **TD(0) Value Update** | $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \, \delta_t$ | $\alpha \in (0,1]$ | | 2 | **TD Error (1-step)** | $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$ | $\gamma \in [0,1)$ | | 3 | **n-step Return** | $G_t^{(n)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \cdots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^n V(S_{t+n})$ | $n \geq 1$ | | 4 | **Average of n-step Returns** | $G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1} G_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1} G_t$ | $\lambda \in [0,1]$ | | 5 | **Eligibility Trace (replacing trace)** | $e_t(s) = \begin{cases} \gamma\lambda \, e_{t-1}(s) & s \neq S_t \\ \gamma\lambda \, e_{t-1}(s) + 1 & s = S_t \end{cases}$ | $\gamma\lambda$ decay | | 6 | **TD($\lambda$) Accumulating Trace** | $e_t(s) = \gamma\lambda \, e_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$ | visits count up | | 7 | **TD($\lambda$) Update** | $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \, \delta_t \, e_t(s)$ | for all $s$ | | 8 | **SARSA Update** | $Q(S,A) \leftarrow Q(S,A) + \alpha\left[R + \gamma Q(S',A') - Q(S,A)\right]$ | on-policy | | 9 | **Q-Learning Update** | $Q(S,A) \leftarrow Q(S,A) + \alpha\left[R + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S',a') - Q(S,A)\right]$ | off-policy | | 10 | **Bellman Optimality (used by Q-Learning)** | $Q^*(s,a) = \mathbb{E}\left[R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(S_{t+1},a') \vert S_t=s, A_t=a\right]$ | contraction mapping | | 11 | **Convergence Condition (Robbins-Monro)** | $\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t(s,a) = \infty \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t^2(s,a) < \infty$ | all $(s,a)$ visited infinitely often | | 12 | **TD(0) Mean Squared Error** | $\text{MSTDE} = \mathbb{E}\left[\delta_t^2\right]$ | convergence metric | --- ## 2.5 SARSA vs Q-Learning — The On-Policy / Off-Policy Distinction > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic:** **S**ARSA uses the quintuple $(S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$ — it includes $A_{t+1}$, so it needs the *next action actually taken* by the behaviour policy. Q-Learning uses the *max* over next actions, decoupling the update from what the agent actually does. | Property | SARSA | Q-Learning | |---|---|---| | Policy updated | Behaviour policy = Target policy | Behaviour $\neq$ Target | | Bootstrap uses | $Q(S',A')$ — actual next action | $\max_{a'} Q(S',a')$ | | Learns | $Q^\pi$ (value of policy used) | $Q^*$ (optimal value) | | Convergence | To $Q^\pi$ for the policy being followed | To $Q^*$ (under standard GLIE) | | Risk sensitivity | Safer on cliff-walking (penalises risky $\epsilon$-greedy actions) | Cuts the corner (more aggressive) | | Update target | $R + \gamma Q(S',A')$ | $R + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S',a')$ | --- ## 2.6 Engineering Utility — Why TD is the Workhorse | Application | Why TD Fits | |---|---| | Real-time control of robotic arms | Online, no model of friction/dynamics required | | Adaptive traffic-light control | Episode = a day; TD updates each cycle instantly | | Stock trading agents | Reward is delayed (P&L realised later); TD can use intermediate price moves | | Dialogue systems | Long-term user-satisfaction reward; immediate turn-level feedback via TD | | Energy-grid dispatch | Continuous state, expensive to enumerate; TD samples on the fly | > [!WARNING] > **Convergence Caveat:** Without visiting all state-action pairs infinitely often **and** satisfying the **Robbins-Monro conditions** on $\alpha$, TD and Q-Learning are **not guaranteed** to converge. This is a frequently tested KTU 14-mark theoretical point. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From the Bellman Equation to the TD(0) Update We begin from the **Bellman equation for the value function under policy $\pi$**: $$v_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma v_\pi(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t = s \right]$$ **Step 1:** Take a single *sample* of the expectation by actually taking one step in the environment. The sampled quantity is: $$\hat{v} = R_{t+1} + \gamma v_\pi(S_{t+1})$$ This is the **TD target** — a *noisy, unbiased* sample of the right-hand side of the Bellman equation. **Step 2:** The TD error is the *deviation* of our current estimate $V(S_t)$ from this sampled target: $$\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$$ *Comment:* $\delta_t$ is the random variable whose expectation under the true distribution is **zero** (by the Bellman equation). **Step 3:** Perform a **gradient-style** update to minimise the squared error $\tfrac{1}{2}\delta_t^2$: $$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) - \alpha \frac{\partial}{\partial V(S_t)} \tfrac{1}{2}\delta_t^2$$ The derivative w.r.t. $V(S_t)$ is $-\delta_t$ (since $\delta_t = \text{target} - V(S_t)$): $$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \delta_t$$ **Step 4:** Substitute $\delta_t$: $$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t) \right]$$ This is the **TD(0) update rule**, derived rigorously from the Bellman equation and stochastic gradient descent on the one-step squared error. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Why TD(0) Converges to the Correct $v_\pi$ We show that the fixed point of the TD(0) operator equals the true value function. Define the **TD operator** $T^\pi : \mathbb{R}^{\vert \mathcal{S} \vert} \to \mathbb{R}^{\vert \mathcal{S} \vert}$: $$(T^\pi V)(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t = s \right]$$ **Step 1:** Show $T^\pi$ is a **$\gamma$-contraction** in max-norm. For any two value functions $V$ and $U$: $$ \begin{aligned} \| T^\pi V - T^\pi U \|_\infty &= \max_s \left\vert \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[ \gamma (V(S') - U(S')) \mid S_t = s \right] \right\vert \\ &\leq \max_s \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[ \vert V(S') - U(S') \vert \mid S_t = s \right] \\ &\leq \gamma \, \max_{s'} \vert V(s') - U(s') \vert \\ &= \gamma \, \| V - U \|_\infty \end{aligned} $$ *Comment:* Contraction by factor $\gamma < 1$. **Step 2:** By **Banach's fixed-point theorem**, repeated application of $T^\pi$ converges to a unique fixed point $V^*$, and that fixed point is exactly the solution of the Bellman equation — which is $v_\pi$. **Step 3:** TD(0)'s stochastic update **samples** $T^\pi V$ once per step, with **diminishing noise** as $\alpha_t \to 0$ (Robbins-Monro). Therefore the iterates converge in expectation to $v_\pi$. $$\boxed{ \lim_{t \to \infty} V_t(s) = v_\pi(s), \quad \forall s \in \mathcal{S} }$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The $n$-step TD Return The $n$-step return rewards the agent $n$ steps into the future, then bootstraps: $$G_t^{(n)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \cdots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^n V(S_{t+n})$$ **Special cases:** - $n = 1$: $G_t^{(1)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$ — this is the **TD(0) target**. - $n = T - t$: $G_t^{(T-t)} = G_t$ — this is the **Monte Carlo return** (no bootstrap). **Derivation of the $n$-step update:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Error: } \delta_t^{(n)} &= G_t^{(n)} - V(S_t) \\ \text{Update: } V(S_t) &\leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \left[ G_t^{(n)} - V(S_t) \right] \end{aligned} $$ *Comment:* Larger $n$ ⇒ less bias, more variance; smaller $n$ ⇒ more bias, less variance. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — The $\lambda$-Return and TD($\lambda$) The **$\lambda$-return** is a geometric average of all $n$-step returns, with weights $(1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1}$: $$G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1} G_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1} G_t$$ **Boundary check (KTU favourite!):** - If $\lambda = 0$: $G_t^\lambda = G_t^{(1)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$ ⇒ **TD(0)**. - If $\lambda = 1$: $G_t^\lambda = G_t$ ⇒ **Monte Carlo**. **Derivation of the eligibility-trace formulation:** Define the **accumulating eligibility trace**: $$e_t(s) = \gamma \lambda \, e_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$$ Then the TD($\lambda$) update is equivalent to applying the $\lambda$-return error to *all* visited states: $$V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \, \delta_t \, e_t(s), \quad \forall s$$ **Equivalence proof sketch:** $$ \begin{aligned} \sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \alpha \, \delta_t \, e_t(s) &= \alpha \sum_{t \,:\, S_t = s} \delta_t \cdot (\gamma\lambda)^{k_t} \end{aligned} $$ where $k_t$ = steps back to the *previous* visit of $s$. This weights each state's update by how recently and how often it was visited — equivalent to backing up the $\lambda$-return to the most recent visit. --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — TD(0) on a 4-State Chain **Setup:** A Markov Reward Process with states $\{A, B, C, D\}$, terminal $D$. The chain is deterministic: $A \to B \to C \to D$. All rewards $R = 1$, $\gamma = 0.9$. **Initialise:** $V(A)=V(B)=V(C)=V(D)=0$. Use $\alpha = 0.5$. **Episode trajectory:** $(A, R=1) \to (B, R=1) \to (C, R=1) \to (D, R=1, \text{terminal})$ **Iteration t=1 (at A, transitioning to B):** $$ \begin{aligned} \delta_1 &= R + \gamma V(B) - V(A) = 1 + 0.9 \cdot 0 - 0 = 1 \\ V(A) &\leftarrow 0 + 0.5 \cdot 1 = 0.5 \end{aligned} $$ **Iteration t=2 (at B, transitioning to C):** $$ \begin{aligned} \delta_2 &= 1 + 0.9 \cdot V(C) - V(B) = 1 + 0 - 0 = 1 \\ V(B) &\leftarrow 0 + 0.5 = 0.5 \end{aligned} $$ **Iteration t=3 (at C, transitioning to D):** $$ \begin{aligned} \delta_3 &= 1 + 0.9 \cdot V(D) - V(C) = 1 + 0 - 0 = 1 \\ V(C) &\leftarrow 0 + 0.5 = 0.5 \end{aligned} $$ **Iteration t=4 (terminal D, no update).** Episode ends. **Second episode — values now: $V(A)=V(B)=V(C)=0.5$, $V(D)=0$:** **t=1:** $$ \begin{aligned} \delta_1 &= 1 + 0.9 \cdot 0.5 - 0.5 = 1 + 0.45 - 0.5 = 0.95 \\ V(A) &\leftarrow 0.5 + 0.5 \cdot 0.95 = 0.5 + 0.475 = 0.975 \end{aligned} $$ **t=2:** $$ \begin{aligned} \delta_2 &= 1 + 0.9 \cdot 0.5 - 0.5 = 0.95 \\ V(B) &\leftarrow 0.5 + 0.5 \cdot 0.95 = 0.975 \end{aligned} $$ **t=3:** $$ \begin{aligned} \delta_3 &= 1 + 0.9 \cdot 0 - 0.5 = 0.5 \\ V(C) &\leftarrow 0.5 + 0.5 \cdot 0.5 = 0.75 \end{aligned} $$ **True values** (geometric series): $v_\pi(A) = 1 + 0.9 + 0.9^2 + 0.9^3 = 2.71$, $v_\pi(B) = 1 + 0.9 + 0.9^2 = 1.91$, $v_\pi(C) = 1$. > [!NOTE] > After enough episodes with $\alpha$ decaying per Robbins-Monro, the estimates will converge to **$V(A) = 2.71$, $V(B) = 1.91$, $V(C) = 1.0$**. This numerical example is the *exact* format KTU expects in 14-mark numerical problems. --- ## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — MC, TD(0), TD($\lambda$) | Property | MC | TD(0) | TD($\lambda$) | |---|---|---|---| | Lookahead | Full episode | 1 step | Geometric, $\lambda$-blend | | Memory | Stores full trajectory | Stores only current step | Stores trace $e_t(s)$ | | Update frequency | End of episode | Every step | Every step (all states) | | Bias (with approx $V$) | None | High | Tunable via $\lambda$ | | Variance | High | Low | Tunable | | Best for | Episodic, short | Online, continuing | Best of both worlds | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 TD Learning — Functional Architecture Flow The following Mermaid block-diagram captures how TD learning integrates perception, prediction, error computation, and parameter update in a continuous loop. ```mermaid flowchart TD start([Start of Episode]) --> initS[Initialize State S_t] initS --> pickA[Policy pi selects Action A_t] pickA --> envStep[Environment Step] envStep --> obsR[Observe Reward R_{t+1}] obsR --> obsS2[Observe Next State S_{t+1}] obsS2 --> fetchV[Fetch V S_{t+1} from Table] fetchV --> computeTd[Compute TD Error: delta_t = R + gamma times V S_{t+1} minus V S_t] computeTd --> computeUpdate[Compute Update: alpha times delta_t] computeUpdate --> applyUpdate[Update V S_t in Table] applyUpdate --> checkTerm{Is S_{t+1} Terminal?} checkTerm -- No --> shiftS[Set S_t to S_{t+1}] shiftS --> pickA checkTerm -- Yes --> endEp([End of Episode]) endEp --> nextEp[Begin Next Episode] nextEp --> initS style start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style endEp fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style computeTd fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#fff style applyUpdate fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.2 The TD($\lambda$) Eligibility Trace Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph timeAxis [Time Axis t increases rightward] sA[State S_a
Time k]:::visited sB[State S_b
Time k+2]:::visited sC[State S_c
Time k+5]:::visited end deltaK[TD Error delta_k] --> eA[Trace e_a = 1]:::trace deltaK --> eB[Trace e_b = gamma lambda squared]:::trace deltaK --> eC[Trace e_c = gamma lambda to the 5th]:::trace eA --> updA[V S_a increases by alpha delta_k times 1] eB --> updB[V S_b increases by alpha delta_k times gamma lambda squared] eC --> updC[V S_c increases by alpha delta_k times gamma lambda to the 5th] classDef visited fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef trace fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** At the moment the TD error $\delta_k$ is computed, *all* states previously visited receive a *credit assignment* proportional to their eligibility trace, which decays geometrically with $\gamma\lambda$ per time step. This is the **backward view** of TD($\lambda$). --- ## 4.3 SARSA vs Q-Learning Decision Branching ```mermaid flowchart TD start([Agent at S_t, takes A_t]) --> envStep[Environment: reward R, next state S_{t+1}] envStep --> branch{Sampling or
Bootstrap Action?} branch -- SARSA: On-Policy --> sampleA[Sample A_{t+1} from
epsilon-greedy pi] sampleA --> sarsaUpdate[Q S A increases by alpha times R + gamma Q S_{t+1} A_{t+1} minus Q S A] sarsaUpdate --> moveOn[Set S_t to S_{t+1}, A_t to A_{t+1}] branch -- Q-Learning: Off-Policy --> greedyPick[Choose a_star = argmax over a of Q S_{t+1} a] greedyPick --> qlUpdate[Q S A increases by alpha times R + gamma times Q S_{t+1} a_star minus Q S A] qlUpdate --> moveOn2[Set S_t to S_{t+1}] moveOn --> start moveOn2 --> start style start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style sarsaUpdate fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000 style qlUpdate fill:#20B2AA,stroke:#000,color:#000 style branch fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.4 The Bias-Variance Spectrum Across Methods ```mermaid flowchart LR m1[TD 0: 1-step
HIGH BIAS, LOW VARIANCE]:::td --> m2[TD lambda with small lambda
MEDIUM BIAS, MEDIUM VARIANCE]:::tdl m2 --> m3[TD lambda with lambda close to 1
LOW BIAS, HIGH VARIANCE]:::tdh m3 --> m4[Monte Carlo
ZERO BIAS, HIGH VARIANCE]:::mc m1b[DP: model given
BIAS, ZERO VARIANCE]:::dp --> m1 classDef td fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef tdl fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef tdh fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef mc fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef dp fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define Temporal Difference (TD) learning. How does it differ from Monte Carlo methods in the way it forms its target for the value update?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (1 mark):** Temporal Difference (TD) learning is a model-free, bootstrapping method in Reinforcement Learning that updates the value of a state using the difference between the *estimated value of the next state* and the *current state's estimate*, after observing a one-step reward. > **Monte Carlo (1 mark):** MC methods form their target as the **actual return** $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \cdots$ collected at the **end of an episode**. They require the entire episode to finish before any value is updated. > **TD (1 mark):** TD forms its target as $R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$ immediately after the next transition — it does **not** wait for the episode to terminate and instead **bootstraps** from the estimate of $V(S_{t+1})$. Hence TD is online and incremental; MC is offline and episodic. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **What is the TD error? Write its mathematical expression and state its expected value at convergence.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1 mark):** The TD error is the difference between the **TD target** (one-step lookahead estimate of return) and the **current value estimate** of the state. > **Expression (1 mark):** > $$\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$$ > **Expected value at convergence (1 mark):** When the value function $V$ has converged to the true $v_\pi$, the expected TD error at any state under policy $\pi$ is **zero**: > $$\mathbb{E}_\pi[\delta_t \mid S_t = s] = 0 \quad \forall s \in \mathcal{S}$$ --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Rule:** In Part B Module questions, you are offered an **internal choice**. You must attempt **one** of the two questions below. Each question has two sub-parts worth **7 marks each**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Explain the TD(0) algorithm for policy evaluation. Derive the TD(0) update rule starting from the Bellman expectation equation and discuss why TD(0) is biased but has lower variance compared to Monte Carlo methods.** #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Consider a 3-state Markov Reward Process with states A, B, and C (C is terminal). The transition is deterministic: A → B → C, with rewards $R = +1$ at every transition. Use $\gamma = 0.8$ and $\alpha = 0.5$. Starting with $V(A) = V(B) = V(C) = 0$, perform TD(0) updates for two complete episodes and tabulate the values. Compare your final estimates with the true values $v_\pi(A)$, $v_\pi(B)$, $v_\pi(C)$.** --- ### **Model Solution — Question A** #### **Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks):** > **Algorithm description (2 marks):** TD(0) is the simplest TD method. It evaluates a fixed policy $\pi$ by updating the value of the *currently visited* state $S_t$ towards the one-step TD target $R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$.
