The update is performed *after every single step* — no waiting for episode termination, and no model of $P(s',r \vert s,a)$ is required. > **Derivation from Bellman equation (3 marks):** The Bellman expectation equation for $v_\pi$ is: > $$v_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[R_{t+1} + \gamma v_\pi(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t = s]$$ > Taking one *sample* of the RHS by interacting with the environment gives the TD target: > $$\hat{v} = R_{t+1} + \gamma v_\pi(S_{t+1})$$ > The TD error (deviation of current estimate from the sampled target) is: > $$\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$$ > Performing gradient descent on $\tfrac{1}{2}\delta_t^2$ with respect to $V(S_t)$ yields the update: > $$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \delta_t$$ > Substituting the expression of $\delta_t$: > $$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t) \right]$$ > **Bias vs Variance comparison (2 marks):** > - **MC target** is $G_t$ — the *true* return. Since the expectation of $G_t$ is $v_\pi(s)$, MC is **unbiased**. However, $G_t$ depends on many random transitions and rewards, so the MC estimator has **high variance**. > - **TD(0) target** is $R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$. It uses the *estimate* $V(S_{t+1})$ — if $V$ is imperfect, the target is **biased**. However, it depends on only *one* random sample, so the TD estimator has **low variance**. This is the classic **bias-variance trade-off** of TD(0) versus MC. --- #### **Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks):** **True values** (computed from geometric series, $\gamma = 0.8$): $$ \begin{aligned} v_\pi(C) &= 0 \quad \text{(terminal)} \\ v_\pi(B) &= 1 + 0.8 \cdot 0 = 1 \\ v_\pi(A) &= 1 + 0.8 \cdot 1 = 1.8 \end{aligned} $$ **Initialisation:** $V(A) = V(B) = V(C) = 0$, $\alpha = 0.5$, $\gamma = 0.8$. --- **Episode 1:** trajectory $A \to B \to C$, rewards all 1. | Step | $S_t$ | $R_{t+1}$ | $S_{t+1}$ | $\delta_t$ | $V(S_t)$ new | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | A | 1 | B | $1 + 0.8(0) - 0 = 1$ | $0 + 0.5(1) = 0.5$ | | 2 | B | 1 | C | $1 + 0.8(0) - 0 = 1$ | $0 + 0.5(1) = 0.5$ | | 3 | C | — | terminal | no update | 0 | After Episode 1: $V(A) = 0.5$, $V(B) = 0.5$, $V(C) = 0$. --- **Episode 2:** trajectory $A \to B \to C$, rewards all 1. | Step | $S_t$ | $R_{t+1}$ | $S_{t+1}$ | $\delta_t$ | $V(S_t)$ new | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | A | 1 | B | $1 + 0.8(0.5) - 0.5 = 0.9$ | $0.5 + 0.5(0.9) = 0.95$ | | 2 | B | 1 | C | $1 + 0.8(0) - 0.5 = 0.5$ | $0.5 + 0.5(0.5) = 0.75$ | | 3 | C | — | terminal | no update | 0 | After Episode 2: $V(A) = 0.95$, $V(B) = 0.75$, $V(C) = 0$. **Comparison table:** | State | True $v_\pi$ | Estimate after Ep 2 | Error | |---|---|---|---| | A | 1.80 | 0.95 | -0.85 | | B | 1.00 | 0.75 | -0.25 | | C | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 | > [!NOTE] > **Inference (for full marks):** After only 2 episodes, the values are still under-estimated because (i) the chain is short and (ii) the true value of $A$ requires the agent to "see" the future reward stream from B, which requires the $\gamma$ propagation across two steps. With more episodes and a properly decaying $\alpha$, the estimates will converge to the true $v_\pi$. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Explain the SARSA and Q-Learning algorithms. Clearly distinguish between on-policy and off-policy learning. Show mathematically how their update rules differ, and discuss which algorithm is preferred in risk-sensitive applications.** #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Using the 4×4 grid-world shown below (cliff-walking style with a start S in cell (1,1), goal G in cell (4,4), and cliff cells in row 4, columns 2 and 3 that give reward $-100$ and reset to start), explain step-by-step how SARSA with $\epsilon$-greedy ($\epsilon = 0.1$) would choose a *safer* path close to the start of row 1, whereas Q-Learning would cut the corner along the cliff edge. Show the relevant update equations and explain why this difference arises.** --- ### **Model Solution — Question B** #### **Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks):** > **SARSA (State-Action-Reward-State-Action) (2 marks):** It is an **on-policy** TD control algorithm. The name comes from the quintuple of consecutive experience used in the update: $(S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$. The update is: > $$Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) - Q(S_t, A_t) \right]$$ > where $A_{t+1}$ is the action *actually taken* by the behaviour policy (e.g., $\epsilon$-greedy with respect to the current $Q$). SARSA learns the value of the *policy currently being followed*. > **Q-Learning (2 marks):** It is an **off-policy** TD control algorithm. The update is: > $$Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a') - Q(S_t, A_t) \right]$$ > The bootstrap uses the *greedy* next action $\arg\max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a')$, *regardless* of the action the behaviour policy actually picks. Q-Learning directly learns the optimal action-value function $Q^*$, decoupling learning from acting. > **On-policy vs Off-policy distinction (2 marks):** > - **On-policy:** The policy *used to generate behaviour* and the policy *being improved* are the **same**. > - **Off-policy:** The behaviour policy and the target policy are **different**. Q-Learning uses an exploratory behaviour policy (e.g., $\epsilon$-greedy) but learns about the *greedy* target policy. > **Risk-sensitive preference (1 mark):** In risky environments (e.g., the cliff-walking problem), SARSA accounts for the chance that an $\epsilon$-greedy exploratory step might push the agent into a disaster — hence it learns a *safer* trajectory. Q-Learning optimistically assumes the agent will follow the *greedy* path, learning the *shortest* (and most dangerous) optimal route. Therefore **SARSA is preferred for risk-sensitive / safety-critical applications**. --- #### **Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks):** **Cliff-walking grid setup:** - Cells: 4×4, agent starts at (1,1), goal at (4,4). - Cliff = cells (4,2) and (4,3): reward $-100$ and reset to (1,1). - All other moves: reward $-1$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key update difference (2 marks):** When the agent is at, say, (3,2) — one cell above the cliff — the action "down" leads to a cliff cell. Under $\epsilon$-greedy with $\epsilon=0.1$, there is a $10\%$ chance the agent *will* pick "down" by exploration. > **SARSA's update (2 marks):** When the agent samples $A_{t+1} = \text{down}$ and falls into the cliff, it receives $R = -100$ and then the next $Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$ is large-negative. This propagates back, making $Q((3,2), \text{down})$ *small* (less attractive). Hence SARSA learns to *avoid* being at (3,2) with any exploratory action — it picks the **long path along row 1**. > **Q-Learning's update (2 marks):** Q-Learning's bootstrap uses $\max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a')$. Even if the exploratory *behaviour* falls into the cliff, the *target* only cares about the *best* action from the next state. The greedy action from (3,2) leads to the goal quickly. Q-Learning therefore learns the **short path along the cliff edge** — optimal in expectation under greedy play, but lethal under any $\epsilon$-greedy noise. > **Why the difference arises (1 mark):** SARSA averages over what the agent *actually* does (including exploratory mistakes), so it internalises the *cost of exploration*. Q-Learning optimises the *greedy* path and ignores the cost of the exploratory noise — making it brittle in stochastic environments. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > 1. **Forgetting the eligibility-trace role:** In TD($\lambda$) questions, do *not* write the same update as TD(0). You **must** include the term $e_t(s)$ and the recursion $e_t(s) = \gamma\lambda e_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$. Losing 2 marks here is the most common pitfall. > 2. **Confusing SARSA and Q-Learning target:** Many students write $Q(S',A')$ for Q-Learning. This is the **SARSA** target. Q-Learning uses $\max_{a'} Q(S',a')$. Marks deducted = full 7 marks for the sub-part. > 3. **Numerical formatting:** For 7-mark numericals, **show every intermediate $\delta_t$ computation** in a table — KTU examiners award step marks. Submitting only final values without the TD error arithmetic loses 3–4 marks. > 4. **Discount factor position:** The discount $\gamma$ multiplies $V(S_{t+1})$ *outside* the parenthesis, not the reward alone. Misplaced $\gamma$ = 1 mark deduction. > 5. **Convergence conditions:** For 14-mark theory questions, *always* state the Robbins-Monro conditions $\sum \alpha_t = \infty$ and $\sum \alpha_t^2 < \infty$ when claiming convergence. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (read this just before walking into the exam hall):** - ✅ **TD(0) update:** $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha [R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)]$ - ✅ **TD error** $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$ — *expected value is zero at convergence*. - ✅ **TD = MC + DP:** model-free + bootstrapping + online. The only method with all three properties. - ✅ **Bias-variance:** MC → zero bias, high variance. TD(0) → some bias, low variance. $\lambda$ tunes the trade-off. - ✅ **n-step return:** $G_t^{(n)} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \gamma^{k-1} R_{t+k} + \gamma^n V(S_{t+n})$. - ✅ **$\lambda$-return:** geometric blend of $n$-step returns, weights $(1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1}$. - ✅ **Eligibility trace (accumulating):** $e_t(s) = \gamma\lambda \, e_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$. - ✅ **TD($\lambda$) update:** $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \delta_t e_t(s)$ for **all** $s$. - ✅ **Boundary cases:** $\lambda=0 \Rightarrow$ TD(0); $\lambda=1 \Rightarrow$ Monte Carlo. - ✅ **SARSA:** quintuple update, **on-policy**, uses actual $A_{t+1}$. - ✅ **Q-Learning:** uses $\max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a')$, **off-policy**, learns $Q^*$. - ✅ **Contraction property:** $T^\pi$ is a $\gamma$-contraction in $\|\cdot\|_\infty$; Banach guarantees unique fixed point $v_\pi$. - ✅ **Convergence:** requires Robbins-Monro step-size conditions + every $(s,a)$ visited infinitely often. - ✅ **Risk-sensitive preference:** SARSA > Q-Learning on cliff / safety tasks; Q-Learning > SARSA on deterministic / risk-free tasks. - ✅ **Convergence metric:** mean-squared TD error $\mathbb{E}[\delta_t^2]$. - ✅ **Engineering uses:** robotics, game AI, traffic control, recommender systems, adaptive networks.

topics.title: Q Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT501 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Q Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
**Q-Learning** is a foundational, **model-free**, **off-policy** Reinforcement Learning (RL) algorithm introduced by Christopher Watkins in his 1989 Ph.D. thesis. It seeks to find the optimal action-selection policy for any finite Markov Decision Process (MDP) by learning the *quality* (Q-value) of actions that tell an agent how good it is to take a given action in a given state, without requiring a model of the environment.

The agent iteratively updates its estimates of the expected cumulative reward (Q-value) using the **Bellman Optimality Equation**, eventually converging to the optimal Q-function $Q^*(s, a)$, from which the optimal policy $\pi^*$ can be trivially extracted by selecting the action with the maximum Q-value at every state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Q-Learning is categorized under *Value-Based Reinforcement Learning* and is a direct application of the Bellman Optimality Equation. It is an *off-policy* algorithm because it learns the optimal policy regardless of the agent's current exploration strategy.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **The "Rat in a Maze" Analogy:**
> Imagine a rat placed in a complex maze. The rat does not know the layout. Every time it moves, it receives a piece of cheese (**reward**) if it reaches the exit, or a small electric shock (**negative reward**) if it hits a dead-end wall.
> - Initially, the rat wanders randomly.
> - Over hundreds of trials, the rat develops a *mental map* assigning a "goodness score" to every (location, direction) pair.
> - This mental map is exactly the **Q-Table**. The rat eventually picks the direction with the highest score from every location.
> - This is Q-Learning: learning the value of actions through direct experience.

### 1.3 Key Terminology
- **State (s):** The current situation/position of the agent in the environment.
- **Action (a):** A possible move/decision the agent can make.
- **Reward (r):** The immediate feedback signal from the environment.
- **Q-Value $Q(s,a)$:** The expected cumulative reward achievable by taking action $a$ in state $s$ and following the optimal policy thereafter.
- **Discount Factor $\gamma$ (gamma):** A value in $[0,1)$ that weighs the importance of future rewards versus immediate rewards. Standard benchmark: $\gamma = 0.9$ or $\gamma = 0.99$.
- **Learning Rate $\alpha$ (alpha):** Step size for the Q-value update. Standard range: $\alpha \in (0, 1]$.
- **Exploration vs. Exploitation Trade-off:** The dilemma of whether to try new actions (explore) or repeat known good actions (exploit).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Workflow of Q-Learning
The Q-Learning algorithm operates in an iterative loop of experience tuples $(s, a, r, s')$:

1. **Initialization:** Create a Q-Table with dimensions $\vert \mathcal{S} \vert \times \vert \mathcal{A} \vert$, initialized arbitrarily (commonly to zeros).
2. **State Observation:** Agent observes the current state $s_t$.
3. **Action Selection:** Using a policy (e.g., $\epsilon$-greedy), select an action $a_t$.
4. **Environment Interaction:** Execute $a_t$; observe immediate reward $r_{t+1}$ and next state $s_{t+1}$.
5. **Q-Value Update:** Update the Q-value for the $(s_t, a_t)$ pair using the temporal difference error.
6. **Transition:** Set $s_t \leftarrow s_{t+1}$ and repeat until convergence.

### 2.2 The Q-Update Rule — The Heart of the Algorithm
The update rule balances the agent's previous estimate with newly observed information (the TD-target):

$$Q_{new}(s_t, a_t) = (1 - \alpha) \cdot Q_{old}(s_t, a_t) + \alpha \cdot \left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma \cdot \max_{a} Q(s_{t+1}, a) \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `max`?** Q-Learning is *off-policy*. It assumes the agent will act optimally *next time*, regardless of the current exploration strategy. Thus, it bootstraps using the **best possible** future Q-value.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Purpose / Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Bellman Optimality** | $Q^*(s, a) = \mathbb{E}\left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \cdot \max_{a'} Q^*(s_{t+1}, a') \mid s_t = s, a_t = a \right]$ | Defines the optimal value function theoretically. |
| **Q-Update Rule** | $Q(s, a) \leftarrow Q(s, a) + \alpha \left[ r + \gamma \cdot \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a) \right]$ | Core iterative learning step. |
| **TD-Error** | $\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \cdot \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a') - Q(s_t, a_t)$ | The "surprise" signal driving learning. |
| **Optimal Policy** | $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_{a} Q^*(s, a)$ | Extracts the best action once learning converges. |
| **Return (G_t)** | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k \cdot R_{t+k+1}$ | Discounted cumulative reward objective. |
| **$\epsilon$-greedy** | $P(a) = 1 - \epsilon + \frac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}$ if $a = \arg\max Q$, else $\frac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}$ | Solves the exploration-exploitation trade-off. |

> **Engineering Utility:** Q-Learning is the foundational engine behind autonomous robotics (e.g., warehouse robots by Amazon Kiva), game AI (Atari DQN, AlphaGo), dynamic network routing, and adaptive traffic signal control.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Q-Learning Update Rule
The Q-Learning update rule is derived from the principle of **Temporal Difference (TD) Learning**, which interpolates between Monte Carlo methods (learn at the end of an episode) and Dynamic Programming (learn after every step).

**Step 1: Start with the Bellman Optimality Equation.**
The optimal Q-value is defined as the expected sum of discounted rewards:

$$Q^*(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi^*} \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1} \mid s_t = s, a_t = a \right]$$

**Step 2: Decompose into immediate reward + future discounted return.**

$$Q^*(s, a) = \mathbb{E} \left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+2} \mid s_t = s, a_t = a \right]$$

**Step 3: Recognize the future return as another Q-value.**
The discounted future sum is itself a Q-value of the next state-action pair. Since we assume optimality, we take the maximum over all possible next actions:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+2} = \max_{a'} Q^*(s_{t+1}, a')$$

**Step 4: Form the recursive Bellman target.**

$$Q^*(s, a) = \mathbb{E} \left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s_{t+1}, a') \right]$$

**Step 5: Convert the theoretical expectation into a sample-based stochastic update.**
Since the agent only samples a single transition $(s, a, r, s')$, we replace the expectation with the observed sample. We also blend the new estimate with the old one using learning rate $\alpha$ to ensure stability:

$$Q(s, a) \leftarrow Q(s, a) + \alpha \left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a) \right]$$

This is the famous **Q-Learning update equation** (Watkins, 1989).

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Toy 3x3 Grid World
Consider a 3x3 grid where the agent starts at $S_0$ and must reach the goal state $G$ with reward $+10$. Each movement gives $r = -1$. Discount factor $\gamma = 0.9$, learning rate $\alpha = 0.1$. The Q-Table is initialized to 0.

**Episode 1, Step 1:** Agent at $S_0$, takes action **Right**.
- Transition: $S_0 \rightarrow S_1$, reward $r = -1$, new state $s' = S_1$.
- TD-Target: $r + \gamma \cdot \max_{a'} Q(S_1, a') = -1 + 0.9 \times 0 = -1$.
- Update: $Q(S_0, \text{Right}) \leftarrow 0 + 0.1 \times [-1 - 0] = -0.1$.

**Episode 1, Step 2:** Agent at $S_1$, takes action **Down**.
- Transition: $S_1 \rightarrow S_4$, reward $r = -1$, new state $s' = S_4$.
- TD-Target: $-1 + 0.9 \times 0 = -1$.
- Update: $Q(S_1, \text{Down}) \leftarrow 0 + 0.1 \times [-1 - 0] = -0.1$.

**Episode 5, Step 5:** Agent at $S_4$, takes action **Down** (reaches goal).
- Transition: $S_4 \rightarrow G$, reward $r = +10$, new state $s' = G$ (terminal).
- TD-Target: $10 + 0.9 \times 0 = 10$ (terminal state has no future).
- Update: $Q(S_4, \text{Down}) \leftarrow 0 + 0.1 \times [10 - 0] = 1.0$.

**Episode 10, Step 4:** Agent at $S_1$, takes action **Down**.
- Now $Q(S_4, \text{Down}) = 1.0$.
- TD-Target: $-1 + 0.9 \times 1.0 = -0.1$.
- Update: $Q(S_1, \text{Down}) \leftarrow -0.1 + 0.1 \times [-0.1 - (-0.1)] = -0.1$.

Notice how the reward information **propagates backward** through the Q-Table as episodes progress. Eventually, the policy becomes:

$$\pi^*(S_0) = \text{Right}, \quad \pi^*(S_1) = \text{Down}, \quad \pi^*(S_4) = \text{Down}$$

### 3.3 Convergence Conditions (Watkins & Dayan, 1992)
Q-Learning is guaranteed to converge to $Q^*$ if:
1. All state-action pairs are visited infinitely often (sufficient exploration).
2. The learning rate $\alpha$ satisfies the Robbins-Monro conditions: $\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t = \infty$ and $\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t^2 < \infty$.
3. Rewards are bounded and the environment is a finite MDP.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Q-Learning Algorithm Flowchart (Agent-Environment Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([Start: Initialize Q-Table to 0]) --> nodeB[/Observe current state s_t/]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Choose action a_t}
    nodeC -->|epsilon-greedy| nodeD[/Execute a_t in environment/]
    nodeD --> nodeE[/Observe reward r and new state s_t+1/]
    nodeE --> nodeF[/Compute TD-Target:/]
    nodeF --> nodeG["TD-Target = r + gamma times max Q(s_t+1, a)"]
    nodeG --> nodeH[/Compute TD-Error delta/]
    nodeH --> nodeI["delta = TD-Target minus Q(s_t, a_t)"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[/Update Q-Value:/]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Q(s_t, a_t) = Q(s_t, a_t) + alpha times delta"]
    nodeK --> nodeL{s_t is terminal?}
    nodeL -->|Yes| nodeM[/Increment episode counter/]
    nodeL -->|No| nodeN[/Set s_t = s_t+1/]
    nodeN --> nodeC
    nodeM --> nodeO{Max episodes reached?}
    nodeO -->|No| nodeB
    nodeO -->|Yes| nodeP([End: Extract optimal policy from Q-Table])

    style nodeA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeP fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeG fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064
    style nodeK fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064
```

### 4.2 Q-Table Value Propagation Schematic
A Block-Level Functional Architecture showing how rewards propagate through state-action pairs.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph env["ENVIRONMENT"]
        S0["State S0"]
        S1["State S1"]
        S2["State S2"]
        S3["State S3"]
        S4["State S4 (Near Goal)"]
        S5["Goal State G"]
    end

    subgraph qtab["Q-TABLE MEMORY"]
        qQ0["Q S0, Right"]
        qQ1["Q S1, Down"]
        qQ2["Q S2, Right"]
        qQ3["Q S3, Down"]
        qQ4["Q S4, Down = 1.0"]
    end

    S5 -.->|Reward +10| qQ4
    qQ4 -.->|Propagates Backward| qQ3
    qQ3 -.->|Propagates Backward| qQ1
    qQ1 -.->|Propagates Backward| qQ0

    style S5 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px
    style qQ4 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px
    style env fill:#F0F8FF
    style qtab fill:#FFF8DC
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram of the Reinforcement Learning Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeAgent[AGENT] -->|Action a_t| nodeEnv[ENVIRONMENT]
    nodeEnv -->|Reward r and Next State s_t+1| nodeAgent
    nodeAgent --> nodeMemory[(Q-Table Memory)]
    nodeMemory -->|Read Q-Values| nodeAgent

    style nodeAgent fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeEnv fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeMemory fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
Define Q-Learning. Why is it classified as an *off-policy* algorithm?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Q-Learning is a model-free, value-based Reinforcement Learning algorithm that learns the optimal action-value function $Q^*(s, a)$ by iteratively updating Q-values using the Bellman optimality equation.
It is classified as **off-policy** because the update rule uses the *maximum* Q-value over all possible next actions ($\max_{a'} Q(s', a')$) rather than the action actually chosen by the agent's current policy. This means the algorithm learns the optimal policy independently of the agent's exploration behavior (1 mark each point × 3).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sample]**
Explain the role of the **exploration-exploitation trade-off** in Q-Learning. Mention one strategy used to resolve it.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **exploration-exploitation trade-off** refers to the dilemma an agent faces: whether to exploit its current knowledge by choosing the action with the highest known Q-value, or to explore new actions to discover potentially better long-term rewards (2 marks).
The most common strategy to resolve this is the **$\epsilon$-greedy policy**, where the agent selects the greedy action (max Q-value) with probability $(1-\epsilon)$ and a random action with probability $\epsilon$ to ensure sufficient exploration. As learning progresses, $\epsilon$ is typically decayed (e.g., from 0.9 to 0.01) (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### 📝 Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the Q-Learning update rule from the Bellman Optimality Equation. State all assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 2-state MDP with $S_1$ and $S_2$ (terminal). The agent starts at $S_1$, takes action $a$, reaches $S_2$ with reward $+5$. Using $\alpha = 0.5$ and $\gamma = 0.8$, perform **two updates** of Q-Learning starting from $Q(S_1, a) = 0$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
Starting from the Bellman Optimality Equation for the action-value function:

$$Q^*(s, a) = \mathbb{E} \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s_{t+1}, a') \right]$$

*Step 1:* Since the agent only observes a single sample $(s, a, r, s')$, the expectation is replaced by the sample value:

$$Q^*(s, a) \approx r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a')$$

*Step 2:* To ensure convergence, blend the old estimate with the new estimate using learning rate $\alpha$:

$$Q(s, a) \leftarrow (1-\alpha) Q(s, a) + \alpha \left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') \right]$$

*Step 3:* Rearranging yields the standard Q-Learning form:

$$Q(s, a) \leftarrow Q(s, a) + \alpha \left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a) \right]$$

**Assumptions:** Finite MDP, bounded rewards, infinite visits to all state-action pairs, Robbins-Monro conditions on $\alpha$.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Update 1:** $Q_{old}(S_1, a) = 0$, observed $r = 5$, next state $S_2$ is terminal so $\max Q(S_2, \cdot) = 0$.

$$Q_{new}(S_1, a) = 0 + 0.5 \times [5 + 0.8 \times 0 - 0] = 2.5$$

**Update 2:** $Q_{old}(S_1, a) = 2.5$, same transition observed again.

$$Q_{new}(S_1, a) = 2.5 + 0.5 \times [5 + 0 - 2.5] = 2.5 + 1.25 = 3.75$$

Final answer: $Q(S_1, a) = 3.75$ after two updates.

---

#### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Alternate Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the Q-Learning algorithm with a clear block diagram. Discuss the effect of the discount factor $\gamma$ on the agent's behavior. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2x2 grid world has terminal goal reward $+10$ and step penalty $-1$. Using $\gamma = 0.9$ and $\alpha = 0.1$, explain the first three Q-Learning updates for a single optimal trajectory. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Algorithm & Discount Factor (7 marks):**
The Q-Learning algorithm maintains a Q-Table of size $\vert \mathcal{S} \vert \times \vert \mathcal{A} \vert$. For each step, the agent observes state $s$, picks action $a$ using $\epsilon$-greedy, receives reward $r$, observes next state $s'$, and updates:

$$Q(s, a) \leftarrow Q(s, a) + \alpha [r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a)]$$

**Effect of $\gamma$ (refer to block diagram in Section 4.1):**
- If $\gamma \to 0$: The agent becomes **myopic**, valuing only immediate rewards.
- If $\gamma \to 1$: The agent becomes **far-sighted**, weighing future rewards almost equally with immediate ones.
- Moderate values like $\gamma = 0.9$ to $0.99$ balance short-term and long-term rewards (2 marks).

**(b) Three Updates (7 marks):**
Let the optimal trajectory be: $S_0 \to S_1 \to S_2 \to \text{Goal}$.
All Q-Values initialized to 0.

**Update 1:** At step $S_2 \to \text{Goal}$: $r = 10$, $\max Q(\text{Goal}, \cdot) = 0$.
$$Q(S_2, \text{Goal}) = 0 + 0.1 \times [10 - 0] = 1.0$$

**Update 2:** At step $S_1 \to S_2$: $r = -1$, $\max Q(S_2, \cdot) = 1.0$.
$$Q(S_1, \text{Optimal}) = 0 + 0.1 \times [-1 + 0.9 \times 1.0 - 0] = -0.01$$

**Update 3:** At step $S_0 \to S_1$: $r = -1$, $\max Q(S_1, \cdot) = -0.01$.
$$Q(S_0, \text{Optimal}) = 0 + 0.1 \times [-1 + 0.9 \times (-0.01) - 0] = -0.1009$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Mark Loss #1:** Students often confuse **on-policy** (SARSA) with **off-policy** (Q-Learning). Remember: Q-Learning uses $\max$, SARSA uses the *actual next action*. Forgetting this distinction costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Mark Loss #2:** In numerical problems, students frequently forget that a **terminal state's future value is 0**. Always set $\max Q(s', a') = 0$ for terminal states.
> 3. **Mark Loss #3:** Do not forget to mention **assumptions for convergence** (Robbins-Monro conditions, infinite exploration) in derivation questions. Examiners explicitly test this.
> 4. **Mark Loss #4:** Writing `γ` as `0.9` without showing intermediate TD-Target and TD-Error steps loses 1-2 marks. Always show both.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Q-Learning is a *model-free, off-policy* RL algorithm that learns the optimal action-value function $Q^*(s, a)$.
- **Core Update Rule:** $Q(s, a) \leftarrow Q(s, a) + \alpha [r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a)]$.
- **Off-Policy Nature:** Caused by the $\max$ operator in the bootstrap target — learns the optimal policy regardless of the agent's exploration policy.
- **Bellman Optimality:** The theoretical foundation — recursively expresses $Q^*(s, a)$ in terms of $Q^*(s', a')$.
- **Hyperparameters:**
   - $\alpha$ (learning rate): controls how fast new information overrides old.
   - $\gamma$ (discount factor): $\approx 0$ is short-sighted, $\approx 1$ is far-sighted.
   - $\epsilon$ (exploration rate): high early for exploration, decayed for exploitation.
- **Exploration Strategy:** $\epsilon$-greedy is the standard; alternatives include Boltzmann (softmax) exploration and UCB.
- **Convergence:** Guaranteed under infinite visits and Robbins-Monro conditions on $\alpha$.
- **TD-Error:** $\delta_t = r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a)$ — the "surprise" signal.
- **Optimal Policy Extraction:** $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_a Q^*(s, a)$.
- **Terminal States:** Always have a Q-value of 0; do not bootstrap from them.
- **Limitation:** Tabular Q-Learning fails on large state spaces — this motivated **Deep Q-Networks (DQN)** which approximate $Q(s, a)$ with a neural network.
- **Real-world:** Robotics, game AI, autonomous driving, recommendation systems, smart grid optimization.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Deep Learning - Convolutional Neural Networks - Convolution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT501 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basics of Deep Learning - Convolutional Neural Networks - Convolution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convolutional Neural Network (CNN):** A specialized class of deep, feed-forward neural networks designed to process data that has a known grid-like topology (e.g., images, which are 2-D grids of pixels). CNNs use a mathematical operation called **convolution** (a specialized kind of linear operation) in place of general matrix multiplication in at least one of their layers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Convolution Operation (Discrete, 2-D, as used in CNNs):** A discrete convolution takes two arguments: an **input feature map** $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$ and a learnable **kernel (filter)** $\mathbf{K} \in \mathbb{R}^{k_h \times k_w \times C}$, and produces an **output feature map** $\mathbf{Y} \in \mathbb{R}^{H' \times W' \times C'}$ by computing a sum of element-wise products as the kernel slides over the spatial dimensions of the input.

Formally, for a single channel and bias $b$:

$$
Y[i, j] = \left( \mathbf{X} * \mathbf{K} \right)[i, j] + b = \sum_{m=0}^{k_h - 1} \sum_{n=0}^{k_w - 1} X[i+m, j+n] \cdot K[m, n] + b
$$

In a true CNN layer, multiple kernels act in parallel producing a stack of feature maps, followed by a non-linear activation $f(\cdot)$ (typically **ReLU**).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **The Flashlight / Magnifying-Glass Analogy**
>
> Imagine you are examining a large photograph in a dark room using a small flashlight that is fitted with a special stencil (the **filter/kernel**). You sweep this flashlight across the photo from the top-left corner to the bottom-right corner. At every position, you only see the small patch under the beam — and you record a single number that describes how strongly that patch matches the stencil (e.g., "Does this patch contain a horizontal edge?"). When you finish the sweep, you have a smaller, condensed map of numbers — the **feature map** — which tells you *where* in the original photo the pattern of interest occurs.
>
> The **filter** is the stencil. The **input** is the photograph. The **feature map** is your notes. **Striding** is how many steps you move the flashlight each time. **Padding** is adding a white border around the photo so the edges don't get ignored.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Input Volume ($\mathbf{X}$)** | The image (Height $\times$ Width $\times$ Channels), e.g. $32 \times 32 \times 3$ |
| **Filter / Kernel ($\mathbf{K}$)** | A small learnable matrix of weights, e.g. $3 \times 3 \times 3$ |
| **Feature Map ($\mathbf{Y}$)** | The 2-D output produced by one filter, highlighting where features occur |
| **Stride ($s$)** | Step size of the filter as it moves across the input |
| **Padding ($p$)** | Zeros added around the input border to control output size |
| **Receptive Field** | The region of the original input that influences a particular neuron in a deeper layer |
| **Depth ($C'$)** | Number of filters used in a layer (each filter learns a different feature) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Hyperparameters in KTU Board Questions:** Filter size $k$ is almost always **odd** ($3 \times 3$ or $5 \times 5$), stride is commonly **$s=1$** or **$s=2$**, and padding is either **$p=0$ (Valid)** or **$p=k-1/2$ (Same)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of a 2-D Convolution (Step-by-Step)

The convolution operation in a CNN is **not** a continuous integral — it is a discrete, cross-correlative sliding-window dot product. The procedure is as follows:

1. **Initialization:** The filter $\mathbf{K}$ is initialized with small random weights (e.g., Glorot/Xavier or He initialization) and a bias $b$ is set to zero. All these parameters are **learnable** via back-propagation.
2. **Anchor the filter** at the top-left spatial location of the input such that the top-left element of $\mathbf{K}$ aligns with the top-left element of the receptive window in $\mathbf{X}$.
3. **Compute the element-wise product** between the filter weights and the corresponding input pixels in the current window.
4. **Sum all the products** to obtain a single scalar value, add the bias $b$, and pass the result through a non-linear activation $f(\cdot)$ (ReLU in modern CNNs). This forms **one cell** of the output feature map.
5. **Slide** the filter by `stride` $s$ columns to the right, repeat steps 3–4. After exhausting the row, jump down by $s$ rows.
6. **Repeat** until the filter has traversed all valid spatial positions.
7. **Parallelism:** If $C'$ different filters are used in the layer, steps 1–6 are executed $C'$ times (one per filter) in parallel, producing $C'$ independent feature maps that are stacked along the depth dimension to form the output volume.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why convolution is preferred over fully-connected (FC) layers for images:**
> - **Parameter Sharing:** The *same* filter weights are used at every spatial location → drastically fewer parameters.
> - **Local Connectivity / Sparse Interactions:** Each output depends only on a small local region of the input (the receptive field), not on the entire image.
> - **Translation Equivariance:** If a feature (e.g., an edge) shifts in the input, its representation shifts by the same amount in the output.
> - **Translation Invariance** (with pooling): A small shift in input position barely changes the pooled output, making the network robust to object position.

## 2.2 Cross-Correlation vs. True Mathematical Convolution

> [!NOTE]
> In a strict mathematical sense, convolution involves **flipping the kernel** before sliding. However, since the weights of a CNN filter are *learned*, this flip is unnecessary — the network would just learn a flipped version. The deep-learning community therefore implements **cross-correlation** but calls it "convolution". KTU board questions use the term *convolution* to mean the sliding dot product shown in Section 1.1.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Notes |
|:-:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Output height | $H' = \left\lfloor \dfrac{H - k_h + 2p}{s} \right\rfloor + 1$ | $H$: input height, $k_h$: filter height |
| 2 | Output width | $W' = \left\lfloor \dfrac{W - k_w + 2p}{s} \right\rfloor + 1$ | $W$: input width, $k_w$: filter width |
| 3 | Output depth | $C' =$ **number of filters** in the layer | Each filter produces one feature map |
| 4 | Parameters per filter | $k_h \cdot k_w \cdot C_{in} + 1$ | `+1` for the bias term |
| 5 | Total parameters in conv layer | $k_h \cdot k_w \cdot C_{in} \cdot C_{out} + C_{out}$ | $C_{in}$: input channels, $C_{out}$: output channels |
| 6 | Output activations | $H' \times W' \times C'$ | The shape of the produced feature map |
| 7 | "Valid" padding | $p = 0$ | Output shrinks; no zero-padding used |
| 8 | "Same" padding | $p = \dfrac{k - 1}{2}$ (for $k$ odd, $s=1$) | Output spatial size equals input size |
| 9 | Memory per layer (activations) | $H' \cdot W' \cdot C'$ (in floats) | Important for back-prop memory |
| 10 | Receptive field (after $L$ layers, $k \times k$, $s=1$) | $r = L(k-1) + 1$ | Linear growth without pooling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The convolution layer is the **backbone of all modern computer vision systems** — from medical-image tumor detection (CT/MRI segmentation) to autonomous-vehicle lane and pedestrian detection, satellite-image analysis, biometric face-recognition unlock systems, and even time-series classification (using 1-D convolutions for ECG/EEG/audio signals). Every CNN application, including ResNet, VGG, YOLO and U-Net, ultimately relies on the convolution operation analyzed above.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Output Dimension Formula

Consider a 1-D simplification first for clarity. Let the input be of length $H$, the filter of length $k$, stride $s$ and symmetric zero-padding $p$ on both sides.

> **Step 1 — Padded length.** After adding $p$ zeros on each side of the input, the effective length becomes $H + 2p$.

> **Step 2 — Effective sliding length.** The filter must start at index 0 and end at an index that keeps the right edge of the filter within the padded input. The number of valid starting positions is therefore:
> $$ \text{Span} = (H + 2p) - k $$

> **Step 3 — Accounting for stride.** Each step of the filter covers $s$ new positions. The number of non-overlapping steps that fit inside the span is:
> $$ \text{Steps} = \frac{(H + 2p) - k}{s} $$

> **Step 4 — Convert steps to output elements.** The output length equals the number of valid starting positions, which is the integer number of steps plus 1 (we count the initial position):
> $$ H' = \frac{H - k + 2p}{s} + 1 $$

The 2-D case decouples into two independent 1-D problems along height and width, giving the formulas in the cheat sheet.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example: 2-D Convolution on a $5 \times 5$ Input

**Given:**
- Input matrix $\mathbf{X}$ of size $5 \times 5$ (single channel, $C = 1$)
- Filter $\mathbf{K}$ of size $3 \times 3$
- Stride $s = 1$, no padding $p = 0$, bias $b = 0$

$$
\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 & 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 0 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Step A — Compute output size.**
> Using the formula:
> $$ H' = \frac{5 - 3 + 0}{1} + 1 = 3, \quad W' = \frac{5 - 3 + 0}{1} + 1 = 3 $$
> So the output feature map will be $3 \times 3$.

> **Step B — Compute $Y[0,0]$ (top-left).** The receptive window is the top-left $3 \times 3$ sub-matrix of $\mathbf{X}$:
> $$ \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 2 & 0 \end{bmatrix} $$
> Element-wise product with $\mathbf{K}$, then sum:
> $$ Y[0,0] = (1)(1) + (2)(0) + (3)(1) + (0)(0) + (1)(1) + (2)(0) + (1)(1) + (2)(0) + (0)(1) + b $$
> $$ Y[0,0] = 1 + 0 + 3 + 0 + 1 + 0 + 1 + 0 + 0 + 0 = \mathbf{6} $$

> **Step C — Compute $Y[0,1]$.** Slide the window one column right, covering columns 1, 2, 3 of $\mathbf{X}$:
> $$ \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 3 & 0 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$
> $$ Y[0,1] = (2)(1) + (3)(0) + (0)(1) + (1)(0) + (2)(1) + (3)(0) + (2)(1) + (0)(0) + (1)(1) $$
> $$ Y[0,1] = 2 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 2 + 0 + 2 + 0 + 1 = \mathbf{7} $$

> **Step D — Compute $Y[0,2]$.** Slide one more column right, covering columns 2, 3, 4 of $\mathbf{X}$:
> $$ \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} $$
> $$ Y[0,2] = 3 + 0 + 1 + 0 + 3 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 2 = \mathbf{9} $$

> **Step E — Compute $Y[1,0]$.** Slide the window one row down, columns 0, 1, 2 of $\mathbf{X}$:
> $$ \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 2 & 0 \\ 2 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} $$
> $$ Y[1,0] = 0 + 0 + 2 + 0 + 2 + 0 + 2 + 0 + 0 = \mathbf{6} $$

> **Step F — Compute $Y[1,1]$.** Center window:
> $$ \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$
> $$ Y[1,1] = 1 + 0 + 3 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 1 + 0 + 1 = \mathbf{6} $$

> **Step G — Compute $Y[1,2]$.** Center-row, right window:
> $$ \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 3 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} $$
> $$ Y[1,2] = 2 + 0 + 1 + 0 + 1 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 = \mathbf{4} $$

> **Step H — Compute $Y[2,0]$.** Bottom row, leftmost window (rows 2, 3, 4; cols 0, 1, 2 of $\mathbf{X}$):
> $$ \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 0 \\ 2 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} $$
> $$ Y[2,0] = 1 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 1 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 2 = \mathbf{4} $$

> **Step I — Compute $Y[2,1]$.** Bottom row, middle window:
> $$ \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$
> $$ Y[2,1] = 2 + 0 + 1 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 1 + 0 + 1 = \mathbf{5} $$

> **Step J — Compute $Y[2,2]$.** Bottom row, rightmost window:
> $$ \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 2 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$
> $$ Y[2,2] = 0 + 0 + 2 + 0 + 1 + 0 + 2 + 0 + 1 = \mathbf{6} $$

> **Final Output Feature Map (with ReLU, $b=0$):**
> $$ \mathbf{Y} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 7 & 9 \\ 6 & 6 & 4 \\ 4 & 5 & 6 \end{bmatrix} $$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effects of Hyperparameters

| Scenario | $H$ | $k$ | $s$ | $p$ | $H'$ | Param count per filter | Engineering Implication |
|---|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|---|
| Standard conv | 32 | 3 | 1 | 0 | 30 | 9 (×$C_{in}$) + 1 | Maximum detail preserved; output shrinks |
| Strided conv | 32 | 3 | 2 | 0 | 15 | 9 (×$C_{in}$) + 1 | Downsamples; faster computation |
| Same padding | 32 | 3 | 1 | 1 | 32 | 9 (×$C_{in}$) + 1 | Spatial size preserved; edges encoded |
| Large filter | 32 | 5 | 1 | 2 | 32 | 25 (×$C_{in}$) + 1 | Bigger receptive field; more parameters |
| Aggressive | 32 | 7 | 3 | 0 | 9 | 49 (×$C_{in}$) + 1 | Heavy downsampling; rarely used alone |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory rule of thumb:** Total memory of activations in one conv layer is dominated by the *output* feature map: $H' \times W' \times C' \times 4$ bytes (for 32-bit floats). For a $224 \times 224 \times 64$ feature map, this is ≈ **3.2 MB per image** — small. But deeper layers and batch processing scale this rapidly.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Single 2-D Convolution Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Image X 5x5x1] --> B[Place 3x3 Filter at 0,0]
    B --> C[Element-wise Multiply and Sum]
    C --> D[Add Bias b and Apply ReLU]
    D --> E[Store Y at 0,0]
    E --> F{Stride Reached?}
    F -- No --> G[Slide Filter by Stride s]
    G --> C
    F -- Yes --> H{End of Row?}
    H -- No --> I[Move Filter Down by Stride s Reset Column]
    I --> C
    H -- Yes --> J[Stack All C Output Feature Maps]
    J --> K[Final Output Volume H prime x W prime x C prime]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — End-to-End Block-Level CNN Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Input Image 32x32x3] --> A1[Conv Layer k 3 s 1 p 1 32 filters]
    A1 --> A2[ReLU Activation]
    A2 --> A3[Conv Layer k 3 s 1 p 1 32 filters]
    A3 --> A4[ReLU Activation]
    A4 --> A5[Max Pool 2x2 s 2]
    A5 --> A6[Conv Layer k 3 s 1 p 1 64 filters]
    A6 --> A7[ReLU Activation]
    A7 --> A8[Conv Layer k 3 s 1 p 1 64 filters]
    A8 --> A9[ReLU Activation]
    A9 --> A10[Max Pool 2x2 s 2]
    A10 --> A11[Flatten]
    A11 --> A12[Fully Connected Layer 128 units]
    A12 --> A13[ReLU]
    A13 --> A14[Softmax Output 10 classes]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Convolution-Output Size Calculation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start Given H, W, k_h, k_w, s, p] --> S1[Compute H prime = floor of H minus k_h plus 2p divided by s plus 1]
    S1 --> S2[Compute W prime = floor of W minus k_w plus 2p divided by s plus 1]
    S2 --> S3{Is Result an Integer?}
    S3 -- Yes --> S4[Valid Configuration Proceed to Forward Pass]
    S3 -- No --> S5[Adjust p or s to Ensure Integer Output Dimension]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Tip:** The integer-output check is a favourite KTU sub-question. If the formula gives a non-integer, the configuration is invalid because the filter would spill outside the input.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2-Mark / 3-Mark Short-Answer Type)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, 3 Marks]**
*Define the convolution operation used in CNNs. What is a kernel in this context?*

**Model Answer:**
The convolution operation in a CNN is a discrete sliding-window dot product between a learnable **kernel** (filter) $\mathbf{K} \in \mathbb{R}^{k_h \times k_w \times C_{in}}$ and the input feature map $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C_{in}}$. At each spatial location $(i,j)$, the filter computes the sum of element-wise products between its weights and the overlapping input region, adds a bias, and (optionally) passes the result through a non-linear activation. The output is the **feature map** $\mathbf{Y} \in \mathbb{R}^{H' \times W' \times C'}$, where $C'$ equals the number of filters used. A **kernel** is the small matrix of learnable weights — typically $3 \times 3$ or $5 \times 5$ — that detects a specific local pattern (e.g., an edge, blob, or texture) by computing its correlation with patches of the input.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, 3 Marks]**
*Differentiate between **"valid"** and **"same"** padding in a convolutional layer.*

**Model Answer:**
**Valid padding** means $p = 0$ — no zeros are added around the input border. Consequently, the output feature map is smaller than the input: $H' = H - k + 1$. **Same padding** means $p = (k-1)/2$ (for odd $k$ and stride 1), so the output spatial dimensions equal the input dimensions: $H' = H$. The "same" convention is used when the architecture designer wants to preserve spatial resolution across multiple conv layers, while "valid" is used when shrinking is acceptable or desired (e.g., before pooling).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14-Mark ESE Type — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) Explain the architecture and working of a Convolutional Neural Network. Discuss in detail the role of the convolution layer, pooling layer, and fully-connected layer. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A CNN is a deep, feed-forward neural network specialized for spatially structured data. Its working can be described as a sequence of stages:

**1. Convolution Layer (Feature Extraction).**
The convolution layer applies a set of learnable filters to the input. Each filter slides across the spatial dimensions of the input and computes dot products, producing one **feature map** per filter. Early layers learn low-level features (edges, colour gradients); deeper layers compose these into high-level semantic features (eyes, wheels, faces). Hyperparameters $k$, $s$, and $p$ control the receptive field, downsampling rate, and output size. A non-linear activation (typically ReLU) is applied immediately after the convolution: $f(x) = \max(0, x)$, which introduces the non-linearity that allows the network to learn complex functions.

**2. Pooling Layer (Spatial Downsampling).**
Pooling progressively reduces the spatial size of the feature maps, decreasing computational cost and providing a degree of translation invariance. The two main types are:
- **Max Pooling:** Outputs the maximum value in each non-overlapping $2 \times 2$ window.
- **Average Pooling:** Outputs the average value in each window.
For input $H \times W$ and pool size $k_p$ and stride $s_p$, the output size is:
$$ H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{H - k_p}{s_p} \right\rfloor + 1, \quad W_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{W - k_p}{s_p} \right\rfloor + 1 $$

**3. Fully-Connected (FC) Layer (Classifier).**
After several conv-pool blocks, the resulting feature maps are **flattened** into a 1-D vector and fed to one or more FC layers. These layers behave like a standard multi-layer perceptron, and the final FC layer uses a **Softmax** activation for $K$-class classification:
$$ P(y = k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}} $$

**End-to-end flow:** Input Image → [Conv → ReLU → Pool] × $N$ → Flatten → FC → Softmax → Class Probabilities. The entire network is trained by minimizing a loss function (e.g., cross-entropy) using **back-propagation** combined with gradient descent, where the gradients of the loss with respect to every kernel weight and bias are computed via the chain rule.

#### (b) For an input image of size $32 \times 32 \times 3$ passed through a convolution layer with 16 filters of size $5 \times 5$, stride 2, and same padding, compute: (i) the output feature-map size, (ii) the number of parameters, and (iii) the receptive field. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given: $H = W = 32$, $C_{in} = 3$, $k_h = k_w = 5$, $C_{out} = 16$, $s = 2$, padding = "same" ⇒ $p = (5-1)/2 = 2$.

**(i) Output feature-map size.**

$$ H' = \left\lfloor \frac{32 - 5 + 2(2)}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor \frac{31}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = 15 + 1 = 16 $$

$$ W' = \left\lfloor \frac{32 - 5 + 2(2)}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = 16 $$

**Output volume = $16 \times 16 \times 16$.**

**(ii) Number of parameters.**

Each filter has $k_h \cdot k_w \cdot C_{in} = 5 \times 5 \times 3 = 75$ weights plus **1** bias, i.e., **76** parameters per filter. With 16 filters:

$$ \text{Total params} = 76 \times 16 = \mathbf{1{,}216} $$

**(iii) Receptive field.**

Since $s = 2$, the effective stride of the receptive field expansion is also 2. Using the simplified formula for one layer with stride $s$ and kernel $k$:

$$ r = k = 5 $$

But because the stride affects the *projection* back to the input, the effective receptive field coverage in input space is:

$$ r_{\text{eff}} = (k - 1) \cdot s + 1 = (5-1)\cdot 2 + 1 = \mathbf{9} $$

So each output neuron "sees" a $9 \times 9$ region of the original input (even though the kernel itself is only $5 \times 5$).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### (a) Define and explain the convolution operation in a CNN with a suitable numerical example. Demonstrate how a $3 \times 3$ filter applied with stride 1 and no padding transforms a $5 \times 5$ input. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition.** The convolution operation in a CNN is a discrete sliding-window dot product between a learnable **filter** (kernel) $\mathbf{K}$ of size $k_h \times k_w$ and an input image $\mathbf{X}$ of size $H \times W$. For each spatial position $(i,j)$:

$$ Y[i,j] = \sum_{m=0}^{k_h-1} \sum_{n=0}^{k_w-1} X[i+m,\, j+n]\, K[m,n] + b $$

The filter slides across the input with a given **stride** $s$ and produces an output feature map $\mathbf{Y}$ of size $H' \times W'$ where $H' = (H - k_h)/s + 1$ and similarly for $W'$.

**Numerical Example (already shown in Section 3.2).**

Let
$$ \mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 & 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 0 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$

With $s = 1$, $p = 0$, the output size is $H' = (5-3)/1 + 1 = 3$. Computing each of the 9 positions yields the feature map:
$$ \mathbf{Y} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 7 & 9 \\ 6 & 6 & 4 \\ 4 & 5 & 6 \end{bmatrix} $$

(Detailed per-position arithmetic is provided in Section 3.2.) This output is a **feature map** that highlights where the pattern encoded by $\mathbf{K}$ (e.g., a cross-shape diagonal detector) is strongly present in the input.

#### (b) For a CNN layer with input volume $224 \times 224 \times 64$, 128 filters of size $3 \times 3$, stride 1, and same padding, calculate: (i) output feature-map dimensions, (ii) total trainable parameters in the layer, (iii) the number of multiply-accumulate (MAC) operations per image, and (iv) compare the parameters with a fully-connected alternative that flattens the input. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given: $H = W = 224$, $C_{in} = 64$, $k_h = k_w = 3$, $C_{out} = 128$, $s = 1$, $p = (3-1)/2 = 1$ (same padding).

**(i) Output feature-map dimensions.**

$$ H' = \left\lfloor \frac{224 - 3 + 2(1)}{1} \right\rfloor + 1 = 224, \quad W' = 224 $$

**Output volume = $224 \times 224 \times 128$.**

**(ii) Total trainable parameters.**

Per filter: $3 \times 3 \times 64 + 1 = 577$. For 128 filters:
$$ \text{Params}_{conv} = 577 \times 128 = \mathbf{73{,}856} $$

**(iii) MAC operations per image.**

Each output element requires $3 \times 3 \times 64 = 576$ MACs. Total output elements = $224 \times 224 \times 128 = 6{,}422{,}528$.
$$ \text{MACs} = 576 \times 6{,}422{,}528 = \mathbf{3.7 \times 10^{9}} \text{ MACs} $$

**(iv) Comparison with a fully-connected layer.**

If the input is flattened to $224 \times 224 \times 64 = 3{,}207{,}424$ neurons and connected to, say, 128 output neurons, the parameter count would be:
$$ \text{Params}_{FC} = 3{,}207{,}424 \times 128 + 128 \approx \mathbf{410 \text{ million}} $$

**The CNN uses ≈ 5,500× fewer parameters** than the equivalent FC layer, demonstrating the dramatic efficiency gained through **parameter sharing** and **local connectivity** — a key reason CNNs are practical for image data.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (as observed in KTU 2022–2024 answer scripts):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the bias term in parameter count** — every filter has **one** bias in addition to its weights; students often write $k \cdot k \cdot C_{in}$ and miss the `+1`, losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing output depth with output volume** — *depth* = number of filters; *volume* = $H' \times W' \times \text{depth}$. Examiners expect the *full* volume to be stated.
> 3. **Integer-output check** — when asked "is the configuration valid?", the examiner expects the student to check that $(H - k + 2p)/s$ is a non-negative *integer*; many students only state the formula without verification.
> 4. **Mixing up convolution with cross-correlation** — use the term "convolution" in answers but be ready to mention the flip difference if asked in Part A.
> 5. **Numerical-example arithmetic errors** — even a single multiplication mistake in a $3 \times 3$ example (Section 3.2) costs 2 marks; students are advised to lay out each term explicitly.
> 6. **Same-padding value** — students often write $p = k$ instead of $p = (k-1)/2$, which is a textbook sign of incomplete preparation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Convolution in CNNs = sliding dot product** of a learnable filter over the input, producing a feature map.
- **Key formula** for output size: $H' = \lfloor (H - k + 2p)/s \rfloor + 1$ (and analogously for $W'$).
- **Three key CNN advantages** over fully-connected networks: **parameter sharing**, **local connectivity**, and **translation equivariance/invariance**.
- **Output depth** of a conv layer = number of filters in that layer.
- **Parameter count per filter** = $k_h \cdot k_w \cdot C_{in} + 1$ (the `+1` is the bias).
- **Total parameters** = (per-filter parameters) $\times$ (number of filters) $C_{out}$.
- **Same padding** ($p = (k-1)/2$) preserves spatial size; **valid padding** ($p = 0$) shrinks it.
- **Stride $s$** controls the rate of downsampling inside the conv layer itself.
- **Receptive field** of a single conv layer with stride $s$ and kernel $k$: $r = (k-1) \cdot s + 1$.
- A non-linearity (**ReLU**) is applied after every convolution in modern CNNs.
- **Pooling** is *separate* from convolution and is used for spatial downsampling and translation invariance.
- The **cross-correlation** (no kernel flip) is what is actually implemented but is called "convolution" in deep-learning literature.
- CNNs are the standard choice for **image classification, object detection, segmentation, and any spatially correlated data** (audio spectrograms, EEG/ECG time-series, etc.).
- Always verify that $(H - k + 2p)/s$ is a **non-negative integer** before declaring a conv configuration valid.
- Remember the difference between **filter** (the sliding window) and **feature map** (the output it produces).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pooling Layers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT501 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pooling Layers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — POOLING LAYERS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pooling Layer (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** A pooling layer is a downsampling operation applied to feature maps produced by convolutional layers in a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN). It partitions each feature map into non-overlapping (or sometimes overlapping) rectangular sub-regions and replaces each region with a single summary statistic, thereby reducing spatial resolution, controlling overfitting, and providing a form of translation invariance.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCITT501 — Machine Learning, Module 4: *Deep Learning Architectures*), pooling is positioned as a **parameter-free (or near parameter-free) dimensionality reduction operator** that sits between successive convolution–activation blocks. Unlike a convolution, a pooling layer contains **no learnable weights and biases** (with the exception of *Global* pooling variants that occasionally use a 1×1×D projection, but this is downstream of pooling itself).

The three pooling operations specified in the KTU syllabus are:
1. **Max Pooling**
2. **Average (Mean) Pooling**
3. **Global Pooling** (Global Average Pooling & Global Max Pooling)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Newspaper Photo Editor":**
> Imagine you are a photo editor at a newspaper. Your job is to shrink a large, high-resolution photograph into a small thumbnail that still preserves the *essential* visual information. You do **not** scrutinize every single pixel. Instead, you divide the image into small square blocks (say, 2×2 pixels each), and for each block you make a quick decision:
> - Either you **keep the brightest/most striking pixel** (this is *Max Pooling* — "what stood out the most?").
> - Or you **average all pixels** in the block (this is *Average Pooling* — "what is the general tone of this region?").
>
> The result is a much smaller image that still tells the same story. The pooling layer does *exactly* this to feature maps, allowing the next convolutional layer to "see" a coarser, more abstract view of the features.

### 1.3 Why Pooling Exists — The Three Engineering Reasons

- **Spatial Downsampling (Computational Efficiency):** Pooling reduces the height and width of feature maps (e.g., from 224×224 to 112×112), drastically cutting memory and floating-point operations in deeper layers. Standard reduction metric: a 2×2 pool with stride 2 reduces activations by a factor of **4×**.
- **Translation Invariance (Robustness):** A small shift in the input (e.g., a cat's ear moves 3 pixels to the right) produces nearly the same pooled output. This makes the network robust to the spatial location of features.
- **Receptive Field Expansion:** Each subsequent neuron "sees" a larger portion of the original input. This hierarchical expansion is critical for capturing high-level semantic features.

### 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU Standard)

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Pool Window / Kernel Size | $k_h \times k_w$ | Spatial extent of the pooling region (commonly **2×2**) |
| Stride | $s$ | Step size of the window (commonly **2**) |
| Input Feature Map Size | $H_{in} \times W_{in} \times D_{in}$ | Height × Width × Depth (channels) |
| Output Feature Map Size | $H_{out} \times W_{out} \times D_{out}$ | Reduced spatial map (depth is preserved) |
| Padding | $p$ | Rarely used in pooling; defaults to **0** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of a Pooling Layer

The pooling operation is a **sliding window transform** applied independently to **every channel** (depth slice) of the input feature map. The mathematical steps are:

1. **Input Reception:** A 3-D tensor of shape $H_{in} \times W_{in} \times D_{in}$ enters the layer.
2. **Window Partition:** The layer slides a window of size $k_h \times k_w$ across the spatial dimensions. For each position $(i, j)$ the window covers the region:
   $$\text{Region}(i,j) = \{ (p,q) \mid i \le p < i + k_h,\; j \le q < j + k_w \}$$
3. **Channel Independence:** The window operates on each of the $D_{in}$ channels separately. Pooling **does not mix channels** (unlike convolution).
4. **Aggregation Function:** A deterministic function $f(\cdot)$ is applied to the values inside the window:
   - Max Pooling: $f = \max$
   - Average Pooling: $f = \text{mean}$
   - L2 Pooling: $f = \sqrt{\frac{1}{k_h k_w}\sum x^2}$ (advanced, not in KTU core syllabus)
5. **Stride Movement:** The window jumps by $s$ pixels to the next region. If $s = k_h = k_w$, the regions are **non-overlapping** (the most common case).
6. **Output Construction:** The aggregated scalars form an output map of shape $H_{out} \times W_{out} \times D_{out}$, where $D_{out} = D_{in}$.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Max Pooling vs Average Pooling

> [!NOTE]
> **Max Pooling (preserves the strongest activations):** When a convolutional filter detects a feature (e.g., an edge, a corner), it produces a high activation. Max pooling keeps the **peak response** in each local neighborhood. This is ideal for tasks like **object detection and classification** (ImageNet, CIFAR) because we care about *whether* a feature is present, not its average intensity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Average Pooling (preserves the global information):** Average pooling smooths the feature map by reporting the mean. It is often used in **deeper layers of classification networks** (e.g., ResNet, DenseNet) just before the fully connected head, where we want a smooth summary of the entire receptive field. Global Average Pooling is the canonical example.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULAS — MEMORIZE THESE FOR KTU EXAMS**

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H_{out} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{H_{in} + 2p - k_h}{s} \right\rfloor + 1$ | Output height |
| 2 | $W_{out} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{W_{in} + 2p - k_w}{s} \right\rfloor + 1$ | Output width |
| 3 | $D_{out} = D_{in}$ | Depth is **preserved** |
| 4 | $y_{i,j,c} = \max_{(p,q) \in R(i,j)} x_{p,q,c}$ | Max pooling operation |
| 5 | $y_{i,j,c} = \dfrac{1}{k_h k_w} \sum_{(p,q) \in R(i,j)} x_{p,q,c}$ | Average pooling operation |
| 6 | $\text{GAP}(x) = \dfrac{1}{H \cdot W} \sum_{p=1}^{H} \sum_{q=1}^{W} x_{p,q,c}$ | Global Average Pooling (per channel $c$) |
| 7 | $\text{GMP}(x) = \max_{p,q} \; x_{p,q,c}$ | Global Max Pooling (per channel $c$) |
| 8 | $\text{Parameter Count}_{\text{Pool}} = 0$ | Pooling has **zero learnable parameters** |

> [!WARNING]
> **For KTU 14-mark derivations, you must show the floor operation $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ explicitly.** Examiners frequently test the case when $H_{in}$ is not perfectly divisible by the kernel, and they expect the student to apply the integer floor.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Imaging (Tumor Detection):** Pooling allows CNNs to scan large CT/MRI slices efficiently while preserving tumor-edge activations (via max pooling).
- **Autonomous Driving (Tesla, Waymo):** Spatial reduction via pooling enables real-time object detection on edge GPUs (NVIDIA Jetson).
- **Mobile & Embedded AI (MobileNet, EfficientNet):** Heavy use of pooling (and strided convolutions) keeps models lightweight for smartphones.
- **Satellite & Remote Sensing:** Pooling allows processing of gigapixel satellite imagery by hierarchically reducing resolution.

### 2.5 Hyperparameters — Common KTU Settings

| Hyperparameter | Standard Value | KTU Expectation |
|---|---|---|
| Kernel size $k_h \times k_w$ | **2×2** | Default answer unless stated |
| Stride $s$ | **2** (equal to kernel) | Produces non-overlapping regions |
| Padding $p$ | **0** (valid) | Almost never used in pooling |
| Type | **Max** in conv blocks, **Average/GAP** at head | Know the architectural convention |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Output Spatial Dimension

We want to determine $H_{out}$ given an input of height $H_{in}$, kernel $k_h$, stride $s$, and padding $p$.

**Step 1 — Conceptual Setup:**
Imagine the input as a 1-D line of length $H_{in}$. Place a window of size $k_h$ at the top, then slide it down by $s$ pixels each step. Count how many full placements fit.

**Step 2 — Algebraic Formulation:**
The number of valid starting positions for the window is:
$$n = \frac{H_{in} + 2p - k_h}{s} + 1$$
This is a direct consequence of the convolution arithmetic framework (same as convolution layers).

**Step 3 — Applying the Floor Operation:**
Since $n$ must be a non-negative integer, we apply the floor function:
$$H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{H_{in} + 2p - k_h}{s} \right\rfloor + 1$$

**Step 4 — Worked Example (KTU Typical Problem):**
Given $H_{in} = 224$, $k_h = 2$, $s = 2$, $p = 0$:
$$H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{224 + 0 - 2}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = \lfloor 111 \rfloor + 1 = 112$$

**Step 5 — Non-Divisible Case:**
Given $H_{in} = 225$, $k_h = 2$, $s = 2$, $p = 0$:
$$H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{225 - 2}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = \lfloor 111.5 \rfloor + 1 = 111 + 1 = 112$$
Note the floor: 111.5 is rounded **down** to 111.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Number of Parameters in a Pooling Layer

A pooling layer has **no learnable weights or biases** because it performs a fixed, deterministic operation. Formally, the parameter count is:
$$P_{\text{pool}} = 0$$
**Comparison with a Convolution Layer** (this contrast is a KTU favourite):
$$P_{\text{conv}} = (k_h \cdot k_w \cdot D_{in}) \cdot D_{out} + D_{out}$$
For a 3×3 conv with $D_{in} = D_{out} = 64$:
$$P_{\text{conv}} = 9 \cdot 64 \cdot 64 + 64 = 36{,}928 \; \text{parameters}$$
This 64× difference is why pooling (or strided convolutions) is essential for deep networks.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Numerical Max Pooling Example

Consider a single-channel input feature map of size $4 \times 4$ and a max pool with $k=2$, $s=2$:

$$X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 & 2 & 4 \\ 5 & 6 & 1 & 2 \\ 7 & 8 & 3 & 5 \\ 4 & 2 & 6 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 1 — Identify the Four 2×2 Sub-Regions (Top-Left to Bottom-Right):**
- Region A (rows 1–2, cols 1–2): $\{1, 3, 5, 6\}$
- Region B (rows 1–2, cols 3–4): $\{2, 4, 1, 2\}$
- Region C (rows 3–4, cols 1–2): $\{7, 8, 4, 2\}$
- Region D (rows 3–4, cols 3–4): $\{3, 5, 6, 1\}$

**Step 2 — Apply the Max Operation:**
- $\max(1,3,5,6) = 6$
- $\max(2,4,1,2) = 4$
- $\max(7,8,4,2) = 8$
- $\max(3,5,6,1) = 6$

**Step 3 — Assemble the Output 2×2 Map:**
$$Y = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 4 \\ 8 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Numerical Average Pooling Example

Using the same input $X$ as above:

**Step 1 — Compute the Mean of Each Region:**
- $\bar{A} = \dfrac{1+3+5+6}{4} = \dfrac{15}{4} = 3.75$
- $\bar{B} = \dfrac{2+4+1+2}{4} = \dfrac{9}{4} = 2.25$
- $\bar{C} = \dfrac{7+8+4+2}{4} = \dfrac{21}{4} = 5.25$
- $\bar{D} = \dfrac{3+5+6+1}{4} = \dfrac{15}{4} = 3.75$

**Step 2 — Output Map:**
$$Y_{\text{avg}} = \begin{bmatrix} 3.75 & 2.25 \\ 5.25 & 3.75 \end{bmatrix}$$

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Global Average Pooling (GAP) Worked Example

Suppose a convolutional block outputs a $7 \times 7 \times 512$ feature map before the classifier head.

**Step 1 — Define the Operation:**
For each of the 512 channels $c$:
$$\text{GAP}_c = \frac{1}{7 \times 7} \sum_{p=1}^{7} \sum_{q=1}^{7} x_{p,q,c}$$

**Step 2 — Resulting Tensor Shape:**
$$7 \times 7 \times 512 \;\xrightarrow{\text{GAP}}\; 1 \times 1 \times 512$$

**Step 3 — Feed to Classifier:**
The 512-D vector is fed directly into a fully connected (Dense) layer with softmax for classification. This is the architecture of **GoogLeNet/Inception-v3** and **ResNet** (after the final conv block).

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Criterion | Max Pooling | Average Pooling | Global Average Pooling |
|---|---|---|---|
| Position in CNN | Early/Middle conv blocks | Late conv blocks | Final block (head) |
| Preserves | Strongest activation | Smoothed average | Channel-wise summary |
| Sensitivity to noise | Higher (only peaks) | Lower (smoothing) | Lowest |
| Output spatial size | $H/2 \times W/2$ (k=2,s=2) | $H/2 \times W/2$ | $1 \times 1$ |
| Use in ResNet/VGG | Yes (VGG-style) | Rare | Yes (replaces FC head) |
| KTU frequency | **Very High** | Medium | High (theory questions) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Pooling Layer Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Feature Map H_in x W_in x D_in] --> B[Initialize Sliding Window of size k_h x k_w]
    B --> C[Position Window at Top Left Corner i=0 j=0]
    C --> D{Window Within Bounds?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Extract Region R sub i,j]
    E --> F[Select Channel c from 1 to D_in]
    F --> G{Pooling Type?}
    G -- Max --> H1[Compute Max of Region]
    G -- Average --> H2[Compute Mean of Region]
    G -- Global --> H3[Compute Global Statistic over entire H x W]
    H1 --> I[Store Output Value y sub i,j,c]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Shift Window by Stride s]
    J --> K{End of Row?}
    K -- No --> C
    K -- Yes --> L[Move to Next Row]
    L --> M{End of Feature Map?}
    M -- No --> C
    M -- Yes --> N[Output Feature Map H_out x W_out x D_out]
    N --> O[Pass to Next Convolutional Layer]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Pooling Inside a Typical CNN

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Image 224 x 224 x 3] --> B[Conv2D + ReLU]
    B --> C[Pooling Layer 2x2 s=2]
    C --> D[Feature Map 112 x 112 x 64]
    D --> E[Conv2D + ReLU]
    E --> F[Pooling Layer 2x2 s=2]
    F --> G[Feature Map 56 x 56 x 128]
    G --> H[Conv2D + ReLU]
    H --> I[Pooling Layer 2x2 s=2]
    I --> J[Feature Map 28 x 28 x 256]
    J --> K[Global Average Pooling]
    K --> L[Vector 1 x 1 x 256]
    L --> M[Dense Softmax Output 1000 classes]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid — Max vs Average Comparison on a 2x2 Patch

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Input Patch 2x2: a b c d] --> Q{Pooling Operator}
    Q --> R1[Max Pooling]
    Q --> R2[Average Pooling]
    R1 --> S1[Output = max a b c d]
    R2 --> S2[Output = a+b+c+d / 4]
    S1 --> T1[Preserves Strongest Activation]
    S2 --> T2[Preserves Smoothed Average]
    T1 --> U[Best for Feature Detection]
    T2 --> V[Best for Global Summary]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid — Training vs Inference Behavior of Pooling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Training Phase] --> Y1[Dropout sometimes applied after pooling]
    Y1 --> Z1[Backprop flows through argmax indices for max pool]
    A[Inference Phase] --> B1[Pure deterministic pooling]
    B1 --> C1[Lower latency on edge devices]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** What is a pooling layer in a CNN? List any two types.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A pooling layer is a downsampling operation in a Convolutional Neural Network that reduces the spatial dimensions (height and width) of feature maps while preserving the depth. It has **zero learnable parameters** and provides a degree of translation invariance. Two types are: **(1) Max Pooling** — which retains the maximum value in each pooling window, and **(2) Average Pooling** — which computes the mean of all values in the window. *(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for "zero parameters" insight, 1 mark for two types.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** State the formula to compute the output dimension of a pooling layer and explain the role of stride.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The output height is given by:
$$H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{H_{in} + 2p - k_h}{s} \right\rfloor + 1$$
The **stride $s$** controls how many pixels the pooling window shifts at each step. When $s$ equals the kernel size, the windows are non-overlapping and the output spatial size is exactly halved (for $k=2, s=2$). A stride smaller than the kernel produces overlapping windows (used rarely). *(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for explanation, 1 mark for the k=2, s=2 special case.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the operational difference between **Max Pooling** and **Average Pooling** with a numerical example on a 4×4 input using a 2×2 pool with stride 2. Show all input regions and the resulting output feature maps.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] For an input feature map of size **32 × 32 × 16** with a pooling layer of kernel **3 × 3** and stride **2**, compute the output dimensions. Discuss why a **3 × 3** pool is sometimes used instead of a 2 × 2 pool.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION FOR Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — Define Operations (1 mark):**
- **Max Pooling:** $y_{i,j,c} = \max(x_{p,q,c})$ over the window.
- **Average Pooling:** $y_{i,j,c} = \frac{1}{k_h k_w}\sum x_{p,q,c}$ over the window.

**Step 2 — Use the Numerical Input (1 mark):**
$$X = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 4 & 1 & 0 \\ 3 & 1 & 5 & 2 \\ 0 & 6 & 2 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 & 7 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Four 2×2 Sub-Regions (1 mark):**
- $R_{11} = \{2, 4, 3, 1\}$
- $R_{12} = \{1, 0, 5, 2\}$
- $R_{21} = \{0, 6, 1, 4\}$
- $R_{22} = \{2, 3, 7, 1\}$

**Step 4 — Compute Max Output (1 mark):**
- $\max(R_{11}) = 4$
- $\max(R_{12}) = 5$
- $\max(R_{21}) = 6$
- $\max(R_{22}) = 7$
$$Y_{\text{max}} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 5 \\ 6 & 7 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Average Output (1 mark):**
- $\bar{R}_{11} = (2+4+3+1)/4 = 2.5$
- $\bar{R}_{12} = (1+0+5+2)/4 = 2.0$
- $\bar{R}_{21} = (0+6+1+4)/4 = 2.75$
- $\bar{R}_{22} = (2+3+7+1)/4 = 3.25$
$$Y_{\text{avg}} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.5 & 2.0 \\ 2.75 & 3.25 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Discuss Difference (2 marks):**
Max pooling **preserves the strongest activations** (peaks like 7) and discards low-magnitude values. Average pooling **smooths the map** and reports the regional mean (e.g., 3.25). Max pooling is preferred for feature detection, while average pooling is preferred for smoother summarization in deeper layers.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION FOR Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Apply the Output Formula (2 marks):**
With $H_{in} = W_{in} = 32$, $k_h = k_w = 3$, $s = 2$, $p = 0$:
$$H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{32 - 3}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = \lfloor 14.5 \rfloor + 1 = 14 + 1 = 15$$
$$W_{out} = 15$$
$$D_{out} = 16 \; (\text{depth preserved})$$
$$\therefore \; \text{Output shape} = 15 \times 15 \times 16$$

**Step 2 — Reduction Factor (1 mark):**
Original activations: $32 \times 32 \times 16 = 16{,}384$
New activations: $15 \times 15 \times 16 = 3{,}600$
Reduction factor: $16{,}384 / 3{,}600 \approx 4.55\times$

**Step 3 — Why Use 3×3 Pool? (2 marks):**
A **3×3 pool with stride 2** creates *overlapping* receptive fields. Adjacent windows share pixels, providing smoother downsampling and reducing "aliasing" artifacts (checkerboard patterns). It is also used in networks like **AlexNet** (which used 3×3 overlapping pools). However, it is **computationally more expensive** than a 2×2 non-overlapping pool and is less common in modern architectures (VGG, ResNet use 2×2 with stride 2).

**Step 4 — Modern Trend (1 mark):**
Modern architectures (ResNet, MobileNet) often **replace pooling with strided convolutions** to allow the network to *learn* the downsampling filter rather than use a fixed max/mean operator.

**Step 5 — Summary Table (1 mark):**
| Property | 2×2, s=2 | 3×3, s=2 |
|---|---|---|
| Overlap | None | Yes |
| Output size (32→?) | 16×16 | 15×15 |
| Smoothing | Low | High |
| Used in | VGG, ResNet | AlexNet, historical |

---

> **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU Pattern]**
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain **Global Average Pooling (GAP)** with its mathematical formulation. How does it differ from a standard average pooling layer? Mention one real-world CNN architecture that uses GAP.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] A CNN architecture has the following sequence: Input (227×227×3) → Conv1 (kernel 11, stride 4) → Pool1 (3×3, stride 2) → Conv2 → Pool2 (2×2, stride 2). Compute the size of the feature map after **Pool1** and **Pool2**, assuming standard valid padding.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION FOR Q.B(a):**

**Step 1 — Definition of GAP (2 marks):**
Global Average Pooling is a pooling operation that reduces each feature map of size $H \times W$ into a **single scalar** by averaging over the *entire* spatial extent. For a feature map of size $H \times W \times D$, the output is a $1 \times 1 \times D$ vector:
$$\text{GAP}(x)_c = \frac{1}{H \cdot W} \sum_{p=1}^{H} \sum_{q=1}^{W} x_{p,q,c}$$

**Step 2 — Difference from Average Pooling (2 marks):**
| Aspect | Average Pooling | Global Average Pooling |
|---|---|---|
| Window size | Local (e.g., 2×2) | Entire feature map (H×W) |
| Output shape | Reduces H,W by factor | $1 \times 1 \times D$ |
| Use case | Mid-network | Replace FC head |
| Preserves | Local smoothness | Channel-level summary |

**Step 3 — Real-World Architecture (1 mark):**
**GoogLeNet (Inception-v1)** introduced GAP to replace the traditional fully connected layers at the head of the network, dramatically reducing parameters and overfitting. **ResNet** and **DenseNet** also use GAP.

**Step 4 — Advantages of GAP (1 mark):**
- Eliminates the parameter-heavy fully connected head.
- Forces the network to learn **channel-wise feature summaries** that are more robust to spatial translation.
- Reduces overfitting by removing millions of FC parameters.

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (1 mark):**
For a 4×4×3 feature map, GAP gives a $1 \times 1 \times 3$ vector:
$$\text{GAP} = \left[ \frac{1}{16}\sum_{p,q} x_{p,q,1},\; \frac{1}{16}\sum_{p,q} x_{p,q,2},\; \frac{1}{16}\sum_{p,q} x_{p,q,3} \right]$$

---

**MODEL SOLUTION FOR Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — After Conv1 (3 marks):**
With input $H_{in} = 227$, kernel $k = 11$, stride $s = 4$, padding $p = 0$:
$$H_{\text{conv1}} = \left\lfloor \frac{227 - 11}{4} \right\rfloor + 1 = \lfloor 54 \rfloor + 1 = 55$$
So feature map after Conv1: $55 \times 55 \times D_1$ (depth $D_1$ depends on filters — assume 96 for AlexNet).

**Step 2 — After Pool1 (2 marks):**
With input $H_{in} = 55$, kernel $k = 3$, stride $s = 2$, padding $p = 0$:
