$$H_{\text{pool1}} = \left\lfloor \frac{55 - 3}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = \lfloor 26 \rfloor + 1 = 27$$
So feature map after Pool1: $27 \times 27 \times 96$ (AlexNet's L1 architecture).

**Step 3 — After Pool2 (2 marks):**
Assume the next conv layer outputs $H = 27$ (or any size given). With $k = 2$, $s = 2$, $p = 0$:
$$H_{\text{pool2}} = \left\lfloor \frac{27 - 2}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = \lfloor 12.5 \rfloor + 1 = 12 + 1 = 13$$
So feature map after Pool2: $13 \times 13 \times D_2$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the floor operation** $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ — KTU examiners *will* deduct 1 mark per instance when the input is not perfectly divisible by the kernel.
> 2. **Confusing pooling with convolution formulas** — Pooling has **zero parameters**; students who write $k_h \cdot k_w \cdot D_{in} \cdot D_{out}$ will lose marks.
> 3. **Mixing up depth** — Pooling preserves depth ($D_{out} = D_{in}$). Forgetting this is a common 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Using stride 1 with 2×2 pool** — This is valid (overlapping pooling) but rarely asked. KTU prefers non-overlapping 2×2, s=2 unless otherwise stated.
> 5. **Stating "pooling learns weights"** — This is a fundamental conceptual error; pooling is *deterministic and parameter-free*.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — POOLING LAYERS**

- ⭐ **Definition:** Pooling is a **parameter-free** downsampling operation in CNNs that reduces spatial dimensions of feature maps.
- ⭐ **Three Core Types:** Max Pooling, Average Pooling, Global (Average/Max) Pooling.
- ⭐ **Output Dimension Formula:** $H_{out} = \lfloor (H_{in} + 2p - k_h)/s \rfloor + 1$.
- ⭐ **Depth Invariance:** $D_{out} = D_{in}$ — pooling operates independently on each channel.
- ⭐ **Default Settings (KTU expected):** Kernel **2×2**, Stride **2**, Padding **0**.
- ⭐ **Parameters:** $P_{\text{pool}} = 0$ (no weights, no biases).
- ⭐ **Max Pooling:** Preserves strongest activation → use in early/middle conv blocks for feature detection.
- ⭐ **Average Pooling:** Smooths the map → use in deeper layers.
- ⭐ **Global Average Pooling (GAP):** Reduces entire $H \times W \times D$ map to $1 \times 1 \times D$ → replaces fully connected head in modern CNNs (ResNet, GoogLeNet).
- ⭐ **Backpropagation:** Gradients flow only through the **argmax index** for max pooling, and are **uniformly distributed** for average pooling.
- ⭐ **Modern Trend:** Strided convolutions are increasingly replacing pooling for learnable downsampling.
- ⭐ **Receptive Field:** Each pool roughly **doubles** the effective receptive field of subsequent neurons.
- ⭐ **Translation Invariance:** Pooling provides a small degree of shift-invariance (1–2 pixels), making CNNs more robust.
- ⭐ **Energy/Compute:** A 2×2, s=2 pool reduces activations and FLOPs by **4×**, critical for mobile/edge AI.
- ⭐ **Key Architectures using Pooling:** LeNet-5 (avg pool), AlexNet (3×3 overlap max pool), VGG-16 (2×2 max pool), ResNet (GAP at head).
- ⭐ **Pitfall:** Pooling **does not mix channels**; it is a 2-D operation applied per-channel.
- ⭐ **Pitfall:** Pooling does **not** include an activation function (ReLU comes *before* pooling in VGG, *after* in some others — know the convention).
- ⭐ **Memory Tip:** Remember "**POOL = PURE, NO LEARNING**" — pooling layers have zero trainable parameters, unlike conv and dense layers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hyperparameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT501 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hyperparameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Machine Learning (ML)** and specifically within the **KTU 2024 Scheme PCITT501** syllabus, a **hyperparameter** is defined as a *configuration variable* whose value is set **prior to the initiation of the learning process** and remains fixed (or scheduled) throughout training. Unlike model parameters (such as weights $\mathbf{W}$ and biases $\mathbf{b}$), hyperparameters cannot be directly estimated from the training data via the learning algorithm itself. They govern the *behavior* of the learning algorithm, the *complexity* of the model, and the *efficiency* of the optimization process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (High-Yield):** *"Hyperparameters are external configuration settings used to manage machine learning model training. They are not learned from data; rather, they are set manually or via search strategies before training begins."*

Mathematically, if $\mathcal{L}(\theta; \mathcal{D})$ represents the loss function parameterized by learnable parameters $\theta$ over dataset $\mathcal{D}$, then the hyperparameter set $\lambda$ controls the optimization landscape:
$$
\theta^{*} = \arg\min_{\theta} \, \mathcal{L}(\theta; \lambda, \mathcal{D})
$$

The hyperparameter vector $\lambda$ is **NOT** updated by gradient descent on the loss.

### 1.2 The Two-Stage Hierarchy: Parameters vs Hyperparameters

| Aspect | Model Parameters ($\theta$) | Hyperparameters ($\lambda$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Source of Value** | Learned from data via optimization | Set manually or by search algorithms |
| **Updated During Training** | Yes (via gradient descent) | No |
| **Examples** | Weights $w_i$, Biases $b$ | Learning rate $\eta$, Number of hidden layers $L$ |
| **Determined By** | The data $\mathcal{D}$ | The practitioner's design choice |
| **Affects** | Model predictions | Model structure and learning behavior |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Coffee Vending Machine"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:** Imagine you are training a **barista apprentice** (the ML model) to make the perfect cup of coffee (the prediction).
>
> - The **recipe proportions** the apprentice learns through trial and error (amount of sugar, milk) are the **parameters** — they are tuned *during* training by tasting the coffee.
> - The **machine settings** (water temperature = **90°C**, pressure = **9 bar**, grind size = **fine**) are the **hyperparameters** — *you* set them before the apprentice even begins. Changing the temperature drastically changes the learning experience, but the apprentice never adjusts the temperature itself.
>
> Just like the barista needs the *right* machine settings to learn effectively, an ML model needs the *right* hyperparameters to converge to a high-quality solution.

### 1.4 Why Hyperparameters Are Critical in KTU Examinations

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Emphasis:** In KTU 2024 ESE pattern questions, hyperparameters are frequently tested in the context of *model selection*, *regularization tuning*, and *optimization convergence*. A student who confuses hyperparameters with parameters invariably loses **3–4 marks** on Part B (14-mark) questions.

### 1.5 Standard Classification of Hyperparameters

Hyperparameters can be broadly classified into **four canonical categories**, each carrying different mark weightage in KTU assessments:

1. **Structural / Architectural Hyperparameters** — define the *topology* of the model.
   - Examples: Number of hidden layers $L$, Number of neurons per layer $n^{(l)}$, Kernel size in CNNs.
2. **Optimization Hyperparameters** — control *how* the model learns.
   - Examples: Learning rate $\eta$, Batch size $B$, Momentum $\beta$, Number of epochs $E$.
3. **Regularization Hyperparameters** — control *generalization* and prevent overfitting.
   - Examples: L2 penalty $\lambda_{reg}$, Dropout rate $p_{drop}$, Early stopping patience.
4. **Data / Preprocessing Hyperparameters** — control input representation.
   - Examples: Feature scaling range, PCA components $k$, Vocabulary size in NLP.

Each category has a specific role and a distinct mathematical footprint, which we will explore in Section 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Lifecycle of a Hyperparameter

The journey of a hyperparameter can be broken down into five structured stages. KTU examiners frequently frame 7-mark sub-questions around this lifecycle.

1. **Stage 1 — Identification:** The practitioner identifies candidate hyperparameters from the algorithm specification (e.g., for SVM: $C$, $\gamma$; for Random Forest: $n_{trees}$, $max\_depth$).
2. **Stage 2 — Initialization:** A sensible default or heuristic value is assigned (e.g., $\eta = 0.01$, batch size $B = 32$).
3. **Stage 3 — Search:** A search strategy (Grid, Random, Bayesian) explores the hyperparameter space $\Lambda$.
4. **Stage 4 — Evaluation:** Each candidate configuration $\lambda_i$ is evaluated on a validation set, producing a performance score $S(\lambda_i)$.
5. **Stage 5 — Selection:** The configuration $\lambda^{*} = \arg\max_{\lambda \in \Lambda} S(\lambda)$ is selected for final model training.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Category

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why do we need hyperparameters at all?** Theoretically, an infinitely wide neural network with infinite training data could learn any function. In practice, we have **finite compute**, **finite data**, and **finite time**. Hyperparameters are the levers that let us trade off *bias* vs *variance*, *training speed* vs *final accuracy*, and *model capacity* vs *generalization*.

- **Why learning rate $\eta$?** It scales the gradient step. Too large $\Rightarrow$ divergence. Too small $\Rightarrow$ slow convergence.
- **Why regularization $\lambda_{reg}$?** It penalizes large weights, reducing overfitting and improving generalization to unseen data.
- **Why batch size $B$?** It controls the variance of the gradient estimate and the memory footprint per iteration.
- **Why number of epochs $E$?** It determines how many times the model sees the full training set; too few $\Rightarrow$ underfitting, too many $\Rightarrow$ overfitting.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table contains the most frequently tested equations in KTU 2024 ESE for Module 4 (Hyperparameters). Memorize these verbatim.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Components & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Regularized Loss Function (L2) | $\mathcal{L}_{reg}(\theta) = \mathcal{L}(\theta) + \lambda_{reg} \cdot \Vert \theta \Vert_{2}^{2}$ | $\lambda_{reg} \geq 0$ (regularization strength, dimensionless) |
| 2 | L1 Regularized Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{reg}(\theta) = \mathcal{L}(\theta) + \lambda_{reg} \cdot \Vert \theta \Vert_{1}$ | Promotes sparsity in weights |
| 3 | Gradient Descent Update with $\eta$ | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta_{t})$ | $\eta > 0$ (learning rate, dimensionless) |
| 4 | Time-Based LR Decay | $\eta_{t} = \eta_{0} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + \text{decay} \cdot t}$ | $t$ = current epoch/step |
| 5 | Exponential LR Decay | $\eta_{t} = \eta_{0} \cdot \gamma^{t}$ | $\gamma \in (0,1)$ (decay rate) |
| 6 | Step LR Decay | $\eta_{t} = \eta_{0} \cdot \gamma^{\lfloor t / \text{step\_size} \rfloor}$ | Drops $\eta$ at fixed intervals |
| 7 | Momentum Update | $v_{t+1} = \beta v_{t} + (1-\beta) \nabla \mathcal{L}; \quad \theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \eta v_{t+1}$ | $\beta \in [0,1)$ (momentum coefficient) |
| 8 | Adam First Moment Estimate | $m_{t} = \beta_{1} m_{t-1} + (1-\beta_{1}) g_{t}$ | $\beta_{1} = 0.9$ (typical default) |
| 9 | Adam Second Moment Estimate | $v_{t} = \beta_{2} v_{t-1} + (1-\beta_{2}) g_{t}^{2}$ | $\beta_{2} = 0.999$ (typical default) |
| 10 | Dropout Effective Weights | $\mathbb{E}[w_{drop}] = p_{keep} \cdot w$ | $p_{keep} \in (0,1]$ (keep probability) |
| 11 | Early Stopping Patience | Stop if $S_{val}(t) < S_{val}(t-p) \quad \forall \, t \in [t-p, t]$ | $p$ = patience in epochs |
| 12 | Cross-Validation Score (k-fold) | $S_{CV} = \dfrac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} S_{i}$ | $k$ = number of folds (hyperparameter) |
| 13 | Grid Search Cardinality | $\vert \Lambda \vert = \prod_{j=1}^{d} \vert \Lambda_{j} \vert$ | $d$ = number of hyperparameters, $\Lambda_{j}$ = grid for hyperparameter $j$ |
| 14 | Bayesian Optimization Acquisition | $\alpha(\lambda) = \mu(\lambda) + \kappa \cdot \sigma(\lambda)$ | $\kappa$ = exploration-exploitation trade-off |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hyperparameters are not merely academic curiosities; they drive every deployed ML system in industry:

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Tesla, Waymo):** The learning rate schedule and batch size in their perception CNNs are tuned via massive Bayesian optimization runs costing millions of GPU-hours.
- **Medical Diagnosis (KTU-adjacent Healthcare AI):** Regularization $\lambda_{reg}$ is the **single most important** hyperparameter for ensuring that a cancer-detection model generalizes across hospitals with different imaging equipment.
- **Recommendation Systems (Netflix, Amazon):** The number of latent factors $k$ in matrix factorization is a critical hyperparameter that directly trades off model expressiveness against overfitting on sparse user-item matrices.

### 2.5 The Bias-Variance Trade-off and Hyperparameters

The expected prediction error of a model decomposes as:
$$
\mathbb{E}\left[ (y - \hat{f}(x))^{2} \right] = \text{Bias}^{2}(\hat{f}(x)) + \text{Var}(\hat{f}(x)) + \sigma^{2}_{\text{noise}}
$$

Hyperparameters are the **knobs** that navigate this trade-off:
- **Increase model capacity** (more layers, more neurons) $\Rightarrow$ **lower bias**, **higher variance**.
- **Increase regularization** ($\lambda_{reg} \uparrow$) $\Rightarrow$ **higher bias**, **lower variance**.
- **Increase training epochs** ($E \uparrow$) $\Rightarrow$ initially lowers both; eventually **raises variance** (overfitting).

This decomposition is a **favourite KTU 7-mark question** and appears in almost every December/May ESE paper.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Impact of L2 Regularization on Gradient Update

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Priority Derivation:** This is asked in 7-mark sub-questions where the student must show *why* L2 regularization prevents overfitting by shrinking weights.

**Step 1 — Start with the regularized loss function:**
$$
\mathcal{L}_{reg}(\theta) = \mathcal{L}(\theta) + \lambda_{reg} \cdot \Vert \theta \Vert_{2}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Expand the L2 norm explicitly** (for a vector of $n$ weights):
$$
\Vert \theta \Vert_{2}^{2} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \theta_{i}^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into the loss:**
$$
\mathcal{L}_{reg}(\theta) = \mathcal{L}(\theta) + \lambda_{reg} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} \theta_{i}^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the gradient with respect to $\theta_{j}$** (a single weight):
$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{reg}}{\partial \theta_{j}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \theta_{j}} + 2 \lambda_{reg} \cdot \theta_{j}
$$

> The second term arises because $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial \theta_{j}} \left( \lambda_{reg} \cdot \theta_{j}^{2} \right) = 2 \lambda_{reg} \cdot \theta_{j}$.

**Step 5 — Apply the gradient descent update rule with learning rate $\eta$:**
$$
\theta_{j}^{(t+1)} = \theta_{j}^{(t)} - \eta \cdot \left( \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \theta_{j}} + 2 \lambda_{reg} \cdot \theta_{j}^{(t)} \right)
$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange to isolate the regularization effect:**
$$
\theta_{j}^{(t+1)} = \theta_{j}^{(t)} \left( 1 - 2\eta \lambda_{reg} \right) - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \theta_{j}}
$$

**Step 7 — Interpret the result:**

The factor $(1 - 2\eta \lambda_{reg})$ acts as a **weight decay multiplier** applied to the weight *before* the gradient step. This is precisely why L2 regularization is also called **"weight decay"** in deep learning literature. Since $\eta > 0$ and $\lambda_{reg} > 0$, we have $0 < (1 - 2\eta \lambda_{reg}) < 1$ (assuming $2\eta \lambda_{reg} < 1$), which **systematically shrinks** the weight magnitude towards zero at every step, thereby reducing model complexity and overfitting.

### 3.2 Derivation: Exponential Learning Rate Decay Schedule

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** KTU frequently tests whether students can derive the closed-form expression for a learning rate schedule and explain *why* decaying $\eta$ helps convergence.

**Step 1 — Define the recurrence relation** for exponential decay:
$$
\eta_{t+1} = \gamma \cdot \eta_{t}, \quad \text{with} \quad \eta_{0} = \eta_{\text{initial}}, \quad \gamma \in (0, 1)
$$

**Step 2 — Unroll the recurrence for $t = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{1} &= \gamma \cdot \eta_{0} \\
\eta_{2} &= \gamma \cdot \eta_{1} = \gamma^{2} \cdot \eta_{0} \\
\eta_{3} &= \gamma \cdot \eta_{2} = \gamma^{3} \cdot \eta_{0} \\
\vdots \\
\eta_{t} &= \gamma^{t} \cdot \eta_{0}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Write the closed-form solution:**
$$
\eta_{t} = \eta_{0} \cdot \gamma^{t}
$$

**Step 4 — Convergence analysis:** As $t \to \infty$ and $0 < \gamma < 1$:
$$
\lim_{t \to \infty} \eta_{t} = \lim_{t \to \infty} \eta_{0} \cdot \gamma^{t} = 0
$$

This guarantees that the learning rate asymptotically approaches zero, which is desirable for fine-grained convergence in the final stages of training. The rate of decay is controlled by the hyperparameter $\gamma$.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Hyperparameter Sensitivity in Common Algorithms

The following matrix summarizes how sensitive each major ML algorithm is to specific hyperparameters. This is **exam-table material** for KTU 2024.

| Algorithm | Most Sensitive Hyperparameter | Typical Default | Effect of Increasing |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linear Regression | Regularization $\lambda_{reg}$ | $\lambda_{reg} = 1.0$ | Reduces overfitting, increases bias |
| Logistic Regression | Regularization $\lambda_{reg}$ | $\lambda_{reg} = 1.0$ | Smoother decision boundary |
| SVM (RBF Kernel) | $C$, $\gamma$ | $C=1.0$, $\gamma=\text{scale}$ | Higher $C$ $\Rightarrow$ tighter fit; Higher $\gamma$ $\Rightarrow$ more local |
| Decision Tree | $max\_depth$ | $max\_depth = \text{None}$ | Deeper $\Rightarrow$ more overfitting |
| Random Forest | $n\_estimators$, $max\_depth$ | $n=100$, $max\_depth = \text{None}$ | More trees $\Rightarrow$ lower variance (diminishing returns) |
| K-Nearest Neighbors | $K$ (neighbors) | $K=5$ | Larger $K$ $\Rightarrow$ smoother boundary, higher bias |
| Neural Network | $\eta$, batch size, $L$ (layers) | $\eta=0.001$, $B=32$ | Affects convergence speed and generalization jointly |
| Gradient Boosting | $n\_estimators$, $learning\_rate$ | $n=100$, $\eta=0.1$ | Lower $\eta$ + more trees $\Rightarrow$ better generalization |

### 3.4 Derivative Walk-Through: Why Batch Size is a Hyperparameter

**Setup:** The mini-batch gradient is computed as:
$$
g_{t} = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{i=1}^{B} \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}_{i}(\theta_{t-1})
$$

**Step 1 — Variance of the mini-batch gradient estimate** scales as:
$$
\text{Var}(g_{t}) \propto \frac{\sigma^{2}}{B}
$$

where $\sigma^{2}$ is the variance of the per-sample gradient.

**Step 2 — Interpretation:**
- Small $B$ (e.g., $B=1$, stochastic GD) $\Rightarrow$ High variance $\Rightarrow$ Noisy updates $\Rightarrow$ Can escape local minima but unstable.
- Large $B$ (e.g., $B=$ full dataset, batch GD) $\Rightarrow$ Low variance $\Rightarrow$ Stable but requires large memory and may get stuck in sharp minima.

**Step 3 — Conclusion:** Batch size $B$ is **not learned** from data; it must be **chosen by the practitioner** as a trade-off between computational efficiency, gradient noise, and generalization. Hence, $B$ is a **hyperparameter**.

### 3.5 The Bias-Variance Derivation (Decomposition)

**Step 1 — Define expected prediction error at point $x$:**
$$
\text{Error}(x) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}, y} \left[ (y - \hat{f}(x; \mathcal{D}))^{2} \right]
$$

**Step 2 — Add and subtract the true function $f(x)$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Error}(x) &= \mathbb{E}\left[ (y - f(x) + f(x) - \hat{f}(x))^{2} \right] \\
&= \mathbb{E}\left[ (y - f(x))^{2} \right] + \mathbb{E}\left[ (f(x) - \hat{f}(x))^{2} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the first term** as the irreducible noise $\sigma^{2}$.

**Step 4 — Decompose the second term** by adding and subtracting $\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}\left[ (f(x) - \hat{f}(x))^{2} \right] &= \mathbb{E}\left[ (f(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] + \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - \hat{f}(x))^{2} \right] \\
&= (f(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^{2} + \mathbb{E}\left[ (\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^{2} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final decomposition:**
$$
\text{Error}(x) = \underbrace{(f(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^{2}}_{\text{Bias}^{2}} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}\left[ (\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^{2} \right]}_{\text{Variance}} + \underbrace{\sigma^{2}}_{\text{Noise}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** This derivation is worth 7 marks on its own. The cross-term vanishes because $\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]] = 0$. Hyperparameters are the *only* practical mechanism for navigating the bias-variance trade-off in real ML pipelines.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
*Define the term "hyperparameter" in machine learning. Differentiate between hyperparameters and model parameters with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **hyperparameter** is a configuration variable in machine learning whose value is set *before* the training process begins and is *not* learned from the training data. It governs the learning process, model complexity, and regularization.

**Difference:**

| Aspect | Hyperparameter | Model Parameter |
|---|---|---|
| Learned from data? | No | Yes |
| Examples | Learning rate $\eta$, regularization $\lambda_{reg}$, number of layers $L$ | Weights $w_i$, bias $b$ |
| Set by | Practitioner / Search algorithm | Optimization (e.g., gradient descent) |

*(Full 3 marks for clear definition + correct distinction + valid example.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**
*What is the role of the learning rate $\eta$ in gradient descent? Explain what happens when $\eta$ is set too large or too small.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **learning rate $\eta$** is an optimization hyperparameter that scales the magnitude of the weight update at each iteration of gradient descent:
$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta_{t})
$$

- **$\eta$ too large** $\Rightarrow$ The optimizer overshoots the minimum, leading to **divergence or oscillation** of the loss.
- **$\eta$ too small** $\Rightarrow$ Convergence is **extremely slow** and may get trapped in **poor local minima**.

A well-tuned $\eta$ ensures fast and stable convergence. *(3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for large case, 1 for small case.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Module Choice Rule:** In Part B, students are offered an *internal choice*. They must answer either **Question A OR Question B** from the same module. Both questions below are designed as full 14-mark problems with sub-parts (a) and (b) carrying 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **regularization hyperparameters** in machine learning. Derive the gradient descent update rule for L2-regularized loss and show how it leads to *weight decay*.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Describe the **Grid Search** and **Random Search** strategies for hyperparameter tuning. Compare their computational complexity and effectiveness in high-dimensional hyperparameter spaces.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

##### **Part (a) — Regularization & Weight Decay Derivation [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 [1 mark]:** Define regularization. Regularization is a technique that adds a penalty term to the loss function to discourage overly complex models and prevent overfitting. The regularization **hyperparameter** $\lambda_{reg}$ controls the *strength* of this penalty.

**Step 2 [1 mark]:** State the L2-regularized loss function:
$$
\mathcal{L}_{reg}(\theta) = \mathcal{L}(\theta) + \lambda_{reg} \cdot \Vert \theta \Vert_{2}^{2}
$$

**Step 3 [2 marks]:** Compute the gradient with respect to weight $\theta_{j}$:
$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{reg}}{\partial \theta_{j}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \theta_{j}} + 2 \lambda_{reg} \cdot \theta_{j}
$$

**Step 4 [2 marks]:** Apply gradient descent update with learning rate $\eta$:
$$
\theta_{j}^{(t+1)} = \theta_{j}^{(t)} - \eta \left( \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \theta_{j}} + 2 \lambda_{reg} \cdot \theta_{j}^{(t)} \right)
$$

**Step 5 [1 mark]:** Rearrange to reveal weight decay:
$$
\theta_{j}^{(t+1)} = \theta_{j}^{(t)} (1 - 2\eta \lambda_{reg}) - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \theta_{j}}
$$

**Conclusion:** The factor $(1 - 2\eta \lambda_{reg}) \in (0,1)$ shrinks the weight at every step, which is known as **weight decay**. Higher $\lambda_{reg}$ $\Rightarrow$ more aggressive shrinkage $\Rightarrow$ simpler model $\Rightarrow$ less overfitting.

---

##### **Part (b) — Grid Search vs Random Search [7 Marks]**

**Grid Search [3 marks]:**
- Tries **every combination** in a predefined grid of hyperparameter values.
- If there are $d$ hyperparameters with $m$ values each, the total combinations are:
$$
\vert \Lambda \vert = m^{d}
$$
- **Advantage:** Exhaustive — guarantees finding the best combination in the grid.
- **Disadvantage:** Computational cost grows **exponentially** with $d$. Suffers from the **curse of dimensionality**.

**Random Search [3 marks]:**
- Samples $n$ random combinations uniformly from the hyperparameter space.
- **Advantage:** Can explore a **wider range** of values per hyperparameter. Empirically (Bergstra & Bengio, 2012), random search is more efficient than grid search in high-dimensional spaces because not all hyperparameters are equally important.
- **Disadvantage:** No guarantee of finding the global optimum. May miss the best combination.

**Comparison Table [1 mark]:**

| Aspect | Grid Search | Random Search |
|---|---|---|
| Coverage | Exhaustive in grid | Probabilistic |
| Cost | $O(m^{d})$ | $O(n)$ (user-controlled) |
| High-D Effectiveness | Poor | Better |
| Parallelizable | Yes | Yes |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What are the different **categories of hyperparameters** in a machine learning model? Explain each category with at least two examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable diagram, explain the **hyperparameter tuning workflow**. Discuss the **bias-variance trade-off** and explain how hyperparameters are used to navigate it.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

##### **Part (a) — Categories of Hyperparameters [7 Marks]**

Hyperparameters are classified into **four main categories** [1 mark for listing]:

**1. Structural / Architectural Hyperparameters [2 marks]:**
Define the *topology* of the model.
- Number of hidden layers $L$
- Number of neurons per layer $n^{(l)}$
- Activation function choice (ReLU, sigmoid, tanh)
- Kernel size in CNNs

**2. Optimization Hyperparameters [2 marks]:**
Control *how* the model learns from data.
- Learning rate $\eta$
- Batch size $B$
- Number of epochs $E$
- Momentum $\beta$, $\beta_{1}$, $\beta_{2}$ in Adam

**3. Regularization Hyperparameters [1 mark]:**
Control *generalization* to unseen data.
- L1/L2 penalty $\lambda_{reg}$
- Dropout rate $p_{drop}$
- Early stopping patience

**4. Data Preprocessing Hyperparameters [1 mark]:**
Control *input representation*.
- Feature scaling range (e.g., $[0, 1]$ or $[-1, 1]$)
- Number of PCA components $k$
- Vocabulary size in NLP

---

##### **Part (b) — Hyperparameter Tuning Workflow & Bias-Variance Trade-off [7 Marks]**

**Workflow [3 marks]:**

The standard hyperparameter tuning workflow consists of these steps:

1. **Split the data** into training, validation, and test sets.
2. **Define the search space** $\Lambda$ for each hyperparameter.
3. **Choose a search strategy** (Grid, Random, Bayesian).
4. **For each candidate $\lambda_i \in \Lambda$:**
   - Train the model on the training set.
   - Evaluate performance $S(\lambda_i)$ on the validation set.
5. **Select the best configuration** $\lambda^{*} = \arg\max_{\lambda} S(\lambda)$.
6. **Retrain on train + validation** with $\lambda^{*}$, then evaluate on the test set.

*(Refer to Section 4.2 for the Mermaid diagram of this workflow.)*

**Bias-Variance Trade-off [3 marks]:**

The expected prediction error decomposes as:
$$
\mathbb{E}\left[ (y - \hat{f}(x))^{2} \right] = \text{Bias}^{2}(\hat{f}(x)) + \text{Var}(\hat{f}(x)) + \sigma^{2}_{\text{noise}}
$$

- **Bias²:** Error from wrong model assumptions. High bias $\Rightarrow$ underfitting.
- **Variance:** Error from sensitivity to training data fluctuations. High variance $\Rightarrow$ overfitting.
- **Irreducible noise $\sigma^{2}$:** Cannot be reduced.

**Role of Hyperparameters [1 mark]:**
- **Increase capacity** (more layers, more neurons) $\Rightarrow$ $\downarrow$ bias, $\uparrow$ variance.
- **Increase regularization** ($\lambda_{reg} \uparrow$) $\Rightarrow$ $\uparrow$ bias, $\downarrow$ variance.
- **Tuning goal:** Find the sweet spot that minimizes total error.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (Leading to Mark Deductions):**
>
> 1. **Confusing parameters with hyperparameters** — A student who says "weights are hyperparameters" loses **2–3 marks** instantly. Weights are *learned*; hyperparameters are *set*.
> 2. **Forgetting the regularization term's derivative factor of 2** — In the L2 derivation, students often write $\lambda_{reg} \cdot \theta_{j}$ instead of $2 \lambda_{reg} \cdot \theta_{j}$. This is a **1-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Stating "Grid Search is always better"** — Wrong. In high-dimensional spaces, Random Search is empirically superior. Examiners test this explicitly.
> 4. **Not mentioning the validation set** — Hyperparameter tuning *must* be done on a validation set, not the test set. Omitting this costs **1 mark** in workflow questions.
> 5. **Mixing up Adam's $\beta_{1}$ and $\beta_{2}$** — $\beta_{1}$ is for the first moment (mean of gradients), $\beta_{2}$ is for the second moment (variance of gradients). Default values are **0.9** and **0.999** respectively.
> 6. **Skipping the bias-variance decomposition** — If a 7-mark question asks about overfitting, you *must* explicitly write the three-term decomposition to score full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Memorize These Bullet Points:**

- **Hyperparameter Definition:** Configuration set *before* training; *not* learned from data. (vs. parameters which are learned.)
- **Four Categories:** Structural, Optimization, Regularization, Data Preprocessing.
- **Learning Rate $\eta$:** Most critical optimization hyperparameter. Too large = divergence, too small = slow.
- **L2 Regularization Formula:** $\mathcal{L}_{reg} = \mathcal{L} + \lambda_{reg} \Vert \theta \Vert_{2}^{2}$
- **Weight Decay Identity:** $(1 - 2\eta \lambda_{reg})$ is the multiplicative shrinkage factor.
- **Exponential LR Decay:** $\eta_{t} = \eta_{0} \cdot \gamma^{t}$, where $\gamma \in (0, 1)$.
- **Momentum Update:** $v_{t+1} = \beta v_{t} + (1-\beta) \nabla \mathcal{L}$.
- **Adam Defaults:** $\beta_{1} = 0.9$, $\beta_{2} = 0.999$, $\epsilon = 10^{-8}$.
- **Dropout:** $p_{keep}$ is a hyperparameter; effective weight is $p_{keep} \cdot w$.
- **Grid Search Cost:** $O(m^{d})$ — exponential in number of hyperparameters.
- **Random Search:** Empirically more efficient in high-dimensional spaces.
- **Bayesian Optimization:** Uses acquisition function $\alpha(\lambda) = \mu(\lambda) + \kappa \sigma(\lambda)$.
- **Bias-Variance Decomposition:** Error = Bias² + Variance + Irreducible Noise.
- **Hyperparameter Trade-off Knob:** Capacity $\uparrow$ $\Rightarrow$ Bias $\downarrow$, Variance $\uparrow$. Regularization $\uparrow$ $\Rightarrow$ Bias $\uparrow$, Variance $\downarrow$.
- **Validation Set Rule:** Tune hyperparameters on validation set; evaluate *final* model on test set *once*.
- **Batch Size $B$:** Affects gradient variance as $\sigma^{2}/B$. Small $B$ = noisy, large $B$ = stable but memory-heavy.
- **Early Stopping Patience:** Stop training if validation score does not improve for $p$ consecutive epochs.
- **Cross-Validation:** $k$-fold CV gives robust hyperparameter selection when data is limited.
- **Final Exam Mantra:** Always distinguish *what is set* (hyperparameters) from *what is learned* (parameters). Examiners *love* testing this distinction.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transformers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT501 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transformers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transformer (Neural Architecture)**: A Transformer is a deep learning architecture introduced in the landmark paper *"Attention Is All You Need"* (Vaswani et al., 2017) that relies entirely on a **self-attention mechanism** to compute representations of its input and output sequences, completely eliminating recurrence and convolution. It uses an **encoder–decoder** structure with stacked identical layers, where each layer contains a multi-head self-attention sub-layer and a position-wise feed-forward sub-layer, connected via residual connections and layer normalization.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 4), the Transformer is positioned as the foundational architecture for modern **Natural Language Processing (NLP)**, **Large Language Models (LLMs)**, and **sequence-to-sequence (Seq2Seq)** learning tasks such as machine translation, text summarization, and question answering.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Parallel Conference Translators**

Imagine a team of 8 translators sitting around a circular table trying to translate a paragraph from English to French. In the **old way (RNN/LSTM)**, each translator could only see the word their colleague just translated — like passing a single sheet of paper around the table one word at a time. This is slow and causes the meaning of early words to "fade."

In the **Transformer way**, every translator can **simultaneously see the entire paragraph**, and each one decides, on the fly, which words are most important to focus on (e.g., the subject, the verb tense, the noun it agrees with). They also "vote" among themselves on which words to weigh heavily. This is **self-attention**. Multiple parallel "voting committees" run at once — this is **multi-head attention**. The paragraph structure (word order) is preserved by giving each word a unique **positional signature** — this is **positional encoding**.

**Result**: Faster training, better long-range understanding, and a foundation for models like **BERT** and **GPT**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Token** | The smallest unit of input (a word or sub-word piece). |
| **Embedding** | A dense vector representation of a token. |
| **Query (Q)** | The "question" vector used to probe other tokens. |
| **Key (K)** | The "label" vector of each token that Q is matched against. |
| **Value (V)** | The actual "content" vector aggregated based on attention weights. |
| **Self-Attention** | A token attends to all tokens in the same sequence (including itself). |
| **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** | The standard attention score function used in the original Transformer. |
| **Positional Encoding** | A deterministic vector added to embeddings to inject word-order information. |

### 1.4 Why Transformers Replaced RNNs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Core Engineering Advantages**
> 1. **Parallelization**: Unlike RNNs, all positions are processed simultaneously, enabling massive GPU throughput.
> 2. **Constant Path Length**: The longest signal path between any two tokens is **O(1)**, so long-range dependencies are not "forgotten" (RNNs have **O(n)** path length).
> 3. **Scalability**: Performance improves predictably with model size, data, and compute — the foundation of modern LLMs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Blueprint (High-Level)

A standard Transformer follows an **encoder–decoder** topology:

- **Encoder Stack**: $N = 6$ identical layers. Each layer has:
  1. Multi-Head Self-Attention sub-layer
  2. Position-wise Feed-Forward Network (FFN) sub-layer
  3. Residual connection + LayerNorm around each sub-layer
- **Decoder Stack**: $N = 6$ identical layers. Each layer has:
  1. **Masked** Multi-Head Self-Attention (causal — cannot peek at future tokens)
  2. Multi-Head **Cross-Attention** (queries from decoder, keys/values from encoder)
  3. Position-wise FFN
  4. Residual + LayerNorm around each sub-layer

### 2.2 Scaled Dot-Product Attention — The Heart of the Transformer

> [!NOTE]
> **How it works (Why + How)**:
> - **Why scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$?** Dot products grow in magnitude with the dimension $d_k$, pushing the softmax into regions of extremely small gradients. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ keeps the variance of the scores at 1, ensuring stable training.
> - **How it computes?** Each token emits a **Query**. We compare it (via dot product) with every other token's **Key** to get a relevance score. Softmax turns scores into probabilities (attention weights). These weights form a weighted sum of all tokens' **Value** vectors.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$ | **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** |
| 2 | $\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(QW_i^{Q},\,KW_i^{K},\,VW_i^{V})$ | A single attention head with learned projections |
| 3 | $\text{MultiHead}(Q,K,V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1,\dots,\text{head}_h)\,W^{O}$ | Concatenation + output projection of $h$ heads |
| 4 | $d_{\text{model}} = 512,\quad h = 8,\quad d_k = d_v = d_{\text{model}}/h = 64$ | Original paper's hyperparameters |
| 5 | $\text{FFN}(x) = \max(0,\,xW_1 + b_1)\,W_2 + b_2$ | Position-wise feed-forward (2 linear layers + ReLU) |
| 6 | $PE_{(pos,\,2i)} = \sin\!\left(\dfrac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}}}\right)$ | Positional encoding — **even** index |
| 7 | $PE_{(pos,\,2i+1)} = \cos\!\left(\dfrac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}}}\right)$ | Positional encoding — **odd** index |
| 8 | $\text{LayerNorm}(x) = \gamma \cdot \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sqrt{\sigma^2 + \epsilon}} + \beta$ | Layer normalization over the feature dimension |
| 9 | $\text{Sublayer}(x) = \text{LayerNorm}(x + \text{Sublayer}(x))$ | Residual + LayerNorm (post-norm, original paper) |
| 10 | $\text{Encoder output dim} = \text{Decoder input dim} = d_{\text{model}} = 512$ | Required for cross-attention compatibility |

> **CRITICAL Mermaid Note on Pipes**: Notice that the `|x|` notation was deliberately avoided in the table above; we use $\vert$ or $\mid$ if needed to keep the table parser safe.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Transformers are the backbone of:
- **Machine Translation** (Google Translate's later versions)
- **Text Generation** (GPT, LLaMA, Mistral)
- **Bidirectional Understanding** (BERT, RoBERTa)
- **Vision Transformers (ViT)** for image classification
- **Time-Series Forecasting** (Temporal Fusion Transformers, Informer)
- **Speech, Code, and Protein Sequence Modeling**

### 2.5 Sub-Layer Walkthrough

**Step A — Input Embedding + Positional Encoding**
$$x_i = \text{TokenEmbed}(w_i) + PE(i)$$

**Step B — Multi-Head Self-Attention**
$$\text{out} = \text{MultiHead}(x,\,x,\,x)$$
Note that in **self-attention**, $Q$, $K$, and $V$ all come from the same input sequence (just three different linear projections of it).

**Step C — Add & Norm**
$$x' = \text{LayerNorm}(x + \text{out})$$

**Step D — Feed-Forward Network (applied independently to each position)**
$$x'' = \text{LayerNorm}(x' + \text{FFN}(x'))$$

**Step E — Cross-Attention in Decoder**
Here, $Q$ comes from the decoder, while $K$ and $V$ come from the encoder output. This is what allows the decoder to "look at" the source sentence while generating the target sentence.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Dot-Product to Scaled Dot-Product Attention

We want a function that, given a **query** $q \in \mathbb{R}^{d_k}$, finds which **keys** $k_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d_k}$ are most relevant, and returns a weighted sum of corresponding **values** $v_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d_v}$.

**Step 1 — Raw Compatibility Score**

Take the dot product of $q$ with each key. This measures raw angular similarity:

$$s_i = q \cdot k_i = q^{\top} k_i$$

**Step 2 — Stack All Scores into a Vector**

For efficiency, we matrix-stack queries into $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$ and keys into $K \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$:

$$S = QK^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$$

**Step 3 — Convert Scores to a Probability Distribution with Softmax**

$$A = \text{softmax}(S),\quad A_{ij} = \frac{\exp(S_{ij})}{\sum_{j'=1}^{n} \exp(S_{ij'})}$$

**Step 4 — Why We Need the $\sqrt{d_k}$ Scaling**

Assume the components of $q$ and $k$ are independent random variables with mean **0** and variance **1**. Then the variance of their dot product is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(q^{\top} k) &= \text{Var}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{d_k} q_i k_i\right) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} \text{Var}(q_i k_i) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} \mathbb{E}[q_i^2]\,\mathbb{E}[k_i^2] \quad \text{(independence, zero mean)} \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} 1 \cdot 1 = d_k
\end{aligned}
$$

So the dot product has **variance $d_k$** and grows with dimension. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ standardizes it back to unit variance:

$$S_{\text{scaled}} = \frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Softmax and Multiply by Values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) &= \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V \\
&= A \cdot V
\end{aligned}
$$

Each output row is a convex combination (weighted average) of all value vectors, where the weights are the attention probabilities.

### 3.2 Derivation: Positional Encoding Formulas

Since self-attention is **permutation-equivariant** (it treats inputs as a bag of tokens, ignoring order), we must inject word-order information manually.

**Step 1 — Choose a Frequency Spectrum**

We want a family of sinusoids with wavelengths forming a geometric progression from $2\pi$ to $10000 \cdot 2\pi$. For dimension index $2i$ (even) and $2i+1$ (odd) at position $pos$:

$$PE_{(pos,\,2i)} = \sin\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}}}\right)$$

$$PE_{(pos,\,2i+1)} = \cos\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}}}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Why Sin/Cos Specifically?**

> [!NOTE]
> - The function $PE(pos+k)$ can be expressed as a **linear function of $PE(pos)$**, allowing the model to learn **relative position** easily.
> - For any fixed offset $k$, there exists a fixed linear transformation $M_k$ such that $PE(pos+k) = M_k \cdot PE(pos)$.

**Step 3 — Worked Example (small $d_{\text{model}}=4$, $pos=0,1,2$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PE(0) &= [\sin(0),\cos(0),\sin(0),\cos(0)] = [0, 1, 0, 1] \\
PE(1) &= [\sin(1),\cos(1),\sin(0.01),\cos(0.01)] \approx [0.841,\ 0.540,\ 0.010,\ 1.000] \\
PE(2) &= [\sin(2),\cos(2),\sin(0.02),\cos(0.02)] \approx [0.909,\ -0.416,\ 0.020,\ 1.000]
\end{aligned}
$$

Lower dimensions oscillate rapidly (fine-grained position); higher dimensions oscillate slowly (coarse-grained position).

### 3.3 Derivation: Multi-Head Attention Output Shape

Let $h = 8$ (number of heads), $d_{\text{model}} = 512$.

**Step 1 — Per-Head Dimension**

$$d_k = d_v = \frac{d_{\text{model}}}{h} = \frac{512}{8} = 64$$

**Step 2 — Project Inputs into $h$ Subspaces**

Each head $i$ has its own learned projection matrices:
- $W_i^{Q} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_k}$
- $W_i^{K} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_k}$
- $W_i^{V} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_v}$

**Step 3 — Compute Per-Head Output**

$$\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}\!\left(QW_i^{Q},\ KW_i^{K},\ VW_i^{V}\right) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$$

**Step 4 — Concatenate All Heads**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h) &\in \mathbb{R}^{n \times (h \cdot d_v)} \\
&= \mathbb{R}^{n \times 512}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Output Projection**

$$\text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1,\ldots,\text{head}_h)\,W^{O}$$

where $W^{O} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_{\text{model}}}$.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Attention Variants

| Variant | Complexity (per layer) | Sequential Ops | Max Path Length |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Self-Attention** | $O(n^2 \cdot d)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Recurrent (RNN)** | $O(n \cdot d^2)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| **Convolutional** | $O(k \cdot n \cdot d^2)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(\log_k n)$ |
| **Self-Attn (restricted, $r$ neighbors)** | $O(r \cdot n \cdot d)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(n/r)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight**: Self-attention is the only mechanism with constant path length $O(1)$ AND constant sequential operations $O(1)$, making it uniquely fast on modern parallel hardware (GPUs/TPUs) for long sequences.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Full Transformer Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    Input["Input Tokens w1 to wN"] --> Emb["Token Embedding"]
    Emb --> AddPE["Add Positional Encoding"]
    AddPE --> EncIn["Encoder Input x dmodel"]

    EncIn --> EncAttn["Multi-Head Self-Attention"]
    EncAttn --> AddNorm1["Add and LayerNorm"]
    AddNorm1 --> FFN1["Feed-Forward Network"]
    FFN1 --> AddNorm2["Add and LayerNorm"]
    AddNorm2 --> EncOut["Encoder Output"]

    EncOut --> DecK["Keys and Values to Decoder"]
    AddNorm2 --> DecIn["Decoder Input from previous layer"]
    DecIn --> MaskAttn["Masked Multi-Head Self-Attention"]
    MaskAttn --> AddNorm3["Add and LayerNorm"]
    AddNorm3 --> CrossAttn["Multi-Head Cross-Attention"]
    DecK --> CrossAttn
    CrossAttn --> AddNorm4["Add and LayerNorm"]
    AddNorm4 --> FFN2["Feed-Forward Network"]
    FFN2 --> AddNorm5["Add and LayerNorm"]
    AddNorm5 --> Linear["Linear Projection to Vocabulary"]
    Linear --> Softmax["Softmax over Vocabulary"]
    Softmax --> Output["Predicted Next Token"]
```

### 4.2 Scaled Dot-Product Attention Internal Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    Q["Query Matrix Q"] --> Mul1["Matrix Multiply QK transpose"]
    K["Key Matrix K"] --> Mul1
    Mul1 --> Scale["Scale by 1 over sqrt dk"]
    Scale --> Mask["Optional Mask future tokens"]
    Mask --> SM["Softmax over Key axis"]
    SM --> AW["Attention Weights A"]
    AW --> Mul2["Matrix Multiply A times V"]
    V["Value Matrix V"] --> Mul2
    Mul2 --> Out["Output Context Vectors"]
```

### 4.3 Multi-Head Attention as Parallel Committees

```mermaid
graph TB
    X["Input X dmodel x n"] --> ProjQ["Linear WQ"]
    X --> ProjK["Linear WK"]
    X --> ProjV["Linear WV"]

    ProjQ --> Split1["Split into h heads"]
    ProjK --> Split2["Split into h heads"]
    ProjV --> Split3["Split into h heads"]

    Split1 --> H1["Head 1 Attention"]
    Split2 --> H1
    Split3 --> H1

    Split1 --> H2["Head 2 Attention"]
    Split2 --> H2
    Split3 --> H2

    Split1 --> Hh["Head h Attention"]
    Split2 --> Hh
    Split3 --> Hh

    H1 --> Concat["Concat all heads"]
    H2 --> Concat
    Hh --> Concat
    Concat --> WO["Linear Projection WO"]
    WO --> Out["Multi-Head Output"]
```

### 4.4 Single Encoder Layer — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    In["Layer Input x"] --> SA["Self-Attention Sublayer"]
    SA --> Add1["Add Residual"]
    In --> Add1
    Add1 --> LN1["LayerNorm"]
    LN1 --> FFNpos["Position-wise FFN"]
    FFNpos --> Add2["Add Residual"]
    LN1 --> Add2
    Add2 --> LN2["LayerNorm"]
    LN2 --> Out["Layer Output"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two sub-layers per encoder block?**
> The original paper chose exactly 2 (self-attention + FFN). Modern variants like **Pre-LN Transformers** swap the order of Add/LayerNorm for better training stability at extreme depths. Both are accepted knowledge for the KTU 2024 syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q1.a]**
> **Q1. Define Self-Attention. How is it different from cross-attention?**

**Model Answer:**
Self-Attention is a mechanism where the Query, Key, and Value vectors all originate from the **same input sequence**. Each token computes a weighted representation of every other token (including itself) in the sequence. This allows the model to capture intra-sequence relationships regardless of token distance.

In **Cross-Attention**, the Query vectors come from one sequence (e.g., the decoder), while the Key and Value vectors come from a **different** sequence (e.g., the encoder output). Cross-attention is what enables the decoder to "attend to" the source language while generating the target language in machine translation.

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q1.b]**
> **Q2. Why do we divide the dot product $QK^{\top}$ by $\sqrt{d_k}$ inside the attention function?**

**Model Answer:**
Assuming the components of $Q$ and $K$ are independent with zero mean and unit variance, the dot product has variance equal to $d_k$. As $d_k$ grows, the dot-product magnitudes grow, pushing the softmax into saturation regions with very small gradients. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ normalizes the variance back to **1**, preserving stable gradient flow and preventing training collapse. This is the core engineering reason for the "**scaled**" in *Scaled Dot-Product Attention*.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q2]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**(a) Explain the architecture of a Transformer in detail with a neat block diagram. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Transformer, introduced by Vaswani et al. (2017), is an encoder–decoder architecture based entirely on attention mechanisms, eliminating recurrence and convolution.

**1. Encoder Block (×$N$ layers, typically $N=6$):**
   - **Input Embedding + Positional Encoding**: Token embeddings are augmented with sinusoidal positional vectors so that the model knows word order.
   - **Multi-Head Self-Attention Sub-layer**: Computes scaled dot-product attention $h$ times in parallel, then concatenates and projects.
   - **Add & LayerNorm**: Residual connection plus layer normalization.
   - **Position-wise FFN**: Two linear layers with ReLU activation applied independently to each position: $\text{FFN}(x) = \max(0, xW_1+b_1)W_2 + b_2$.

**2. Decoder Block (×$N$ layers):**
   - **Masked Multi-Head Self-Attention**: Prevents attending to future positions (causal masking).
   - **Multi-Head Cross-Attention**: Queries come from the decoder; Keys and Values come from the encoder output.
   - **Position-wise FFN** and **Add & LayerNorm** sub-layers (identical to encoder).

**3. Final Output:**
   - Linear projection to vocabulary size, followed by **softmax** to produce token probabilities.

**4. Hyperparameters (original paper):**
   - $d_{\text{model}} = 512$, $h = 8$, $d_k = d_v = 64$, $d_{\text{ff}} = 2048$, dropout = 0.1.

> *(Attach the Mermaid diagram from SECTION 4.1 as the block diagram for full 7 marks.)*

**(b) Derive the Scaled Dot-Product Attention formula and explain each term. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given input matrices:
- $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$ (queries)
- $K \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$ (keys)
- $V \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$ (values)

**Step 1 — Compute raw compatibility scores** via matrix multiplication:
$$S = QK^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$$

**Step 2 — Scale to control variance** (variance of $QK^{\top}$ is $d_k$):
$$S_{\text{scaled}} = \frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply softmax row-wise** to get attention weights summing to 1:
$$A = \text{softmax}(S_{\text{scaled}}),\quad A_{ij} = \frac{\exp(S_{\text{scaled},ij})}{\sum_{j'} \exp(S_{\text{scaled},ij'})}$$

**Step 4 — Weighted aggregation of values**:
$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = A \cdot V$$

**Final formula**:
$$\boxed{\;\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V\;}$$

**Term-by-term meaning**:
- $QK^{\top}$ — pairwise similarity between every query and every key.
- $\sqrt{d_k}$ — stabilizer preventing softmax saturation.
- $\text{softmax}$ — converts scores into a valid probability distribution.
- $V$ — content to be retrieved; the output is a soft lookup based on attention weights.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) Explain the concept of Multi-Head Attention. Why is it preferred over a single attention head? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Multi-Head Attention runs **$h$ attention functions in parallel**, each on a different learned linear projection of $Q$, $K$, $V$. The outputs are concatenated and linearly projected.

**Mathematical Formulation**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{head}_i &= \text{Attention}(QW_i^{Q},\ KW_i^{K},\ VW_i^{V}) \\
\text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) &= \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1,\ldots,\text{head}_h)\,W^{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why multiple heads?**
1. **Diverse Subspaces**: Each head can specialize in a different linguistic relationship — e.g., one head tracks syntactic dependencies (subject–verb), another tracks coreference, another tracks positional proximity.
2. **Stable Average**: Multiple heads provide redundancy; if one head underperforms on a certain pattern, others can compensate.
3. **Richer Representations**: Concatenation across heads gives the next layer access to multiple "views" of the same sequence simultaneously.
4. **Empirical Performance**: Single-head attention underfits; the original paper demonstrated that 8 heads gave the best translation BLEU scores in their experiments.

With $d_{\text{model}} = 512$ and $h = 8$, each head works in a 64-dimensional subspace, keeping the total compute comparable to a single 512-dim attention.

**(b) What is Positional Encoding? Derive the sinusoidal positional encoding formulas used in the original Transformer. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition**: Positional Encoding is a deterministic vector $PE(pos) \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}}}$ added to each token embedding to inject information about the token's position in the sequence. Without it, self-attention would be permutation-invariant and could not distinguish "dog bites man" from "man bites dog."

**Why Sinusoidal?**
- The wavelengths form a geometric progression, allowing the model to attend to **relative positions** easily.
- The function $PE(pos+k)$ is a linear function of $PE(pos)$, which means relative offsets can be learned.

**Derivation:**

We want a set of sinusoids of varying frequencies. For position $pos$ and dimension index $i$:

$$PE_{(pos,\,i)} = 
\begin{cases}
\sin\!\left(\dfrac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}}}\right) & \text{if } i \text{ is even} \\
\cos\!\left(\dfrac{pos}{10000^{2(i-1)/d_{\text{model}}}}\right) & \text{if } i \text{ is odd}
\end{cases}
$$

**Equivalently (using $2i$ for even index, $2i+1$ for odd)**:
$$PE_{(pos,\,2i)} = \sin\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}}}\right), \quad PE_{(pos,\,2i+1)} = \cos\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}}}\right)$$

**Output for $pos=0$** is the zero vector, anchoring the start of every sequence.

**Final embedding**:
$$\text{Input}_i = \text{TokenEmbed}(w_i) + PE(i)$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Module 4: Transformers)**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{d_k}$**: A shocking number of students write $QK^{\top}$ *without* the scaling factor. This loses at least **2 marks** in any derivation.
> 2. **Confusing Q, K, V sources**: In self-attention, all three come from the **same** input. In cross-attention, only Q comes from the decoder. Mixing this up costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **No diagram in 7-mark architecture questions**: Always draw the encoder–decoder block diagram (use Mermaid or clean hand-drawn boxes). A 7-mark question without a diagram typically caps at 5 marks.
> 4. **Skipping masking**: When describing the decoder's first attention, you **must** mention causal masking (or "look-ahead mask"). Examiners expect this explicitly.
> 5. **Confusing LayerNorm with BatchNorm**: LayerNorm normalizes across the **feature** dimension per sample. BatchNorm normalizes across the **batch** dimension per feature. Getting this wrong loses 1 mark.
> 6. **FFN equation**: Writing $\tanh$ instead of ReLU. The original Transformer uses ReLU: $\max(0, xW_1 + b_1)$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**
> - **Transformer = Encoder + Decoder**, each with $N=6$ identical layers, all using **self-attention** (no recurrence, no convolution).
> - **Scaled Dot-Product Attention**: $\text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$. The $\sqrt{d_k}$ is **non-negotiable**.
> - **Multi-Head Attention** = $h$ parallel attentions, concatenated and projected via $W^O$. Default: $h=8$, $d_k=d_v=64$.
> - **Positional Encoding** is **sinusoidal** (sin for even, cos for odd indices) and **added**, not concatenated, to token embeddings.
> - **Self-Attention** = Q, K, V all from same input. **Cross-Attention** = Q from decoder, K & V from encoder.
> - **Decoder's first sub-layer is MASKED** so the model cannot see future tokens during training.
> - **Each sub-layer wrapped with Residual Connection + LayerNorm** (post-norm in original, pre-norm in modern variants).
> - **FFN** is position-wise: 2 linear layers with ReLU in between, inner dimension = 2048.
> - **Complexity per layer**: $O(n^2 \cdot d)$ — quadratic in sequence length, which is the Transformer's main bottleneck for long sequences.
> - **Max path length = $O(1)$** — this is the architectural property that makes Transformers capture long-range dependencies better than RNNs.
> - **Applications to remember for KTU**: BERT (encoder-only), GPT (decoder-only), T5/mBART (encoder–decoder), ViT (vision), Time-series Transformers.
> - **Equation to memorize verbatim**: $\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$.
